headline: Home news Our beer's so strong it's binge drink cure
author: Jamie Beatson and Gordon Tait
text:
Fury at brewer's bizarre claim

A BREWERY boss last night launched Britain's strongest beer - claiming it would CURE binge drinking.

James Watt, of Brewdog, said their Tokyo beer - at 12 per cent proof nearly as strong as wine - will curb boozing as people will drink less because it is so strong.

The brew from Fraserburgh, Aberdeenshire, will cost Pounds 4 a bottle. Managing director Mr Watt said it tasted better than other brands.

And he claimed that this would make people guzzle less of the brew.

Hardcore

Mr Watt, 25, said yesterday: "We produce hardcore beer, which has a lot of flavour and bite, consequently people drink much less of it.

"They drink them to enjoy the spectrum of flavours, not to get drunk.

"Mass-produced beers taste so bland that people tend to drink a lot of them. We in fact are the cure - not the problem."

His comments were slated by alcohol awareness groups and health campaigners who last night branded his marketing strategy "irresponsible".

Jack Law, of Alcohol Focus Scotland, said the strong brew would be especially tempting for teens and alcoholics.

He said: "Alcohol should be marketed on taste and quality rather than strength.

"Why can't they use their technology and skills to produce a great-tasting low-alcohol beer instead?

"Super-strength drinks are often favoured by young people and problem drinkers. Is this really who the brewery wants to target?"

One 330ml bottle of Tokyo - made with jasmine and cranberries - will bring drinkers up to four units.

That is a man's daily recommended limit - and almost TWICE the maximum of what a woman should have.

But Mr Watt, left, insisted higher prices and more units per bottle will prevent people from bingeing.

A spokeswoman for BMA Scotland said: "Many people don't realise how many units of alcohol they drink and it's unlikely this beer will help."

Brewdog - launched last year by ex-law student Mr Watt and brewing graduate Martin Dickie, also 25 - has previously clashed with the industry watchdog over ads.

Its "beer for punks" and Rip Tide campaigns were said to glorify anti-social behaviour.

gordontait@the-sun.co.uk

(C) The Sun, 2008

headline: Trawl you need after a hard day making millions; BEER TYCOON CHILLS OUT... ON FISHING BOAT
author: MATT BENDORIS
text:
YOUNG millionaire and TV star James Watt will put his life on the line when he swaps his pampered lifestyle —o work on a fishing boat.

The 26-year-old entrepreneur is co-owner of trendy beer label BrewDog.

He starred in the BBC show Last Millionaire and this month will also feature on BBC 2's Oz And James Drink To Britain.

But last night, James set sail on the 200ft, 1,000-ton fishing vessel Ocean Quest.

Lure

He will spend the next six weeks hauling nets in the storm-lashed North Atlantic.

James faces being battered by 80ft waves, while pods of killer whales are never far behind the boat.

He said: "People have said I'm crazy and ask why I do it. But it's my father Jim's boat. I come from a family of fisherman and there is something about the lure of the ocean.

"When I've been away from it for a long time, I just have to get back to the sea." Watt's two worlds couldn't be further apart. When he's not at sea, he flies to trade shows in America and the Far East to thrash out megabucks deals.

But instead of first-class travel and five-star hotels, Watt will be crammed into a bunk bed living alongside 10 other crew members.

Not that he'll get much time to sleep.

He explained: "Like the rest of the crew I'm normally on deck for around 20 hours a day —sually in the middle of a storm.

"Actually at this time of year a force eight or nine gale is nothing really. It gets much worse.

"We regularly see waves in excess of 65ft.

"The biggest I have seen was maybe 82ft. It can get pretty scary out there. But the worst was last January.

"We were sailing out from the north of Scotland on quite a poor night when got hit by a rogue wave.

"We have inch-thick steel on the side of the boat, and it managed to smash that it in.

Capsized

"The lights from the top of the mast were washed away.

It was as if we'd hit a brick wall. I thought we'd capsized.

"My dad has been at sea for 45 years and said that was the worst.

"It can be terrifying. But you know and accept the risks. It just come with the job." He added: "We also get lots of killer whales popping up alongside the boat. They keep you on your toes.

"And a couple of years back I nearly came a cropper when a line snapped.

"We were hauling 400 tons of mackerel when the boat took a heave.

"A 14in-thick nylon rope snapped a foot away from my head. If it had hit me I wouldn't be here today." BrewDog was formed in Fraserburgh three years ago by James and his pal Martin Dickie, 26.

It now produces 200,000 bottles a month and sells to 11 countries. They are also on the shelves at Tescos.

They became a big hit thanks to their 'edgy' beer names like Speedball, Tokyo, Paradox, Rip Tide, Trashy Blonde and Punk IPA.

Last year they won an eight-month court battle with the drinks forum The Portman Group after they complained BrewDog's flagship product Punk IPA was described on the label as an "aggressive beer" —inking it with anti-social behaviour.

But an appeal agreed with the microbrewery that the phrase actually described the beer's flavour.

On January 20, Watt and Martin will appear on Oz And James Drink To Britain, featuring wine expert Oz Clarke and former Top Gear presenter James May.

Tramps

Watt said: "The guys travel around in an old caravan meeting drinks producers.

We met them in Princess Street and end up sharing our beers with them on a park bench like tramps.

"It was good fun and the guys are really funny." When Watt returns from his fishing trip mid-February, he will immediately jet off across the world promoting the BrewDog brand.

But single James said: "I still call myself a fisherman first and foremost.

"No matter how well Brew- Dog is doing, I'll always go back to sea."

{I'm normally on deck for around 20 hours a day —sually in a storm|

HARD CASE ... James is packed for the Ocean Quest, top, and with May and Oz on new show

headline: BEER BANNED
text:
A BEER called Speedball is being pulled from shelves after a watchdog ruled it promotes the drug mix that killed film stars John Belushi and River Phoenix.

Speedballing is a term for combining heroin and cocaine. Brewdog, of Fraserbugh, Scotland, calls its drink a "Class A Ale" containing "a vicious cocktail of active ingredients" which creates a "happy-sad" effect. Watchdog The Portman Group said the marketing was "grossly irresponsible".

headline: Speedball beer 'jibe'
text:
A BREWERY said yesterday it named a beer after a lethal drugs cocktail to "wind up" watchdogs.

Brewdog was forced to remove any of its 1,184 bottles of Speedball —amed after a cocaine-heroin mix —eft on shelves after a Portman Group probe.

Martin Dickie, boss of the Fraserburgh, Aberdeenshire, brewery, said: "They should be focusing on more important things."

headline: The only deckhand who had a teddy bear; SAYS JAMES WATT
text:
AT just 26, James Watt is managing director of his own award-winning Scottish brewery, BrewDog. He also shone on the BBC3 show The Last Millionaire.

He's never been afraid of hard work though, starting his North Atlantic fishing career at just seven years old.

He reveals to LINDSEY JOHNSTONE that he was well-paid for his efforts . .

What was your first job? I used to spend all my summer holidays working on my dad's North Atlantic trawler.

Where was it and how old were you? I spent my first summer at sea aged only seven. I think I must have been the only fisherman in the fleet who still slept with a teddy.

Job description? Makeshift ship's cat/tea-maker/fish counter.

First pay packet? Skeletor model, from He-man And The Masters Of The Universe.

I spent a week on my dad's fishing boat when I was seven for this most extravagant of remunerations. I could have happily retired that very day.

What was your greatest achievement in the job? I became a fully-qualified captain aged 23. The teddy now stays on dry land though. When I am not running BrewDog I relax by spending January on the North Atlantic.

Biggest mistake? Taking the aforementioned teddy to work — source of great amusement for some and massive embarrassment for others.

Was there someone who played an important role, good or bad, in your job? The whole experience of spending time on a North Atlantic trawler instils a strong work ethic and team mentality.

Compared to 24 hours on deck in a January storm, sitting at a desk all day making calls is easy.

When and why did you leave? I didn't — still spend two months a year at sea.

Apparently running Scotland's largest independent brewery is not quite stressful enough for me.

Best bit of advice given to you? Always believe 100 per cent in yourself and your ideas —f you don't, no one else will.

You need a level of almost unrealistic confidence in yourself and your own ability.

Top tip for getting a job? Find something you are passionate, something you are excited about.

Employers are looking for passion and commitment far more than qualifications.

SAYS JAMES WATT

headline: Fishing tycoon's ex to get £million
author: DAVE FINLAY
text:
A WEALTHY fisherman was yesterday ordered to pay his ex-wife nearly £.4MILLION in a divorce settlement —ver double what he first offered her.

James Watt, 53, must give wife of 25 years Ann £million immediately —nd has six months to rustle up the remainder.

The payout comes after she told a divorce hearing that at the time they split in 2002 he tried to force her to accept just £.5million, despite saying he was worth £0 to £2million.

And she said he'd also told her he'd signed a "document" that meant she would get NOTHING.

Entitled

But judge Lady Smith yesterday said Mrs Watt was entitled to £.238million under a 48/52 per cent split in favour of Mr Watt. She said as Mrs Watt already has some of Mr Watt's property, he must pay £.369million.

Mr Watt, of Rosehearty, Aberdeenshire, made his millions fishing and as a businessman. Mrs Watt, of Peterhead, is a teacher.

They married in 1977, and have a son, James, a co-founder of Fraserburghbased brewery Brewdog.

Mrs Watt had sought half the couple's wealth.

Granting a divorce, Lady Smith told the Court of Session in Edinburgh the main issue in debate had been the value of Mr Watt's "business interests."

She ordered the payout after telling how Mr Watt saw "impressive" profits with a firm, Mewstead, which built and ran two "super trawlers", Ocean Quest and Ocean Venture.

She said he signed the 2002 "document" with partners, thinking it would help resist a divorce claim.

headline: Cheers to eco beer
text:
BEER bosses last night revealed ambitious plans to build the world's first carbon neutral brewery —n Scotland.

BrewDog, which saw profits rocket 450 per cent last year, claims its new "self-sufficient" base will churn out ZERO waste.

And its workforce will almost double, with 15 extra workers hired to man the site at Potterton, near Aberdeen.

Head brewer Martin Dickie, 26, said: "It will make about eight brews a day —hat's ten times more beer than we're producing at the moment.

We're struggling to produce enough."

headline: Blast for 18% ale
text:
A BREWERY was slammed last night for producing Britain's strongest beer —ith 18.2 per cent alcohol content.

A 330ml bottle of Tokyo* contains six units of alcohol, twice the recommended daily dose.

A British Liver Trust spokeswoman said: "Glamorising the strength of beer, or any alcoholic drink, will not alleviate irresponsible binge-drinking culture."

James Watt, whose BrewDog firm make Tokyo* in Fraserburgh, Aberdeenshire, actually claims it could cut bingeing.

He said: "Mass market lagers are so bland you are seduced into drinking a lot of them."

headline: STORM BREWS OVER 18% BEER; Alarm at ale that's '3 pints in a bottle'
author: LUCINDA CAMERON
text:
BREWERY chiefs were branded "irresponsible" last night for launching an 18.2 PER CENT beer which they claim is Britain's strongest.

Just one bottle of powerful Tokyo* contains six units of alcohol —he same as THREE PINTS of normal-strength lager.

Its Scots creators BrewDog insist the ale will actually CURE binge-drinking —ecause it's so potent, punters will down less of it.

But dismayed health experts rubbished those claims last night.

Jack Law, chief exec of Alcohol Focus Scotland, said: "They're completely deluded if they think an 18.2 per cent beer will solve Scotland's alcohol problems.

"It's utterly irresponsible to bring out a beer that is so strong when Scotland is facing unprecedented levels of alcohol-related harm."

BrewDog, based in Fraserburgh, Aberdeenshire, sparked controversy when it launched its original 12 per cent Tokyo beer last year.

But now, it's brewed an even stronger Tokyo*, using jasmine, cranberries, malt and hops fermented with a champagne yeast.

Boss James Watt —ho tuns the operation with Martin Dickie —aid his firm was helping to promote responsible drinking by educating people about booze.

He said: "Our hardcore beers are loaded with flavour, bite and body, so you drink less of them.

"Industrially-brewed lagers are so bland and tasteless that you're seduced into drinking a lot of them. The beers we make at BrewDog, including Tokyo*, provide a cure to binge beer-drinking."

But health campaigners insist quaffing such a strong brew could trigger problems.

Damaging Mr Law added: "Just one bottle of beer contains six units of alcohol —wice the recommended daily limit."

The British Liver Trust pointed out a pint would contain 12 units —he equivalent of a bottle of wine.

A spokeswoman said: "Glamorising the strength of beer won't deter people from drinking heavily. On the contrary, the notion of binge-drinking is to get drunk quick, so surely this beer will help people on their way."

A BMA Scotland spokeswoman said: "A high-alcohol content is equally damaging as drinking vast quantities."

BrewDog has produced 3,000 limited edition bottles, which will only be available through its website or specialist retailers.

Earlier this year, watchdog the Portman Group wanted the firm's Speedball drink withdrawn because it shared a name with an infamous drugs cocktail.

scottish-sun@the-sun.co.uk

Potent ... Scots ale Tokyo* Brew two ... beer creators Dickie and Watt

headline: BEER NUTS
author: BRIAN LEWIS
text:
Sun man takes 18% lager challenge and can't finish 3rd bottle

AS PUNTERS supped their normal lagers, spirits and wines, I sat there staring down the barrel of an 18.2 per cent proof bottle of Tokyo* beer.

My drinking buddie Darren Love ordered up a pint of Tennent's at Oscars Bar & Grill in Edinburgh.

While I had the latest Brewdog creation that they claim is the strongest ale in the country.

Burn The first sip gave no indication of the strength, just that it was not your normal tipple.

It has a slight burn as it goes down —imilar to a spirit —nd I could feel it affecting me after just half a bottle.

I wouldn't say I was drunk after finishing the equivalent of three pints of normal lager, but I certainly wouldn't drive.

I then tried to neck my second bottle of the sickly-sweet rocket fuel and it was definitely not pretty.

I struggled to get it down and by the end I was definitely tipsy, if not worse.

I tried to go for a third ... but couldn't face the whole thing.

Brewdog claim it will stop people drinking too much and curb binge boozing as they will sup slower. Well, that is true.

I certainly drank a lot slower and Darren, 37, easily necked a couple of pints before I had finished my first.

Its sweet stout flavour might not be to everyone's taste but it certainly has a kick.

Though why anyone would pay a tenner for the experience is totally beyond me.

Darren left happy. I ordered a pint of cooking lager to take away the taste then rushed home to brush my teeth.

And I may be late into the office this afternoon.

headline: And the winner is ... Ruby, a nutty but nice ale that will go down a treat with the ladies
author: Jack Malvern
text:
A silence befalls the judging panel of qualified beer drinkers. One lifts his glass to the light to examine hue and clarity. Another examines the slippage of the head down the side of the glass. Others sniff, swill, quaff and smack their lips before cleansing their palates with water biscuits.

The ritual for choosing the Champion Beer of Britain, at the Great British Beer Festival in Earls Court, West London, is as serious as it is longwinded, but after several hours a winner emerges. A baritone cheer erupts from the crowd of barrel-chested beer enthusiasts as Rudgate Ruby Mild is named the victor. Roger Protz, editor of the Good Beer Guide, says: "I've just come from tasting nine beers. I'm astonished I can actually speak."

Equally astonished are Craig Lee and Jamie Allen, in charge of the seven-man brewing operation in Tockwith, North Yorkshire, where Ruby Mild is made. They have been brewing the beer to the same recipe for 14 years —o little interest, with the exception of a silver medal in the mild category last year. Suddenly they will face a surge in demand that will stretch their capacity to the limit.

At present they produce 40 barrels a week —he equivalent of 11,520 pints —nd have the capacity to produce 20 more. Previous winners such as the Coniston Brewing Company in Cumbria had to get a larger brewery to help to make their Bluebird Bitter, because demand outstripped supply.

Mr Lee, brewer, administrator, hops masher and delivery man, said that he hoped that orders would not all come in at once. "We're only working a five-day week at the moment, so we can work a seven-day week, although I don't know what some of the lads will say about that," he said. "There may be an initial demand that could be difficult to keep up with. We may have to say, 'You'll have to wait a couple of weeks'."

The beer, which is described in the Good Beer Guide as a "nutty, rich ruby ale, stronger than usual for a mild", won by the biggest margin in living memory. One judge remarked that it "stood out a mile" from its nearest competitor.

Mr Lee is bemused by his victory, given that mild beers, which were originally created to slake the thirst of miners, are unfashionable compared with golden ales. "We've certainly not changed our tastes. Perhaps tastes have caught up with us."

He believes that the sweeter taste could also attract "the lady drinker" —hose who, according to the Campaign for Real Ale (Camra), have recently embraced real ale. A survey suggests that 30 per cent of women drinkers have tried it, compared with 16 per cent in 2008.

The real ale enthusiasts at Earls Court, however, appeared to be mostly white, middle-aged men who would be an asset to any tug-of-war team, and occasionally their wives, who could intervene if proceedings became rowdy. Stalls selling "fresh pork scratchings" and pork pies seemed to be beating the credit crunch. The Camra shop was selling an unusually large number of XXXL T-shirts.

Kirrily Waldhorn, one of the judges, and editor of an Australian real ale magazine, observed: "Beer isn't particularly friendly to women. You get a pint glass with beer slopping over the side. Women don't want that." But selling beer to women in Britain may still be an easier task than selling real ale in Australia. "When you talk about beer in Australia they think, 'Chuck it down your throat and get drunk'. Things like milds and bitters are not really drunk. Anything served above 5C does not go down well."

The winners

Champion beer of Britain Rudgate Ruby, pictured Mild bitter Surrey Hills Ranmore Ale Best bitter Southport Golden Sands

Golden ale Dark Star American Pale Ale Strong bitter West Berkshire Dr Hexter's Healer Speciality Nethergate Umbel Magna Winter beer Oakham Attila

Thirst for knowledge

Beer has been brewed since Neolithic times, when Man began harvesting cereals

Britain now has 660 breweries producing real ale, the highest number since the Second World War. Between them they brew approximately 2,500 different beers each year

Seventy breweries were founded last year alone, but harsh trading conditions mean that 57 pubs are closing every month 6 24 million pints of beer are consumed in Britain every day

Four men founded Camra, the Campaign for Real Ale, in 1971. This week it enrolled its 100,000th member. Its Good Beer Guide was first published in 1974 and sold 30,000 copies within seven months

Camra has 647 overseas members in countries including New Zealand, Vietnam, Costa Rica, Saudia Arabia and Taiwan. More than 1,600 of its members are doctors and at least 80 have been ordained

Peterborough is Camra's largest branch, with about 2,500 members

BrewDog, the Aberdeenshire brewery, last month introduced Tokyo, Britain's strongest beer at 14 per cent. Only 3,000 bottles were made

The Great British Beer Festival runs at Earls Court until Saturday Sources: Camra, The Publican, British Beer and Pub Association, Times archives

Real ale is finding a new market among women, brewers believe

headline: CHARGE your glasses and raise [...]
author: Donald MacLeod
text:
CHARGE your glasses and raise them to BrewDog, the Aberdeenshire-based brewery, who in answer to its critics after making high-strength beer Tokyo, made a low-alcohol beer called Nanny State.

Brilliant! Why stop there? Let's have other full-strength varieties like Interfering Gits and Goodbye Brown ale on our shelves soon!

headline: Food for thought
author: DOMINIC WALSH
text:
Ready, steady... but who will go? Amid mounting speculation, I have opened a book on the next chief executive to move to pastures new. My each-way punts are two of the longest-serving FTSE 100 bosses —raham Mackay, of SABMiller, and Paul Walsh, of Diageo. Both appear to be edging towards retirement, although each has one final deal in him. Better bets may be Giles Thorley, of Punch, and Alan Parker, of Whitbread.

At Punch, the succession has been spiced up by the appointment of Roger Whiteside, from Theshers, and Mike Tye, from Whitbread, to run its two divisions. Mr Thorley told me last week: "I'm not going to do this for ever, but there's a determination to see through the turnaround." So next year? "I don't know."

My favourite to go first is the evergreen Mr Parker. What about it, Alan? "I can't get away from the place," he laughs. "I love it." We'll see.

Bringing back the good old days Canteen, the fashionable all-day dining concept that focuses on serving "Great British food" in stylish surroundings, has opened its fourth London outlet, at Canary Wharf. The place was crammed on Thursday, its first day of trading, and further expansion is on the menu. Expect another couple of outlets in Central London before Canteen turns its attention to the suburbs and, down the line, other cities. Based on the bacon, eggs and bubble and squeak I scoffed on Thursday, Canteen deserves all the plaudits.

An epic story of, um, not sure what All eyes tomorrow will be on BrewDog, the maverick Scottish company behind such potent beers as Punk IPA and Hop Rocker. It is planning a splurge to launch "a business proposal of epic proportions that will truly revolutionise the beer industry" while announcing "two prestigious US billionaire boys as new investors". Beyond that, it is keeping schtum. Should be fascinating.

Size matters if you sink or swim Trading remains tough for restaurants, but the big boys continue to keep their heads above water. The Coffer Peach Business Tracker, which collects figures from the likes of Mitchells & Butlers and Pizza Hut, says that like-for-like sales rose by 1.7 per cent in September compared with the same month last year. Mind you, that is well below the 5.2 per cent increase for the first nine months and it is clear that the chains are discounting like crazy to keep moving forwards.

DOMINIC WALSH

headline: A little something to savour? City Diary In the blue corner James Watt and Martin Dickie
author: Dominic Walsh
text:
Given the off-the-wall nature of their business, it was inevitable that James Watt and Martin Dickie, the founders of BrewDog, the Scottish brewer, would find an unusual way to raise new funds. The duo, who launched BrewDog in 2007 after tiring of "industrially brewed lagers and stuffy ales", are raising £.3 million by offering drinkers the chance to buy 10,000 shares at £30 via an online equity-raising.

The purveyor of Punk IPA and Trashy Blonde hopes its growing fan base will result in a strong uptake. The issue follows the sale of a 12.5 per cent stake for £00,000 to Keith Greggor and Tony Foglio, the entrepreneurs who turned Skyy Vodka into a billion-dollar brand before selling out to Campari and acquiring an American drinks distributor that imports, among other things, BrewDog beers.

The money raised will fund construction of new £ million "eco-brewery" near Aberdeen, with five times the capacity of its present plant in Fraserburgh.

BrewDog, which exports to 15 countries, hopes to use its American investors to boost current US sales of 100,000 bottles a month.

Watt, the managing director, said: "Within a year we could be doing five times that amount."

headline: BEER MONEY; Brew Dog's shares offer..with ale discount
author: STEVE HAWKES
text:
BREWERY bosses are offering drinkers a fifth off the price of their beers for LIFE if they become shareholders.

Controversial BREWDOG hopes to raise £.3million through the scheme.

The company —hich makes the UK's strongest beer —as put 10,000 shares up for grabs after launching its Equity For Punks initiative.

Sale The Fraserburgh-based firm claims the £30-a-share internet sale is the first of its kind in the UK —iving punters a chance to part-own a brewery.

And investors get a lifetime 20 per cent discount on beer bought at the BrewDog website.

The share launch has already received backing from US drink tycoons Keith Greggor and Tony Foglio, who helped turn trendy tipple Skyy Vodka into a billion-dollar brand before selling it to CAMPARI in 2007.

They aim to help launch BrewDog's Equity for Punks sell-off by jetting in for a bash in Edinburgh today.

Speaking ahead of his visit, Mr Greggor said: "During our many years in beverage alcohol, we have only seen a few game-changing players come in fresh to the industry. Brew-Dog is one of them.

"Coming out of the recession, so many investors are disenchanted with their past institutional strategies. But Equity for Punks is so different, refreshing and tangible that for anyone remotely interested in good beer this will be their most fun investment in 2009.

"This is an extraordinary opportunity to participate in the success and future of BrewDog."

BrewDog made headlines across the world last month after brewing Britain's strongest beer Tokyo*, at 18.2 per cent alcohol.

Health campaigners blasted the firm for "irresponsibly promoting binge-drinking". Cheeky bosses at BrewDog responded by releasing a beer with just 1.1 per cent alcohol called Nanny State.

But the storm meant the first batch of Tokyo* quickly sold out and BrewDog has posted a 250 per cent growth in profits so far this year.

Global It's now Scotland's biggest independent brewery, employing 27 workers. BrewDog plans to use money raised from its share scheme to build the world's first carbon neutral eco-brewery in Aberdeen. James Watt, who founded the firm with Martin Dickie, said: "Our business is on the cusp of phenomenal global success.

"We wanted to create a community of 10,000 BrewDog-loving individuals to share in our future growth and share in our vision for BrewDog." Q FOR more info on BrewDog's Equity For Punks share scheme go to www.equityforpunks.com or www. brewdog.com or

Top dogs... James and Martin & Tokyo*, right

headline: Food for thought
author: DOMINIC WALSH
text:
Champagne days for BrewDog James Watt and Martin Dickie, the youthful founders of BrewDog, the Scottish brewer behind Punk IPA and Trashy Blonde, are not only very good at making beer. They are also exceptional when it comes to boosting the value of their business. Last week, they announced plans to raise £.3 million by offering drinkers the chance to buy 10 per cent of the shares via an online equity-raising. As recently as June, they sold a 12.5 per cent stake for £00,000 to Keith Greggor and Tony Foglio, the entrepreneurs who turned Skyy Vodka into a billion-dollar brand. By my reckoning, that means the value of BrewDog has jumped from £.8 million to £3 million in the space of less than six months. Frothy stuff.

And if one green bottle should ... The furore over Diageo's plans to close its Johnnie Walker bottling plant in Kilmarnock with the loss of 700 jobs —he latest development is a union ballot over strike action —as clearly not stopped the drinks giant from taking tough commercial decisions. In Canada, it has announced plans to close its Dorval bottling plant in Quéec with the loss of 65 jobs. So far there is no sign of the sort of political hurricane unleashed in Scotland, but give it time.

Johnson sees right time to deal This month's purchase of 11 Tootsies restaurants by the Giraffe chain was the third deal this year for Risk Capital Partners, Luke Johnson's private equity firm. But Johnson, who recently raised a new £5 million fund, is already on the lookout for new acquisitions. These will be bolt-on deals for existing businesses, such as Giraffe, and acquisitions of new ventures able to be expanded into much bigger businesses. For the outgoing Channel 4 chairman, the rationale is simple: "Current economic uncertainties means now is a great time to do deals."

Foodies and the generation game If ever Pierre Koffmann, once the holder of three Michelin stars, needed a reason to return to the stove full-time and open a new restaurant, it is surely the remarkable response to his "pop-up" restaurant on top of Selfridges. Having been scheduled to run for five days, it has ended up being extended by three weeks. Ewan Venters, food and restaurants director at Selfridges, said: "We underestimated the relevance Pierre has had on a whole generation of foodies." DOMINIC WALSH businessfeatures@thetimes.co.uk

headline: What a brewser; WORLD'S STRONGEST BEER SPARKS FURY
author: KENNY McALPINE
text:
FURIOUS health chiefs last night slammed brewery bosses after they launched the strongest beer in the WORLD.

Booze maker BrewDog's new tipple —alled Tactical Nuclear Penguin —s an incredible 32 per cent.

But bosses of the controversial drink firm, based in Fraserburgh, Aberdeenshire, were branded irresponsible for selling the potent brew, just weeks after they also launched Britain's strongest beer.

Record

Jack Law, chief executive of Alcohol Focus Scotland, said: "It is child-like attention-seeking by a company that should be more responsible.

"The fact that they have achieved a new world record is not admirable.

"It is a product with a lot of alcohol in it —hat's all. To dress it up as anything else is cynical.

"It's as strong as whisky, so you have to ask whether this is actually a beer or a spirit —t's clearly a spirit."

Tactical Nuclear Penguin beats the previous world record of 31 per cent which was held by German beer Schorschbraer. It was placed in the Guinness Book of World Records yesterday after being tested by experts at Edinburgh's Heriot-Watt University.

The beer began as a 10 per cent imperial stout then was aged for 16 months in two different whisky casks.

It was then stored in a freezer at a chilly -20° for three weeks to reach its massive alcohol level.

BrewDog boss James Watt said: "This beer is about pushing boundaries.

It is about achieving something which has never before been done and putting Scotland firmly on the map for progressive, craft beers.

"This beer is bold, irreverent and uncompromising."

Only 500 bottles of the beer are available with prices starting at £0.

kennymcalpine@the-sun.co.uk

'Bold'... boss James

headline: 18% beer banned
text:
A SUPER-strong beer was banned from sale yesterday after booze watchdogs ruled its label encouraged excessive drinking.

The Tokyo* brand, made by Scottish firm BrewDog, has an 18.2 per cent alcohol content, or six units per 330ml bottle.

Watchdogs the Portman Group said its slogan containing the words "you must, from time to time, have excess" broke booze marketing rules. BrewDog blasted the "nanny state".

headline: Brewery defends 'connoisseur beer'
text:
Aberdeen The Portman Group drinks watchdog has urged retailers not to stock Tokyo beer, 18.2 per cent alcohol by volume, saying that its marketing promoted excessive drinking.

It is brewed by BrewDog, of Fraserburgh. Martin Dickie, brewery director, said that at £.99 for a 330ml bottle it was a connoisseur's beer, sold at only five specialist beer shops in the UK.

"If you go into a supermarket you can pick up 24 cans of lager at 5 per cent alcohol for £. It's clear which of the two will be abused."

headline: STRONGEST ALE IN BRITAIN IS BANNED; 18% beer's 'drink to excess' rap
author: GEORGE MAIR
text:
THE strongest beer in Britain was banned from sale yesterday for promoting "excessive drinking".

BrewDog's controversial Tokyo* stout —ith 18.2 per cent alcohol —s four times stronger than normal beers.

A single 330ml bottle of the £0 tipple contains six units of alcohol —wice the recommended daily limit.

Charity Alcohol Focus Scotland complained over a message on the beer's label.

It read: "Everything in moderation, including moderation itself. What logically follows is that you must, from time, have excess. This beer is for those times."

Alcohol industry watchdog the Portman Group ruled it breached their code on booze packaging.

Chief executive David Poley said: "It's obviously unwise for any company to urge consumers to drink to excess.

"We won't allow irresponsible marketing whether it's for a big brand or a niche product."

BrewDog, based in Fraserburgh, Aberdeenshire, now have to change the Tokyo* label if they want it back in the shops.

Specialist The oak-aged imperial stout has been on sale since July.

But BrewDog director Martin Dickie, 27, insists the ban won't hurt sales.

He said: "Only 3,000 bottles were produced and only 500 stayed in specialist UK shops.

"I'd imagine there are only a handful of bottles still available in the UK. You'd spend a long time hunting one down."

He insists it does not promote excessive drinking.

He added: "Tokyo* is a niche product for connoisseurs, who pay £0 for it for a special occasion and savour it."

BrewDog has become notorious for its highstrength beers —hile also winning AWARDS for business success.

scottish-sun@the-sun.co.uk

Label row ... Martin Dickie with Tokyo* beer

headline: Consumer goods; Need to know
text:
Shoe import duties: European Union ministers voted to extend import duties on shoes from China and Vietnam by 15 months. The decision was taken despite a vote last month by the EU's anti-dumping committee to reject plans to extend the tariffs. Page 47 Amer Sports: The Finnish sports equipment maker said that Roger Talermo, its chief executive, will leave the company and will be replaced by Heikki Takala. Mr Talermo has headed Amer Sports —hose global brands include Atomic, Salomon and Wilson —ince 1996. Mr Takala is the commercial director at Procter & Gamble, the American consumer goods group.

Swiss watches: The Federation of the Swiss Watch Industry said exports had dipped in November by 10.6 per cent year-on-year to SwFr1.4 billion (£35 million), after falling by 23 per cent in October. BrewDog: The independent Scottish brewer may have to cancel plans to raise £.3 million via an equity issue unless it can attract more investors in the next two weeks. Most of the money would go towards funding a new brewery. Hot Tuna: The AIM-listed surfwear and fashion brand reported a full-year pre-tax loss of £.3 million, compared with a £.8 million loss a year ago, mainly because of writedowns to intangible assets. Revenue dipped by 1.6 per cent to £.1 million.

headline: Oh blast.. our beer's exploding; BREW BLOWS IN HEAT
author: KRIS GILMARTIN
text:
A BREWERY issued a safety alert yesterday after bottles of the world's strongest beer started EXPLODING.

BrewDog warned their 32 per cent ABV Tactical Nuclear Penguin must be kept in a fridge —s heat can make it blow its top.

Bosses at the firm, of Fraserburgh, Aberdeenshire, sent an urgent message to customers who paid £0 a bottle.

It states: "There is a very small risk that these bottles may pop their cap or explode. Do not put them under the Christmas tree. Keep them in the fridge!"

BrewDog Owners James Watt and Martin Dickie admit six customers have had burst beers. But they said there is only a problem in one batch of 500 bottles.

Mr Watt said: "It's only affected one per cent of the first batch but you don't want it happening.

"We bottled the beer at -10°. CO2 becomes more active the warmer the solution. You end up with nowhere for the gas to go."

scottish-sun@the-sun.co.uk

headline: Pub plan brewing
text:
THE brewery bosses behind the world's strongest beer plan to open a chain of pubs next year.

BrewDog owners James Watt and Martin Dickie, both 27, bought the Marischal Bar in Aberdeen this month and will re-name it BrewDog Aberdeen.

Mr Watt said they are aiming for Glasgow and Edinburgh "by the end of 2010". The pubs will have micro-breweries and sell only their own beers.

The Fraserburgh firm, behind 32 per cent beer Tactical Nuclear Penguin, saw turnover rise £00,000 to £.8million in 2009.

Mr Watt added: "This is what we planned when we set up the business."

headline: Consumer goods; Need to know
text:
Constellation Brands: The seller of Mondavi wine, Svedka vodka and Corona beer, said third-quarter profit fell 47 per cent on restructuring and weaker sales. It earned $44.1 million (£7.7 million), in the quarter to November 30, against $83.5 million a year ago. Sales fell 4 per cent to $987.7 million from $1.03 billion.

Shanghai Panda Dairy Co: Chinese authorities kept concerns over the safety of the Shanghai dairy's products secret for nearly a year before announcing last week that it had been shut for selling contaminated milk, an official said. The dairy was one of 22 that produced tainted milk in 2008.

Drinks industry: An influential committee of MPs will call for the introduction of minimum pricing on alcoholic drinks and ask for a rise in the duty on spirits and industrial white cider today.

Cadbury: The UK chocolate maker's share price dipped below the value of the hostile takeover bid by Kraft for the first time since the bid was mooted in September.

BrewDog: The independent Scottish brewer, which makes Punk IPA beer and an 18 per cent proof beer called Tokyo, has appointed Ian Hope as finance director.

headline: BLITZKRIEGED; Super-strength German beer is served up here..at £0 a shot
author: LINDA ENGELS
text:
SCOTS drinkers could soon be getting blitzed on this potent German brew —elieved to be the strongest beer in the world.

Glasgow's WEST bar is the first boozer in the UK to stock Schorschbock —hich has an eye-watering 40 per cent alcohol content.

But anyone hoping to get tanked up on the super-strength tipple is in for a shock —t costs TEN QUID for a 25ml shot.

The bar's German owner Petra Wetzel decided to stock it at the pub on Glasgow Green after reading about controversial Scots firm BrewDog's new 32 per cent alcohol beer Tactical Nuclear Penguin. Last night Petra, below, said: "I just knew there had to be one that was even stronger than that. You have to drink Schorschbock like whisky —hough it really tastes like beer. It's a bit like light, golden beer.

"It's a very special drink.

There are only 40 bottles of it in the world. We have two. We can serve about 25 glasses. They're going to cost £0 a glass." But furious alcohol watchdogs said the beer encourages "irresponsible" drinking.

Jack Law, chief executive of Alcohol Focus Scotland, said: "It's disappointing this establishment has to resort to marketing tactics based on the alcoholic strength of a product at a time when Scotland is facing severe alcohol-related problems.

Effort "A more responsible brewery would have made an effort to find a good-quality, low-strength beer for customers to enjoy."

But Petra insists customers will be limited to one shot of Schorschbock per night.

And she added: "I am delighted to introduce Schorschbock —he world's strongest beer —o the UK to show my support for the tremendous work done by my fellow countrymen and brewers."

scottish-sun@the-sun.co.uk

Brew ha-ha ... the WEST bar

headline: Brewer's 41% ale
text:
A BREWERY criticised for making powerful booze claims to have reclaimed the title of the world's strongest beer with a 41 PER CENT ale.

BrewDog was slammed last year over a 32 per cent beer called Tactical Nuclear Penguin.

But a German brewery beat that with a 40 per cent strength variety.

BrewDog, of Fraserburgh, Aberdeenshire, said its 41 per cent Sink the Bismarck would cost £0 a bottle and would only be sold online.

Managing director James Watt said: "A beer like Sink the Bismarck should be enjoyed in spirit-sized measures."

headline: BREWDOG  have unveiled their 41 [...]
author: Donald MacLeod
text:
BREWDOG have unveiled their 41 per cent ale called Sink The Bismarck.

I don't know about sinking German battle ships but a good swally of this will have you sinking to your knees with your head down the loo before you can say . . . well,anything really.

headline: The GREAT BRITISH PINT; Once the preserve of whiskery men in woolly jumpers, Britain's brewing scene is buzzing thanks to a new generation of enthusiasts. Adrian Tierney-Jones raises a glass to our liquid heritage and its vibrant future
author: Adrian Tierney-Jones
text:
It's early Tuesday evening in a trendy city centre bar in Leeds. North Bar's interior is fashionably minimalist and you'd think the bright young things would be sipping Manhattans or champagne. They're not. They're drinking beer.

A range of stainless steel taps offers rare beers from Bavaria, California and Flanders, while four hand pumps present a quartet of real ales from closer to home. The chalked-up menu has information on the ales including the beer miles: Abbeydale's Black Mass, a rich strong stout, is brewed a mere 30 miles away in Sheffield.

The tired old stereotypes surrounding beer are changing. Those who enjoy good beer are no longer just whiskery middle-aged men in woolly jumpers that strain to cover ample stomachs. The British beer scene is vibrant, eclectic and packed with vitality. Whatever your age, taste or gender, you can find a beer to suit - and great places in which to drink it - from edgy city bars to cosy country inns.

Back in Leeds, the barmaid at North Bar, dressed in cool black, discusses the merits of chocolate stout with a lad who wouldn't look out of place in an indie rock band. A smart couple (media creatives?) approach the bar, beer menu in hand. He finally opts for a Bavarian Weissbeer in a tall glass; she cradles a half of Black Mass.

"When we opened North Bar in 1997 we initially wanted a European-style venue," says cofounder Christian Townsley. "But then we got some good Belgian beers in and people showed a real interest and it grew from there. The quality of real ale has improved immeasurably over the past few years and consumers are also more interested in the beer they drink."

It's not just Leeds. Up and down the country, real ale brewers are reporting increased sales and a buzz of interest in our national drink. "The traditional microbrewery market is the healthiest it's been for years," says Rudgate Brewery's Tony Smith, whose Ruby Mild is currently Champion Beer of Britain. "Year on year our figures are off the chart."

Members of the Society of Independent Brewers (SIBA) have recorded a sales growth of nearly four per cent, despite the last year being one of deep recession. "More than 60 per cent of our member breweries were founded after 2000 so the current recession is the most severe they have traded through," says the Society's chief executive Julian Grocock. "That the vast majority managed a sales uplift last year and are anticipating the same this year speaks volumes about the resilience and resourcefulness of the UK's quality independent brewers."

Consumers are increasingly seeking out beers with taste and provenance. Some pubs concentrate on local breweries bringing in environmentally aware drinkers, while others focus on an eclectic mix of styles and tastes. Beer festivals run by the Campaign for Real Ale (Camra) are loaded to the gunnels; some even run out of beer. Even mainstream pub chains such as Wetherspoon's run regular beer festivals with a huge range of interesting ales.

Young professionals tweet excitedly on their iPhones about the likes of Thornbridge, Dark Star or BrewDog. The latter currently produces the strongest beer in the world at a dizzying 41 per cent. "We like to push the envelope and at the moment we are ageing an 18 per cent beer in whisky casks with fresh raspberries," says the brewery's co-founder James Watt.

Women in smart gastropubs enthuse over fruit beers, stouts and pale ales. Last August, a Camra survey reported that 30 per cent of women drinkers had tried real ale, compared with 16 per cent in 2008.

"A lot of women like longer drinks but they don't want to feel bloated," says John Roberts, managing director at Fuller's, London's largest brewery. "Our Honeydew has been a great product for that trade, as has Discovery, which is fruity, light and refreshing." Over at Rudgate, Tony Smith agrees: "In my local, young women are drinking our mild."

Meanwhile, beer's symbolic role in our nation's life is represented by the Beer for the Boys campaign. Many breweries are donating their wares so that every serviceman and woman returning from Afghanistan has a beer thrust into his or her hands as soon as they board the flight home. "It's a very simple way of saying thank you," says Wing Commander Alastair Green, commanding officer of 216 CONTINUED OVERLEAF

I'll have what she's having: top-quality locals such as the Black Boy in Winchester, above, are seeing an increasing number of women enjoying what was once very much a man's drink

headline: 55% ale for €00
text:
A FIRM has brewed the world's strongest beer —oasting a staggering 55 per cent alcohol content.

The Belgian blonde ale, named The End of History, is also the world's most expensive at '500 for a single 330ml bottle.

The super-strength tipple —ade by BrewDog —eats the firm's own previous record Sink The Bismarck by a mouthwatering 14 per cent.

Brew Dog boss James Watt said: "This is the beer to end all beers."

The ale —rewed in Scotland —s made using a freezing technique to squeeze out water and make it stronger.

The bottles come inside stuffed squirrels.

headline: BREWER'S 55% ALE; £00 beer world's strongest
author: JANE HAMILTON, Consumer Editor
text:
A BRITISH firm has brewed the world's strongest beer —oasting a staggering 55 per cent alcohol content.

The Belgian blonde ale, named The End of History, is also the world's most expensive at £00 for a single 330ml bottle.

The super-strength tipple —ade by BrewDog —eats the firm's own previous record-holder Sink The Bismarck by a whopping 14 per cent.

It is made using an extreme freezing technique to squeeze out water and make it stronger.

Hundreds of pints are needed to create one bottle as the process has to be repeated scores of times.

BrewDog boss James Watt said: "This is the beer to end all beers." The ale —rewed in Fraserburgh, Scotland —s infused with juniper berries and nettles from the Highlands. The bottles come inside stuffed animals, such as squirrels and hares. A beer-lover in Denmark has snapped up the first two.

Mass-produced beers rarely top 5.5 per cent ABV. Belgian ales brewed with traditional techniques are usually about eight per cent.

BrewDog admitted the drink was "intentionally shocking", but claimed it will help "cure the binge-drinking culture, not fuel it".

j.hamilton@the-sun.co.uk

Squiffy squirrel ... bottle

headline: Tasteless PR stunt or daring [...]; City Diary
author: Dominic Walsh
text: Tasteless PR stunt or daring marketing coup? BrewDog, the Scottish brewer that has been censured for the strength of its ales, has launched a beer with an ABV of 55 per cent. Only 12 bottles have been made of The End of History — name as unusual as the packaging. The 330ml bottles, costing £00, have been made with stuffed stoats, squirrels and a hare dressed in eccentric outfits. Although the animals died naturally, animal rights groups are up in arms. But for James Watt, BrewDog's co-founder, the bottles are "at once beautiful and disturbing". He says it is "the beer to end all beers —n audacious blend of eccentricity, artistry and rebellion". It is also easily the world's most expensive pint, at £61 a pop.

headline: YOU'LL BE STOATIN' HOME AFTER THIS; 55% beer..in critter
author: STUART PATTERSON, LAUREN CROOKS; STUART PATTERSON; LAUREN CROOKS
text:
BREWERY bosses yesterday launched the world's strongest and most expensive beer - sold inside a dead STOAT.

Potent 55 per cent tipple The End Of History - a blonde Belgian ale - will cost boozers up to £00.

And makers BrewDog have created just 12 bottles of the super-strong drink - packaged in seven stoats, four squirrels and a hare.

The Fraserburgh-based firm's founder James Watt, above right, described it as a "perfect marriage between taxidermy and brewing".

He added: "We have pushed brewing and beer packaging to its absolute limits."

But the bottles were last night slammed by animal rights activists.

Ross Minett, of Advocates for Animals, said: "Using shock tactics is out of date, especially when this involves degrading animals."

BrewDog say the beer should be sipped. Four bottles - two squirrels and two stoats - have been sold so far.

scottish-sun@the-sun.co.uk Shock

Paw me a drink ... the bizarre bottle

headline: Roadkill bevvy is a sellout
text:
A CONTROVERSIAL super-strength beer costing up to £00 a bottle and packaged inside dead animals has sold out —n less than four hours.

We told yesterday how the 55 per cent alcohol tipple sparked fury from animal rights groups, who branded the roadkill packaging as "perverse".

But makers BrewDog yesterday sold all of their 12-strong batch of bottles —tuffed inside critters like stoats and squirrels.

Last night James Watt, 26 —o-owner of the brewery based in Fraserburgh, Aberdeenshire —aid: "We're pleased.

"We've proved that we've managed to blend together beer, art and taxidermy to produce a pretty unique product."

The rare booze —alled The End of History —s believed to be the world's strongest beer.

headline: Fury over brewer's free beer for tattoo
author: OLIVER FARRIMOND
text:
A SCOTS brewer yesterday sparked outrage after offering punters free beer for life —f they get the firm's logo TATTOOED on their bodies.

BrewDog is planning the stunt on the opening night of its first pub —he BrewDog Bar —n Aberdeen in September.

But Scottish Licensed Trade Association chief Steve Mudie branded the stunt "abhorrent".

He said: "I'd have to question whether it is entirely legal."

And Aberdeen's city licensing board acting chairman Cllr Bill Cormie added: "I don't think any board would consider this promotion to be encouraging responsible drinking."

But BrewDog cofounder James Watt branded the comments "a bit of naivety".

He added: "You might think they'd embrace something forward-thinking and innovative."

Row... logo

headline: Dutch beer is world's strongest; World Bulletin
text:
A Dutch brewer has created a 120 proof beer —r 60 per cent alcohol by volume —eating a Scottish attempt to create the world's strongest brew. Nijboer's, an Almerebased brewery, sells the beer, Start the Future, in one-third litre bottles for €5 (£9) each.

Jan Nijboer, the brewer, said he developed it to keep up with The End of History, a 55 per cent beer announced by the Scottish brewery BrewDog, which is to be sold in a £00 limited edition, with each bottle housed inside a stuffed animal.

headline: A welcome taste of reality; Analysis
author: Real ale Dominic Walsh
text:
Forget images of bearded sandal-wearers or men in f latcaps with whippets by their side. Real ale has become seriously cool. Not only are the likes of Madonna and Gwyneth Paltrow falling over themselves to be seen with a pint of ale in their hands, but celebs are brewing the stuff. Guy Ritchie, Madonna's former other half, has announced plans to open a brewery on his Wiltshire estate, while Tony Hadley, the Spandau Ballet frontman, co-owns the Red Rat brewery in Suffolk.

Over the past two or three years, cask ale sales have returned to growth, helped by the Treasury's decision to bring in a duty escalator in 2002 that cuts the tax bill for smaller brewers. The result has been a mushrooming of craft breweries such as Thornbridge, BrewDog and Dark Star. Despite the rush of closures by the likes of Scottish & Newcastle, Vaux and Young's , Britain now has more breweries than the 1940s.

But tax breaks are pointless if customers won't drink the stuff. While the reduced duty has undoubtedly made it easier for independent brewers to get their ales into pubs, the return to growth is also down to a wider move by consumers towards local products with heritage, provenance and taste. The new generation of brewers is also marrying tradition with innovation, testing new ingredients and f lavours.

The return to growth of cask ale has been all the more remarkable given that it has happened against the backdrop of the worst trading conditions for pubs in living memory.

Cask ale still accounts for little more that 8 per cent of total beer sales in this country and 16.4 per cent of draught beer sales in pubs. But that share is slowly but surely increasing and there are growing hopes among the glass-half-full brigade that the level in the glass can continue to rise for the foreseeable future.

1.2 Real ale pump badges lined up ready for the Great British Beer Festival in Earl's Court, London

headline: Sabbath nosh-free
text:
A RESTAURANT boss is refusing to open his trendy new eaterie on a Sunday —ecause he's a Christian. Jimmy Elliot, 26, who runs Musa in Aberdeen, has left customers baffled by staying closed on the Sabbath. But Jimmy, who grew up in a religious household, said: "My beliefs are far more important than the £00 we'd take on a Sunday afternoon." His stance has been backed by Musa's owner James Watt, of the Brewdog brewery.

He said: "When he told me, I said that we would give it a go.

It's worked out pretty cool."

headline: A beer that gives you that sinking feeling
text:
I have in front of me a small glass of the world's strongest beer. Roughly the colour of creosote and smoker's spit, it seems to be saying: "Come and have a go if you think you're hard enough." It is 41 per cent ABV but, hell, I'm a seasoned drinker. I can take it.

So I raise it to my lips and drink. And now the top of my head seems to have blown off. A minute later I still have one eye closed and the facial expression of a French gargoyle. I take a second swig: less of a shock but my temples appear to be fizzing. And one gulp is enough to put you over the drink-drive limit.

Sink the Bismarck, for this is the name of our friendly brew, launched last year, was made by a Scottish company, BrewDog, to take the world's strongest beer title from the German brewers Schorschbrä.

It is a quadruple IPA that contains four time the hops and is frozen four times to create an alcohol volume that could stagger a bull. BrewDog, run by Martin Dickie and James Watt, says its approach has the same contempt for the mass beer market that old-school punks had for pop culture. So, not fans of Boddingtons. I offer it to my colleagues, who gather round to test-taste. Before long we are all gurning like bulldogs, with a couple of us uttering bad swear words. One person says it tastes like liquorice; another like tarmac. It doesn't taste horrible —ust very malty, as if you might be curdling your brain. If you're interested it is available online and in selected bars. You should drink it in short measures, like whisky, thus the 330ml bottle comes with a stopper. And at £0 a bottle, it's unlikely to encourage binge-drinking. Carol Midgley

headline: I'll dr-ink on cheap
text:
BEER fan Robert Hanson has earned a lifetime discount on his favourite ales —y getting a tattoo of the brewery's logo.

Design student Robert, 20, had the BrewDog symbol put on his calf.

The £00 inking will earn him 20 per cent off at the brewery's new pub in Cowgate, Edinburgh.

Robert, of North Berwick, East Lothian, said: "I didn't get it done for the discount, that was just a perk. The first thing my mum said was, 'I might get one of those myself'."

headline: A Trashy Blonde, a packet of crisps and some shares, please; Microbrewery wants £.2m cash injection
author: Patrick Hosking Financial Editor
text:
It is not the most conventional of share prospectuses. Strictly, it shouldn't be called a prospectus at all but a financial promotion. BrewDog, the edgy independent Scottish brewer behind tipples such as Punk IPA, Trashy Blonde and Sink The Bismarck, is today launching an attempt to sell shares in itself to raise £.2 million for expansion.

"Ever wished you owned your own brewery?" goes the blurb. "Want to share in the potential future growth of BrewDog? Well keep reading bitch. This is your lucky day."

Prospective investors logging on to the share issue website are given the choice of clicking on three options: "Please tell me more", "Download prospectus" and "Fxxx that, I just want to buy some shares!" With more than a few spelling mistakes and missing words, this is not a document painstakingly crafted by a Cazenove; nor has it been vetted by the London Stock Exchange, which oversees conventional share issues.

BrewDog, which bills itself as "a beacon of non-conformity in an increasingly monotone corporate desert", has struggled to get sufficient bank finance and has been horrified by the cost of a conventional stock-market flotation.

Instead, it hopes to tap members of the public, enticing them with perks including a lifetime discount of up to 20 per cent from its online shop and a 5 per cent discount in its bars, of which there are currently two.

James Watt, who founded the business four years ago with his childhood friend Martin Dickie, believes that the share issue will raise capital relatively cheaply. A conventional float on the Alternative Investment Market would cost about £50,000 plus ongoing expenses of around £50,000 a year. BrewDog's costs will be £00,000 plus £0,000 to £5,000 of ongoing expenses. "It took a lot of bartering and crates of free beer," he said.

The share issue is not quite as alternative and shoestring as it might appear. The lawyers CMS Cameron McKenna advised on the transaction and the company has been audited by Crowe Clark Whitehill.

BrewDog has done it before. It successfully raised £00,000 from a similar share offer in 2009, recruiting 1,200 individual shareholders, although its target was £.3 million. The shares then cost £3 (adjusting for a share split). This time the shares are priced at £3.75, with the minimum subscription for four costing £5. The clearest downside, apart from the obviously high risk of investing in such a small, young company, is that there is no market in the shares. Indeed, they cannot be sold until 2013 at the earliest. Even then, with no listing, they will be traded only on a "matched bargain" basis via the company's website.

BrewDog has been a stellar success thus far, its sales rocketing after it scooped the top prizes in a Tesco beer competition and persuaded the supermarket giant to sell its products. Sainsbury's, Asda and Morrisons also sell the products, while the beer is on tap in some Mitchells & Butlers pubs.

Turnover rose by 87 per cent to £.33 million last year while after-tax profits reached £70,000 from a small loss in 2009. In the first half of this year, sales grew to £.6 million with profits reaching £00,000, Mr Watt said. He wants the capital to help to finance a new brewery at Peterhead. The existing one at Fraserburgh is stretched to full capacity. BrewDog also wants to speed up its opening of new bars. Its first south of the border opens in Camden Town, London, in six weeks. Business commentary, page 33

Martin Dickie, left, and James Watt want to use the capital to finance a new brewery in Peterhead and to open bars

headline: Business; Opinion, Columns Liquid assets worth a flutter; Business commentary
author: Patrick Hosking
text:
BrewDog's decision to bypass the City and oversee its own share issue is intriguing. So long as investors understand this is a high-risk investment and that the company is not bound by the rules of disclosure and behaviour applying to a listed company, there is no reason why more capital should not be raised this way.

But its approach is likely to remain a minority sport. Iconoclastic, mischievous BrewDog has a high profile and strong consumer following, and not just in the UK. It also has a great, albeit short, sales growth record.

The rare combination of brand recognition and stellar performance might well persuade some of its customers to overlook the absence of a liquid market for the shares, give it the benefit of the doubt and flutter a hundred quid or so.

Given the high flop rate of debutants to the more formal AIM market, they could hardly do worse.

headline: BrewDog  in demand
text: A Scottish microbrewery has raised more than half a million pounds from over a thousand investors in three days after offering its shares direct to the public. BrewDog said that total subscriptions had grown to £70,000 yesterday. The biggest application was for the online maximum of £2,000.

headline: FT WEEKEND SUPPLEMENT - MONEY Microbrewery's online share sale beats expectations
author: Jonathan Moules
text:
A Scottish microbrewery's ambitious plan to raise £.2m through an online share sale has beaten even the owners' expectations by raising £00,000 in four days.

The fundraising exercise was launched by BrewDog in part because of its frustration in trying to get a bank loan for a major expansion of its brewing operations on a site just outside Aberdeen.

The company ran a similar investment project in 2009, but took five months to raise £50,000.

James Watt, co-founder and managing director, said mentions on Twitter and Facebook appear to have got the message out much more quickly, and further afield, this time. The share sale, which is approved by the Financial Services Authority, is currently limited to the UK, but has garnered interest from across the world, Watt said.

"There has been a huge buzz about it on social networking sites," he said.

"That has definitely driven the share sale." The company has made 90,000 shares available to buy in batches of four for £5 from its website, www. brewdog.com.

Investors get a discount on BrewDog's beers as well as a say in product designs and bar locations. The stock will be non-transferable until 2013, when BrewDog will launch a system to match buyers with sellers.

The founders are also planning to list the business on the Alternative Investment Market (Aim) by 2016.

It is not the first case of an entrepreneur using the internet to raise money directly - a process now known as crowdfunding. In 2005, Alex Tew, a student from Wiltshire, raised money for his university education by selling all the pixels on his Million Dollar Homepage.

But BrewDog has differentiated itself by selling equity stakes. It plans to use the money raised to leverage further bank finance to build an ecofriendly brewing operation costing £m.

Mr Watt formed BrewDog with Martin Dickie in 2007.

The business, which has courted controversy in the past over some of the names of its beers, now has 65 employees and sells its products through the major supermarket chains and its own bars in Edinburgh and Glasgow. It is due to open a flagship venue in Camden, north London, and last year made a £50,000 pre-tax profit on a turnover of £.4m.

headline: Microbrewery's online share sale beats expectations
author: Jonathan Moules
text:
A Scottish microbrewery's ambitious plan to raise £.2m through an online share sale has beaten even the owners' expectations by raising £00,000 in four days.

The fundraising exercise was launched by BrewDog in part because of its frustration in trying to get a bank loan for a major expansion of its brewing operations on a site just outside Aberdeen.

The company ran a similar investment project in 2009, but took five months to raise £50,000.

James Watt, co-founder and managing director, said mentions on Twitter and Facebook appear to have got the message out much more quickly, and further afield, this time. The share sale, which is approved by the Financial Services Authority, is currently limited to the UK, but has garnered interest from across the world, Watt said.

"There has been a huge buzz about it on social networking sites," he said. "That has definitely driven the share sale."

The company has made 90,000 shares available to buy in batches of four for £5 from its website, www. brewdog.com.

Investors get a discount on BrewDog's beers as well as a say in new product designs and new bar locations. The stock will be non-transferable until 2013, when BrewDog will launch a system to match buyers with sellers. The founders are also planning to list the business on the Alternative Investment Market by 2016.

It is not the first case of an entrepreneur using the internet to raise money directly - a process now known as crowdfunding. In 2005, Alex Tew, a student from Wiltshire, raised money for his university education by selling all the pixels on his Million Dollar Homepage.

But BrewDog has differentiated itself from other such ventures by selling equity stakes. It plans to use the money raised to leverage further bank finance to build an eco-friendly brewing operation costing £m.

Mr Watt formed BrewDog with Martin Dickie in 2007. The business, which has courted controversy in the past over some of the names of its beers, now has 65 employees and sells its products through the major supermarket chains and its own bars in Edinburgh and Glasgow. It is due to open a new flagship venue in Camden, north London, and last year made a £50,000 pre-tax profit on a turnover of £.4m.

headline: Business; Opinion, Columns SINKING A FEW PINTS
author: STEVE HAWKES
text:
A BEER maker has created the first ever tipple to be brewed under water Sunk Punk, a strong Indian Pale Ale created by BREWDOG, is fermented in a tank 60ft under the North Sea.

It also contains a number of maritime-themed ingredients, including sea salt, buckthorn and rum.

The company's co-founder, James Watt, told Sun City: "I come from a family of fishermen, so I thought 'Why not brew a beer under water?' "It's not just a gimmick. The sea adds to its flavour."

James set up BrewDog, Scotland's largest independent brewery, in Fraserburgh, Aberdeenshire, with a pal in 2007.

He added: "Brewing Sunk Punk wasn't cheap. We had to modify the equipment for use under water. But it's worth it."

A run of 1,000 bottles is now on sale through their website.

Tipple . . Sunk Punk Sunken treasure . . brewing ale in the sea

headline: Drinkers help fund microbrewery's £m plant
author: Louise Lucas
text:
BrewDog, the independent Scottish brewery, is opening a £m facility and five new craft beer bars, funding the expansion largely through its drinkers.

So-called "fanvestors" have so far poured almost £m into the brewer's coffers through its "equity for punks" online share offer.

Other small and medium-sized companies, particularly those with strong brand loyalty or customer bases, have also turned to their consumers as a source of cash in the face of tight bank lending.

BrewDog's new plant in Aberdeenshire will increase its capacity 10-fold, as well as creating 25 new jobs immediately.

"Through their investment in BrewDog, craft beer fans are helping to shake the dust from the UK drinks industry and spark a craft beer revolution," said James Watt, co-founder of the brewery.

Investors have come from as far away as New Zealand and south-east Asia, he added. "There will be more than 8,000 people behind our expansion in 2012 - all of whom will literally get richer with every beer they drink."

headline: 'Fanvestors' retain thirst for  BrewDog
author: Dominic Walsh
text:
The small Scottish craft beer company behind such tipples as Punk IPA and Trashy Blonde is to push the button on plans for a new brewery and five pubs after tapping its drinkers for £ million of funding.

BrewDog, which describes itself as a "beacon of non-conformity in an increasingly monotone corporate desert", turned to its customers after struggling to get sufficient bank finance and balking at the cost of a conventional stock market flotation.

Its online share offer, Equity for Punk, gives beer drinkers the chance to buy shares in Brew-Dog for a minimum investment of £5. Perks include a 20 per cent discount on beers bought from its online shop and 5 per cent off purchases in any of its bars.

The brewer, which raised £50,000 by a similar means two years ago, said that after raising £ million from "fanvestors" in the first month after July's launch, the total raised had now reached almost £ million and it planned to leave the offer open to the end of the year.

James Watt, who founded BrewDog four years ago with his friend Martin Dickie, said the offer had attracted 8,000 investors from as far afield as New Zealand and South Africa who would "literally get richer with every beer they drink".

He said that BrewDog had applied for planning consent for a state-of-the-art craft brewery in Balmacassie, Aberdeenshire. The low-carbon, £ million plant would create 25 jobs initially, with another 50 over the next five years.

"The new facility will give us ten times the brewing capacity, allowing us to meet demand that is growing all the time," Mr Watt said.

The group, which has three bars in Aberdeen, Glasgow and Edinburgh, will open a venue in Camden, North London, next month and has sites in Manchester and Newcastle. It is also considering opportunities in Leeds, Birmingham and Sheffield.

Mr Watt said the fundraising showed drinkers were "turning their backs on the limp, pale-yellow lagers peddled by giant global brewers".

headline: Pint to pint; Our guide to the best British pubs. This week: The Three Tuns, Bristol
author: Adrian Tierney–ones; Adrian Tierney–ones
text:
The Three Tuns stands on a corner, lonely in a landscape of recently built high–ise offices; a Travelodge to its back and a wedgeshaped college nudged against its hip. Once inside, the friendly barman tells me that a pub has existed on this site for two centuries. Buildings may come and go but the Three Tuns is that rare beast – city pub that has remained rooted in its community as others have fallen.

It's been a close thing. During the Seventies, it had a reputation as a bit of a dive. I'm told it was then an "honest boozers' pub" about 10 years ago. It also went dark for a while and the future seemed hazy. But now it has reemerged into the modern world as a strikingly handsome brewery tap for Bristol–ased Arbor Ales –n island of good beer, food and conversation.

First impressions are bold: the pub declares itself with a strikingly vivid colour scheme of mustard yellow. Within the main bar it's light and airy, the hum of chatter providing a pleasing background and adding to the civilised atmosphere. Scrubbed pine tables stand on an artfully distressed wooden floor while red mock–eather sofas add to the air of easy–oing comfort. There's an understated sense of design, pleasing in its modesty.

At the back another smart innovation shows the pub's inherent good taste. A proper coffee–aking machine glitters, the kind you will find hissing steam and grinding beans in most barista bars. I'm sure it's delicious coffee, but I'm in a pub for the beer.

Back at the bar, seven real ales thrust forward, eager to be chosen. There's an intelligent selection of bottled beers from the UK and elsewhere (I wouldn't recommend BrewDog's 18 per cent Tokyo at lunchtime, though).

Beers from Arbor Ales naturally appear on the counter. On my visit there are three and they are joined by a selection of guest beers from the likes of Dark Star and Saltaire. But I ask for Arbor's Oyster Stout and get my reward – wonderful dark beer whose rich chocolate notes are counterpointed by hints of fruitiness, roastiness and bitterness; I even get a briny undercurrent at the back of the palate. A simple but delicious menu featuring such staples as bangers and mash and chilli con carne complements these beery delights.

And as I sit there with my pint, surrounded by folk enjoying themselves, I think about the British pub as a palimpsest – bricks–ndmortar equivalent of those ancient manuscripts that kept having their main features scrubbed away for reuse. I doubt that the Travelodge will be still here in two centuries, but I'm willing to bet the Three Tuns will. It's a born survivor.

Adrian Tierney–ones The Three Tuns, 78 St George's Road, Bristol (0117 907 0689)

headline: BEER NECESSITIES
text:
IF you are trendy, a hard worker and love a pint, this could be your dream job.

Funky brewers and pub chain BrewDog are recruiting staff to work in their new bars.

Locations include Camden, north London, and Manchester, with three more to follow around the UK next year. Positions on offer include bar staff and managers, with 40 new jobs in total.

Bar manager Bruce Gray said: "To work in the BrewDog Bars you need first and foremost a serious passion for Brewdog and craft beer in general, and a burning desire to evangelise about what we do to as many people as possible."

Email your CV to bruce@brewdog.com.

headline: Perfect plonk; Whatever your TIPPLE, we've found the BEST ones to pair with your turkey DINNER
author: ROB BUCKHAVEN
text:
SPARKLING

Philippe Michel Créant du Jura Sparkling Chardonnay £.99, Aldi (1). Made using the same production method as champagne, this lemony tipple will taste great with the crab and avocado mousse.

Lindauer Sparkling Sauvignon Blanc £.49, Waitrose. A Sauvignon Blanc with a gooseberry and passion fruit twist. The perfect festive aperitif.

Nyetimber Classic Cuvé, £7.99, Ocado. This nutty British fizz is famed for beating top champagnes in a major taste-off.

WHITES

Asda Western Cape Chenin Blanc, £.47. An explosion of mango and lime, made from South Africa's star white grape, makes this "no-frills" tipple a sure-fire festive hit.

Piropo Pinot Blanc, £.99, M&S (2). A mediumbodied white that's full of orchard flavours and lemon tang.

Condrieu La Petite Côe Yves Cuilleron, £5, Majestic. Made from the Viognier grape, this "fine wine" is worth splashing out on. It's packed with peach and apricot to enhance the turkey trimmings.

REDS

Sainsbury's Sicilian Red, £.98 (3). With easy-guzzling damson and cherry fruits, this will suit the delicacy of turkey. Quality minus the price tag!

Tesco Finest Central Otago Pinot Noir, £0.29. Bursting with plum and cinnamon-infused cherries, and rounded off with spiced strawberry.

Clos de L'Oratoire des Papes, £9.99, Co-op. A blend of Syrah and Grenache from the Châeauneufdu-Pape region, the pepper and damson flavours work in harmony with a roast bird.

BEERS

Wiltshire Rum Beer, £.99, M&S. The deep notes of malted toffee over caramel are great with a roast. And a touch of rum brings out the beer's sweetness.

Brewdog The Physics, Laid Back Amber Beer, £.39, Sainsbury's (4). The bar is set high with the delicious plum and fudge flavours. Best served with a hunk of Cheddar.

Animé Clear Filtered, £ for 4, Tesco. This has a light hoppy flavour with a lemon kick. It's also low-calorie and only four per cent alcohol, so perfect for post-lunch.

CIDERS

Frome Valley Henneys Cider £.70, Tesco (5). Herefordshire apples give this a custard and butterscotch bite. Dry but not bitter, it'll go down a treat with your turkey.

Duche de Lon'Villie Cider, £.03, Sainsbury's. Made from 15 types of apple, a dry, delicious blend made for maplekissed parsnips.

Waitrose Heston Spiced Mulled Cider, £.99. Aged in oak and infused with cinnamon, cloves and ginger, it's a spicy alternative to mulled wine - great with a mince pie (or three!).

DESSERT WINES

CYT Otorio Late Harvest Sauvignon Blanc, £.99, Tesco (6). Gives a mouthful of peach and aniseed, which makes it a perfect match for the ice cream dessert.

Torres Floralis Moscatel d'Oro, £.99, Morrisons. Brimming with rich marmalade flavours that'll taste heavenly with fruit-based puds.

Seifried Sweet Agnes Riesling £3.29, Waitrose. Peach and quince notes blend with a yummy dash of honeysuckle - delicious with a post-dinner cheese board.

headline: SMEs hail success of retail bonds
author: Louise Lucas
text:
Retail bonds and equity - whereby smaller companies go directly to their customers for financing - have notched up a strong start to the year, with two issuers exceeding their investment targets.

BrewDog, Scotland's biggest independent brewery, on Thursday closed its "equity for punks" scheme ahead of schedule after reaching its £.2m target early. Investors, who could subscribe for a minimum £5 worth of shares, bought an average £72 allotment apiece. The biggest investment was £5,000.

This came on the heels of Ecotricity exceeding the £0m target set for its retail bond. The independent green electricity supplier on Tuesday said it had pulled in offers totalling £6.2m for its second retail bond - surpassing the 43 per cent oversubscription on its inaugural bond.

Retail bonds and equity are gaining in popularity as small and medium-sized enterprises struggle to raise financing from banks. Since the first retail bond was launched in mid-2009 by King of Shaves, which sells male grooming products, more SMEs are turning to their customers.

BrewDog, which is raising capital to build a new low-carbon brewery and open more bars, benefited from craft beer fans touting the equity deal on social media sites, including Facebook and Twitter.

James Watt, co-founder, said the early take-up of its shares showed that people were not just looking for an alternative to mass-market beer but that "for an alternative way to invest their money, a way that bypasses banks, brokers and global mega corporations".

Dean Mayer, lead adviser at Fidelitas, the specialist retail bond advisory business that managed Ecotricity's brace of bonds, said the growing use of these products demonstrated that "UK SMEs do have real and attractive alternatives when seeking finance".

He added: "It's arguably still a nascent market and fundraising tool but one that is already proving popular with companies and investors alike and gaining ground fast.

"We're now talking to a very diverse range of companies who are interested in and excited about using this method . . . and anticipate further launches in the months ahead."
