headline: BrewDog  bites back at Diageo's tactics
author: Louise Lucas
text:
Diageo, the world's biggest distiller, was on the receiving end of a torrent of wrath on Twitter after blocking an award to feisty independent brewer BrewDog.

The David and Goliath spat, which pits the maker of Johnnie Walker whiskey and Smirnoff vodka against a Scottish upstart that raises capital from its own drinkers, began in Glasgow's Grand Central Hotel on Sunday.

BrewDog had expected to carry off first prize at the industry's annual awards dinner, sponsored by Diageo. The trophy even had the company's name engraved on it.

However, as guests sat down to dinner Diageo staff approached the organisers and threatened to withdraw future sponsorship if the prize went to BrewDog.

The distiller was forced into a humiliating apology on Wednesday after the brouhaha went viral and drinkers from as far afield as Chile and China vowed never to touch a Diageo drink again.

The company, with net sales of £0bn last year, admitted to "a serious misjudgment by Diageo staff".

A spokesman said: "We would like to apologise unreservedly to BrewDog and to the British Institute of Innkeeping for this error of judgment and we will be contacting both organisations imminently to express our regret for this unfortunate incident."

BII Scotland, organisers of the event, were not available for comment. But the organisation's chairman, Kenny Mitchell, told BrewDog: "We are all ashamed and embarrassed about what happened."

BrewDog, which has turnover of £2m, said it was "gobsmacked" by Diageo's tactics.

"This is clear evidence of the dirty tricks used by global corporations to derail young competitors they fear," said James Watt, who cofounded BrewDog in 2007. "This is another clear indication that some organisations feel they are big enough to be kingmakers, controllers of everyone else's fate."

He said the "only plausible conclusion" to explain the blocked prize was a vested interest in making sure small companies such as BrewDog were not successful. BrewDog brews craft ale and has seven of its own bars which do not serve non-craft beers such as Diageo's Guinness.

Tweeters took up the cudgels after BrewDog published a blog post berating the behaviour of big corporations and vowing to steer clear of Diageo's stable of drinks. Diageo is itself a big user of social media for advertising and promotion.

However, not winning the trophy might have left BrewDog holding the bigger prize.

As Gary Kelly tweeted: "Very poor form from Diageo for sabotaging an indie award show. A marketing dream gifted to BrewDog though!"

People tagged their Twitter postings #AndTheWinnerIsNot to poke fun at Diageo.

BrewDog said in October it was opening a £m facility and five new craft beer bars, after funding the expansion largely through its customers. So-called fanvestors poured almost £m into the brewer's coffers through its "equity for punks" online share offer, it said at the time.

headline: BEER BULLY; Diageo fixes ale awards to hijack rival's prize
author: BEN ARCHIBALD
text:
DRINKS giant Diageo used shameless "bullying tactics" to rig an awards bash —nd hijack a rival's top prize.

Scots firm BrewDog was set to be crowned Bar Operator of the Year 2012 —nd even had its name ENGRAVED on the trophy.

But, just as the glitzy Glasgow dinner started, a rep from sponsor Diageo threatened to pull its advertising unless judges SWITCHED the winners.

Last night sickened Brew-Dog boss James Watt, 30, slammed the "dirty tricks" by the Johnnie Walker and Guinness owner.

He said: "Diageo's actions are shameless, misguided and embarrassing. Diageo abused their position to make a small and stupid statement."

Mr Watt told how last Sunday's British Institute of Innkeeping awards descended into farce when the eventual 'winners' refused the gong.

He claimed BII chairman Kenny Mitchell then revealed the full extent of the scandal two days later.

Mr Watt added: "Mr Mitchell told me directly, 'Diageo approached me and said if BrewDog won they'd pull sponsorship and their representatives wouldn't present awards on the night. We were blackmailed and bullied'."

Last night Diageo admitted the incident as it was forced into an embarrassing apology.

A spokesman said: "There was a serious misjudgment by Diageo staff which does not reflect Diageo's corporate values and behaviour. We apologise unreservedly to BrewDog and the Institute."

BrewDog, based in Fraserburgh, Aberdeenshire, was set up by Mr Watt and pal Martin Dickie in 2007.

It now sells 12,000 bottles of beer a month but has been rapped by health watchdogs for its advertising and superstrength ales.

The BII was not available for comment last night.

scottish-sun@the-sun.co.uk

headline: Drinks giant apologises to local rival over award
author: Rhoda Buchanan; Mal Siret
text:
Diageo, one of the world's largest drinks companies, has apologised after being accused of "dirty tricks" in a row over an industry award for a much smaller rival company.

BrewDog, an independent brewery based in Aberdeen, said that an independent judging panel at the British Institute of Innkeeping had named it Bar Operator of the Year 2012.

But, the company claimed, Diageo, which sponsored the event, put pressure on BII Scotland to withdraw the award, saying that Diageo would pull its sponsorship and refuse to present any awards if it did not.

Diageo admitted last night that some of its staff had been involved in a "serious misjudgment" and said that it would "apologise unreservedly".

BrewDog was told before the event that it had won the award and that the trophy had been inscribed with its name. The company's representatives at the ceremony were then shocked when the winner was announced as Diageo.

James Watt, co-founder of Brew-Dog, claimed that the row was clear evidence of the dirty tricks used by global corporations to derail young competitors. "It's shameless and embarrassing," he said.

"Some organisations feel they are big enough to be kingmakers, controllers of everyone else's fate. As a sponsor, Diageo had no right to interfere with the independent judging process, but they abused their position."

A spokesman from Diageo said: "There was a serious misjudgment by Diageo staff at the awards dinner on Sunday evening in relation to the Bar Operator of the Year Award, which does not reflect in any way Diageo's corporate values and behaviour.

"We would like to apologise unreservedly to BrewDog and to the British Institute of Innkeeping for this error of judgment and we will be contacting both organisations imminently to express our regret for this unfortunate incident."

The British Institute of Innkeeping was unavailable for comment.

BrewDog, which was founded in 2006 by Mr Watt and his school friend Martin Dickie, held its first AGM in Aberdeen last month for its community of "Equity for Punks" investors, combining financial reports with beer tastings, rock bands and gourmet burgers.

headline: COMPANIES - WEEK IN REVIEW Also in the news...
text:
Red faces at Diageo this week as the distiller was forced into a climbdown. An overzealous employee had intervened to prevent Brewdog, a feisty young brewer, from winning an award at an industry event that Diageo was sponsoring. Twitter users were outraged, Diageo apologised and Brewdog got lots of publicity.

There was good news for workers at General Motors ' Ellesmere Port plant when it emerged that the company might limit production of the Astra to Ellesmere Port and a plant in Poland. GM's European unit is undergoing a strategic review and, with plant closures possible, Astra production would safeguard thousands of jobs at the site.

High interest loans are now available to a whole new class of borrower as Wonga , the online lender, launched a service for small businesses. The company has grown rapidly, but its consumer rates have been criticised by MPs. It will charge between 0.3 and 2 per cent a week for business loans of up to £0,000.

Prudential chief executive Tidjane Thiam started the week insisting that the threat to move the company's HQ overseas because of new European regulations was not an empty one. Nevertheless, it emerged on Thursday that he is a leading candidate to be the next chairman of the Association of British Insurers.

headline: A set-to as old as the Old Testament
author: Andrew Hill
text:
Last Thursday I was, briefly, head of communications for a large Canadian widget maker, coping with a wave of Twitter-borne rumours about the arrest of its chief executive.

It was a simulation –n fact, it was a simulation of a simulation: the sort of virtual exercise PR companies put their clients through to prepare them for bad news breaking out on social, online media.

But while I was enjoying “o-commenting”o fictitious journalists using Weber Shandwick’ FireBell tool, a real five-alarm social media inferno was raging at Diageo. A day earlier, BrewDog, an independent brewery, had blogged about the big drinks company’ efforts to stop it receiving the accolade of “ar operator of the year”y threatening to withdraw sponsorship from future awards ceremonies. Amplified through Twitter, Facebook and other online media, at one point on Wednesday the story was “rending globally”–he sort of situation that multinationals’ommunications and brand chiefs have learnt to fear.

Many people have described this as the model of a modern media relations crisis. In what other age would skulduggery at a Glasgow awards ceremony have generated bad publicity from Chile to China? But what is instructive about BrewDog v Diageo is how traditional the media tactics, many of the tools and even the stand-off itself turned out to be.

First, the tactics. If the world were truly dominated by online social media, the fact that a trophy already engraved with BrewDog’ name had gone to a rival would have started circulating on the Sunday night, straight after the ceremony. But it didn’, because BrewDog saw the value in taking a few days to build a multimedia campaign around Diageo’ shameful attempt to throw its weight around. It was opportunistic, but it was also considered. In fact, it was what you would expect from a company that, for all its “unk”mage, has proved expert at aggressive self-publicity, from brewing “he world’ strongest beer”o driving a tank through London to promote a new bar.

Second, the tools. Diageo should, by rights, have been forewarned by a contrite employee on Monday morning. It wasn’. But it wasn’ alerted by a jump in unpleasant references to the company on Twitter, either. The multinational’ press office first heard about the problem when it was called by journalists three days later, after they received a BrewDog press release pointing to the company’ blog.

The story may have spread faster and further than it would have pre-internet but, bar a few tweets by the two parties, the use of a witty Twitter hashtag – AndTheWinnerIsNot –y BrewDog, and some online monitoring of the public reaction by Diageo, it played out as old-fashioned scandals always have. A Diageo apology aimed to damp the fire on Wednesday; a flurry of mainstream media stories stoked it on Thursday; and analysts and commentators will rake over the coals until they go cold.

Finally, the stand-off itself. Diageo prides itself on its social media savvy. Recently, a rather smug Diageo marketing man told a conference of advertisers that size matters online. “e figured it takes a community of a million on a brand to even start paying back on a social platform,”aid Philip Gladman, the group’ head of white spirits marketing for western Europe.

He’ right in principle. As he noted, Procter & Gamble had to spend millions to turn its Old Spice “an Your Man Could Smell Like”ampaign viral. But it’ sometimes wrong in practice. BrewDog, with annual turnover of £0m, a thousandth of Diageo’, may look outmatched. But since before an Old Testament teenager smote a Philistine giant, champions have had to worry about the ability of smaller challengers to use speed and agility to exploit their mistakes.

In the 1980s, Ben & Jerry’ took on Hägen-Dazs ice-cream, then owned by Pillsbury, with its famous Doughboy character. At the height of a set-to over access to freezer space, Jerry Greenfield, one of the founders, picketed Pillsbury’ headquarters with a placard reading “hat’ the Doughboy afraid of?”he grass-roots protest helped force a settlement and fuelled the growth of the tiny upstart’ product and profile.

BrewDog’ brilliant shaming of its bigger rival, for all its online firebells and whistles, has more in common with that pre-internet campaign than most digital marketing punks want to admit.

andrew.hill@ft.com

headline: DIAGEO'S BOOZER LOOZER; IT MAKES ME WEEP
author: SHAF Rasul
text:
THE spat between independent beer firm BrewDog and the world's biggest drinks company Diageo once again shows the arrogance of major companies who think they can rule the roost over small start-ups.

Social media sites were full of criticism for Diageo after they blocked an award meant for the popular Aberdeen brewer.

Why a global corporation like Diageo feels the need to resort to dirty tactics is beyond me.

But BrewDog's bite proved stronger than Diageo's bark and, after a scathing response from founder James Watt, the drinks giant apologised.

headline: By the people, for the people ..but screw the little people
author: BILL Leckie
text:
THERE are now officially more Olympics-themed adverts on telly than there will be medals up for grabs at London 2012.

A statement that may or may not be true. But which sure as hell feels like it.

In every break of every show on every commercial channel, you can't put the kettle on without them trying to sell you chocolate bars or burgers or toothpaste or cars off the back of the dangled promise of tickets for the Underwater Badminton or whatever else they've got spares for.

My favourite's the one with the tagline: No Tampax, No Glory.

A thought that could put a bit of a crimp in Usain Bolt's training programme.

At the heart of this wall-towall, station-to-station, credit card-to-deodorant-to-feminine hygiene product sell-sell-sell frenzy are, of course, the five Olympic rings. The summation of everything these Games are about, a symbol of continents and cultures, languages and colours linking together to produce the finest of human endeavour.

Just one thing. Don't try and incorporate that symbol into anything that doesn't make its owners a fast buck or they'll pin you to the wall with Tessa Sanderson's javelin.

Ask the ladies of the Scottish Women's Rural Institute, who've been banned by London 2012 chiefs from turning the Olympic logo into ... flower arrangements.

And this is only the tip of the iceberg. Between now and the flame being lit, scores of little people all over the world will fall victim to the same heavy-handed, globalised bullying tactics; you know, like the International Olympic Association invented circles.

Which is why the WRI and anyone else who wants to use the rings without permission from Lord Coe and his snooty chums should take courage from the tale of a Scottish company called BrewDog.

Last week, they were up for an award as Bar Operator of the Year, a mammoth achievement for a small player in a multi-billion pound industry.

Their name had been engraved on the trophy, which sat twinkling on the podium.

Just before the official announcement, a representative of bevvy giant Diageo approached the organisers, said they weren't having BrewDog as winners and "suggested" an alternative recipient.

Diageo were backers of the awards ceremony. Diageo products are not sold in BrewDog premises. Work out the links for yourselves.

Anyway, the good news is that to say the move backfired on them is like suggesting necking two litres of Johnnie Walker might slightly reduce your ability to operate heavy machinery.

Not only did the alternative winner tell them to sling it, but social networking sites went bonkers with so much criticism of Diageo they ended up issuing a sheepish apology to their tiny, Scottish-run nemesis.

In short, it's turned into the kind of worldwide PR job a company like BrewDog could never afford to pay for in a month of Sunday opening.

Now, why an empire the size of Diageo should feel threatened by the flea-bite to their operation comes as no surprise, because we live in a country where it seems the bigger they are, the smaller their outlook on life.

What IS a shocker is their bumbling stupidity in trying to bully people in this ham-fisted fashion, the same thick, playground mentality that has now seen the Olympic thought police stamp take their truncheons to the WRI's hanging baskets.

Lord Coe and Co should be chuffed that some wee wifie in Auchtermuchty is turned on enough by a sports event 500 miles away that she celebrates it with a garland of chrysanthemums.

They should be seeing it as proof that they're connecting with people all over Britain, not just those actually going to this summer's events.

But then, this is the same Lord Coe who I was with at a Scout hut in Rutherglen a couple of months back, where the kids had spent ages setting up their own mini Olympic events —n archery range, a tug'o'war, that sort of thing —nd he very politely refused to take part in any of them.

For me, his stand-offish attitude said everything about London 2012. On the surface, a sporting carnival BY the people of Britain FOR the people of Britain; but which, come the cold light of the Opening Ceremony, will keep the riff-raff as far away from the action as inhumanly possible.

That's why the Scottish Women's Rural Institute MUST carry the torch for the rest of us by taking the BrewDog approach to being put upon by The Man.

They should keep on arranging their flowers into those five rings, no matter what anyone tells them. Better still, they should lump all their blooms in together, weave it into a giant effigy of Sebastian Coe.

And turn it into a bonfire the night the Games begin.

Bonkers

headline: Message on bottle
text:
A BEER firm barred from getting an award by a drinks giant is to take revenge by naming an ALE after the scandal.

BrewDog asked Twitter followers to coin a moniker for their latest tipple in honour of Diageo. It apologised last week after vetoing an industry gong won by the firm, of Fraserburgh, Aberdeenshire. Suggestions for the new beer include Fail Ale.

BrewDog's James Watt said they are still waiting for the award, adding: "Diageo's CEO should hand deliver it to us."

headline: Brewery jobs joy
text:
BEER firm Brewdog announced yesterday it is opening an £million lowcarbon brewery and creating 40 new jobs.

The firm, based in Fraserburgh, Aberdeenshire, is also adding three more pubs to its chain of eight.

Boss James Watt joked that five years ago the company comprised himself, co-founder Martin Dickie, "some second-hand equipment and Bracken the dog". He added: "Now we're Scotland's largest independent brewery."

The firm has caused controversy with its super-strength ales —nd packaging bottles in dead animals.

headline: Leisure; Business Need to know
text:
Maybourne Hotel Group: Paddy McKillen, the Irish property tycoon who owns 36 per cent of the luxury hotelier, vowed to lodge an appeal after losing his legal battle with the Barclay brothers for control of the company.

BrewDog: The Scottish brewer and pub operator has signed a partnership deal to open its first bar overseas in Stockholm.

Manchester United: Shares have begun trading at just over $14, the price the club was forced to cut to last night ahead of the IPO as demand from investors failed to live up to expectations.

headline: 10 THINGS RUFUS HOUND REALLY CAN'T DO WITHOUT
text:
{My EVERYONE knows boys love their toys. So each week we ask a male celeb to share the must-have things in their life, from gadgets and getaways to people and pastimes. is see when This week, we speak to comedian, TV presenter and actor Rufus Hound.

away, films the Having made his breakthrough by presenting Top Of The Pops alongside Fearne Cotton in 2005 and 2006, Hound is now starring in Brit comedy movie The Wedding Video. Previously, he's appeared on television shows such as Argumental, Celebrity Juice and The Apprentice: You're Fired.

Here, the 33-year-old - real name Robert Simpson - tells LEE PRICE about the ten things he can't live without.

NEON SIGN (Priceless) My wife had a neon sign made for my birthday, which says "Grace under pressure" - my favourite song by Elbow. It's my actual favourite thing.

MACBOOK PRO (from £99) My laptop has become the way I catch up on TV, the way I see my family and kids when I'm away, where I watch films at night, where I read the news and where I store photos, videos and music. It's everything now.

BREWDOG and JIM BEAM RED STAG I love BrewDog beer. Similarly, I think Red Stag is phenomenal - all the flavour of Jim Beam with the added twist of black cherry.

So my ideal drink would be a pint of BrewDog's 5am Saint and a chaser of Red Stag.

FLAT PLANET It's a little restaurant on Great Marlborough Street in London, where they do flatbread pizza. I had the El Diablo once, which is chorizo, parmesan and rocket. Their tomato sauce is u n be l i e v -able.

SMARTPHONE I've had every version of smartphone over the years, and they're now finally able to do what they've always promised they could.

Having a smartphone now means you could never be lonely.

TWININGS 1706 BLEND This is a proper cup of tea. One of my personal bugbears is when you ask for a tea in a cafe and they ask if you want English Breakfast. We all know what tea is, but we live in a namby pamby world of other teas. If I want a caramel tea, I'll ask for that.

THE WEDDING VIDEO POSTER I made a film last summer and the poster has just gone up so, right now, it's one of my favourite things.

It makes the film finally feel real after working my backside off.

NINTENDO 3DS (from £49.99) I've always been a Nintendo fan so when I met some of their people I "fan-boyed" all over them. After wiping themselves down, they asked me to make videos for their search for Britain's Fastest Family - trying to encourage people to play Mario Kart.

TRIUMPH T100 (from £,250) It's an exact replica of the bike Steve McQueen had in The Great Escape - and it's the sexiest bike in the universe.

PREACHER COMICS My collection of Preacher comics. It's not for kids - it's about a preacher who inherits the voice of God and sets about making him accountable for all the terrible things in the world.

headline: FOODIE STORM'S BREWING
text:
THE controversial Scots duo behind beer firm BrewDog are to present a new TV show —crutinising the world's biggest food and drink firms.

James Watt and Martin Dickie are to host Channel 4's new eightpart series Food Unwrapped, which starts on Monday.

They set out to shock viewers with truths about mass-produced grub and drink consumed in Britain. James, 30, said: "For too long the UK food and drink industry has been dominated by faceless international corporations built on a foundation of profit rather than passion.

Legs "Food Unwrapped is a landmark for the craft beer revolution, as we continue to expose the mass-produced lagers that have dominated the UK beer scene in front of a national primetime television audience.

"It is an opportunity to give people more information about what they are buying."

James poked fun at co-founder Martin, also 30, for his presenting skills.

He said: "Martin is like the Davina McCall of the craft beer scene, only with better legs."

Three years ago, James starred in BBC3 show Last Millionaire and he also appeared in BBC2's Oz And James Drink To Britain.

headline: How to get ahead in brewing...; BOOMING BEER TRADE MEANS JOBS ALE OVER THE UK
text:
CHEERS! UK breweries are booming - and that's hop-ful news for jobseekers.

New figures from the Campaign For Real Ale show there are now 1,009 breweries across the UK, the most for years.

Many of the start-up firms are small craft and microbreweries employing just a handful of staff.

But they are bringing jobs to unemployment blackspots, including inner cities and depressed rural areas.

Bigger firms including Brewdog and Cumberland Breweries are growing too —roviding jobs in hospitality, building and marketing.

While struggling pubs are closing at a rate of 12 a week, others are struggling to find enough staff.

The latest figures show the beer and pub sector supports almost a million jobs in the UK, with 650,000 directly employed by pubs or breweries.

Industry experts estimate real ale will create around 1,500 jobs this year in breweries and related businesses.

Sara Barton, 47, left, is the owner of Brewsters Brewery in Grantham, Lincs.

She learned her trade working for brewery giants, including Courage. Sara founded the company in 1998 and now employs six staff.

She also runs Project Venus to encourage more women into the industry and beer drinking.

She says: "Having worked for major brewers, I have a lot of qualifications but this isn't always necessary.

"The key qualities are enthusiasm and tenacity —lus, of course, a passion for beer.

"A lot of craft breweries are run by people who have made the transition to commercial brewing from home brewing or are just beer enthusiasts."

Roger Protz, CAMRA Good Beer Guide editor, said: "Britain's beer industry is such a vibrant and exciting environment to work in at the moment.

"The huge growth in the number of breweries opening has meant a welcome addition of more women working in the sector."

Current vacancies in brewing include 78 roles at fullers.co.uk, ranging from lab technicians to bar staff, and ten jobs at shepherdneame.

co.uk/join-us/work-pub/jobs.

The UK's biggest breweries advertise vacancies on their websites but it is best to contact smaller firms directly. And look online at familybrewers.

co.uk and brewingwork.com.

Find out more about Sara's firm atbrewery.wordpress.com.

INDEPENDENT landlords can enter Glidden Trade's Paint And A Pint competition to win a £,000 makeover for their boozer. There are some great runners-up prizes too, so check outglidden.co.uk/entryform.

Vibrant

headline: RAISE A GLASS
author: Rob Buckhaven
text:
The complex tastes of beer make it a perfect match for flavoursome curry. Whatever your order, there''s a bottle to match.

LEMONY Tesco Finest Belgian Wheat Beer (£.99) gives chicken korma a malted-lemon lift. Blagger's guide Wheat beers are typically subtle in flavour, making them ideal for pairing with lighter curries.

RICH Purity Mad Goose Pale Ale (£.99, Ocado.com) is a creamy grapefruit gift to tikka masala. Blagger's guide This ale is produced from English Maris Otter barley, bred in the '60s for its superior malting properties.

BUTTERY Brewdog 5A.M. Saint (£.75, Sainsbury's) adds a touch of butterscotch to rogan josh.

Blagger's guide Brewdog once made a 55 per cent alcohol beer, which they claimed was the world's strongest beer.

headline: Leisure; Need to know
text:
EXCEL London: The East London exhibition and conference centre has promoted its managing director, David Pegler, to chief executive. Mr Pegler, who is a former rugby player and captained Wasps, will replace Kevin Murphy, who has been appointed chairman. The moves follow the most successful year in the history of the EXCEL London centre, which in 2012 hosted 5 million visitors, seven Olympic events, six Paralympic events and contributed more than £.6 billion to the UK economy.

BrewDog: The Scottish brewer and bar operator has launched a search for sites for new bars in Liverpool, Sheffield, Brighton, Cambridge, Oxford and Cardiff, and for extra sites in South and West London.

Cromlix House: Andy Murray, the Scottish world number three tennis player, is reported to have acquired the 14-room country house hotel near his home town of Dunblane. The hotel, which ceased trading last year, was up for sale through Savills with an asking price of £.8 million.

headline: Leisure; Need to know
text:
Pubs 'n' Bars: Administrators from Grant Thornton have put 20 outlets that formed part of the failed pub operator up for sale through Christie + Co, at a combined asking price of £.25 million. Travelodge: More than £20 million will be invested in the budget hotel operator over the next 12 to 18 months.

BrewDog: The Scottish brewer has revealed plans to open bars in Sweden, Belgium, Germany, Brazil and the United States as part of an overseas push to run in parallel with further British openings.

headline: We blew booze-up
text:
RED-faced brewery bosses admitted they couldn't organise their own boozeup —fter they ran out of beer at an all-day bash.

Cult ale maker Brew-Dog's bar was twice drunk dry by 1,500 revellers during last weekend's Brew At The Bog festival near Inverness.

Organisers admitted they were caught out by demand —fter fans guzzled 2,552 pints with bizarre names like Trashy Blonde and Hello, My Name Is Beastie.

BrewDog's Johnny Reid said: "We didn't expect to get through so much beer so quickly."

headline: Leisure; Need to know
text:
BrewDog: The controversial Scottish group has topped an index of Britain's fastest-growing bar, pub and restaurant groups compiled by the corporate finance house Zolfo Cooper and M&C Report.

British Hospitality Association: At its annual summit, the trade body called on the Government to take the potential of the industry more seriously, saying that hospitality and tourism would create more than 30,000 Jobs by 2015.

Guoman: Nine years after its launch as a sister brand to Thistle Hotels, the unloved and unrecognised hotel brand is to be scrapped by its Asian owners. ? 6 ? Punch Taverns: Senior bondholders have called for the launch of a new independent restructuring process after reJecting the board's latest proposal as unacceptable.

headline: Consumer goods; Need to know
text:
BrewDog: The Scottish brewer has launched what it claims is "a new category of spirit", made from an IPA-style beer. It is seeking to patent the "radical brewing and ice-distillation process" used to produce the spirit, called WattDickie.

Hook Norton Brewery: Jonathan Paveley, chairman of David Cameron's favourite brewer, has accused the Government of creating "an increasingly anti-business environment" and warned that it was putting thousands of pubs at risk of closure. Page 49

author: SIMON ENGLISH
text: BEER maker BREWDOG wants to raise £million from fans of its drinks. It is offering 42,000 shares at £5 each in a scheme dubbed Equity for Punks. Cash raised will fund an expansion of its Aberdeenshire brewery, bar division and a new line of shops. Shareholders get a lifetime discount in BrewDog bars.

headline: BEER maker  BREWDOG  wants to [...]; Sun CITY
author: SIMON ENGLISH
text:
BEER maker BREWDOG wants to raise £million from fans of its drinks.

It is offering 42,000 shares at £5 each in a scheme dubbed Equity for Punks.

Cash raised will fund an expansion of its Aberdeenshire brewery, bar division and a new line of shops.

Shareholders get a lifetime discount in BrewDog bars.

headline: Brewer opens fire with Equity for Punks issue
author: Dominic Walsh
text:
The Scottish brewer behind such tipples as Punk IPA, Libertine Black Ale and Dead Pony Club is to set up an online share-trading facility en route to an eventual public listing (Dominic Walsh writes).

BrewDog, founded five years ago by childhood friends James Watt and Martin Dickie, yesterday launched a £ million equity issue that gives fans of its beers the chance to buy shares at £5 each. This is four times the price of a similar £.2 million fundraising in 2011, launched by driving a tank, supported by a "punk army", through London, right.

The company, which calls the scheme Equity for Punks, is once again using a so-called crowdraising system to raise funds, going directly to its customers for cash rather than using a more expensive stock-market listing.

Mr Watt said that the company hoped to have its own trading system by the end of the year, costing more than £0,000. He said he was talking to suppliers including BritDAQ about setting up a share-matching service, but added: "It will be an interim measure. Our goal is a public listing."

The price of the shares has been determined by valuing BrewDog on a multiple of 25 times its net profit of £ million. This values the company at £00 million, excluding debt of £ million. Mr Watt predicted turnover of £0 million this year.

headline: Punk rocks
author: SIMON ENGLISH
text:
FANS of BREWDOG beer are putting their money where their mouths are —y investing £million in the company.

The firm's Equity For Punks scheme raised the cash in just 24 hours after it announced it was selling 42,000 shares —ith a lifetime discount at its bars —t £5 each. The brewer, set up in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, in 2007 by James Watt and Martin Dickie, expects £0million sales this year.

headline: NATIONAL NEWS A thirst for unorthodox schemes
author: Lucy Warwick-Ching
text:
From communities clubbing together to buy their local pub to drinkers buying shares in their favourite brewery, savers are looking for more unorthodox places to put their money in an era of ultra-low interest rates, writes Lucy Warwick-Ching .

"Crowdfunding and peer-to-peer lending is proving incredibly popular as investors are attracted by the prospect of returns far exceeding those available on savings accounts," said Patrick Connolly at Chase de Vere, the financial adviser.

One recent crowdfunding success story is BrewDog, the Scottish brewery . Its Equity for Punks scheme - which is offering 42,000 shares at £5 each to raise capital for expansion and investment - raised more than £m in its first 24 hours last month. Patrons can buy shares in the company over the bar.

James Watt, the brewery's co-founder, said: "This proves that there is a viable alternative to the financial establishment, and as the self-interested banks continue to stunt economic growth, people are looking for better places to put their money."

Although crowdfunding is still in its infancy, there are already several specialist web operations including Civilisedmoney and Crowdcube.

"In this environment of low interest rates and very unpredictable markets it's not surprising that more investors are turning to alternatives in pursuit of decent returns," said Justin Modray, founder of Candid Money, an advice website.

However, he warned that individuals needed to choose carefully, especially as many alternative investments were not regulated by the Financial Conduct Authority. "Far more fledgling businesses fail than succeed so ensure you thoroughly understand the venture in which you're investing and the risks involved," Mr Modray said.

headline: Crowdfunding draws angel investors
author: Jonathan Moules, Enterprise Correspondent
text:
Up until recently, Gilberto Tarantino's only investments had been made in his own business: a small independent beer distributor in his native Brazil.

But then he discovered crowdfunding - the method of fundraising that invites thousands of individuals to back a project or business with small personal contributions, co-ordinated over the internet.

It was through crowdfunding that he became one of 6,000 investors in BrewDog, a specialist Scottish brewery. And he is taking his role seriously.

Every day, he spends time reading the BrewDog blog, written by the brewery team for their small investors, who they call "Equity Punks". Mr Tarantino also chats regularly on the phone, or via online video calls, with BrewDog co-founder James Watt. In fact, he has become so taken with the business that he is planning to open BrewDog's first South American bar in Sã Paulo.

"I'm not just an investor," Mr Tarantino says. "I think my role is to propagate the craft beer movement and try to make BrewDog better than it is already as a brand."

He is not alone. Crowdfunding has grown in popularity as a way to connect with amateur "angel" investors like Mr Tarantino, who want to do more than just put money into a venture.

Companies and individuals worldwide are estimated to have raised £.7bn from the public in 2012, an 81 per cent increase on the previous year, according to Massolution, a research firm. This year, the total is forecast to hit £.3bn, with total investments expected to reach £5bn within three years.

Last week, Kevin McCloud, of Channel 4's Grand Designs, and Nicola Horlick, the fund manager, became the latest to use crowdfunding, seeking backers for a housebuilding company and a film investment fund, respectively.

However, not all crowdfunding schemes offer their backers the same rewards or levels of involvement.

For some business owners, the input of thousands of stakeholders is not something they want - or can cope with.

While BrewDog is content to give its backers similar rights to conventional shareholders - it welcomes hundreds of them to its annual general meetings - others prefer to keep them at a distance.

UK crowdfunding platform Seedrs, for example, attempts to make managing a fundraising simpler for a business by bundling all the investors into a single "nominee structure". As a result, those raising funds only have to go through Seedrs rather than attempting to communicate with an army of individual investors.

Its approach was preferred by Brian Taylor, the founder of PixelPin, which has developed a system to use pictures instead of passwords for security.

The tabular content relating to this article is not available to view. Apologies in advance for the inconvenience caused.

<>To date, Mr Taylor has raised £05,000 through two crowdfunding rounds, the second of which was run by Seedrs.

He says he appreciates the implicit validation of his business model provided by the thousands who have invested, but also likes the fact that the share structure created by Seedrs means he does not have to maintain direct links with his backers.

"To be honest, if we were inundated in the way that others are, it would be a distraction," Mr Taylor admits.

Investors in PixelPin stand to make a financial return in the future, as shareholders would, if the company grows - but their voting rights are transferred to Seedrs under the nominee structure.

Jeff Lynn, chief executive of Seedrs, argues that many of those attracted to crowdfunding do not want too much involvement, as they lack the time to micromanage a small investment, or spend hours interacting with the founders.

"The peak is professional people in their 30s or 40s, who work in the City, IT or the creative industries and - to put it bluntly - would love to start up something themselves but have got a job that pays well and don't have the drive and spare time."

For business owners who want more active angels, though, crowdfunding can supply them.

BrewDog's founders - who raised £.2m from its Equity for Punks programme in 2011 and last month launched a plan to raise £m by selling 42,000 more shares - make a virtue of the input they get from backers.

Mr Tarantino may be an extreme example, Mr Watt admits, but he says many others have offered practical advice. A couple of investors recently helped him and co-founder Martin Dickie choose between locations for a BrewDog pub in Liverpool.

Nicola Horton is a partner at accountancy firm Crowe Clark Whitehill, who advised Severn Valley Railway on crowdfunding that raised £.5m through a share offer to upgrade some of the buildings.

"It canvasses these shareholders about which projects to prioritise," she explains.

"Basically, it is a very low-cost source of marketing, so why would you not want to communicate with these people?"

headline: Thirst for fuller-flavoured craft ales catches bigger breweries on the hop; Tax breaks and more discerning tastes have real ale outperforming the beer market, writes Mal Siret
author: Mal Siret
text:
So what's it to be? A Horn Dog, a Simpleton, a Smog Rocket, an Anarchist Alchemist or an Einstö Doppelbock? Don't worry, we're not talking Old Arthur's Stale Ale or Muggy Brown, admittedly fictional names for the kind of revolting, musty liquid served in a warm glass that once gave beer a bad name.

Times have changed. Real ale has become fashionable, the tipple of choice for veteran aficionados and trendy twentysomethings alike. The UK's craft beer industry is now worth hundreds of millions of pounds and is the main growth category in the sector.

This micro industrial revolution has been sparked by a thirst for experimentation, leading in turn to an explosion in independent breweries, from Brew-Dog in Aberdeenshire to Newport's Tiny Rebel, from Yorkshire's Magic Rock to Beavertown in London.

It has been a long time coming. Craft ale, largely inspired by the growing popularity of hoppy, fuller-flavoured beers in America, has been fermenting in cellars and under bridge arches since Gordon Brown introduced tax breaks for small brewers more than a decade ago.

"The revolution actually started in places like Sheffield and Derby way before it hit London," Pete Brown, beer enthusiast and author of Hops and Glory and Shakespeare's Local, said.

"It's not a sequence of events, as such, it's a range of different things. Believe it or not, the recession has helped. Huge duty rebates have allowed small businesses to grow, while regeneration proJects such as the 2012 Olympic legacy are breathing new life into the industry. There were two breweries in London in 2006 —ow that number is approaching fifty."

The latest figures from the Campaign for Real Ale suggest that there are 1,009 breweries operating in Britain, more than twice as many as a decade ago and the highest number in more than 70 years. This figure is likely to increase when it is updated this month.

While beer sales in the UK have slipped by more than 500 million pints in the past three years, real ale has outperformed the market and enJoyed a rise. In the same period, the number of women trying real ale for the first time has grown from 14 per cent to 34 per cent.

Last month, as more than 50,000 punters and about 400 British breweries took part in the 36th Great British Beer Festival, Camra announced a campaign aimed at replacing wine at dining tables with a renewed focus on matching beer with food.

Will Hawkes, author of Craft Beer London and the man behind the popular beer-related smartphone app of the same name, believes that brewing has found its place alongside the drive for more interesting food. "Beer has become very fashionable. The quality of beer is going up and up. Remember, we're talking about traditional British products, which is exciting. We're rediscovering our heritage."

The rise of the microbrewery has come at a time when consumers are more discerning and communities are looking to support local businesses.

Sam Smith, of East London's Pressure Drop brewery, which sold its first beer this year, also believes that a change in the nation's drinking habits is forcing bigger, well-established breweries to rethink their strategies. The latest figures from the British Beer & Pub Association show that pub closures rose to a record 52 a week in July, with 2,377 pub closures in the past year. Yet the appeal of craft ale can be seen with the expansion of The Draft House, The Craft Beer Co, BrewDog and Brodie's pubs.

As Plato said: "He was a wise man who invented beer." Well, now beer is being reinvented.

Home brewer found strength in next step Case study Caveman Brewery Last year, while renovating the George and Dragon pub in Swanscombe, near Dartford, James Hayward, right, and Nick Byram "stumbled on a tribe of cavemen in a walled-up section of the pub's cellar" (Mal Siret writes). That might not be entirely accurate, but visitors to the Caveman Brewery website will ENJOY an anecdote in keeping with the brewer's branding.

Caveman, inspired by the discovery in 1935 of scraps of 400,000-yearold bones and tools in nearby Barnfield Pit, was formed shortly after Mr Hayward, 30, started home brewing.

Mr Hayward completed a three-day course with Brewlab, which teaches the practical processes to making beer. "My family had Just bought the pub. A local business, Kent Brewery, let us use their equipment as they were not running at full capacity."

The first Caveman beer was chosen as West Kent Camra's favourite beer at last year's Spa Valley Rail and Ale Festival. After that, and a £0,000 investment, the cellar of the pub became home to the Caveman Brewery.

"It's an industry that supports itself," Mr Hayward said.

headline: Editorial; Opinion, Leading articles PUNKS DAFT FOR BREWER; Fast £m from  BrewDog 's equity scheme
text:
A BREWER has raised £million from its own drinkers with a revolutionary "Equity for Punks" scheme.

Money direct from punters is turning the world's strongest beer-maker BREWDOG into one of Britain's fastest-growing food and drink firms, bosses claim.

And the innovative Aberdeenshirebased craft beer enterprise —n track for £0million turnover this year —eckons other firms should follow its lead by skirting banks and the City to raise finance.

BrewDog raked in £million on the first day of its Punks scheme after capturing the public imagination by driving a tank around the outside of the Bank of England at its launch.

Two months on, bosses at the 187-employee firm —hich also has 12 bars —ay it is racing towards its £million target.

Co-founder James Watt said yesterday: "Raising £million in just two months demonstrates the epic support behind the craft beer revolution.

Equity for Punks is rewriting the rules of business finance and it's putting our customers right at the heart of our operation."

Investors can put in a minimum of £5 —he price of a single share.

Shareholders get up to a fifth off in BrewDog's online shop and a lifetime 5 per cent discount in BrewDog bars.

There are 12 so far —ne each in Aberdeen, Edinburgh and Glasgow, eight in England and one in Sweden.

The independent firm's beers include Punk IPA, Dead Pony Club, Trashy Blonde, Tactical Nuclear Penguin and Hello My Name Is Ingrid. Its bottled beers sell in UK supermarkets and are exported worldwide. BrewDog, which launched in Scotland in 2007, has sold out of The End of History —aid to have been the strongest beer in the world at 55 per cent alcohol by volume.

Its bosses claim to draw inspiration from APPLE founder Steve Jobs and quote him in their share prospectus saying: "Here's to the crazy ones. The misfits ... the ones who see things differently."

headline: Corporate culture: Soft skills are needed to keep your imprint on a growing business
author: Jonathan Moules
text:
Unlike many bosses, Tamara Littleton has no problems with her staff logging on to Facebook during working hours.

Encouraging employees to read and comment on her social media posts is a vital way for Ms Littleton, the founder of eModeration, which manages online discussion groups on behalf of companies, to stay connected to employees spread across the globe.

The company has grown in 10 years from a start-up to a 350-person operation with staff in a variety of locations in Europe and the US. Her chief operating officer is based in the Spanish city of Valencia, while the company’ chief production officer works from the northeast of Scotland.

“s you grow, you can lose the family feel which was so powerful in the early days,”s Littleton says. “t is important for us to be connected to everybody.”

Social media is an obvious choice, given the nature of the business. The Facebook interactions let Ms Littleton maintain a connection as a guiding force in the company. “ can’ share all my inner thoughts,”he explains. “ut I am very keen to share who I am and what I stand for.”

Many founders understand that to grow their business they may need to become less hands-on in day-to-day operations and instead act as more of a guiding force.

How to make this happen depends not only on the type of business operated but the way it has evolved.

BrewDog, the Aberdeen-based brewer and pub operator, has grown from a small operation that in 2009 was barely breaking even, to a 190-person business with net profit of £m on turnover set to reach £0m by the end of the year.

The company’ headcount has risen eightfold since 2009 thanks to its strategy of building up a network of bars. Since opening the first in its home city three years ago it has added a further 10 venues, including one in Stockholm in Sweden.

Many of those who have come to work for the company share a love for its beers, which often have quirky names such as Dead Pony Club, 5am Saint and Punk IPA.

According to Neil Simpson, finance director, this makes maintaining the buzz about the business as its operation grows much easier. “t’ quite lucky that most of the people here have a great passion for the beer,”e says.

That enthusiasm is cultivated by involving staff in the creation of new beers. Staff from its network are often invited to Aberdeen to work on new brews, which helps foster personal links within the business.

“e will say we are thinking about doing a Berliner Weiß beer and they will suggest adding apricots or berries to it,”r Simpson says.

There is no financial reward for such innovations; enterprising employees are instead namechecked in BrewDog’ blogs, which are widely read by customers.

For Tim Hall, founder of the Pod Food chain, staying in touch means visiting each of his 23 sites at least once every eight weeks. “t is very important to the culture that people feel they are working for an entrepreneur, rather than a corporation,”e says.

Mr Hall tries to schedule his regular meetings with senior managers in Pod outlets so he can see how business is faring. He even holds some of his meetings with managers in rival food chains to ensure that his team keep abreast of what the competition are up to.

One of the biggest challenges in managing a fast-growing business is recruiting people who can develop their own leadership abilities as the task increases, says Mr Hall. “aving someone with the ability to run two stores is not the same as having someone who can run 50,”e notes.

He has sought to manage this problem by introducing a formal system of staff appraisals and reviewing everyone’ performance every three months.

“ather than assume people will grow into a role we write down what they need to do to reach that level and review how they are doing,”e says. “hat manages their expectations as much as ours.”

Founders may also need to develop their own skills in leadership. Some do this through formal training while others work through peer networks.

Ms Littleton at eModeration joined The Supper Club, a networking group for entrepreneurs of established companies, who meet to discuss their challenges at private dinners.

“t’ like having an executive board,”he says. “ome of my best ideas come from that group.”

headline: Camden Town Brewery, London
author: Nicholas Lander
text:
London's restaurants have never been so attractive for the beer drinker. Chefs are busy writing special menus that match plates with pints, sommeliers are compiling extensive beer lists, and independent breweries are enjoying a boom.

My own favourites include Kernel Beer, based under a railway arch in Bermondsey, south London; BrewDog, originally from Ellon, Aberdeenshire; and Beavertown and Redchurch, both in east London. But none of these quite encompasses the massive change in beer brewing in the UK, or its increasingly close affiliation with good food and restaurants, as much as the story behind another favourite, the Camden Town Brewery in north London. Started by an Australian, Jasper Cuppaidge, its most important product, Hells Lager, is now so popular with British drinkers that each week an extra 50,000 pints are trucked back from a brewery outside Munich.

All of this comes as something of a shock to the 37-year-old Cuppaidge, who cleverly registered the name Camden Town Brewery while surfing the internet from a rented flat in Camden Square.

Beer courses through his veins. His late grandfather was a renowned Australian brewer whose company managed 60 pubs from Rockhampton to Darwin. A surfing trip eventually brought Cuppaidge to England in 1997 and, having missed his plane home, he began work collecting dirty glasses at The Westbourne, one of the first of the new wave of London pubs serving good beer and equally good food.

Six years later and by then general manager of the pub, Cuppaidge realised that though he enjoyed the hospitality business he wasn't organised enough to run a restaurant. He needed the focus that brewing beer was to provide, and in 2006 took over The Three Horseshoes pub in Hampstead. He renamed it The Horseshoe, improved the food and, most significantly, began to brew his own beer in the basement.

"It wasn't easy," he recalls. "I didn't appreciate that by inheriting an old pub we also took on the wakes whenever one of the regulars passed on. We called it 'Heaven's Waiting Room'."

Today, Cuppaidge's HQ is a £.5m turnkey brewery built to his specification under the railway arches in Kentish Town by Braukon, a German specialist. A 40-strong team brews 80,000 pints a week supplemented by the beer imported from Germany. A significant quantity is enjoyed at restaurants ranging from Gordon Ramsay at Royal Hospital Road and Hix Soho to the Byron hamburger outlets.

Taste aside, Cuppaidge puts the success of the brewery down to four factors. The first is that from the outset he and his team have concentrated on a narrow range of beers. They brew only four year-round: the bestselling Hells Lager, a pale ale, a stout and a white beer with bergamot called Gentleman's Wit. "Lager is the hardest beer to brew," Cuppaidge explains, "because there is no surfeit of hops or grain to mask the flavour. It has to be absolutely correct, but when it's good - like ours is, I feel - it's very, very good."

Then there is the Camden Town name and its association with a part of London that is one of the capital's biggest tourist attractions. "The name not only opened doors for us, particularly in Japan, but it also anchored us in a very specific geographical location. It put us on a par with the Brooklyn Brewery and Sierra Nevada in the US."

Then came another break. Brawn, the popular restaurant by Columbia Road Market in east London, had listed the beer and it was selling well. It was also being drunk with great pleasure by Eric Narioo and Doug Wregg, whose wine-importing company Les Caves de Pyrèe owns Brawn. They decided to list the beer and distribute it to their many other restaurant customers. "Being a distinctive beer on a respected wine merchant's list rather than just one more beer on a wholesaler's list has been very sweet indeed," says Cuppaidge.

The final factor in Camden beer's success has been food, although Cuppaidge has managed to steer clear of the life of the restaurateur. Instead, he and his team have installed The Brewery Bar, and on Thursdays, Fridays and Saturdays, trucks parked on the street outside dispense food inspired by the cooking of Mexico, Italy and Korea.

What delights Cuppaidge, other than the sales, is what his bar has become: "A real focal point for people of all ages. Like a local pub."

nicholas.lander@ft.com

www.camdentownbrewery.com

My favourites include Kernel Beer, based under a railway arch in Bermondsey, south London; BrewDog, originally from Aberdeenshire; and Beavertown and Redchurch, both in east London. But none of these encompasses the change in beer brewing in the UK, or its increasingly close affiliation with restaurants, as much as the story behind another favourite, the Camden Town Brewery in north London. Started by an Australian, Jasper Cuppaidge, its most important product, Hells Lager, is now so popular with British drinkers that each week an extra 50,000 pints are trucked back from a brewery outside Munich.

All of this comes as something of a shock to 37-year-old Cuppaidge, who cleverly registered the name Camden Town Brewery while surfing the internet in a rented flat in Camden Square.

Beer courses through his veins. His late grandfather was a renowned Australian brewer whose company managed 60 pubs from Rockhampton to Darwin. A surfing trip eventually brought Cuppaidge to England in 1997 and, having missed his flight home, he began work collecting dirty glasses at The Westbourne in Notting Hill, one of the first of the new wave of London pubs serving good beer and equally good food.

Six years later and by then general manager of the pub, Cuppaidge realised that though he enjoyed the hospitality business, he wasn't organised enough to run a restaurant. He needed the focus that brewing was to provide, and in 2006 took over The Three Horseshoes pub in Hampstead. He renamed it The Horseshoe, improved the food and, most significantly, began to brew his own beer in the basement.

"It wasn't easy," he recalls. "I didn't appreciate that by inheriting an old pub we also took on the wakes whenever one of the regulars passed on. We called it 'Heaven's Waiting Room'." Today, Cuppaidge's HQ is a ￡2.5m turnkey brewery built to his specific - ation under the railway arches in Kentish Town by Braukon, a German specialist. A 40-strong team brews 80,000 pints a week supplemented by the beer imported from Germany. A significant quantity is enjoyed at restaurants ranging from Gordon Ramsay at Royal Hospital Road and Hix Soho to the Byron hamburger outlets.

Taste aside, Cuppaidge puts the success of the brewery down to four factors.

The first is that he and his team have concentrated on a narrow range of beers from the outset. They brew only four year-round: the bestselling Hells Lager, a pale ale, a stout and a white beer with bergamot called Gentleman's Wit. "Lager is the hardest beer to brew," he explains, "because there is no surfeit of hops or grain to mask the flavour. It has to be absolutely correct, but when it's good - like ours is, I feel - it's very, very good." There is also the Camden name and its association with an area that is one of London's biggest tourist attractions.

"The name not only opened doors for us, particularly in Japan, but it also anchored us in a very specific geographical location. It put us on a par with the Brooklyn Brewery and Sierra Nevada in the US." Then came another break. Brawn, a popular restaurant in east London, had listed the beer and it was selling well. It was also being drunk with great pleasure by Eric Narioo and Doug Wregg, whose wine-importing company Les Caves de Pyrene owns Brawn. They listed the beer for distribution to their many restaurant customers.

"Being a distinctive beer on a respected wine merchant's list rather than just one more beer on a wholesaler's list has been very sweet indeed," says Cuppaidge.

The final factor in Camden beer's success has been food, although Cuppaidge has steered clear of the life of the restaurateur. Instead, he and his team have installed The Brewery Bar, and on Thursdays, Fridays and Saturdays, trucks parked on the street outside dispense food inspired by the cooking of Mexico, Italy and Korea.

What delights Cuppaidge, other than the sales, is what his bar has become: "A real focal point for people of all ages. Like a local pub." nicholas.lander@ft.com More columns at www.ft.com/lander

headline: Consumer Goods; Need to know
text:
Dairy Crest: Adjusted profits at the dairy and spreads manufacturer rose by 18 per cent to £1.9 million, largely driven by strong sales of Cathedral City cheese.

Tate & Lyle: Profits fell in the first half but Tate said it expected to report growth for the full-year. It said that higher sales and profits in its speciality food ingredient business, which sells products such as Splenda, would drive performance in the second half.

BrewDog: A scheme by the Scottish brewer and pub operator to raise £ million from its customers has been extended to the maximum £.25 million amid strong demand.

headline: Bags, cabs and wellies top growth list
text:
They don't Just look good to shoppers and lovers of fashion, they look pretty attractive to the market, too (Nic Fildes writes). The likes of Mulberry, Hunter Boots and Cath Kidston are among the fastest-growing companies in Britain, overshadowing the technology sector, according to a report from the London Stock Exchange.

The likes of the clothing company Boo Hoo, right, Addison Lee, the taxi company, and the beermaker BrewDog were also included in the 1,000 fastest-growing companies.

Aardman, the animator of Wallace &Gromit, was also highlighted as setting the pace in the small and medium-sized business ranks, alongside Metro Bank and Moonpig, the digital card company.

The Stock Exchange, which worked with Growth Intelligence, the data analytics company, to compile the report, used revenue growth, staff numbers, workspace, contract wins and patent applications to discover which small and mid-cap companies were on the rise.

Surveys of revenue growth often propel technology and digital businesses to the top of such surveys, but the LSE's report had only 10 per cent coming from that sector. Manufacturing accounted for a fifth of the fastest risers.

"This research happily dispels certain myths about the British economy," Xavier Rolet, chief executive of the LSE, said.

He added that technology businesses remained in "rude health", with app and game designers and data centre businesses making the list.

More than a quarter of the companies listed are based in London. The gap between the South East and the North West was narrower than expected, with only 18 more companies coming from the South.

The list included only companies that have been in existence for at least three years with sales of between £ million and £50 million in the past year.

Deloitte's Fast 50 list, another survey of growth companies, focuses on sales and is dominated by digital media, software, telecoms and internet businesses. The best-known company in its top ten was Monitise, the listed mobile payments developer, which grew 2,319 per cent over five years.

headline: Editorial; Opinion, Leading articles £.25M BAR TAB
author: SIMON ENGLISH
text:
SCOTTISH brewer and bar operator BREWDOG has drank a toast to its loyal customers after they raised £.25million in record time.

The firm closed its funding scheme after hitting its target early. The brewer, behind Punk IPA, Libertine Black Ale and Dead Pony Club, shunned the banks and turned to craft beer "fanvestors" to raise cash.

Investors could buy 42,000 shares at £5 each. More than £million of shares were snapped up in 24 hours of the "crowdfunding".

Part of the money will help to expand capacity at a new £.5million brewery in Ellon, Aberdeenshire.

headline: Premium beer becomes the flavour favoured in Brazil's favelas
author: Samantha Pearson
text:
When Marcelo Ramos opened a bar in one of Rio de Janeiro's biggest slums selling Belgian beer for R$200 ($83) a bottle, his friends thought he had lost his mind.

"They all told me: 'you're crazy, you're throwing your money away - people here are poor!'," says Mr Ramos, a 39 year-old mobile phone technician who set up the bar in his father-in-law's garage at the end of 2012.

Just over a year later his Bistrôstaçã R&R (Bistro Stop R&R) in Rio's Complexo do Alemã favela now sells more than 200 beers from around the world - the most expensive of which is Belgium's Deus champagne beer.

But it is not just the residents of Rio's slums that have started to venture from the country's notoriously bland mainstream beers.

While total lager sales across Brazil rose 92 per cent from 2007 to $44.37bn in 2012, premium lager sales jumped 131 per cent over the same period. Imported premium lager sales surged 184 per cent.

For investors, the niche market offers one of the few remaining bright spots in Brazil, where slowing economic growth and rising indebtedness have cast a shadow over mainstream beer sales and many of the country's other previously buoyant consumer industries.

Lauren Torres, a beverage analyst at HSBC, estimates that premium beer still only accounts for 5 to 6 per cent of the market, compared with around 15 per cent in countries such as the US, leaving vast room for growth in Brazil - the world's third-largest beer consumer and producer.

After acquiring Brazil's second-largest beer group, Schincariol, for R$6.3bn in 2011, Japan's Kirin is expected to start brewing its Kirin Ichiban beer in the country early this year.

Smaller craft producers such as Scotland's BrewDog are also planning to try their luck in Brazil. The Aberdeen-based producer and pub operator is planning to open its first bar in Sã Paulo this month, selling local beers from the Brazilian craft brewery Way Beer alongside its own.

Ambev, the Latin American arm of Anheuser-Busch InBev and the region's biggest brewer by sales with around 70 per cent of the Brazilian beer market, has also pursued the premium sector. It has boosted the marketing of its more expensive beers such as Bohemia and launched an even more upmarket version - Bohemia Royale - last month.

"We saw the same trend in the US and Europe years ago but the Brazilians and Latin Americans in general were always very brand-loyal," says HSBC's Ms Torres. "However, over the last five years Brazil has really started opening up," she says, pointing to similar moves in Chile, where foreign brands such as Corona have recently found success.

Almost 40m people have risen from poverty into the lower middle classes over the past decade in Brazil, creating a large market for aspirational brands as Brazilians look for ways to show off their new status.

Pernod Ricard's Chivas whisky, for example, recently ranked Brazil's north-eastern city of Recife as the top market in the world in terms of per capita whisky consumption.

Mr Ramos has seen this phenomenon first-hand at his bar in Complexo do Alemã. "People always dress up to come here and it's a popular place to celebrate birthdays or weddings," he says. Many customers prefer to stand outside to drink, not only to stay cool but also in the hope that someone they know might see them, he says.

Fledgling businesses in favelas such as Complexo do Alemã have also benefited from government pacification programmes over the past few years that have driven out violent drug gangs and brought investments in infrastructure.

However, the growth of Brazil's premium beer industry comes just as the wider market braces itself for a slowdown. According to data this month from the Brazilian beer industry association Cervbrasil, beer production fell 2 per cent last year - the first decline in at least three years. Ambev has said that it expects consumption to have also dropped last year for the first time in a decade.

"There was a reduction in consumers' disposable income as people had to spend more paying back debt," says Paulo Petroni, head of Cervbrasil, adding that the 2 per cent decline was equivalent to one fewer can of beer per person per month.

However, the World Cup and the late timing of Brazil's Carnival this year should help boost sales, encouraging Brazilians to keep drinking large quantities of beer through to July, even as the weather begins to get colder, says Mr Petroni.

For Mr Ramos, it means he may finally be able to quit his job at the mobile phone company and open the bar seven days a week. "Now everyone applauds me - I even got a prize from Rio's governor for business innovation last year," he says. "No one calls me crazy now."

headline: ON MANAGEMENT Lushversus Amazon: a big foe can unite teams
author: Andrew Hill
text:
The possibility that a senior Amazon executive may find his name on a range of "non-medicated toilet preparations" has considerably brightened my week. Not that I have anything against Amazon. But Lush, the British handmade cosmetics company, does.

It has just won a London court case against the US-based internet group's UK and Luxembourg arms for using the word "lush" to direct search engine users to cosmetics on its site, even though Amazon does not sell Lush products in Britain.

A close reading of the judgment led to the revelation that Lush had trademarked "Christopher North" - the name of Amazon.co.uk's managing director - for a new shower gel and other products. The judge described the move as "pretty bizarre", though he deemed it irrelevant to the case. Amazon plans to appeal against the decision. But whether the judgment sticks or not, Mr North's name will for ever feature in the list of corporate pranks, stunts and rivalries that leaven the general dullness of books about management and motivation.

Such episodes are not always as spontaneous as they appear. It takes time to apply for a trademark, as Lush did, then mock up taglines for a shower gel that aim lame jokes at Amazon, its senior executive, and the controversy over the US company's tax affairs ("it's not taxing to take care of your skin with this product" and so on). Similarly, BrewDog's social media campaign to shame Diageo into an apology for trying to prevent the independent brewer winning an industry award in 2012 was carefully orchestrated. It even included a Lush-style plan for a special commemorative ale.

Both these episodes started as genuine efforts to right a wrong. But it is no coincidence that Lush's legal team sued Amazon under the name "Cosmetic Warriors Ltd". By identifying an "enemy", companies can rally staff and customers.

The "Get a Mac" advertising campaigns that Apple used to run - pitting trendy Mac against doltish PC - were the polished outward expression of a rougher rivalry between Steve Jobs and Microsoft's Bill Gates that gave staff at both companies a sense of crusading purpose. Over the years, Larry Ellison has galvanised himself and his company Oracle by attacking technology rivals, from SAP to Hewlett-Packard.

Virgin has taken on incumbents including Coca-Cola (Sir Richard Branson rolled a tank down New York's Fifth Avenue to launch Virgin Cola) and British Airways (he scrambled an airship emblazoned with "BA can't get it up", when the rival airline ran into problems erecting the BA-sponsored London Eye in 1999).

These strategies run into problems, however, when a "bit of fun" - with its obvious benefits in public relations and private motivation - turns to pettiness, or worse.

Stunts have the virtue of taking place out in the open, but research and history both show how intense competition can degenerate into skulduggery . Stealth tactics never look good when they are exposed, as Facebook discovered after word got out that it had secretly hired a PR firm to attack Google in 2011, or BA learnt after its "dirty tricks" campaign against Virgin was revealed in the 1990s.

The other obvious risk is that poking a larger competitor with a sharp stick tends to trigger a reaction.

Amazon has earned a reputation for neutralising companies that occupy niches it would like to own, from Zappos in shoe retailing, to Diapers.com, in nappies. It eventually bought both.

Lush may have won round one, but I would advise it to bank that victory - as BrewDog, which dropped its anti-Diageo beer plan, did - and return to what it does best.

As for the motivational effect of targeting a common foe, Lush may find it is not as far from Amazon as it believes. Jeff Bezos's internet "everything store" draws its energy from mounting a challenge to the system - at first, it was just book distribution, but increasingly it has attacked the superstructure of traditional retailing. Lush is mainly energised by its founders' desire to challenge the cosmetic manufacturing establishment.

The UK company says it may yet push its "Christopher North" line into full production. But, having made its point "about how upsetting it is to have something personal to you, used by someone else", Lush should now leave the newly fragrant Mr North in peace.

andrew.hill@ft.com Twitter: @andrewtghill

headline: Medium-sized companies are Britain's overlooked success story
author: Brian Groom, Business and Employment Editor
text:
Britain's medium-sized companies are outperforming both smaller and larger businesses and are one of the overlooked success stories of the economic recovery, research has found.

Their numbers have expanded by more than 5 per cent since before the 2007 financial crisis, outpacing the 1.6 per cent increase in larger companies and outshining the 0.9 per cent decrease in small businesses, according to the research by business advisory group Grant Thornton.

They are doing so with little official support, but their growth could be boosted further with more help to increase exports and close the skills gap, the research suggests.

Unlocking this potential is a big target for John Cridland, director-general of the CBI employers' group, and now Lord Livingston, the former BT chief who recently became trade minister.

Lord Livingston said there should be "a real focus" on helping midsized businesses "who don't export today who can, and who do export and for whom we can do more".

Fast-growing examples range from the Cambridge Satchel Company, which makes leather bags, to Scottish brewer BrewDog and restaurant operator Cafféoncerto.

Their total turnover was estimated at £12bn, up 7.5 per cent in 2013 - ahead of the 6.5 per cent growth for large businesses and 0.8 per cent for smaller ones.

Over the next 12 months, medium-sized businesses expect faster growth in turnover, exports and employment than the UK business population as a whole.

Grant Thornton calculates the UK mid-market comprises 34,000 companies of 50-499 employees, making up more than a fifth of private sector turnover. Its research was done in partnership with the Centre of Economic and Business Research, a consultancy.

"The biggest barriers for UK MSBs is actually appetite and strategy; they have got to want to grow, and they have got to get the partners to help them grow," Mr Cridland said.

He said the CBI was arranging export missions for medium-sized companies and running "M-clubs" around the country "so that the mid-market is no longer the forgotten army".

The tabular content relating to this article is not available to view. Apologies in advance for the inconvenience caused.

<>While mid-market companies have proved resilient during the downturn, they tend to have a conservative attitude to risk and a low appetite for international expansion. They also face difficulties in finding the right external funding.

A report last year by GE Capital showed that UK medium-sized companies were narrowing the gap in revenue growth with the Mittelstand, Germany's admired cadre of midsized businesses.

Grant Thornton said the mid-market was now Britain's most productive segment, with average turnover per employee 5.7 per cent above the UK average - slightly ahead of larger companies and 32 per cent better than smaller ones.

However, productivity lags behind Ireland, Austria, France and Germany. French midsized companies are 21 per cent more productive.

Only 29 per cent of UK mid-market companies have international operations, and just one in five expected to expand their international operations.

UK Trade and Investment, the government's trade promotion agency, pledged recently to boost support for 9,000 midsized businesses.

Grant Thornton calls for finance initiatives to be extended to midsized companies, a national insurance exemption for hiring apprentices and better official data on the mid-market segment.

headline: NATIONAL NEWS Medium-sized companies are an overlooked success story; Economic recovery
author: Brian Groom, Business and Employment Editor
text:
