However, productivity lags behind Ireland, Austria, France and Germany. French mid-sized companies are 21 per cent more productive. Only 29 per cent of UK mid-market companies have international operations, and just one in five expected to expand abroad.

Grant Thornton calls for finance initiatives to be extended to mid-sized companies, a national insurance exemption for apprentices and better official data on the mid-market segment.

Room to grow if policy makers show more understanding, say businesses

Lorraine Heggessey, a former BBC 1 controller, believes Boom Pictures, the independent television production company of which she is executive chairman, can grow its £5m turnover by more than 30 per cent annually, writes Brian Groom . Last autumn Boom acquired Plymouth-based Twofour, maker of shows including Splash! and Educating Yorkshire , taking it into the top 10 UK indie producers. "We have got all the building blocks now to grow well in the UK. Growing in the States is going to be important as a next stage," Ms Heggessey says. Her company employs more than 350 permanent staff and many freelances, and has offices in London, Los Angeles and Abu Dhabi.

Ms Heggessey believes businesses need "more understanding" from policy makers when introducing regulations. Her company has just one HR manager to cope with auto-enrolment of pensions. She would also like grants to hire apprentices and trainees.

headline: TRUFFLE BREW iS £00 A PiNT
author: TOBY McDONALD
text:
A PAIR of Scots brewers created the world's most expensive beer —osting almost £00 a pint.

James Watt and Martin Dickie used vanilla beans, saffron, black truffles and flakes of GOLD to make the ale.

And they even spent two weeks brewing their 1½litre magnum of the flashy booze at Caesar's Palace hotel and casino in Las Vegas.

The pair —ho launched the controversial BrewDog drinks firm in Ellon, Aberdeenshire —eatured the pricy pint on their US TV show.

The series, broadcast on the Esquire Network, follows them as they visit a different city in the States every week. James said: "In each place, we make a beer that's never been brewed before."

Martin added: "We fell in love with Las Vegas. It would be nice to open a bar here eventually."

The four per cent brew —hich was decanted into a handmade glass bottle —as later auctioned for £,100 at a local US bar.

BrewDog infamously created a super-strength 55 per cent beer called The End of History.

It now claims to be Scotland's largest independent brewery, producing about 120,000 bottles per month for export.

headline: BEER BOSS BLAST OVER ALE'S BAN
author: AIMEE BEVERIDGE
text:
A BREWERY boss hit out at watchdogs for forcing one of its ales off the shelves and said he "couldn't give a s***".

Fraserburgh-based BrewDog co-founder James Watt delivered a withering response to the Portman Group after it ruled against the label on its Dead Pony Club beer, which reads "rip it up down empty streets".

Mr Watt said: "The Portman Group is a gloomy gaggle of killjoy jobsworths, funded by navel-gazing international drinks giants.

"They treat beer drinkers like brain dead zombies and vilify creativity and competition. While the Portman Group lives out its days deliberating whether a joke on a bottle of beer is responsible or irresponsible use of humour, at Brew-Dog we will just get on with brewing awesome beer."

Portman's Independent Complaints Panel ruled the beer's marketing breaks rules because it associates the booze with "bravado" and retailers have been asked not to order it after July 8.

Portman boss Henry Ashworth said the rules exist to "make sure that humour is used responsibly".

headline: IT'S DOG'S ABUSE
author: SIMON ENGLISH
text:
BEER firm BREWDOG has attacked a responsible drinking lobby group funded by alcohol industry giants —arcastically labelling its members "killjoy jobsworths".

The Portman Group accused the Scottish brewer of using advertising that encouraged anti-social behaviour and binge drinking.

James Watt, co-founder of BrewDog, replied: "I would like to issue a formal apology to the Portman Group for not giving a s*** about today's ruling.

"Indeed, we are sorry for never giving a s*** about anything it has to say, and treating all of its statements with callous indifference and nonchalance."

He accused Portman of treating beer drinkers like "brain-dead zombies" —dding: "We will get on with brewing awesome beer and treating our customers like adults."

headline: Bold new Scots firms aim high
author: SHAF Rasul
text:
AS you all know I am a staunch supporter of entrepreneurs and growing SMEs.

In my latest backing of young business talent, I will be taking the stand as keynote speaker at a new annual conference which aims to reinvent the way the small and medium enterprise sector in Scotland is financed.

Hosted by RBS, Opportunity Knocks will bring together industry minds to highlight the different initiatives that exist to support start-ups in understanding and gaining access to funding.

I'm looking forward to joining Ian Cowie, chairman of RBS SME Bank, in sharing my expertise in investment at the Edinburgh event on May 29.

Like RBS SME Bank, I've always believed anyone with the right experience should help nurture the next generation of entrepreneurs and do their bit to generate a cycle of success within the Scottish business eco-system.

Struggled It's no secret that when investment is carefully managed it can set an SME on a powerful trajectory of growth.

This was the case for me when I first started out. Fresh out of university with limited experience I managed to win my first IT contract with the RAF.

Mentally I was ready to take on the challenge, but financially I did not have the savings required to fulfil the contract.

I struggled to find backing for the bulk of cash I desperately needed and had to negotiate a payment package and take the work on in instalments.

This allowed me to reach out to the bank and gain access to smaller loans over time.

Without these funds I could not have taken on the work and grown my business. I know firsthand how imperative it is that banks take the lead from RBS and release funding so SMEs can turn their ideas into workable businesses.

Creation, ambition and business acumen runs in our blood in this country. I still draw inspiration from entrepreneurial successes like Andrew Carnegie, which are weaved in our history. Nowadays BrewDog is just one company that embodies our entrepreneurial spirit. It's no coincidence the company was able to increase staff numbers from just two to more than 200 in seven years following a £million injection of wealth. They recently hit the headlines for their Vladimir Putin beer, below.

The brewer's financial controller Neil Simpson will be at Opportunity Knocks to reveal how this prominent Scots firm bagged and harnessed vital backing.

There's no doubt that the Scottish start-up scene is once again booming. Last year the Scottish Government revealed SMEs accounted for 99.3 per cent of all private sector enterprises and 54.7 per cent of private sector employment. SMEs in Scotland are a force to be reckoned with the potential to create thousands of jobs and business opportunities.

It's up to the Opportunity Knocks experts to cultivate this growth and crystallise the world of finance for the growing numbers of bright new business minds seeking support.

Not only will backing from the likes of RBS and Angels Den have a positive effect on individual businesses, it will breathe life into our economy and broaden the job market.

The Crowdfunding Centre say the first quarter of 2014 saw the launch of more than 2,600 crowdfunding projects. The number of new businesses is on the up and financiers' appetite for the exciting investment prospects is also expanding —hese brilliant ventures attracted more than £.7million in investor pledges.

Opportunity Knocks is testament to this optimistic economic landscape and will help grassroots businesses locate the investment opportunities that are out there.

It's up to you to do the leg work and earn that all-important knock at the door —ut with the correct financial guidance more people than ever will have the tools to seize that chance at success.

headline: UK closes the gap on Germany's growth machine
author: Brian Groom, Business and Employment Editor
text:
Germany's Mittelstand of middle-sized companies is widely admired, not least in the UK, which aims to emulate its practices in business banking and apprenticeships in its quest for sustainable growth.

Britain's mid-sized businesses were once described by John Cridland, director-general of the CBI employers' group, as the "forgotten army" of the UK economy.

That is changing. After years of neglect, policy makers are focusing on the sector. The government's British Business Bank is aimed partly at mid-sized companies' needs and Lord Livingston, trade minister, is concentrating support on medium-sized exporters.

The UK mid-market is playing an important part in the recovery. Having edged towards the Mittelstand's growth rate last year, British companies expect to outstrip their German counterparts during the coming year, according to research by GE Capital.

British and German companies are also more optimistic than their French and Italian counterparts, reflecting their countries' stronger recoveries.

"In the UK and Germany there is quite a similar level of confidence in the national economy, and that means people are going to be more willing to invest at home," said Stephen Roper, professor of enterprise at Warwick Business School, the report's author.

But the strengths of the Mittelstand have been hard to export, even to the former East Germany. By specialising in advanced niche markets, retaining skilled workers and keeping debt low, these largely family-owned companies have thrived. They account for more than half of Germany's economic output.

The Mittelstand learnt to export when Germany was a patchwork of sovereign states. They are based in strong regional clusters and supported by a banking system, and public policies, attuned to their needs.

UK companies would need several years of strong growth to close the gap, particularly in areas such as skills and productivity.

Skills shortages emerged as the biggest problem in the GE research. On average British businesses have half as many apprenticeships and internships as their German counterparts.

"While the mid-market is thinking bigger, it still has critical challenges to overcome, particularly as the competition for talent becomes much tougher," said Ilaria del Beato, chief executive of GE Capital UK.

Companies are trying, however. Five times as many expect to increase apprenticeship and internship programmes as expect to cut them.

By GE's calculations, the UK mid-market accounts for 1.7 per cent of companies but contributes more than a third of private sector output, revenues and employment.

Fast-growing examples include the Cambridge Satchel Company, which makes leather bags, brewer BrewDog and restaurateur Cafféoncerto.

At the larger end are companies such as Poundland, a discount retailer, Sir Robert McAlpine, the construction company, and department store operator House of Fraser.

The research also found evidence of a regional divide. London was the best-performing region, with 86 per cent of mid-market companies reporting revenue growth in the past year. Businesses in the south generally grew faster than those in the north.

"This may, in part, be explained by a stronger focus on export markets in the south," Prof Roper said.

Previous research for the CBI by McKinsey, the consultants, pointed to management weaknesses in family-owned businesses. Only 10 per cent of family companies in Germany were run by the eldest son, versus 50 per cent in the UK, it found. Those that appointed the eldest son to run the company, it said, were drastically restricting their chances of finding the right talent.

A third of UK mid-sized companies believed their growth had been limited by access to finance, though they were reducing their reliance on bank loans and embracing a wider mix of funding methods.

A separate report by the CBI and accountants Grant Thornton said medium-sized businesses were being held back by the UK tax system.

"Medium-sized firms are not able to benefit from the incentives that small firms do, and at the same time most cannot afford to have an army of tax consultants on speed dial to help them wade through the complexities of the system," Mr Cridland said.

It called for the threshold at which companies must pay taxes quarterly rather than annually to be raised; for access to research and development tax relief to be widened; and for the threshold for exemption from transfer pricing rules to be raised from companies with up to 250 employees to 500.

headline: The best of Britain's new brewers
author: Jane MacQuitty
text:
Much as I love wine, this year's long, hot summer has made the perfect excuse to dive into the dozens of new niche beers that have washed up here. Beer, weighing in at about 4 to 7 per cent alcohol, slakes the thirst and refreshes the soul in the way that wine, averaging 13 per cent alcohol, never can. Not so long ago, the only way you could drink great, traditionally brewed beers was to visit the pub. Now even Camra, the Campaign for Real Ale, admits that if you want a good, reasonably priced pint, you'd be better off buying one to drink at home. As a pub enthusiast, I have had my fair share of badly kept beers and it's worth noting that once a cask of ale is broached, it can turn after three days. Provided you know what to buy, the only downside to drinking at home is to watch out for "skunked" bottles of beer that have been oxidised by sunlight.

Camra has done a grand job in encouraging beer drinkers to avoid carbonated dross, but even it must be amazed by the wealth of new craft breweries. Over recent years the number of breweries in the UK has greatly increased and now there are almost 1,150. It is not just new beer drinkers that have boosted sales, but cuts in beer duty for the past two years and the demise of the duty escalator. Sales in pubs have risen but are outpaced by the 17 per cent surge in supermarkets and off-licences, so that late last year more beer was drunk at home than in pubs for the first time.

So what can new beer drinkers expect? An extraordinarily flavoursome, innovative range of home-grown, hoppy, floral, bitter ales. What surprised me was just how many live, yeasty, bottleconditioned beers are available now, easily the measure of the live, caskconditioned British beer on sale in pubs. As it's summer, the glorious new British lagers are especially refreshing. Try the gorgeous citrus zest and herb-spiked floral 4.5 per cent Hogstar lager (Majestic, 12 330ml bottles, £1.60). Or try the more widely available, pilsnerstyled, delicate, fruity 4.6 per cent Camden Hells lager (330ml, Waitrose £.89). Another great beer from Hogs Back, rapidly becoming one of my top five British brewers, is the rosewood hue'd 5 per cent Rip Snorter, bursting with a rich, creamy, tangy, figgy flavour (500ml Waitrose £.97, Tesco £.99). India pale ale, brewed originally for British India, with more hops and higher alcohol to preserve it for the journey, is the most food-friendly of all beers. So tuck into BrewDog's terrific 5.6 per cent Punk IPA, with lots of zesty, floral, coriander-styled pizzazz (330ml, Sainsbury's £.75, Tesco and Waitrose £.97, Morrisons £.99). Finally, scoop up Young's London Gold, a fine 4.8 per cent light, tangy, smoky, bottle-conditioned delight (500ml, Asda £.75, Sainsbury's and Waitrose £). Lucky Londoners can taste the lot at the Great British Beer Festival at Olympia from August 12-16; £0 for a day pass.

This week's star buys 2

012 Lucien Marcel, Vin de Pays du Gers Blanc, France Tesco, £.99

This juicy, green pepper, lime twist and crystallised angelica-edged white is a keenly priced summer holiday swig. From Wednesday, like the red below, it's a steal at £.49.

2011 Seifried Nelson Pinot Noir, New Zealand Marks & Spencer, £1

Chris and Heidi Seifried, Nelson's largest wine producers, make a wide range of tasty whites and reds and this bold, beefy, gamey French barriqueaged red is a gem.

2012 Lucien Marcel, Vin de Pays de l'Héault Rouge, France Tesco, £.99

Soft, easy-slurping summer red, with tingling plum and damson-styled fruit: perfect with everything from garlicky salami to barbecued sausages.

2012 Mauregard Bordeaux Rouge, France Marks & Spencer, £

Yvon Mau's bordeaux hit the spot and this juicy, unoaked claret, made from 20-year-old vines, oozes plummy merlot fruit, topped up with more austere cabernet.

The keeper

2007 Barbaresco Borgese, Piero Busso, Italy Lay & Wheeler, £1.86 (01473 313300)

Barolo and barbaresco are the king and queen of Piedmont, both made from the majestic nebbiolo grape. Good barbarescos, like this one, have that bit more elegance and finesse than a beefy, brooding barolo that makes them better suited to summer drinking. So tuck into this gorgeous, spicy, damask rose and tar-scented barbaresco, from a first-class Piedmont vintage, and make your own mind up. With so much complex, perfumed, richly spiced fruit on board, you could enjoy this fine barbaresco with next month's game birds, or cellar it for another five years.

The number of breweries in the UK has greatly increased in recent years

headline: Jump in start-up distillers shows UK thirst for boutique booze
author: Scheherazade Daneshkhu, Consumer Industries Editor
text:
The number of would-be distillers applying for a licence in the UK has risen threefold in the past 12 months, according to research published on Monday, as start-ups hope to mirror the success of craft beer by capitalising on growing consumer thirst for boutique alcohol brands.

Applications for distillers licences increased to 20 in the fiscal year from April 2013 to April 2014, from seven the previous year, according to UHY Hacker Young. In the beer market, registrations for beer duty have trebled in five years from 101 in 2009-10 to 291 in the last fiscal year.

Roy Maugham, head of tax at UHY Hacker Young, said: "The increasing number of new start-up brewers and distillers demonstrate just how dynamic the drinks market has now become."

Niche brands, such as Sipsmith, which became the first copper-pot distillery to open in London for 200 years in 2009, and Hoxton Gin, a brand that mixes coconut and grapefruit into gin, have helped spark a gin renaissance in the UK.

While mass market gins, such as Diageo's Gordon's and Pernod Ricard's Beefeater have suffered stagnant or falling sales, artisan alcohol brands are among a fast growing segment of the food and drinks market, notes UHY Hacker Young.

The popularity of craft beer is reflected in growing demand, 23 per cent of adults in the UK have drunk a craft beer in the past six months and in London the figure is 38 per cent, according to Mintel, the market research group.

Some independent brewers, such as BrewDog, have expanded overseas, selling to Japan, Brazil and Sweden and the craft phenomenon has also extended to cider.

"There's definitely a return to craft, to heritage, to taste complexity," acknowledged Ivan Menezes, chief executive of Diageo, which makes mass-market spirits that include Johnnie Walker scotch, Smirnoff vodka and Gordon's gin.

In gin, it is focusing its efforts on Tanqueray, a smaller, niche brand compared with Gordon's. "We lost share in gin but [we are] building on the craft trend in gin with our unique and authentic recipes in the Charles Tanqueray Recipe Book," said Mr Menezes.

The large distillers face a big challenge in the US where some 200 start-up vodka makers have turned into upstart challengers to Diageo's Smirnoff vodka and Pernod's Absolut.

Pierre Pringuet, Pernod's chief executive, said last month: "The reality is that there are several hundred new entrants into the market. Basically, 99 per cent if not more of these brands do not survive, but the problem is, they do take market share."

headline: UK's new finance regulations face their first big test in crowdfunding
author: Andrew Sharman
text:
When the UK's financial watchdog introduced new rules governing crowdfunding in April, there were fears that the fledgling industry would be regulated out of existence.

As the new regime comes into force this month, the alternative finance sector is still debating whether the Financial Conduct Authority has legitimised a market worth almost £bn last year, or taken the crowd out of crowdfunding.

While the industry has broadly welcomed the new rules, fears remain about their potential to deter investors. Meanwhile, questions have been raised over whether traditional regulatory oversight is suited to an industry using social media to change the fundamental nature of banking.

Crowdfunding's rise to prominence has been remarkable. In little more than a decade, it has become a recognised way for companies, both young and established, to raise finance from a wide body of investors using the power of the internet. Over 12 months, the alternative finance market grew by 91 per cent to reach £39m in 2013, according to a report by Nesta. It is expected to top £.6bn this year, the think-tank reports, though this represents a tiny fraction of conventional bank lending.

The process, in which online platforms match a wide crowd of investors to projects, has risen on the back of two trends: the advance of the internet and social media, and the retreat of the banks, which scaled back lending in the wake of the financial crisis with tighter regulation and higher risk aversion.

As an investment case, crowdfunding has matured. "This isn't a 50 quid punt any more," says Julia Groves, chairwoman of the UK Crowdfunding Association, a trade body set up last year. "This is a real alternative for your savings, which aren't getting much in the way of interest payments at the moment."

By far the largest and best-known form of crowdfunding is peer-to-peer lending, popularised by platforms such as Zopa, RateSetter and Funding Circle. P2P, whether donation-based, lending between individuals or business loans, accounted for almost 85 per cent of the UK alternative finance market in 2013.

Much of the controversy over the FCA rules relates to the regulation of securities-based crowdfunding, which is a small portion of the total - about £8m last year - but is growing at several times the rate of the wider industry and has been used by companies from beer maker BrewDog to Kent winery Chapel Down.

This year, the FCA introduced a "10 per cent" rule, whereby retail investors who are neither "sophisticated" nor "high net worth" must certify that they are not committing more than a tenth of their net investable assets, excluding their home, pensions and life insurance. This rule does not apply to P2P loans.

"If we're talking about a start-up in Shoreditch, nobody should invest more than 10 per cent of their assets anyway," says Ms Groves, who backs the rules. Investors can self-certify as "sophisticated" after just two investments.

The FCA says consumers need protection if they are to have confidence in backing start-ups. Early-stage investors are more likely to be diluted as a company grows and attracts capital from new backers. Seed investments offer limited liquidity; the only exit, the distant chance of an IPO or sale.

Much worse, investors could suffer the same fate as the backers of Bubble & Balm, a fair trade soap manufacturer that raised £5,000 via equity crowdfunding platform Crowdcube in 2011, and ceased trading two years later.

Supporters of the new rules say the FCA has given the UK a distinct advantage over the US, where the concept of debt-based P2P lending was invented, but where equity crowdfunding remains illegal for retail investors.

"I certainly think that's allowed the UK to steal a march and really pioneer the industry," says Luke Lang, co-founder of Crowdcube, the leading equity crowdfunding platform by amounts raised and deals done.

However, there are concerns that the new self-certification process for investors might deter some. Dan Hird, head of corporate finance at ethical bank Triodos, agrees that online investors might be susceptible to "fatigue", as they go through the process of asserting that they are aware of the risks involved.

This month, Triodos Renewables, a UK subsidiary of the Dutch lender, is launching an equity crowdfunding campaign on Trillion Fund - a platform run by Ms Groves - to raise money for wind turbines in what will be one of the first big tests for the new regulations.

There are concerns that this tech-savvy industry might be hindered by the FCA's rules on financial promotion. Any company inviting investment must make any communication complete - including the small print, such as a risk warning. This presents problems when trying to spread the word to the crowd via Google adverts or tweets, which are limited to 140 characters.

"The lack of clarity is a big issue," says Barry James, founder of the Crowdfunding Centre research group and a critic of the new rules. "No one's quite sure. If I tweet or retweet, is that financial promotion and banned?"

headline: Brewer spreads the word to Barcelona as Spanish lap up Scottish artisan beer
author: Graham Keeley
text:
El vino has a new rival. Brewdog, Scotland's largest independent brewer, is about to serve up its premium beers in Spain. The company is to open a new bar in Barcelona next week, capitalising on the fashion for "artisan" drinks.

The bar will be Brewdog's first in the country and will open its doors on Wednesday in a former bank.

Brewdog, which started life eight years ago when two friends from Aberdeen started their own brewery, has become an international phenomenon, with the company exporting to Japan, Brazil, Italy and Sweden and opening bars there. Another opening is planned in Helsinki before Christmas.

Spain, traditionally a wine-drinking country, is now seeing a huge demand for craft beers. James Watt, the Brewdog co-founder, said Barcelona was the obvious choice to open the first bar on the Iberian Peninsula.

"Barcelona needs no introduction. Its reputation as a cultural centre precedes it. It's young, it's vibrant, it's artistic, it's experimental, it's proud, it's unapologetic, it's Brewdog," said Mr Watt. "The demand for craft beer has been growing year on year in Spain and our exports to the country reflect that trend. We have listened to our fans and set up our first outpost in the Iberian Peninsula."

The rival Arran Brewery, which exports its craft brews from the island on the west coast of Scotland, are also selling in Spain. Gerald Michaluk, the managing director, joined other British craft beer makers at a conference in Barcelona last week.

"The fashion for craft beer is growing all over the world and we wanted to take advantage of the fact it is becoming more popular here in Spain," said Mr Michaluk.

Arran Brewery also plans to open Britain's first sakérewery this year to export the rice-based wine to Japan.

Spanish craft beer lovers are opening their own bars and breweries. Oscar Fuentes, owner of Zyphos, which opened last week in Barcelona, said: "I would not have taken a chance like this if I did not think that there was a real market for this type of beer. The Spanish like it because it is a bit different." On sale at Zyphos are home-grown brands alongside Brewdog, Spitfire and Bishops' Finger. Drinkers in Spain are taking craft beers more seriously than other brews, said Richard Sanz, of the Menade brewery in Valladolid.

"Beer has woken up. For years it was the poor relation of wine which to many seemed a more serious product. This has changed," he said.

'Beer has woken up. For years it was the poor relation'

headline: Colouring book for grown-ups is a huge draw
text:
Colouring books are no longer Just for children. An illustrator has sold more than a million copies of a version that she designed purely for grown-ups (Lucy Holden writes).

Johanna Basford, below, from Ellon, Aberdeenshire, was asked to create a colouring book for children but suggested that one aimed at their parents might be more popular.

The first colouring book, The Secret Garden, was so popular that it has been translated into more than 14 languages and is sold in countries including Japan, Ukraine, Russia and Thailand. In Korea, more than 30,000 copies sold in nine weeks and in Paris it is outselling popular cookery books.

The second book, The Enchanted Forest, is doing equally as well, at £.95.

Ms Basford, 31, said people kept telling her that they craved colouring in her intricate, black-andwhite designs. "Colouring in appeals to people because it's a chance to do an analogue activity that's not on a screen and it's nostalgic," she said. "Everyone is creative deep down but as you get older you get less confident in your abilities."

Her books have been inspired by Arran, where Ms Basford spent holidays as a child. Her grandfather was head gardener at Brodick Castle, the National Trust property on the island.

She said: "The first book was inspired by the castle gardens, which were formal and full of mazes and lily ponds, and the second book was inspired by the sprawling woodlands that extended from the house on the grounds."

Ms Basford studied textile design at Duncan of Jordanstone College of Art and Design in Dundee, before becoming a commercial illustrator. She is married to James Watt, the owner of BrewDog.

headline: Craft brewery to hire 130 staff as popularity soars
text:
A craft beer company that has been described as a remarkable Scottish success story is to expand its operation and create 130 jobs in Scotland. BrewDog, which is based in Aberdeenshire but has bars all over the world, capitalised on the trend for independently brewed beers and sales of its Punk IPA have soared.

After winning a regional grant of £.5 million, the company will boost its staff in Ellon from 90 to 220.

The company was founded in 2007 by James Watt and Martin Dickie, then both 24, who disliked the "stuffy ales" that dominated the drinks market. John Swinney, the deputy first minister, visited the brewery yesterday and said he was delighted that the company was expanding.

headline: Barfly A-lister snubbed me...he knows me now
author: MARC DEANIE
text:
SUN TALKS TO OUTLANDER STAR AT UK PREMIERE Says Sam Heughan

OUTLANDER star Sam Heughan revealed he once worked minding booze for a chain-smoking Scots A-lister.

The 34-year-old was a barman pulling pints in a Glasgow joint when he was drafted in to hold the drinks every time the Hollywood actor went outside for a fag.

And Sam claims the big shot never once acknowledged him for his lowly, embarrassing odd job.

But the hunk is now an A-lister in his own right.

Sam —ho plays Highland warrior Jamie Fraser in the blockbuster TV series, which finally arrives in the UK this week —aid he's reminded the actor of their previous encounter when they met recently.

He said: "I won't say who the actor is but I know him now.

'It's a bit scary' "It was a gig that night. I'd been serving and then this job came along —o stand at the door holding his drink while he went for a cigarette. He would just come back in and take it without recognising me. It felt weird just standing there.

"It's funny now looking back on it and I've told him about it."

The Scottish Sun spoke to Sam as he prepared for £0million fantasy epic Outlander's UK premiere in London last night.

The actor, from New Galloway, Dumfriesshire, spent the afternoon speaking to the world's media in a room in the city's plush Soho Hotel. There's no mistaking his star status now. He's gone from a bit-part actor in River City to a leading man in a global hit, snapped up in Britain by Amazon Prime Instant Video —ith his own army of crazy fans called the Heughligans.

Sam said: "I suppose it is judgment day for us. It's a bit scary but exciting at the same time.

"I hope it goes down as well as it has across the world."

The screen hunk played footballer Andrew Murray in BBC soap River City in 2005. And a decade later he reckons Montego Street fame prepared him for the big time.

Sam said: "River City has its own fan base and I had a great time on it. It was a learning curve for me.

"I remember walking down the street and this guy hung his head out of his car and shouted, 'W***er!' At least in Scotland they tell it like it is. I'm prepared for people at home to let me know exactly what they think of the show."

Outlander is based on author Diana Gabaldon's books and is mostly shot in a warehouse in Cumbernauld. It is undoubtedly his biggest role yet.

Sam, who turns 35 next month, has ambitions of making it in movies. His next project is When The Starlight Ends, an indie flick with Scream star David Arquette. Sam said: "Outlander has definitely opened a lot of doors. For instance, the film I've just done was pretty much financed because of Outlander.

"Hopefully that will continue."

But his career won't be complete until he and co-star Caitriona Balfe, who plays nurse Claire Randall, appear on chat show host Jimmy Kimmel's 'Celebrities Read Mean Tweets' segment.

That is when stars face up to insulting socialmedia comments about themselves. Sam joked: "I hope it happens one day. Apparently I pluck my eyebrows too much.

"That's what someone on Twitter said. I found it quite amusing. I've never once plucked my eyebrows.

"I guess I've got very feminine eyebrows."

l The first eight episodes of Outlander are available to watch on Amazon Prime Instant Video from this Thursday, with a new episode released weekly every Sunday from April 5. marc.deanie@the-sun.co.uk 6I HOPED SCOTS WOULD GET GIG7 SAM admits he questioned the decision by TV bosses to hold Outlander's premiere in London —espite it being shot north of the border.

Amazon put on glitzy bash in the English capital at Shoreditch's White Rabbit.

A bagpiper greeted Sam and his gorgeous co-star Caitriona Balfe as they graced the red carpet alongside show supremo Ron Moore.

Inside kilt-wearing waiters served whisky and Scots beer BrewDog to the guests who included Press, telly executives and Outlander fans.

On the choice of location for the premiere, Sam said: "I asked questions about it myself as a proud Scotsman but I think it was down to everybody's schedules.

"I would love to have seen something happen at home.

"I believe they might be looking at doing something in Scotland for the second series. As long as they want us."

Sam and his co-stars were also helpless over the show's controversial UK blackout last year. Fans here cracked up when the rights to the drama were sold to 87 countries but not Britain. But last month Amazon announced it was to screen Outlander on its streaming service.

Last night Irish actress Caitriona, 35, said: "It's crazy that it's finally here. I'm really lucky that it's been shown in Ireland and all my friends and family have seen it.

"It's set in Scotland and it's about Scotland so it seemed strange that people couldn't see it."

headline: BREWDOG  TOASTING EPIC YEAR
text:
STRONG beer firm BREWDOG had an "epic" 2014 with annual profits soaring 69 per cent to £.9million.

Turnover was up 64 per cent to nearly £0million.

The Scottish brewer is behind the Punk IPA brand that has an alcohol content of 5.6 per cent. It also opened 12 bars in cities such as Tokyo, Sao Paulo and Helsinki, and opened its first craft beer bottle shop, BottleDog, in King's Cross, London The company said its growth is due in part to its crowd-funding scheme Equity for Punks. It closed a third round of the scheme in December 2013 after raising £.25million from "fanvestors".

Co-founder James Watt said: "2014 was an epic year for BrewDog and the craft beer industry."

headline: Craft brewer  BrewDog  launches UK record £5m crowdfunding appeal
author: Barney Thompson
text:
Independent craft brewer BrewDog has launched a UK record crowdfunding appeal with a target of £5m as it pursues an ambitious expansion scheme in the UK and abroad.

The Aberdeenshire-based brewery, which raised £.25m in its most recent crowdfunding round ending in December 2013, said it was offering 526,316 shares with a minimum investment of £5 for two shares. It said the capital would be used to expand its brewery and international bar division, as well as to fund new projects such as a craft beer hotel and a distillation plant.

BrewDog is selling the shares on its own platform, Equity for Punks, rather than through an equity crowdfunding site such as Crowdcube. Capitalising on a worldwide enthusiasm for craft beers, the company recorded turnover of more than £9.6m in 2014, up from £8m the previous year.

However, an equity crowdfunding programme on this scale has never been attempted before. Last year, only £4m was raised in total in the UK via equity crowdfunding, according to the innovation charity Nesta.

"We are not Rockefeller. We are Guy Fawkes," said James Watt, BrewDog co-founder, as the appeal was announced on Wednesday at the company's own bar in Shepherd's Bush, London. "We are putting the fat cats out to pasture and empowering everyone to be masters of their own destiny by investing in our passion for craft beer."

He added: "Equity for Punks puts the people who really care about our beer in control and keeps the passion and integrity in people's beer glasses."

Although the sector is minuscule by traditional investment standards, equity crowdfunding - as opposed to its alternative finance cousins, donation-based crowdfunding or peer-to-peer lending - has grown enormously in popularity as traditional bank lending retreated after the financial crisis.

It is typically used by small companies or start-ups, who as well as a share in the group also offer rewards to shareholders such as free or discounted products in return for their investment.

Founded in 2007, BrewDog exports to 55 countries and has opened 27 bars, with more planned this year for the UK, Berlin and Rome.

headline: LOMBARD Flair of the dog
author: Jonathan Guthrie
text:
Lombard last encountered James Watt at an enterprise contest seven or eight years ago. There, he denounced the judges for failing to give his start-up BrewDog first prize. Maybe he had a point: the Aberdeenshire brewery has done brilliantly since. Thanks to the popularity of craft beers and Mr Watt's aggressive style of marketing, his Punk IPA is widely gulped.

There is further evidence of the former fishing boat skipper's self-belief in the pricing of a £5m crowdfunding for the business.

The trailing price/earnings multiple for shares costing £7.50 each is a steep 116 times, or 61 times earnings before interest and other nasties.

Mr Watt points out that the New York burger chain Shake Shack attracted an equally stratospheric valuation.

The difference is that Shake Shack shares are freely tradable. BrewDog's are not, though you have to admire Mr Watt's chutzpah.

jonathan.guthrie@ft.com

headline: NATIONAL NEWS Brewer says £5m crowdfunding is no small beer; World-record target
author: Barney Thompson
text:
Independent brewery BrewDog has set a world-record £5m crowdfunding target as it pursues an ambitious expansion scheme in the UK and abroad.

The Aberdeenshire-based company, which already has more than 14,500 shareholders, is attempting to benefit from both the worldwide thirst for craft beer and the increasing enthusiasm of amateur investors for putting their money into businesses they believe in.

"We are not Rockefeller. We are Guy Fawkes," said James Watt, BrewDog co-founder, at the launch of the appeal at the company's own bar in Shepherd's Bush, west London. "We are putting the fat cats out to pasture and empowering everyone to be masters of their own destiny by investing in our passion for craft beer."

BrewDog, which raised £.25m in its previous crowdfunding appeal ending in December 2013, said it was offering 526,316 shares with a minimum investment of £5 for two shares. The capital would be used to build a new brewhouse with three times the capacity of its existing facility in Ellon, it added, as well as to open more UK bars, create a new beer and fund projects including a craft beer hotel and a distillation plant.

The BrewDog share offers, dubbed Equity for Punks after its signature Punk IPA , "puts the people who really care about our beer in control", Mr Watt added.

BrewDog recorded turnover of more than £9.6m in 2014, up from £8m the previous year. Founded in 2007, BrewDog exports to 55 countries and has opened 28 bars, with more planned this year for the UK and Europe.

Mr Watt valued the company at more than £80m - an ambitious claim given this is about 70 times its operating profits for last year.

BrewDog's share offer - its fourth round of crowdfunding - had been accredited by the UK Listing Authority, a branch of the Financial Conduct Authority, he added, which also meant the share offer was open to European investors.

Even with the company's recent success, an equity crowdfunding appeal has never been attempted on anything like this scale. In 2014, only £4m was raised in total in the UK via equity crowdfunding , according to the innovation charity Nesta. About £m to £m is usually considered the top end of what can be raised.

"By the standards of equity crowdfunding, this is the big time," said Stian Westlake, director of policy and research at Nesta. "This allows them to go out to people who really value the BrewDog brand and pitch to them. Being able to appeal to a constituency of investors who are really into the product is very important."

Although the sector is minuscule by traditional investment standards, equity crowdfunding has soared in popularity as traditional bank lending retreated after the financial crisis.

Typically used by small companies or start-ups, investors are often offered rewards such as free or discounted products in return for their investment. In BrewDog's case, shareholders can get discounts in the company's bars or when buying its beers online, and receive a free beer on their birthday.

"We have had a lot of hardcore beer geeks investing for the beer benefits," said Mr Watt.

"But now we are getting serious financial investors who are interested more and more from the business perspective."

Martin Love, a London-based engineer attending the event to announce the appeal, said he had bought a share in round three of Equity for Punks "mainly because I like BrewDog and what they do, but also for the discount in the bars and online, and the invite to their punk AGM".

But he added he was not expecting to make a profit. "This is more of a beer club than an investment," he said. "The AGM lasts about 30 minutes, followed by a six-hour beer festival."

headline: Q&A -  BrewDog 's Equity for Punks
author: Judith Evans
text:
Craft breweries are lapping up alternative finance faster than a hipster can sink a pint of hoppy ale. But if Scotland's BrewDog succeeds in its new crowdfunding round, it will leave previous efforts in the shade: the makers of Punk IPA are seeking £5m from the public over the next year.

Set up in 2007, BrewDog quickly became Scotland's biggest independent brewer, and last year had operating profits of £.9m. It is inviting drinkers across Europe to "love the beer, own the company". Should you join the party?

What exactly is on offer?

BrewDog is selling B-shares for £7.50 each, with a minimum investment of £5 for two shares. They come with discounts and loyalty bonuses; for example, all investors get at least 5 per cent off in BrewDog's bars. The offer has been dubbed Equity for Punks, after its signature Punk IPA.

They can also attend the brewery's annual general meeting, which goes far beyond the usual votes and sandwiches: this year's will feature live performances from indie band Idlewild and alt-rockers Twin Atlantic, along with beer tastings.

"BrewDog is widely known, and therefore this funding will in my view attract plenty of interest," says Gervais Williams, small-cap fund manager at Miton Group.

What's not to like?

If you're after discount beer and a good time, not much. If you're seeking a financial return, it is worth looking more closely at the numbers.

BrewDog, a crowdfunding pioneer, already has 14,500 individual shareholders; it says those who bought in its first funding round were able to cash in at a return of more than 500 per cent in a recent selling opportunity.

However, the current fundraising values the company at an ambitious £00m-plus, creating a trailing price-to-earnings ratio of 116 times.

This valuation "could limit the upside potential for investors in this round," says Emanuela Vartolomei, chief executive of All Street, a crowdfunding analysis service.

While she expects the company to keep growing, she says investors should be realistic about their goals. "For example, if you want to make 10 times your money, you need the company to be worth over £bn at your exit point."

Mr Williams highlights concerns over valuations in the crowdfunding sector. "Often there isn't a market price, so the pricing of the issue is justified on theoretical grounds," he says.

Another brewery, Camden Town, recently raised £.8m on Crowdcube, but slashed its valuation from £5m to £0m when it sold 20 per cent of the company to a separate group of investors.

Can I sell my shares whenever I like?

No - BrewDog is unquoted, so you can't sell shares on an exchange, but there is an annual opportunity to sell them on the Asset Match platform. However, this depends on buyers coming forward who are prepared to buy at a price acceptable to you.

The tabular content relating to this article is not available to view. Apologies in advance for the inconvenience caused.

You will have to pay Asset Match three per cent of the price of your shares for this, while the buyer must pay the same amount.

BrewDog founder James Watt says the company will eventually list on a market, but that is a "longer-term aspiration" and there are no current listing plans.

This is regulated, right?

BrewDog's prospectus is approved by the UK Listing Authority, but its Equity for Punks platform is not regulated by the Financial Conduct Authority, so it does not have to fulfil requirements imposed on big crowdfunding platforms such as Crowdcube or Seedrs.

Among other things, this means you cannot see live updates on how much the company has raised, and investors are not required to demonstrate that they understand the risks, as they are with other crowdfunding rounds.

So how risky is it?

Crowdfunding investments are risky. The FCA warns that "you are very likely to lose all your money" in the sector.

While BrewDog is comparatively well-established, Ben Yearsley, head of investment research at Charles Stanley Direct, highlights that there are more strictly regulated ways to invest in unquoted companies, such as through venture capital trusts, private equity investment trusts, or enterprise investment schemes.

"You also need to think through whether you will actually make money out of [this investment], and how and when do you exit," he adds.

Will I get dividends?

BrewDog has a policy of not paying dividends, instead reinvesting excess cash back into the company.

Will I have voting rights?

Yes, but more than three-quarters of the company is owned by founders and staff, so they could have the ability to push through initiatives you don't like.

Where will my money go?

BrewDog has set out its priorities, starting with building a bigger brewery, increasing its canned beer range, and opening more bars in the UK. If it raises less than the £5m it hopes for, the company will start with the top priorities on its list.

headline: BrewDog  admits errors in crowdfunding promotion
author: Judith Evans
text:
BrewDog has been forced to admit errors in material sent to potential investors after the City watchdog intervened over the promotion of the Scottish brewer’ record £5m crowdfunding pitch.

The company emailed shareholders to retract the claim that the “quity for Punks”nvestment was “pproved”y the UK Listing Authority, whose role is only to check that a company’ prospectus conforms with the rules.

Investors were also told to “gnore the point made in our email about the past increase in value that investors in [Equity for Punks 2] made” since this claim had not been approved as part of the company’ prospectus, according to the email from founder James Watt and his team sent on Monday.

Shareholders can withdraw their investments within two working days if they change their mind as a result of the new information, Mr Watt said.

The “pdate”ollowed an intervention by the Financial Conduct Authority after BrewDog launched its fundraising round last week.

Material sent out to promote the fundraising had not been approved by an FCA-authorised person and did not fully comply with financial promotions rules.

BrewDog, which is Scotland’ biggest independent brewer, is seeking to raise £5m from the public over a year, with a minimum investment of £5. If successful, the exercise will break global crowdfunding records.

The eight-year-old Aberdeenshire company last year made operating profits of £.9m. Known for its Punk IPA and general anti-establishment image, it was an early adopter of crowdfunding and most recently raised £.25m in a 2013 appeal.

The company is unusual in that it offers its shares directly to investors rather than through a third-party platform. This means Equity for Punks is not regulated as a crowdfunding platform; unlike Crowdcube, Seedrs and other crowdfunding venues, it does not have to carry out appropriateness tests or check that investors understand the risks.

However, it still has to conform with rules such as those governing financial promotions and equity offers.

“ey guys, this is an important email so please don’ ignore or delete it    he really great legal team at RW Blears who helped us with the offer and the documents have informed us that we need to provide you all with a few legal updates,”he email read.

“hen sending emails which are deemed to pertain to the selling of shares we are only allowed to make reference to things which are in our approved prospectus. Furthermore you should also note that the future is always uncertain,”t added.

Mr Watt said: “e’e happy that we’e completely compliant now. As a public company we take our responsibility to investors very seriously.”

He said that the fundraising was going “ell”nd had so far attracted about 60 per cent new investors and 40 per cent individuals who already held equity in BrewDog.

The fast-growing craft brewing industry —here are now more than 1,400 microbreweries in the UK —as increasingly turned to alternative finance over the past year, tapping into enthusiasm among drinkers for involvement in breweries’evelopment.

However, there have been questions over the valuations attached to brewers’quity offerings, including the more than £80m at which BrewDog now values itself.

Camden Town Brewery recently raised £.8m on Crowdcube, but slashed its valuation from £5m to £0m when it sold 20 per cent of the company to a separate group of investors.

headline: BrewDog  raises record £m in crowdfunding round
author: Judith Evans
text:
The Scottish independent brewer BrewDog has smashed equity crowdfunding records by raising £m from the public in the first three weeks of its fundraising round.

The craft brewer celebrated the milestone with a typically unorthodox PR stunt, dropping taxidermy “at cats”ith parachutes over the City of London to mark “ new dawn for beer and a new dawn for small business finance”

However, All Street —he first analyst firm dedicated to analysing crowdfunding investments —ut a damper on the celebrations by saying it was “ifficult to see how investors will make a financial return on this deal”

Emanuela Vartolomei, chief executive of All Street, underlined the “igh”aluation of £05m and lack of financial forecast disclosures. “here is very little clarity as to how the company will hit the revenue targets required to generate a risk adjusted return for investors,”he said.

She said, however, that the company had performed “xceptionally well”n the past. “014 was the company’ sixth consecutive year of growth, with annual turnover climbing 64 per cent to £9.6m. It is a strong company with sustainability at its heart and a talented workforce,”he said.

Seven-year-old BrewDog is seeking to raise £5m over a year in what is its fourth round of crowdfunding. It uses its own platform, Equity for Punks, rather than a regulated third-party platform such as Crowdcube or Seedrs, and raised £.3m in a previous round in 2013.

“any questioned our target of raising £5m, but we’e passed the record we set in 2013 in a fraction of the time,”aid BrewDog co-founder James Watt.

“t’ proof that there is a thirst for alternative finance out there, and that people are sick and tired of the fat cats controlling everyone’ money.”

Equity crowdfunding has grown fast, securing £4m of investments last year, according to Nesta, an innovation charity. The sector offers individuals the chance to take stakes in unlisted companies, with minimum investments starting as low as £0, although BrewDog asks investors to put in at least £5.

Like many other companies seeking crowdfunding investment, Aberdeenshire-based BrewDog offers extra rewards such as discounted beer for investors.

However, its fundraising has raised eyebrows in the crowdfunding industry, both because of its valuation and because it has opted to sell shares through its own platform.

Julia Groves, chair of the UK Crowdfunding Association, has written to the Financial Conduct Authority to raise concerns over the fact that BrewDog is not using a regulated platform and thus does not have to screen investors for “ppropriateness”nd present them with the same risk warnings that other crowdfunding websites do.

BrewDog opted to issue a prospectus under European securities rules and its fundraising is not being regulated as crowdfunding because it has not used a third-party platform. Its shares cannot be traded on an exchange, but there is an annual opportunity to buy and sell them using Asset Match, an online service.

The brewer plans to use the money for a series of projects including a new brewery, developing its canned beer range and opening more bars.

Crowdfunding investors often combine different motives, including being part of the “ourney of cutting-edge brands” noted Ms Vartolomei.

headline: SABMiller targets UK craft beer sector with Meantime buy
author: Nathalie Thomas, Kadhim Shubber and John Aglionby
text:
SABMiller, the global brewer behind brands such as Miller Lite, Grolsch and Peroni, has moved into the UK's increasingly popular craft beer sector by buying Meantime Brewing Company for an undisclosed sum.

SABMiller said it planned to increase Meantime's sales in the UK and to look at export opportunities in Europe but that it would be run independently to maintain its "entrepreneurial spirit".

The big beer groups have come under increasing pressure in markets such as the UK and US from craft brewers, as drinkers have become more interested in locally produced and artisan beers.

In the US, big beer groups that tried to tap into the craft beer movement by buying up smaller breweries came under fire from connoisseurs who slammed them as "crafty".

Sue Clark, managing director of SABMiller Europe, rejected the suggestion that the deal undermined the craft beer credentials of Meantime, which was founded in a Greenwich tram shed in 1999 by Alastair Hook.

She said SABMiller had a record in nurturing local beer companies and added that the craft label was becoming less relevant.

"The whole craft definition is one that over time we will see disappear. Craft for us is more about style, authenticity, than it is about the kind of label," she said. "At SABMiller we love local variety and carefully nurture our 200 local and heritage beers."

Craft and local beer is the fastest growing segment of the UK beer market. Last year, Meantime increased its beer sales by 58 per cent by volume to 64,500 hectolitres, or 11.35m pints, SABMiller said, outpacing the UK beer market's 1 per cent growth.

Meantime's products include London Pale Ale, London Lager, Yakima Red, Pilsner, India Pale Ale and London Porter.

Mr Hook said the partnership with SABMiller would enable him to realise his vision that "British beer would one day become world leading".

"I wanted to see London-brewed beers given an opportunity to take the world stage by combining a respect for our great brewing history with an approach that embraces modern technology and production," he said.

Nick Miller, Meantime's chief executive, insisted that Meantime would stay true to the values that have underpinned its success and that SABMiller's resources would give it the "chance to develop and break some boundaries".

The tabular content relating to this article is not available to view. Apologies in advance for the inconvenience caused.

"Why would Meantime suddenly not become a craft beer because it has a different set of shareholders?" he added.

The London brewery had £7m of turnover in 2014, up from £2m in 2013, and was already growing even faster in 2015, said Mr Miller.

About 60 shareholders, including Mr Miller, Mr Hook, employees and independent investors, had sold 100 per cent of the company to SABMiller, he added.

Although Ms Clark declined to be drawn on the valuation of the deal, she said it was "absolutely nowhere near" the multiples for Scottish brewery Brewdog's crowdfunding campaign, which has valued the company at more than 100 times earnings.

Meantime will open a pilot brewery that will become a centre for SABMiller's European innovation and new product development, SABMiller said.

"We want to harness the great work they have done to develop new beer styles," the company said.

Last month SABMiller reported that sales of soft drinks continued to outpace those of lager in the first three months of the year, continuing a recent trend.

headline: Taste for craft ale is anything but small beer for SABMILLER
author: Dominic Walsh
text:
When Nick Miller ran SABMiller's British operation, he took more than a passing interest in a few craft breweries, including the Meantime Brewing Company in London.

Four years after Mr Miller took "the biggest risk in my career" and quit the world's second-largest brewer to run Meantime, it was pints all round yesterday as SABMiller paid a sum estimated to have been well in excess of £0million for the maker of London Pale Ale, London Lager and Yakima Red.

Both parties dismissed fears that the ethos that has made Meantime a favourite among east London's bearded trendsetters might be lost as the small brewery near the mouth of the Blackwall Tunnel is swallowed up by the £8 billion international giant.

They emphasised that Meantime would remain a standalone business under the SABMiller umbrella, albeit one able to tap into its parent's resources to realise its ambitious growth plans to cash in on the craft beer revolution.

Mr Miller, who will continue to run the operation alongside Alastair Hook, the founder and brewmaster, said: "I would hope the hipsters will see through share ownership.

"Can you imagine some SABMiller suit coming in and telling Alastair Hook to change his beer? There'd be fur and feathers flying. The philosphy behind this business will not change."

Sue Clark, the managing director of SABMiller Europe, said: "The hipsters are right. Meantime beers are lovely and we're not going to change them, but they will be available in more places around the UK so more lovers of fine beer can join the party —nd you don't even have to have a beard."

However, not everyone was as ecstatic. Camra, the real ale lobby group, fretted that big might not necessarily beautiful. It was concerned that "choice for beer drinkers may be reduced" after the deal, as SABMiller might be tempted to focus on a "handful of core beers rather than a wide range".

The owner of Peroni and Pilsner Urquell, which has never had its own brewery in Britain, pointed out that its brands already included several craft brews that had been "carefully nurtured" since acquisition, including Leinenkugel's and Blue Moon in the US, Fat Yak in Australia and St Stefanus, a Belgian abbey beer.

Under SABMiller, Meantime's sales push beyond its London heartland will be accelerated in Britain and overseas. "I believe we can use this deal as a platform to fly the flag for Britishness and authentic beer around the world," Mr Miller said.

In addition to the main brewery, the deal includes its tasting room, and two local pubs.

Meantime was founded by Mr Hook in 1999 in a former tramshed in Charlton. Unlike most craft breweries, it focuses on keg and bottled beers rather than real ale.

Like SABMiller, which sells unpasteurised Pilsner Urquell lager from its brewery in Pilsen for sale in pubs with large tanks, Meantime sells "brewery fresh" beer to four "tank pubs" in London.

Last year, Meantime, produced about 11.4 million pints, up 58 per cent on the year before, and reported turnover of about £7.5 million, although both are forecast to rise sharply this year.

Neither party would be drawn on the price, although Ms Clark said that it was "absolutely nowhere near" the earnings multiple of more than 100 implied by the crowdfunding for BrewDog, the Scottish brewer based in Aberdeenshire, which recently raised £ million, giving it a valuation of £05 million.

Meantime has only £ million of debt, which will enable its 60 investors to divide up the lion's share of the proceeds.

The biggest shareholders are Bill Bottriel, a recruitment entrepreneur, and Mr Miller and Mr Hook, though none has more than 18 per cent.

Mr Miller said that there was no earnout: "It's a clean deal —e're staying on because we want to."

Going mainstream Pret A Manger In 2001, the ethically minded sandwich chain sold a 33 per cent stake to McDonald's for about $50 million. The uneasy relationship with the burger giant ended in 2008, when Pret was sold to Bridgepoint.

Innocent Drinks In 2009, a decade after mashing £00 of fruit into a giant smoothie on a kitchen table, Richard Reed, Adam Balon and Jon Wright sold an 18 per cent stake to Coca-Cola for £0 million. A year later, Coke swallowed a further 30 per cent for £6 million, then pushed its stake past 90 per cent in 2013, paying a further £00 million or so.

Body Shop Anita Roddick was accused of betraying her principles in 2006 when she and her husband Gordon made about £30million after selling the ethical retailer for £52million to L'Orél Group.

Green& Black'sCraig Sams, a hippy from Nebraska and organic food pioneer, and his wife, Jo, sold their chocolate business to Cadbury in 2005 for an estimated £0 million.

Ben & Jerry's Founded by Jerry Greenfield and Ben Cohen after they completed a correspondence course on frozen desserts, the ice cream maker was sold in a £00million deal to Unilever in 2000. The co-founders said they hoped their company would "expand its role in society".

Doom Bar Sharp's Brewery, whose main tipple is named after the notorious sandbank at the mouth of the Camel estuary in Cornwall, was swallowed by Molson Coors, the American brewer, in 2011 for £0 million. £0 million.

headline: BREW ARE THE TOPS AT STUNTS
text:
Shaf Rasul Shaf Rasul One in a million shaf@the-sun.co.uk

IT MAKES ME LAUGH I OFTEN enjoy having a chuckle at the latest PR stunts devised by business big shots, and this week was no different.

I tip my hat to Scottish brewing company, Brew-Dog, which stole the limelight with a clever stunt - telling its own story while tying it in with the current news agenda.

BrewDog dropped 'taxidermy fat cats' out of a helicopter over the City of London, in order to gain publicity for its latest crowdfunding venture.

The cats, clutching BrewDog's manifesto, nodded to the current political environment. The stunt was described by Brew-Dog founder, James Watt, pictured, as "showcasing the viability of alternative forms of finance" and having already raised £m in crowdfunding, it's certainly proving its point!

IT MAKES ME LAUGH ME CRY LAST week marked the start of exams for our secondary school students — memory which, in itself, makes me weep.

But it was the related news that really concerned me - more young people are turning to legal highs to cope with stress, potentially creating long-term physical and mental health problems.

I only hope that the government further clamps down on legal highs, and that our education system continues to teach our young people the dangers.

While I'll never forget the fear of an impending exam, hard work and dedication during those times helped me get through, and I hope that others can do the same without resorting to dangerous substances.

headline: Investors pour £.3m into bubbly mini-bond
author: Judith Evans
text:
The Hambledon vineyard in Hampshire has raised £.3m through a convertible mini-bond issue on the crowdfunding site Crowdbnk, becoming the latest in a series of drinks companies to seek funding from the public.

The sparkling wine growers plan to use the funds to expand their output and build up stocks.

The five-year bond will pay an annual yield of 8 per cent —ll paid on redemption —ith the option of converting to equity at maturity: investors must put in at least £,600 and will receive half a case of wine a year as part of the deal. The bond is secured against the vineyard’ assets.

Analysts warn, however, that mini-bonds are not traded on an index and can be risky.

Small drinks companies have increasingly turned to crowdfunding to raise money, often from enthusiastic customers, over the past three years.

Naked Wines, an online retailer, raised £m through a mini-bond issue two years ago, while more recently a string of breweries and vineyards have sold equity online, sometimes with free or discounted drinks included.

Brewdog, the Scottish independent brewer, has raised £m so far in a year-long equity crowdfunding drive, while Camden Town Brewery raised £.7m this year on Crowdcube. ISDX-listed Chapel Down raised £.95m on Seedrs in 2014.

headline: Corporate finance houses turn to crowdfunding
author: Judith Evans
text:
Corporate finance houses including FinnCap and Grant Thornton are to list early-stage equity deals on a crowdfunding website in the first tie-up of its kind.

The brokers plan to list a portion of investments that have typically only been available to wealthy investors and venture capitalists, offering the same terms to individual investors as institutions. People will be able to invest as little as £00.

The arrangement demonstrates the extent to which City firms have begun to take equity and debt crowdfunding seriously as its growth accelerates.

InvestingZone, a crowdfunding site backed by venture capitalist Jon Moulton, will begin by offering equity in Every Cloud, a UK-based cloud computing company, through the Manchester-based broker Acceleris.

“hese are deals that the public wouldn’ normally get to see, deals that have already had quite a bit of institutional investment,”aid InvestingZone chief executive Jean Miller.

Ms Miller said the brokers, which also include Nexus and CMR, agreed to take part because companies are increasingly turning to crowdfunding for finance in their early stages.

“hey [the brokers] realised that if they don’ have a profile in this growing market they could lose out. This is a good way to get sensible companies in the pipeline and it also increases investment capacity.”

Some £4m was raised through online equity crowdfunding platforms last year, according to Nesta, an innovation charity, while three big platforms have between them done £8m of deals this year, according to the data provider AltFi.

The Financial Conduct Authority has warned that crowdfunding is very risky for investors, saying “ou are very likely to lose all your money”n the asset class, as most start-ups fail.

Simon Thorn, corporate finance executive at Acceleris —hich advised on the £5m Aim flotation of RedX Pharma in March —aid his company expected to offer about five deals through InvestingZone this year.

The company is keen to support efforts to professionalise crowdfunding, he added. “rowdfunding is a fantastic way for early-stage or start-up companies to attract a first round of funding. But if that’ not carried out professionally —f it was at, say, a very high valuation —hen that could be a problem later. It needs to be done with a commonsense approach.”

Crowdfunding has offered companies the chance to raise money cheaply from relatively undemanding retail investors. High-profile fundraisers have included BrewDog, the independent Scottish brewer, which runs its own crowdfunding platform.

But the sector is increasingly forging links with established finance. SyndicateRoom, another platform, offers deals led by “ngel”nvestors, who negotiate terms with start-ups on behalf of the crowd.

“ompanies need to understand that taking on investment is serious, it’ not just cheap cash,”aid Ms Miller.

headline: Raise your glass for crafty profits
author: Shaf Rasul
text:
WE all know that whisky is one of Scotland's biggest international exports, but there's another drink catching up and making its mark abroad. Scottish craft beer is swiftly becoming a big industry with the stuff being exported to all corners of the globe.

Interestingly enough, some of the business-savvy breweries conquering international markets are relatively new kids on the block.

Edinburgh's Innis & Gunn - founded in 2002 —s a significant name in the marketplace. It exports more product than it sells here, with 70 per cent going off to markets like Canada and Sweden.

And up there amongst the leaders of the pack is of course BrewDog, which was founded in 2007.

When it comes to business strategy, this company in particular has shown it really knows how to make its mark both at home and abroad.

Brewdog's rise is incredible Not only does it have a massively successful Aberdeenshire brewery and chain of bars, it also exports to an incredible 55 countries.

In fact, in the last week alone, it announced it's going to open a brewery in America, employing 100 people in Columbus, Ohio.

It doesn't stop there either. BrewDog has shown it means business when it comes to further growth.

fur-It recently appointed its first ever head of international sales, Andrew Hatherell, to help spot opportunities in new global territories.

When you think about this Scottish company's rapid rise to success, it really is quite incredible.

It wasn't so long ago that it seemed to spring out of nowhere in a flurry of headline-grabbing 'gimmicks' and stunts.

I suppose the unconventional job titles of the men at the helm —Captain and co-founder' James Watt and 'Beer pirate and co-founder' James Dickie —ere the first clue that this was not going to be your run-of-the-mill drinks manufacturer.

They seem to have gone out their way to be controversial and push the boundaries.

They've parachuted taxidermy cats into London, driven a tank into Camden, and brewed beer under the sea to name just a few notable moments.

Were these the moves of an attention-hungry company that simply wanted to grab a few headlines? I don't think so. It's clearly much more strategic than that.

The endless PR stunts and quirky product launches actually show the strong business acumen of the BrewDog bosses.

They've learned that without constant creativity and innovation, it is impossible to thrive in a competitive marketplace.

BrewDog's tiny voice might never have been heard above those of the big multinational drinks companies, and their massive budgets, without bold marketing. They obviously recognised that, and devised a comalone, way to stand out from the crowd. As a result, the company that started out with a £0,000 bank loan has become a multi-million pound success story.

They celebrate jobs created Now the headlines about BrewDog don't just revolve around the strength of the beer, or the company's latest crazy marketing antics.

They focus on its phenomenal financial performance, like the 69 per cent profit rise or whopping £9.6million turnover it enjoyed last year. They dis-cuss the company's ambitious financial plans, like the £5million crowdfunding appeal it recently launched.

They celebrate the jobs it is creating, like the 130 it announced for Ellon in March.

And they talk about the work the company is creating for other Scottish firms - like Paisley's Barr Construction which won the £million contract to expand its Aberdeenshire headquarters. With that in mind, I'd disagree with anyone who reckons

headline: Chart that tells a story - brewing old and new
author: Judith Evans
text:
What does this show?

It shows five smaller companies producing British beer - the old-school real ale brewers Young's (founded 1831) and Adnams (1872) and the unlisted craft brewers BrewDog, Camden Town Brewery and Delavals, all founded in the last eight years.

A decade of decline in UK beer sales came to an end last year, and we now have more breweries per person than any other country in the world, according to the Good Beer Guide.

The veteran brewers are both listed - Young's on Aim and Adnams on ISDX. But the newer companies have opted for the fashionable fundraising method of crowdfunding. The brewers are shown by valuation, using market capitalisation for the listed companies and post-fundraising valuations for the others.

So is it happy hour?

Not exactly. BrewDog and Camden Town have both taken some flak over their valuations, with analysts suggesting crowdfunding investors - who take on high levels of risk when they invest - would find it hard to make a financial return. For example, BrewDog's valuation implies a trailing price-to-earnings ratio of 115 times, against 22.7 for Young's and 16.2 for Adnams.

The comparison is not straightforward, as this is a backward-looking figure and the new breweries are faster-growing. Nevertheless, there are useful points to note. Adnams turned over £0m last year, while BrewDog's turnover was just below £0m and Camden Town's turnover was £m.

After Camden Town raised £.7m through crowdfunding in February and March, it told investors it had reduced its valuation from £5m to £0m when additional private investors came on board, increasing the size of investors' stakes.

"Recent crowdfunding deals appear to add further spice to valuations in the sector," noted Greg Johnson, an analyst at Shore Capital.

What about Delavals?

The youngest company of the group, Delavals, is valued at just £.3m. Jon Moulton, who runs the private equity firm Better Capital, has invested.

A smaller business that revamped itself from a "lifestyle" company in 2012, Delavals has a big point in its favour: it is the officially licensed brewer to the National Trust and operates the heritage organisation's beer club.

The tabular content relating to this article is not available to view. Apologies in advance for the inconvenience caused.

The company has just four staff and does not run any pubs or bars, unlike the others - but it does have access to the National Trust's 4m members. It wants to sell a 29 per cent stake for a total of £00,000 to expand its distribution. InvestingZone, which is hosting its crowdfunding bid, says investors will be able to access significant future growth.

Delavals is eligible for tax relief under the Enterprise Investment Scheme, while Camden Town has also been seeking that status.

So where to invest?

It depends on your goals (and whether free or discounted beer is a priority - both BrewDog and Camden Town offer these perks to investors).

Adnams and Young's may not offer the potential for explosive growth - or that craft brewing hipster cachet - but they are tradeable, stable and you can't argue they don't know their beer.

With crowdfunding, you may well lose all your cash. Unlisted companies are highly illiquid and you could find it very difficult to dispose of your investment when you want to. On the other hand, there could be growth potential - as long as the initial price isn't too frothy.

Amendment: This article should have made clear that Young's is now purely a pub company, having sold its brewery in 2006 - so while Adnams and BrewDog also run pubs and bars, Young's has a different business model.

headline: Scottish craft beer squares up to Scotch
author: Mure Dickie in Edinburgh
text:
Beside the cured salmon, prime beef and gourmet chocolate, there was a more unusual addition to the menu at this year’ Scottish food and drink industry awards —n ice-bucket of beers on every table.

The craft beers, supplied by eight founding members of the fledgling Brewers Association of Scotland, were a statement of intent for a small but fast-growing sector that hopes to establish itself as a central part of a national food and drink industry that generated £.1bn in exports last year.

Scotch accounted for nearly £bn of that and founders of the brewers’ssociation say that banding together and working more closely with the Scottish government will help craft beer win a place alongside whisky as an alternative national drink.

“e want to put Scottish craft brewers on an international level, as a group and with government backing,”ays Scott Williams of Williams Bros Brewing, an association founder.

Alloa-based Williams Bros has nearly tripled its capacity during the past five years to an expected 36,000 hectolitres, or 6.3m pints, this year — tale of rapid expansion common in the Scottish craft beer sector.

Scotland’ craft beer boom is part of a wider UK trend, since former chancellor Gordon Brown in 2002 introduced progressive beer duty relief that made small producers more competitive against the mass market brewers.

It also reflects the influence of independent US brewers that have developed distinctive new beers that use hops to stronger effect than in traditional brews.

Details of the Scottish industry are scanty, and one of the new association’ top priorities is to survey the 85-100 craft brewers operating in Scotland to establish the size of their operations and get a sense of their plans.

“here’ a bit of an information vacuum,”ays Dougal Sharp, founder of Edinburgh-based Innis & Gunn and the first chairman of the brewers’ssociation, which was established with government support in January.

The group aims to make it easier to emulate the whisky industry’ international brand building and tourist tie-ins, while also working to maintain quality standards, Mr Sharp says.

Innis & Gunn is planning to start building its first brewery this year after previously relying on outsourced production for beers that it ages in oak casks. Other independent brewers have already expanded output.

After years of struggling to keep up with demand, Fyne Ales, situated near a scenic loch of the same name in western Argyll, opened a £m brewery in November.

The Highland location is an important part of Fyne Ales’haracter, says sales manager Chris Black, pointing at a small river rushing off the heather-clad mountain behind the brewery. “hat’ our water supply,”r Black says.

headline: FT WEEKEND SUPPLEMENT - MONEY Illiquid breweries; Chart that tells a story
author: Judith Evans
text:
What does this show?

It shows five smaller companies producing British beer - real ale brewers Young's (founded 1831) and Adnams (1872) and the unlisted craft brewers Camden Town Brewery, BrewDog, and Delavals, all founded in the past eight years.
