So is it happy hour?

Not exactly. BrewDog and Camden Town have both taken some flak over their valuations, with analysts suggesting that crowdfunding investors - who take on high levels of risk when they invest - would find it hard to make a financial return. For example, BrewDog's valuation implies a trailing price/earnings ratio of 115 times, against 22.7 for Young's and 16.2 for Adnams.

The comparison is not straightforward, as this is a backward-looking figure and the new breweries are faster-growing. Nevertheless, there are useful points to note. Adnams turned over £0m last year, while BrewDog's turnover was just below £0m and Camden Town's was £m.

What about Delavals?

The youngest company of the group, Delavals, is valued at just £.3m. Jon Moulton, who runs the private equity firm Better Capital, has invested.

A smaller business that revamped itself from a "lifestyle" company in 2012, Delavals has a big point in its favour: it is the officially licensed brewer to the National Trust and operates the heritage organisation's beer club.

It wants to sell a 29 per cent stake for a total of £00,000 to expand its distribution. InvestingZone, which is hosting its crowdfunding bid, says investors will be able to access significant future growth.

So where to invest?

It depends on your goals (and whether free or discounted beer is a priority - both BrewDog and Camden Town offer these perks to investors).

Adnams and Young's may not offer the potential for explosive growth - or that craft brewing hipster cachet - but they are tradeable, stable and you can't argue they don't know their beer.

headline: NATIONAL NEWS Scottish craft beer aims to emulate whisky as alternative national drink; Food and drink: Brand-building
author: Mure Dickie in Edinburgh
text:
Fledgling brewers association is squaring up to Scotch in bid to establish global reputation

Beside the cured salmon, prime beef and gourmet chocolate, there was a more unusual addition to the menu at this year's Scottish food and drink industry awards - an ice-bucket of beers on every table.

The craft beers, supplied by eight founding members of the fledgling Brewers Association of Scotland, were a statement of intent for a small but fast-growing sector that hopes to establish itself as a central part of a national food and drink industry that generated £.1bn in exports last year.

Scotch accounted for nearly £bn of that and founders of the brewers' association say that banding together and working more closely with the Scottish government will help craft beer win a place alongside whisky as a national drink. "We want to put Scottish craft brewers on an international level, as a group and with government backing," says Scott Williams of Williams Bros Brewing, an association founder.

Alloa-based Williams Bros has nearly tripled capacity during the past five years to an expected 36,000 hectolitres, or 6.3m pints, this year - a tale of rapid expansion common in the Scottish craft beer sector.

Scotland's craft beer boom is part of a wider UK trend, since former chancellor Gordon Brown in 2002 introduced progressive beer duty relief that made small producers more competitive against the mass-market brewers. It also reflects the influence of independent US brewers that have developed distinctive beers that use hops to stronger effect than in traditional brews.

Details of the Scottish industry are scanty, and one of the new association's top priorities is to survey the 85-100 craft brewers operating in Scotland to establish the size of their operations and get a sense of their plans.

"There's a bit of an information vacuum," says Dougal Sharp, founder of Edinburgh-based Innis & Gunn and the first chairman of the brewers' association, which was established with government support in January.

The group aims to make it easier to emulate the whisky industry's international brand building and tourist tie-ins, while also working to maintain quality standards, Mr Sharp says.

Innis & Gunn is planning to start building its first brewery this year after having relied on outsourced production for beers that it ages in oak casks.

Other independent brewers have already expanded output. After years of struggling to keep up with demand, Fyne Ales, situated near a scenic loch of the same name in western Argyll, opened a £m brewery in November.

The Highland location is an important part of Fyne Ales' character, says sales manager Chris Black, pointing at a small river rushing off the heather-clad mountain behind the brewery. "That's our water supply," Mr Black says.

Fyne Ales is a founding member of the association and with its on-site bar and shop, and participation in festivals and other events is well-placed to become a fixture of Argyll's tourist scene.

Richard Lochhead, the Scottish cabinet secretary responsible for food and drink, says he is supporting the association so it can "provide a strong voice" for the craft beer industry. "Scottish craft beer is a real success story," Mr Lochhead said. "The wider Scottish economy has benefited from the increased income through exports, new jobs and the enhanced reputation of Scottish food and drink on the world stage."

The Scottish government has offered backing to some brewers, in March awarding a £.5m grant to national craft sector leader BrewDog , an iconoclastic brand based in Aberdeenshire with an international marketing arm that includes bars from Sã Paulo to Tokyo.

With capacity of 250,000 hectolitres a year, BrewDog is too big to qualify as a member of the association and brewery co-founder Martin Dickie says a Scottish group "may be too small a niche".

Instead, BrewDog is trying to establish a UK-wide definition of craft beer through another new association of British craft brewers that it plans to establish with English producers including Camden Town Brewery and Magic Rock Brewing.

Big brewers are causing confusion by marketing craft beers that do not reflect the values of the independent sector and have less hops and taste, Mr Dickie complains. "For them to be allowed to call it 'craft' is dishonest," he says.

Ale and arty

Fermenting sector bubbles over with good ideas

The Scottish craft beer sector is a ferment of innovation, bubbling with new products, marketing methods and fundraising strategies.

To promote its heather ale, William Bros commissioned a comic book retelling the 19th-century poetic tale that inspired its invention, and one of its biggest-selling beers is a fusion of lager and India pale ale. Edinburgh's Stewart Brewing offers a craft brew kitchen where drinkers can make batches of beer to their own taste.

Innis & Gunn focuses on the "curiously distinctive" taste its beers get from being aged in oak barrels or percolated through wood chips, an approach shared for some brews by Harviestoun, which describes its Ola Dubh 12 as tasting of "smoky Worcestershire sauce, leather, iodine and grilled steak".

BrewDog smashed crowdfunding records by raising £m in the first three weeks of its "Equity for Punks", despite being forced to correct errors in material sent to potential investors.

headline: LESSONS FOR THE GREEK BANKS; COMMENT
author: ALEX BRUMMER CITY EDITOR
text:
Whatever the final outcome of the emergency euroland summits on Greece markets already have made up their minds.

The euro is at its best level against the dollar for several weeks, equity markets across Europe have jumped, Greek bond yields have been trimmed to just over 11pc and Greek bank shares are up 20pc.

As far as the markets are concerned, a Grexit and challenge to the credibility of the euro has been dodged despite some of the inflammatory rhetoric from both sides.

So what now? If ruling party Syriza is interested in a more permanent fix to the nation's problems it could start with the banking sector.

The invisible or wholesale run on the four big banks - Alpha, National Bank of Greece, Piraeus Bank and Eurobank - reflects deep worries not just about the financial system but about Greece itself.

Deutsche Bank estimates that the European Central Bank (ECB) is propping up the banks to the tune of €22bn in emergency and other funding. A further €7bn has been sunk into longer-term Greek bonds. Cash is still leaving the banks, which have imposed informal curbs on withdrawals - a step short of official capital controls.

A potential institutional repair, which would ease the credibility threat to the ECB, is to fund a restructuring of the Greek banks using the European Stability Mechanism (ESM). This is the firewall set up when the whole eurozone looked in danger in 2012.

If there is to be a genuine recovery then confidence in the functioning of Greek banks has to be restored.

All those countries that have recovered from crisis, including Britain, Ireland, Iceland and Spain, have had to go through painful bank recapitalisations. Without functioning banks, commerce is moribund and IOUs and other temporary measures have to be deployed.

It is reckoned that at least 40pc of the existing loans by Greek banks are rotten, and these losses need to be recognised. If it means the Greek government or ESM taking stakes in the banks, as part of the recapitalisation, that has to be done.

What cannot happen very much longer is for funds to pour out of the banks.

Without the capacity to lend, the incipient recovery, seen earlier this year, cannot be supported. But recapitalisation and restoration of the banks requires forceful government intervention, something of which the showboating government of Alexis Tsipras seem incapable.

What the past few days has illustrated is that despite IMF bluster and the tough line of Germany's finance minister Wolfgang Schäble, there is a determination in official circles to get a deal done. Last night the US Treasury Secretary Jack Lew was on the blower urging action for the sake of stability.

The fix is in.

BRITONS are the world's largest consumers of chocolate per head, leaving the US and China, the other big global gluttons, in the dust.

It seems unthinkable that with the exit of Thorntons, which is about to be gobbled up by Ferrero Rocher, all that we will be left with is a few niche players.

Rowntree Mackintosh vanished into Nestle in 1988. Cadbury vanished into the anonymous arms of Kraft, now Mondelez, in 2010 for £1.5bn, leaving behind a trail of destruction included a closed factory in Bristol, Wispa bar manufacturing in Poland, a changed taste for Cadbury Dairy Milk and the horror of chocolate flavoured Philadelphia cheese. Yuk.

The difficulty in defending Thorntons is that it has never been and has shown no signs of becoming a national champion. Its chocolates have never been distinctive. The fillings at least are cocoa based, unlike the pasty hazelnut extrusions that made the Ferrero family very rich.

Thorntons' retail operation has floundered and its efforts to exploit the supermarket route melted away.

The approach of a chocolate-daddy, offering a 66pc premium, is obviously irresistible to investors. It is interesting that Ferrero, which was seen as a potential white knight at the time of the Cadbury deal, sees some value in Thorntons. It plans severe pruning and shifting of resources overseas, although promising to keep open the Alfreton factory.

How worried should we be? Hotel Chocolat has demonstrated that Britain still has the ability to build an enterprise around quality and some crowd-funding techniques with the creation of chocolate bonds.

Similarly, Brewdog and other craft brewers have moved into the vacuum left by the departure of bigger players such as Scottish & Newcastle.

But Britain has to ask itself why it is always second best in enterprises such as chocolate and brewing when it once led the world.

SKY shares climbed in latest trading following reports of bid rebuffed bid approaches from Vivendi and Vodafone.

The Murdoch family, with 39pc of the stock, has the ability to kill unwanted attentions. But the Vivendi offer of £8 a share is also a poison pill for Murdoch's 21st Century Fox. If it wanted to buy the minority it would have to pay more, or face an almighty shareholder revolt.

©aily Mail

headline: NATIONAL NEWS French develop nose for future of Scotch; Whisky: Export market
author: Mure Dickie in Speyside
text:
Pernod Ricard defies downturn by putting its faith in sector with opening of £5m distillery

Pernod Ricard has opened a £5m whisky distillery in Scotland, shrugging off worries about the future of the sector caused by recent falls in the global sales of Scotch.

Nestled near the banks of the River Spey, the Dalmunach Distillery is an architecturally impressive symbol of a surge in production driven by years of booming demand. The plant is expected to produce 10m litres of whisky a year.

Opening the French group's distillery yesterday, Nicola Sturgeon, Scotland's first minister, hailed it as a "real vote of confidence" in the sector.

Scotch exports fell 7.4 per cent to £.94bn last year, the second successive annual decline and the biggest fall since 1998, with the slide reawakening memories of past whisky sector downturns in what is a notoriously cyclical business.

Alexandre Ricard, the chairman of Pernod Ricard and grandson of its founder, acknowledged at the event that demand had been hurt by factors such as the economic turbulence in Europe and an anti-corruption purge in China.

But Mr Ricard said his company's Scotch business, Chivas Brothers, had defied the downturn, with its global exports volume growing 4 per cent last year compared with a 3 per cent decline in the sector as a whole.

The group was confident demand would match supply after years of pressure on stocks, particularly of single malt whiskies, Mr Ricard said. "We believe we now have a nice balance," he said, adding that Chivas had been enjoying strong growth in demand for single malts in the US.

Pernod Ricard, which has increased capacity by 17 per cent in the past few years and accounts for about a fifth of Scotland's distilling output, says it will also continue to at least double the production capacity of its Glenlivet malt from just over 10m litres a year.

"Malts are still undersupplied," Mr Ricard said, adding the industry still saw great potential for growth in markets such as India, where he said the population of drinking-age consumers was growing by 22m people a year.

Other leading whisky producers also insist the downturn in demand for Scotch reflects temporary factors. Diageo, which has doubled its production of Scotch in the past decade, is reported to be reviewing some of its investment plans . A spokesperson for the group said it would "continue to invest".

"We manage our Scotch business for the long term," they said. "Growth will not always come in a straight line."

While the Dalmunach plant boasts copper stills and old machinery, it is an advanced energy-efficient production line in a vaulted glass-walled space that has picked up architectural awards .

Not a whisky drinker, Ms Sturgeon happily nosed some "new make" spirit after opening the distillery, which has been in production since last year.

The facility will mainly produce spirit for use in Pernod Ricard blends such as Ballantine's and Chivas Regal. "It's a very, very floral fruity spirit," said Trevor Buckley, operations manager.

Some observers say heritage-focused Scotch producers have been too conservative in product development and marketing, citing this as a factor in the better performance of US whiskeys in key markets such as the US and France.

But Mr Ricard insisted Chivas was capable of innovation, citing the launch last year of Ballantine's Brasil, a "spirit drink" made of Scotch steeped in Brazilian lime peel and marketed with a suggested cocktail dubbed the "Highland Samba".

Chivas was also launching Barrelhound , a sweeter-tasting Scotch to compete with US bourbons.

BrewDog

Scottish brewer snaps at heels of big producers

From an industrial estate north of Aberdeen, BrewDog, the Scottish beer company, aims to snap at the heels of the UK spirits market with a new whisky distillery.

Far from being dismayed by falling global demand for Scotch , Martin Dickie, left, BrewDog's co-founder, says the decline illustrates a lack of innovation among Scotland's big producers - and likens it to the beer sector before the influx of craft brewers.

"There's a lot of history and heritage and a few big players that have been doing the same thing day-in, day-out for the last 50, 100, 150 years," Mr Dickie says. "There's been very little change to the distillation process in terms of how they make their wash and how they distil it."

BrewDog this month ordered custom-made stills that will make small-batch spirits using its own beers as a base. Mr Dickie says it plans to come up with distinctive new takes on Scotch and other spirits. "It's all about the flavour and the taste that comes off the raw materials," he says.

BrewDog will also bring its irreverent marketing to a sector that tends to play to established themes.

The company's biggest seller is its Punk IPA and it cites as a possible future product a gin based on its Jack Hammer beer, which is marketed as the "hopped-up, pucker-mouthed terminal extreme of craft brewing". Mure Dickie

headline: Money Mail INVESTING WITH THE CROWD CAN EARN YOU 6.5%
author: HOLLY BLACK
text:
WOULD you invest in your favourite sports team? What about backing a mobile phone app or your favourite TV chef in an attempt to earn some extra interest on your cash?

New breeds of investment have captured the imagination and allow you to take a punt on trendy, aspirational British businesses, as well as more established names.

The investments —alled crowdfunding and retail bonds —an offer high returns.

The idea behind both schemes is the same: you give the business money and in exchange they promise you a set return.

This can be a standard rate of interest or sometimes discounts or offers for that firm.

Crowdfunding is generally for start-ups and businesses hoping to grow. Essentially, you're part of a crowd of people funding the business through smallish sums.

Retail bonds are usually for better-known companies looking to expand and pay down debt.

The bonds they issue promise to pay a set rate of interest for an agreed number of years —nd you should get your capital back at the end, too.

The attraction is that both offer returns that should be higher than you'll find anywhere else on the High Street.

But these investments aren't like standard savings deals from banks —ven if they do tend to look like them.

Your money is at risk. So, savers need to consider whether the projects they are backing are really investment-worthy.

Crowdfunding is particularly appealing to those who are already customers of a business and would like to chip in £00 or more to help it to take off.

Chef Hugh Fearnley-Whittingstall raised £ million from loyal customers in just 36 hours to expand his chain of River Cottage restaurants last summer.

The 283 savers who provided a minimum of £00 each are promised an annual 7 pc return in  ddition to discounts when dining at the chain.

But not all investments of this kind pay cash returns.

Kent-based winemaker Chapel Down raised £ million from 1,400 people in just three weeks last year using crowdfunding.

The firm used the money to plant more vines, build a new winery plus brewery, and set up better distribution and storage. In exchange, savers get discounts on wine, a tour of the vineyard and other exclusive offers —ut no cash return.

The independent beer producer BrewDog has just raised £ million through crowdfunding, having previously attracted £ million in the same way.

Savers get discounts on beer and the promise of a cash profit if the firm is ever listed on the Stock xchange.

Emanuela Vartolomei, chief executive at All Street, which analyses these types of investment, says: 'BrewDog has performed exceptionally well —014 was the company's sixth consecutive year of growth, with annual turnover climbing 64 pc to £9.6 million. But it is difficult to see how investors will make a financial return on this deal because the company seems to be over-valued.'

Crowdfunding is expected to be worth £0 billion by 2020.

Projects are typically listed on websites such as Crowd Cube or Kickstarter, which act as middlemen between a company and investors. You invest by signing up and pledging money to the project you like.

The middleman takes a cut of the cash the company raises. The main risk for  avers is the incredibly high rate of failure among start-ups —alf of all new companies go bust in their first five years, and if this happens your money is gone.

On top of this you're locked in for the agreed term, so it's hard luck if you decide you want your money back early.

Recent retail bonds include those offered by supermarket giant Tesco, Severn Trent Water and HSBC bank.

When English rugby club Wasps wanted to expand, it raised an impressive £5 million when it issued a retail bond promising to  ay interest of 6.5 pc for seven ears.

That sounds enormously enticing, particularly as rates on five-year fixed-rate bonds pay only 3 pc before tax. But it is also attractive to Wasp fans who want the club to do well.

You buy retail bonds through a stockbroker, such as Canaccord Genuity or Killik & Co, before they list on the stock market or when they are trading (and in this case, you may pay more or less than they are worth, depending on their popularity).

Neither type of investment is protected by the Financial Services Compensation Scheme.

Julia Groves, chair of trade body UK Crowdfunding Association, says: 'It's natural that in the future there is not going to be that divide between the company and the customer —t makes sense that if you use a product, you will want to own a share of it, too, and it makes sure everyone's interests are aligned.'

But it is risky and not for everyone. City regulator the Financial Conduct Authority has ruled that ordinary savers should not be allowed to put more than 5 pc of their money into crowdfunding.

Ms Vartolomei says: 'Anyone looking to put money into a crowdfunding project should make sure that they've done their  esearch. Many of the opportunities may offer a great return, but a high return usually comes with a high risk.'

h.black@dailymail.co.uk

©aily Mail

headline: Heavyweights turn to craft beer as microbrewers taste success
author: Robbie Hurley
text:
Their beers are being sidelined for bottles of beautifully branded craft ales, produced by artisan brewers, but Scotland's big breweries are fighting back. Tennent Caledonian, which is famed for its lager and bitter, and Caledonian Brewery are creating new drinks to entice pub customers back to their products. Scotland's craft brewing scene has been booming for more than a decade, led by firms such as BrewDog and Innis & Gunn. Beer fans reacted positively to ales and lagers produced on a small scale, using full-flavoured American hops in an age when pubs were closing and UK consumption was falling.

Scotland's older brewers are trying not to cede any more territory at the bar. Caledonian, which is based in Edinburgh, is opening a microbrewery —alled "Wee George" after George Lorimer, one of the company's founders in 1869 —o produce a range of experimental beers, while Tennent's is promoting a range of lager-based cocktails aimed at a younger audience.

Andy Mitchell, the trade marketing manager at Caledonian, said the microbrewery was a chance to embrace the changing market. He said: "This launch is a chance to speed up our innovation. Wee George allows us to put our thinking hats on and take the shackles off. "It allows us to remain fleet of foot in the market and help Caledonian to enter the world of modern craft. Our team is certainly excited about being able to indulge in their passion for crafting top quality and original brews. It's time to get a little bit creative."

Tennent's, best known for its traditional pale lager, introduced its line of beer-inspired cocktails at the Tennent's Arms Bar at T in the Park earlier this month. Alongside its established beer, drinkers sampled "lagerita" and "piñ colager made with the usual ingredients and topped with lager.

A spokesman said: "Any publicans who fancy recreating them in their own bars are more than welcome and we're sure they'll prove as popular as they did at T in the Park.

"While the craft market is in growth demand pales in comparison when set against more traditional beer offerings."

Scotland now has only three main brewers left, Caledonian, Tennent's and Belhaven, near Dunbar, and they still command the biggest market share. However, there are at present between 85 and 100 craft breweries in Scotland.

One of the first, Innis & Gunn, was established in Edinburgh in 2003. With a turnover of £1.8 million last year, it has released plans to open bars and restaurants. Its new venture, the Beer Kitchen, will open in Edinburgh next week in a part of the city becoming populated with craft-ale bars.

Demand has been so great that the company is planning to open its own £ million brewery near Edinburgh.

BrewDog was opened in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, in 2007. From selling cases of beer from the back of a van, to recording sales of £8.9 million last year, the company has proved the appetite for Scottish craft beer remains strong. It has bars in Brazil, Sweden and Japan, as well as four in Scotland.

A spokesman for the Campaign for Real Ale said it welcomed renewed interest in the beer market but questioned the authenticity of large companies launching new ranges.

He said: "We would hope any breweries launching new ranges or products to take advantage of the current interest in craft beers do not damage this resurgence by simply piggy-backing on a marketing term, rather than setting out to produce a quality beer which will interest and excite drinkers."

Jo Stewart, the co-founder of Stewart Brewing, said: "As the market for craft brewing booms, some of the big boys are starting to feel a little threatened. In terms of the public reaction to these drinks, there's quite a lot of beer snobbery out there —here are going to be people who aren't going to want to put these drinks in their fridge."

Brigid Simmonds, of the British Beer & Pub Association, said: "Small scale producers have outperformed the market in recent years, so it is natural that established Scottish brewers, such as Caledonian, are responding, often by installing microbreweries on their own sites. This enables them to combine decades of expertise in brewing with the ability to meet the demand for authentic niche products, as well as their famous brands."

headline: Independents lift jaded palates
author: Allan MacLean
text:
Back in 1900 there were about 200 breweries in Scotland. Beer is produced today at only three: Belhaven on the edge of Dunbar, Caledonian Brewery in Edinburgh and Tennent's, of Wellpark, Glasgow.

About 100 new, smaller Scottish breweries are busily producing refreshment, many starting in recent years. Some are likely to be the giants of tomorrow, a prospect that may have encouraged the giants of today to look to their laurels.

The independents are producing some extraordinarily popular beers, with demand fuelled by their distinctive taste that appeal to Jaded palates. BrewDog, with eye-catching marketing techniques, has expanded to become a notable international operator. Innis & Gunn led the way in oak-aged beer and so successful has it been it now plans a new brewery of its own.

That's not to say that the older breweries are not still dominating the market. Caledonian, established in 1869 at Slateford Road in Edinburgh, was saved from closure in the 1980s and now belongs to Heineken. Its popular brews include Edinburgh Castle, an 80-shilling ale in an old style, and Deuchars, an intriguing revival of an old name. Belhaven still brews on the site it has occupied since 1719, although monks brewed there long before then. Belhaven is now part of Greene King, from Suffolk, but still points proudly to its Scottish roots.

Over in eastern Glasgow, the mighty Tennent's also has a long history. Its modern brewery dates from the years since 1963 but it is on an older site, a fact exploited to commendable effect by its owners, Cantrell & Cochrane. Heritage sells. As well as the famed Tennent's lager, other brews from Wellpark add interest.

Several modern breweries are thriving as producers of traditional ales, including Harviestoun, Inveralmond and Stewarts among many others. Scotland's beer scene is lively once again —nd taste rules.

headline: TOP DOG FOR PAY
text:
GOVERNMENT plans for a Living Wage from next year have caused controversy, but firms already offering the pay deal are benefitting. Scots brewery BrewDog has been a Living Wage employer since October 2014. It pays more than the £.20 an hour set by the Government and does not limit the deal to workers aged 25 and over. James Watt, BrewDog co-founder, below, says: "We pay our London staff £.15, and our team outside the capital £.85, in line with the Living Wage Foundation's calculations. "This is the figure we see as valuable to employees, and the Government's proposal is more of an increased minimum wage rather than a realistic living wage.

Also, unlike the Government's recommendation - which is only applicable to those aged 25 and above - we do not discriminate on age. "Anyone who works for us receives the same wage, whether they are 18 or 25 or older.

We reward passion and drive, and that can come at any age.

We are proud to be a Living Wage business and will continue to support our team and help them thrive, both professionally and financially." BrewDog has 48 vacancies including bar managers, sales managers, packaging technicians and brewers. Apply at brewdog.com/about/ work-for-brewdog/vacancies.

headline: Brew a bit too Bovril-y for my taste
text:
HERE'S The Scottish Sun's very own intrepid newshound GRAEME DONOHOE'S verdict on the beagle brew ... 6I'm pretty partial to a pint of Brewdog but I thought my boss was barking when he ordered me to try this beer for dogs.

It's fair to say that Snuffle did not hit the spot with this rover-ing reporter in quite the same way as the canine customers it's actually intended for. Never one to knock back the chance of a swally in the boozer I took a big gulp of the tailwagger's tipple.

Yuck! I thought I was going to be sick as a dog as the meaty concoction hit my tastebuds.

It tasted like cold chicken bovril. Snuffle might be enjoying a lot of puppy love but it wasn't for me.

To be honest, I thought it was howling.7

headline: Sour grapes? Sommeliers shunned as foodies opt for ideal beer instead
author: Gabriella Bennett
text:
That velvety burgundy or crisp sauvignon blanc no longer has to be your drink of choice as you contemplate your food. Diners are now being encouraged to pair a hoppy number with their meal instead.

Scottish craft beer is gaining in popularity, with gourmands on the hunt for new tastes —ot only to enjoy on their own but also to match with dishes.

Drygate, Glasgow's newest craft brewery, is holding pairing nights combining its beers with food from its inhouse restaurant, The Vintage. In Edinburgh, Mark Greenaway is poised to introduce set beer-paired tasting menus in his eponymous restaurant from next month. The chef Hardeep Singh Kohli, the broadcaster turned chef, is already running a restaurant that matches beers and curry. The trend has even forced industry bodies to create training courses. The Beer Academy has produced 89 beer sommeliers —alled cicerones —ince its creation in 2011. Three Scottish candidates have graduated.

Bars north of the border are also training staff under the American Cicerone Certification Programme, the beer equivalent to sommelier training.

BrewDog, the Fraserburgh brewery that now has bars and off-licences across Scotland, requires all its staff to be trained to at least level one of cicerone training.

Melissa Cole, who runs cicerone classes at Craft Beer Rising, Scotland's artisan beer festival, said: "Beer works very naturally with food —t isn't as strong in alcohol as wine or over-dominating. The range of beers available to be paired with food is large, with sweetness to fruitiness to grain-like characters and everything else in between. It's also more flexible —ou can open one small bottle compared with a full bottle of wine. There's so much creativity and flavour with beers these days, in particular with some of the Scottish breweries who have been pioneers in the scene."

She added: "The minute you put beer with food, it immediately brings people in who didn't think they liked beer.

That's the first big door to push open, the perception that all beer is just a mass-produced, tasteless product.

"In actual fact, these beers are artisan, refined products. They have strong provenance and thanks to the flavourwheel for beer being significantly larger than wine, they can be paired with an awful lot more."

The complex flavour profile of beer allows flexibility in matching it to food —here are fewer restrictions than with wine. Curry dishes are commonly teamed up with wheat beers to cut through spice, while a hoppy IPA is the perfect foil for creamy dishes.

Mr Greenaway said: "We still do the traditional tasting menu paired with wine but we see more and more customers saying 'my wife's having the wine —an I match mine with beer?' or 'My husband's quite happy with the wine but can you match a couple of my courses with a few different beers?'" However, Guy Chatfield, a wine writer, said: "When a wine is paired well with food it creates a balance, a level of harmony. Beer has many excellent flavours but the structural attributes of fruit, acidity, tannins and dryness in wine brings out the corresponding flavours and textures in food that I don't think beer can."

headline: Thinking out of the box pays off for author
author: Jack Malvern
text:
A colouring book for adults has become an international sensation after selling three million copies in China in two months.

Johanna Basford's Secret Garden, which features intricate line drawings of flowers and owls, has sold 6.8 million copies worldwide, including 478,000 copies in Britain. Sales have also soared in Brazil (1.2 million), America (650,000) and France (313,000). In South Korea, sales of half a million mean that 1 per cent of the population owns a copy.

Basford, 31, from Ellon, Aberdeenshire, had been asked by a publisher to do a colouring book for children but suggested that parents might enjoy the activity. She was inspired by holidays on the Isle of Arran, where her grandfather was head gardener at Brodick Castle.

She said yesterday that the idea that her book would be a hit had dawned on her slowly since Secret Garden was published in 2012. "When I heard we were approaching the one million mark I realised this crazy little idea, colouring books for grown-ups, might just have caught on."

The artist, who is married to James Watt, owner of the craft beer company BrewDog, said that she had high hopes for Enchanted Forest, her second book, which has already sold two million copies worldwide, and Lost Ocean, scheduled for publication in October.

headline: Picks for getting crafty in campsite
text:
WELLIES, check. Rain jacket, check. Sun cream, I can dream, can't I?. Beer... mmm, this one has me stumped almost every year Electric Picnic rolls around.

The craft selection is maybe twice what it was last year, but unfortunately due to the majority of breweries limited to glass-bottle production, a lot of that choice is wiped out due to EP's sensible 'no glass' policy (necessary due to some people's ignorance).

So where does that leave us? Well, to be fair to Heineken, the selection inside the arena has improved massively over the past two years with bars around the festival now stocking some of it's more 'worldly' brands. l Paulaner —erman wheat beer, a decent choice for a sunny laidback Sunday afternoon l Birra Moretti —talian lager, a good choice for those who like a slightly more malty substitute to Heineken l Murphy's Stout and Red are, well, a stout and a red ale.

It's the options to bring in to the campsite that I'm interested in and I've come up with some canned craft brews that are sure to get your day under way in the best possible taste.

l Black's KPA, 5 per cent, 330ml —his session ale from the Kinsale brewer is well balanced with Cascade and citra hops giving a hint of citrus and tropical fruit. l Metalman's Equinox, 4.6 per cent, 330ml —rand new to the market and a great follow-up to the delicious Pale Ale. This wheat lager brewed with orange and lemon peel has a light citrus body and a dry finish. l Brewdog's Dead Pony Club, 3.8 per cent, 330ml — gift from the Scottish. This may be the lightest in terms of alcohol but it's packed to the brim with American hoppy goodness, a prefect all-day drinking beer. l Grolsch, 5 per cent, 500ml —kay, it's not craft, but sometimes you can't spend all your money on beer for the camping area.

So if you're after a solid pilsner with hoppy bitterness and clean finish look no further, Tesco are currently selling 12 cans for €5.

headline: Co-op Bank shares to be traded in online auction
author: Judith Evans in London
text:
Shareholders in the Co-operative Bank will be able to trade their holdings in an online auction this month, offering a visible market in the shares for the first time since the bank’ near-collapse in 2013.

Asset Match, an online platform for shares in unlisted companies, will hold a seven-day auction from September 10. This will determine the price at which Co-operative Bank shares will be traded, including those held by hedge funds which had their bonds converted into equity as part of the bank’ rescue.

“he idea originally came from shareholders who contacted us,”aid Ian Baillie, co-chief executive of Asset Match, adding that owners of between £,000 and £50,000 in bank shares had been in touch.

Shareholdings in the bank date from 2013 after the lender was rescued in a recapitalisation led by hedge funds, cutting the stake owned by the Co-operative Group from 100 per cent to 30 per cent. As part of that deal, tranches of junior bondholders accepted conversions to shares.

Direct retail bondholders largely escaped that conversion after a campaign to keep them in income-generating investments. But hedge funds were not the only creditors to undergo a switch to shares, with some 80 per cent of the bank’ current share capital held by nominee accounts, including wealth managers and direct-to-consumer investment platforms, according to Asset Match.

Institutions have traded Co-op Bank Shares privately through brokers, leading to a last indicative price of 200p, implying a market capitalisation of about £00m. But Mr Baillie said his company believes there is “ large number of investors who don’ have access to that market” with more than 450m shares outstanding.

Under Asset Match’ system, would-be buyers and sellers register limits on the prices at which they are prepared to buy and sell. This leads to an indicative sale price that is visible throughout the auction, enabling traders to adjust their bids and offers if they wish.

At the conclusion of the auction, an algorithm determines the final price and number of shares to be transacted. Buyers with bids at or higher than the final price, and sellers with offers lower than or equal to that price, are able to complete their trades.

The Co-operative Bank said it would not comment on trading of its shares. It has considered the idea of listing on a stock market, but this has not so far materialised. On August 20 it reported a pre-tax loss of £04.2m for the first half, nearly triple the loss in the same period a year earlier.

Asset Match’ system has been used for private company investments from the 95-year-old Isles of Scilly Steamship Company to BrewDog, the “unk”eer maker that has crowdfunded at least £0m of investment from the public. BrewDog holds annual share auctions via Asset Match.

headline: Concern over mini-bonds’ove to the mainstream
author: David Stevenson
text:
Over the past year or so the world of alternative finance has thrust itself into the mainstream of investment. Barely a week goes by without some new crowdfunding campaign or peer-to-peer loan launch.

Some are sensible, some less so. But Zopa, the sector pioneer, has built a very solid franchise that increasingly smells like a mainstream financial services business online. Its deadly rival Ratesetter has also branched out into new forms of lending.

At the other end of the alternative finance spectrum we have the riskier crowdfunding campaigns, some of which are offering equity investments at ludicrous valuations, and even more ludicrous projections for future growth. Yet I have no doubt that lurking on the platform of players such as Crowdcube, Seedrs and Syndicate Room, there will be more than a handful of great new businesses that will make the leap on to the public markets (not least the platforms themselves).

But some innovations leave me slightly worried. Mini-bonds are one of them. Now, I’ a great fan of retail friendly bonds. The Order book for Retail Bonds (Orb) at the London Stock Exchange has been a very fruitful marketplace for bond investors and I’ very happy with my own investments in bonds issued by Tesco Personal Finance, EnQuest, Premier Oil and Provident Financial bonds.

As the yield on many of the best issues has fallen to below 5 per cent, many investors have gone in search of yield higher up the risk spectrum, chasing all sorts of new issuers, many of them using alternative finance platforms.

Let’ compare two recent issues —woca’ bond placing on the UK Bond Network, and Scottish company BrewDog’ immensely popular listing on Crowdcube.

UK Bond Network tends to focus on structuring issues that look and feel a bit more like traditional bond structures. There’ a focus on more established businesses, with asset backed security, and higher yields for the extra risk being taken. The Iwoca issue, raising £m, offered sophisticated private investors a return of 9 per cent for one year, with yield payments secured on Iwoca’ constant stream of short term loans to small and medium-sized businesses.

Crowdcube has also done a brilliant job in bringing bonds into the UK market through its own mini-bonds platform, giving investors a fairly constant stream of issues that pay a yield of between 6 per cent and 9 per cent depending on the nature of the business.

BrewDog is an established business. Using its own fundraising platform it has raised countless millions via its “quity for Punks”rogramme —he 2015 issue was the fourth iteration of equity fundraising. But having raised £4m via this equity route —ot uncontroversially it must be said, given some of the fine detail in its fundraising documents —he Scottish brewer is now looking to raise money for a “BrewDog Bond” This is a mini-bonds and will hopefully raise up to £m, and pay out a 6.5 per cent fixed rate per annum.

In many respects there’ much to like about this issue. Bonds are more secure than equities (usually), and the fact that Crowdcube has agreed to market the BrewDog bond should give investors some comfort. Crowdcube has even tied up with debt rating firm Moody’ Analytics to attach a “robability of Default”POD) score to mini-bonds, judging the issuer’ likelihood of default on any existing credit obligations (not including the mini-bond). At the launch of the BrewDog programme, the average POD for Crowdcube mini-bonds was 3.3 per cent. Moody’ has awarded the BrewDog Bond a 0.3 per cent POD.

Like virtually all mini-bonds this issue is unsecured and non-transferable. Interest repayments will be made biannually. The mini-bond will follow the standard bullet repayment schedule, with principal returned to investors at maturity. The term appears to be four years and the minimum investment is £00.

For me, investors need to ask two questions. The first is whether they will get their money back. The second is whether they are adequately rewarded for the risk. With the first I’ say that both Iwoca and BrewDog probably pass the test, in as much as one can say that about any business that is less than ten years old. BrewDog in particular has been growing like gangbusters —n average of 138 per cent a year since 2007 —nd is a well-known brand. It’ also raised a huge amount of equity, which will need to be burnt through before the bonds are threatened. All those rounds of crowdfunding have made this much lower risk.

But I’ not sure I am being adequately rewarded for the risk. 6.5 per cent for four years strikes me as parsimonious. Iwoca’ 9 per cent for one year is a much better bet. A lot can happen in four years, including interest rate rises, global recessions, regulatory changes and the chance of good old-fashioned business failure.

If we the investors were on the receiving end of rates of more than 10 per cent a year for lending to early-stage businesses over multiple years, I’ be happy to lend a small part of my portfolio. Frankly, yields of 6 to 8 per cent can be had from investing in stock market funds such as P2P Global Investments and SQN Asset Finance which lend to hundreds of small businesses. Unless issuers up the rates I’ be very cautious about committing your capital to many of these mini-bonds.

David Stevenson is an active private investor writing about his own investments. He may own shares in the companies mentioned. adventurous@ft.com

headline: Rating agency seeks to cut out noise around crowdfunding
author: Judith Evans
text:
The fast-expanding equity crowdfunding sector is aiming to add a professional sheen to its investor offering with a new rating agency that will assess every company raising money on the three biggest UK platforms.

The launch of Crowdrating comes as data show that companies have raised £46m through equity crowdfunding in the year so far, an increase of more than 50 per cent over the £1m raised in 2014, according to data provider Crowdsurfer.

Crowdrating will produce free short reports on every company listed on Crowdcube, the market leader by fundraising volumes, and Seedrs and SyndicateRoom, two of its biggest competitors. Start-ups and other unlisted companies sell stakes to retail investors on these platforms, sometimes for as little as £0.

Its launch comes after industry figures and the Financial Conduct Authority, the City regulator, raised concerns about the risks involved in the fast-growing asset class, which has attracted thousands of inexperienced investors.

The FCA has warned investors that “t is very likely you will lose all your money”n crowdfunding, since most start-ups fail, while there are also concerns over high valuations and deals structured to favour institutional investors over individuals. Until this year, there has been no independent research available on the sector.

Modwenna Rees-Mogg, co-founder of Crowdrating and a former corporate financier, said: “ndependent comment is a real challenge in small-business equity, and particularly acute in the world of ‘ngel’nvestors.”

Many crowdfunding investors have had to rely on diversification in the hope of making a return, she says, but should at least be able to “ilter out”he least promising deals.

Since Crowdcube launched in 2011, equity crowdfunding has attracted at least 200,000 individual investors and helped to launch companies such as JustPark, a parking app, and to expand consumer brands including several breweries and restaurant chains.

Unlike websites such as Kickstarter, where money is handed to companies as donations or in exchange for small rewards, equity crowdfunding sites offer the chance to make a financial investment —lthough few companies have so far offered investors successful “xits”rom their holdings.

About half of all the start-ups seeking venture capital funding in the UK have either raised money through crowdfunding or plan to do so, according to Tim Hames, director-general of the British Venture Capital Association, in a sign of the sector’ move into the mainstream.

“quity crowdfunding is here to stay,”aid Alex Heath, co-founder of Crowdrating and former chief executive of Interactive Investor, a DIY investment platform for funds and listed shares. “t is part of the funding mix now, but there will be bumps in the road over the next couple of years.”

Crowdfunding already has one rating agency, All Street, which launched in March and produces in-depth reports on selected equity offers. It issued a sceptical report on the latest fundraising by BrewDog, a craft brewery that values itself at £66m, saying it was “ifficult to see how investors will make a financial return on this deal”

Crowdrating will differ to All Street by producing quick-fire one-page reports based on data responses to 60 questions submitted to a “atings engine” plus analyst comments.

“rowd rating agencies will pressure some platforms to improve their standards,”aid Goncalo de Vasconcelos, founder of SyndicateRoom.

Crowdrating will initially make money by charging start-ups for “ompany health checks”nd ratings reviews. It acknowledges potential conflicts of interest but says it will disclose where it has been paid by a company that it analyses. All Street is funded purely by investors.

Mr Heath says he decided to launch the service when he was looking for investments to make as an “ngel”arly-stage investor and repeatedly found himself “azumped”y the lure of crowdfunding.

“ spent a lot of time working with a new entrepreneur, we agreed terms and then everything went quiet. And then up he popped on a crowdfunding platform with a charismatic video, a valuation three times higher than we agreed, and no business plan. He was over-funded within three days,”r Heath recalled.

Crowdfunding already has one rating agency, All Street, which launched in March and produces in-depth reports on selected equity offers. It issued a sceptical report on the latest fundraising by BrewDog , a craft brewery that values itself at £66m, saying it was "difficult to see how investors will make a financial return on this deal".

Crowdrating will differ from All Street by producing one-page reports based on data responses to 60 questions, plus analyst comments.

"Crowd rating agencies will pressure some platforms to improve their standards," said Goncalo de Vasconcelos, founder of SyndicateRoom.

Crowdrating will initially make money by charging start-ups for "company health checks" and ratings reviews. It acknowledges potential conflicts of interest but says it will disclose where it has been paid by a company that it analyses. All Street is funded purely by investors.

Mr Heath says he decided to launch the service when he was looking for investments to make as an "angel" and repeatedly found himself "gazumped" by the lure of crowdfunding.

"I spent a lot of time working with a new entrepreneur, we agreed terms and then everything went quiet. And then up he popped on a crowdfunding platform with a charismatic video, a valuation three times higher than we agreed, and no business plan. He was over-funded within three days," Mr Heath recalled.

headline: Brittelstand stymied by lack of growth and skills
author: Brian Groom
text:
John Cridland once described medium-sized companies as the UK’ “orgotten army” Four years on, the CBI director-general is blunt about how much further there is to go to build a British version of Germany’ Mittelstand —ts famed cadre of exporting powerhouses.

“hey are not quite as forgotten as they used to be, but they are still starved of ammunition, pay and rations,”ays Mr Cridland, who steps down as head of the employers’rganisation in November. “ think there is a latent capability in the British Mittelstand that has not yet been realised.”

The CBI defines the sector —he “rittelstand”—s companies with between £0m and £00m annual turnover. Largely private or family-owned, these businesses represent just 1.8 per cent of companies, but generate nearly a quarter of private-sector revenue and make up 16 per cent of total employment. The CBI believes they could add £0bn to annual economic output by 2020 if their potential were realised.

Fast-growing companies in the £0m-£00m range include BrewDog, the craft brewer, Pure Gym, the gym operator and Notonthehighstreet.com, the online retailer.

Some analysts define the sector more widely as companies with turnover up to £00m, which brings in the likes of Aston Martin, the carmaker, the Pret A Manger sandwich chain and Edinburgh Woollen Mill, the clothing retailer.

Millward Brown, the research agency, says 51 per cent of 200 midsized companies surveyed grew by 10 per cent a year in the past three years. GE Capital found the average UK mid-market company increased revenue by 3.9 per cent last year, almost matching Germany’ 4 per cent, but there were indications of slower growth in the coming year.

While mid-market companies can prove resilient in a downturn, they tend to have a conservative attitude to risk, in some cases, a small appetite for international expansion and face difficulties in finding the right external funding.

The government is trying to help the sector, which often complains of being ignored. George Osborne, the chancellor, has abolished stamp duty on buying shares traded on AIM, the alternative investment market. He is also introducing an exemption from withholding tax for interest on private placements.

This year the government launched Help to Grow, a £00m pilot for the British Business Bank to supply loans to companies needing between £00,000 and £m to achieve their potential. The banks’£.5bn Business Growth Fund, set up in 2011, makes long-term equity investments of £m to £0m.

The CBI believes long-term finance is still in short supply and is urging the Treasury to create a Long Term Lending Trust, which would offer tax incentives to savers investing for at least five years. These trusts would offer returns based on yield, instead of capital gain.

Mr Cridland says too many mid-market businesses are “rowth sleepers”hich “o not have the appetite to grow, because they are not talking to the people who can enthuse them and help them with the consequences of growth”

They face skills shortages in areas such as professional management and project leadership. Mr Cridland believes organisations such as universities could do more to work with them.

Ian Stuart, HSBC’ head of UK commercial banking, says growing companies often face a “repidation factor” They reach a certain size and the next step may involve significant investment, expanding into new markets or bringing in management. “ometimes you have just got to put your foot on the ice and go for it,”r Stuart says.

But, he adds: “ think the foundations are there for these companies to really push on and have a good few years.”

Trying to match Germany’ performance does not mean copying its model. The Mittelstand has historical roots that make it hard to imitate. Companies are based in strong regional clusters and supported by a banking system, and public policies, attuned to their needs. The UK has different strengths including Californian-style “azelles” or fast-growth companies, that create a large proportion of new jobs.

“t’ very difficult to transfer culture,”ays Bob Bischof, chairman of the German-British Forum. “n the UK we have a shareholder value model but in Germany the company is there for the stakeholders, including employees.”

He says UK midsized companies lack support in areas such as export finance, while a dislike of formal structures stops the UK matching Germany’ strength in training and apprenticeships.

Mr Bischof adds: “t would probably be better to try to go by British strengths and use entrepreneurs and support them better with tax write-offs and other stuff, try to make them take a long-term view and support them with better facilities, particularly in exports, rather than trying to copy something built up over generations.”

headline: Equity crowdfunding market no longer the preserve of start-ups
author: Brian Groom
text:
For enterprises seeking seed capital or money to expand, intriguing possibilities are on offer by the fast-growing UK equity crowdfunding market, which enables individual investors to buy stakes in companies online —ometimes putting in as little as £0.

Equity crowdfunding websites such as Crowdcube , Seedrs and SyndicateRoom, enable businesses to pitch to communities of backers. They offer companies an innovative route to raising capital, often from enthusiastic customers, and open new investment areas to the public.

Questions have been raised, though, about whether inflated valuations are leaving investors at risk of failing to make a return in proportion to risks and whether in some cases they have adequate protection against dangers, such as heavy dilution of their shareholdings.

The Financial Conduct Authority , which regulates the sector, has warned that crowdfunding is risky for investors, with the message: “ou are very likely to lose all your money,”ecause most start-ups fail.

The sector, barely five years old, is small but expanding. Last year it raised £4m, up from £.9m in 2012, according to Nesta, an innovation charity .

That was out of a total UK alternative finance market estimated at £.74bn. The UK is ahead of the US, which is still developing rules for retail equity crowdfunding.

“quity crowdfunding is quite capable of raising £bn a year, maybe more,”ays Stephen Hazell-Smith, who co-founded Aim and is chairman of Business Agent, a crowdfunding aggregator.

But, he adds: “eople will be eagle-eyed looking for failures.”

There are more than 30 platforms. Nesta found the average amount raised was almost £00,000, with 125 investors putting in £,599 each.

Augmentum Capital, a venture capital fund backed by Lord Rothschild and Neil Woodford , the fund manager, recently led a £0m investment round into Seedrs , while corporate brokers Numis bought 8.49 per cent of Crowdcube as part of a £m funding round.

Equity crowdfunding is also attracting established companies. Sugru, a company that makes a mouldable putty, raised more than £.5m via Crowdcube , the largest platform, in July. ISDX-listed Chapel Down winery, a UK wine producer, raised nearly £m via Seedrs last year. Crowdcube plans to offer retail investors the chance to invest in London stock market floats.

“t’ about having options and being able to get the money quickly,”ays Luke Lang , Crowdcube ’ co-founder. “t can be quicker than protracted conversations with venture capitalists and angel investors.”

Companies see a benefit in having customers as shareholders, who can turn into “rand ambassadors”or their products and services. Investors often support businesses they identify with, whether it be craft beer, solar energy or computer games. It is less clear how well that might work for, say, a machine tool manufacturer.

Crowdfunding can help to fill the equity gap for start-ups but it is not necessarily easy. Crowdcube rejects 85 per cent of businesses that apply, for reasons such as being unable to substantiate claims in a pitch. Nearly half of pitches on the platform fail to reach their targets, meaning fundraising fails.

“hey have to work at it,”ays Tom Davies, chief investment officer at Seedrs , where six out of 10 fail to reach their target. “hey have to do a lot of marketing around the crowdfunding campaign.”

Platforms have various models: on Crowdcube , investors hold shares in companies directly. Seedrs acts as a nominee shareholder on behalf of investors, managing the investment for them. On SyndicateRoom, a “usiness angel”cts as lead investor and negotiates terms before funding is opened on the same terms to the crowd.

On Crowdcube , all but the largest investors receive “lass B”hares, with no voting rights or contractual protections against dilution, such as “re-emption rights” whereby existing investors must be offered shares before they are made available to anyone else. Crowdcube says the 2006 Companies Act gives adequate protection from unlawful dilution.

Some companies have been criticised over valuations, notably craft brewers BrewDog and Camden Town Brewery. Valuations matter, because even if investors have diversified portfolios they need to earn good returns from the few that do well.

But Stian Westlake, executive director of research at Nesta, says: “t isn’ clear to me that there is something inherent about equity crowdfunding that is more likely to generate unrealistic valuations.”

It may take at least five years before rates of return can be properly gauged as the initial cohort of businesses is sold or floated. “t is likely that the bad news will come before the good news,”r Westlake says. “y then we hope there will have been some success stories, even if these are anecdotal.”

headline: FT REPORT - THE UK'S ENTREPRENEURS Equity crowdfunding market no longer the preserve of start-ups; Expansion

headline: LIFESTORE Hazel Davis, James Hurley
text:
working lunch 1884 Dock Street Kitchen Humber Dock Street, Marina, Hull Expect Hull marina is a spectacular spot and this place steps up to its surroundings.

Food is locally produced and varied What to order At two courses for £4 and three for £8, you can't go too far wrong, but try the chilled cucumber and mint soup to start, followed by a fillet of Charles Ashbridge Gloucester Old Spot Pig pancetta, braised gem, anchovy, caesar dressing and ryecrisp, and (at £.50) some beefdripping chips on the side. For pudding, opt for Keyingham Strawberry Mess book of the week Business for Punks James Watt Brewdog is the country's fastest-growing beer business and here James Watt, its co-founder, seeks to explain how a company that started in a shed in Scotland became an operation with an annual turnover of £0 million and sales in 55 countries. His tone won't be to everyone's taste, being an odd mixture of down-with-the-kids irreverence and business jargon, and the book is milked for every opportunity to promote the Brewdog brand, but there are some useful tips for a fledgeling entrepreneur with a bright idea but little experience.

headline: World's strongest brew breaks industry code, says charity
text:
Scotland's national anti-alcohol charity has hit out at a brewery in Aberdeenshire which has launched the world's strongest canned beer.

BrewDog released Black Eyed King Imp imperial stout yesterday, which has 12.7 per cent alcohol by volume (ABV).

It now holds the record for the strongest canned beer in the world.

James Watt, co-founder of BrewDog, which is based in Ellon, said that the launch was about challenging people's perceptions of beer.

Alcohol Focus Scotland said that the stress on alcohol content went against the industry code.

Jennifer Curran, the acting chief executive, said: "A drink, its packaging and any promotional activity should not in any way give the high strength undue emphasis.

"This is why we need an independent body to regulate alcohol promotion."

headline: INVESTORS TOAST THE BREWERS
author: HOLLY BLACK
text:
TWO of the world's leading brewers toasted one of the biggest corporate deals in history this week, leaving investors wondering whether there is any fizz left in the sector.

In a £8bn deal London-based SABMiller agreed to a takeover by Belgian brewer Anheuser-Bush InBev on Tuesday.

SAB had held off, knocking back several offers from its Belgian rival before agreeing to a deal worth £4 a share. Share prices shot up 8pc on the news, before closing 0.3pc up at 4006p, as savers clamoured to take advantage of the valuation.

The price rose from £6.60 to £9.78 over the past week.

SAB produces big names such as Fosters and Peroni, while AB InBev is behind pub favourites such as Stella Artois and Budweiser.

The deal has been dubbed Megabrew. The newly enlarged firm would account for one in every three pints drunk across the globe if it goes ahead, and would take 50p in every £ of profit earned by the beer industry.

But with an increasingly health-conscious population, savers may be wondering whether there are many more pennies to be made from a pint.

Crucially, beer tends to be a younger person's drink, with consumption peaking between aged 18 and 35. Currently the typical westerner drinks a whopping 75 litres of beer every year, but with an ageing population, the beverage could fall flat. Yet in Africa, where 60pc of the population is aged 25 or under, the typical drinker consumes just nine litres a year.

SABMiller has already made great inroads in Africa and AB InBev may be seeking to utilise this presence as the consumption of beer in developing countries continues to rise.

Simon Young, UK Equity manager at Aviva, says: 'AB InBev generates around a third of its profits in North America where mainstream beer brands like Budweiser have seen their volumes decline for the last eight years and this shows no sign of abating.

'Its next biggest profit pool comes from Brazil, which is experiencing a consumer recession, putting pressure on disposable incomes. So the outlook for its own profits looks pretty subdued for the next few years.

'And as a result SAB, with its large exposure to southern Africa and China is a rare beacon of growth.'

He is one of several fund managers who have tapped into this trend. The Lindsell Train UK Equity fund has 21pc of its £.6bn of assets in beverage companies.

It invests in Heineken and Diageo, which produces Guinness.

The Aviva Investors UK Equity fund, almost £bn in size, has 15pc of its cash in the sector.

Both funds have returned 62pc over the past three years.

Savers, too, have been seduced by the growing trend for craft beers in recent years. Small UK brewers such as London-based Camden Town Brewery and Aberdeenshire's Brewdog have raised millions through crowdfunding appeals to expand their brands.

Though these companies are not listed, so savers can't share in their financial gains, investors are offered discounts on the products and invitations to members-only parties in return for their cash.

Beer is not the only drink that may pull a profit for punters.

Nick Train, who runs the Lindsell Train UK Equity fund, has just bought shares in Remy Cointreau, which owns liquor brands including Cointreau, Metaxa and Mount Gay rum.

He says: 'We like beverage brands - off-hand I can't recall over my career ever having made a poor investment into a soft drinks, beer or spirits company.

'Heineken and Remy are both venerable, founded in 1864 and 1724 and both controlled by a family with a majority shareholding.'

Remy offers diverse global exposure. Last year over a third of its profits came from premium cognac sales in China and its brandy business continues to grow in the US.

Simon Young also likes mixer drink company Fever Tree, which produces organic tonic and soda waters. He says: 'Fever Tree has innovated with bold new packaging and strong natural flavours. It has stolen a march on Schweppes, taking huge volumes.'

©aily Mail

headline: 48,000 jobs up for grabs
text:
LOOKING for work? Our Sunemployment Roadshow is just the job.

We've teamed up with scores of the UK's top employers and job experts to lay on five events across the country next week and help YOU find work. There will be almost 48,000 jobs up for grabs.

Firms including John Lewis, Jaguar Land Rover, Ocado, GLAXOSMITHKLINE, Tesco and Thomas Cook are waiting to hire you.

Many companies, such as Travelodge, Premier Inn, KFC and Costa Coffee, are even offering interviews on the spot —o you could walk away with a job.

Entry to the events is FREE and there is expert help on hand to help you secure a dream job.

Panel debates will feature the likes of Ann Summers CEO Jacqueline Gold, former Apprentice winner Michelle Dewberry and Dragons' Den tycoon Sarah Willingham.

Sarah says: "The Sunemployment Roadshows are a great opportunity for jobseekers. People need to take this chance to speak to the experts and meet potential employers. This could be the beginning of a whole new life for you."

If you need help with your CV, are considering an apprenticeship or want to know which employers are hiring in your area, we will give you free advice.

Representatives of the National Careers Service, National Apprenticeship Service, JobcentrePlus and Skills Development Scotland will all be on hand and ready to listen.

You can even put questions direct to Minister for Employment Priti Patel, Skills Minister Nick Boles and Shadow Work and Pensions Minister Emily Thornberry.

Career ladder Labour MP Emily said: "I'm so pleased to take part in the Sunemployment Roadshow.

"We need more initiatives like this, which focus not on pushing people into just any job that's available, but on helping people find a job that's right for them and will help rebuild our economy."

And you can also speak straight to some of the UK's top company executives.

These include Judith Nelson, HR boss at Tesco, which is looking for 2,000 new staff; Jim Slater, retail chief at Costa Coffee, which is seeking 1,500 employees; Ed Evans, recruitment head at KFC which wants 5,000 new starters; and Jon Hendry Pickup, chief operating officer at Travelodge with 550 jobs on offer.

Judith Nelson, UK and ROI People Director at Tesco, said: "Over the years we've found some great new colleagues thanks to the Sunemployment Roadshow and I'm hoping we will again this year.

"I'm a great believer in helping people get a foot on the career ladder —o matter their age or experience."

Jon Hendry Pickup added: "As the UK's biggest independent hotel chain, we're delighted to support the Roadshow. Joining a hotel company opens the door to training, career progression and even roles in new countries."

Jim Slater agreed: "I love the fact I will meet our new generation of talent face to face, at the start of their careers."

Thousands have already found work at our jobs events - so get on the road with the Sunemployment Roadshow.

headline: CITY PEOPLE
text:
The feuds, the faces and the farcical Dominic Walsh @walshdominic

Business big shot name wayne sheppard age 55 position chief executive of ibstock Not satisified with being the chief executive of Britain's largest brickmaker and as such on the cusp of a production boom the industry has not seen in at least in a generation, Wayne Sheppard today goes to work officially £6 million richer.

Just ten months after Ibstock was sold by its former owner CRH, the Irish building materials group, for £14 million, the Midlands brickmaker was yesterday floated on the stock market for £70 million by Bain Capital. As rapid-fire private equity flips go, the transactions rate as among the fastest and more profitable in history. For the management, who left CRH to go on the Ibstock adventure, the sale and float has represented a nice little earner too.

Mr Sheppard, who has been in the building game for nearly 20 years after initially training as an engineer out of Brunel University, cashed in one-seventh of his shares to bag £.6 million. He kept stock in the business worth more than £2 million. His finance director Kevin Sims sold shares worth £.3 million and retains a £4.5 million holding.

The £70 million float was lower than advisers had first hoped, however. Bain Capital is retaining a holding of about 53 per cent. The shares priced at 190p closed the day at 196p.

A humble farewell? never the thain shall meet Modesty was never John Thain's thing. Nor, indeed, was selfawareness. Announcing his retirement as chief executive of the financial group CIT this week, Mr Thain noted that he enjoyed "fixing things that someone else broke". Given his record as chief executive of Merrill Lynch, that's quite some statement.

At Merrill, he initially drew acclaim for cutting costs and raising capital but was forced to resign after it was taken over by Bank of America amid heavy losses during the financial crisis. It emerged that he had authorised huge bonuses to Merrill employees (even as the bank continued to lose money) and had spent $1.2 million redecorating his office and equipping it with a $35,000 antique commode.

in need of a time machine An email arrives from a certain investment trade body that I don't propose giving any publicity to. "Back To The Future: what investment trusts should Marty have bought in 1985." BTTF day was on Wednesday, as anyone sentient and in possession of any sort media outlet at the time will have been aware. You are a day out of date to jump on that particular overloaded bandwagon. Back into the gull-winged DeLorean, then…ixing it with the chinese The Chinese will soon be able to toast the £0 billion of deals signed with Britain this week with a posh G&T. Fever-Tree, the upmarket tonic and mixers maker, has just returned from a trip to China where it held discussions with several potential importers. The company reckons its tonic will also go well with baijiu, the local grog.

bonfire of the profanities The relentless, occasionally tiresome PR machine behind BrewDog is plugging a book penned by the Scottish brewer's co-founder, James Watt. Business for Punks: Break All the Rules - the BrewDog way will be launched on Guy Fawkes Night at the group's new bar in Soho.

Expect verbal fireworks within its pages such as: "Don't waste time on bullsh*t business plans."

headline: We can help you craft new career
author: JANE HAMILTON
text:
SUPER Sun ROADSHOW ROLLS INTO GLASGOW

TODAY sees the launch of the Sunemployment Roadshow 2015.

Every day this week we'll be bringing together Sun readers searching for a job with the UK's biggest companies.

There are almost 48,000 vacancies on offer, with some firms even hiring on the spot. From noon today in Glasgow, Apprentice winner Michelle Dewberry will lead a Q&A session with Cabinet Secretary for Fair Work Skills and Training Roseanna Cunningham, Nicolle Sinclair, head of HR at craft beer firm BrewDog, Gordon Smart, editor of The Scottish Sun and Danny Logue from Skills Development Scotland. The National Careers Service, National Apprenticeship Service and Jobcentre Plus are all on hand to offer advice, and there's a chance to win a £00 Tesco gift card.

Here, seven employers tell us why the Roadshow could boost your career.

Find out more at jobs.thesun.co.uk.

JUSTIN MORRISON, talent and development manager at Thomas Cook, said: "Come along to the Sunemployment Roadshow 2015 and you could see your career really take off in some of the best locations in the world."

105 JOBS

60 JOBS

5,000 JOBS

MATT DODD, recruitment and talent advisor at Premier Inn North said: "It's an invaluable opportunity for Premier Inn to meet, interview and potentially recruit new team members from around the country."

100 JOBS

6,400 JOBS

NICOLLE SINCLAIR, BrewDog's head of HR, said: "The Roadshow gives people opportunities and brings talent to local companies. We are hunting kick-ass recruits that will help us in the craft beer revolution."

55 JOBS

headline: You're hired!
author: JANE HAMILTON
text:
employment Sun ROADSHOW IN GLASGOW TODAY WITH 48,000 JOBS UP FOR GRABS YOU MIGHT HEAR..

LOOKING for work? Then look no further.

Our super Sunemployment Roadshow rolls into Glasgow's Grand Central Hotel today —nd Scotland's top bosses are waiting to recruit YOU.

Every day this week we'll bring together Sun readers searching for a job with the UK's biggest companies hiring NOW. There are almost 48,000 vacancies up for grabs with some firms even offering jobs on the spot.

Doors open at 10.30am and at midday, Apprentice winner Michelle Dewberry, far left, will give you the chance to quiz Roseanna Cunningham, Cabinet Secretary for Fair Work, Skills and Training, Nicolle Sinclair, Head of HR at BrewDog, Gordon Smart, Editor of The Scottish Sun, left, plus Danny Logue from Skills Development Scotland. Below, the panel's Roseanna, Danny and Nicolle reveal why our roadshows are so important and some of the employers who'll be at the event today urge readers to come along ...

6There is still work to be done7 By ROSEANNA CUNNINGHAM Cabinet Secretary for Fair Work, Skills & Training 6WE know that the jobs market is improving.

More young people and women are finding jobs, which reflects an increasingly positive picture for the Scottish economy. However, there is still work to be done and my ambitions for Scotland go beyond aiming for a return to pre-recession levels of unemployment. I am very pleased to see The Sun continuing to make an effort to help make the join between employers and their future employees —his is key if we are to get even more people into work. We will keep using every power at our disposal to grow the economy, create more jobs and help people get into and stay in work. As the UK government continues make unfair welfare cuts, we instead will continue to support people towards fairly paid employment.7 6I helped folk last year too7 By DANNY LOGUE Operations Director, Skills Development Scotland 6AT Skills Development Scotland we are working to help people build the skills and confidence needed to help them grasp the best opportunities available.

That is why we welcome the chance to once again take part in The Sunemployment Roadshow. I look forward to bringing my experience to the panel and having the opportunity to help others with their future employment.7 6Join our craft beer revolution7 By NICOLLE SINCLAIR Head of HR at BrewDog 6AT BrewDog, our mission is simple —o make other people as passionate about great craft beer as we are.
