headline: Hire purpose
author: JANE HAMILTON ; NIAMH ANDERSON
text:
SUNEMPLOYMENT ROADSHOW SWARM TO EVENT FOR NEW START HUNDREDS OF SCOTS JOBHUNTERS SWARM TO EVENT FOR NEW START

LUCKY Charley McGlinchey became the first person to land a job at the 2015 Sunemployment Roadshow —ust 20 MINUTES after arriving. The 18-year-old was snapped up by hotel chain Premier Inn to work in the housekeeping team.

Charley, from Castlemilk in Glasgow, had been looking for work for three months.

She said: "I'm so excited, I can't believe I got a job. I was hoping I'd have a bit of luck today. I'm absolutely delighted."

Charley was among hundreds of jobhunters who attended our event at Glasgow's Grand Central Hotel yesterday as 20 top firms fought to hire new recruits. Almost 48,000 roles are up for grabs as our Roadshow tours the UK this week.

Chicken chain KFC plucked 25 readers for jobs with them, while Premier Inn are also trialling 30 more on work placements over the next week.

Among those hired by KFC was mum-of-two Gina Hopes. The 28-year-old, from Knightswood in Glasgow, was looking to get back into work after having children.

She said: "I'm so happy I was successful. I was nervous at first but it was such a relaxing environment so it kind of takes the pressure off. It will be nice to work in a group and to make new friends as well."

Ex-hairdresser Dylan Terrey, 22, from Clydebank, was also hired within an hour of arriving at our jobs show.

Dylan said: "I'm absolutely ecstatic. You need to think on the spot but I'd recommend the Sunemployment Roadshow to anyone. Just come along and just go for it. Don't be afraid to find out about new companies and what they're all about."

Mum-of-one Nicole Lawson, 24, from Paisley, was desperate to find paid work for her family and was offered a fulltime job at her local KFC branch.

Nicole said: "I've been doing voluntary work so I came here looking for a job, any job, and I'm absolutely delighted to say I got one. It's very good timing with Christmas coming up."

Jobhunters also heard from our panel of experts —ncluding Apprentice star Michelle Dewberry, Government Minister Roseanna Cunningham, Brewdog head of HR Nicolle Sinclair and Danny Logue from Skills Development Scotland.

headline: Tales of the unexpected: entrepreneurs break the rules
author: Jonathan Moules
text:
Businesses can achieve success by defying the wisdom of the crowd.

Take the example of Brompton Bicycle, one of the UK’ largest mass bike makers despite assembling its idiosyncratic folding two-wheelers in the west London neighbourhood of Brentford.

Not only has it defied the conventional wisdom that manufacturing is unsustainable in high-cost locations, but Brompton has achieved record sales while pushing up the price of its basic model by more than 60 per cent.

The belief that business rules are there to be broken is emerging as a theme among the eclectic mix of founders who are contributing to Start-up Stories, a Financial Times podcast series that started last week.

Among them is Andy Harter, pictured, founder of Cambridge-based RealVNC, which lets computer users “ake over”nother machine remotely. Instead of the usual method of seeking backers or begging his bank for a loan to fund development, he sold mugs and T-shirts with the company logo to loyal users around the world: “he idea came that if you love the software, why don’ you keep doing it and buy a T-shirt saying I love VNC?”

The thousands of pounds the company netted as a result enabled it to expand without outside funding. Mr Harter also pioneered crowdfunding —undraising from an online audience.

Another participant in the series will be BrewDog co-founder James Watt, who told the FT on Wednesday how he set out to “reak the rules” It should be noted, though, that the founders all still pay attention to basics such as cash flow.

The ability to tell stories like these is a valuable skill because it helps to engage customers, investors and business partners. As any storyteller knows, the best anecdotes are those with an element of the unexpected.

headline: BOLLAND BUYS HIMSELF TIME
author: ALEX BRUMMER
text:
ALMOST everyone in Britain has an opinion about Marks & Spencer. The critics latch on to every misstep with gusto and fans see only the upside.

So it is with the latest results.

The haters latch on to the 1.9pc drop in non-food sales, noting that M&S, the nation's leader in clothing for women, continues to ship market share.

What really matters to shareholders are profits, earnings and dividends. In this, M&S came up trumps in the first half of the year.

Indeed, if one strips out international profits (suffering as a result of uncertainty in Russia and Turkey and stagnation in the EU), operating profits at the company were up 15pc, which is not to be dismissed.

For good measure there is a dividend increase.

So what about the negatives? It is clearly unsatisfactory that M&S continues to lose clothing sales to competitors. Several factors have been at work here.

There has been a conscious decision by chief executive Marc Bolland to focus on margins rather than sales, so there was very little discounting, particularly in the second quarter. The other force is the changing shape of the High Street and shopping channels.

M&S ruled fashion for many years because of the lack of competition. Challenger brands such as Zara, H&M and Primark have changed that - together with the rise of digital retailing and the likes of Asos. There has been secular change: M&S is a victim of that and is playing catch-up, especially among younger shoppers. Nevertheless, the fact it competes so well in lingerie suggests it is not all hopeless just yet.

Where M&S does still show leadership is in food, with 24 successive quarters of same-store sales growth at a time when the grocery market is going through a revolution with Tesco, Asda and others challenged by incomers.

If Bolland could do for clothing, through innovation, what he has done for food, then he would eventually leave a golden legacy.

Ideas for taking the company to new levels are still there and the improved cashflow and stronger balance sheet will allow that.

It would not be surprising if M&S, like any company, were seeking to add existing iconic brands to its core offer to provide some excitement, although there is no intention to become a department store where control over the supply chain is lost.

Other areas for innovation are in financial services and in technology with a number of ideas, including tying M&S more closely in to the local economies where it has stores, very much on the cards.

As for Bolland himself, he still appears to be enjoying the job despite the sniping from the side-lines. The most recent internet glitch was limited to a small cluster of customers and has been fixed, as has the downward projection of earnings.

The latest results buy him some more time to complete one of the most closely monitored transitions for a British enterprise.

Germany's much-praised Rhineland-Westphalia capitalism is meant to be all about the long term.

In the case of Volkswagen, the sluggish response of management to crisis has led to an old-fashioned collapse in the share price - now down almost one-third, with the latest disclosures on the fuel usage cheating on 800,000 cars adding to the misery.

Investors should be in no doubt about how serious this scandal has become. The Macondo oil platform explosion in the Gulf of Mexico has so far cost BP shareholders an estimated £7bn. As the fines rolled in and the share price shrank, the company came within touching distance of bankruptcy. BP escaped by some rapid-fire asset sales and releasing cash by renegotiating its energy interests in Russia.

With American government agencies and litigation specialists readying cases against VW, it has to be ready for a deluge of fines, penalties and judgments.

One way that it might prepare itself for this is by divesting its upmarket subsidiaries Porsche and Audi so as to raise cash and limit the contamination which already has begun.

One fears, however, that new chief executive Matthias Mueller and his board may lack the rapid-fire capitalist skills for dealing with what is becoming a very Anglo-Saxon crisis.

A positive bit of fallout from the great financial crisis has been the rise of crowd-funding that matches risk-taking investors directly with new enterprises.

Much of the fast-expanding craft-beer industry - firms such as Brewdog - are being funded in this way.

It is now starting to make a contribution to the nation's housing crisis with residential firm Property Partner bringing to the market 42 flats in the converted Sandars Maltings mill at Gainsborough, Lincolnshire, with more than 40pc of the properties crowd funded.

Eat your heart out, banks.

©aily Mail

headline: CITY PEOPLE
text:
The feuds, the faces and the farcical Dominic Walsh @walshdominic

not so much a leak as a flood from the cbi Vote Leave, the group set up to campaign for Britain to quit the European Union, has been getting up the nose of Paul Drechsler, the CBI's president, ahead of its annual conference next week. In an email to members of the CBI president's committee on Tuesday evening, Mr Drechsler laments the "daily media attacks on the CBI" and warns them that the following morning's papers would be carrying a story "based on a leak of the minutes of the July meeting of the president's committee". How do we know this? Because his email has been leaked to Vote Leave.

cameron flies the flag Forbes' annual Most Powerful People list usually makes for interesting reading and yesterday's is no exception. Poor old Britain has only one representative, David Cameron at No 8, up two on the previous year but still short of the top three of Vladimir Putin, Angela Merkel and Barack Obama. Of the full list of "73 people that matter from the 7.3 billion on the planet", a decidedly excessive Business big shot name tim fitzpatrick age 56 position chief executive, chaps An HSBC veteran for 24 years, Tim Fitzpatrick is taking on one of the trickiest Jobs in the banking world (Katherine Griffiths writes). CHAPS, the Clearing House Automated Payment System, is not at the forefront of most people's minds, but it manages a vast number of transactions. Individuals use it when they make a payment for a house or car, but otherwise it is used by banks to clear thousands of daily trades. Its average daily turnover is £69billion and last year it processed £8trillion, or 36.5 million payments.

Mr Fitzpatrick, who starts on January 18, will be in familiar territory. He is HSBC's global payments and cash management chief operating officer and head of payment services.

30 are American (Forbes is American, in case you didn't know), with eight from China and four each from Russia and Japan.

Pity poor Martin Winterkorn, the now-former chief executive of Volkswagen, who finds himself in a short list of "drop-offs" from last year's ranking, alongside Sepp Blatter, the scandal-hit Fifa president.

someone's a cider drinker There was a certain April Fool's Day flavour about the booze industry yesterday. First, BrewDog, the Scottish brewer, announced the launch of "the world's first transgender beer", using hops that it claims have undergone a "sex change" from female to male prior to harvest. Then a craft cider company called Orchard Pig, based near Glastonbury, said it was testing a delivery service using a drone incorporating a model of a Gloucester old spot pig, left. Its aim? "To make pigs fly."

do as i say, not as i do The Libor-fixing trial in America heard evidence that Tom Hayes, the convicted British trader, warned other brokers and traders not to put their plans to fix the interest rate in writing. Sage words —hough, oddly, he put this advice in writing.

headline: Henny Pennyish hack panic turned TalkTalk into CluckCluck
author: Jonathan Guthrie
text:
Movie superhero Mr Incredible fails at his dull office job because he helps the customers. “hat about the stockholders?”sks his nasty boss. If, like Lombard, you jumped to your feet in the cinema shouting, “eah! What about the stockholders?”ou will have doubts about TalkTalk. The budget broadband company has shouldered costs of £0m-£5m for dealing with a smallish cyber attack.

TalkTalk made headlines last month when chief executive Dido Harding warned that 4m customers’ata could have been stolen. It was the only situation in which TalkTalk would make headlines. Commentators thundered against the dangers of that web thingy. Questions were asked in the House.

Since then, the number of customers who had data nicked has been revised down as drastically as the profits of a bombed-out Aim company. The figure released by TalkTalk with patchy half-year results on Wednesday was 156,959, compared with 1.2m mid-crisis. Of the total, 15,600 customers lost bank details that could be used to scam them, though not steal money directly.

Baroness Harding, a vigorous and charismatic boss, says it was necessary to warn that the breach might be large so customers would be on their guard. Necessary, too, she says, to offer all of them free upgrades to lessen an exodus triggered by blanket bad publicity.

Her model was Johnson & Johnson’ 1982 Tylenol recall. But no one was at risk from poisoned painkillers here.

Henny Penny and transatlantic cousin Chicken Little doubtless felt it was necessary to warn fellow poultry that the sky was falling. TalkTalk could have couched its own tocsin in more neutral terms and issued figures when it was sure they were correct.

The shares are bouncing back. Customers’nterests have been served better than those of investors. The two interests, contrary to quasi-religious belief in consumer-oriented organisations, are not perfectly aligned.

Alepocalypse now

AB InBev’ £1bn purchase of SABMiller looks more certain, thanks to a recommendation from the target. But the name of the combined group is undecided. The City has christened the brainchild of AB InBev boss Carlos Brito “egabrew” Here are alternatives:

Lagerviathan. The group would have operations in every important beer market and pro forma historic revenues of $64bn. Mr Brito has already moved to quell US competition concerns by agreeing to sell SABMiller’ stake in MillerCoors to JV partner Molson Coors for $12bn. That’ $2bn more than estimates and a decent 12 times earnings ex-nasties.

In Asia, the new group might be dubbed The No Snow Show. Mr Brito will probably have to agree the sale of a stake in Snow, China’ biggest beer brand, to get antitrust clearance. A high price —8bn is mooted —ould modestly lower the purchase multiple paid for SABMiller.

Growth Hopportunity sums up the deal rationale extolled by the Brazilian. Forecast synergies are a skinny $1.4bn. So Mr Brito is bigging up prospects for the guzzling of premium beer by new middle classes in developing markets.

BrewDog is a scrappy Scottish craft brewer. But investors may apply the same handle to the new group’ stock if opportunities fail to materialise and AB InBev turns out to have overpaid. As it is, Bernstein analysts reckon it will take the company six years to recoup its cost of capital.

Trencher warfare

Store chains turn storytellers at Christmas. J Sainsbury and its rivals air adverts meant to melt the cold hearts of consumers and get them spending. The grocers are seeking to bolster another narrative: that of commercial success. But this remains elusive, as indicated by an 18 per cent half-year drop in underlying profits before tax at Sainsbury to £08m.

Analysts hailed a “eat”— result less bad than feared. But successive numbers from the likes of Sainsbury leave one doubting how much control chief executives such as Mike Coupe exert over their companies’ates.

Traditional volume supermarkets are shrinking —ainsbury’ half-year sales were 2 per cent lower at £3.6bn —s consumers defect to discounters and specialists such as Waitrose. Their business model is impaired rather than broken. But it remains hard to tilt towards growth.

Last year, Sainsbury’ Christmas ad celebrated a break in trench fighting during the first world war. There will be no such respite for supermarkets. All we can guess is that their market share will slip for years and stabilise at levels lower than the perma-optimists of broking anticipate.

Mr Coupe is the continuity candidate who replaced Justin King in 2014. It is easier for him to dream up narratives for advertising than for Sainsbury itself. Dave Lewis at Tesco and David Potts at Wm Morrison can at least deploy the well-worn tale of the troubleshooter who tears down and rebuilds, even if the prevailing trend in their industry is slow diminution.

jonathan.guthrie@ft.com

headline: Prime properties
text:
Duplex apartment Edinburgh, Midlothian WHAT YOU GET A four-bedroom flat occupying the ground and first floor of 15 Moray Place. The apartment has a drawing room, dining room, kitchen and large bathroom. There is also a toilet and a cloakroom.

WHERE IS IT? On one of Scotland's most exclusive streets, in the west of Edinburgh. The Water of Leith and Dean Gardens are moments away. Edinburgh centre is a 20-minute walk. UPSIDE A large balcony has views to the Dean Valley and Fettes College. There is shared access to Lord Moray's Feuars Gardens.

DOWNSIDE There's on-street permit parking, but not a private space.

PRICE £.3 million CONTACT D J Alexander, 0131 652 7313, djalexander.co.uk Castle and coach house Ellon, Aberdeenshire WHAT YOU GET Dating from 1724, Ellon Castle is a B listed mansion formerly owned by Sir James Reid, Queen Victoria's doctor. It is now a seven-bedroom home with reception rooms and a separate coach house. WHERE IS IT In Ellon, Aberdeenshire. Scottish brewery BrewDog has its HQ on the outskirts of the town. Aberdeen City is half an hour by car.

UPSIDE Off the kitchen there is a wine turret housed within a circular tower. Ellon Castle is in the middle of the town. DOWNSIDE The long driveway means that most family members will need a car. PRICE £50,000.

CONTACT Simpson & Marwick, 01224 622622. aspc.co.uk One-bedroom flat Notting Hill, W11 WHAT YOU GET An elegant flat in a period building with high ceilings, original cornicing and bay windows. The bedroom has fitted cupboards and doors that lead to a decked garden. WHERE IS IT? On Elgin Crescent, around the corner from Portobello Road and Westbourne Grove. Ladbroke Grove Tube station is a short walk away.

headline: Biotech entrepreneurs embrace crowdfunding
author: Andrew Ward, Pharmaceuticals Correspondent
text:
Millions of pounds are pouring into fledgling UK life science companies through crowdfunding platforms, providing a new financing option for early-stage biotechnology entrepreneurs trying to commercialise medical innovations.

Cambridgeshire-based Axol Bioscience is the latest to use equity crowdfunding to bridge the notorious “alley of death”—he funding gap that often imperils start-up companies, especially in the biotech sector.

Axol, which produces customised human cells for use in medical research, is seeking £00,000 through an equity crowdfunding platform called SyndicateRoom which has raised over £6m for life science companies.

Crowdfunding has taken off in many sectors as an alternative to traditional finance and, for investors, an adventurous way to deploy capital in an era of low interest rates and volatile equity markets.

Life science companies have been relatively slow to take advantage —n large part because their businesses are harder to understand and take longer to produce a return compared with many sectors.

However, this has begun to change in the past two years as dozens of European biotech and medical technology companies have raised money through platforms such as SyndicateRoom, Crowdcube and Seedmatch.

Apta Biosciences, a research tools company based in the UK and Singapore, raised £.8m in two separate rounds with SyndicateRoom last year.

Others that have raised more than £m include Cambridge Nutraceuticals, which is developing a “omato pill”ontaining powerful antioxidants from tomatoes. Meanwhile, Warwick-based Fertility Focus is developing technology to more accurately track the ovulation cycle.

Advocates believe crowdfunding can help tackle the shortage of finance that has long hindered UK biotech start-ups — problem often blamed for Britain’ relatively poor record in turning its world-class science into commercial successes.

However, the spread of alternative finance into the high-risk world of medical research could also heighten the concerns of regulators about the perils facing investors. The UK’ Financial Conduct Authority last year warned crowdfunding investors that “t is very likely you will lose all your money”

Goncalo de Vasconcelos, chief executive of SyndicateRoom, said his company’ platform was aimed at more sophisticated investors than the “£0 punts”n consumer ventures synonymous with crowdfunding. BrewDog, the Scottish independent brewer, raised a record £0m this year from 35,000 people in return for shares and discounted beer.

“e have a minimum investment of £,000 which makes people think before they invest,”aid Mr de Vasconcelos.

All SyndicateRoom ventures have an experienced lead investor who conducts due diligence and negotiates terms. All other investors receive the same class of shares, on the same terms as the lead.

The Axol fundraising is led by serial Cambridge entrepreneurs Jonathan Milner and David Cleevely, whose previous start-ups include Abcam, a research tools company now valued at $1.2bn on London’ junior Aim market.

Mr Milner said crowdfunding allowed angel investors to harness more funds than they could alone while allowing smaller investors to “ag along”ith experienced entrepreneurs.

headline: Business; Opinion Columns Crowdfunding has a role to play but it must come up with more winners
author: Patrick Hosking
text:
There are, mercifully, other ways for would-be entrepreneurs to raise money apart from being humiliated and patronised for 12 weeks on The Apprentice. As Joseph Valente, a 25-year-old plumber from Peterborough, secured the promise of £50,000 of start-up capital from Lord Sugar, another company was demonstrating the power of a completely different method of raising capital.

'' The Camden Town Brewery, launched from underneath a couple of railway arches in north London five years ago, announced that it was being taken over by AB InBev, the world's biggest brewer, the group behind Stella Artois, Budweiser and Becks.

The deal triggered two immediate reactions. One was horror. Sellout, howled assorted hipsters and other customers of Camden Town's Hells Lager and Gentleman's Wit. For some, the whole point about craft beer is that it is the antithesis of the stuff churned out by the megalitre by the likes of AB InBev. Camden Town would face pressure to compromise on quality, they warned. One bar owner, BrewDog, immediately removed Camden Town products from its craft beer menu.

The second reaction was one of utter astonishment about the price being paid. AB InBev appears to be offering about £5 million for the company. That puts its value on a multiple of nine times last year's sales and 270 times pre-tax profits. That is dotcom territory. It may be only a rounding error for the Belgian-Brazilian collossus, which is in the process of buying SAB Miller for £0 billion, but it is a fabulous price, and not merely for Jasper Cuppaidge, the founder of Camden Town. Only in June, nearly 2,200 private investors decided to back Mr Cuppaidge when he raised £.75 million of expansion capital on a crowdfunding website. The details of the latest deal haven't been confirmed, but those small investors look set to make a return on capital of 68 per cent in the space of six months. If they opt for an alternative earnout offer, and targets are met, they could make 94 per cent.

At the time of the fundraising, Mr Cuppaidge was accused of being unrealistic and the investors were accused of being credulous mug punters for paying as much as they did. They are having the last laugh.

The deal will give Camden Town powerful distribution potential. The question is whether those edgy, carefully nurtured brands lose some of their consumer appeal under the ownership of a megabrewer. It's the Pret A Manger dilemma. The independent sandwich bar chain received the same kind of criticism when it sold a large stake in itself to McDonald's in 2001, only to end the relationship seven years later.

What isn't in much doubt is that this is an undeniable coup for equity crowdfunding, an industry where investor coups have been in short supply. No one doubts the ability of crowdfunding websites to raise money for start-ups and capitalhungry small companies with big ambitions. Crowdcube, the website used by Camden Town, has helped 300 companies to raise £30 million in its first five years. The past year alone has generated £0 million.

Of those 300, however, until this week only one had crystallised a positive return for investors. That was E-Car Club, an electric car-sharing business, which was bought by Europcar this summer. The 63 investors who had put in £00,000 two years earlier are understood to have tripled their money. The Camden Town deal, if concluded, will bring the number of successful deal exits to a paltry two.

The number that progressed to a flotation is an even more disappointing: zero. There are some promising share market debutants in the wings. Cell Therapy, which develops regenerative medicines for heart failure, is understood to be considering a flotation, possibly next year. It raised £00,000 this year through crowdfunding.

Of the 300, Crowdcube says that only 16 have definitely failed, but it won't say now many others are zombies —till alive in theory, but with their cash reserves long exhausted and their directors perhaps more interested in other ventures. It seems likely to be a growing number.

Investors are, for the most part, grown-ups. They are patient and supportive. They understand that this is a long-term game. They know that the losers will outnumber the winners.

But they do need to see the occasional winner. And they have to be very big winners to make up for the many more companies likely to fail. Sadly, neither of Crowdcube's winners has achieved the status of a "ten-bagger" —n investment that grows tenfold, which is the benchmark for serious success among stockpickers in the risky world of start-ups and early-stage capitalism.

If anything, investors are more forgiving of setbacks and slow progress at these nascent unlisted investments than they would be of conventional listed company shares. For one thing, there are considerable tax advantages. Most of the prospective investments qualify for the Enterprise Investment Scheme or Seed Enterprise Investment Scheme status giving investors income tax relief, capital gains tax advantages and loss relief.

Second, they get pleasure from backing small companies, where they can see their money immediately generating jobs or where they just love an ingenious product or idea.

In theory, equity crowdfunding has a commendable role to play in widening the capital pool for businesses too small or esoteric to interest traditional venture capitalists or formal "angel investor" networks (and too proud, shy or sane to want anything to do with Lord Sugar). However, it must come up with a good few more Camden Towns if investor interest is to be sustained for much longer.

Patrick Hosking is Financial Editor of The Times. @HOSKINGTHETIMES

headline: Let's build on what we all do best
text:
IT'S been an amazing year all told —t times surprising, occasionally depressing, but ultimately rewarding with plenty of lessons learnt.

For every high, there's been a low. For every up, a down.

And that was just my 2015.

Filling the year's headlines have been general elections, royal babies, terror incidents, diesel disasters, coal catastrophes, summer heatwaves, winter floods, tennis triumphs, and footballing failures.

Nationwide, we've seen our businesses making major strides as the worst ravages of the recession have been mostly consigned to the past.

We've witnessed supermarket wars that have benefitted consumers, and falling petrol prices that have seen commuters and enterprise alike put firmly in the driving seat once more.

We've toasted our vibrant whisky and gin industries, Scotland's numerous entrepreneurial successes, and looked on proudly as recruitment plans have been ramped up, encouraging fresh employment.

We've celebrated Scots women being ranked second in Europe in employment league tables, and shop vacancy rates hitting a four year low, meaning our high streets are busier than they have been in a long time.

We've championed notable successes And keep calm ladies, we even had George Clooney jetting in to celebrate our best enterprises at the Scottish Business Awards.

In this column, we've championed notable Scottish successes across a variety of sectors.

The likes of Brewdog, Skyscanner, Rockstar, Alexander Dennis, Kinnect2, Esh Border Construction, Thorntons Solicitors, and Integrated Cloud Solutions have all stood out as leaders in their field and significant contributors to the national economy.

Yet, it's clear that there's still plenty of work to be done in 2016.

Indeed, on the flipside of those many successes, we saw plenty of less encouraging headlines —— difficult year for our flagship festival T in the Park, the Forth Road Bridge closure causing economic and traffic chaos, Donald Trump axed as our business ambassador causing serious repercussions across his Scottish golf empire, and sustained legal fallout from the past troubles of Rangers FC, to name just a few.

But the problems have extended beyond the big headlines to smaller enterprise. I was particularly disap-pointed to see a recent survey indicating that many start-up companies are still depending on family, friends and credit cards to provide them with support in their early years.

In publishing its findings, the Institute of Directors called on the government to make the EIS and SEIS tax relief schemes easier to use for small-stake investors.

In other words, we still need more help applied at grass roots level — New Year's resolution from our ministers ——nd budding entrepreneurs aren't always seeing their enthusiasm matched by realistic support.

The climate remains just as challenging and ever-changing for those in more established positions. Would any predictions help Scots businesses get ahead in 2016? Personally, I believe that the convergence of companies turning to cloud technology to cut costs is no flash in the pan.

If software is applied correctly, then amazing benefits will be realised for heightened productivity and eased admin, freeing up staff to pursue exciting business leads and genuine growth.

Glasgow-based Integrated Cloud Solutions, which I mentioned earlier, has been a key exponent of moving business processes to the cloud as the shift away from physical computing infrastructure has gathered pace. Certainly, now is the time to invest in leading cloud storage to ensure that your business remains competitive in 2016.

Business automation software was once an added edge in the marketplace; now it's a must for those looking to stay ahead of the pack.

Increasing technological connections will, however, never be a substitute for those real human connections which must of course remain paramount.

Such are the key tenets of smart business that will never change.

Good business is built on relationships, so make building them your business in 2016.

headline: Crowdfunding rides to the rescue of many SMEs
author: Emma Dunkley
text:
The retrenchment of banks from riskier forms of finance —uch as to new companies —ollowing the financial crisis has helped raise the status of crowdfunding platforms such as Kickstarter in the US and Seedrs in the UK.

Jeff Lynn, founder of Seedrs, says: “rowdfunding has fuelled a tremendous transformation in the way SMEs [small and medium-sized enterprises] think about finance.”

Nesta, an innovation charity that provides research on the start-up funding industry, says crowdfunding offers “n opportunity to bypass traditional funding streams such as grant applications or bank loans”

Crowdfunding falls into three broad categories. The simplest sees investors hand over cash in return for goods and services.

The second is debt crowdfunding, which allows investors to lend money that, in theory, they receive back with interest. Finally, in equity crowdfunding investors buy shares that they hope will be worth more in the future.

Figures show that the value of crowdfunding expanded globally by 167 per cent to $16.2bn in 2014, up from $6.1bn raised in 2013. In 2015, the industry is on track to more than double once again, according to a report by Massolution, a US research firm.

“K really leads the world on crowdfunding,”ays Mr Lynn.

Craig Asano, founder of the National Crowdfunding Association of Canada, says the concept is struggling to get off the ground in many countries, including Canada, due to slower adoption rates and regulatory constraints.

However, investing in early stage ventures, especially as an equity investor, is high risk. Many start-ups eventually fail, often meaning investors will not be able to get their money back.

In the UK, one in five companies that raised money on equity crowdfunding platforms between 2011 and 2013 has since gone bust, according to a study by AltFi Data and law firm Nabarro.

But investors with an appetite for risk can gain high returns. Limited data are available, but Bill Morrow, co-founder of Angels Den, which describes itself as an angel-led crowdfunding platform, says investors can triple their original investment.

Last year, the independent brewer BrewDog broke equity crowdfunding records in the UK by raising £m in the first three weeks of its fundraising round, using its own platform —quity for Punks.

headline: BrewDog  plans funding round for US push
author: Aliya Ram
text:
Scottish craft brewer BrewDog plans to tap the US for cash as it readies for expansion into America.

The maker of Punk IPA on Thursday said that it planned to run an “quity for punks”rowdfunding campaign in the US in May. It is hoping to emulate the success of its British funding round last year, which raised a record £m in the first three weeks.

BrewDog said that it aimed to increase volume sales almost tenfold by the end of 2016 compared with the beginning of last year, on the back of a “raft beer revolution”nd planned entry in to the US market.

“015 was a great year for BrewDog in and of itself, but it was also about laying the foundations for the next five years of growth,”aid co-founder James Watt.

“e built an amazing new brewery in Ellon [Aberdeenshire], increasing our capacity there fivefold, and we also started building an amazing new brewery in Columbus, Ohio.”

BrewDog, which is due to release full results at the end of February, said that annual turnover in 2015 had risen 52 per cent to £5m, with operating profit rising 112 per cent.

UK revenues rose 130 per cent, boosted by strong sales of the popular Punk IPA pale ale, which it said was “ow the best-selling craft beer in UK supermarkets”

As well as opening its first US brewery in Ohio, the company this year plans to open bars in Amsterdam, Warsaw, York, Berlin and London.

British investors will be watching the success of the US crowdfunding campaign after criticism that BrewDog had an inflated valuation. The brewer, which values itself at more than £00m, has raised almost £0m since 2010 through crowdfunding, more than any other business in the UK.

But in the US it will face stiff competition. According to research from the Brewers Association, last year a new craft brewery opened on average every 16 hours in the US. Sales of craft beer rose 17 per cent in the US last year, compared with 0.5 per cent for mainstream lager.

headline: BrewDog 's new crowdfunding push to finance US expansion
author: Greig Cameron
text:
BrewDog, which is to launch a fresh crowdfunding drive to help to finance its expansion into America, has revealed that its turnover jumped by more than 50 per cent last year.

The company has set up a dedicated US subsidiary and is building a brewery in Ohio. Now the craft brewer has unveiled plans to sell up to 20 per cent of the equity in its venture there.

James Watt, the co-founder of the business, said that the brewery and launching a chain of bars in America was expected to cost about $25 million.

The company intends to reveal the full details on the crowdfunding, dubbed Equity for Punks USA, in May once it finalises them with the Securities and Exchange Commission. The new brewhouse in Columbus is about eight times the size of BrewDog's existing facilities in Ellon, Aberdeenshire. Mr Watt said that a number of US wholesalers were lined up to sell the beer once the Ohio site opened, which could be as early as this summer.

According to Mr Watt, the company's US lawyers and advisers were "very confident" in the proposed pricing for the American business, which would give it a valuation of $300 million.

The company said in its draft financial results for last year that turnover was up 52 per cent to £5 million. It reported a near-55 per cent rise in pre-tax profit in 2014 to £.65 million on turnover of £9.6 million. Brewdog's existing Equity for Punks scheme in the UK is scheduled to close at the end of April.

It had hoped to raise up to £5 million and an update last month said £3.5 million had been raised from more than 20,000 investors around Europe. That was said to be a world record for equity crowdfunding. The investors get beer discounts and invitations to the annual meeting, which features tastings, giveaways and a rock concert. It attracted a crowd in excess of 6,000 last year.

More than 37,000 people have bought in through four crowdfunding rounds since 2010. As well as its beers, BrewDog, Scotland's largest independent brewer employing 580 people, runs dozens of bars around the world and some retail outlets specialising in craft beer.

It plans to launch a spirits business called Lone Wolf Distillery in April, offering small-batch gin and vodka.

British investors will be watching the US crowdfunding campaign with interest after criticism that BrewDog had an inflated valuation. The brewer - which values itself at more than £00m - has raised almost £0m since 2010 through crowdfunding, more than any other business in the UK.

But in the US it will face stiff competition. According to research from the Brewers Association last year a new craft brewery opened on average every 16 hours in America. Sales of craft beer rose 17 per cent in the US last year, compared with 0.5 per cent for mainstream lager.

headline: news SCOTTISH craft beer king [...]

The brewer has seen international success and Hong Kong is the latest addition to its world wide selection of bars.

A short distance from the iconic Victoria Harbour and a 10-minute walk from Central Station, it is the perfect location for a cold beer after work.

headline: Boldness in Business 2016: winners and nominees
text:
Person of the year

Andy Jassy

Amazon Web Services has become the most closely watched division of Amazon after it was broken out as its own business segment last year. The idea for AWS came from Andy Jassy and the division was founded in 2006, when AWS began providing web hosting services to start-ups. AWS has grown to become one of the leading providers of cloud computing services and, with its low-cost strategy, rivals huge, well-financed competitors such as Microsoft and IBM. Given AWS’ $8bn revenues in the last year and healthy profits, Jassy thoroughly merits the person of the year award.

Drivers of change

Fanuc, Japanese industrial robots maker

The Japanese company, founded in 1950s, is the world’ largest producer of industrial robots. Last summer, it announced it was developing machines that will be able to clean and repair themselves. Long-admired but with a tradition for secrecy, Fanuc has taken the path toward openness during the past year, creating an investor relations department and promising higher transparency. This decision could prove to be a leader for public Japanese companies across industries. The group is introducing new “ollaborative robots”o work more closely with humans.

Nominees:

Amazon Web Services, US web services provider

Blued, Chinese online dating service

Citymapper, UK live public transport data adviser

Gravity Payments, US credit card payment processor

Stripe, US online payments specialist

Corporate responsibility / Environment

Toyota, Japanese automotive group

In 1997, Toyota became the first to mass-produce a hybrid electric vehicle, the Prius. In 2011, Toyota announced a concept fuel-cell vehicle, which was developed into a commercial product in 2014 when it launched in Japan. The car uses hydrogen as fuel, which reacts with oxygen in the fuel cell to develop electricity. The Mirai, meaning “uture” was launched in Europe late last year with the aim of bringing fuel-cell technology into the mainstream. In October, the company laid out its goal of virtually eliminating petrol and diesel engines from its fleet by 2050.

Nominees:

Compagnie de Saint-Gobain, French building materials supplier

Ikea, Swedish home furnishings retailer

Kering, French luxury goods group

OCI, South Korean chemical and clean energy engineer

Tesla Motors, US electric cars manufacturer

Technology

Tencent

One of China’ most-used internet service portals, Tencent provides a wide range of online offerings, including social networks, ecommerce and games. It led last year’ launch of WeBank, China’ first online-only bank, which offers loans to small and medium-sized private companies that struggle to borrow from mainstream banks. Tencent’ WeChat service has grown to the extent that the product has become practically synonymous with the smartphone, with 650m monthly users. It also provides Tencent with a gateway into China’ huge service economy.

Nominees:

Biocartis, Belgian life sciences experts

Cellectis, French biotechnology specialist

DJI, Chinese drone manufacturer

Everledger, UK diamond database services

Silent Circle, Swiss data encryption provider

Tencent, Chinese internet services group

ZMP, Japanese domestic and educational robots producer

Entrepreneurship

WeWork, US workspace and office services provider

WeWork is a leading US exponent of the fast developing gig economy and provides shared workspaces and office services for start-ups and small businesses. Transforming mundane offices into industrial chic workplaces, it grew rapidly with the rising numbers of freelance workers after the 2008 financial crisis and opened 78 locations in 23 cities in the US, Europe and Israel. London is anticipated to compete with New York as a WeWork hub, with 14 locations expected by the end of 2016. Valuation of the company early this year had risen to $15bn.

Nominees:

BlaBlaCar, French car-sharing service

Coupang, South Korean ecommerce specialists

Hello Alfred, US home services provider

SoFi, US peer-to-peer lender

Teeling Whiskey Company, Irish spirits producer

Smaller company

Farfetch, UK fashion ecommerce platform

Farfetch is an ecommerce website providing a platform for bricks-and-mortar fashion boutiques across the world to sell their luxury goods. In 2008, Portuguese entrepreneur Joséeves founded the company in London to provide a solution for independent fashion shops that struggle to bring customers through the door, but also do not want to invest heavily in moving online. Farfetch now ships garments to 190 countries and, while its clients include many leading labels, a lot of the 400 boutiques and 1,600 luxury designers paying for its services are small independents.

Nominees:

BrewDog, Scottish craft brewer

CD Projekt Red, Polish video games developer

James Cropper, UK high-quality paper manufacturer

Spiber, Japanese biomaterials producer

ZeroLight, UK automotive computer graphics specialists

Developing markets

M-Kopa, Kenyan solar energy provider

This Kenyan company uses mobile money platforms to deliver affordable solar power to people who are not on the grid and are using expensive kerosene for lighting. In 2010 Jesse Moore, Nick Hughes and Chad Larson founded the Nairobi start-up that became M-Kopa the following year. The goal was to sell 1,000 solar power units a week. Sales are now up to four times that. Customers pay for electricity using mobile phone credit, with the company’ cloud platform tracking payments. M-Kopa has more than 300,000 customers in Kenya, Uganda and Tanzania.

Nominees:

BRCK, Kenyan internet connectivity developer

Clip, US-based, Latin America-focused payment processor

Edutel, Indian education management specialist

Jana, US mobile internet service provider to emerging markets

Tencent, Chinese internet services group

headline: Edinburgh’ entrepreneurs offer a taste of start-up culture
author: Jess Webb, University of Edinburgh Business School
text:
Having worked in global organisations most of my career, I have had limited exposure to the start-ups. But many MBA graduates are lured to this world. We are constantly exposed to new ideas, innovations and trends in management, catalysed by a network of creative and dynamic thinkers at the business school, and thus the entrepreneurial spirit ignites.

This week the Edinburgh university business school’ E Club, which fosters entrepreneurship, hosted a programme of free events for businesses and students to celebrate the 10th anniversary of the club’ start-up network. The day-long festival included talks from James Watt (below), co-founder of BrewDog, and Gavin Dutch, founder of Edinburgh-based Kotikan, an app developer. Our cohort attended talks, a speed-networking breakfast and two pitching competitions, sponsored by Grant Property and Santander, with a combined prize fund of £,000. Social enterprises Punjabi Junction and Social Bite put on quite the spread at The Big Social Lunch, an informal entrepreneurial gathering.

E Club success stories such as GearedApp, an app developer, Glam Candy, a make-up school, and Etch’, an object tattooists –hich includes laptop laser etching –ere all set up in the school.

I found the event fascinating as passionate entrepreneurs shared their stories, pitched their ideas, and networked with like-minded people from Edinburgh’ business community and university. I took a minute to sit back and admire the creative, tenacious individuals in the room, driven to challenge the status quo.

A highlight was meeting renegade alumnus Mr Watt, to hear about his passion for “ringing good beer to good people”n a break-all-the-rules “unk”ind of way. This is a company he started several years ago with his business partner Martin Dickie, filling beer bottles in a garage, and it is one of Scotland’ fastest-growing food and beverage companies, valued at more than £06m in 2015. The company also set a crowd funding world record of £0m in October last year.

The day culminated with a celebration not just of successes but also of failed ideas which, ultimately, have led these entrepreneurs to brilliance in one way or another. Failure is valuable and in this cabaret-style event we celebrated the mistakes, mishaps and learning curves, all integral to the entrepreneurial journey.

Next week I am taking New Venture Creation – class that reveals the nature of the entrepreneurial process, including market analysis, financing, and management of new ventures. The class will engage with local start-ups at their various stages of growth. We even have to pitch an idea ourselves.

I love that we are exposed to this entrepreneurial world while on our MBA programme. No matter what our ambition post-MBA, it is crucial we understand and harness entrepreneurial skills and qualities. Managers cannot afford to be complacent and must foster innovation or they will be left behind. Even if we are not game to take that leap of faith into start-up land once we graduate, we still need to think like entrepreneurs to innovate beyond the realm of day-to-day business operations.

headline: ALL ALE TO 'DOG'
text:
BRITAIN'S best-selling craft beer BREWDOG is going increasingly global —hipping 41million bottles worldwide last year. The Scottish outfit also opened a £7.5million brewery in Columbus, Ohio, as it prepares to tap into the US market.

Bosses also launched bars in the UK, Hong Kong and Barcelona.

Sales for the year were £5million, up 51 per cent on 2014, while UK sales rocketed 131 per cent.

Overall pre-tax profits for the year were 5 per cent down due to the firm's ongoing expansion and hiring of staff.

headline: Punks, bands and cheap beer? It must be time for the AGM
author: Sharlene Goff, UK companies editor
text:
On Saturday, 4,000 punks met up in Aberdeen. Not to relive the anarchic heyday of the 62 Club, but to hold an unlikelier convention —he annual shareholder meeting of Scotland’ largest craft beer company.

These were “quity punks”—he name BrewDog gives to its investors who have helped it raise more than £0m without, it says, “elling out to faceless suits at the bank”

The 11.30am-11pm all-dayer sounded a lot more fun than most whinge-and-eat-warm-sandwiches shareholder meetings. Brewdog’ punks were entertained by bands Frightened Rabbit, Idlewild and the “railblazers of the original Punk movement” U.K. Subs. Beers were on offer from BrewDog (of course) and Danish craft brewer To Ø. Food was provided by the trendy Pieminister.

The perks of equity punkdom go beyond the PUNKAGM2016. There’ the free birthday beer and a 10 per cent bar discount for anyone with 10 shares or more. Step up your investment to £,000 and you get a free beer a month for five years, at £0,000 you get one a month for life. Above £0,000, and a beer truck will roll up to your front door with a fully stocked bar.

The financial rewards are less clear. On April 20 BrewDog will close its fourth crowdfunding round. For the past year, it has been selling shares for £7.50 each (investors must buy at least two) and is targeting £5m. In January it said it had raised £3.5m from more than 20,000 investors. That was, it said, a crowdfunding record —ut still a fair way short of its target with three months to go.

Other small businesses are following suit. English winemaker Chapel Down said this month that its beer and cider business, Curious Drinks, had raised more than £.7m, mostly through Seedrs —ne of the UK’ largest equity crowd funding platforms. The almost 900 customers who took part —aining them the slightly more distinguished title of “urious Drinks enthusiasts”—ill also receive discounted beer.

Equity crowd funding is growing fast —he amount raised in the UK more than tripled last year to £32m, according to a recent report from Cambridge university and UK charity Nesta. It accounted for 15.6 per cent of UK seed and venture-stage equity investment. But the returns remain uncertain.

BrewDog is unquoted, so investors cannot sell shares on an exchange. New issues will dilute their holdings. The company says investors can trade once a year on an “sset Match”latform, although setting a price is difficult. Trading is dependent on finding an existing shareholder —he only people who can buy through the platform, according to Brewdog’ prospectus —ho is prepared to pay the price. A flotation may be a longer-term aspiration.

BrewDog says investors have made up to 443 per cent from selling shares from earlier funding rounds —lthough it was unclear over what specific period.

The company is certainly expanding fast. The latest fundraising values it at £05m, almost triple the £11m pricetag in a similar campaign in 2013. Brewdog’ annual results out this week showed revenues rose by more than half in 2015 to £4.7m. But the cost of its rapid growth —t bulked up its executive team, opened 16 bars and a new warehouse —eant pre-tax profit fell almost 5 per cent to £.5m.

Keen drinkers do have beery alternatives for their cash. A £25 investment —he average amount invested in BrewDog's funding —n listed brewer Shepherd Neame a year ago would have earned about 12 per cent —r £3. To make an equivalent return from BrewDog discounts would mean drinking 114 pints of Punk IPA in a year (receiving 55p off the £.50 London bar price).

And that presumes the value of the investment does not fall —y no means a certainty given the financial regulator has warned the high risks of crowdfunding mean investors are more likely to lose their money than make any.

BrewDog says its fundraising scheme provides it with a “uge army of supporters who have literally bought into what you stand for” Other supporters of the model say it provides democratic access to capital for small businesses, for which other financing options are scarce.

Given that banks have shied away from lending to SMEs partly because they have found it increasingly difficult to assess the risks involved, it seems unlikely that inexperienced investors —owever hip —ill do better.

For cheaper beer and free parties it might be fine. But as a serious investment? Well, at least shareholders are well placed to drown their sorrows if it all goes wrong.

This article has been amended to reflect the fact that the AGM took place in Aberdeen not Glasgow.

headline: Hops are the latest big headache for craft beer brewers
author: James Hurley
text:
Brewers of craft beers are being hit by price rises and shortages of a critical ingredient after a weak European hop harvest (James Hurley writes).

Some hops varieties have soared in price by up to 50 per cent because of the impact on crops of last summer's hot and dry weather, producers say.

Charles Faram, the hop merchant, is warning brewers of a "challenging" year ahead because of global supply problems. An industry presentation by the company, seen by The Times, said that there has been a poor yield in countries including Germany, Slovenia, the Czech Republic and Poland, with hops in some areas said to have been "visibly stressed and wilting in the heat".

The International Hop Growers' Convention said that Germany's harvest had fallen by 27 per cent last summer.

Some brewers are understood to be considering passing price rises on to drinkers, but the Society of Independent Brewers said that a bigger problem was the risk of shortages of particular varieties hitting small brewers.

Britain's craft beer industry has enjoyed rapid growth in recent years and there are now more than 1,300 breweries, more per capita than any country in the world, the Campaign for Real Ale said.

The threat posed by the hops shortage is being compounded for smaller brewers by increased competition from multinational brewers such as Anheuser-Busch InBev and SABMiller, which have acquired craft breweries in an attempt to capitalise on the popularity of independent beer.

Neil Walker, of the independent brewers' society, said: "Scarcity is playing on people's minds. For our members, if your beer gets picked up by a national pub chain or supermarket and you can't get enough of the hops, that is a big worry." He said that "single hop" beers were becoming rarer for this reason.

Larger craft brewers, such as Scotland's Brewdog, have long-term contracts with producers that offer protection from price fluctuations, but many smaller breweries are more exposed.

headline: Charity brewer clears its first [...]
text:
Charity brewer clears its first watershed A charity-orientated beer project that plans to invest all its profits in bringing clean water to the world's poorest regions has reached its crowdfunding target.

Brewgooder has been launched by Josh Littlejohn, the entrepreneur behind the ethical sandwich shop chain Social Bite, and Alan Mahon, a business development manager. Having raised £00,000, it will distribute 200,000 cans of beer to bars and shops in Scotland. Working in partnership with BrewDog, the craft brewery, it aims to produce its first run of beer next month. James Watt and Martin Dickie, the founders of BrewDog, will sit on Brewgooder's board of trustees, while Sir Tom Hunter is to be a philanthropic partner.

Brewgooder aims to provide a million people with clean water in its first five years, based on projected sales of its Clean Water Lager. The initial sum raised will go to Oxfam, WaterAid and MercyCorps to provide water at a school in Malawi.

headline: Business; Opinion Columns BrewDog  raises £9m
text:
news in brief

BrewDog has fallen short of its £5 million crowdfunding target but said that it was delighted with having raised £9 million in the biggest equity round the craft brewer has completed to date. The Fraserburgh-based company, which was founded by James Watt and Martin Dickie in 2007, believes that the most recent Equity for Punks scheme, which closed on April 20 after being open for 12 months, is a world record for crowdfunded equity investment.

headline: Editorial; Opinion; Leading articles BRIT WINE MAKER'S FINE YEAR
text:
ENGLISH wine and beer maker CHAPEL DOWN sold £.2million worth of booze last year, a 34 per cent jump on 2014.

Wine was up 27 per cent while its Curious Drinks beer and cider division grew 50 per cent.

Despite the sparkling sales, its pre-tax losses grew to £79,000 for the year as it expanded.

The Kent company spent £.32million on 90 acres of "world class" vineyards, saying wine remained the bedrock of its business.

It supplies retailer MAJESTIC WINE and THE ROYAL OPERA HOUSE in London, among others.

Chief executive Frazer Thompson said yesterday: "Demand is continuing to rise and we are developing a very strong brand to ensure we continue to maximise the potential."

SCOTS craft beer maker BREWDOG raised £9million in its latest Equity for Punks crowd funding push.

headline: Pub groups wake up and smell the coffee
author: Paul McClean and Aliya Ram
text:
A man walks into a bar and asks for a latte macchiato.

That might have been a joke in the past. But, faced with high rents, falling alcohol consumption and competition from supermarkets, pub groups and breweries are increasingly relying on alternative revenue streams —uch as quality coffee —o entice customers through their doors.

Fuller’, the independent brewery, opened its first standalone coffee shop in 2014 and now sells almost 2m hot drinks a year. Wetherspoons, the pub chain, began serving filter coffee with free refills for 99p last year, and says it now sells more than 1m cups every week.

“e are clearly seeing that the purpose of visiting a Fuller’ pub is as likely to be for food or a coffee as it is for an alcoholic drink,”ays chief executive Simon Emeny. “he smoking ban was the catalyst for pubs to broaden their potential market, and we now take coffee incredibly seriously. Sales are growing by double digits every year.”

The trend is also working in reverse, as UK mass market and independent coffee shop chains fire back by introducing alcoholic drinks.

“offee shops and pubs are moving into each other’ territory because they’e both under pressure,”ays Jeffrey Young, chief executive of research group Allegra. “offee shops are doing it because growth is slowing, and pubs are doing it to survive.”

One factor, according to people in the industry, is that “ipster”onsumers are making the same demands —or organic ingredients and local sourcing —hatever they are drinking.

Nick Tolley, founder of independent London-based coffee house Taylor St. Baristas, says one motivation behind the move into alcohol is “o make use of dead rent, to exploit a different part of the day and to get more bang for your buck”

But he adds: “he other is that it’ a tangential alignment of values —e’e not selling Kronenberg or Stella, the wines and beers we’e stocking are from small wineries and breweries, and they’e organic and green, much like our coffee.”

BrewDog, the Scottish craft brewer that this month bought a 33 per cent stake in Edinburgh-based upmarket coffee chain Third Wave, says “he people who appreciate craft beer often also have a taste for speciality coffee”

Big coffee chains Starbucks and Pret-a-Manger are also embracing the trend for craft beer, introducing it alongside wine in certain stores to accompany hot evening meals.

Starbucks, which already sells alcohol in 300 US stores, says a successful trial in Stansted airport led it to introduce menus “or the late afternoon and evening”n three other franchises at airports and train stations in the UK.

It says it intends to expand the scheme as it looks to respond to changing consumer habits.

Analysts say that particularly for the bigger coffee sellers, the move into alcohol is motivated by a slowdown in the growth of coffee sales.

“here’ been so much growth in coffee that for ages the big players didn’ need to do much else —hey were struggling to deal with the demand for coffee as it was, and were focused on keeping a clear brand identity,”ays Mr Young at Allegra. “ut now, with growth slowing, and under increasing pressure from small independents, they’e had to act.”

Data from Euromonitor show that growth in coffee sales is slowing: between 2010 and 2014, total sales at UK coffee shops grew by an average of 12.5 per cent year on year. In 2014-2015, that dropped to just 2.2 per cent.

Costa Coffee, the coffee chain owned by Whitbread, is one of the groups struggling in the face of increased competition from independent coffee houses selling artisanal brews. The company, which has more than 2,000 branches, has lost almost a third of its value over the past year. Last month, Alison Brittain, chief executive, said a decline in high street footfall was hurting coffee sales.

Stonegate, one of the UK’ largest pub companies, says it has attempted a format that blends the atmosphere of a pub with that of a coffee shop to appeal to more demanding student customers.

“tudents don’ just want cheap beer and lots of volume now —hey want something more sophisticated,”e says.
