Last month, Alison Brittain , chief executive, said a decline in high street footfall was hurting coffee sales.

Stonegate, one of the UK's largest pub companies, says it has attempted a format that blends the atmosphere of a pub with that of a coffee shop to appeal to more demanding student customers.

"Students don't just want cheap beer and lots of volume now - they want something more sophisticated," says Simon Longbottom , chief executive.

headline: 60 SECONDS WITH... JAMES WATT, 33 CO-FOUNDER OF  BREWDOG
text:
James Watt, 33, is a co- founder of independent brewery BrewDog. It began in 2007 with two employees, and now has 540 and makes 65 beers. He is married and lives in Scotland.

Who had the biggest influence on you?

Legendary beer writer Michael Jackson. A chance meeting led to Martin Dickie [BrewDog's other co-founder] and I deciding to take the plunge and start our very own renegade brewery. Michael told us to quit our jobs and start brewing beer.

What has been your most important decision so far?

Starting a revolution - we were bored of the industrially brewed lagers and stuffy ales that dominated the beer market and decided to do something about it.

What's the most satisfying part of your job?

Seeing so many people turn away from bland mass-produced beer is what we thrive on.

What's the toughest decision you've had to make?

Throwing caution to the wind and starting an ambitious small business with next to no capital was gritty and intense. It resulted in endless periods of hard work and little sleep.

What's been your worst job?

My legal career in my early twenties. It was very brief. I quickly came to realise that conforming was painful at best.

the best piece of advice you've ever been given?

Be selfish, do what you want and ignore advice.

When was the last time your job kept you awake?

A few weeks ago our Inverness brewery played host to the BrewDog AGM (Annual General Mayhem). A chance for our backers to get a behind the scenes look. A lot of beer was consumed and no sleep was had.

what is the most expensive thing you've bought?

Our canning line where we produce cans of beer, but it's worth every penny.

When did you last worry about money?

Yesterday, today and tomorrow - cash is king.

How do you handle stress?

Our Jack Hammer beer at 7.2pc strength solves anything.

your biggest extravagance?

We've just spent £5m on a brewery in Columbus, Ohio. When we sealed the deal, I'd only spent 24 hours there.

Where are you happiest?

BrewDog HQ in Ellon, Aberdeenshire. You need to surround yourself with like-minded, awesome people.

If your 20-year-old self could see you, what would he think?

I think he'd be incredibly happy that craft beer has taken off and he doesn't have to drink Tennent's ever again.

©aily Mail

headline: Keeping tradition alive in new Wild Atlantic ways...
author: ARAN BRAZIL
text:
IN AN unassuming industrial estate in the quiet surroundings of Ballymote, Co Sligo, a small group of people are busy filling bottles, printing labels and handpacking boxes to fill orders across the country.

This is the hub of one of Ireland's small business success stories: White Hag Brewery, established in 2014, run by just three full-time staff and a few part-timers, and already winning awards for its beers.

Since its arrival on the scene at the Irish Craft Beer Festival in 2014, White Hag has won fans with its big, bold and boundary-pushing releases, from the 10.2%ABV barrel-aged Black Boar imperial stout to its heather and bog myrtle-infused brew (inspired by ancient techniques used by monks).

White Hag's beer styles are deliberately different to the red ales, pales ales and stouts emerging from other Irish breweries.

"We started as a group of likeminded individuals with very different skills, all with the loose ambition of opening a brewery," says managing director Paul Mullin.

"We purposefully set out to brew beers others wouldn't, or couldn't. We wanted to drink really great beers, so we made them ourselves."

The team went to the US to find their master brewer, given that's where the best alternative beers have come from over the past few years.

"It's a coincidence that he's thirdgeneration Irish, and was keen to make traditional Irish styles of beer, but in a modern craft brewery," Mullin says.

The head brewer in questions is Joe Kearns, his soft-spoken American twang drawing a crowd as we stand in the shadow of towering stainless steel fermentation tanks.

When Kearns started here, he was manually stirring the wort by hand — collection of broken wooded rakes in the corner lies testament to just how difficult it is to hand-mix the heavy, gruel-like substance.

He jokes that if he'd had to stir wort by hand any longer, he would have packed his bags. Nowadays the work is done in a stainless steel mash tun. But rakes and pulled muscles aren't the only hint at the brewery's truly artisanal roots.

Beann Gulban, a beer that won second-runner-up in this year's Irish Beer of the Year awards, was originally brewed using wild heather that was hand-foraged locally by the staff.

Nowadays, they import their heather from Belgium as it became too time and labour intensive —ut they are working on going local again.

Beer enthusiasts have taken to White Hag with a thirst and put their money where their mouth is.

A recent crowd-funding campaign to expand the brewery through Linked Finance was two-times over-subscribed.

Scotland's Brewdog had taken a similar route, albeit on a much bigger scale, to help grow its brewery and branded pub chain, but White Hag's ambitions are more focussed.

"Our ambition is to create the best Irish craft beer in the world. Not to run pubs or distribution companies, or to be a tourist attraction," says Mullin.

"Those are all distractions that may seem attractive just to make some cash, or break even. Instead, everyone at our brewery is working towards making great beer and getting it into the best hospitality outlets in Ireland and abroad."

Public brewery tours, such as the one I attended last month, are also limited to special events for the time-being.

In the US, the healthy craft beer industry is partly financed by a brewery's right to sell their wares on their premises, making tours more attractive and earning tidy profits without disrupting the local pub trade.

In Ireland, however, the laws governing direct sales by breweries to the public are grossly out-dated, so it's hard for small outfits such as White Hag to justify obtaining a licence.

Mullin says: "We'd love to see this change, but we'd faced massive opposition to tap rooms in breweries. "That's despite the fact that the Wild Atlantic Way brewery tour, with 17 breweries, garners huge interest from tourists, as well as people who want hold their stags, hens and work events in one. The Government will have to get real on this if they are genuine about supporting our craft beer industry."

For now, we will just have to enjoy a bottle of White Hag from the growing number of good stockist across the country.

Meanwhile, the firm is planning its second birthday celebrations —ollow @whitehagbrewery for updates.

headline: Notts bounty
text:
NOTTINGHAM'S PARK PLAZA HOTEL IS IDEAL

GO: NOTTINGHAM STAYING THERE: Accommodation for up to two adults and two children costs an average of £42.50 per night.

Further details at parkplaza.com and oaksnottingham.co.uk.

nhsn LOCAL legend states Robin Hood robbed from the rich and handed out his spoils to the poor. What an unnecessary kerfuffle.

He need only have hailed a stagecoach from Sherwood Forest to Nottingham city centre and treated them to a slap-up night down Maid Marian Way.

That's where you'll find the Park Plaza Hotel and OAKS restaurant.

And you won't have to look far to find a band of Merry Men on either premises.

Robin and Marian probably wouldn't recognise Nottingham these days. The old place has had a real facelift over recent times and has a lively, bustling heart.

So many of its visitors are still attracted by nearby Sherwood Forest and the traditional and historic attractions.

The bronze statues of Robin and his Merry Men at Nottingham Castle are magnets for tourists. A long and winding trail runs from the castle to the forest, passing many attractions associated with Robin.

It goes through the Clumber Country Park and past Rufford Abbey, as well as Robin Hood Hills, Thieves Wood and Fountain Dale. Other draws include the Sherwood Forest Art and Craft Centre, the 1,000-yearold Major Oak, and Thynghowe, a medieval meeting place where people came to resolve disputes.

I prefer to resolve my disputes in a little more comfort, though. And that's where the Park Plaza came in.

The recently-refurbished bedrooms looked as though Maid Marian had prepared them herself.

The four of us shared a top-floor family room with inter-connecting doors and spectacular views across the city. It allowed the kids - aged 14 and 11 — little privacy and meant Mum and Dad could relax, too. You don't need to steal from the rich to be able to stay here either. This d hotel is stylish and comfortable 4H hotel is competitively-priced and superbly located right in the heart of the city.

Accommodation for up to two adults and two children costs an average of £42.50 per night. And the handy location means you can save on taxis —r even the stagecoach - by hoofing it around on foot.

The hotel is within walking distance of the main shopping districts and the train station. With plenty to do with the family, Nottingham makes a fantastic destination for a city break. Catch a show at the Theatre Royal or Nottingham Playhouse, take a tour of the underground City of Caves, enjoy a boat trip along the river or discover the history of the city and the origins of Robin.

If you can't be bothered walking even that far though, just nip next door to OAKS for a truly special meal. Hold on, I hear you cry. Doesn't The Scottish Sun already have a resident restaurant critic? me Do ha h W fo f r ar Well, yes, but I've had to step in for Tam Cowan in the Nottingham area ever since that silly misunderstanding when he was mistaken for Friar Tuck.

as sta Fr of pe a ca to Otr tw g There was no mistaking the quality of the menus at OAKS, a new independent restaurant which has proved massive hit in Nottingham. It's a carnivore's dream —rom the Bottomless Brunch and Rumpday to OAKS Sunday Roast and the lunch treat of any burger or sausage and two sides for just £.50.

That mouthwatering scent from the grill strikes you as soon as you haul open the wooden doors. The welcome from OAKS' staff is just as warm and enticing. Live music and cocktail specials all add to the experience.

Tam and the good Friar would have loved the rib eye steaks which my son Mark and I ordered.

My wife Fiona opted for the pork, pancetta and smoked paprika sausage and daughter Katie had a bumper Angus beef burger.

I thought I was quite adventurous trying the "Curious Lager" - until I noticed that the alternatives included Brewdog Dead Pony Pale Ale and Pullers Organic Honey Dew.

Back next door at the Park Plaza, the multi-award-winning Pan-Asian cuisine at Chino Latino Restaurant Bar looked every bit as good. Merry men, women and children all round.

ROGER HANNAH

headline: I'LL DRINK TO BOOZE SUCCESS
text:
SCOTLAND'S drinks industry has, for centuries, been inextricably linked to whisky.

b bp As I said in this column last week, our whisky industry's reputation has been well earned and its produce is now in demand in all four corners of the globe, generating billions of pounds worth of exports every year.

p However, it seems that the Scottish drinks industry is unwilling to rest on these laurels.

There has been, in recent years, a huge surge in the popularity of craft beers produced in Scotland. Emboldened by the success of breweries such as BrewDog and Innis and Gunn, there has been an explosion of new Scottish beers.

I half expected to see this bubble burst. Supply would surely, at some point, outstrip demand? However, the craft beer juggernaut continues to gather speed. Just this week, it was announced that two Scottish craft beer producers plan to invest more than £00,000 in expanding their operations. Dundee-based 71 Brewing announced the creation of eight new jobs, while Fierce Beer, based in Aberdeen, will create three new jobs.

Meanwhile, Waitrose has introduced 49 new Scottish craft beers to its stores, raising the number of Scottish breweries it works with from 13 to 21.

Business body Scottish Enterprise recently estimated that the UK premium bottled ale market is currently worth £90million a year, and expects this market to grow to be worth more than £ billion annually by 2020.

It just goes to show that Scotland has not lost its edge when it comes to exporting quality food and drink to the rest of the world. We should all raise a glass to that.

headline: SMALL DISTILLERIES ON THE RISE AS DRINKERS TURN TO TRENDY GINS
author: HOLLY BLACK
text:
THE growing popularity of trendy gins - dubbed a 'ginaissance' - has led to a sharp rise in the number of distilleries operating in Britain.

A report by accountancy group UHY Hacker Young reveals that the total jumped by a third last year, from 21 to 28.

There has also been a surge in the number of vineyards and breweries opening up across the country as discerning drinkers turn to small independent producers and away from big international brands.

Supermarkets are now expanding their ranges to cater for changing drinking habits, with discounter Aldi planning to stock 18 craft beers.

Gin - which is also known as 'mother's ruin' - now comes in a range of flavours including rhubarb, cocoa and raspberry as well as the mysterious 'Unicorn Tears' variety. Cambridge-made Anty Gin is said to contain the 'essence' of 62 wood ants in each £00 bottle.

Earlier this year, Surrey-based Silent Pool Distillers unveiled what is believed to be the world's most expensive bottle of gin. Its £,000 nine-litre bottle is hand-painted and signed by artist Laura Barrett. It took the title from the £,000-a- bottle Japanese Watenshi Gin produced by Cambridge Distillery.

'The popularity of boutique alcohols such as craft beer or artisan gin has been a factor in the strong numbers of newly licensed producers over the past five years,' said the UHY report. It found that 37 new wine producers have set up in England over the past year while some 285 new breweries have sprung up in recent years - piling pressure on well-established players such as Heineken and Carlsberg.

Scottish micro-brewery Brewdog, known for its beers and ales, recently set up a distillery named Lone Wolf which will produce vodka, gin and whisky. Its 32pc proof Tactical Nuclear Penguin beer retails for £06 per 375ml bottle, while the limited edition 55pc End Of History beer cost £00 per 330ml bottle and came encased in a taxidermy squirrel or stoat.

Kent-based English wine producer Chapel Down - which supplied wine to the Royal Wedding of the Duke and Duchess of Cambridge - has raised £.7m to build its own brewery for its Curious Drinks beer and cider business.

©aily Mail

headline: Technology usurps business in honours list
author: Henry Mance, Political Correspondent
text:
Internet entrepreneurs featured heavily in the Queen’ 90th birthday honours , sweeping aside the FTSE 100 chief executives and chairman who have traditionally been recognised.

The founders of Ocado, Zoopla, Skyscanner and Raspberry Pi were all honoured, part of a deliberate move by David Cameron’ government towards fashionable sectors.

It comes against a backdrop of hostility towards big business, with parliamentary inquiries into the retailers BHS and Sports Direct and questions about the amount of tax paid by multinationals.

Conservative MPs are considering calling for Sir Philip Green to lose his knighthood after BHS collapsed months after he sold it to a former bankrupt with no experience of retailing.

Sir Ian Cheshire, the chairman of Debenhams, who oversaw the business nominees, said the list sought a “uch more realistic recognition”f the UK’ future growth.

In a sign of the changing emphasis, James Watt and Martin Dickie, the co-founders of Scottish craft brewer BrewDog, received MBEs. Richard Reed, who co-founded Innocent Drinks, the smoothie-specialist bought by Coca-Cola for an estimated £20m in 2013, received a CBE.

Lucian Grainge, chairman and chief executive of Universal Music Group, received a knighthood and there was an OBE for Phillip Monks, chief executive of challenger bank Aldermore.

The list was greeted with fury by advocates of a British exit from the EU, who accused the government of rewarding “ampaign cronies and donors” Vote Leave said that 22 of those receiving honours were linked to the Remain campaign, among them Mr Reed, who is also the deputy chairman of Britain Stronger in Europe.

In total the honours list contained more than 200 names, excluding public officials and those honoured for community work.

Another award with political overtones was the Knight Grand Cross received by Sir Ian Wood, chairman of engineering company Wood Group. Sir Ian took a prominent role in the 2014 referendum on Scottish independence, warning that a Yes vote could leave a “ightmare legacy”or the oil industry.

Recognising tech entrepreneurs has been complicated by the fact that many internet companies are started by more than one person. In addition, their business success is often hard to gauge. Shazam, the song recognition app whose executive chairman Andrew Fisher received an OBE, remains lossmaking.

The honours list included an OBE for Alex Chesterman, chief executive of property listings site Zoopla, and MBEs for Ben Medlock and Jon Reynolds, whose predictive text application SwiftKey was acquired by Microsoft for $250m this year. Sarah Wood and Scott Button received OBEs after selling their video advertising company Unruly to News Corp for up to £14m.

Sir Ian Cheshire said the honours committee had strived to convince tech entrepreneurs “hat it’ OK to come forward”o accept honours. Last month Rohan Silva, Mr Cameron’ former technology adviser, said he had turned down a nomination because he believed the system had an “nfair bias”owards civil servants and the heads of publicly listed companies.

Celebrities on the list include Rod Stewart, the musician, who received a knighthood; Alan Shearer, the footballer, and Alastair Cook, England cricket captain, CBEs; Jamie Murray, the tennis player, who follows his brother Andy in receiving an OBE; and Stuart Broad, the cricketer, MBE.

Julia Peyton-Jones, who stepped down as co-director of the Serpentine gallery after 25 years, becomes a dame; while Tim Peake, the astronaut, receives an overseas CMG for his services to space research and scientific education. There were also knighthoods for Angus Deaton, the Princeton economist and Nobel Prize winner, and Roger Scruton, the conservative philosopher.

Fewer than 10 per cent of those on this year’ list were public employees. They include three civil servants involved in implementing Universal Credit, the beleaguered benefits reform overseen by Iain Duncan Smith, the former minister.

In total about 2,000 people were nominated for honours, of whom nearly 1,149 will receive them. A further 71 people received overseas awards. Women made up nearly half of the list.

Hilda Lyons, who turns 96 on Saturday, received a British Empire Medal for her work setting up a swimming club for disabled people. She plans to announce her award to the neighbours of her maintained flat in Woodford, London. “ shall probably put it on the noticeboard,”he told the FT, adding jokingly, “ust to annoy a few people.”

headline: A call from Earth sends Major Tim over the moon
author: David Sanderson;Valentine Low
text:
Awarding honours usually involves nothing more exciting than sending a letter in a brown envelope asking someone if they will accept a gong. This year it involved making a call into space.

The astronaut Tim Peake was aboard the International Space Station when he received the call informing him of his recommendation for Companion of the Order of St Michael and St George.

"A particular highlight for me was phoning Tim Peake to ask if he would accept an honour," Sir Simon McDonald, head of the Diplomatic Service, said.

Major Peake, who returns to Earth next weekend, said in a message from the ISS: "I am honoured to receive the first appointment to the Most Distinguished Order of St Michael and St George for extraordinary service beyond our planet."

He added: "I am only one privileged person in a complex team of technicians, scientists, engineers, educators, trainers and flight directors, all working in pursuit of one of the greatest scientific and technical challenges of our time: exploring our solar system for the benefit of people on Earth. This award is for them."

Other people whose achievements have perhaps been less stellar but which have had more impact on people's lives include Margaret Calvert. The graphic designer co-created the system of UK road signs, including the pictograms such as "men at work".

In what is probably another first, Shahzada Saleem, known to his supporters as King Shazad, has been appointed MBE for his services to stone lifting. There are also honours for Jonathan Maltus, a British sommelier in France, and for a pair of brewers who had set out to provoke the establishment.

Martin Dickie and James Watt, the co-founders of BrewDog, have attracted repeated controversy. They produced what was the world's strongest beer in 2010 at 55 per cent alcohol-byvolume, named Speedball after the heroin and cocaine cocktail that killed River Phoenix and, for the 2011 royal wedding, released a beer containing socalled natural aphrodisiacs which they called Royal Virility Performance. They are now being appointed MBEs.

Aberdeenshire-based BrewDog has grown from two employees to 580 and opened about 30 bars across the UK and 15 more around the world.

Mr Watt said: "It's amazing to have been awarded something so prestigious —t's incredible to have the craft beer industry recognised at this level. It shows just how far we have come in our nine short years."

headline: NEW FACES OF BOOMTIME BRITAIN; AS THE ESTABLISHED CORPORATE NAMES TAKE A BATTERING, THE QUEEN HANDS GONGS TO ...
author: EMILY DAVIES
text:
ENTREPRENEURS who have made millions from posh bottled beer, men's face cream, smoothies and an app that speeds up text messaging are among those who received birthday honours from the Queen.

The awards give official recognition to a number of vibrant start-up businesses which are increasingly rattling the big High Street names. And it comes as anger grows at the behaviour of some of the established giants of the corporate world.

Sir Philip Green faces questions about his involvement in the sale of BHS to a three-times bankrupt, Mike Ashley was quizzed by MPs about the alleged Victorian working practices at a Sports Direct warehouse, and WPP boss Sir Martin Sorrell faced a revolt over his £0m pay deal.

MBEs went to James Watt and Martin Dickie, who set up the beer company BrewDog in 2007 when they were both 24, and have turned it in to a business which employs 500 and exports to more than 50 countries. Last year the company's sales grew more than 50pc to £5m.

Watt, who is 34 and married to adult colouring book creator Johanna Basford, said: 'We've built an incredible team of passionate, beer-loving people both at home and internationally, with all of us on a mission to make other people as passionate as we are about great craft beer.'

However, BrewDog has courted controversy in its time.

In 2010 it brewed what was the world's strongest beer at 55pc, it named a beer Speedball after the drug cocktail that killed Stand By Me actor River Phoenix aged 23 in 1993, and during the Royal Wedding in 2011 released a tipple that contained aphrodisiacs and was named Royal Virility Performance.

Other recipients of an MBE are 30-year-old Jon Reynolds and 26-year-old Ben Medlock who founded the keyboard app SwiftKey.

It uses artificial intelligence and typing prediction technology to allow customers to write faster on mobile phones.

The company started by three Cambridge University friends was bought by Microsoft in February for £70m. However, the announcement will be another blow to the third member of SwiftKey's founding trio, Chris Scott-Hill. The 29-year-old from Buckinghamshire left SwiftKey in 2008 and gave up his stake in the company - for a bicycle. It is a decision Scott-Hill has described as 'the biggest mistake I ever made'.

The co-founders of men's skincare brand Bulldog have also received MBEs.

Friends Simon Duffy, 39, a former product designer at Saatchi & Saatchi, and former investment banker Rhodri Ferrier, 37, launched the brand in 2007.

Duffy was buying natural moisturiser for his wife Annabel and realised there were no equivalent cosmetics for men.

The product is sold in thousands of stores worldwide and this year sales will hit £1m.

Bonamy Grimes, co-founder of SkyScanner, receives an MBE for services to technology and travel.

The 44-year-old, who lives in Norfolk with his wife Juliet, co-founded the flight prices comparison website with Manchester University friends Barry Smith and Gareth Williams, who is the firm's chief executive.

They came up with the idea in the pub in 2001, and last year revenues reached £20m.

The co-founder of Innocent smoothies, Richard Reed, was awarded a CBE for services to the food industry and to charity.

The 43-year-old founded Innocent with Cambridge University friends Adam Balon and Jon Wright when they decided they wanted to run an ethical, healthy business.

They spent a week selling home-made smoothies at a jazz festival in 1999 and sold 24 on their first day. Innocent now trades in 18 countries.

Reed and his two co-founders sold their stakes to Coca-Cola in a deal that valued the company at £30m. They now run Jam Jar Investments, a business that backs and mentors young entrepreneurs.

Aldermore bank chief executive Phillip Monks was given an OBE for services to banking.

The 56-year-old, who lives in Essex with his wife Patricia and their two children, founded the bank in 2009 and has worked in the industry for more than 30 years.

Aldermore, which focuses on tailored lending services for its customers, has grown from 30 employees seven years ago to 850 today.

©aily Mail

headline: WE'RE GONG FOR A PINT
author: GORDON TAIT
text:
THE founders of craft beer firm BrewDog last night described their MBE awards as "incredible".

James Watt and Martin Dickie, who set up the company in 2007 when they were both 24, were stunned to get the gongs.

James said: "It's amazing to be awarded something so prestigious —ncredible to have the craft beer industry recognised at this level.

"We're building a team of passionate, beer-loving people both at home and internationally, with all of us on a mission to make other people as passionate about great craft beer as we are."

BrewDog, based in Fraserburgh, Aberdeenshire, has grown from two employees to 580, with around 30 bars across the UK and 15 more around the world.

It drew controversy in 2010 for producing the world's strongest beer at 55 per cent-strength alcohol. And for Prince William's wedding in 2011, they released a beer with so-called natural aphrodisiacs called Royal Virility Performance.

headline: CITY PEOPLE
author: Dominic Walsh
text:
The feuds, the faces and the farcical

Business big shot

NAME ANDY MURRAY

AGE 29

POSITION ADVISORY BOARD MEMBER, SEEDRS

He is the world No 2 at tennis and Andy Murray hopes to become a top seed in business. The Scot, who is building up to the start of Wimbledon this month, announced yesterday that he had made three more investments through Seedrs, a crowdfunding platform where he became an advisory board member this time last year.

He has invested in Dog Tracker Nano, a device that allows owners to follow their pets' movements, and blow LTD, which calls itself an "Uber for beauty". Murray, whose career winnings total more than £0 million, now has 15 investments through Seedrs.

He also has spent £ million on Cromlix House Hotel, near his home town of Dunblane, where his wedding reception was held last year.

THE SONG REMAINS THE SAME DESPITE THE NEW OWNER

It is more than three months since the lease on Brocket Hall was acquired from administration by an unidentified Chinese investor. Unidentified until now, that is. City People can reveal the new owner of the stately pile in Hertfordshire is Yu Songbo, a Shanghai billionaire with interests in property, energy, logistics and shipping.

Not that the rescue of the hospitality and golf venue has put an end to the rum goings-on at Lord Brocket's ancestral home. Dieter Klostermann, the previous leaseholder until it went bust, continues to oversee the venue (much to the chagrin of the 920 golf members evicted as soon as the sale went through) and the word among the remaining staff is that it is suffering cashflow difficulties again.

RAIN, RAIN, GO AWAY

The woes of Vijay Mallya continue. Indian authorities, who are pursuing the drinks tycoon over more than $1 billion in unpaid loans against his failed Kingfisher Airlines, have seized about $200million of assets. Mr Mallya, who is in London in self-imposed exile, took time out from his nonstop tweeting about cricket, Formula One and Kingfisher beer to accuse authorities of "holding me guilty without trial", insisting that there had been "absolutely no misappropriation or diversion of funds". Quoting Vivian Greene, the American R&B singer-songwriter, he tweeted: "Life isn't about waiting for the storm to pass. It's about learning to dance in the rain."

DON'T SAY THEY'RE SELLING OUT

There's no disputing that BrewDog founders James Watt and Martin Dickie, below, have built a very successful company and doubtless their recognition in the Queen's Birthday Honours is deserved. Yet somehow the concept of two friends who have made a virtue of getting up people's noses and attacking established rivals going to the palace to accept MBEs doesn't feel right. Next they'll be selling out to AB InBev or Carlsberg.

HOLD THE FRONT PAGE

Engineers are meant to present solutions, not problems. This year's agenda-setting National Women in Engineering Day has been set for June 23. Yes, the same day as the EU referendum. As the lady deputed to promote the event conceded: "Good planning someone!"

headline: BEER VID PITCHES
text:
CRAFT brewery BrewDog is seeking beer buffs to join its graduate scheme - but you have to be nominated by your mates.

The Scots-based firm needs a 30-second video pitch explaining why you are perfect to join its two-year business or production programmes.

BrewDog co-founder James Watt said: "It's easy to pitch yourself in job interviews, but it's a lot more convincing if it comes from someone who knows you inside out.

"Call this an extreme reference. We want to know that the people we're hiring are the real deal.

"We don't care about your age or background. We just want the next generation of beer lovers to take up the craft beer baton."

Submit your entry by midnight tomorrow alongside a CV and cover letter to grnh.se/kct068.

headline: Dying for a pint
text: A brewery is encouraging drinkers who buy its Born to Die pale ale to sign up for an organ donor card. BrewDog, which is based in Aberdeenshire, is giving out donor cards with every pint. The beer contains 7.5 per cent alcohol by volume and has a shelf life of only 35 days. James Watt, the co-founder of the brewery, said: "All good things come to an end, including this beer —nd its drinker."

headline: TAKEAWAY DELIVERY SERVICE THAT'S RULING THE ROADS; ...AND NOW BRINGING WINE TO YOUR DOOR
author: LAURA CHESTERS
text:
DELIVEROO is conquering Britain one meal at a time - and the takeaway business has just struck a deal to deliver wine and beer to your door.

The firm, founded by two Americans based in London, has an army of cycle delivery couriers who distribute orders to customers from a number of restaurants it has a partnership with.

It recently sealed an exclusive international deal to deliver for Pizza Express, and yesterday launched a full alcohol delivery service.

Customers will be able to order wines from eight Majestic Wines stores across London and from 20 BrewDog brewery locations around the UK.

It is also working with independent businesses Lea & Sandeman, HonestBrew, Mother Kelly's and Vagabond Wines.

Delivery times for orders of craft beers, cocktails and wine will be around 20 minutes.

The firm began trialling alcohol sales from small businesses earlier this year and after strong demand has expanded the range.

Smaller wine merchants available through Deliveroo include Albion Wine Shippers, Fine Cocktails, Caps and Taps, White Lyan and Jeroboams.

Former investment banker Will Shu and computer programmer Greg Orlowski - both 36 - created the business that delivers food from restaurants that do not already offer takeaway delivery service.

Shu, who was born in the US to Chinese parents, was previously an investment banker in Manhattan and was used to ordering food to his office at Morgan Stanley, where he regularly worked up to 100 hours a week. But when he worked in Canary Wharf he could not find the same number and range of restaurants that would deliver to his office, so he came up with the idea of Deliveroo.

He has always said that he saw other people's laziness as his opportunity. Orlowski has now left the firm but Shu, who started the business from his home near Victoria Station in London, plans to stay on and lead its expansion.

Since being formed in 2013 it has expanded rapidly and operates in 40 countries with over 500 staff and 10,000 couriers on its payroll. The delivery cyclists and bikers are self-employed but are trained by the firm and are checked by the Criminal Records Bureau.

The firm pays riders in London a fee of £ per hour plus £ per drop and 100pc of tips, with all petrol costs reimbursed for scooters. But in some of its busiest zones in the capital, riders are paid a fixed fee of £ per delivery plus 100pc of tips plus petrol.

Dan Warne, UK managing director of Deliveroo, said: 'Both of these models have been designed to increase flexibility and give riders the potential to maximise fees at our busiest times - like evenings and weekends.'

Deliveroo has also won a following from celebrities such as Tom Hiddleston.

The riders' black and green uniforms with kangaroo logo are a common site across London but the business has also expanded to Bristol, Oxford, Aberdeen, Exeter, Newcastle, Bournemouth and Belfast. The group has a huge range of chains that have signed up, from Wagamama and Gourmet Burger Kitchen to Italian Rossopomodoro - firms that traditionally would not have provided take-away deliveries.

Customers order using a mobile phone app and track their purchase on the app. Deliveroo takes a £.50 profit and the average order is delivered in around 30 minutes.

Shu said he stays hands on in the business by doing occasional deliveries by bike. He has joked that he orders about 1.7 times a day on average because he usually orders from it at least once a day, though sometimes more often.

On Fridays Shu often orders lunch for everyone in his office.

Deliveroo is backed by a group of investors that also backed Facebook and Twitter.

Accel Partners and Index Ventures - which is also a shareholder in rival Just Eat - are among the investors that have helped it raise around £40m since it was founded in 2013 and it is now valued at more than £00m.

And the business has even greater plans to expand. Warne said it has also recently launched breakfast deliveries. He added: 'Innovation is key to our growth. We're incredibly excited to boost partnerships with stand-alone wine and beer retailers, which we think our customers are going to love.'

©aily Mail

headline: The toast of drinks
text:
MILKSHAKES, juices and craft beers have topped the list of drinks ordered from the capital's eateries, cafes and bars.

Food delivery service Deliveroo revealed the list of popular drinks that it has now added to its order options.

Pog's Strawberry Blonde Smoothie topped the list, with a four-pack of Brewdog Punk IPA from Baggot Street Wines and Cocu's Super Green juice also in the top five.

headline: THE DAILY BRIEFING
text:
Speeding up??Britain's car industry is still racing along, according to latest industry figures.

The number of new cars registered in the UK rose by 2.8pc on the previous year following a strong first quarter as Britain shrugged off the impact of Brexit, the Society of Motor Manufacturers and Traders said.

Paper losses??Johnston Press has seen its shares tumble after swinging to a loss in the last six months.

The publisher, which bought The i newspaper in April, reported a loss of £83.7m in the half-year to July 2 compared with a profit of £.2m a year ago. It said the loss was down to one-off charges after it moved costs from head office to its separate publications, as well as writing down the value of some of its assets.

Mill trouble??Production problems for Randgold Resources have pushed the gold miner into one of its worst-ever quarters.

Randgold shares tumbled 12pc as profit from mining was flat in the three months to July. Gold production was hit by mechanical breakdowns at a mill in its Ivory Coast mine and problems at its operations in the Democratic Republic of Congo. Shares closed down 3.8pc, or 345p at 8635p.

Bupa ails??Private healthcare firm Bupa has seen half-year profits slump 45pc after becoming the latest big name to be hit by a slump in oil and gas.

Bupa's business in Saudi Arabia was particularly affected as profits tumbled from £55.4m to £39.6m in the first six months of the year, with the company taking a £12.3m hit from an early redemption of historic debt.

Drink up??Trendy beer company BrewDog has set its sights on breaking into America and is seeking to raise £8m in a crowdfunding campaign to launch across the pond.

The craft brewing company, which was founded in 2007, asked investors in the US to buy at least two shares for 3620p each in a new company that will be valued at £90.6m.

Lender fears??Investment bank Goldman Sachs has warned it could be forced to restructure its operations following the Brexit vote.

The lender said the referendum might have a negative effect on its European business, depending on the result of negotiations.

Cold steel??The UK steel industry has welcomed a move by the European Union to impose anti-dumping measures on a steel product from China and Russia.

Tariffs will be in place for five years on cold rolled steel, an industrial product used by industries including car-making and construction.

©aily Mail

headline: Brewer is in with a $1m crowd
text:
BrewDog has sold more than $1 million of shares in the first days of its crowdfunding drive in the US (Greig Cameron writes).

The Scottish beermaker is hoping to raise up to $50 million over the next six months to help to build a brewery in Columbus, Ohio, as well as rolling out its bars across America.

More than 1,200 people have signed up to invest. The shares cost $47.50, with a $95 minimum investment.

BrewDog said the response "shows the demand for our beer in America and sets us up with a community of like-minded individuals ready and waiting to help us to make our US business an explosive success".

The company has previously run four crowdfunding rounds in Britain. The most recent one, which closed in April, raised £9 million. Although the total was short of a £5 million target, it still helped to fund the expansion of its Aberdeenshire brewer y.

BrewDog, founded by James Watt, above, and Martin Dickie, employs more than 600 people and has more than 40 bars.

headline: Brewer's plan for US-style whisky stirs controversy
author: Marc Horne
text:
It has shaken up the beer barons and stirred controversy but now BrewDog is looking to take on the captains of Scotland's mighty whisky industry.

In less than nine years the Aberdeenshire company has gone from being a two-man operation to a global empire that sells tens of millions of bottles of its heavily hopped craft beer.

The company, known for its anarchic outlook and bombastic advertising, is planning to send shockwaves through the world of Scotch whisky.

The business has unveiled its own spirits company, the Lone Wolf Distillery, and declared its intention to "disrupt" time-honoured traditions by producing its own version of whisky. It will adopt a host of experimental methods including heating barrels, to mimic the temperatures of Kentucky, where American bourbon whiskey is made, to speed up the maturation process at its headquarters in Ellon.

The Scotch Whisky Association (SWA) warned that efforts to overhaul traditional processes could fall foul of the law. James Watt, BrewDog's cofounder, suggested that the company was keen to slay some "sacred cows", telling an industry magazine: "Scotch whisky needs to be disrupted. When you peel back the layers, the truth is that most of the industry is owned by multinational mega-corporations."

Steven Kersley, its head of spirits and distillation, revealed that BrewDog was looking at adjusting the temperature, humidity and pressure within its barrel store to replicate the conditions used to produce bourbon in the American south. He said: "Why just rely on the environment that the cask is maturing in?"

They are also considering using barley roasted with burning cherry or apple wood, with different ingredients such as rye and corn.

Mr Kersley said: "We really want to experiment with different types of spirit. It's a question of what we call whisky."

While he aims eventually to create a high-quality single malt, the Obanborn distiller revealed that he would also produce unconventional whiskies, which he claimed would be "crazy and interesting stuff.

"They [the SWA] do a great job of flying the flag for Scotland but personally I think they're a little bit out of date with whisky in terms of the global market.

"Scotch is not immune to challenge.

If distillers think their whisky can stand still and forever rest on the laurels of its fortuitous location on Caledonian soil then they are either naive or stupid. Our drive to experiment and innovate will separate us from the rest."

However, Rosemary Gallagher, the SWA's head of communications, said that there were strict rules in place as to what can and cannot be labelled and sold as Scotch whisky.

She said: "UK law helps protect the quality reputation of Scotch, ensures it is produced to consistently high standards and protects consumers so they know exactly what they are buying and that it is made in Scotland.

Ms Gallagher added: "Scotch exports are worth around £ billion a year, reach 200 markets and sell three times as much as its nearest rival, American whiskey."

Scottish company with a strong global pull

BrewDog was founded in Fraserburgh, Aberdeenshire, in September 2007 by James Watt, below, and Martin Dickie. The pair met at school and launched their venture when they were both 24.

In 2010, the small brewery produced the strongest beer in the world at the time, called 'The End of History'.

At 55 per cent alcohol-by-volume the brew, a "blond Belgian ale infused with Scottish Highland nettles and fresh juniper berries" was stronger than most vodkas, whiskies and gins.

The bottles, artfully concealed inside a stuffed wild animal —toats and grey squirrels —old in a limited run of 11 and cost between £00 and £00.

After only being in business for only eight years, the company sells 30 million bottles and cans each year of its ale, stout, IPA and lager and has been named Britain's fastestgrowing food and drinks company three years in a row.

The company exports to more than 50 countries and has recently started working on a new brewery in Columbus, Ohio, as well as setting up a global network of bars.

BrewDog is considered a "progressive" brewery that has significantly contributed to the marked improvement of the British pint over the past decade.

Its Fraserburgh brewery relocated to Ellon in 2012.

headline: Need to know
text:
1 Key measures in the fight against childhood obesity have been ditched entirely as Theresa May insists that the economy must take priority. Restrictions on advertising and promotional deals on junk food have been axed from a plan published today. Page 1 2 It has shaken up the beer barons and stirred controversy but now BrewDog is looking to take on the captains of Scotland's whisky industry. In less than nine years the Aberdeenshire company has gone from a two-man operation to a global empire that sells tens of millions of bottles of its heavily hopped craft beer. Page 19 3 An engineer accused of stealing American nuclear secrets for a company owned by the Chinese state that is investing in Hinkley Point nuclear power station in Somerset has said that the FBI tricked him into making incriminating statements.

Page 22 4 China has accused Australia of protectionism after two of its companies were blocked from taking part in a $A10 billion (£ billion) auction of the country's biggest power grid. While brands such as San Miguel remain on the shelves, the victims of last October's cull included Carlsberg and Carlsberg Export brands.
