headline: Embrace the can do spirit
author: ARAN BRAZIL
text:
My Round

CANS were once the preserve of cheap supermarket lager, festivals and lazy Sunday nights in front of the TV, washing down pizza.

The humble can has never quite managed to transmit the 'quality within' message the way glass does.

This is despite the fact that brewers almost universally agree cans are the much better option for beer.

They're easier to store, they transfer temperature faster (hence they cool down faster), they contain less oxygen than bottles, staying fresher longer, and they block out sunlight.

The last point may seem trivial but may be the most important —unlight really affects how beer tastes by breaking down the hops and 'skunking' the beer.

The tide of public perception has recently turned in favour of cans, thanks in part to UK brewers such as Brewdog, which has turned to cans, and Beavertown, with their funky graphic branding and almost total devotion to canning.

Craft beer in cans is now an integral part of the Irish market —n fact it's booming.

Last week, Franciscan Well released three of their core range in cans after installing a canning line at their new Cork brewery.

And they're not alone: Wicklow Brewery is set to can its award-winning HopKnut pale ale, with the help of mobile canning specialist WeCan Solutions.

Dublin's Rascals has also added to its all can line-up, with the release of the fab Pacific Secret dry-hopped saison.

So next time you're picking up a few beers for a lazy Sunday, make them craft brews, and carry that bag o' cans proudly.

n WICKLOW BREWERY will host its own beer festival at Mickey Finn's, its brewpub in Redcross, next weekend, featuring local breweries such as Wicklow Wolf, Manor Brewing and Wexford's Yellowbelly. Admission is free. Wicklowbrewery.ie.

headline: Borders brewer named the best in Scotland
text:
A small brewery in the Borders that started out in an old dairy has been named the best in the country. Tempest Brewing Company was the big winner at the inaugural Scottish Beer Awards, picking up the top prize along with four gold medals.

The company, based at Tweedbank in Galashiels, also won the growth category after sales rose 200 per cent.

Gavin and Annika Meiklejohn started brewing when they took over The Cobbles in Kelso and transformed it into a gastropub.

Tempest was born when the couple bought an old dairy building and a small brewing kit.

In February last year it moved to a larger facility at Tweedbank.

Other winners included BrewDog, Williams Bros, Tennent's, Black Wolf, Orkney Brewery, Windswept, Fallen, St Andrews Brewing Co, Dead End Brew Machine and Drygate.

Graham Nicolson, of Aldi Scotland, which is backing the awards, said that "the quality of the beer produced in Scotland is truly world class".

headline: Sport; Opinion Columns Firms splash the cash for good causes
text:
I SPEND a lot of time in this column talking about the cut and thrust fortunes of Scottish enterprise. I'll discuss profit, loss, who's buying, who's selling —ut rarely do I talk about how businesses give back to the communities in which they work.

Naturally, many don't shout about the good that they do behind the scenes, but corporate social responsibility is alive and well in Scotland.

It's easy to accuse big businesses of wanting to soften their image in the public eye —ut then, equally, it's difficult to be cynical about the good they do when they really don't have to do anything at all.

Only recently, I attended the Edinburgh Grace Banquet, an annual charity event organised by multi award-winning legal practice Gilson Gray, raising funds for some of Scotland's most disadvantaged and seriously ill children. This year, Grace supported the work of Children's Aid and The Teapot Trust, and the evening is thought to have raised over £0,000 — fantastic total that will surely help to change many lives for the better.

Not to be outdone, chartered surveyors Graham+Sibbald held a superb 'Rio Lunch' last month to announce the latest addition to its amazing Rising Star programme that supports up-and-coming sporting talent.

Those in attendance were told of the achievements of previous Rising Stars Craig Benson, right, and Callum Skinner, as well as new addition Scott Meenagh.

Swimmer Craig reached the semi-finals of the 200m breaststroke at the Rio Olympics, while cyclist Callum won gold in the men's Team Sprint.

Not all of the Rising Stars have followed traditional routes into the programme —nd Scott Meenagh is a wonderful example of what makes it so special. In 2011, he was serving in Afghanistan when he stepped on an explosive device and lost both legs.

Inspired by sport and driven by the memory of lost colleagues, Scott was invited to train full-time with GB Rowing in the run-up to the Rio 2016 Paralympics.

He has since appeared at two Invictus Games and has now re-focused on cross country skiing. Graham+Sibbald has taken real pride in helping these athletes achieve their dreams, and I was only too happy to play a small role in supporting those efforts.

At the Rio Lunch, I got the opportunity to bid on a signed shirt worn by Callum in Rio, right, which I then donated to Social Bite. It will now be auctioned at the Scottish Business Awards in November —here I will be judging several categories —aising much-needed cash for the homeless, so please get bidding if you can.

A sandwich shop with a big difference, Edinburghbased Social Bite has been an incredible success story thanks to its simple model of offering good food for a good cause.

With 100 per cent of profits going to charity, it's an example of what big achievements can be made with just a little imagination.

The people behind Social Bite have now gone further by opening their brilliant HOME restaurant, which uniquely offers a higher end but affordable dining experience with a homeless-only serving period of 3-5pm on Mondays. They've also become involved in Brewgooder, a social beer label with BrewDog where all profits are used for clean water projects in the developing world.

It's no coincidence that George Clooney lent his support to Social Bite by visiting one outlet following an appearance at the Scottish Business Awards last year, while Leonardo DiCaprio is expected to do the same next month.

Talking of the Awards, I was pleased to see Livingston-based Esh Border Construction shortlisted in the Corporate Social Responsibility category for its award-winning Building My Skills initiative.

This is helping to prepare Scottish youngsters for the world of work through a dedicated programme of learning. Like Social Bite, Esh has been able to belie its relatively small size thanks to the engagement of big partners like Tesco, Pricewaterhouse-Coopers and Travis Perkins.

It all goes to show that giving back really does work as part of a sustainable, sociallyconscious business community.

headline: Kiltearn tops Investec mid-market rankings
author: Greig Cameron
text:
Kiltearn Partners has become the first Scottish company to top the UK rankings in the Investec Mid-Market 100 list. The Edinburgh investment manager had a compound annual growth rate of 209 per cent.

Companies have to be profitable over a four-year period and have a minimum turnover of £0 million in the final year. Kiltearn, founded in 2011, had a turnover of £6.2 million in the 12 months to March 31. It made a profit before tax and partner remuneration of £7.8million. The member of the limited liability partnership with the largest share of the profit received almost £.5 million.

GreenTech Distribution, a Buckinghamshire-based mobile technology distribution company with a growth rate of 143 per cent, was second on the list.

BrewDog, the beermaker and bar owner, was the second-highest Scottish company at No 28. Others included Signature Pubs, Intercede and QTS Group. Ed Cottrell, head of corporate lending at Investec, said: "Scotland's success ... shows how important the country is to the UK economy."

209 Compound annual growth rate in percentage terms of Kiltearn Partners

headline: Business; Front Page HEINEKEN TO HOUSE
author: RHODRI PHILLIPS
text:
Sun CITY

HEINEKEN has struck a deal with DELIVEROO for home delivery of its booze.

The Dutch giant is setting up a brand called Brew House, selling only Heineken products on Deliveroo's websites.

Orders will be filled by off-licences and independent stores and delivered by Deliveroo's riders within 20 minutes.

It will operate in London, Bath and Cardiff, but could be rolled out across the UK by the end of 2017.

Deliveroo started taking standalone alcohol orders in the summer.

Its partners include MAJESTIC WINE and craft brewer BREWDOG.

headline: THE DAY CHARLES WAS NEARLY RUN DOWN BY A BUS AT CAMBRIDGE
author: REBECCA ENGLISH ROYAL CORRESPONDENT
text:
THE Duchess of Cambridge has a secret hobby - colouring books for grown-ups - it emerged yesterday.

Kate, 34, has apparently joined the rising number of adults who relax by picking up crayons, her husband told leading illustrator Johanna Basford.

The artist was picking up an OBE at Buckingham Palace for services to art and entrepreneurship when Prince William, standing in for his grandmother, the Queen, revealed that his wife is a fan of her work.

Adult colouring books have become popular in recent times, with the 'colour therapy' craze capturing the imagination of those looking to de-stress and spend time away from looking at mobile phones and screens.

Bestselling illustrator Miss Basford, 33, who lives in Ellon, Scotland, said: 'I think we've just seen the colouring community flourish. And Prince William actually said that his wife likes to colour in the Secret Garden, which was really sweet.'

Secret Garden was Miss Basford's first book and has sold more than a million copies.

Kate studied history of art at university and has frequently said how much her children - Prince George, three, and 18-month-old Princess Charlotte - love to paint and draw. Spending most of her time with her young family at Anmer Hall in Norfolk means Kate clearly has time on her hands.

Reflecting on the popularity of the activity, Miss Basford said: 'I think people are just craving a digital detox.'

She was joined at the investiture ceremony by her husband James Watt, co-owner of craft beer company BrewDog, who was also picking up an honour - an MBE for services to the brewing industry.

Joking about the Duke and Duchess's potential interests, Miss Basford said: 'Maybe she's a fan of my work and he's a fan of my husband's.'

The artist, who started drawing the colouring book illustrations as a way to relax in 2013, said her trip to the palace will inspire her current project.

'I'm working on a new book just now and it's set in a castle, funnily enough, so I will definitely try to remember everything,' she said. 'I'm sure little snippets of today will feature in the book.

'I love all the really ornate picture frames and all the beautiful sculptures, and the candelabras are just stunning.'

Colouring books for grown-ups are taking the nation by storm, selling millions of copies and racing up book stores' bestseller lists.

Fans say setting aside a few hours for colouring is relaxing and a good antidote to so-called 'tech stress'. Hobbycraft, the UK's largest craft retailer, recently said its sales of the books are up by 374 per cent.

TV property and crafts expert Kirstie Allsopp has maintained that hands-on tasks such as colouring help people relax, adding: 'It's the perfect antidote to the business of modern life and I'd recommend it to anyone feeling stressed.' Other celebrity fans are said to include Nigella Lawson.

Even the Ordnance Survey mapping organisation has joined in the craze, releasing a set of black and white maps of major UK cities, which can be downloaded from its website to be coloured in.

Anna Grace-Davidson, of lifestyle consultants Grace Guru, has said the books 'help to relax your mind and connect with your inner self?...?almost like yoga practice, only you don't have to sweat to feel balanced'.

©aily Mail

headline: Firms making changes leave a sweet taste
text:
Shaf Rasul One in a million shaf@the-sun.co.uk

BIG business spends millions on employing trend analysts to look at ways consumers are parting with their money.

Basically, they're paying people to figure out what the next big thing is going to be.

Well, I predict 2017 is going to see a BIG shift in how some businesses approach the market —'m thinking of those who produce and sell goods which are generally acknowledged as being bad for you.

Sugar tax, minimum pricing, and a ban on advertising —overnment is hitting these guys hard.

Sugar, alcohol, tobacco —hey are all going to have to reposition themselves if they are to survive. And as 2016 comes to an end, we are already seeing some making the first moves.

Obesity has been linked to 13 types of cancer and is estimated to cost the NHS in Scotland a belt-busting £00million a year.

Last week scientists at sweet treat giant Nestle claimed they had found a process to naturally reduce sugar in their products.

The Kit-Kat manufacturers are making a break with the industry by cutting sugar content by up to 40 per cent.

Boffins for the confectionery giant —hich also happens to be the world's largest food and drinks company —ave altered the structure of sugar to make it sweeter in less amounts.

The candy was tried out by a panel of tasters and apparently tastes just as good.

Nestle are currently securing a patent for the innovation and plan to introduce it from 2018. They said this is to allow time for the shift. But the point is they have recognised where the market is going and are making the announcement now —head of the competition.

While obesity is stretching the nation's waistlines, alcohol abuse has long been a downer on the country's finances.

Scotland's relationship with booze is estimated to cost the NHS at least £00m a year —ith some saying it could be as high as £00m.

As regular readers will know, I was in attendance at the recent Scottish Business Awards, and as you would imagine, there is no shortage of drink at these events.

Beer on the night came courtesy of Scotland's latest social enterprise organisation, BrewGooder.

These craft beer lads are literally turning beer into water with their Clean Water Lager. The label donates 100 per cent of its profits to clean water charities around the world.

Following the craft beer trend, the product —hich is developed with BrewDog —s a great example of re-positioning.

Craft beer has already shifted the traditional image of drinkers away from that of boozed-up blokes with their ales and lagers. But BrewGooder has gone one step further by marketing itself as a social enterprise alternative to commercial beers.

And last but not least, smoking is responsible for one in four of all deaths in Scotland and a bill of some £00m a year for the NHS.

The tobacco industry and many smokers have long railed against moves designed to force them to stub out their habit.

So it was certainly a bit of a surprise when industry giant Philip Morris International launched a new nonburning cigarette alternative in the UK last week.

The Marlboro producer's iQos device uses real tobacco, but it is heated rather than burned.

The company insists it is healthier than traditional products, although there has been some scepticism about just how much safer it is.

Make of it what you will, they spent an eye-watering £.4billion on it, so whilst it might just be a very expensive PR stunt, it does show the brand is recognising a need to adapt to healthier consumer demands and not be a drag on our economy.

It's great to see these companies responding to demand and providing more responsible options —nd why not? It's a true win-win for health and business.

headline: Crowdended
author: RHODRI PHILLIPS
text:
THE FTSE 100 ROSE 22.66 TO 6954.21 CITY Sun EDITED BY RHODRI PHILLIPS Tel: 020 7782 4050 e-mail: rhodri.phillips@the-sun.co.uk BRENT CRUDE $54.21 CLAMPDOWN ON 'RISKY' ONLINE FUNDING SCHEMES

THE £.5billion crowdfunding boom is facing a clampdown amid fears about the investments it offers.

Crowdfunders have helped grow some of Britain's most promising businesses while banks have been reluctant to lend.

The schemes have raised £billion so far in 2016, with small investors putting up cash in return for a stake.

Successes include brewers CHAPEL DOWN and BREWDOG, challenger bank MONZO and estate agent EMOOV - but one in five investments fail. City watchdog the FINANCIAL CONDUCT AUTHORITY said yesterday it wants to apply "mortgage-lending" standards.

The FCA found it is difficult for investors to compare platforms with each other to assess risks and returns.

The watchdog also said investments do not always meet requirements to be "clear, fair and not misleading". And it is concerned that there are inadequate provisions when a firm goes bust. FCA boss Andrew Bailey said: "We believe it is necessary to strengthen investor protection in areas."

In 2014 the FCA limited the amount an investor can spend on a crowdfunded company.

headline: LEND-TO-SAVE FIRMS FACE A CRACKDOWN
text:
THE Wild-West crowdfunding industry is facing a crackdown as concerns mount over risks for investors.

Crowdfunders - dubbed lend-to-save firms - allow consumers to pool their money and give it to businesses as a loan or in exchange for shares.

It has spawned some high-profile successes, including craft beer firm Brewdog, which has sold equity to more than 46,000 supporters to fund rapid expansion.

But the industry has also been heavily criticised for lacking transparency and promising sky-high returns it cannot deliver.

There have been failures where firms went bust and were accused of misleading investors.

Now the Financial Conduct Authority has pledged to give investors better protection.

Yesterday, the watchdog said savers often did not fully understand the risks and struggled to compare different crowdfunding platforms.

FCA chief executive Andrew Bailey said: 'Based on our findings to date, we believe it is necessary to strengthen investor protection in a number of areas.'

The watchdog wants to make sure investors are treated fairly and given enough information.

After taking over at the FCA, Bailey told MPs he was 'pretty worried' about the peer-to-peer lending industry, which crowdfunds loans.

©aily Mail

headline: FANS OF BREWER HOP TO IT
author: RHODRI PHILLIPS
text:
CITY Sun THE FTSE 100 ROSE 5.52 TO 7017.16 EDITED BY RHODRI PHILLIPS Tel: 020 7782 4050 e-mail: rhodri.phillips@the-sun.co.uk £ BUYS €.19, US$1.24. BRENT CRUDE $55.00

BEER maker BREWDOG has pulled in £.2million in its latest crowdfunding round which still has nearly four weeks to go.

Investors pouring in £00 each were yesterday told by the self-styled punk brewer its turnover for 2016 will hit £2million —p 80 per cent year on year.

James Watt, who founded the Aberdeenshire firm with pal Martin Dickie, said: "Our mission is to make other people as passionate about great craft beer as we are.

"We have smashed record after record with the number of people we're reaching, many of whom have joined the ranks of our crowdfunded beer revolution.

"The money raised will accelerate our growth over 2017, in order to meet the crazy demand for our beer both in the UK and internationally."

headline: Housebuilder has grand designs for 'mini bond' fundraising
author: James Hurley
text:
enterprise news

A housebuilder run by the Grand Designs presenter Kevin McCloud is returning to crowdfunding with a plan to secure up to £ million via "mini bonds".

Ordinary investors are being offered an interest rate of 8 per cent over up to five years, as HAB Housing looks to raise funds to support its ambitions to be building 600 homes a year by 2020.

It marks the second time that HAB has used alternative finance, after it secured £.9 million from the public by selling shares via equity crowdfunding in 2013.

"Small housebuilders do not have large cash cushions and this is an incredibly cash-hungry business," Mr McCloud said.

The mini bonds will finance HAB Land, a separate company that will provide funds for various HAB Housing projects, including land acquisition and construction costs. The minimum investment is £,000, with a minimum two-year term.

While the bonds offer an apparently attractive rate of return, they are unsecured. If the company went bust or suffered financial difficulties, the investment could be at risk. The bonds are also illiquid, meaning that investors cannot trade them, so their money is locked away for the term of the mini bond.

Mr McCloud said: "Maybe minibonds is a bad term for this type of investment, as they are not really [conventional] bonds. We are offering these only to seasoned and mature investors."

Mr McCloud said that small housebuilders would be key to the government's ambition of more homes being constructed. While he expected there to be volatility in the housing market related to Brexit, he added: "House prices will continue going up in the long term until we [increase] the supply. No large housebuilder has an interest in doing that. So we need an army of small housebuilders."

Mini-bonds, whereby private companies turn to their customers and other retail investors for a loan offering a fixed income, have become popular in recent years, with companies including the Eden Project, the Cornish visitor attraction, and Brewdog, the craft brewer, using them to raise finance. However, there has been a decline in their use recently. In the year to November 2016, there was a 62 per cent fall in the amount of money raised via mini bonds on Crowdcube, the leading marketplace.

The HAB Housing mini-bond is listed on Code Investing, an investment marketplace. HAB Housing has developments in Swindon, Stroud and Oxford.

headline: UK investors thirsty for  BrewDog , but US may prove a tougher sell
author: Aime Williams
text:
BrewDog, Scotland’ largest craft brewery, closed the year by confirming its place as the most successful crowdfunded business in the UK —ut its expansion to the US is less assured.

The brewer, which has nurtured a maverick image with its “quity for Punks”hare offering and eye-catching promotional stunts, recently sold a £0m unsecured bond to UK investors for an annual rate of 7.5 per cent for four years.

Over seven years, the company has raised £5.3m from UK retail investors, mostly by selling equity. This puts them well ahead of Camden Town Brewery, which also raised cash from “he crowd”o the tune of £.8m before being bought by brewer AB InBev for an undisclosed amount.

Analysts have previously questioned the high valuations achieved by the BrewDog, pointing out that retail investors are unlikely to make much profit on sale if shares are bought for a high price to begin with.

The so-called “ini-bond”— non-transferable, illiquid instrument —s only the fledgling brewer’ second debt issuance to retail investors.

A company’ valuation has a direct impact on the potential return of an equity investment, but the coupon payment on a debt investment is an expression of default risk.

Whether investors want to buy the debt or not ultimately depends on “n how bullish [they] are about BrewDog’ international expansion,”aid Pedro Madeira, an analyst at alternative finance specialist Beahurst.

“or retail investors, 7.5 per cent interest is a good deal if BrewDog do pay back, but the risk is really hard to measure,”aid Mr Madeira.

While BrewDog says it sees “aking people on a journey”s part of its motivation for offering the new debt instrument, one analyst questioned why the company would borrow from retail investors when it could just borrow from a bank.

“t’ much cheaper to [raise debt] from retail investors,”aid Goncalo de Vasconcelos, chief executive of online equity investment platform Syndicate Room. “’ confident that if they went to a bank they would have to pay a lot more to borrow.”

According to accounts filed in Companies House, BrewDog already has nearly £0m of debt from HSBC, secured against its assets. This security means the bank’ debt is less risky than the unsecured debt offered to retail investors.

Neil Simpson, BrewDog’ finance director, said the company’ bank debt package is cheaper than the 7.5 per cent rate he can offer to retail investors —ut he adds that banks “end under certain criteria”

“n another three to six months they may be amenable to give us more finance,”aid Mr Simpson, who said that he did not approach HSBC —r any bank —or finance before BrewDog’ recent retail bond offering.

Much of the money from BrewDog’ bond will go to financing the company’ operations in the US, where it has begun work on a new brewery and taproom in Columbus, Ohio. Some of the cash will help with the final costs of this new £0m site, which boasts 250 car parking spaces and a restaurant.

BrewDog has ambitions for another site in Columbus, as well as more bars in the UK. It shortly plans to open a new cellar brewery in Scotland.

Whether its bonds are good investments or not depends on whether BrewDog can crack the US market and remain solvent enough to pay back all of its debt. BrewDog’ inaugural US equity crowdfunding campaign has been considerably less successful than its UK efforts, suggesting less interest in the brand across the Atlantic. The company raised $3m rather than an expected $50m; in previous UK equity crowdfunding initiatives the company has drummed up £m in a little over three weeks.

Mr Simpson says this is because BrewDog has not yet developed the kind of brand recognition in the US that it has in the UK —nd points out that BrewDog’ first UK fundraising push was for £69,000, significantly less than its recent US effort.

But BrewDog could still have a tough time ahead. According to research from the Brewers Association, in 2015 a new craft brewery opened every 16 hours on average in the US.

“he US market is a good 20 years more developed than the UK market,”ays Mr Simpson. “here is far more competition and far more good guys, but if we’e going to be a global player we need to crack that market.”

Mr de Vasconcelos of Syndicate Room said he saw no reason for investors to be put off the bond as long as they understood the risks fully. However, he warned prospective investors against confusing “iking beer”ith “nderstanding beer manufacturing”

headline: Brewer fights council over land for £m expansion
author: Marc Horne
text:
Scotland's largest craft brewer is locked in a battle with planning officials and has accused them of thwarting its plans for expansion.

Since it was founded in 2007 Brew-Dog has grown from being a two-man operation in the northeast of the country to a global business selling tens of millions of bottles of its heavily hopped ales.

The company now wants to build a top-quality hotel, restaurant, conference centre and distillery next to its brewery in Ellon, creating 80 jobs, but it has accused Aberdeenshire council of being "deluded" and of undermining plans for the £ million expansion.

Yesterday it said that it was looking to take the development elsewhere.

The authority has accused the brewer —nown for its publicity stunts and self-styled punk persona —f attempting to weaken the council's position and breaking confidentiality.

The company, founded by James Watt and Martin Dickie, issued a statement saying: "We want to build a world-class hotel, outstanding restaurant, conference centre and bespoke distillery just behind our brewery in Ellon.

"This project would see us invest £ million into the local area and create over 80 new local jobs.

"However, the project cannot go ahead because the council are refusing to sell us the land at fair market value. Indeed, they are insisting on charging over 60 times fair market value for the land we need to make this exciting local project happen."

The company, which last month announced that its projected turnover for the 2016 fiscal year was £0 million, said that it had the land independently valued by DM Hall, the surveying firm, at £,000 an acre.

The statement added: "We submitted our plans to the council for the distillery and hotel and they demanded £00,000 per acre from us. Over 60 times what an independent surveyor valued the land at.

"We want to invest in our local area and create muchneeded jobs yet the council insist on making it impossible for us to do so."

The company said that it was perverse for the council to turn down investment at a time when the North Sea oil downturn meant that unemployment in the area was rising.

"Regrettably we will now look to construct the distillery, hotel and restaurant somewhere else," it added. "We will look for land in a council area with a pro-business approach, rather than anti-investment and anti-job creation we find locally."

Hamish Partridge, an independent councillor, expressed frustration at the impasse: "Disgusting behaviour by Aberdeenshire council. I have requested an investigation."

Jim Savege, the council's chief executive, said: "There are ongoing protracted negotiations with BrewDog and this announcement appears to be intended to weaken the council's position.

"We're disappointed that the company has sought to break confidentiality during what we regarded as live and ongoing discussions to achieve an agreement which is fair to both parties and which protects the interests of the local taxpayer, as well as creating opportunities for residents.

"We cannot sell land vastly below market value and the figure BrewDog has been asked to meet is what the land is worth right now.

"Their suggested land value is more representative of agricultural value than a site which has permission for an alternative use."

It is not the first time that Aberdeenshire council has been involved in a planning controversy.

In 2008 councillors voted to reject Donald Trump's plans to create a golf resort at the Menie Estate, but the decision was later overruled after the Scottish government intervened.

Referring to the incident, one Twitter user wrote to Brew-Dog: "You should ask President Trump how to deal with the council."

'They are insisting on charging over 60 times fair market value'

headline: Clarkson parking is driving me up the wall
text:
Carol Midgley Notebook

If bitching over other people's parking makes you a grumpy old woman then I plead guilty. Selfish parkers incense me to an absurd degree given the real woes of the world and yet, Ms Hyundai Hatchback, what makes you so special that while everyone else uses one space in the multistorey you flabbily bestride two? At a retail park I considered, yet again, leaving an angry note on the windscreen of a BMW slewed across two spaces but remembered this would make me the sort of person who leaves angry notes on cars (next stage: shaking fists at youths). I've long said that hoggish parkers are multiplying and it's a vehicular manifestation of the me-first age. Now comes proof.

"Clarkson parking" (so-called because Jeremy Clarkson jokingly tweeted a photo of an atrociously parked Golf) is on the rise, say the AA and the British Parking Association. It's not just because cars are getting bigger so spaces seem smaller. People deliberately park over two spaces "to protect their car from bad parkers". Don't you thrill to the irony? One man shamed on the Facebook page "Parking Like a T**t" for leaving his Vauxhall Astra GTR in this way shrugged and said he did so because there were too many "clowns" out there. Another reason, says the AA, is obesity: people need extra room to heave themselves out of the door.

I see that, but the first lot puzzle me. If by "Clarkson parking" you hope to safeguard your precious car your logic is skewed. You're asking to be keyed.

Dull spirits Some of my friends used to swear by psychics, and though I'd be scathing I was secretly gripped when they were told something spookily accurate (though I'd always say it was a lucky guess). Anthea Turner this week reminded me of something else: how prosaic they can be. The TV presenter consulted psychics and mediums who warned that her husband was cheating on her (again, pretty safe punt?) and also claimed to be in touch with a dead relative. One message from beyond the grave was "Your underwear drawer is a mess".

I've noticed this about the dead: they don't seem to have much of a life. When friends have told me of alleged communiqué from the spirit world what's struck me is their mundanity. The car's oil needs changing; there's a damp patch in the kitchen. Is this what the afterlife is like: fretting over someone's plastering? Being dead may be even duller than we feared.

Animal alert If someone is convicted of cruelty to animals why don't their names go on an abusers' register? It makes perfect sense even if you don't give a damn about animals. This month's BBC Wildlife magazine says the system has been adopted in New York because of the proven link between animal cruelty and violence against humans. Apparently up to 71 per cent of battered women say their pets had been killed or threatened by their abusers. Ian Brady, Jeffrey Dahmer and Ted Bundy are just some of the killers who practised their early sadism on animals. It's like a klaxon warning.

As the article says, their offence is not "out of character", as many argue, but part of their character. Yet the sentence for the gravest case of animal cruelty is six months and a fine. Many barely even get a wrist slap. An animal abusers' register is a great idea but don't hold your breath.

Dry January For anyone slogging through Dry January (which I'm not but I am cutting down) I offer a crutch. My usual placebo, Becks Blue, has served me well but I'm utterly sick of it. Step forward Nanny State, a "hoppy ale" by Brewdog that is easily the best alcohol-free product (well, 0.5 per cent) I've ever had. It looks treacly, tastes malty and citrusy and, most crucially, like a grown-ups' drink. It could be the real thing. God, that name though. I get the joke but it's a hell of a buzz kill.

@carolmidgley

headline: Craft brewer is big enough now to buy its own round Alex Massie
text:
Imagine if a multinational corporation —P, say, or Diageo —hreatened to abandon a multimillion-pound investment in Scotland unless local councillors agreed that it could purchase the land needed for its new facilities at only £,000 an acre. I have a hunch that public opinion would reckon that the company concerned was "at it". The threat to take its business elsewhere would be seen as a sorry, if all too typical, example of corporate blackmail.

Such is the situation in which BrewDog, the company that in only a decade has become Britain's largest craft brewer, now finds itself. The company wants to build a hotel, restaurant, conference centre and distillery on a 2.5-acre site next to its premises in Ellon. BrewDog says the £ million project would create 80 jobs but that those jobs would go elsewhere unless Aberdeenshire council sells Brewdog the land it needs at a heavily discounted rate.

The company believes that the site is worth £,000 an acre —oughly what an acre of grazing land costs in Aberdeenshire —hile the council's valuation estimates that the site, which is earmarked for expanding a local cemetery, is worth £00,000 an acre if converted to business use.

This, the brewer says, is deluded.

BrewDog fancies itself as a scrappy up-and-coming business; the little guy punching above his weight in a world dominated by corporate behemoths. How ironic, then, that it is adopting the tactics long favoured by just the kind of companies against which BrewDog has defined itself.

James Watt, one of BrewDog's founders, has said that the company "will never sell out to some monolithic drinks giant". Other brewers have customers; BrewDog has followers. That has helped it to grow. In 2015 it reported gross profits of £7 million on sales of £4.7 million. Its latest round of crowdfunding valued the company at £00 million. That scrappy underdog is a big dog now.

But there is no good reason to offer sweetheart deals to corporate interests —ven when those interests are local champions.

headline: Five of the best: Snack start-ups
author: Will Hawkes
text:
Karkli ( chomponkarkli.com )

Made primarily from lentils and rice flour, these deep-fried tubular snacks deliver a spicy hit, from the gentle cumin-led heat of the original version to the appropriately named Fiery Ghost Naga. The best of the lot might be those made with coriander, which are a perfect accompaniment to a Belgian witbier. Founder and beer-lover Kumar Kolar has collaborated with breweries on special editions, like the ginger-and-lemon snack he produced to complement Weird Beard’ Mariana Trench pale ale.

Cleaver and Keg ( cleaverandkeg.co.uk )

Dan Searle, the publican-turned-snack entrepreneur behind Cleaver and Keg, wanted something more exciting than a bag of crisps to sate his punters’unger: these meat snacks are the result. Of the four varieties, the smoky, spicy Chorizo Cuts and hot, peppery Strips O’eef are particularly good. The products’ebt to the craft-beer revolution is acknowledged in the name and on the packaging, which offers beer-matching tips (such as a Rye IPA or Amber Ale for Chorizo Cuts).

Smo-Fo Smoked Snacks ( twitter.com/smofosmokedfood )

Yorkshire butcher Simon Barnes had planned to use his new smokehouse for bacon but a customer’ demand for smoked snacks set him on the road to creating Smo-Fo. You’l find his products (hickory-smoked scratchings, chilli-dusted smoked roast corn and crispy smoked broad beans) in pubs and bars all over the North (including the Brewdog chain).

Soffles Pitta Chips ( soffles.com )

If you’e been to a beer event in London in the past year, chances are you’e spotted the pale-blue Vespa 400 MPV from which founder Sophie Harvey and her colleagues sell their oven-baked pitta chips. Harvey started making them for pals who wanted a bite before dinner: now you can pick them up not only at beer festivals but also at Waitrose and Harvey Nichols. There are four flavours, of which the best are Chilli & Garlic and Rosemary & Thyme; they’e best served with hummus.

Woza Biltong ( eatwoza.com )

Biltong is as Afrikaner as the Springboks, which is perhaps why it’ not more popular in the UK. This gently spiced version, made with West Country bavette, will surprise those who expect it to be a dry, jaw-aching chore: it’ moist and almost Bresaola-like in its delicacy. Look out for it —nd its venison cousin —t Beavertown and Brew By Numbers’aprooms in London. Like a number of the new breed of pub snacks, it works particularly well with hoppy pale ales.

headline: WHISKEY'S DISTILLERY SEASON
author: ARAN BRAZIL
text:
By ARAN BRAZIL A PINT of beer and a dram of whiskey have made great bedfellows for countless generations of drinkers, brewers and distillers.

Yet despite being very similar in process, equipment and ingredients, very few companies have been involved in producing both beer and spirits on the same premises.

This has changed somewhat in recent years with a number of smaller-scale breweries straddling both disciplines at once, including Cork-based Black's of Kinsale, which produces craft beer and gin. In 2015, meanwhile, Scottish craft producer Brewdog ventured into the hallowed halls of vodka, gin and whisky-making with the Lone Wolf brand. However, for the most part it's still very much 'never the twain'.

All of which makes Guinness's announcement that it is to start making its own whiskey all the more unexpected.

The black stuff's parent company Diageo last week unveiled a bold plan to re-enter the Irish whiskey market with the launch of Roe & Co, named in honour of George Roe, a neighbour of Guinness, whose distillery was located on Thomas Street, next to St James's Gate.

The company is aiming to complete a new distillery by 2019 in a refurbished historic building in the brewery grounds known as the Guinness powerhouse, above.

Before its closure in 1926, the original distillery, located just a stone's throw away from the planned site, was one of the most productive in Ireland, if not the world.

It is now a part of the Digital Hub and remains home to St Patrick's Tower, the distillery's iconic smock windmill - the sole visual reminder of its existence.

The new addition is the third distillery announcement for Dublin's Liberties, a fitting location given it was once famously known as the 'golden triangle' of distilling. Nearby is Teeling Distillery, which opened in 2015 and practically next door to Guinness is Alltech's Pearse Lyons Distillery, which is set to open in the next few months.

Diageo are no strangers to the Irish whiskey market having formally owned Bushmills before selling it in 2014 to Mexican company Jose Cuervo, best known for its tequila. The first release of Roe & Co will be made by sourcing a blend of grain and malt whiskeys from existing distilleries across Ireland and will be available from March onwards.

headline: And they called it, puppy leave ...
author: Marc Horne
text:
Scotland's largest craft brewer has become the first big British company to offer "puppy parental leave".

Since it was founded in Aberdeenshire in 2007 BrewDog has grown from being a two-man operation to a global empire selling tens of millions of bottles of its heavily-hopped ales.

Now the company, known for its publicity stunts and self-styled punk persona, is offering a week's paid holiday to employees who get a dog or puppy. It already encourages staff at its headquarters in Ellon to bring their dogs to work.

A spokesman said: "Having dogs in our offices makes everyone else more chilled and relaxed, but we know only too well that having a new arrival —hether a pup or unsettled rescue dog —an be stressful for human and hound both. Puppy parental leave will support nervy canines and their owners alike in those all-important first few days."

The perk will also be available to its staff in the US, where paid maternity leave is not guaranteed.

The Scottish SPCA praised the initiative but advised employees that taking on a dog should not be done lightly.

Mike Flynn, the animal welfare organisation's chief superintendent, said: "We welcome BrewDog's new puppy parental leave policy. We would advise people to think very carefully before getting a dog as it is a lifelong commitment. You will also need the time required to train a new dog and also to exercise it."

The company is building its US headquarters, and a second brewery, in Ohio. It employs almost 1,000 people globally.

headline: BREWERY'S TOP DOG
author: Amy Jones
text:
Sun employment

INTRODUCING pupternity leave.

Craft beer company BrewDog is set to give all staff an additional week of leave when they bring home a new pooch.

The Scottish brewery was started by two men and a dog back in 2007, and both human co-founders are commonly found accompanied by canines Simcoe and Dr Gonzo.

The Aberdeenshire company encourages all staff to bring their (wellbehaved) pets to the workplace.

Co-founder James Watt said: "It's not easy trying to juggle work and settle a new dog and many members of our crew have four-legged friends at home.

"We always want to raise the bar when it comes to offering our staff the best possible benefits. We care about two things above all else - people and beer.

"We also just really, really like dogs."

headline: Staff get a week's paws for new dog
author: STEVE KENNEDY
text:
CRAFT beer company BrewDog is offering staff a week's paid leave if they get a new pooch.

Workers can claim "pawternity" if they take in a puppy or rescue dog.

James Watt and Martin Dickie founded the firm in 2007 and are often seen with pet dogs Simcoe and Dr Gonzo.

James said: "It's not easy trying to juggle work and settle a new dog into your life. We wanted to take the stress out of the situation."

BrewDog, based in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, has over 50 staff animals in its HQ on work days.

The brewery has 44 bars and 540 staff and is expanding into the US.

James said: "We care about two things above all else: People and beer. We also just really, really like dogs."

headline: PAPPY LEAVE IS VITAL
author: Shaf Rasul
text:
PAPPY LEAVE IS VITAL IT raised a smile when I heard the news that Brewdog has introduced 'Puppy Leave' for employees within the company.

The initiative will mean any new dog owners on its staff will receive a whole paid week off to spend quality time with their puppies.

Great news for any dog lovers, however it throws up another more important question - is two weeks' paid paternity leave for fathers really enough? One week for a puppy, two weeks for a child. Does two weeks give fathers enough time to really bond with their newborns and help share the load of child care? I don't think so.

In a time when there is a real focus on gender equality and equal rights for all - as it should be - why is gender-indifferent leave so lacking in 2017? With less than 10 per cent of men in the UK exceeding the statutory two weeks, stating their fear of being left behind within the workplace, surely it's time to change this mind-set and highlight exactly what every new father will gain instead.

Research has shown fathers who take paternity leave are more likely to feed, dress, bathe and play with their child long after their leave has ended, not to mention being more likely to read to them as toddlers.

Early interaction also has longer-term benefits for a child's learning abilities, with studies showing that paternity leave improved children's performance at secondary school.

We must give every child in Scotland the best start in life and in the name of social progress in the workplace, let's increase paid paternity leave for fathers.

headline: Rise of premium beer in China boosts small brewers
author: Tom Hancock in Shanghai
text:
Ignoring the cheap beer on tap in a dim and smoky Beijing bar, Cheer Qiu reaches into a fridge bursting with craft ales and pulls out a bottle of locally made Panda Brew, flavoured with honey.

“’ interested in brewing culture,”ays Ms Qiu. She is not alone in shunning cheaper beers in favour of more distinct brews. Volume sales in China at Danish brewer Carlsberg fell 6 per cent last year, while Panda says its sales tripled.

Kurt Xia founded Panda Brew in 2013 and is now selling his products in 60 cities across the country and has attracted venture capital investment worth Rmb20m ($2.9m). “onsumers are not satisfied only with lager but want to have something unique and which makes them look cool,”e says.

The two brewers’ontrasting fortunes underline the challenge of China’ more than $70bn-a-year beer market. Total volumes have been falling about 5 per cent annually for the past three years.

The drop has been driven by the rising popularity of wine and other alternatives, as well as a decline in the numbers of working age people who consume most of the cheap lager, which is priced at less than $1 a bottle and accounts for more than 90 per cent of volumes.

At the same time, the overall value of Chinese beer sales has risen slightly as younger and more affluent consumers switch to premium drinks, especially craft beers. Many of these beverages are from abroad —ome consumption of imported beer rose 40 per cent last year, according to consumer research company Kantar Worldpanel, to comprise 3.6 per cent of the market.

That has been a boost for small European brewers. Premium brands have experienced “xponential growth”n recent years, says Antoine Bolly, China marketing manager for Vandergeeten & EG Distriselecta, which distributes Chimay beer and Huyghe Brewery’ flagship Delirium Tremens beer in China.

“he consumer goes more and more for premium instead of value,”e says.

Brand recognition and loyalty for beers such as Chimay, brewed by Trappist monks in Belgium, are rising, Mr Bolly adds, with consumers “sking for the brand in Chinese” However, in a country ruled by the officially atheist Communist party, “ou cannot communicate that it’ made by monks. They refuse to brand it like that.”

Dutch brewer eineken said this month that its sales volumes in China increased slightly last year on the strength of its more expensive offerings. “e are totally super premium with the Heineken brand in China,”ays Jean-Françis van Boxmeer, chairman. “t’ a promise for the future.”

Scottish “unk”rand BrewDog has also expanded in China. “t seems to have a following,”ays Gary Brown, managing director for Topshelf Asia, which distributes the beer in the country. “hey are serious about the market here. There’ only a few big markets that haven’ been tapped.”

Despite the decline in volumes, Carlsberg says sales of its premium Tuborg and 1664 Blanc brands are strong in China. “he gain is not volume but value and premiumisation, and we are well placed for that and in that respect optimistic for China,”ays Cees ’ Hart, chief executive.

But Chinese beer groups appear to be struggling with the shift to premium drinks. Urban sales volumes of Snow, Tsingtao and anjing, the country’ biggest domestic brands, contracted 3, 7 and 8 per cent respectively last year, according to Kantar.

Revenues at China Resources Snow Breweries, the world’ biggest beer group by sales volume, fell 2 per cent to Rmb15bn in the first half of last year. China Resources Beer acquired SABMiller’ 49 per cent stake in Snow for $1.6bn last year.

Yanjing’ 2016 sales were down more than 10 per cent to Rmb6.3bn and profits fell one-quarter compared with the previous year, to Rmb451m. The company is “eavily indebted and the market believes they are looking for a strategic investor to bail them out” writes Gordon Orr of McKinsey.

“owever much they try, their brands are just not premiumising well. Consumers view them as the cheap brew that their parents and grandparents turned to. They want something different,”r Orr adds.

Tsingtao posted an 8 per cent fall in revenues to Rmb14.7bn in its most recent financial report, with profits down 6 per cent. Japan’ Asahi is considering selling its 20 per cent stake in the company.

But the Chinese market is not all smooth sailing for premium brands. A lack of refrigeration in bars and distribution vehicles can spoil the taste, and distributors say “arallel”mports of foreign beer brands that avoid paying customs duties are a big challenge.

“hen Heineken is sold in Europe under promotion some containers are shipped to China. It’ very difficult to control,”ays Heineken’ Mr van Boxmeer.

Ceinwen Michael, a sales manager in China for Vandergeeten, says the problem of parallel imports is extensive: “ll big popular beers are getting brought in through other methods.”

Lax enforcement of rules against unofficial vendors has exacerbated the problem. “e’e given up reporting [parallel imports] to the authorities,”ays Mr Brown of Topshelf Asia.

headline: COMPANIES Top China beer brands hammered as tastes shift; Food & beverage: Drinking culture
author: Tom Hancock in Shanghai
text:
Snow, Tsingtao and Yanjing sales volumes fall while imports and craft brews gain ground

Ignoring the cheap beer on tap in a smoky Beijing bar, Cheer Qiu reaches into a fridge bursting with craft ales and pulls out a bottle of locally made Panda Brew, flavoured with honey. "I'm interested in brewing culture," Ms Qiu says.

She is not alone in shunning cheaper beers for more distinct brews. Volume sales in China at Carlsberg, the Danish brewer, fell 6 per cent last year while Panda says its sales trebled.

Kurt Xia founded Panda in 2013 and is selling his products in 60 cities across the country. He has drawn venture capital investment of Rmb20m ($2.9m). "Consumers are not only satisfied with lager but want to have something unique and which makes them look cool," he says.

The brewers' contrasting fortunes underline the challenge of China's more than $70bn-a-year beer market. Volumes have been falling about 5 per cent annually for the past three years.

The drop has been driven by the rising popularity of wine and other alternatives, and a decline in the working-age population that consumes most of the cheap lager, which is priced at less than $1 per bottle and accounts for more than 90 per cent of volumes.

At the same time, the value of Chinese beer sales has risen slightly as younger and more affluent consumers switch to premium drinks, especially craft beers .

Many of these are from abroad. Home consumption of imported beer rose 40 per cent last year, according to Kantar Worldpanel, to comprise 3.6 per cent of the market.

That has been a boost for small European brewers.

Premium brands have experienced rapid growth, says Antoine Bolly , China marketing manager for Vandergeeten & EG DistriSelecta, which distributes Chimay beer and Huyghe Brewery's flagship Delirium Tremens beer in China. "The consumer goes more and more for premium instead of value."

Brand recognition and loyalty for beers such as Chimay, brewed by Trappist monks in Belgium, are rising, Mr Bolly says, with consumers "asking for the brand in Chinese".

But in a country ruled by the atheist Communist party, "you can't communicate that it's made by monks. They refuse to brand it like that".

Scottish "punk" brand BrewDog has also expanded in China. "It seems to have a following," says Gary Brown , managing director for Topshelf Asia, which distributes the beer. "They are serious about the market here. There's only a few big markets that haven't been tapped."

But China beer groups appear to be struggling with the shift to premium drinks. Urban sales volumes of Snow, Tsingtao and Yanjing, the biggest domestic brands, contracted 3, 7 and 8 per cent respectively last year, according to Kantar.

Revenues at China Resources Snow Breweries - the world's biggest beer group by sales volume - fell 2 per cent to Rmb15bn in the first half of last year. China Resources Beer acquired SABMiller's 49 per cent stake in Snow for $1.6bn last year.

Yanjing's 2016 sales were down more than 10 per cent to Rmb6.3bn and profits fell one-quarter compared with the previous year to Rmb451m.

The group is "heavily indebted, and the market believes they are looking for a strategic investor to bail them out," Gordon Orr of McKinsey writes. "However much they try, their brands aren't premiumising well. Consumers view them as the cheap brew that their parents and grandparents turned to. They want something different."

Tsingtao posted an 8 per cent fall in revenues to Rmb14.7bn in its most recent report, with profits down 6 per cent. Japan's Asahi is considering selling its 20 per cent stake.

But the China market is not all smooth sailing for premium brands. A lack of refrigeration in bars and distribution vehicles can spoil the taste, and distributors say "parallel" imports of foreign beer brands that avoid paying customs duties are a challenge.

"When Heineken is sold in Europe under promotion, some containers are shipped to China - it's very difficult to control," says Mr van Boxmeer says.

Ceinwen Michael, a sales manager in China for Vandergeeten, says the problem of parallel imports is extensive: "All big popular beers are getting bought in through other methods."

Lax enforcement of rules against unofficial vendors has exacerbated the problem. "We've given up reporting [parallel imports] to the authorities," Mr Brown says.

headline: ROOM WITH A BREW
author: RHODRI PHILLIPS
text:
CITY Sun THE FTSE 100 FELL 8.09 TO 7374.26 EDITED BY RHODRI PHILLIPS Tel: 020 7782 4050 e-mail: rhodri.phillips@the-sun.co.uk Twitter: @rhodri76 BRENT CRUDE $55.62

A brewer is planning to build a craft beer hotel in the US.

BREWDOG said its £.9million DogHouse in Columbus, Ohio, is aimed at "ale geeks".

Co-founder James Watt, left, said rooms will have beer taps and there will be fridges stocked with craft beer in the shower. The Aberdeenshire-based company is using crowdfunding to get the project off the ground.

The move comes after Brewdog failed to reach an agreement with Aberdeenshire Council to build a hotel next to its existing brewery in Ellon.

headline: It's not just froth, craft breweries say as launches rise by 55%
author: Callum Jones
text:
small business news

The number of brewery launches leapt by 55 per cent in Britain last year. A record 520 sites started operating in 2016, up from 336 the previous year.

With ten openings each week, budding entrepreneurs appear to have been spurred on by a string of success stories from start-up brands that have become a hit with consumers.

UHY Hacker Young, which conducted research into the craft beer boom, claims that the dominant players in the alcohol industry have become concerned by the slow progress of their established brands, while the popularity of craft beers continues to rise.

James Simmonds, a partner at the accountancy firm, said that its analysis showed how far the industry had come over the past decade.

The Camden Town Brewery, which produces brands including Hells, Pale Ale and Pils, was snapped up by AB InBev for £5 million in 2015, having set up shop only five years beforehand. SAB Miller bought the Meantime Brewery, which was founded in Greenwich, southeast London, in 2000, for a reported £20 million in 2015, only to sell it to Asahi, of Japan, last year.

James Watt and Martin Dickie, who set up Brewdog in 2007, still own the Scottish company, which now owns dozens of bars across the UK and overseas.

Brewdog opened a brewery in Ohio in the United States to complement its main site in Ellon, outside Aberdeen, last year.

"Craft beer has proved it is no flash in a pan," Mr Simmonds said. "Both supermarkets and pubs realise that they are going to lose sales if they do not make way for some craft brands "So far the pricing discipline of cult beer brands has held steady. They have been largely untouched by the supermarket wars that have savaged margins elsewhere in the food and drinks sector.

"Recent high-profile takeovers by larger breweries have given entrepreneurs and their backers proof of what can be achieved and proper benchmarks of how craft breweries can be valued. It is a long way from the cottage industry of a decade ago."

While craft beers have a share of about 8 per cent of the total beer market in the UK, they control more than a fifth of the market in the US.

headline: Brewdog ’ investors discover dilution
author: Kadhim Shubber
text:
Remember Brewdog? They’e the independent Scottish beer business who run obnoxious advertising campaigns , have a penchant for crowdfunding and have promised to never sell the company “o a monolithic purveyor of industrial beer”

Well, at the end of this month, Brewdog is holding a general meeting to approve several important changes to its articles of association, which it says will pave the way for an injection of cash from an unnamed investor.

Those resolutions include the temporary removal of pre-emption rights, so current shareholders can’ stop their stakes being diluted by the fundraising, and, more exciting still, the creation of generous preferred shares that give the new investor a ton of downside protection.

On a liquidation, return of capital, a sale of the Company or its business or a listing of the Company, they are entitled to receive the higher of (i) a preferred return preference of 18 per cent per annum compounding annually and (ii) such amount as would be applied to the holders of the C shares if they ranked pari passu with the A Ordinary Shares and B Ordinary Shares.

According to a letter to shareholders on Monday , Brewdog could sell up to 30 per cent of the company to the investor. Certain “ignificant shareholders”ill be able to sell up 20 per cent of their holdings too.

Other, insignificant shareholders will just have to hold on and hope.

headline: Professional investors join the crowdfunding party
author: Aime Williams
text:
Crowdfunding has traditionally offered ordinary investors the chance to buy shares in unlisted companies —ut now venture capital funds are muscling in.

Professional investors, including VC funds and corporate finance houses, provided equity to about 14 per cent of start-ups backed on crowdfunding websites in 2016, more than double compared with two years earlier, according to research company Beauhurst.

In separate figures, Crowdcube, the largest crowdfunding platform by deal volume, said the number of deals involving institutional investors alongside retail investors had quadrupled in the past two years.

At least one major crowdfunding platform, Syndicate Room, only features deals that have won the backing of professional investors. Chief executive Goncalo de Vasconcelos said having a professional investor onboard was a “o brainer”or investors.

“rofessional investors carry out their own due diligence and negotiate the investment terms to the extent they are happy to invest their own money,”aid Mr Vasconcelos. “hey also add a huge amount of value post-funding round with their sector expertise and contacts.”

Syndicate Room claims that 97 per cent of the companies funded via its website are still trading, which it attributes to its model of allowing professionals to lead funding rounds.

The growing institutional interest in equity crowdfunding follows the trend seen in the peer-to-peer lending space, where institutional lenders have moved in to buy up debt as the lending market has matured.

Robert Wardrop, executive director for the Cambridge Centre for Alternative Finance, said that in the case of equity crowdfunding, fund managers were beginning to realise that some deals could “enefit from investor evangelism” with returns from investing in some companies potentially improved by the enthusiasm of the so-called crowd.

Many companies raising funds have looked to leverage retail investors’nthusiasm for a particular product by offering perks.

Scottish brewer BrewDog, which has raised £5.3m from UK retail investors, offers “pic discounts”n its bars and a lifetime discount in its shop. Chilango, a restaurant chain, similarly offers investors discounts on burritos.

Pedro Madeira, head of research at Beauhurst, said the growing size and sophistication of companies turning to crowdfunding was partly responsible for attracting professional investors.

According to Beauhurst’ data set, a shift is under way with more companies registering as “rowth stage” rather than smaller “eed stage”usinesses.

“Growth] stage companies are the preferred hunting grounds for institutional investors such as venture capital funds,”aid Mr Madeira. “t’ therefore no surprise that institutional investors are now keener for a piece of the action.”

Mr Wardrop added that professional investors were also moving towards crowdfunding as part of the broader context of “igitalisation” “t’ not surprising,”e said.

“e’e dealing with the broad context of the digitalisation of the economy, the digitalisation of the financial system, and you’e got to expect the channels will change in a digital environment.”

Equity crowdfunding has been the subject of a market review by the Financial Conduct Authority, along with peer-to-peer lending.

In February 2015, the FCA said that crowdfunders were giving a “isleading or unrealistically optimistic impression of the investment”hile attracting retail customers with little experience of investing.

The FCA had previously warned investors: “t is very likely you will lose all your money”n equity crowdfunding, which involves taking stakes in unlisted companies that are often in their early stages.

A recent study in the UK by AltFi Data and law firm Nabarro found that one in five companies that raised money on equity crowdfunding platforms between 2011 and 2013 had gone bankrupt. Last year, claims management group Rebus was the iggest failure to date of a UK crowdfunded company.

headline: Money Mail HOW TO DRINK IN PROFITS FROM THE CRAFT BEER BOOM
author: PAUL THOMAS
text:
ALE drinkers were once ridiculed for wearing sandals and drinking warm, lifeless beers.

But not anymore.

Sales of craft beers are booming —nd not just among men in their 20s and 30s and hipsters, but women and pub regulars too.

Greene King, one of Britain's biggest breweries, has even set up a craft beer academy where young apprentices can create ales with distinctive tastes and colourful labels.

Craft beer is now so popular that 130 breweries are launched each year to meet demand.

There are currently 1,540 in Britain, or one for every 40,000 of us —ore per head than in any other country.

Craft beer —ypically beer made in a traditional or non-mechanised way by a small brewery —ccounts for around 5 pc of sales in our pubs and restaurants. And experts say the figure will top 13 pc within three years.

Most popular among younger drinkers tend to be smaller breweries such as BrewDog, based in Ellon, Scotland, and Camden Town Brewery, in London.

But now larger brewers are getting in on the act to boost their profits —nd that's where investors can cash in. Greene King's Craft cademy was created to expand the company's range beyond its well-known IPA and Old Speckled Hen ales.

One of the FTSE 250-listed brewer's newest beers is the limited edition Jungle Punch, a double IPA created by two academy apprentices. It went down a storm at London's Craft Beer Rising festival last month. The brewer, whose share price has risen by more than a third in five years, says craft beers helped to boost its share of the ale market to 10.5 pc last year.

On top of this it achieved a record turnover of £96.9 million last year.

For prospective investors, Darius McDermott, f Chelsea Financial Services, tips nicorn Outstanding British Companies.

The fund invests £.30 of every £00 in the Suffolk-based brewer.

Chris Hutchinson, the fund's manager, says: 'I grew up in Greene King country and admire its commitment to quality. When I buy a pint of IPA, I know it'll be reliable, familiar and good to drink.

'It's a business that's fiercely proud of its heritage and one that has benefited from the shift away from fizzy, tasteless, mass-produced lagers to beers brewed using more traditional methods.'

Mr Hutchinson has turned £0,000 into £6,820 over the past five years.

Marston's is one of Britain's largest pub chains, running 1,559 sites across the country. However, the Wolverhampton-based firm also brews around 4.3 million pints of beer a week at five breweries.

This includes its own brand brewery, which makes the popular amber ale Pedigree.

Marston's also owns craft breweries Ringwood and Wychwood, which brew Razorback and Hobgoblin —avourites among ale lovers.

The pubs also have exclusive UK rights to Shipyard, one of the most popular craft beer imports from the United States.

The firm's sales of craft beers have jumped by a quarter over the past two years to more than 213,000. Over the past five years its share price has risen by 31 pc.

Laith Khalaf, of stockbroker Hargreaves Lansdown, tips Marlborough Multi Cap Income, which invests £.11 in every £00 in Marston's. The fund has turned £0,000 into £8,660 in five years.

Ben Yearsley, of adviser Wealth Club, says Threadneedle UK Equity Income is another fund backing the craft beer boom. It turned £0,000 into £7,390 in the past five years.

The fund invests in J D Wetherspoon, the FTSE 250 pub chain with more than 900 premises, whose share price has risen by more than 130 pc over the past five years. The firm has built its reputation offering cut-price food and booze to punters —ut it's also leading the way in Britain's craft beer boom.

It started stocking craft ales four years ago after sending a small group of staff members to the U.S. to observe the craft beer revolution unfolding there.

Now the Watford-based pub chain stocks beers from as far away as Maine and Virginia, to local beers such as Green Devil IPA, made by award-wining Peterborough-based brewery Oakham Ales.

One of its pubs, The Counting House in Glasgow, stocks more than 100 types of craft beer in bottles, cans and on tap.

Guinness, owned by FTSE 100 drinks giant Diageo, and famous worldwide for its creamy, dark Irish stout, has also hopped on the back of the craft beer craze.

In 2014, Guinness launched The Brewers Project, a series of new beers based on 200-year-old recipes from the diaries of former brewers at its premises in St James's Gate, Dublin. The first of these were the smooth Dublin Porter, the hoppy West Indies Porter, Guinness Golden Ale, and craft lager Hop House 13.

Guinness's sales grew 2 pc in the UK last year. Diageo puts this down to the success of Hop House 13.

Jason Hollands, of fund broker Tilney, tips Evenlode Income, which invests a hefty £.36 in every £00 in Diageo. It has turned £0,000 into £9,500 in the past five years.

headline: FT REPORT - TOMORROW'S GLOBAL BUSINESS: RAISING CAPITAL Security concerns take fizz out of frothy 'mini-bond' market
author: Aime Williams
text:
Financing Crowdfunding debt is an attractive route for start-ups, but the rewards for investors are less clear, reports Aime Williams

Chilango, the Mexican-themed restaurant chain, decided to appeal directly to its customers when it needed cash to expand. But unlike most ambitious crowdfunded companies, Chilango decided to issue debt instead of equity.

The four-year "burrito bond", as it became known, was the first crowdfunded debt finance instrument in the UK when it launched in 2014. Investors, who were drawn to its 8 per cent yield, committed just over £m.

Several other organisations soon launched similar bonds, including BrewDog, a craft ale maker, and Surrey County Cricket Club, which wanted a new stand. These debt vehicles - which are generally unsecured and illiquid - have been dubbed "mini-bonds". Chilango's use of a crowdfunding website was the latest innovation in a market that has attracted small numbers of issuers for the best part of a decade .

The pros for investors are obvious. In an era of rock-bottom yields and scarce income opportunities, the bonds carry "juicy interest rates, much better than you'd get elsewhere," says Pedro Madeira, head of research at data provider Beauhurst.

But the structure is attracting criticism as its drawbacks become more obvious. The investment case, for one thing, is weakening. Analysts argue that the bonds - which are often issued by young, fast-growing companies - carry equity-like downside with bond-like upside. In other words, investors are not paid enough money for the risk they are taking.

This dynamic is "not so clever" for bondholders, says Claire Madden, partner at private equity firm Connection Capital. "Very often, the ability for the company to pay back the capital is highly dependent on growth in trading and increased cash generation," she says.

Rupert Taylor, chief executive of AltFi Data, an alternative finance research provider, believes young companies should be letting investors buy shares rather than issuing debt. "If risks of failure are high then one would at least hope to be compensated for that risk with the promise of significant capital gains," he says.

There are also questions over whether investors understand their place in the pecking order. Holders of unsecured instruments are behind secured lenders in the queue when it comes to divvying up assets if the company defaults.

As the risks of investing in mini-bonds have become better understood there has been a sharp decline in issuance through crowdfunding websites. Crowdcube, one of two crowdfund websites offering mini-bonds, listed only two bonds in 2016, having offered seven in 2015, according to AltFi.

But there has also been a steady increase in the size of bonds offered. The single bond Crowdcube offered in the second half of 2016 aimed to attract £3m of financing, more than three times the size of its second largest issuance to date.

Darren Westlake, chief executive of Crowdcube, says the company made a decision 18 months ago to "be more selective" about the bonds it offers. "Unlike with equity, we do a huge amount of due diligence to make sure [the issuer] can repay," he says.

"We're at pains to make clear that equity is highly risky, there's a good chance you'll invest in a company that will go bust," he says. "People expect equity to be risky, but they don't really expect debt to be risky."

Investors in a mini-bond issued by Secured Energy Bonds, an unregulated company that went into administration in January 2015, know the risks all too well. The 900 investors, who collectively lost more than £m, were offered the bond by Independent Portfolio Managers, an advisory company regulated by the UK's Financial Conduct Authority.

It is unclear whether any regulator can look at the Secured Energy Bonds case. Investors were told by the UK's Financial Ombudsman Service, the body that deals with investor complaints against regulated financial companies, that the collapsed bond did not fall within its remit. The FOS later changed its mind and agreed to consider the case following appeals and the threat of legal action from investors - but it has not yet made its final decision.

The case highlights the lack of regulatory oversight of mini-bonds, despite the involvement of FCA-regulated advisers that recommend them.

Despite the drop in interest for mini-bonds, an illiquid and high-yield cousin has grown in popularity. Two debt crowdfunding companies - Abundance and Downing Crowd - offer products that are similar to mini-bonds, but these so-called " crowd bonds " tend to be secured against company assets.

However, such bonds leave a lot of hard work to be done by the investor, especially when issued by start-ups with little or no record.

"The big problem with any of these bonds is that they depend on the investor to analyse them and work out whether they offer good value," says Ben Yearsley, a wealth manager.

Mr Yearsley, who has personally invested in secured crowd bonds, says the fact that they are backed by assets gives them an important advantage over mini-bonds. "If something goes wrong and the company falls into financial difficulty, the bonds I'm investing in have recourse to those assets to repay the loan," he says.

Yet the lack of security has not stopped investors snapping up the recent £0m unsecured bond issued by BrewDog, Scotland's largest brewer of craft beer. Mini-bonds look far from dead.
