Investors in a mini-bond issued by Secured Energy Bonds, an unregulated company that went into administration in January 2015, know the risks all too well. The 900 investors, who collectively lost more than £m, were offered the bond by Independent Portfolio Managers, an advisory company regulated by the UK’ Financial Conduct Authority.

It is unclear whether any regulator can look at the Secured Energy Bonds case. Investors were told by the UK’ Financial Ombudsman Service, the body that deals with investor complaints against regulated financial companies, that the collapsed bond did not fall within its remit. The FOS later changed its mind and agreed to consider the case following appeals and the threat of legal action from investors —ut it has not yet made its final decision.

The case highlights the lack of regulatory oversight of mini-bonds, despite the involvement of FCA-regulated advisers that recommend them.

Despite the drop in interest for mini-bonds, an illiquid and high-yield cousin has grown in popularity. Two debt crowdfunding companies —bundance and Downing Crowd —ffer products that are similar to mini-bonds, but these so-called “rowd bonds”end to be secured against company assets.

However, such bonds leave a lot of hard work to be done by the investor, especially when issued by start-ups with little or no record.

“he big problem with any of these bonds is that they depend on the investor to analyse them and work out whether they offer good value,”ays Ben Yearsley, a wealth manager.

Mr Yearsley, who has personally invested in secured crowd bonds, says the fact that they are backed by assets gives them an important advantage over mini-bonds. “f something goes wrong and the company falls into financial difficulty, the bonds I’ investing in have recourse to those assets to repay the loan,”e says.

Yet the lack of security has not stopped investors snapping up the recent £0m unsecured bond issued by BrewDog, Scotland’ largest brewer of craft beer. Mini-bonds look far from dead.

headline: Brewdog  raises £00m from American private equity firm
author: Kadhim Shubber
text:
Brewdog, the independent Scottish brewery, has raised £00m from TSG Consumer Partners, the American private equity firm.

In a message to shareholders on Saturday, the beer maker said TSG had acquired 22 per cent of the company. Brewdog said the transaction had given it a £bn enterprise value.

“SG has acquired approximately 22% of the company in a transaction where £00m in proceeds went into the Company for continued expansion and the balance to create early shareholder equity in a transaction that valued Brewdog plc at £billion enterprise value,”he message said. “he deal is designed to deliver long term capital with 10 year time horizon.”

Brewdog was founded by James Watt and Martin Dickie in 2007 and is known for its brash marketing campaigns. Until now it has relied heavily on crowdfunding.

Watt and Dickie have said they would never sell the company to a multinational brewery, or as they put it: “ monolithic purveyor of industrial beer” Private equity companies, on the other hand? Not so bad it seems.

A spokesperson for TSG did not immediately respond to a request for comment. A spokesperson for Brewdog did not immediately comment.

Update, April 9: According to The Sunday Times , TSG is “lso buying £13m of shares from existing investors” which brings the total value of the deal to £13m:

The founders are expected to bag the bulk of the bonanza. Yesterday, BrewDog said Watt’ stake would reduce from 35% to 25% and Dickie’ holding from 30% to 22%.

Related link:

 Brewdog’ investors discover dilution —T Alphaville

headline: GREAT SCOTS!
author: ANGELA MULLIN
text:
WHISKY YOURSELF OFF TO THE HIGHLANDS

ABERDEEN isn't the first city that comes to mind when planning a Scottish break.

I'd visited Edinburgh and Glasgow, but all I knew of Aberdeen was that an old school pal moved there for university and loved it so much she never left.

So I reckoned she had discovered something I hadn't - and as I found out on a whirlwind visit, Scotland's third largest city has plenty to offer with history, food, culture, beer, shopping and friendly locals. The coastal city is just a 90-minute hop from Dublin, and chilly air hits us as soon as we land. But the weather is quickly forgotten when we check in to the cosy Aberdeen Altens Hotel.

The four-star accommodation, just three miles south of the city, features a gym and leisure club, pool, sauna and spa. The large rooms have luxurious bathrooms and a huge walk-in wardrobe. There's even a Nespresso machine.

Half an hour later, we're wearing safety goggles and inhaling the malty smell of hops as we tour BrewDog brewery.

Set up by pals James Watt and Martin Dickie in 2007, the craft beer company has expanded from two employees and a dog, to almost 600 staff and multiple dogs (you can bring your pooch to work in BrewDog HQ - and all staff get a week's paid puppy leave). And did I mention the MBEs James and Martin were awarded last year on the Queen's Birthday Honours list? Not bad for two lads who began brewing in their garage.

Our tour guide brings us through the labour intensive brewing process and gives us snippets of the colourful —nd controversial —tory behind the makers of the famous Punk IPA brew.

In 2009, BrewDog's beer Speedball was slammed amid claims it promoted the drug that killed River Phoenix.

The following year, the lads roped in a taxidermist to produce a limited edition beer for their shareholders, served inside dead animals. Road kill and chill —t's certainly a headline grabber. From hipster brewers to haunted houses - we moved on to the spooktastic Fyvie Castle, famous for appearing in several TV shows including the celeb reality series I'm Famous and Frightened.

Our guide reveals that the 800-year-old castle is haunted by the Green Lady —ame Lilias Drummond. In the late 1500s, Dame Lilias had four daughters with husband Lord Alexander Seaton. When she failed to produce a son, Alexander felt peeved and his eye started to wander.

He locked his wife in a tower and starved her to death, before marrying her niece six months later.

On their wedding night, Alex and his young bride were disturbed by wailing outside their bedroom window —nly to awake to see 'D. Lilias Drummond' carved into the windowsill. The words are still visible today. The castle is also home to a painting of General William Gordon, painted in 1776 by Pompeo Girolamo Batoni, and worth an astonishing €5million.

But if ghoulish spirits aren't your thing, perhaps liquid ones are.

Glen Garioch distillery in the picturesque village of Oldmeldrum is another worthwhile visit. Established in 1797, Glen Garioch specialises in the Single Malt whisky and offers tours, tastings and a chance to bottle your own personalised tipple.

One of its most expensive whiskies, the 21-year-old, sells for €60 a bottle, which sure beats a last-minute Toblerone from the airport.

In the evening, we dine at Eat On the Green — two AA Rosette restaurant in the picturesque Aberdeenshire countryside.

al celeb, havdisplayed culinary ls on eral Head chef Craig Wilson - known as The Kilted Chef - is a bit of a local having displayed his skills several TV shows over the years, including an ws s, udan appearance on RTE's The Afternoon Show.

Craig has cooked for the likes of Rod Stewart, Sean Connery and Patrick Stewart, so we're expecting an A-list meal - and we get one.

The beef has been slow roasted over 12 hours, the scallops are succulent and I have my first taste of haggis —or me it's not far off black pudding.

We sit at the Chef's Table, a private dining room that has a TV with a live feed from the kitchen so you can watch your food being skilfully prepared.

There's a cracking food scene in Aberdeen, and Meldrum House in Old Meldrum gives us another taste of what the region has to offer.

The award-winning Country Hotel and Golf Course —amed AA Hotel of the Year for Scotland in 2014 —ocusses on fresh, local produce.

The hotel recently opened a Whisky Club in its Cave Bar, where Alex Ferguson is a member. With 44 individual oak lockers, anyone can join for around €75 a year. Ferguson, I can attest, has good taste. If you're not convinced, go see for yourself.

—NGELA MULLIN GETTING THERE are twice daily flights with Aer Lingus Regional from Dublin to Aberdeen from €50 return. aerlingus.com Hotel accommodation is €5 for double room including breakfast.

Brew Dog Tour approx €7.50 and Glen Garioch Distillery Tour about €.50 Fyvie Tour, adult approx. €4.50, family approx €5 Eat on the Green — courses €5 or 3 courses €2. Go to visitabdn.com.

headline: 'Punk' brewer in doghouse over sale to private equity
author: James Hurley
text:
Brewdog, Britain's largest craft brewing business, is to offer its crowdfunding investors up to £ million this week as it seeks to allay concerns over the sale of a stake to an American private equity giant.

The self-styled "punk" brewer, whose co-founder once likened raising institutional funds to "selling your soul to the Devil", has secured £13 million after it sold a 22 per cent stake to TSG Consumer Partners, owner of brands including Popchips and Vitamin Water. The deal will mean a bumper payout for James Watt and Martin Dickie, the friends who set up Brewdog in a garage in 2007 after a beer writer tasted one of their home-brews and told them to quit their jobs.

TSG is investing £00 million in Brewdog, and buying £13 million of shares from existing investors, with Mr Watt and Mr Dickie due to share more than £00 million. The deal with TSG values Brewdog at £ billion.

However, 55,000 smaller shareholders who supported the Scottish company over four equity crowdfunding rounds are being restricted in how much of their stakes they can sell.

Crowdfunders will be allowed to sell 15 per cent of their shares, up to a ceiling of 40 shares. Shareholders have also temporarily had their "preemption rights" removed, which meant that backers were not given the opportunity to maintain the size of their holding in the business.

Rob Murray Brown, an equity crowdfunding expert, suggested that Brewdog had "sold out its equity punk army" and said the deal would result in a "dilution bonanza" for small investors.

However, last month 95 per cent of Brewdog's investors voted to back the amendments to the company's corporate structure that allowed a private equity investment to go ahead.

Mr Watt insisted that the investment represented a good deal for Brewdog's crowdfunders. Those who invested in the company's first public share sale in February 2010 have enjoyed a 2,765 per cent increase in the value of their shares, he said. Small investors who bought into the company last year have seen a 177 per cent improvement in the value of their shares in less than 12 months, Brewdog added.

TSG will own 22 per cent of the business after the investment and Mr Watt will remain its largest shareholder.

He said there had been concern about the deal with a private equity firm among a small minority of crowdfunders, but that there was "strong" support for the deal from the vast majority.

"Whatever we do we are going to get criticism, but we announced the deal to 7,000 of our [crowd backers] at our AGM in Aberdeen on Saturday and they were delighted with what it means for our business."

Crowdfunders will be contacted this week by Brewdog with details of how they can sell their shares.

Mr Watt, 34, said the company is aiming for a flotation within five years. He has previously been reported as saying that taking on private equity leads to "companies having to make compromises they don't want to" and that working with institutional investors "feels like selling your soul to the Devil".

"[That was because] we hadn't met a partner that we wanted to do a deal with," Mr Watt explained. "We turned down a couple of higher offers from people who weren't right for us."

Brewdog made a pre-tax profit of £ million last year from revenues of £1 million.

headline: BREWERS' SCOOP
author: MIKE MERRITT
text:
US deal nets beer firm pals £00m

BREWDOG'S founders are toasting a £00million carry-out after flogging part of their craft beer firm to US investors.

The bonanza for Martin Dickie and James Watt, both 34, comes 10 years after they tapped in to a £0,000 loan to start making ale.

The Aberdeenshire firm later raised more than £0million through online crowdfunding appeals backed by around 55,000 small shareholders.

Now the deal with TSG Consumer Partners values the company at £billion.

The San Franciscobased giant will pump £00million into the company and buy £13million of shares —ostly from its co-owners.

James said anyone who invested in 2010 will see their holding's value soar 2,800 per cent.

He added: "This shows crowdfunding is a good model for any consumer-focused firm. "We don't see ourselves as having 55,000 investors —e have 55,000 ambassadors." Brew-Dog began by selling bottles of its Punk IPA ale before opening its first pub in Aberdeen.

It now has over 800 staff, bars in Europe, is launching a US brewery and moving into Asia and Australia. TSG's MD Blythe Jack said: "BrewDog will pave the way in the craft beer revolution in Europe and beyond."

scottish.sun@the-sun.co.uk

headline: Punk brewers cash in with £00m from US investor
author: Mike Wade
text:
Two old school friends whose beer company has achieved cult status are £00 million richer after selling a stake in their business to an American investor.

James Watt and Martin Dickie, both 34, needed a £0,000 bank loan to set up BrewDog, but rapidly established it as one of the world's most recognisable —nd controversial —eer brands.

Yesterday it emerged that TSG Consumer Partners is splashing out £13 million on a 22 per cent stake in the brewer, giving the business an enterprise value of £ billion.

Mr Dickie, a trained brewer, is responsible for production, while Mr Watt, who studied law at Edinburgh University, drives the financial and logistical side of the business.

BrewDog's best-known product is Punk IPA, and its beers typically are heavily hopped. Clever, if occasionally edgy, marketing has gained the company attention. In 2009, their Speedball brand was criticised because it was said to refer to the drugs mix that killed actors John Belushi and River Phoenix. Founded in Fraserburgh, Brewdog's main brewery is in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, built partly with money raised in three ground-breaking crowd-funding exercises. Much of its sales are overseas and the company has plans to open breweries in Australia and Asia soon. Business, page 38

headline: Crafty move
author: Robert Lea
text:
The difference between real ale and craft beer is about the age of 45. That is, they are both produced using traditional, nonsynthetic, authentic means but brewers don't market real ale to the under 45s just as the older more experienced drinker is dubious of anything labelled craft beer. Or put another way, both sets of enthusiasts have beards but the craft beer drinker's abdominals haven't slipped south —et.

Brewdog always had that authenticity, producing on their premises, laced with an anarchist styling of calling themselves punk brewers. They mocked the opposition when they sold out to the big corporates —eantime Brewing Company took the shilling of SAB Miller, Camden Town Brewery succumbed to the big bucks of the Budweiser group AB Inbev —ven stating that to sell shares to a corporate "feels like selling your soul to the Devil".

Now Brewdog's owners have sold a 22 per cent stake for £13 million to a faceless US private equity firm. It's the sort of deal that many a small business owner quite rightly dreams of.

But all of a sudden Punk IPA tastes a bit sour, like someone's changed the hops. Now you are looking at their prices of more than £ a pint, the head is clearing and you are thinking "that's awfully steep".

Authenticity in the food and drink game allows businesses to charge a premium. Brewdog's founders now need to prove to customers and their crowdfunding investors that the recipe hasn't changed, that they haven't become just another diabolical corporatist rip-off.

headline: A case of compulsive political fiddling while the economy burns
text:
Alf Young

Politicians, of whatever stripe, like to think they still exercise, through their policy interventions, significant influence over economic performance. While that instinct may remain relatively well founded in countries where state intervention is still the norm, in more market-driven economies, such as Scotland's, the strength of such influence is increasingly debatable.

Scotland has just lost Sir Arnold Clark, the UK's first billionaire car dealer, at the age of 89. And over the weekend an upstart international brewer, Brewdog, created just a decade ago in a Peterhead garage and largely crowdfunded since, hit a billion-pound valuation after its two founders sold a 22 per cent stake to a Californian private equity house. The political realm wasn't even a bit player in either corporate journey.

It has also been suggested that Lloyds might have turned its back on the 2008 rescue of HBOS had it known what fraudulent shenanigans where going on at the Bank of Scotland vehicle's Reading branch. With the main culprits convicted, the Financial Conduct Authority has now reopened its investigation into who knew what, when.

The spotlight will fall on the HBOS board at the time, not the Brown/Darling Labour leadership that brokered the Lloyds merger. However, if generations of politicians continue to have the rest of us believe they are big players in shaping our economic destiny, they must expect to be held accountable when things go wrong.

And in Scotland last week things went badly wrong. Our devolved government revealed a surprise 0.2 per cent contraction in output growth in the final quarter of 2016.

That made for a miserable year overall. The Scottish economy flatlined while the UK economy as a whole grew by 1.9 per cent.

Ministers blamed the uncertainty caused by Brexit headwinds. The Scottish government's press release even provided a link to the now discredited HM Treasury pre-EU referendum analysis claiming a Leave vote would cost the average British household £,300 a year. And it headlined GDP growth in Scotland last year, not of zero, but of 0.4 per cent. How so? That's the result you get if you compare average growth over the four quarters of 2016 with the equivalent 2015 average. But as its own statisticians point out, it is "a less sensitive measure of short-term change".

And in any case the equivalent UK growth figure is still considerably higher, at 1.8 per cent.

As the Fraser of Allander Institute has pointed out, the longer-term trends are also disturbing. Growth underperformance, compared with the UK economy as a whole, has been widening since Q2 2015.

Given these warning signals, what have Scottish government ministers been doing to turn things round? Well, our economy minister, Keith Brown, has been trying to rearrange the furniture. True to the centralising instincts of this administration, he's been conducting a review of four existing statutory bodies with key roles in the economy: Scottish Enterprise, Highlands & Islands Enterprise, Skills Development Scotland and the Scottish Funding Council.

Mr Brown was minded to bring them all under a single strategic statutory board. But faced with mounting scepticism he's now backed down. In a statement to MSPs on the day the Scottish parliament broke for the Easter recess he assured the four bodies they would retain their present status. A new non-statutory, business-led board with some kind of oversight is still on the cards.

Let's hope it has more visible impact on Scotland's growth story than the first minister's Council of Economic Advisers, first set up in 2007 to bring the insights of leading international academics to bear on improving the competitiveness of the Scottish economy, and which normally meets twice a year. According to its own website, its last meeting took place in August 2016.

The CEA chairman, Crawford Beveridge, has been assisting Keith Brown on his abortive enterprise and skills review. Who's been getting to grips with Scotland's growth challenge? Who knows.

Alf Young is a visiting professor at the international public policy institute at the University of Strathclyde

headline: 'Rebel' Scots brewery is accused of selling out
author: Mike Wade
text:
The UK's best known craft beer company has sold out to the capitalists it once despised, according to a leading academic.

Scott Taylor, of the department of entrepreneurship and local economy at the University of Birmingham, said that Brewdog had come to embody the system it set out to challenge.

On Monday the Ellon-based brewer announced that TSG Consumer Partners, an American private equity firm, had acquired a 22 per cent stake in its £ billion business.

The self-styled punk beer company recently backed down after its lawyers threatened action against the owners of a pub called The Lone Wolf, the name of a Brewdog product.

Dr Taylor responded with an article entitled: "Punked? How an upstart brewing company sold up and sold out." He wrote: "Want to be a multimillionaire? Start a 'rebel business', generate brand controversy —nd then sell it to the capitalists you appeared to despise. That's one way to do it."

He claimed that the Scottish multinational brewery —hich brews in Aberdeenshire and in Columbus, Ohio —ad found that "it's not easy trading on rebellion".

James Watt, who co-founded Brewdog, dismissed the criticism. Mr Watt, who remains the largest shareholder in the business, said that the new investment would delight small shareholders and help to promote quality beers around the world.

In an interview with The Times, he revealed his political ambitions, saying that he would consider entering politics in about ten years' time.

While he did not declare a party affiliation, Mr Watt has leadership aspirations. "I wouldn't consider doing it, if I didn't feel I could make it," he said.

His motivation was the lack of choice currently on offer, he said. "Far too often it is not political arguments," Mr Watt added. "There are arguments about who can run public services more efficiently, which is a long way away from having political choices." Brewer with bark and bite, page 23

headline: Creative UK distillers ferment an indie explosion
author: Conor Sullivan and Cat Rutter-Pooley
text:
The number of distilleries in the UK rose by a sixth last year, thanks to the growing preference for gin and whisky made by “raft”roducers rather than established corporations.

Forty distilleries opened in the UK in 2016, bringing the total to 273, according to a Wine and Spirit Trade Association report based on HM Revenue & Customs data. The growth means the number of distilleries in the UK has more than doubled since 2010, the WSTA said.

The trend for boutique gins and other “rtisan”pirits follows the fashion for craft beer, as consumers opt for niche, local brands over those made by big multinational brewers and distillers.

“oth the craft spirits and the craft brewery sectors are going through a period of explosive creativity,”aid James Simmonds from the accountancy firm UHY Hacker Young, which helped to compile the figures.

“he quality of the product is streets ahead of their big brand competitors. It is no wonder that the global drinks giants are worried.”

He also said the number of M&A deals in the sector was encouraging entrepreneurs to start companies.

Daniel Szor, who founded the Cotswolds Distillery in 2014, said the trend was based on “ot just on the product but on where it comes from, how it was made —he artisanal values”

He said it was in the US that he first noticed that “here was a new trend of local distilleries focused on premium brands in small batches”

“t’ like craft brewing in the US: I asked why is the same thing not going to happen in distilling?”

In December, Japan’ Beam Suntory, the third-largest distiller in the world by sales, bought Sipsmith, a small gin producer founded in a London garage seven years ago. Sipsmith’ varieties include Lemon Drizzle Gin and Sloe Gin, and the company says it uses “raditional, hand-crafted”echniques and recipes.

The trend for high-quality, niche spirits has in turn sparked growth in the market for mixers. Fever-Tree last month said its revenues grew 73 per cent in 2016 and announced it planned to expand from tonic waters into mixers for dark spirits such as whisky.

The craft beer sector has also seen interest from private equity firms. Earlier this month, BrewDog, the independent Scottish brewery known for its brash marketing, raised £00m from the American private equity firm TSG Consumer Partners in a deal that gave it an enterprise value of £bn.

headline: Mergers provide a tonic for gin makers
author: Will Humphries
text:
A surge in British gin and whisky drinking has led to the number of new distilleries increasing by almost a fifth last year.

According to Revenue & Customs 45 new distilleries opened last year, an increase of 17 per cent, and gin sales exceeded £ billion.

Miles Beale, chief executive of the Wine and Spirit Trade Association, said: "We have already seen how successful and in-demand British gin is to 139 countries around the world.

"But we believe that with the right support the industry can go further to access more markets overseas."

UHY Hacker Young, the accountancy group, said that activity was being spurred by merger and acquisition deals in the artisan alcohol sector.

Sipsmith, one of the leaders in the new wave of boutique gins, was acquired by Beam Suntory, of Japan, in December. Edinburgh Gin was bought by Ian Macleod Distillers, the tenth largest Scotch whisky producer, last September.

James Simmonds, of UHY Hacker, said that the £ billion valuation put on the craft beer brand Brewdog by TSG Consumer Partners, the American private equity company, was likely to lead to further activity in craft distilleries and breweries.

headline: My Round
author: ARAN BRAZIL
text:
My Round By ARAN BRAZIL HOW NOT TO PUNK BIGGEST BEER FANS AT its AGM three weeks ago, Brewdog, those crafty Scottish-based punks, announced they would sell a 20 per cent stake to US-based private equity firm, TSG Consumer Partners, for around €40million.

This came as something of a head-scratcher to many. The self-styled 'beer for punks' brand had run a series of very successful 'equity for punks' public share offers and previously stated it would never 'sell out'.

In fact, it had mocked numerous other breweries for their buyouts, proclaiming "independence or death".

Admittedly a 20 per cent stake is not even close to a controlling interest in the company. But it did split fans on the issue given its rebellious 'hardcore' marketing campaigns, the memory of which has left core fans with the strong taste of hypocrisy in their mouths.

That said, Brewdog is a company that's growing at an incredible rate. It's planning a new US-based brewery and hotel, breweries in Australia and Asia, upgrades to its existing brewery, and continual product launches (including a delve into the spirits market). No doubt, many had already been questioning if the equity raised could fund such a pace of growth.

Closer to home the same questions are being asked after independently owned Carlow Brewing Company (makers of O'Hara's) sold a 32 per cent stake to Hijos de Rivera, a family-owned Spanish business best known for its Estrella Galicia beer. However, the partnership, as founder and CEO Seamus O'Hara put it, "can make a significant contribution to the future success of Carlow Brewing Company".

From a beer lover's standpoint, any move that breweries make to secure their future is fine by me. If, however, it leads to cost-cutting, tap-blocking and poor beers as a result, they'll quickly find themselves losing the very customers that got them to where they are.

headline: Small investors risk being lost in the crowd
author: Aime Williams
text:
The dream of crowdfunding is to democratise finance —he little guy gets to decide what wins and what loses, not just the seasoned professionals.

To its acolytes, it allows businesses in need of fast cash to bypass bankers, private equity and venture capital and go straight to the people, overthrowing the fat cats and making investors a shedload of money along the way.

FT Money readers may not be surprised to hear that’ not the whole story, though. High valuations dreamt up by entrepreneurial mavericks is one problem, and an unorthodox approach to selling debt versus equity is another.

I’l take the example of Scottish independent brewery BrewDog, a favourite among millennial drinkers. It has been phenomenally successful at playing the crowdfunding game. First of all, it realised way back in 2015 that everyone, at heart, just wants to overthrow the ruling elites and came up with a suitable marketing strategy —eferring to its investors as “unks” for example.

In a weird and slightly sinister-sounding PR stunt, the group dropped taxidermy “at cats”n parachutes over the City of London to celebrate “ new dawn for beer and a new dawn for small business finance”

BrewDog’ launch was one of the most successful crowdfunding campaigns of any business, raising £5.5m. But turning the spectre of an idea into a success takes grit and cash. Lots of businesses that crowdfund fail. The reason is not because they’e terrible companies, but because their projected growth rates are based on their wildest dreams.

This failure rate is fine —t’ fun, even. Nowhere is investing more thrilling than in the high stakes world of early-stage, high-growth companies. You’e taking a punt on the next big thing, and if it takes off you’l make loads of money.

In reality, things are not so simple. Let’ think about the dream from an investor’ point of view. You see the potential of a brilliant start-up and you decide to throw them a few quid. How much do you pay for their shares? If it were a listed company, the stock market would decide. In crowdfunding land, the start-up tends to value itself, based on its own projected accounts.

---------------

More from Millennial Money

This is why millennials hate letting agents

Why does pension investing have to be a moral dilemma?

How Facebook can affect your credit score

How Airbnb has lost its soul

---------------

Old private equity hands typically make sure valuations are realistic when they invest, because they understand that in order for them to get their money back, they need to sell their stake for more than they paid. Put another way, the valuation has to rise as they hold it. Beauhurst, a data company, did some research in 2015 showing that investors on crowdfunding platforms were paying a much higher price for start-up shares than professional investors.

On the valuation front, BrewDog has been an unlikely success story. The company boasted that early investors would have seen the value of their shares increase by 2,800 per cent following a fresh cash injection of £00m by US private equity group TSG Consumer Partners.

The catch is that while BrewDog founders, James Watt and Martin Dickie, have both sold about a third of their personal stakes to the new backer —ashing in ahead of the crowd —ther investors have only been able to sell a maximum of 40 shares, earning themselves about £00. If the company lists, they will, of course, be able to sell shares then, assuming the valuation holds.

Another problem is that about half of BrewDog backers didn’ buy shares. They bought debt, and this means they don’ get to enjoy the new high valuation of their brewery. If you’e a bondholder, the new private equity injection is practically irrelevant.

That would be fine, if in exchange for the lower rewards, bond investors took less risk. In traditional models, bonds are less risky than equities because if the company goes bust, the bondholders are ahead of the equity holders when it comes to being paid back. The problem is that crowdfunded bonds are usually unsecured: if the start-up goes bust, secured bondholders —sually “at cats”uch as bankers and private equity investors —re ahead in the queue of people wanting their money back.

This is the case with BrewDog. According to filings in Companies House, holders of its bonds would find themselves behind HSBC, a global bank, in the event of a default. HSBC holds around £0m of BrewDog debt, all secured against its assets. This security means the bank’ debt is less risky than the unsecured debt offered to retail investors.

The question for investors is how much HSBC has been compensated for the risk of lending to BrewDog? BrewDog says the bank has not been paid more than crowd investors, although HSBC’ coupon has not been divulged. Neil Simpson, BrewDog’ finance director, told the FT that banks “end under certain criteria”

Bonds would be appropriate if you can see stable, reliable cash flows that don’ need to grow for you to be paid back. It’ the difference between lending money to a friend who will pay you back from their salary and lending money to a friend who says they will need to sell 40 bottles of lemonade a day to pay you back.

If you’e backing a new company with big dreams, whose founders say they can repay by growing very fast, generating lots of new money, then you’e taking a fairly big risk. You want the chance to win big? In a nutshell, if there’ a decent chance of losing all of your money, you should be buying shares.

Aime Williams is a reporter on FT Money. aime.williams@ft.com; Twitter: @Aime_Williams

headline: FT WEEKEND SUPPLEMENT - MONEY Investors risk being lost in the crowd; MILLENNIAL MONEY
author: Aime Williams
text:
The dream of crowdfunding is to democratise finance - the little guy gets to decide what wins and loses, not just the seasoned professionals.

To its acolytes, it allows businesses in need of fast cash to bypass bankers, private equity and venture capital and go straight to the people, overthrowing fat cats and making money for small investors.

That's not the whole story, though. High valuations dreamt up by entrepreneurial mavericks is one problem and an unorthodox approach to selling debt versus equity is another.

I'll take the example of Scottish independent brewery BrewDog, a favourite among millennial drinkers. It has been phenomenally successful at the crowdfunding game. It realised back in 2015 that everyone, at heart, wants to overthrow the ruling elites and came up with a suitable marketing strategy - referring to its investors as "punks", for example.

In a weird, slightly sinister-sounding PR stunt, the group dropped taxidermy "fat cats" on parachutes over the City of London to celebrate "a new dawn for beer and a new dawn for small business finance".

BrewDog's launch was one of the most successful crowdfunding campaigns of any business, raising £5.5m. But turning the spectre of an idea into a success takes grit and cash. Lots of businesses that crowdfund fail. The reason is not because they're terrible companies, but because their projected growth rates are based on their wildest dreams.

This failure rate is fine - it's fun, even. Nowhere is investing more thrilling than in the high stakes world of early-stage, high-growth companies. You're taking a punt on the next big thing, and if it takes off you'll make loads of money.

In reality, things are not so simple. Let's think about the dream from an investor's point of view. You see the potential of a brilliant start-up and decide to throw them a few quid. How much do you pay for their shares? If it were a listed company, the stock market would decide. In crowdfunding land, the start-up tends to value itself, based on its own projected accounts.

Old private equity hands typically make sure valuations are realistic when they invest, because they understand that for them to get their money back, they need to sell their stake for more than they paid. Put another way, the valuation has to rise as they hold it. Beauhurst, a data company, did some research in 2015 showing that crowdfunding investors paid a much higher price for start-up shares than professional investors.

On the valuation front, BrewDog has been an unlikely success. The company boasted that early investors would have seen the value of their shares increase by 2,800 per cent following a fresh cash injection of £00m by US private equity group TSG Consumer Partners. The catch is that while BrewDog founders, James Watt and Martin Dickie, have both sold about a third of their stakes to the new backer, other investors have only been able to sell a maximum of 40 shares, earning themselves about £00. If the company lists, they will, of course, be able to sell shares then, assuming the valuation holds.

Another problem is that about half of BrewDog backers didn't buy shares. They bought debt , and this means they don't get to enjoy the new high valuation of their brewery. If you're a bondholder, the new private equity injection is practically irrelevant.

That would be fine, if in exchange for the lower rewards, bond investors took less risk. In traditional models, bonds are less risky than equities because if the company goes bust, the bondholders are ahead of the equity holders when it comes to being paid back. The problem is that crowdfunded bonds are usually unsecured: if the start-up goes bust, secured bondholders - usually "fat cats" such as bankers and private equity investors - are ahead in the queue of people wanting their money back.

This is the case with BrewDog. According to filings in Companies House, holders of its bonds would find themselves behind HSBC, a global bank, in the event of a default. HSBC holds around £0m of BrewDog debt, all secured against its assets. This security means the bank's debt is less risky than the unsecured debt offered to retail investors.

The question for investors is how much HSBC has been compensated for lending to BrewDog? The brewer says the bank has not been paid more than crowd investors, although HSBC's coupon has not been divulged. Neil Simpson, BrewDog's finance director, told the FT that banks "lend under certain criteria".

Bonds would be appropriate if you can see stable, reliable cash flows that don't need to grow for you to be paid back. It's the difference between lending money to a friend who will pay you back from their salary and lending money to a friend who says they will need to sell 40 bottles of lemonade a day to pay you back.

If you're backing a new company with big dreams, whose founders say they can repay by growing very fast, generating lots of new money, then you're taking a fairly big risk. You want the chance to win big? If there's a decent chance of losing all of your money, you should buy shares.

Aime Williams is a reporter on FT Money. aime.williams@ft.com ; Twitter: @Aime_Williams

headline: Our future depends on their ideas
author: Shaf Rasul
text:
Shaf Rasul One in a million shaf@the-sun.co.uk

IT'S not often that entrepreneurs get to go back to the classroom, but we could all do with brushing up on our skills —very day is a school day, as they say.

The Scotland Can Do SCALE 2017 summer school is set to return for a third year from July 30 to August 2, and it's great news for Scottish business.

The programme comprises sessions from world-leading experts in entrepreneurship and business practice and is a fantastic opportunity for self-starters to receive support and advice to help grow their business.

Since its launch in 2015, 127 businesses have benefited from the scheme in terms of increasing turnover, employing more staff and fuelling ambition.

Two women, whose business aimed at young parents is going from strength to strength, attended the programme last year.

Julie Wilson and Amy Livingstone set up Cheeky Chompers in 2013 with a range of chewy dribble bibs for teething babies. Since then, they have launched in John Lewis, appeared on Dragons' Den, expanded the product range and won awards including Entrepreneurial Scotland's Emerging Entrepreneur of the Year.

The savvy businesswomen have no doubt gained from taking advantage of the support available from programmes like Scotland Can Do SCALE 2017.

Sessions like these are definitely worthwhile and a superb source of inspiration when looking to take your business to new heights.

Further great news for entrepreneurs included the announcement that the Great British Entrepreneur Awards has expanded the number of UK cities where it hosts ceremonies to include Edinburgh. The awards, now in their fifth year, have recognised some phenomenal entrepreneurs including Scotland's James Watt, founder of BrewDog.

Hopefully this announcement will see more Scottish talent celebrated for their achievements and will spur on others to follow in their footsteps.

The creator of the awards, Francesca Russell, said she believes it is important to champion entrepreneurs because "their businesses change the ways in which we live and work".

I think is it vital for entrepreneurs to contribute to more than just the economy.

Josh Littlejohn, right, is a sterling example.

His vision to build a social business has not only seen the creation of five sandwich shops, but a first-class restaurant and he has plans to build accommodation for homeless people. Supporting those less fortunate has always been at the core of Social Bite, where Josh pledged that 25 per cent of his workforce would come from homeless backgrounds.

This was despite the complications that arose, including having to put some of his staff up at his flat so they had an address.

I've meanwhile been focusing my attention on my "resident entrepreneur" role at the University of Strathclyde with the goal of supporting tomorrow's gen-eration of businessmen and women, above. In partnership with the university, I've just launched the PropTech Innovation Challenge, offering a share of £5,000 in start-up funding to the best five Dragons' Den-style business pitches.

I can't wait to take a look through the submissions and hear the pitches of those shortlisted. I see it as a huge responsibility to support the future of Scottish business and it's something I'm very proud to have the opportunity to do.

I've seen other Dragons' Den-inspired programmes that give back in other ways. The cheekily titled Dragons' Glen offers budding entrepreneurs a unique way to fundraise for Children 1st. Groups or individuals can pitch a business idea for the chance to receive £00 funding. The successful entrepreneurs then have five months to turn it into £,000 for the charity.

The initiative has raised £58,758 for Children 1st since the competition began four years ago and there are lots of examples of groups who have smashed the £,000 target. Staff from Thorntons Law raised £,544.16 this year which takes their running total to an impressive £7,000 since 2012.

Increasing the support on offer to promising entrepreneurs in Scotland can only be a good thing for the overall wellbeing of the country.

headline: BREWER CHEERS CRAFT BEER BOOM
text:
BOOMING demand for craft beer has boosted business at BrewDog.

The British brewer, which was valued at more than £bn earlier this year, said its UK sales jumped 97pc in 2016.

Total turnover around the world rose 61pc to £2m while profits were up 8.6pc to £.8m.

The firm is now one of the fastest-growing drinks companies in the world, after outpacing US craft breweries Lagunitas, Ballast Point and Stone Brewing.

It shipped around 131,000 barrels of beer in 2016 while 65m bottles of beer were shipped worldwide.

The firm is now eyeing further expansion in China. It is also opening the world's first craft beer hotel in the United States - The DogHouse, which will feature beer breakfasts, spa treatments, and hot tubs filled with Punk IPA beer.

©aily Mail

headline: Sales keep pouring in at  Brewdog
text:
Its recent £ billion valuation may have caused sceptics to splutter into their pints, but Brewdog's impressive sales growth continues (Dominic Walsh writes).

The Scottish brewer behind Punk IPA, co-founded by James Watt, right, reported a 61 per cent jump in 2016 revenues to £1.9 million, as pre-tax profits grew by 8.2 per cent to £.8 million.

It said growth had suffered from capacity constraints as its new brewery in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, had come on stream only in September. Underlying earnings leapt 46.5 per cent to £.2 million.

Brewdog, which has 47 bars around the world, forecast strong revenue growth this year thanks to the expansion in Ellon and a new brewery in Ohio.

headline: TAPPING NEW MARKET
author: ARAN BRAZIL
text:
My Round

IF you've ever visited Germany, you'll have noticed their love affair with the amber nectar.

From the murky wheat beers that kept the monks energised in times of fasting to their Reinheitsgebot (beer purity law) introduced in 1516, brewing tradition is upheld to this day. In fact, traditionalism dominates the beer landscape to the extent that you'll only find certain styles in certain regions: pilsner up north, helles down south, and some styles that can even be narrowed down to individual townships.

It's these long-standing traditions that have influenced German brewers for generations. More recently established craft breweries have been attempting to push the envelope of tradition, creating hybrid styles and flavours based on old-world beers. However, it seems that even in one of the world's main beer birthplaces, the progress and innovation of the microbrewing industry has been somewhat stifled. Craft brews and the associated bar scene has been slower to take off and any talk of the 'craft bubble' experienced in the US and the UK is yet to emerge.

Devin Duffy Meade, a Dubliner who cut his craft teeth with Galway Bay Brewery before moving to Berlin to work with BRLO Brewhouse, says: "Craft beer, at least our definition of the term, is very regionalised in Germany.

"It tends to be confined to the larger metropolitan areas like Berlin, Hamburg, Bremen or Munich." BRLO began as a craft start-up and, having gypsybrewed since 2014, has just opened its own 20-hectolitre brewery and restaurant in the German city. Meanwhile, Stone Brewing, the Californian craft trailblazer, recently opened in Berlin —ts first brewery outside the US —hile Brewdog opened its first German-based pub there too last year.

Germany's strong beer culture, teamed with its liberal licensing laws can play a massive part in the success of young breweries starting out.

Duffy Meade says: "We can brew and sell directly to our customers without the need to go through a distributor or third party. Customers can purchase crates and kegs at our brewery door, or we can fill and sell directly on our premises. The more money we take in directly, the more we can reinvest in the company.

"In Germany, the craft segment is still relatively small. We have not yet reached a level where we are fighting for the same hearts and minds.

"There is plenty of room for new breweries to form and for the market to grow and develop. In fact, many brewers can make a healthy living with their own tap room, without the pressure to grow aggressively." n BRLO and Stone Brewing will be at White Hag's international festival, Hagstravaganza on July 29 in Sligo along with many more: thewhitehag.com/hagstravaganza

headline: These girls have recipe for success
author: Shaf Rasul
text:
1SM Monday, Shaf Rasul One in a million shaf@the-sun.co.uk

I'VE always believed that entrepreneurial spirit is a calling —nd I was delighted to read that it has taken a more literal turn for two young bakers in Argyll.

Friends Holly Ford and Bron Campbell decided to think inside the box by using their talents for baking and spreading a little sweetness to Cladich, a small village of 30 residents, and opening their new shop.

Making use of BT's excellent Adopt A Kiosk scheme, Holly and Bron gave a disused phone box some much needed TLC with little more than a lick of paint, some newly installed shelves and added decorations.

Now, with their shop 'Cakes in the Call Box' cooking up a storm, they're able to bring a selection of homemade cakes and tray bakes to sell to locals and visiting tourists every day.

The box also doubles as a gallery for local artist Hilary Ollason where she sells her prints and cards.

What a fantastic idea! Not only turning their passion into a business, but transforming a neglected old phone box into a moneymaking, uniquely standout tourist icon.

Although not a new idea, with the Adopt A Kiosk scheme launching in 2008, more opportunities for likeminded Scots are certainly on the horizon.

Big ideas can launch for £ BT last month announced it was taking nearly 1,000 phone boxes out of service over the next three years due to falling usage.

Reading the figures, you certainly get a sense of just how much mobiles have taken over.

Telephone box usage has fallen by a massive 90 per cent over the last ten years.

However, what an ideal opportunity it is for entrepreneurs. Many of the iconic red phone boxes in Scotland have already been transformed into mini art galleries, libraries, information points and defibrillator kiosks, making a huge difference to their communities. Big ideas can launch for £ —he cost to take over the phone booth —nd start-up in a space 8ft by 3ft.

Who knows where those ideas might lead.

You just have to look to BrewDog. I'm sure owners Martin Dickie, left, and James Watt, near left, probably had a go at making their first craft beer in a bath, not much smaller than a phone box, before jumping head first into the industry.

Ten years on and what was simply a passion for the pair has turned into a global success story as BrewDog becomes one of the fastest-growing craft beers in the world, surpassing US brands such as Lagunitas, Ballast Point and Stone Brewing.

Earlier this year, it was announced that BrewDog was worth more than £billion, and last week it saw a 61 per cent surge in turnover for 2016.

New figures show that the breweries' global sales rose to £1.8million, leading with an eight per cent rise in pre-tax profits to £.76m. Not bad for a small start-up that kicked off in Fraserburgh.

With eyes on further expansion into Asia, the business-savvy entrepreneurs certainly are flying the flag for Scotland and represent a great example for any budding business people.

I'm not sure if you can start a brewery in a phone box, but who's to say that in ten years' time we won't revisit Holly Ford and Bron Campbell and find them posting seven figure profits, having moved on from a their tiny cake shop to much bigger things.

headline: Look no father
author: Mrs Crunch
text:

WANT to give your dad something a little more adventurous than socks this Father's Day? Read on.

To be fair to mums, there isn't much choice when it comes to gifts for dads, but if he isn't into socks and you want to get him a gift, you could always try something a little different this year. headline: Crowd pours money into craft beer by the barrel
text:
Craft beer fans have poured almost £0 million into breweries through Ccrowdfunding investments (Dominic Walsh writes). More than 65,000 individuals have invested in 40 breweries and halfa-dozen retailers that used crowdfunding to raise money for expansion over the past four years.

OFF3R, the investment comparison site, said that at least six breweries had raised more than £.7 million, with beer ventures making up four of the top twenty crowdfundings in 2016.

More than half the total was raised by Brewdog, the Scottish brewer, while Chapel Down raised £.66 million for its Curious Brew business.

While crowdfunding has a mixed reputation, the 2,173 investors in Camden Town Brewery were not complaining in 2015 when Anheuser-Busch InBev, swallowed the business in an £5 million deal.

Redchurch Brewery, above, in east London, has just closed its second fundraising drive through the Crowdcube platform, raising another £33,000 from 688 investors to add to last year's £97,000.

headline: Brewdog  gets US boss  Brewdog  [...]
text:
news in brief

Brewdog gets US boss Brewdog has appointed a managing director for its operations in the United States. Tanisha Robinson will be responsible for running the company's brewery in Columbus, Ohio, which is to open officially next month. Ms Robinson has previously been involved in several start-ups including the e-commerce firm Print Syndicate and Ticketfire, a mobile app that lets users sell paper tickets.

headline: 'Beer ain't nothing but a  Brewdog '
author: STUART MacDONALD
text:
FIRM'S ELVIS ALE KO'D

BEER giants BrewDog yesterday lost a legal fight with Elvis Presley's estate over one of their top tipples.

Trademark chiefs ordered the Scots ale firm to rename Elvis Juice IPA or get permission from the singer's lawyers.

The artist's legal team demanded the switch when the firm tried to register the booze title —rguing punters may believe The King backed the beer. UK intellectual property officer Oliver Morris ruled: "Presley is such an iconic figure. He's the most famous Elvis. "I consider most average consumers will think of him."

BrewDog bosses James Watt and Martin Dickie insisted the title had nothing to do with the rocker and changed their names to Elvis by deed poll in protest.

A hearing was told the beer made nearly £million last year and the firm had "no need to ride on the coat tails of others".

Elvis's estate has earned millions from the trademark since he died in 1977. BrewDog, of Ellon, Aberdeenshire, was told to pay its £,500 costs.

scottish-sun@the-sun.co.uk

headline: Elvis beer alerts suspicious minds
author: Scotland Staff
text:
A brewer has lost a legal battle with the estate of Elvis Presley over the name of one of its beers.

Brewdog, which launched its Elvis Juice IPA in 2015, says the drink has become one of the Scottish company's bestselling products.

However, it was contacted by lawyers from the estate of Elvis Presley who demanded that it changed the beer's name. In response, James Watt and Martin Dickie, founders of Brewdog, changed their names by deed poll to Elvis in an attempt to prove that the name was not exclusive. They insisted that the Elvis Juice IPA had no connection with the singer.

Presley died in 1977 but his name and likeness have been trademarked by Elvis Presley Enterprises, which earns millions of dollars a year through a licensing programme that grants the right to manufacture and sell Elvis Presley merchandise worldwide.

It objected after Brewdog applied to register the names "Elvis Juice" and "Brewdog Elvis Juice" as trademarks. The Elvis estate said the application overlapped with its registered trademark "Elvis" and that people could mistakenly believe that it had endorsed the beer.

The UK Intellectual Property Office, which rules on trademark disputes, has found in favour of the Elvis estate. Oliver Morris, the trademark hearing officer, said in a ruling: "Notwithstanding that Mr Presley died nearly 40 years ago, he was such an iconic figure that I would be surprised if many people had not heard of him.

"Given that Mr Presley is the most famous of Elvises, I consider that most average consumers, on seeing the name Elvis alone, are likely to conceptualise that on the basis of Elvis Presley."

The ruling means that Brewdog will have to change the beer's name or apply to the Elvis estate for official permission to use it. The brewer's finance director, Neil Simpson, said that turnover for its Elvis Juice product was £.9 million last year.

Brewdog, based in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, said that the drink was famous in its own right and had "no need to ride on the coattails of others".

headline: Brewdog  "punks" crash top 100 chart
author: DANIEL JONES
text:
A CRAFT beer firm has made it into the list of Britain's top 100 booze brands for the first time.

Scottish "punks" Brewdog entered the annual industry chart at 65 after sales rose by 170 per cent in a year to £8.6 million.

The firm, which sells Punk IPA, beat names like Jagermeister and Cobra, shifting it from hipster to mainstream.

Ranking is determined by "off-trade" sales, such as supermarkets, and not pubs or restaurants.

Big riser in The Grocer list was posh gin Tanqueray, up from 99 to 73. And Scottish yobs' favourite Buckfast entered at 91 after becoming trendy in the south.

TOP TEN 1. Stella Artois, 2. Smirnoff, 3. Foster's, 4. Budweiser, 5. Carling, 6. Hardys, 7. Strongbow, 8. Jack Daniel's, 9. Gordon's, 10. Famous Grouse.

headline: No thanks, Bud
author: Alistair Osborne
text:
Who'd have thought it? A beer even worse than Budweiser. Yes, it's called Budweiser Prohibition Brew: zero alcohol but more calories, the sort of combo you could only get from AB Anheuser Busch. Still, even allowing for that, Bernstein analysts may be on to something. They've gone on a bit of a pseudo-bender and now think "No & Low" alcohol will be "one of the faster-growing categories in beer". The Office for National Statistics reckons one in four 16 to 24-year olds are teetotal (or at least very good liars). And low-alcohol beer has heady margins: roughly the same price as the real stuff, but with zero or little excise duty to pay.

But doesn't it taste terrible? Well, among beers of 0.5 per cent ABV or less, the Bernstein "willingness to drink again" test justifiably has Brewdog's Nanny State near the top, surpassed only by Erdinger Alcoholfrei. No sign of Bud. Natch.

headline: Brewdog  down under  Brewdog  is [...]
text:
news in brief

Brewdog down under Brewdog is scouting locations for a brewery in Australia and is recruiting staff for operations there. Zarah Prior, who worked at the company's Aberdeenshire headquarters in marketing, sales and human resource positions, will head the operation. Brisbane and Newcastle are said to be the frontrunners for the site's location. Ms Prior said she was open to either redeveloping a site or finding land for a new-build.

headline: THE DAILY BRIEFING
text:
Cyber clarity??Banks will be forced to come clean over major security breaches and cyber-attacks under rules proposed by the City watchdog. It is part of a package of proposals outlined by the Financial Conduct Authority to make it easier for customers to compare services offered by different banks.

Space boost??Warehouse owner Segro said a shortage of warehouse space helped profits rise 23pc - as retailers hunt out more space to store their goods due to the growth in internet shopping. Profits came in at £1.2m for the half year ending in June 30, compared to £4.2m for the same period last year.

TV demand??Noel Edmonds is demanding £00m from Lloyds over claims that criminal bankers at HBOS's Reading branch wrecked his business in 2007. A Lloyds spokesman said: 'We are determined to compensate customers appropriately, swiftly and as fairly as possible.'

Paper loss??The Guardian owner, Guardian Media Group, has reported annual losses of £4.7m compared to a loss of £8.7m the previous year. Revenues nudged up 2.4pc to £14.5m.

Greek bond??Struggling Greece has sold £.7bn of bonds in its first sale since 2014 - a major milestone for the troubled eurozone nation, which had to abandon international debt markets after its economy was almost destroyed by recession.

Vote off??A vote on the proposed £0m takeover of fallen British tech star Monitise by US predator Fiserv has been postponed after a shareholder backlash.

OZ invasion??Craft beer maker Brewdog has appointed Australian Zarah Prior, who worked in its Aberdeenshire HQ, to identify locations for its third brewery in the country.

Record surge??Orders at internet firm Wandisco surged 73pc to a record £.8m for the first six months of 2017. The firm has won its first contracts in retail and healthcare.

Deal netted??Five-a-side football pitch operator Goals Soccer Centres has teamed up with the owner of Manchester City to roll-out eight football pitches in America. Goals' two sites in California made a profit of £00,000 in 2016.

Profits soar??Yorkshire chemicals company Croda's profits are up 14.3pc to £69.7m. Sales are also up, 16.2pc to £07.3m.

©aily Mail

headline: Hip brewer plans hotel
text: Scotland's largest independent brewer has struck a deal that could pave the way for it to create a £ million beer-themed hotel, restaurant and conference centre next to its brewery in Ellon, creating 80 jobs. Brewdog had previously accused Aberdeenshire council of making its plans untenable by asking for a "delusional" sum for land, but a price has now been agreed. Proposed features could include beer on tap in every bedroom and beer spa treatments that include the use of "bespoke hop oils".

headline: Beer bar bid to foil Trump's wall plan
author: CONOR RIORDAN
text:
A SCOTS brewery plans to open a craft beer bar on the US-Mexico border.

BrewDog hopes to cross the divide by serving their range of booze in Texas and Chihuahua.

The geographical border, where President Donald Trump vowed to build a wall, will be represented by a dotted line in the Bar on the Edge.

James Watt, co-founder of the Aberdeenshirebased firm, said: "Beer has always been a unifying factor between cultures. So we thought it would be fun for the bar to actually cross the US-Mexico border too.

"It would be difficult to build a wall if there's a BrewDog bar in the way."

The boozer, built from old shipping containers, will be classed as a temporary mobile building. James added: "We're planning on putting it there anyway until someone tells us to move it."

headline: Scottish brewery plans new bar on US-Mexico border
text:
A Scottish brewery plans to open a new bar on the US-Mexico border in an effort to bring together craft beer fans from the two countries.

Brewdog hopes to build its Bar on the Edge, half in Texas and half in Chihuahua.

It would serve Mexican beers on the US side and American drinks on the Mexican side, as well as Brewdog's range of craft beers brewed in Columbus, Ohio.

James Watt, co-founder of the Aberdeenshire-based brewery, said: "Beer has always been a unifying factor between cultures —nd our business was born from collaboration and an inclusive approach —o we thought it would be fun to place the bar a few feet further to actually cross the USMexico border too.

"We will request official permission from the local authorities to put it there and adhere to any red tape stuff, but I guess it would make it more difficult to build a wall if there's a Brewdog bar in the way.

"We're planning on putting the bar there anyway until someone tells us to move it.

"It was [former US president] JFK that said, 'conformity is the jailer of freedom and the enemy of growth'. We love the US, and we already have an amazing army of fans over here, but we do not intend to bring Brewdog 'lite' to the US.

"The Bar on the Edge is our line in the sand, quite literally."

headline: NOTEBOOK Divisions dog Trump's Nafta negotiations; Mexico Notebook
author: Jude Webber
text:
If you want a friend in Washington, so they say, get a dog. With Mexico certainly in the market for friends as renegotiation of the North American Free Trade Agreement gets under way next week, BrewDog, a Scottish craft beer producer, has trotted up with a cross-border friendship plan.

It is vowing to "make beer, not walls" by building a "Bar on the Edge", half in Texas and half in Chihuahua, Mexico, the northern state where the midget pooch of the same name is thought to have originated.

This sounds unlikely to appeal to Donald Trump, and not just because the US president is a famous teetotaller. He is sticking to a campaign pledge to build a border wall to keep out migrants, drugs and crime, and insists he will make Mexico foot the bill. But finding himself "in a little bit of a political bind" over his inability to make Mexico agree to cough up, according to the newly leaked details of a call in January with Mexican President Enrique Peñ Nieto , Mr Trump may yet be open to amicable solutions in the tough talks.

The transcript of the conversation , published in the Washington Post, offers a fascinating glimpse into the self-styled arch dealmaker's mix of dogma and pragmatism as he sought to pressure Mr Peñ Nieto not to say publicly that Mexico will not pay. While the unpopular Mexican leader politely stuck to his guns, he took flak from some at home for appearing more poodle than Rottweiler.

Mexico has drawn clear red lines: an update of the 1994 pact with the US and Canada must preserve tariff-free access and boost, not restrict, trade.

Nafta has forged close links between the two neighbours who build and buy each others' goods, eat each others' food and rely on each others' skills. But it is not just cars, manufacturing supply chains and corn exports that will be on the line when negotiators face off on August 16 in Washington. Mexico is worried about Mr Trump's "extreme fixation with trade deficits", as Ildefonso Guajardo, economy minister, told senators this week. Increasingly, the gap between US and Mexican wages looms large. The two are linked: Mr Trump takes exception to the $64bn US trade deficit with its neighbour. He does not see Mexico's cheap wages as an advantage for US manufacturers, who remain competitive by outsourcing labour-intensive production processes to Mexico, but only as a threat to US jobs.

When Nafta was signed, Mexico was the poor relation. The pact was expected to help close the wage gap but failed. Mexico has the lowest wages in the OECD, with salaries roughly a third of average pay in Canada and almost a quarter of that in the US. Securing an improvement would go down well with the many Mexicans fed up with Mr Peñ Nieto and his corruption-plagued government, now feeling pinched by a rise in inflation .

Alfonso Navarrete, the labour minister, oozed frustration last week when he said that if Mexico's trade partners could figure out how to deliver the buying power of dollars to workers on peso salaries, "we'll do it".

The Nafta wage discussions come as debate brews at home over how Mexico can appeal to investors while boosting salaries. Coparmex, a business lobby whose 36,000 members represent nearly a third of Mexico's gross domestic product, wants the government to slash corporate taxes and gradually increase a minimum wage that it says, at under $4.5 per day, is the worst in Latin America bar socialist Venezuela, now in meltdown, and communist Cuba. These domestic discussions could take even longer than Nafta's negotiations, which Mexico wants to wrap up by early next year.

The three Nafta partners want to jointly host the 2026 World Cup and BrewDog co-founder James Watt said he hoped his bar - complete with a dotted line running down the middle marking the international border, and built from old shipping containers - would be a unifying factor.

"I guess it would make it more difficult to build a wall if there's a BrewDog bar in the way," he said. "We're planning on putting the bar there anyway until someone tells us to move it." Mr Watt wasn't too worried about the "red tape stuff".

Finding a win-win situation to bridge Nafta gulfs over walls, wages, trade and tariffs will be a bit more challenging.

jude.webber@ft.com

headline: Breweries boom as thirst for craft beer goes unquenched
author: Greig Cameron
text:
A growing thirst for craft beer has resulted in the number of breweries in Scotland more than doubling in six years. Research shows that by the end of 2016 there were 115 breweries operating, compared with 55 in 2010.

It was a similarly positive trend for Scottish gin with a rise in demand leading to a 31 per cent increase in the number of distilleries from 145 to 190.

The boom in craft beer has led to a rise in employment in the sector from about 580 workers in 2011 to about 950 last year. The data, compiled by the SNP from reports by the Scottish Parliament Information Centre and Office for National Statistics, found that 21 of Scotland's 32 local authorities have a brewing or distilling business.

Edinburgh, the Highlands and Moray had the highest concentration of breweries while Moray, Highland and Argyll and Bute had more distilleries.

Demand for craft products, typically thought of as being produced by independent companies using traditional methods, is evident with brands such as BrewDog, Innis & Gunn, Williams Brothers and Stewart Brewing increasingly stocked in supermarkets. But while Scotland's brewers are increasing in number, craft brands are thought to make up only 2.5 per cent of all beer sold in the UK.

Rachel Athey, of the Brewers Association of Scotland, believes there is plenty of room for further growth and said: "Consumers are bored of generic tasteless lager and are looking for quality beer with a story they can believe in and new Scottish breweries are delivering this."

James Withers, of Scotland Food and Drink, said: "We have seen huge growth in new breweries in Scotland over the last few years. Consumers are more conscious of buying local authentic products."

Scotland has also benefited from the increasing popularity of gin across the UK. It produces about 70 per cent of gin made in the UK and dozens of distilleries have opened in recent years.

There have also been new entrants into the Scotch whisky market as well as some existing companies opening new distilleries.

David Williamson, of the Scotch Whisky Association, said that there were new facilities in Hawick, Fife and on the islands of Raasay and Islay. He said: "The number of new Scotch whisky distilleries, including around eight malt distilleries this year alone, reflects the long-term global opportunities for the sector and is providing welcome investment, jobs and tourism in communities across Scotland."

Kate Forbes, SNP MSP for Skye, Lochaber and Badenoch, said: "Scotland's breweries are providing a wealth of job opportunities in my constituency, with three distilleries and a brewery on Skye alone, and increasing our offering of homegrown products for the rising tourist numbers."

A wee dram, page 15 Leading article, page 31

headline: THE BEER HUNTER
text:
GRAD'S CAREER AT THE BAR Hywel lands dream job as ale tester

LUCKY Hywel Evans landed every student's dream job when he graduated —rinking beer for a living.

Hywel, 25, tastes hundreds from around the world every month as Britain's youngest ale expert.

Asda signed him up after he finished a chemistry master's degree because of the demand for craft beer.

Part of his course involved a six-month research placement in California sampling American craft beers. Ale and Craft Beer Buyer Hywel, who still enjoys going out for a drink at night, said: "It's a dream job.

"Craft beer is booming and I feel lucky to be at the heart of the action.

"I have the pleasure of tasting some of the world's finest beers. My friends are green with envy!" Leeds-based Hywel, who graduated from Southampton University, added: "The skills I gained in my degree can easily be applied to my job, as brewing beer is all about experimenting with ingredients and flavours."

Hywel, from Swansea, added: "Friends send pictures of beer they've had telling me to get it in the Asda range.

"I can't keep up with the recommendations!" HIS TOP 5 BEERS Pride and Joy Crisp, aromatic pale ale Brewdog-Elvis Juice IPA loaded with grapefruit peel Piston Head Amber Lager 6% full amber beer with bitter twist Stone IPA Hoppy, highly rated IPA Brewgooder Clean Water Lager Tastes good and profits fund clean water charities graduated from Southampton

headline: Only here for the alcohol-free beer
author: EMMA PIETRAS
text:
WE TEST BRANDS FOR BANK HOL DETOX

DRINKERS are getting a taste for the soft stuff as low and noalcohol beers fly off the shelves.

Brits have sunk a record 32million pints in the past year, with sales hitting £4.7million.

Meanwhile, high percentage alcoholic brews have dropped by nearly 15.5million. Non-alcoholic beer —rinks with a volume of 0.05 per cent or below —nd low-alcohol brews usually appeal to health-conscious drinkers.

And they are perfect if you have over-indulged over the Bank Holiday and want a detox.

Drinks expert Joe Wadsack, above, says: "People now see alcohol-free beer as a viable option because some of them taste convincingly of beer."

But Joe warns drinking the soft stuff is not necessarily always healthier. He says: "Some use sugar instead of alcohol so the calorie content may still be the same."

Here, Joe rates leading non-alcoholic and lowalcohol beers out of ten.

Beck's Blue 3

STRENGTH: 0.05%.

PRICE: £.50 per six pack, Tesco.

SUGAR: 0.55g per 275ml bottle.

CALORIES: 38.5 per 275ml. Joe says: "This one tastes like someone has left their beer dregs and then mixed it with water.

"The flavour is also a little bit salty and doesn't seem to have the sugar to fill the void of real alcohol.

"Overall, this beer is a complete let-down."

Czech Lager 8

STRENGTH: 0.5%.

PRICE: £.50 per bottle, M&S.

SUGAR: 2g per 500ml bottle.

CALORIES: 20 per 500ml bottle.

Joe says: "It smells and tastes like Czech beer. If you can taste the provenance, then that's good.

"It's not complex but it's crisp and clean. The hops are fresh.

"For best value, this has got to be it."

St Peter's 9

WINNER STRENGTH: 0.05%.

PRICE: £.50 per bottle, Oddbins.

SUGAR: 8g per 500ml bottle.

CALORIES: 135 per 500ml bottle.

Joe says: "It's malty, like Horlicks, on the nose and tastes like an ale. It's got a caramelised sugar taste to it like tarte tatin which I like.

"There's also a nice, fruity character.

"I would drink it as drink in its own right - but it is pricey."

Erdinger 8

STRENGTH: 0.5%.

PRICE: £.30 per bottle, Tesco.

SUGAR: 18g per 500ml bottle.

CALORIES: 125 per 500ml bottle.

Joe says: "I got a whiff of meal and bran - that's what a wheat beer should smell of.

"Not too sweet and the flavour is good quality hops. It's really well-balanced."

Foster's 4

STRENGTH: 2.0%.

PRICE: £.75 per four pack, Asda.

SUGAR: 17.1g per 300ml bottle.

CALORIES: 117 per 300ml bottle. Joe says: "I got a nice lemony scent but it is very sweet.

"For a better taste, try mixing an ordinary beer with lemonade."

Franziskaner 6

STRENGTH: 0.05%.

PRICE: £.30 per bottle, Tesco.

SUGAR: 9.2g per 500ml bottle.

CALORIES: 105 per 500ml bottle.

Joe says: "I'm being hit by a lovely fruity, floral smell. There's also a hint of spice.

"But it tastes little bit sour.

"It has the same texture as an alcoholic wheat beer but it's not quite there on the palate."

Brewdog 5

STRENGTH: 0.5%.

PRICE: £.50 per four pack, Tesco.

SUGAR: 0g per 330ml bottle.

CALORIES: 27 per 330ml bottle.

Joe says: "I got an interesting aroma of thyme and sage.

"Without the alcohol the hops taste overdone. It's very bitter.

"I'm a little disappointed."

Krombacher 8

STRENGTH: 0.5%.

PRICE: £.25 per bottle, Oddbins.

SUGAR: 9.2g per 330ml bottle.

CALORIES: 86 per 330ml bottle.

Joe says: "It does smell like beer, like sweet porridge.

"I can tell the alcohol is missing but it tastes like a pilsner. I'm quite impressed with it."

Bavaria 2

STRENGTH: 0.0%.

PRICE: £ per four pack, Tesco.

SUGAR: 10.2g per 330ml bottle.

CALORIES: 79.2 per 330ml bottle. Joe says: "I get a scent of a brewery - but there's damp wood on my palate.

"At least it has more texture than the San Miguel."

San Miguel 1

STRENGTH: 0.0%.

PRICE: £ per four pack, Tesco.

SUGAR: 5.6g per 330ml bottle.

CALORIES: 79.2 per 330ml bottle.

Joe says: "There's almost no trace of beer on the nose. It smells like soda water combined with a fake hoppiness.

"I get a faint hint of Edam cheese and bruised apples.

"It's horrible. I could not drink a whole bottle."

CRAFT brewery BREWDOG has pledged to give away 20 per cent of its profits.

Its Unicorn Fund will hand ten per cent to its workers and another ten per cent to charities.

Half the causes will be chosen by its investors, known as "punks", and the others by staff.

headline: BREWER'S CHARITY CHEER
text:
CRAFT beer company BrewDog has pledged to give away 20pc of the profits it makes to staff and charities every year.

The British brewer, which was valued at more than £bn earlier this year, said 10pc of its earnings will be shared among its staff and a further 10pc will be issued to charities chosen by its 1,000-strong workforce and 57,000 investors. The company said the payouts under its new Unicorn fund will exceed £5m over the next five years alone.

The company delivered £.1m in profits in 2016.

BrewDog co-founder James Watt said: 'This is a call to arms for businesses to democratise the impact their charitable contributions can have.'

©aily Mail

headline: Brewdog  pumped up by good causes
author: Dominic Walsh
text:
Brewdog has unveiled plans to give away 20 per cent of its profits as part of the Scottish brewer's ambition to "build a radically new type of business" (Dominic Walsh writes).

The company said that 10 per cent would be shared equally among its employees, with another 10 per cent being given to charities chosen by its staff and investors.

James Watt, the co-founder of Brewdog, said that if the brewer achieved its projected targets it would end up giving away at least £5 million over the next five years via what it calls its Unicorn Fund.

"We want to create a blueprint for a completely new type of 21st-century business and use craft beer to make the world a better place for everyone," Mr Watt said.

"Giving away 20 per cent of our profits, for ever, is not about altruism. It is about impact. It's not about profits. It is about purpose."

Mr Watt said that he and his co-founder Martin Dickie wanted to "inspire a new kind of business ... that doesn't measure profit in purely monetary terms".

"Outdated CSR [corporate social responsibility] policies have zero consideration for their real-world impact, existing merely for ... an oversized cheque and an awkward photoshoot. This is a call to arms for businesses to democratise the impact their charitable contributions can have on their community, their people and the world."

Brewdog, which was founded in 2007 and is best known for its Punk IPA, achieved underlying earnings last year of £.2 million, up from £.9 million in 2015, and turnover up 61 per cent to £2 million.

Of the 10 per cent of profits donated to good causes, half will go to charities chosen by its 1,000 global staff and the other half to those selected by the 57,000 "Equity Punk" investors who have bought shares in crowdfunding schemes. The balance of 80 per cent would be reinvested in the business for at least the next seven years, Mr Watt said.
