headline: Brewdog ’ ambitious projected profits
author: Kadhim Shubber
text:
Scottish craft brewery Brewdog made an unusual announcement this week : It said it would give away 20 per cent of its profits every year from now on until forever. Half of that would go to staff and the other half would go to charities selected by Brewdog’ investors and employees.

The company’ founders James Watt and Martin Dickie also said they would reinvest the remainder of their profits into the business “or at least the next 7 years” So that’ healthy bonuses for staff, a good dose of corporate philanthropy, and a long wait for any dividends, which is not what you would expect from a business part-owned by a private equity firm.

But Watt and Dickie seem serious and they reckon if Brewdog hits its targets, it will give away more than £5m over the next five years, which is where things get interesting.

Brewdog is one of the UK’ most successful startups in recent years. It has mostly avoided traditional investors in favour of crowdfunding, and made £2m in revenue last year along with £.4m in post-tax profits. Earlier this year, it sold a 22 per cent stake to TSG Consumer Partners in a deal that valued Brewdog at £bn.

In short, it’ doing pretty well. But even so, Brewdog would have to generate £25m of profits by 2022 in order to reach its goal of giving away £5m.

Let’ see how much of a tall order that is.

According to a company spokesperson, the “rofits”eferenced in its announcement are pre-tax profits, which, as an aside, would make the giveaway tax deductible.

Last year, Brewdog made £.8m in pre-tax profits, so Watt and Dickie would need to basically double it for the next five years to generate £25m in total over that period.

Nothing is impossible, obviously, but let’ look at how BrewDog has done historically.

From 2011 to 2016, its average yearly pre-tax profit growth rate has been a little over 90 per cent, which is good going, but not quite good enough for the £5m target.

And that average is being boosted by one particularly good year of growth. In 2013, pre-tax profits jumped around 390 per cent as Brewdog went from making £85,000 before tax to £.4m. If we look at things since then, we find that average profit growth has been ticking along at a more sober 20 per cent, partly because of investment spend.

If Brewdog can shift growth higher and does hit its targets, the company would be generating £15m before tax in 2021. But that’ a big “f”

headline: HIGH PRICE FOR A BIT OF VARIETY
author: ARAN BRAZIL
text:
My Round

UPON visiting a suburban pub — normal run-of-the-mill establishment — took a quick stroll along the bar to check the tap line-up.

Nothing really stood out, the usual suspects were there, but not even some of the new ranges. Asking to see what was on offer in bottles instead, the reply was much the same, save for a lone craft choice: an O'Hara's IPA. That's fine, it's a lovely beer and I hadn't had one in a while. "That'll be €.50" said the barman. Seven euro and fifty cent, for a 500ml bottle of locally produced beer. A beer you can pick up in an off-licence for around €.50.

The acceptable price of a pint is an argument that divides the nation. Aggrieved punters post photos of receipts from all corners of the country on social media, paper evidence of what they reckon is an outrageous price for a beer. Even in this very newspaper, we once dedicated a whole page to the eye-watering € late-night pint of Heineken at one Temple Bar establishment. But cries of price-gouging are met by sobering retorts about freedom of choice. Punters can pay or simply go elsewhere. I'm no stranger to spending what others see as loopy amounts on a single beer. The €.50 I dropped on a 475ml glass recently would send others spluttering into their pints. The beer in question was an imported 8.5% New England IPA V2 by Brewdog and Cloudwater, two world-renowned breweries producing some of the best beer in the world. It was incredible and worth every cent. I would have bought a second but it had sold out. If you were lucky enough to find it in an off-licence, it was €.50 a 660ml bottle. By comparison, I wouldn't consider that gouging at all.

While not everyone will wish to opt for sometimes-silly 'üer beers', the idea that all craft beer deserves a premium mark-up should be challenged. Some special releases are expensive because of the sheer costs involved in producing them, while core ranges of countless craft breweries are quality alternatives to big beer, at near-comparable price. It can be done.

I'm happy to pay more for something I enjoy but I hate to be gouged. I avoid such pubs now in favour of supporting establishments that respect not only craft breweries but customers' hard-earned dime too.

headline: Seed for start-ups
text:
news in brief

A Scottish technology accelerator has been launched with backing from entrepreneurs including Sir Tom Hunter, Chris van der Kuyl and James Watt, of Brewdog. Seed Haus, an Edinburgh venture founded by Calum Forsyth and Robin Knox, has picked five startups for a six-month development programme, including Securityctrl, a specialist in cybersecurity for cloud computing, Sansible Wearables, which works in body monitoring, and Drinkly, an on-demand drinks service.

headline: The not-for-proit pioneers
author: Laura Whateley
text:
Do good, make money, reinvest in doing good. It's a simple formula and the reason that so many social enterprises are thriving, says Laura Whateley

Business is changing. Once, companies would have to choose between turning a proit or ofering ethical products and services. Now, some of the most successful new businesses are growing - and attracting the most talented employees - precisely because they are prioritising their social impact.

At the end of last month, hip Scottish craft beer company Brewdog unveiled plans to give away 20 per cent of its proits with 10 per cent shared equally among its employees and the other 10 per cent to charities chosen by stafand investors. James Watt, the co-founder of Brewdog, said he wanted to create a blueprint for a completely new type of 21st-century business, one that doesn't measure proit in purely monetary terms.

This is a pattern being repeated across the private and third sector, with social entrepreneurs on the rise.

Of the social enterprises that exist today, 25 per cent are less than three years old and almost four in ten are ive years old or less, according to new igures from Social Enterprise UK. "It is easy to understand why," says Lord Victor Adebowale, chair of Social Enterprise UK. "The social enterprise movement is demonstrating that you can thrive as a business and at the same time tackle some of the biggest problems we face. They show it is possible to compete commercially, but also improve people's lives and communities."

Meanwhile, there is growing pressure for prit-making companies to consider their social, ethical and environmental outputs believes Tifany Kelly, founding partner of RoundTable Global, one of the organisations shortlisted in the Positive Social Impact Award category.

RoundTable Global ofers consultancy for both private sector companies in industries such as banking and pharmaceuticals, as well as charities and social enterprises. Proits made from its private-sector consultancy are fed back into funding its philanthropic work.

"We work with young professionals and graduates who are now interviewing companies, rather than the other way round," says Kelly. "When they are looking for a new job they want to know what an organisation's core values are and how they are restoring the global eco-systems.

"The internet means there is no hiding place any more," Kelly continues. "When a company is publicly outed as unethical, there are boycotts. Transparency is forcing organisations to make more ethical decisions. Similarly, smaller organisations in a supply chain will not work with massive corporations who do not consider their social impact."

Another of the shortlisted organisations, Ashley Community Housing, started in 2008 with one employee and an annual turnover of £4,000. Now it turns over £.1m and employs more than 50 people. It is a social enterprise ofering accommodation, training and support for refugees and other displaced people.

Fuad Mahamed, chief executive oicer of Ashley Community Housing, agrees that there is a greater move towards considering the ethical, environmental and social aspects of a business. "Not every business is on this page yet, but more and more are taking notice of it," he says.

"We primarily work with refugees, and the past year has seen global businesses including Starbucks, Airbnb and Ben & Jerry's showing their support for refugees via their business practices and good citizenship. There are examples across Europe of companies such as Capgemini, BMW, Coca-Cola, and Ikea changing their recruitment processes to ensure labour market integration of refugees.

"Most social enterprises are not-forproit and they seem to think this means they can't strive to grow. We want to be a proit-making business so that we can reinvest back into the company and ultimately help more people. As such, we work hard to increase our inancial growth and, over the past year, turnover has risen by 22 per cent - ensuring we can continually invest in our core purpose."

Sophie Seddon, of Novus Property Solutions, thinks that the commitment to positive social impact has to be built into the fabric of a company. The property maintenance, refurbishment and new-build contractor has a strong CSR programme which it sees as being "far from just another box to tick". Charitable activities within the company include planting allotments in local schools, to improving facilities in South African villages.

"Anyone can donate to charity, but businesses must make CSR a part of their culture," says Seddon. She also believes that by committing fully to social responsibility, businesses are in the best position to attract the most talented people entering the job market at a time when competition is high.

Young people now interview companies, rather than the other way around

headline: THREE CHEERS FOR JOSH
author: Shaf Rasul
text:
Monday, Shaf Rasul One in a million shaf@the-sun.co.uk

SOCIAL Bite founder Josh Littlejohn has clearly been extremely busy recently.

Not only is he flat out organising Sleep In The Park —he world's largest sleep-out to raise money that will help to tackle the root causes of homelessness —ut his craft beer firm Brewgooder has ramped up its presence in the Scottish grocery trade after securing a listing with the Co-operative Group.

The Edinburgh-based label, whose beer is made by BrewDog in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, has challenged itself to raise enough funds to provide clean water for one million people facing water poverty.

Alongside his business partner Alan Mahon, Josh said the company's initial focus has been on supporting clean water projects in Malawi, with the company reporting in June that it had provided water to more than 5,000 people after installing two wells in the African country.

Craft beer The beer will now appear on shelves in 100 Co-op stores from this month under what it termed its 'largest deal to date' in the convenience store sector. This builds on an existing distribution deal that Brewgooder has with supermarket giant Asda, which was extended to cover more than 350 stores in June.

It's been a good week for the craft beer business in Scotland in general. Edinburgh-based Bellfield Brewery has also secured an agreement with Aldi for its two gluten-free beers.

The deal will see its Lawless Village IPA and Bohemian Pilsner beers placed on sale in 75 Aldi stores north of the border. The company has upped production as a result of the extra business in Scotland.

Here's to further success for our hard-working indigenous brewers.

headline: Changes brewing in IP protection
author: Neeraj Thomas
text:
Is the landscape of intellectual property enforcement in Scotland changing? It's a question that, at first glance, might seem curious.

There have been no seismic changes to the legislation that governs the enforcement of IP rights and no landmark cases in the Scottish courts that might justify that question being asked.

However, digging a little deeper reveals a growing trend of potential IP infringers fighting back through the mainstream and social media. Their angle is that the "big bad" IP rights holder is trying to bully the small business owner into giving up a name or product by threatening court action.

Undoubtedly, while there is an element of truth in some of these cases, it must give brand holders (and their lawyers) reason to have second thoughts when deciding whether or not to take action against potential IP infringers.

There have been several instances in Scotland involving large brands being on the wrong end of press attention for taking "overly aggressive" steps to enforce their IP. Brewdog, and the National Trust for Scotland's pursuit of Hilltrek Outdoor Clothing over a jacket called Glencoe are good examples.

Recent headlines also queried why L'Orél was concerned by a smaller Scottish based business, Naked Soap Company, which was said to be infringing the multinational group's IP rights. While this is certainly not solely a Scottish problem —s highlighted by a story involving Sainsbury's and a convenience store called Singhsbury's —here does seem to be a growing trend of these stories gaining traction in the Scottish media. This is causing some rights holders and their advisers to reconsider sending any correspondence to potential infringers. Public relations is playing an increasingly significant role in IP enforcement and the negative reputational issues have to be carefully thought through before any action is taken.

There is no doubt that the advent of social media has led to an increase in these kinds of letters being shared widely. Despite the fact that lawyers often mark "cease and desist" letters as "strictly private and confidential", this is often disregarded when posting them on social media.

Mindful of the possible negative connotations associated with what might be perceived as overly aggressive tactics by rights holders, Netflix chose an unorthodox approach to enforcing their rights over Stranger Things, one of its original programmes, which has since gone viral. The company sent a Stranger Things-themed cease-and-desist letter, full of puns and references to the show, to the potential infringers, a pop-up bar in Chicago, in an attempt to avoid appearing heavy-handed.

The approach has received widespread praise for dealing with a potentially difficult issue in a light-hearted way, and Netflix has come out of this situation with a lot of goodwill.

One might question, however, whether Netflix would take the same approach had the bar in question not been a pop up, or if the operators of the bar had not agreed to the settlement terms proposed by Netflix.

That said, Netflix does deserve a lot of credit —nforcing IP rights is not something one would ordinarily associate with humour, so to be able to take action while still being perceived as the good guy is not an easy thing to achieve.

Having said that, if the cease-and-desist letter is ignored, it may be questioned whether that provided the alleged infringer sufficient notice of Netflix's complaint. Perhaps a less fun version follows, if the original does not achieve the desired response.

The fact that infringers, perhaps more so in Scotland than elsewhere, appear so willing to tell their side of the story to the media does put a new spin on how we advise rights holders.

While doing nothing is very often the wrong move —nd indeed can cause issues in terms of trying to assert IP rights in the future —he traditional approach of issuing a standard ceaseand-desist letter could well cause reputational issues for the rights holder. That said, brand holders will often spend significant time and money in growing their brand and others should not be allowed to ride on their coattails without any action being taken.

There are no easy answers to these issues, but part of the challenge is to get the counter-story out to the media, explaining why it is so important that IP rights are protected and perhaps showing the importance of a particular brand or product to a local economy.

When all of the relevant considerations are taken into account, and as you'll no doubt see again on social media soon, it is likely the Netflix approach will be something that we see more of in the future.

Neeraj Thomas is a senior associate at Burness Paull

headline: Policy call is Nicola's Duel in the crown
author: Andrew Nicoll
text:
Andrew Nicoll andrew.nicoll@the-sun.co.uk and

ye IF, like me, you are an elderly person, you may never have heard of FanDuel.

-ssk I've only just heard of it and I still have no idea what it is. However, as far as I can make out, it's an internet platform which allows fans of American Football to pick fantasy teams to play each other for money.

r ly g m, Why do you care? Because FanDuel is a multi-million dollar company which employs 400 people, servicing six million registered customers from New York, Edinburgh, Glasgow, Orlando, and Los Angeles. Oh, and because it was established with Scottish Enterprise cash.

d -es A firm, set up with help from the Scottish taxpayer, with more customers than there are people in Scotland.

deot Funnily enough, if you looked hard enough, FanDuel was right at the heart of Nicola Sturgeon's big conference speech last week.

tts, I'm not sure why we reporters go to party conferences. Politicians say a lot and, for the most part, if they said it at Holyrood, we wouldn't write it down. We go to party conferences hoping for drama —ike a bad cough, a P45 and bits falling off the set.

-gt It rarely happens so we are prepared to settle for Sturgeon saying something moderately exciting about IndyRef2 instead.

ncs She didn't even mention it and, in its own way, that was pretty dramatic too. When you think about it, that's pretty astonishing, actually.

None of it is new Instead the First Minister spoke of, wait for it, actual policy. Policies like a National Investment Bank.

,a -y Dry as dust? Not really. Look at Fan-Duel, look at the ticket company Sky Scanner. Look at the beer makers Brew Dog. If you bought one £30 share in BrewDog's start-up, it would have soared in value to £,590 by last April.

wn e .

est., It's lovely that Scottish Enterprise gets behind young firms with grants but, if we had a National Investment Bank, it wouldn't be giving out grants. If we had a National Investment Bank, we'd own some of FanDuel.

otcd Success stories like that are rare. So rare they are known as unicorns but you only have to raise one or two unicorns to get really lucky.

e-.

So we've got the bank, the public sector bid to run ScotRail and "unprivatise" the railway again and the plan for a not-for-profit energy company to break the cartel of the Big 6. It's all so Corbynite and a big stick to hit Labour with when the Scottish elections come along in four years' time. But none of it is new.

kh 'Not a word of this was a surprise conference announcement. We knew it all.

All the big announcements were re-announcements plucked out of the manifesto and the current Programme for Government, polished up again and presented as lovely shiny things for us to look at.

More importantly, they were distracting budgie mirrors for the party faithful to enjoy and help them forget that they lost almost two dozen MPs last time round —lmost entirely because of IndyRef2.

Big £5k winnings tanning so And they don't distract from the "day job" stuff, like class sizes soaring abo e hen ere above 40 when the Nats were elected on a promise to have no more than 18 pupils in a class.

In my view, Sturgeon's conference speech is a turning point for the SNP. After ten years in power, the numbers are really pretty good for the party. It is amazing for any government to be 17 points ahead of its nearest rivals a decade after taking the reins.

But Sturgeon isn't stupid. It might have rated barely a whispered mention from the conference platform, but she knows that the election result was a warning shot for her party. If she had chosen to ignore it, there would undoubtedly be a price to pay.

So she is changing course, or at least changing emphasis.

The next time Scotland goes to the polls, she will have a policy platform to offer that's a good deal bigger and broader than just independence.

The question now is how will the rivals respond?

headline: Brewdog  opens bar to investors again
text:
Brewdog is seeking to raise up to £0 million. The Aberdeenshire-based craft beer producer has already raised £1 million through four rounds of its "Equity for Punks" crowdfunding scheme. In the latest round, it is releasing 421,052 new B shares at £3.75 each, with a minimum investment of two shares.

The company was valued at £ billion in April, when TSG Consumer Partners, an American private equity firm, paid £13 million for a 22.3 per cent stake. Brewdog said then that it would look to become a listed company in the next four or five years.

Brewdog wants to raise an initial £0 million to build a new brewery in Ellon to fulfil orders from the UK and Europe. Money raised above that will be used to pay for international expansion.

headline: Can  Brewdog  keep on delivering?
author: Kadhim Shubber
text:
Scottish brewer Brewdog has shown a remarkable ability to raise money from the public. Over the past eight years, it has raised £1m from some 50,000 shareholders across the world.

Now it is coming to the market again for £0-50m to fuel its ambitious growth plans overseas at a punchy £.7bn valuation, which would make Brewdog the most valuable consumer-facing “nicorn”n the UK, ahead of Deliveroo.

The fundraise comes just five months after Brewdog revealed a £13m investment from TSG Consumer Partners, the American private equity firm, its first and so far only institutional investor. That deal valued the brewer at £bn and allowed James Watt and Martin Dickie to sell £10m of their own shares, with the rest of TSG’ investment going into the company.

The new valuation is 70 per cent higher than the price tag TSG gave the company —n return for shares that guaranteed an 18 per cent annual return on exit —ut Watt, Brewdog’ chief executive and largest shareholder, says the business has grown leaps and bounds since that deal was agreed in January. UK sales have risen 100 per cent, international sales 50 per cent, and its US brewery is in production, with new sites planned for Shanghai and Brisbane, he says.

The company is planning some £63m worth of investment by the end of 2018, funded by the TSG cash injection, whatever is raised in this latest crowdfunding campaign, and borrowing to fill the gap. The expansion of Brewdog’ production capacity should help improve its operating efficiency, which Watt concedes hasn’ been stellar in recent years.

From 2013 to 2016, sales more than tripled but gross margins fell from 38 per cent to 34.5 per cent and operating profits dropped from 14 per cent of revenue to just a little over 6 per cent.

“e didn’ have any capacity, so the volume pressure actually meant we had to abuse the hell out of our system to get to where we needed to get to, which means we weren’ able to operate efficiently,”ays Watt. He adds that a rise in input costs as a result of sterling’ decline following the Brexit vote, as well as faster growth in UK sales, which have a higher cost of sales than international revenue, has weighed on its margins.

Back in 2015, research firm All Street said it was difficult to see how investors would make a return on their investment in Brewdog “iven the high valuation of £05 million” And yet earlier this year TSG invested at a £bn market cap. It is easy now to make similar arguments to those All Street made back then.

Sales for 2017 are forecast to grow 40 per cent to £00m, a slowdown on the 60 per cent growth rate of the previous year. At £.7bn, Brewdog’ market cap will be about 17 times revenues for 2017 and, assuming profits double this year, a dizzying 265 times net income.

But then it’ clear that people aren’ backing Brewdog based on the sort of metrics that analysts use. Just 3 per cent took up the offer to cash in some of their stake in the TSG deal, amounting to around £00,000 worth of share sales, according to Watt. Indeed, even he says of the investors who use Brewdog’ forum: “ lot of them are not that financially savvy and so they don’ understand that to double sales requires working capital”

They’e investing on the sense that they’e getting into something that’ going to be big in the future, which is a pretty normal reason for investing, and so far, Watt and Dickie have been able to deliver on that dream, despite the sceptics. The question is whether they can continue to do so.

Brewdog’ ambitious projected profits —T Alphaville

Brewdog’ investors discover dilution —T Alphaville

headline: Female drinkers on the rise
author: ANNA GAULT
text:
BEER BACK IN STYLE FOR GIRLS pint, say CAMRA of women enjoy a pint39% 54% of women go to the pub regularly

By ANNA GAULT Features Writer BEER is back in vogue for women —nd the brewing industry is cashing in on it.

Female pint drinkers were a common sight in the 1930s and 1940s but fell out of fashion as drinks companies promoted more 'ladylike' Martini and Lambrini girls. But now —s the number of UK breweries has broken through the 2,000 mark for the first time in 80 years —hose days are back.

The surge in craft beers has reignited women's love affair with ale as more and more producers tailor their tipples to appeal to female tastes.

Innis and Gunn founder Dougal Sharp believes the bitterness of traditional ales was turning girls off from a pint.

rom enjoying craft beers an itie er-—. s: re rir And he reckons beer —hich can lighter and fruitier with notes of elderflower and peach —s more appealing. He says: "Women are more sensitive to bitterness than men. "As a result, their perception of beer, especially in early stages when they start to drink, it's much more bitter that can be quite off-"But I like to think attitudes are changing and that female consumers are beginning to consider beer in a different way.

er, the whe nk, tter off th een is r and f-putting.

hink that "I think craft beer can really help to do that.

"There is so much choice and more information to choose what works for them.

"The beauty of beer is that we are not limited to one ingredient —e have malt, hops, yeast and water and then we have our fifth element, which is barrels. "They bring an absolute explosion of other favours, textures and aromas."

Dougal also points out that many women avoid beer because they mistakenly believe that drinking it will make them gain weight more than other alcoholic beverages such as wine.

He says: "Both men and women have a negative stereotype associated with beer, which is that it makes you fat. It's a load of rubbish —eer millilitre for millilitre —ontains fewer calories than skimmed milk or orange juice.

"Alcohol is the second-most calorific thing you can put in your body.

"It has seven calories per gram, compared to fat which is nine, so piling into a wine at 14 per cent along with all the sugars in it, you are consuming a lot of calories, whereas beer doesn't have the same alcohol content, but still has all the flavour."

A recent study by the Office for National Statistics showed wine is still women's favourite tipple, followed by spirits and then beer in third place.

But things are changing with nearly 40 per cent of women now admitting to drinking real ale while Heriot-Watt University in Edinburgh's brewing course now has women making up 10 per cent of their students. Meanwhile, a spokesman for Scottish beer company BrewDog insists everyone enjoys the tipples in their pubs, regardless of gender.

He says: "We see plenty of women in our bars and we'd always welcome more to come and enjoy craft beer.

"Women are just as likely to enjoy beer as men, especially if it's served in a way that places zero emphasis on gender."

author: BEN LEO
text:
HERE'S the latest piece of kit for cats —pace-age bags and backpacks so they can join in your travels.

But not every pet is feline enthusiastic about the U-Pet bubble carriers, which cost from £0. Fans are sharing snaps of their mogs peering out of the domed windows, and some are enjoying the ride.

headline: Ambitious firms have thirst for US
author: James Hurley
text:
Fast-growing businesses are looking to export to the United States as the appetite for sales to the European Union wanes, according to Deloitte (James Hurley writes).

The US has overtaken the EU to become the most popular market for so called "scale-ups", typically early stage companies with aggressive growth ambitions, the accountancy firm said. Its survey of 500 organisations found that more than half were targeting America for export growth, compared with only one in five before the Brexit referendum.

About one in three still rated the EU as one of the most attractive overseas markets, down from three quarters before the vote.

David Noon, UK and global Brexit leader at Deloitte, said that "elevated uncer-tainty around the future UKEU trading relationship is prompting aspiring UK scale-ups to look more closely at the US market".

"Our research found that market size, familiarity with the language and culture, and access to talent were the key drivers for businesses looking for growth overseas," he said. "The US scores highly on all these fronts. In addition, the majority of those scaleups we surveyed, 62 per cent, were from the technology sector, where the US offers particular opportunities."

There is, however, a fear of protectionism from the US government and even big companies often struggle to gain a foothold there. Tesco , Marks & Spencer , and Dixons are among those who have found it a difficult market.

Brewdog, the craft brewer, is among the latest British companies trying their hand at American expansion. The Scottish business, which was backed by private equity and crowdfunding, built a brewery in Ohio last year. James Watt, the co-founder has said the site will "lay the foundations for our American home".

Mr Noon said that Europe would remain a key market for fastgrowing start-ups. "Its fundamental strengths, which have made it our biggest trading partner for a long time, will remain," he said.

headline: Firms' £m head Sturt
author: ROBERT McAULAY
text:
NICOLA Sturgeon serves up a £million business scheme in front of a cartoon robot waiter.

The First Minister unveiled the start-up firm fund in Edinburgh.

She said: "We want to ensure the most talented entrepreneurs have the best opportunities."

BrewDog chief James Watt was among gurus promoting the project.

Wonder if they make robot ministers?

headline: Sturgeon launches £m fund to attract more entrepreneurs
author: Greig Cameron
text:
A scheme to attract the brightest entrepreneurs to Scotland is getting £ million of public money.

Nicola Sturgeon announced the outline for the Unlocking Ambition Challenge at the Startup Summit event in Edinburgh yesterday.

The project hopes to bring up to 40 people with ideas for businesses onto a 12-month development programme.

Applicants can come from anywhere in the world but must commit to moving to Scotland as well as showing how their idea can create jobs and benefit society here.

The first minister said: "We want to ensure the most talented entrepreneurs, from Scotland and elsewhere, have the best opportunities to develop their ideas here, bring them to market and create the successful new companies we need for our economy to continue to grow.

"The Unlocking Ambition Challenge is a fresh and innovative approach to how we can make this a reality.

"By tapping into the experience of Scotland's most successful businesspeople, as well as the great companies and institutions based here, we can unlock the potential of the next generation of entrepreneurs."

Ms Sturgeon and her party have been under fire from political opponents for Scotland's slow pace of economic growth in recent years.

Plans for a national investment bank and a publicly-owned energy company are among the initiatives announced in recent months. A revamp of enterprise support services has also taken place.

A panel of businesspeople will select the Unlocking Ambition candidates and offer mentoring to those who make the cut.

The group includes James Watt, cofounder of BrewDog; Chris van der Kuyl, chairman of Entrepreneurial Scotland; Leah Hutcheon, founder of Appointedd; Cally Russell, founder of Mallzee; and Amy Livingstone, cofounder of Cheeky Chompers. Mr Watt said the programme offered "time, money and amazing support". He added: "It's an extraordinary opportunity that will unearth the gamechangers of the future and I'm excited to be a part of it."

Ms Hutcheon said mentoring had played an important role in the growth of the online appointment scheduling software company she founded in Edinburgh in 2011.

She said: "My own mentors' advice and support has been invaluable, particularly over the last 18 months as we have progressed from start-up to scaleup.

"I look forward to being able to challenge others to unlock their ambition." Public sector and private sector business support organisations will also be involved in the nurturing process.

Chris van der Kuyl is a panel member at the Institute of Directors Scotland conference at Gleneagles tomorrow. The debate is being staged in association with The Times Scotland

headline: Beers to the Earth
text:
A BREWERY has made a protest beer from melted polar ice caps and Arctic cloudberries in response to the US withdrawal from the Paris Accord.

Scottish craft brewer Brewdog even sent some of Make Earth Great Again to the White House. Profits go to environmental charity 10:10.

headline: Good buy or advent scandal?
author: DANIEL JONES
text:

THEY used to be just cheap chocolate affairs for kids - but advent calendars are now big business.

Firms see them as a way of selling high-end treats for megabucks.

This year, big-name brands and retailers have teamed up to offer calendar-packed goodies including whisky, premium beauty products, craft beers and cheese.

But are they too gimmicky? We joined forces with MoneySaving Expert.com to find out.

The website's deals editor Gary Caffell said: "There is a quirky advent calendar craze with everything from wine and cheese to Baileys and Pringles —nd even Kellogg's breakfast cereal hiding behind the doors. In some cases, it's cheaper buying the products as part of an advent calendar rather than individually.

"But they're not always the best value and some are just an overpriced novelty.

"If you want a small daily treat of your favourite tipple, cheese or crisps, these advent calendars are a bit of festive fun."

Here is our verdict on whether the most popular calendars are worth the money.

headline: A DRAM OF WHISKY NEWS
text:
If you've ever fancied owning shares in a distillery, now is your chance. The Lakes Distillery, in the heart of the Lake District National Park, is about to offer £m worth of shares in a crowdfunding exercise aimed at small investors. This new kid on the block was launched three years ago with £0m raised from wealthy investors. This time shares will be available in smaller quantities via Crowdcube, allowing a minimum investment of £0. The scheme is similar to the one deployed by craft beer maker BrewDog.

There is a joke told about the famous Speyside distillery, Glenlivet - if you find it by accident, then you're lost. In a remote area of the Cairngorm mountains, its isolated location pays tribute to Glenlivet's moonshining past. Today, its famous Standard 12-year-old malt epitomises the soft, smooth, lighter Speyside whisky loved by fans. Now Glenlivet has added to its offering with the Cellar Collection, showcasing superb older malts from the archives.

headline: Monzo plans crowdfunding push to deepen ties with customers
author: Hannah Murphy
text:
Fast-growing bank plans to raise headcount and offer more retail banking services

Banking app Monzo is planning to launch one of the UK’ largest crowdfunding efforts next year to give customers in the fast-growing bank what it calls “ greater share of ownership”

Tom Blomfield, chief executive, said the start-up, which raised £1m in a private equity fundraising earlier this month, intends to make a further cash call of between £0m and £0m in 2018.

The group wants to expand its 200-strong team to between 300 and 400 people next year to prepare to offer more retail banking services, he told the Financial Times.

However, the main driver of the crowdfunding push would be “o enable the customer base to own part of the bank” Mr Blomfield said, adding that crowdfunding created a “enuine sense of ownership”

The challenger bank, which was founded in 2015, offers pre-paid cards that monitor users’pending to help people to manage their finances. It is now in the process of rolling out current accounts to its half a million customers after receiving a full banking licence in April this year, and aims to close its pre-paid cards business next year. It is among a number of app-based fintech companies such as Revolut, Starling and Atom, that have gained banking licences recently.

Monzo completed a more traditional funding round of £1m earlier this month from existing investors including Passion Capital and a number of new US backers such as Goodwater Capital, Stripe and venture capitalist Michael Moritz, through his charitable investment vehicles. The round valued the company at £80m.

While Monzo did not give an exact date for its crowdfunding launch, the move reflects continued appetite among some growth stage companies for alternative and often lightly-regulated mechanisms for raising capital.

Crowdfunding allows ordinary investors the chance to buy shares in unlisted companies via online platforms. The UK’ largest single crowdfunding to date stands at £9m, raised by brewer and pub chain BrewDog in 2016.

In 2016, UK start-ups raised about £3.3m across more than 17,500 crowdfunding campaigns, according to data from tracking website The Crowdfunding Center. The average raised per project stood at £4,227.

Mr Blomfield said Monzo was preparing a formal prospectus for the latest crowdfunding campaign, in order to comply with EU rules around larger raises using the mechanism. The digital bank has already raised about £m in total via the crowdfunding route, he added.

The absence of a secondary market has been one of the major downsides to equity crowdfunding to date, with investors being required to hold their shares in a start-up until it lists on the stock market or is bought out.

Mr Blomfield he expected the company to float on the stock market in the next three to five years.

Over time, Monzo plans to provide additional services, from discounts at shops to mortgages offered by third parties. However, it has experienced several operational hiccups in recent months, including system failures.

headline: Brewdog  raises £m A brewery [...]
text:
news in brief

Brewdog raises £m A brewery has raised more than £ million in its latest crowdfunding venture after nearly 9,500 people bought shares. Brewdog is seeking at least £0 million from the share issue to expand its operations. About half has been earmarked to extend its main brewery in Ellon, Aberdeenshire. A further £ million will help to fund a sour beer operation at the site. The remaining £ million will be used to hire staff and improve systems as the company expands in its domestic and overseas markets.

headline: WHAT better way to recharge [...]
text:
WHAT better way to recharge after a hectic festive season and beat the post-Christmas blues than with a relaxing hotel or spa break? Stobo Castle, set in the beautiful Borders countryside, offers excellent service, fine food, a fabulous spa and fitness classes.

Splash about in the 25-metre ozone pool, with panoramic views, or try out the new crystal healing and Chakra balancing treatment. Prices start from £45.

Indulge yourself with the Suite splendour package at Glasgow's Grand Central Hotel, which includes a bottle of Champagne, luxury gift-boxed toiletries, a fruit plate and delicious chocolatedipped strawberries. Prices start from £16. Or retreat from the outside world to the elegant Blythswood Square Hotel, also in Glasgow, which offers sophisticated dining and two bars.

Try out holistic face and body treatments in the fabulous spa, slip into the range of relaxation pools - or head to the exclusive Cinema club. Prices start from £40.

Malmaison in Glasgow is offering fabulous 'his and hers' experiences. The Bisol Prosecco Lunch —or ladies —tarts with a Prosecco and canapé reception, followed by a delicious three-course meal paired with favourite tipples including Brut Jeio, Brut Crede and Jeio Rosé

A guest speaker from Bisol Wines will host the February 10 event and guide you through the tasting. Cost: £5 each.

For guys, the Bavarian Brewdog Brunch on March 3 is sure to be a hit.

After enjoying the flagship Punk IPA and nibbles on arrival, Brewdog ambassador and event host Patrick will give guests the lowdown on their range of quirky crafts, ales and lager —ach one paired with an exciting fusion dish of German and Scottish cuisine.

Cost: £9 each.

headline: BREWING IN THE NAME OF...
author: ARAN BRAZIL
text:
My Round

THOSE masters of beery marketing have been at it again and this time BrewDog have taken square aim at our national pride with the release of their new beer —t's exclusively for us.

Aptly named 'Hello my name is Niamh', it's a 8.2% ABV double IPA brewed with American hops and infused with elderflower. It comes as a part of the company's celebration to all those involved in its Equity for Punks crowdfunding initiative and a drive to seek out more people who enjoy independent craft beer with its latest stock offering.

There were nine country beers brewed for the series, each with its own specific ingredient. Others include lingonberries for Sweden, cherries for Germany and lemons for Italy. Unfortunately for those like myself wanting to try them all, each is for its local market only.

In recent years BrewDog has also been hinting at opening a bar in Dublin —ut there's little information about when. So for now, you'll have to make do with picking up a bottle in your local off-licence.

headline: CHEERS FOR A GREAT YEAR OF BREWING
author: ARAN BRAZIL
text:
AS we come to the end the year, it's time for my Golden Pint awards — personal retrospective of all things beer from the 12 months.

Overall it's been a fantastic year for the art of craft brewing with the quality and range of locally produced beer lifting all the time making for my toughest list yet. Here's Part One.

Best Irish Cask Beer WEST Kerry Brewery/Beoir Chorca Dhuibhne, Uncle Columb's Mild Red Ale (3.5%ABV); A good mild ale on cask is one of those simple pleasures that makes you appreciate good beer. Lighter in alcohol but not in flavour with soft carbonation, light mouth-feel, sweet roasted malt and a touch of bitterness.

Best Overseas Bottled or Canned Beer RODENBACH canned (5.2%ABV) While BrewDog/Cloudwater New England IPA V2 bottle was fantastic, it was extremely limited and had a short fresh shelf life. So instead I picked a beer available year-round which is now in available in cans. Rodenbach is an iconic Flemish red ale, a sour blend which is tart, fruity and a great starting point for those looking for a stepping-stone into the world of sour beers.

Best Branding HOPFULLY Brewing Co, Dublin.

It's fantastic to see the passion for brewing going beyond the beer alone. They produced some wonderful and eye catching labels thanks to a creative process involving the brewery's art director who commissioned artists based on the name and story behind the beer.

Best New Irish Brewery HOPFULLY Brewing Co, Dublin. Following up those great labels was some equally great beers. Launching with three core beers, including a love-it-or-hateit sorachi ace single hop California common. They continued to take chances with beet juice, a beetroot saison with sage and lemon thyme and a Rascals Brewing collaboration, King Kumquat, a tart fruit IPA. While some of the releases may not be everyone's taste, they should be applauded for brewing quality beer with a difference.

Pub/Bar of the Year CARIBOU, Galway. If it weren't for the neon red sign outside you might miss it. Despite its pitch black exterior and equally dark interior, this place has a warm chic vibe, lively social atmosphere and huge craft beer selection. You're just as likely to end up in a game of battleship with a stranger as you are to find that hidden gem of a beer. Almost makes a visit to Galway worth it alone.

Best New Pub/Bar UNDERDOG, Dublin. You can't get any more central. This is a cozy basement bar tucked away below Brogan's on Dame Street deliveries a great range of everchanging Irish and international beers across 20 taps. The staff are quick to serve, love a good chat and you can even check what's currently pouring via their twitter.

Beer Festival of the Year WHITE Hag's Hagstravaganza. A show-stopping list of more than 50 world class beers, a simple and effective one token equals one beer system and luck with the weather made this head and shoulders stand out of the year.

headline: MY GOLDEN WONDERS OF YEAR IN BEER
author: ARAN BRAZIL
text:
My Round

THE end of the year is Golden Pint Awards time —y personal retrospective of all things beer from the past year. Here's Part 2: BEST IRISH KEG BEER Yellow Belly, Citra Pale Ale (4.8% ABV) IT'S been quite the year for the Wexford brewery. A steady stream of top-notch releases and the completion of a new, higher-capacity brewery. Their core range Citra Pale Ale is an outstanding example of a hop-forward pale that is ideal for draught pints. Medium carbonation and soft mouth-feel awash with refreshing citrus.

BEST IRISH BOTTLED BEER DOT Brew, Seasons of Saison TECHNICALLY not an individual brew, but a series of four releases. It highlights the diversity of experimentation within a single style, in this case, saison. Summer is a classic saison, after which the releases are tweaked to reflect the other periods, ending with a port and rum cask-aged Black Winter Saison.

BEST IRISH CANNED BEER White Hag, The Púa Berry Hibiscus & Ginger (3.5% ABV) THIS release is a stroke of genius, and the non-beer drinkers' beer. A tweak of their existing dry-hopped lemon sour, it looks and smells like nothing you've come to expect of beer. The sweetness of the berries and hibiscus balances out the sour base. The lower ABV makes it an enjoyable lighter option.

BEST OVERSEAS DRAUGHT BrewDog, Elvis Juice (6.5%) ONE of the best fruit IPAs on the market. The pithy tang of the grapefruit perfectly complements the pine and citrus of American hops, while a malt base balances it back to finish on the slightly sweeter side for the style.

BEST IRISH COLLABORATION BREW White Hag/Kinnegar, Hare and The Hag (6.3% ABV) IT was encouraging to see collaborations between Irish breweries this year, a positive sign of openness and innovation in the brewing community. My choice just pipped the equally excellent Sullivan's/Hellfire Brew Club Kiwi Lime Pale Ale, due to its broader reach. This coffee nitro milk stout is an espresso martini in a bottle, ideal for an end-of-night beer.

BEST OVERALL Yellow Belly, Citra Pale Ale (4.8% ABV) SIMPLE, enjoyable and widely available. Craft beer doesn't have to be about huge ABVs or exotic ingredients, just good quality brewing.

BEST IRISH BREWERY Yellow Belly, Wexford THERE were some great releases from Whiplash, DOT Brew, Galway Bay and the much under-represented Boundary. However, my choice is Yellow Belly for the second year in a row, it continues to impress with a range of styles, wide availability and well-brewed beers.

BEST OVERSEAS BREWERY Northern Monk, Leeds, UK THEIR special releases have been non-stop. Fruit lassiinspired D-IPAs to the hops/malt/ yeast trilogy series that highlights the profiles of each ingredient. Add countrywide availability and it's a winner.

headline: Bosses offer 'pawternity' leave to let workers bond with new pets
author: Will Pavia
text:
Campaigners for workers' rights have long lamented that the US is the only industrialised nation that fails to mandate paid leave for new parents. But at least some provisions are now being made for workers who have a new dog. In Manhattan several companies are offering paid leave for employees in the first blush of pet-ownership.

"Paw-ternity leave", as it is known in some quarters, is intended to give owners time to get to know their terrier, chi-huahua or labrador. Pet companies and tech start-ups are among the first to recognise the need to give their workers leave for new dogs as well as children.

When Laurel Peppino recruits for the data platform provider Mparticle she can promise them two weeks' paid leave should they adopt a rescue dog, which may require training and will almost certainly need to be taken for walks. "We offer maternity and paternity leave and a pet is just another member of the family," Ms Peppino, the company's head of talent acquisition, said. She added that the policy ensured a level playing field for workers, whether they had acquired a dog or child."We don't discriminate just because they aren't human," she told The Wall Street Journal.

Meredith Heller, the company's events marketing director, said she had taken a fortnight's leave after adopting Bodie, a beagle mix, from a New York shelter. He settled in well and Ms Heller and the dog were able to spend the time visiting a local dog run and meeting other dog-owners on the pavements of Brooklyn. "It was nice to have that option to really get to know him," she said. Mparticle is based in the Flatiron District, a part of Lower Manhattan now known as Silicon Alley for its large number of tech start-ups, many of which have similar policies towards dogs. The rental company Loftey offers paid leave for workers whose pet has died and time off for veterinary appointments. "We love our pets," Ori Goldman, a co-founder, explained. Some of the start-ups and creative agencies in the neighbourhood also allow dogs to accompany their owners to work.

The policy of offering paid leave for pet owners has been pioneered by a department of the Belgian company Mars, which owns Whiskas and Pedigree, and by the Scottish beer company Brewdog, which offers leave to employees at its brewery in Ohio. Employees can take a week's leave to make the most of those precious bonding moments that come and go so quickly in the first stages of dog ownership.

headline: WE HAVE NO BOOZE FREE FEARS
author: ARAN BRAZIL
text:
My Round

HAPPY New Year! I hope everyone had a fulfilling festive break.

As is tradition, a lot of folks have made their New Year resolutions, usually wildly ambitious targets of daily nausea inducing crossfit gym sessions or quitting smoking cold turkey, battling cravings that resemble something from Trainspotting. I say more power to you, if even you only last a short period; it's an achievement to be proud of. Dry-January — no-drink monthly pledge —s the one that I've heard most of people in my social circles are happy to take up. It joins no-alcohol-November as a targeted health driven absence month. It's a great idea for many reasons. It's an achievable goal thanks to its temporary nature, both months are on the shoulders of the festive season — period in which we all tend to over-indulge and get the guilts —nd it works on the principle that once a larger group of people take up an idea, it will become mainstream and more successful.

Of course, it isn't the only method to making a healthier change, instead of one solid month of alcohol absence, what about just one week each month? This is what I'll be aiming to achieve myself this year. Stretching it over a year should by all means make the task easier and I should accumulate roughly two and a half months alcohol free. It's better suited to my lifestyle as it's more flexible, allowing any single week to be selected within that month to avoid clashing with the fancy dinner night out or a big birthday bash. That's not to say I'll be a holed up in my house for the chosen week, avoiding the pubs and beer completely either. I enjoy the flavour of beer and quality nonalcoholic beers are readily available these days, some almost passable as the 'real thing'. Unfortunately there's no Irish produced N/A beer yet, so here are three imports to quench that beer thirst. ERDINGER, WEIZEN ALKOHOLFREI. (GERMAN)

Probably one of the most widely available N/A beers in the country. It's full on foam during pouring with the head almost magically growing back after each sip. Slightly sweet with loads of banana, cereal, and a lingering fuzz finish. STIEGL, FREIBIER ALKOHOLFREI. (AUSTRIA) Relatively new to Ireland having launched in June last year. Pours clear with firm white head. There's a bang of sweet grain on the nose but the flavour is well balanced, starting somewhat dry and finishing with the taste of mild black tea and honey. BREWDOG, NANNY STATE IPA. (SCOTLAND) Hands down the hop-head's N/A beer of choice. Inspired by a US west coast pale ale, it's a touch watery in body but it's got a lovely pine and citrus taste expected of the style with a refreshing bitter finish.

headline: SHARES THAT GET 25PC OFF BOOKS AND MEALS .?.?.?OR EVEN A DISCOUNT ON A MULBERRY HANDBAG! INVESTMENT EXTRA
author: HOLLY BLACK
text:
By backing the right business, investors can get more than just a financial return on their shares.

Many companies offer perks and discounts to those who buy their shares.

Depending on how many shares you own and when you buy them, these perks could entitle you to some serious discounts.

But investors seeking a bargain need to watch out for the small print, as some companies are more generous than others - owning enough shares to qualify for perks could cost more than you thought! If you have just one share in publishing company Bloomsbury you get 35pc off the retail price of its books, while pub chain Mitchells & Butlers will send 12 vouchers to its investors to use across its outlets. Other perks sound generous but may not be quite so appealing once you calculate the cost of owning enough shares to qualify for them.

For example, pub group Fuller Smith and Turner supplies investors with a shareholder Indulgence card, giving a 15pc discount on food and drink in its pubs and hotels. But to get it you have to own 500 shares - and they are 951p each, so you'd need to spend £,755.

You require 250 shares in Restaurant Group to get a booklet of 12 vouchers offering 25pc off at its chains, which include Chiquito and Frankie & Benny's.

At 301p each, that's a total spend of £53.

Luxury brand Mulberry also requires investors to own 250 shares before they are entitled to a 20pc discount in some stores. Its shares are priced at 1052.5p each, meaning you'd have to spend £,631 to earn the discount - for that you could buy three of its Bayswater Tote bags (£25 each) and a Darley Satchel bag (£50).

A Merlin annual pass gets you into attractions including Thorpe Park and Madame Tussauds, and costs £79 for adults.

But buying 317 shares in the business will get you a 40pc discount on two of these. With shares currently 357p each it will cost £,132 to qualify for a discount worth about £40.

Hold 1,000 shares in housebuilder Persimmon for at least 12 months and you could get 2pc off one of its new homes, up to a maximum discount of £,000. But, at 2778p each, it will cost £7,780 to own enough shares to w.

Others are less demanding. All shareholders in suit retailer Moss Bros get 20pc off its full-price merchandise, while investors in toy and game maker Character Group get 20pc off online orders.

Even some companies not listed on the stock exchange offer perks. Craft beer maker Brewdog gives investors in its Equity for Punks scheme 5pc off in its pubs.

Experts say that while perks can be an attractive bonus, they should not form a major part of your reason for backing a business. Buying shares is more about the potential for money to grow.

Those Persimmon shares, which might not seem worth it based on the discount they offer, are up more than 50pc over the past year, and would have turned a £0,000 investment into £5,200 over that period. Meanwhile, the generous Moss Bros shares have dipped more than 10pc during that time.

Russ Mould, investment director at AJ Bell, says: 'Perks can be a bonus on top of any share price growth or dividends but there is no point buying for the benefits alone as the share price could collapse and offset their value.'

It's also important to bear in mind that buying shares in individual companies is very risky - it means you are depending on the fortunes of a single business for your returns.

By contrast, investing in a fund gets you none of the perks of investing directly in a company but it means your money is spread across the shares of around 100 companies. These are unlikely to all rise and fall at the same time, giving you a steadier return.

©aily Mail

headline: Bar is still open for would-be investors in  Brewdog
text: Brewdog has extended its latest crowdfunding campaign after raising £1.5 million from more than 24,000 investors. The brewer launched the funding drive in October and has met its minimum target of £0 million, although it is still some way short of the £0 million upper limit that it had been aspiring to. The company, which is based in Aberdeenshire, is going to keep the offer of shares, which are priced at £3.75 each, open until October in an attempt to raise more money. The cash raised will go towards new breweries in Asia and Australia, as well as opening more Brewdog bars.

headline: Brewers called Elvis win trademark fight with Presley estate
author: Stuart MacDonald
text:
Aleading Scottish brewer has won a victory in its battle with Elvis Presley's estate over the name of one of its beers (Stuart MacDonald writes).

Brewdog had an earlier ruling overturned that banned the company from calling one of its products Elvis Juice.

The company, based in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, launched its grapefruit and blood orange Brewdog Elvis Juice IPA in 2015 and says that it has become a bestseller.

However, they were contacted by lawyers from the Presley estate who demanded they change the beer's name. In response, James Watt and Martin Dickie, founders of Brewdog, changed their names by deed poll to Elvis in an attempt to prove the name was not exclusive. They insisted their beer had no connection to the singer.

Presley's estate objected after Brewdog applied to register the names Elvis Juice and Brewdog Elvis Juice as trademarks. The estate said the application overlapped with their registered trademark, Elvis, and people could mistakenly believe they had endorsed the beer.

The UK Intellectual Property Office (IPO), which rules on trademark disputes, found in the estate's favour last June but Brewdog appealed against the decision. Following an appeal hearing, it has now been given permission to trademark Brewdog Elvis Juice and a ruling ordering it to pay Presley's estate £,500 in costs has been scrapped. Brewdog was not allowed to trademark the name Elvis Juice as the IPO said there would still be a likelihood of confusion if the company name was not included in the title.

Phillip Johnson, who conducted the appeal hearing, said: "On balance I do not think that the hearing officer was entitled to take judicial notice that beer consumers who see the word Elvis will always think of Elvis Presley. It is well beyond day-today knowledge and is based on supposition. Such a finding therefore would require evidence.

"The two marks are too different for there to be direct confusion. Even with imperfect recollection the average consumer will not mistake Brewdog Elvis Juice for Elvis.

"In general, consumers are more likely to remember the first word of a mark, particularly where it is invented or unusual, and so in any comparison between the two marks the average consumer would be aware of the difference."

Mr Johnson said the issue was whether consumers would consider Brewdog Elvis Juice "to be from the same stable as Elvis. That is, put more formally, whether the two are economically linked. Put simply, the common element of Elvis is not enough on its own to make consumers think there is a link between the mark Elvis and Brewdog Elvis Juice.

"Accordingly, I find there would be no likelihood of confusion between Elvis and Brewdog Elvis Juice and allow the appeal in this respect only."

Presley died in 1977 and his name and likeness have been trademarked by Elvis Presley Enterprises. It earns millions of dollars a year through a licensing programme that grants the right to manufacture and sell Presley merchandise worldwide.

Brewdog is valued at up to £ billion and the founders were made MBEs in 2016. It has 47 bars and shops and exports to 55 countries.

headline: Presley's estate ale shook up over loss
author: ROSIE TAYLOR
text:
BEER maker BrewDog has won a legal battle with Elvis Presley's estate over the name of an ale.

It can now call the IPA BrewDog Elvis Juice —ith its own name first.

A ban was overturned by Phillip Johnson, who was appointed by the UK's Intellectual Property Office to hear the appeal.

Elvis Presley Enterprises claimed drinkers would think the estate of the late singer —nown as the King —ndorsed the ale made by the Ellon, Aberdeenshire, firm.

But Mr Johnson said: "Even with imperfect recollection, average consumers won't mistake this beer for Elvis Presley."

After the ban, BrewDog founders James Watt and Martin Dickie changed their names to Elvis to prove it was not exclusive and said EPE was "All Shook Up over our beer".

headline: BREWDOG 'S AUSSIE EXPANSION
text:
Brewdog is opening a brewery in Australia as it continues an expansion which has seen its price tag reach £bn in just over ten years.

The Scottish craft brewer, which was launched by two 24-year-old entrepreneurs in 2007, will build the facility in Brisbane, which will be between 2,000-4,000?sq?m.

The Brewhouse will include a canning facility, taproom and restaurant. And Brewdog plans to collaborate with local craft breweries in a bid to help them grow their market share in Australia.

Brewdog has also agreed a franchise tie-up with TRG Concessions in the UK to open pubs and bars in airports and train stations.

Brewdog runs 32 bars in the UK and 17 overseas including in Barcelona, Berlin and Sao Paulo. In 2017, private equity firm TSG Consumer Partners bought a 22pc stake for £13m, giving Brewdog its £bn valuation.

©aily Mail

headline: XXXX marks the spot for  Brewdog
author: Greig Cameron ;Dominic Walsh
text:
Brisbane may be the home of Castlemaine XXXX, but Brewdog aims to convert the locals to more challenging beers such as Punk IPA and Dead Pony Club after choosing the Queensland capital as the location for its first brewery in Australia (Greig Cameron and Dominic Walsh write).

The ambitious Scottish craft brewer, which had been looking at locations in Brisbane and in Newcastle, New South Wales, since last year, said yesterday that it had picked a site near the Brisbane River, in the Murarrie area.

Brewdog was founded in 2007 by James Watt, right, and Martin Dickie. Its Punk IPA is the biggest-selling craft beer in the UK, while it has 47 bars, of which 16 are overseas. In April, it will open its biggest bar, complete with a microbrewery, near the Tower of L ondon.

Last year, the Brewdog business was valued at more than £ billion after selling a 22 per cent stake for £13 million to TSG Consumer Partners, an American private equity group.

It has existing breweries at Ellon, near Aberdeen, and Columbus, Ohio, and intends to build a brewery in China at some point. The stateof-the-art Australian brewery will include a canning facility, as well as a restaurant and visitor centre. Construction is expected to begin in the second half of this year.

The site initially will serve the Australian market only, but in time it could cater for the wider Asia-Pacific region.

About 150 jobs are expected to be created over the next five years.

headline: Investors get their fingers burnt after 'pie bond' crumbles
author: James Hurley
text:
Fresh concerns have been raised over "mini bonds" offered to ordinary investors by small businesses after a restaurant company collapsed, with 324 bondholders standing to lose hundreds of thousands of pounds.

In the first example of a failure of a business which issued a mini bond via Crowdcube, the crowdfunding site, Square Pie collapsed this month with the investors who backed it in 2015 likely to lose all of the £55,500 they put in. The "pie bond" was promoted on Crowdcube as offering 8 per cent annual interest. Investors were invited to "get a slice of the pie" and told that the four-year bond would fund new restaurants and products to be sold in main supermarkets.

Rob Croxen and Will Wright from KPMG were appointed as joint administrators of Square Pie Limited on February 5 and closed all five restaurants immediately, resulting in about 50 redundancies.

Crowdcube told bondholders to contact KPMG about the failure. However, administrators told the investors that the bond had nothing to do with them at this stage because of the way it had been structured as a separate business.

The bond is held by a company called Square Pie Bonds Plc, which is not insolvent. It remains under the control of Square Pie's directors, Martin Dewey and his wife Lucy.

Once Square Pie Bonds is wound up, a claim can be filed to KPMG which would effectively make bondholders creditors to Square Pie Limited, although this may be futile as it is understood that there is very little prospect of them seeing any return.

Using Moody's data, Crowdcube had told investors thgat there was a probability of Square Pie defaulting on its credit obligations other than the bond of only 0.7 per cent.

A spokesman for Crowdcube said it had been made clear to investors that mini bonds were unsecured and put investors' capital at risk.

However, the demise of Square Pie, which had annual sales of £ million, is likely to raise questions over mini bonds. This form of funding, which has emerged in recent years, allows small and medium-sized businesses to tap their own customers for growth funds.

They typically offer an interest rate of between 6 per cent and 8 per cent a year. Returns are sometimes even higher if investors take them in the form of the products sold by the business. Credit on chocolate, beer or coffee have all been offered by companies.

There have been warnings that these returns do not fairly reflect the risks being taken since investors are effectively taking the same level of risk as any unsecured supplier. In essence, critics argue, the bonds are quasi-equity in terms of the risk they carry, without offering the chance of a significant return that shares might. Unlike more conventional bonds, the products are also "illiquid" —ou can't sell them on.

Other examples of mini bonds include Hugh Fearnley-Whittingstall's restaurant and food business, River Cottage, which secured almost £ million in less than 36 hours in 2014. After initial interest there has been a marked decline in mini bonds. Crowdcube said the last it issued was for Brewdog, the beer business, in 2016.

Rob Murray Brown, who has been following the equity crowdfunding industry since its emergence seven years ago, questioned whether Square Pie had been suitable for issuing a mini bond. "We all thought these bonds were safer than the equity offers on Crowdcube. Turns out they are not."

Mr Dewey, founder of Square Pie, told investors he was devastated that he could not make the "restaurants work in the current economy" having spent 15 years building the business.

headline: Puppies, protest training and other perks that interest employees
author: Rhymer Rigby
text:
From new-dog leave to help to build a home office space, some staff treats are surprisingly high value

Unlimited holiday allowance; no set work hours; on-site massage and yoga; paid sabbaticals. Such perks were novelties two decades ago and largely confined to start-ups, but now they are increasingly mainstream.

At Dropbox, the US-based file sharing company, some staff enjoy unlimited vacation, which they can take as they like so long as their work gets done. Many of its sites have gyms, game rooms, free snacks and sometimes professional chefs and on-site restaurants. Other benefits include counselling.

There is also a monthly wellness benefit, says Adrienne Gormley, head of customer experience at Dropbox, “ sum that every employee can claim for physical activities once a month” They can spend it on anything from running shoes to a massage.

Dropbox is indicative of how perks have changed. Where once companies offered benefits (pensions and cars), which were valuable, and perks (free cake), which were fun, the two now form a spectrum —nd the value of some apparently frivolous perks is surprisingly high.

John Lamphiere, managing director for Europe, the Middle East and Africa at Glassdoor, the job site and company rating company, says lavish perks are common in sectors such as technology. They can include allowances for “ellness, starting a family and building your own home office space”

However, Mr Lamphiere adds, Glassdoor research suggests that traditional “ore”enefits such as pensions, health insurance and time off are still the ones that matter most to staff. Those for certain groups —uch as parental leave—ave less of an impact. “iche benefits only apply to a fraction of your staff at any one time,”e says.

The value of individual items can be high. In the UK, for instance, the monetary value of a free healthy lunch might match that of private healthcare contributions.

Some perks are more about quality of life. Ikea gives all staff their children’ first day at school off. It was one of the first retailers in the UK to guarantee staff at least one weekend off in four.

At Patagonia, the outdoor clothing manufacturer, perks include the chance to go surfing at lunchtime but also support for employees’ocial activism. It has pledged to pay bail for employees if they are arrested during a peaceful protest, so long as they have undergone its training for protesting safely.

Ultimately, Glassdoor has found that fun perks are some way down the list of reasons that people join companies. They rarely cost much, however, and they also have publicity value.

If you provide quirky perks, from “ew-puppy”eave (offered by BrewDog) to days off to protest, people will discuss it on social media. They might join your organisation for the pension and the healthcare, but it may be the on-site sauna (TransferWise) that first brings you to their attention.

headline: ALL THE FUN OF THE TOP BREWS FAIR
author: ARAN BRAZIL
text:
PEARSE Lyons, the beverage industry entrepreneur, has had a busy year since his flagship company, Alltech, hosted the last Craft Brews and Food Fair at Dublin's convention centre.

Since then, he has opened the Pearse Lyons Distillery in a converted church (with a flashy glass spire) in the Dublin Liberties' historic 'golden triangle' —n area once famed for its whiskey production.

There, he began to produce Pearse Irish Whiskey and Pearse Reserve, and is only the second genuine Dublinbased whiskey producer (the other being Teelings, also in Dublin 8).

The company also announced plans to relocate its Station Works Brewery from Newry to the former Macardle Moore brewery site in Dundalk with plans that could see it become one of the country's largest craft breweries.

l Station Works already brews Finn Irish Craft Lager, the Foxes Rock ales, stout and lager, and a collection of supermarket ownbrands.

sob y ttBB i This year's Alltech Craft Brews and Food Fair is back at the Dublin Convention Centre from March 8-10, and the line-up shows that it's a constantly evolving event.

CMany of last year's exhibitors, especially those more established names, have been replaced with newer, and in some cases smaller, local producers. Meanwhile, the number of international breweries in attendance has grown and expanded to include some of the industry's trailblazers.

The British brewers Beavertown, Brewdog and Five Points Brewery are making their festival debuts, and all are actively seeking to expand their shelf and pub presence in Ireland.

Undoubtedly, one of the biggest buzzes will be about the first ever appearance of Californian craft powerhouse Ballast Point Brewing Company. The multi-award winning craft brewery has been admired for the taste and quality of its beer in the US but is only known this side of the pond since it made headlines after it was purchased for $1bn (€00million) in 2015. Debate still rages as to whether the firm was worth the huge sum paid.

Unsurprisingly, given the huge growth in small batch whiskey and gin over the past few years, the event will for the first time feature a dedicated Distillers Quarter. Alltech's own Pearse Whiskey and Ha'penny Dublin Dry Gin will be on show, along with a rather eclectic mixture of styles and brands including St Patrick's Sloe and Honey Gin, distilled from Lady Clare and Rosetta potatoes.

Interestingly it's Brewdog's relatively new LoneWolf gin that caught my attention, as the brewer/distiller seems to be pitting us its spirits offering via a new Irish premium drinks distributor, Barry & Fitzwilliam. All told, it's looking to be quite the festival.

headline: Brewer set to put guests in Doghouse
text:
Brewdog is pressing ahead with plans to build a 22-room hotel at its Scottish brewery. It also intends to add a new brewhouse and additional canning and packaging facilities at the site in Ellon, Aberdeenshire.

James Watt and Martin Dickie founded Brewdog in 2007 and its Punk IPA is the biggest selling craft beer in the UK. The business, which also has 47 bars, was valued at more than £ billion last year after selling a 22 per cent stake for £13 million to an American private equity group. Plans for a hotel, to be named The Doghouse, have been on the drawing board for more than a year but the company felt that Aberdeenshire council was asking too much for adjacent land. Brewdog will instead acquire a 3.25-hectare site from the manufacturing firm Power Jacks and hopes to open the hotel next year.

headline: There's a helluva draught in my room
author: TRACEY BOLES
text:
ALE-ON-TAP HOTELEN SUITE BEER PUMPS

BOOZE firm BrewDog is opening the world's first craft ale hotel —ith beer taps in each room.

The DogHouse, with 26-bedrooms, will also have builtin shower fridges so guests can sup as they bathe.

And they will be able to watch the stuff being made at the firm's brewery next door —ffering the "ultimate immersive beer experience".

Co-founder James Watt said: "It's our gift to craft beer fans making the pilgrimage to our brewery. A beer hotel's always been high on our agenda.

"Now we can realise that dream. It will be the ultimate for fans seeking hops with holidays — beer nirvana."

BrewDog says it will ensure room beer taps are not "abused" SipAdvisor . . how it could look " once it opens in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, early in 2019 as part of an HQ expansion.

Booze will be mainly its own ales but it will also offer rival beers. It plans a US hotel later next year.

@IrishSunOnline

headline: My Round
author: ARAN BRAZIL
text:
DESTINATION BREW HOTEL

SCOTLAND'S Brewdog is set to open a hotel —ptly named The Doghouse —t its Aberdeenshire brewery, the company HQ.

Ever the masters of beer PR, Brewdog drew our attention to the fact that each of the 26 rooms will be fitted with its own in-room beer tap, so guests can happily tuck into their favourite brew while they watch a movie, take a bath, or whatever you fancy.

The opening came mired with paperwork problems, however. Last March, the brewer announced it would resort to building its first flagship hotel at its new brew site in Columbus, Ohio, after a spat with the Aberdeenshire council over the purchase of land. Scotland's loss became America's gain. Not that the US opening wasn't a wise business choice, given that the US is the birthplace of the modern craft beer renaissance and beer tourism is well established there, with enthusiasts often planning holidays around the locations of their favourite craft breweries. Whether you're a fan of Brewdog's beer or not, it was encouraging to see that the company continued to push on with, and succeed, in its plans to open its Scottish hotel, showing its belief in its home market.

But the wider issue is that craft beer has done the unthinkable, breaking the barrier between the craft drinks industry (relatively niche) and tourism (entirely mainstream). Craft beer now has brands that are large enough, with enough of a loyal fan base, to trust that beds in a hotel under its name will be filled.

Other industries have done it —he UK/St Lucia chocolate producer Hotel Chocolat has not only retail stores, but hotel and restaurant businesses using the same name, all feeding into one another as a tourism brand that originated in a foodstuff. Wine, of course, was the pioneer of cross-pollination, transforming entire regions of France, the US and Australia into tourism destinations in their own right, simply for the presence of vineyards, tasting centres and restaurants hung on the domain or label. Craft beer tourism is officially now a thing too.

It does beg the question, will any Irish brewery have the confidence, or investment, to dip a toe in the hotel or restaurant business? Breweries such as Galway Bay, Metalman and Brúave already set up their own breweryrun pubs, while West Kerry Brewery already offers punters B&B accommodation on-site.

WIN The proposed Craft Drinks Bill, which would allow breweries to sell its products on-site to tour groups, is still making its way through the Dail and hopefully will be passed in the near future, but until then, a beer-branded hotel is some way off. So while we await that string in our own tourism bow, we'll have settle for the minute flight Scotland to see if the beer hotel concept really works.
