headline: Ale brewhaha
author: ROB PATTINSON
text:
CAMRA PROBE INTO GOAT TOSSER BEER

BEER group bosses asked brewers to explain the name of their "Goat Tosser" ale over fears it could be offensive.

The Campaign for Real Ale demanded an explanation before letting the beer be served at a festival. The organisation had animal welfare concerns over Goat Tosser and thought another beer called Happy Paranoids could insult people with mental health issues. But brewer Angles Ales said they chose names with stories behind them and both were sold with no problems at last year's Leicester Camra Beer Festival.

Boss Paul Dickinson explained that Goat Tosser refers to an ancient Fenland practice of throwing goats over dykes when they were afraid to cross bridges.

And Happy Paranoid relates to the sideeffects he experienced from malaria tablets on a business trip to Vietnam.

Paul, who runs the brewery in Peterborough with Dr Nicholas Ashley, added: "We couldn't believe it when they phoned up and asked for an explanation.

"The bloke was apologetic but said Camra was insisting on full details about anything with a questionable name.

"This is just another example of snowflakes getting carried away with something harmless."

Real ale brewers often give their beers humorous or peculiar names. Angles Ales previously entered a beer called Feral Nun into a Camra festival. And others available include Cairngorm Brewery's Sheepshaggers Gold and BrewDog's Dead Pony Club.

Paul added: "Beer is supposed to be fun."

Camra said it carried out the checks on names to "uphold our core values and beliefs".

Its head of communications Tom Stainer added: "Labels and images that are offensive or derogatory have no place in the modern beer world.

"We need to ensure everyone feels comfortable attending our festivals, regardless of background, sex, age or origin."

robert.pattinson@thesun.co.uk

headline: They all ad up to good business
author: Shaf Rasul
text:
I'VE had a serious sense of déàu over the past week.

Some brands just can't help themselves when it comes to marketing their products - they simply have to poke the bear.

Sometimes controversy-stoking campaigns work, and sometimes they don't, but many companies have made a virtue of this approach. The likes of BrewDog, Ryanair, and Paddy Power spring to mind.

You might recall that in 2012 the Advertising Standards Authority received complaints regarding an Irn-Bru advert which featured a baby named Fanny.

Social media criticism at the time included comments such as, 'That ad is an embarrassment to Scotland.' But the backlash didn't stop the iconic Scottish brand from selling thousands of bottles of Irn-Bru emblazoned with that word around a year later.

The campaign followed in the footsteps of Coca Cola's 'Share a Coke' which saw the US giant's logo replaced with popular names.

Limited edition collector's item This proved a huge success for the brand, with people all over the UK keeping an eye out for their name and sharing photos on social media.

Not to be outdone, Irn-Bru's slyly 'Scotified' version included names such as Rab, Tam and Senga.

Just last month, someone cheekily listed one of the bottles of 'Fanny' Irn-Bru on eBay, describing it as a limited edition collector's item. Bids reached a mammoth £,700.

In 2013, the Advertising Standards Authority received even more complaints about another one of the company's adverts.

This one featured a 'MILF', her son, and his friend. Complainers branded the advert offensive and irresponsible, due to the sexual and inappropriate scenario between the mother and young men.

Once again, Irn-Bru was cleared following an ASA investigation.

Another of Irn-Bru's controversial adverts to face scrutiny in 2013 was one depicting a Scottish father drinking Irn-Bru to keep his temper in check as he met his daughter's England-supporting boyfriend. The advert was labelled 'anti-English'.

Fast forward to 2018 and a new advertising campaign from Scotland's other national drink has —urprise, surprise —ome under fire.

The campaign is focused around the slogan 'Don't be a can't, be a can'.

Before I go on to discuss my thoughts on this latest advert in more detail, what is the one age-old saying in the marketing and communications industry? There's no such thing as bad publicity!

If anything, I think the backlash-baiting has been very positive for Irn-Bru.

Its 'close to the bone' adverts have almost become expected and while they do attract complaints from a minority, I think most Scots would be disappointed if the drinks company played it safe.

Regarding the 'can't' campaign, it's obviously a clever play on words to conjure connotations with a less savoury word. However, the key element is that it's up to the viewer how they interpret it.

It's not innately offensive in itself —t requires the viewer to take a step and infer offence.

Much like the latest Irn-Bru slogan, in business a can-do attitude gets you noticed.

It's the companies and individuals who say they can and do deliver on this that make the biggest impact.

Over the years, Irn Bru has positioned itself as the Scottish brand with that can-do attitude —t's the drink that 'gets you through' and 'the soft drink for hard men' made from girders.

That cheeky, dry Scottish wit is what gives it personality and creates the phenomenal impact it has enjoyed for decades.

Yes, there are definitely boundaries that should not be crossed when it comes to advertising, but Irn-Bru does a fantastic job of treading this fine line and letting people's own imaginations lead them to an alternative meaning.

I for one hope that the company continues to Bru up a storm with its memorable adverts.

headline: 'Beer for girls' row
text:
A CRAFT brewer which created a pink "beer for girls" to highlight the gender pay gap has seen its campaign backfire.

BrewDog claims the rebranding of its Punk IPA beer is an attack on lazy sexist marketing. But it has been accused of a "lazy" marketing stunt.

The beer will cost a fifth less to women, to highlight the 18.1 per cent average pay gap.

Critics hit out on Twitter. One said the Scottish firm had created "what it set out to avoid".

Global marketing chief Sarah Warman said: "We anticipated some people might not appreciate the irony of Pink IPA."

headline: CITY PEOPLE
author: Robin Pagnamenta
text:
The feuds, the faces and the farcical Robin Pagnamenta @Robin_Pag

Business big shot

NAME RICK HAYTHORNTHWAITE AGE 61 POSITION LONDON-BASED ADVISORY PARTNER, MOELIS & COMPANY

Since being founded 11 years ago, Moelis & Co has established itself as one of the world's leading investment banking advisory boutiques (Harry Wilson writes).

Much of its reputation has been built on hiring some of the world's best advisers and, with the firm working on the possible stock market listing of Saudi Aramco , it is not surprising that it has now hired a top energy industry veteran in Rick Haythornthwaite.

The Aramco listing is expected to value the company at more than £ trillion, making it the biggest flotation in history, so Mr Haythornthwaite's nearly 40 years of corporate experience should help Moelis. He spent 17 years with BP , including spells in the North Sea, Alaska, France and Venezuela, is a former chief executive of Invensys and chairs Mastercard and Centrica.

Ken Moelis, chairman and chief executive of the advisory business, called Mr Haythornthwaite a "highly regarded industrialist", and added: "Our clients will undoubtedly benefit from Rick's deep relationships and unique background."

THE OPEC DINNER THAT MIGHT HAVE BEEN HARD TO SWALLOW

The movers and shakers in the oil business have descended on Houston for Cera Week, one of the industry's annual shindigs. On Monday, Mohammed Barkindo , Opec's secretary-general, and other senior officials had a private dinner with executives from the US shale industry. With rising shale output undermining Opec 's efforts to prop up prices, it must have been an awkward affair.

Though details of the meal remain secret, oil traders were happy to speculate about what had been served up. One fake menu on social media yesterday suggested that diners had tucked into starters featuring a "mixed message served on a bed of half-promises", followed by a "Game of chicken with essence of Standard Oil". To finish it off, Opec -style, a "Viennese fudge to a traditional recipe". It was a prix fixe, naturally.

SEEING RED OVER PINK BEER

Brewdog, in a spirit of satire and/or sisterhood, is launching a pink "beer for girls". The independent brewer says that its new IPA Pink "might look different from the outside, but it's exactly the same on the inside …ust like the female workforce". It adds women will be able to buy the beer in its bars at a 20 per cent discount, to give men a taste for what the gender pay gap feels like.

Twitter 's reaction was, er, mixed.

Jo Stevens, Labour MP for Cardiff, tweeted: "I'm a huge @Brewdog fan but oh dear me … "Beyond shocking! Huge Fail," another user tweeted.

MAKE THE HEADLINE SING

City AM, the free financial paper, had workers in the Square Mile laughing yesterday after its ingenious front-page headline to illustrate the potential trade war between the US and European Union , a play on the Don McLean song American Pie. Christian May, the editor, said that the headline was a "team effort", but was also due to his decision to play the Don McLean back catalogue during meetings. "This decision was mocked at first, but I think we can all agree it's paid off," he added. We presume he's joking, but you never know …

headline: FEMALE TROUBLE BUBBLES
author: ROBERT McAULAY
text:
DRINK GOES PINK lCut-price 'beer for girls' lStunt sparks sexism row

BOOZE giant BrewDog was caught up in a sexism storm last night after launching a cut-price "beer for girls".

The drinks firm said rebranding its flagship Punk IPA as Pink IPA was a dig at marketing stereotypes and gender pay gaps. In a cheeky swipe at critics, bosses at the brewer, based in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, tweeted: "We've created a beer for girls. And it's pink.

"Because women only like pink and glitter, right? #Sarcasm."

But some unhappy punters complained the stunt to mark International Women's Day tomorrow left a bitter taste in the mouth.

Dr Heather Moran tweeted: "This has the right motivations but the wrong execution."

Liam Cheeseman wrote: "I really appreciate the sentiment, but I'm a little confused by this strategy."

The pink-labelled, 5.6 per cent ABV ale will cost a fifth Irony ... BrewDog's Sarah less than its blue big brother — nod to the 18.1 per cent average pay gap.

Global marketing chief Sarah Warman said it was an "over-the-top ridiculing" of sexist ads.

But she added: "We anticipated some people might not appreciate the irony." Twenty per cent of proceeds will be donated to charities fighting inequality.

robert.mcaulay@thesun.co.uk

headline: GOING UP
author: Mrs Crunch
text:
GOING DOWN

WELL AISLE NEVER: Morrisons will open more than 500 "mumsonly" checkouts across the UK this Sunday. The store wants to speed up shopping trips so mums can go home and put their feet up.

HOME HELP: Parents start encouraging children to help out with household tasks from the age of six, with half believing chores are the best way of promoting positive behaviour, according to a new survey.

BEER-FOR-ALL: BrewDog is giving away a million beers to raise the popularity of IPA-style beers, which it believes will overtake lager as the UK's favourite in the next decade. To claim, enter details online at brewdog.com/1million.

CRIMINAL CODE: Bank account logins, passport details and even Netflix passwords are worth money to online fraudsters, who can buy identity details for £20, according to the Virtual Private Network.

FEELING THE HEAT: Get set for rising energy prices. Mark Todd, the cofounder of Energyhelpline, says we should brace ourselves for hikes of around five per cent. "This would add up to £0 a year to energy bills," he warned.

CATCH THE THREAD: One in five of us can't sew on a button, according to Hobbycraft. That rises to nearly a third of 18 to 24-year-olds compared with just five per cent of those aged over 55.

CRIMINAL CODE: Bank account logins, passport details and even Netflix passwords are worth money to online fraudsters, who can buy identity details for £20, according to the Virtual Private Network.

FEELING THE HEAT: Get set for rising energy prices. Mark Todd, the cofounder of Energyhelpline, says we should brace ourselves for hikes of around five per cent. "This would add up to £0 a year to energy bills," he warned.

CATCH THE THREAD: One in five of us can't sew on a button, according to Hobbycraft. That rises to nearly a third of 18 to 24-year-olds compared with just five per cent of those aged over 55.

headline: 'SEXIST' RAP FOR GAL ALE
author: BEN ARCHIBALD
text:
BREWDOG AD ROW Cheap beer deal 'snubs fellas'

BOOZE giant Brewdog faces a sexism rap over claims their cutprice "beer for girls" is prejudiced against men.

The drinks firm's rebrand of flagship Punk IPA as Pink IPA has already been slammed by women as a marketing stunt.

Now ad chiefs are probing three complaints that dropping prices for female drinkers is biased against fellas. An Advertising Standards Agency spokeswoman said: "The complaints have been that it's discriminatory to sell something to women at a lower price, or that it was targeting under 18s.

"We have not made a judgement yet on whether the ads have broken the Advertising Code." But a spokesman for the brewer was defiant last night. He said: "We want everyone to be vocal about inequality, so we aren't arguing against the complaints —parking this conversation is exactly the point of Pink IPA.

"It's a parody of genderbased marketing and a criticism of inequality. Our 20 per cent discount is a reversal of the gender pay gap."

The move marked International Women's Day yesterday. The drinks firm, based in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, had tweeted: "We've created a beer for girls. And it's pink. Because women only like pink and glitter, right? #Sarcasm."

But some felt it had backfired. Dr Heather Moran wrote: "This has the right motivations but the wrong execution." And Liam Cheeseman added: "I'm a little confused by this strategy." It comes after Irn-Bru and Tunnock's both fell foul of the watchdog over cheeky ads.

Nine complaints were made over AG Barr's "Don't be a can't, be a can" campaign and the firm later apologised.

And a poster by Tunnock's was judged demeaning to women after one person moaned. It showed a female tennis player gripping a Tea Cake close to her bare thigh.

ben.archibald@the-sun.co.uk

headline: It's time to place bets on a life of leisure
text:
TempusBuy, sell or hold: what's hot in the leisure industry

Leisure can cause serious stress. As a consumer, it's not just about what you're going to do with your free time —ub, restaurant, cinema, short weekend hop or longer trip abroad —t's also about whether you're going to be able to afford it. Particularly, if you're one of the "squeezed middle", feeling the effects of higher prices and a scant increase in your real wages.

As a business hoping to make money from being involved in the diverse, and fragmented, leisure sector, you have to be tuned in to your customers' changing preferences —hink millennials and their "experiences" —s well as how much they're prepared to pay to have a spot of fun.

As a sector, leisure has been under the cosh for two years, amid worries about the economic outlook after Brexit, rising inflation and declining consumer confidence, and all in a marketplace that has become increasingly competitive. The recent woes of the casual dining sector are obvious testament to how overcrowded the sector had become.

There are signs, though, that the outlook is changing. Britain's services businesses —ffectively the engine room of the leisure industry —ere back on song last month after suffering the weakest January in a decade, according to the latest snapshot purchasing managers index survey by IHS Markit/CIPS.

Jobs grew in the sector, which accounts for 80 per cent of the economy, at the fastest pace in five months and price inflation, which can be a proper headache for the bar and restaurant trade, fell to a six-month low, the survey found.

While it's true that consumer confidence in Britain is suffering (GfK's confidence index shed a point in February as households remain worried about their finances, for example), we remain prepared to dip into our wallets and purses in the interests of a good time.

According to Greg Johnson, a travel and leisure equity research analyst at Shore Capital: "People are still spending on the essentials, of course, but the signs are they are also still going on holidays, still going out and enjoying cheaper 'experiences', such as ten-pin bowling and the cinema, what we call the affordable treats."

Outside Britain, consumers are feeling good. Analysts at UBS upgraded the European leisure sector yesterday, citing corporate earnings upgrades across the sector, rising consumer confidence and household-friendly tax breaks in America.

If it's true that the leisure market is poised for a resurgence, there are British businesses that look as if they are well placed to benefit.

TUI, the travel operator that still uses the First Choice brand but has folded Thomson Holidays into its "masterbrand", is in a strong position to capitalise on any growth in tourism. The FTSE 100 group has adapted well to the move by holidaymakers away from package deals and now makes half of its profits from tailored getaways on its own cruise ships and at its branded hotels.

TUI's shares have been on the up for the past two years, but have fallen back from a high of £6.04½eached in January to trade at about £5.34 yesterday because of worries about terrorism. If tourists return to Turkey, as it believes they will this summer, then it will do well.

GVC, the acquisitive gaming operator that is poised to swallow Ladbrokes Coral in a £ billion deal, has placed winning bets on the rise of the online punter through acquisitions including of Bwin.party and parts of Sportingbet. The shares have risen with it, of course, almost doubling from 474p to about 936p in the past two years. If GVC deals successfully with Ladbrokes' underperforming high street bookmakers, then, having moved up to the FTSE 100, it will be a force to be reckoned with.

For years drinkers have sought out themed pubs and gastro bardiners, but the traditional boozer is back in favour, particularly if it serves up a craft ale. That augurs well for JD Wetherspoon, which offers no-nonsense nosh at affordable prices but is happy to host real ale festivals and to pour a pint of Brewdog's Punk IPA.

The shares aren't cheap —ot least because it lifted its profits forecast in January —rading at more than 25 times earnings and with a yield of less than 1 per cent. Buying (and holding) on weakness has its merits.

headline: Law firm teams up with crowdfunding site
author: Greig Cameron
text:
A crowdfunding platform is aiming to expand its activities in Scotland through a partnership with a law firm.

Crowdcube is teaming up with Harper Macleod to encourage more businesses to consider equity crowdfunding. The two will work together closely and refer potential customers to each other. Since it was formed in 2011, 17 Scottish companies have raised £9.8 million on Crowdcube's platform. Last year there were three companies from Scotland which used it, to raise a total of £.7 million.

There are more than 500,000 investors registered at Crowdcube and the 630 businesses it has funded have secured more than £90 million.

Both partners believe that many companies in Scotland are still reticent to go down the crowdfunding route even though there have been success stories. Brewdog and Innis & Gunn, two craft beer companies, have raised millions through various equity schemes opened directly to the public.

Matt Cooper, the chief commercial officer at Crowdcube, said that there was a huge opportunity for Scottish businesses, while Paula Skinner, a partner in Harper Macleod's corporate team, said: "We've been impressed by Crowdcube's success rate for businesses seeking funding."

headline: Making a splash Times photographer [...]
text: Making a splash Times photographer James Glossop was named Bloomberg business photographer of the year at the UK Picture Editors' Awards for images including this of James Watt, founder of Brewdog brewery. He was also shortlisted for news photographer of the year. See more winning images on tablet and at thetimes.co.

headline: BREW'S NEW BAR
author: TRACEY BOLES
text:
CSunITY Edited by TRACEY BOLES Tel: 020 7782 4050 e-mail: tracey.boles@thesun.co.uk THE FTSE 100 ROSE 7.07 TO 7139.76 £ BUYS €.13, US $1.39. BRENT CRUDE $65.09

DOORS opened yesterday at the latest pub for craft beer firm BREWDOG.

Ut The bar in Reading, Berks, is their third new UK venue this year since the ambitious brewery raised £3million via a crowdfunding campaign.

rcr Co-founder James Watt said the town was an sa ideal location. He added: "The craft beer scene has been exploding over the past few years."

Last year the company announced plans for a boozer on the US-Mexico border. It also hopes to open a 26-room hotel in 2019 next to its HQ in Ellon, Aberdeenshire.

headline: Brewdog 's growth costs knock profit
author: Greig Cameron
text:
Profits at Brewdog have more than halved in a year of heavy investment at the drinks and pubs company (Greig Cameron writes).

Turnover increased by 55 per cent to £11.6 million in 2017 but soaring costs ate into profit. Brewdog was founded by James Watt, right, and Martin Dickie in 2007 and has breweries in Scotland and the US as well as plans for another in Australia. Its Punk IPA is the biggest selling craft beer in the UK and it runs 46 bars.

Last year Brewdog was valued at more than £ billion after TSG Consumer Partners, a US private equity group, paid £13 million for a 22 per cent stake.

Brewdog's annual report, published yesterday, cites the investment in new bars, its brewery in Columbus, Ohio, and the establishment of Lonewolf, its spirits arm, among reasons for the profit fall. There was also an increase in employee costs as staff numbers rose from 593 to 777.

A near 70 per cent rise in overheads to £5.1 million, brought pre-tax profit down to £.4 million, compared with £.8 million in the previous 12 months.

The UK is Brewdog's largest market, with turnover up by 54 per cent to £9.9 million. European sales were up 33 per cent to near £3 million and rest of the world was up 31 per cent to £.7 million.

headline: Brewdog  buys another
text:
news in brief

Brewdog has agreed to buy the Draft House chain of bars, which will add four sites to the Scottish drinks and leisure group's portfolio of more than forty bars. The transaction valued Draft House, which operates mainly in London, at close to £5 million, according to sources, though the purchase price was undisclosed. Profits at the Aberdeenshirebased Brewdog more than halved to £.4 million last year as it invested in an American brewery and a new spirits business.

headline: Cutting losses in translation
author: Greig Cameron
text:
A translation company has narrowed its losses and is forecasting a return to profit this year. Revenue at Lingo24 increased by 8 per cent to £.5 million in 2017.

The company was founded by Christian Arno in 2001 and initially was run from Aberdeen. Its headquarters are now in Edinburgh and it employs more than 160 people, with offices in London, the Netherlands, Romania, Panama and the Philippines. Customers include Adidas, Hunter Boots and Brewdog.

Mr Arno said that the company had cut losses to £00,000 last year, compared with £.8 million in the previous 12 months. He said that Lingo24 had invested heavily in the past three years in technology, as well as in developing its senior management and sales teams. He is anticipating a return to significant profit this year as a result.

About 70 per cent of revenue comes from the company's top 30 customers, which typically buy Lingo24's consultancy and technology services as well as its translation products. Mr Arno said that annual spending by its 30 largest clients had risen by 60 per cent compared with 2015.

"For many of our customers, for whom Amazon is both an opportunity and a threat, there's an increased sense of urgency to translating better," Mr Arno said.

headline: Editorial; Opinion; Leading articles Plan Souts us
text:
IF there's one man in Scotland who knows how to take a small business and turn it into a multi-million pound international success story it's Sir Brian Souter.

Using his dad's redundancy money he established the Stagecoach Group in 1980, running buses from Dundee to London.

They offered no-frills travel with homemade sandwiches in paper bags at a price people could afford. Now he has transport concerns running across the world. Now, Sir Brian is backing a tax incentive that would make it easier for other families to make the kind of investment that set him on his way with a 25 per cent top up from the Government.

It's a radical plan. Why should the taxpayer risk all that money to help entrepreneurs get a leg up, after all? The answer is simple: we've got to do something to kick start the Scots economy.

We lack business start-ups. The success of a firm like BrewDog is all the more eye-catching precisely because it is so rare.

Economic growth has been flatlining for a decade and we don't have a Google or a Facebook growing in Scotland.

What the country needs more than anything else is just one of those "unicorn" companies to bring money pouring in and, above all, create jobs.

If agencies like Scottish Enterprise have tried everything already, the sad truth is it's just not working.

Now it's time to try something else, something bold and creative and maybe a wee bit dangerous.

Maybe the Treasury should grab Sir Brian's idea —efore somebody else does.

headline: FLAG BREWHAHA
author: STUART PATTERSON
text:
Beer giant's Rising Sun design is 'like swastika'

BOOZE firm BrewDog has riled drinkers in Asia by using an old Japanese flag on its latest beer —een there as offensive as the swastika.

Cans of the Kamikaze Knitting Club stout feature a woman in a warplane with the controversial Rising Sun symbol on the side.

The company, based in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, had launched the tipple in their online store. But bosses pulled it and issued a grovelling apology after punters lined up to lay into the imagery.

Jeong Min Kim raged: "Are you crazy? The kamikaze and Rising Sun symbols are not welcome in Asia. They have the same meaning as the Nazi pattern." Hung Woon Kim posted: "It's a crazy company. I'll never have one of their Punk IPAs again."

Discussing ingredients, Jin-Ki Cho suggested: "Nazi sausage? It will defo go well together."

The imperial Rising Sun flag —itched in 1945 —nd word kamikaze are still linked by some with atrocities committed by the Japanese during World War Two. BrewDog apologised on their Asian Facebook page and said they're in talks with their South Korean teams about the blunder.

But it's thought to be still on sale in some UK pubs.

A spokesman said: "We recognise artwork for Kamikaze Knitting Club contained symbols that were inconsiderate of historical events in Asia. We're sorry and are doing everything we can to ensure this doesn't happen again."

Bosses were accused of sexism this month after rebranding flagship Punk IPA as Pink IPA and offering it cut-price to women.

stuart.patterson@the-sun.co.uk

headline: CITY PEOPLE
text:
The feuds, the faces and the farcical Dominic Walsh @walshdominic Business big shot name laura flowerdew age 42 position finance director-elect, bristol water For an industry that is judged on the purity and clarity of its product, these are murky times for the water sector.

Jeremy Corbyn and John McDonnell's Labour Party may, or more likely may not, get the chance to nationalise the water industry. But their interest in the sector has certainly moved the meter on the way people are thinking about it: not least the regulator, which has toughened its stance towards its charges but also populists on the other side of the House of Commons who don't like fat cat executives and foreign companies turning up for the dividend.

Laura Flowerdew, who is to become finance director at Bristol Water, is no shrinking violet when it comes to knowing which way things are flowing. A decade ago as a utilities analyst at Deloitte she was warning the industry might be ripe for an investigation by the competition authorities.

The West Country supplier has been through several hands since privatisation nearly 30 years ago and is owned by the Icon infrastructure fund and Itochu, the Japanese trading company.

Ms Flowerdew joins from Bristol Energy, where she has been finance director. She starts at Bristol Water in October, just in time for the industry's quinquennial review.

oh, to be in england ... or maybe down under's better Andy Haldane, the Bank of England's chief economist, and Ben Broadbent, the deputy governor, are due to deliver speeches this week. Nothing out of the ordinary there. What is a little unusual is that they are both speaking in Australia. First up, today, is Mr Haldane in Melbourne, followed by Mr Broadbent in Sydney. Both a short hop from the Gold Coast, where the Commonwealth Games are being held. A coincidence, I'm sure.

sounds about right The new chief executive of Deutsche Bank is Christian Sewing, a 47-year-old company man who started out as an apprentice at the German lender. A lifetime in finance was clearly his destiny —is surname is pronounced "saving". Nominative determinism with a German twist.

a-grade work ethic Michael Grade, aka Lord Grade of Yarmouth, may have hung up his broadcasting boots but the former chairman of the BBC and ITV is not putting on his slippers just yet. Yesterday he took on two new jobs. Arora Group, the privately owned hotel and property group, named him chairman of its Heathrow expansion advisory board while One Media iP Group, the Aimlisted digital media company, appointed him a non-executive director.

lapping it up Since its launch in 2007, Brewdog has set out its stall as the antithesis to the big "industrial" brewers. As time has passed, however, the Scottish maker of Punk IPA has itself got much bigger, building new breweries in Scotland, the US and (shortly) Australia. And now it's accelerating its expansion through acquisitions. Two weeks ago it bought the Draft House pub chain for about £5 million, then yesterday it confirmed it had bought into Hawkes, a craft cider maker based in Bermondsey, near London Bridge. It also told investors at Saturday's annual meeting that it would probably float in 2020. Global domination beckons.

headline: CITY PEOPLE
text:
Business big shot name duncan painter age 47 position non-executive director, itv

The feuds, the faces and the farcical Dominic Walsh @walshdominic There has been a bout of soul-searching at ITV since the broadcaster announced a slide in pre-tax profits in March (Gurpreet Narwan writes). As advertising revenues continue to decline and pressure from streaming specialists intensifies, Dame Carolyn McCall, its chief executive, has promised a "strategic refresh" that will help to reposition the business.

The appointment of Duncan Painter, a consumer intelligence expert with years of experience in data analysis and marketing, is part of the drive to revive the company by finding new audiences and opening up new revenue streams.

Mr Painter has been chief excutive of Ascential, a media and events company, since 2011 and is a non-executive director of Investis, a digital communications company.

Before that, he spent two years running Sky IQ, a part of the satellite broadcaster that collates information on customers to improve marketing strategies. Mr Painter joined Sky from Experian, a data analysis company, which he joined after leading the management buyout of Clarityblue, a consumer intelligence company, in 2003 and selling it to the creditchecking specialist three years later.

sainsbury's boss strikes a chord across the city If Mike Coupe, the guitar-playing Sainsbury's chief executive, decides to do a charity album, We're in the money would be top of the list, obviously. With the help of the Twitterati, here are some other potential tracks: Nick Goodway, a retired business hack, suggests It Asda to be you, while Damien McCrystal, a PR consultant, puts forward the theme song to The Waltons and the inspired Walmart again, don't know where, don't know when. With apologies to the Jacksons, I offer Aisle be there.

out and about Ross Marshall may be best known as the boss of Your Golf Travel, but he is also an investor in the biggest LGBT travel firm in the world. OutOfOffice.com, founded by Darren Burn, a former ITN News journalist, yesterday acquired Out4You, the company behind the TravelGayAsia.com and TravelGayEurope.com websites, for a six-figure sum and is aiming to raise £0 million to invest in growth. Given that Grindr, the gay dating app, was valued at $380 million in its recent sale, it could be money well spent.

along for the ride With less than a year before Britain launches an independent trade policy for the first time in four decades, many have urged the government not to run, let alone cycle, before it can walk. Two senior civil servants appeared undeterred yesterday on a trip to Boston. Antonia Romeo, below, permanent secretary at the trade department, started her day with what she called a "jet-lagged early morning spin class" alongside Alex Chisholm, her counterpart at the business department.

one that got away Brewdog, the Scottish brewer, has revealed that in 2009 it was turned down by the producers of Dragons' Den after making it past the initial application process. If it had made it on to the show, it would have offered the Dragons 20 per cent for a £00,000 investment. That stake would now be worth £60 million.

headline: Dragons' Dog miss
text:
A DRAGONS' Den rejection "still burns" the founders of BrewDog, one of its bosses admits.

James Watt and Martin Dickie were set to swap a 20 per cent stake in their Aberdeenshire brewery for a £00,000 investment.

But BBC producers did not select them in 2009 —eaning the 'Dragons' missed a chance to grab a share that would now be worth £60million.

James said: "It was a huge kick in the teeth at the time. That stinging rejection still burns today.

"But we're really happy about it now. It forced us back to the drawing board and led to people who enjoy our beer owning a part of our company."

headline: Brewdog  drafts in former Coca-Cola vice-president
author: Greig Cameron
text:
Brewdog has appointed its first chief financial officer as it prepares for more expansion overseas.

Jason Marshall, 48, joins the independent drinks and pubs group from Coca-Cola European Partners. He has held several senior roles at Coca-Cola, including most recently as vice-president of finance for its supply chain. Brewdog was founded in Aberdeenshire in 2007 by James Watt and Martin Dickie. It has breweries in Scotland and the United States and has plans to open one in Australia. It runs dozens of bars in the UK and overseas and acquired the Draft House group in March. Its best-known beer is Punk IPA and it has moved into spirits and cider production.

Brewdog said Mr Marshall would take charge of widening the company's finance function as it prepares for growth, including opening 17 bars around the world and expanding its distribution into more American states. Mr Marshall said: "You can't help but be impressed with Brewdog's track record over the last ten years.The rise of the craft beer scene in the UK owes a great deal to Brewdog's bold, resourceful and idiosyncratic approach." Mr Watt, 35, said that Mr Marshall's experience of multinational business was what the company needed. Neil Simpson, 47, was the company's first finance director when he joined in 2012. He remains with Brewdog as business development director.

Last year the company was valued at close to £ billion after TSG Consumer Partners, an American private equity group, paid £13 million for a 22 per cent stake. Brewdog also said yesterday that the latest crowdfunding drive under its Equity for Punks banner had raised £5 million from 33,000 investors.

headline: THE DASTARDLY MR DEEDES
text:
Cheery publican Charlie McVeigh is planning for life as a devoted political spouse after flogging his Draft House chain of watering holes to Brewdog for £5m. Sophia, the wife of the whiskery Old Etonian, has been elected as a Tory councillor for Kensington. McBoozer, 50, announces: 'I'm modelling myself on Denis Thatcher.'

The distinguished Wincott Foundation awards, which recognise 'outstanding achievement in the field of economic, business and financial journalism for television', didn't present its usual award for best television journalist during this week's ceremony at Mansion House. Why so? Because none were this year deemed worthy of a gong. A flying kick in the crutch to our puffed-up broadcasters, no doubt about it.

IAG chief executive Willie Walsh, 56, is quoted on fancydrawers Chelsea trattoria Ziani's website championing their Coda di Rospo alla Graticola - charcoal-grilled monkfish to you and I - as 'the best I've ever tasted'. Well, well. Wee Willie's certainly come a long way since he was flying rickety Aer Lingus 737s out of Donegal airport, hasn't he?

Charismatic Vodafone chief Vittorio Colao's newly announced replacement, £.4m-a-year chief financial officer Nick Read, might look like your average bean-counter, but he does at least have a compelling back story. The son of a humble market stall trader, Read, 53, grew up under the Heathrow flight path in listless Feltham. He attended the local comprehensive, long since demolished, where he was one of only two pupils in his year to sit A-levels.

News that those rug-chested property twins the Tchenguiz brothers - Robert and Vincent - are on non-speakers due to a row over their family trust is nothing new. Germans Adi and Rudi Dassler, respective founders of sportwear giants Adidas and Puma, fell out during a 1943 bombing over Bavaria. Clambering into the air raid shelter, Adi remarked: 'There come those pig dogs again.' He meant our RAF boys, but Rudi thought he was referring to him. Result: the two brothers never spoke again. TV's Edmund Blackadder once observed: 'The Germans are such a cruel race.'

©aily Mail

headline: CYBG expects Virgin Money shareholders to realise less is more
author: Mathew Vincent
text:
Challenger bank’ ‘weetened’ffer turns a touch sour

“’l give you £.59 for it.”

“ell    ”

“K, OK, how about £.54?”

“eal!”

This is not a negotiating tactic that typically works. Cary Grant tried it at an auction in the film North By North West, with little success. Housebuyers probably still try it in parts of North and North West London, with much the same outcome. But it looks like it could pay off in a deal that is more North By North East: Clydesdale and Yorkshire Banking Group ’ bid for Newcastle-based Virgin Money .

On paper, Monday’ revised proposal from CYBG is worth less, at 354p per Virgin share, than the original, at 359p. And that, of course, is because it is all paper. As the price of the CYBG shares being offered has gone south, the “weetened”eal has become bitterer in pure monetary terms —eading to the odd sight of the recipient’ share price falling.

In fact, CYBG is now proposing a 7 per cent higher share exchange ratio which, if accepted, would deliver Virgin investors a 38 per cent stake in a combined group, up from 36.5 per cent. Virgin also welcomed “alue creation through an upfront premium”

But that premium appears more west than north of Friday’ closing price: it is only 3 per cent higher. So, even though it is some 28 per cent above Virgin’ six-month volume-weighted average price before the bid interest, the market was disappointed. Analysts noted that Virgin’ shares had traded above the value of CYBG’ initial proposal in recent days, in the hope of an additional cash element — hope that also seems to have gone west.

Hence, an emphasis on deal synergies —rom removing bank branch and IT spending duplication —hich analysts estimate at north of £00m, and CYBG appears to consider in the right area. That could mean another 60p-90p of additional value for Virgin Money shareholders, according to Panmure Gordon.

Hence, also, an emphasis on the value of CYBG’ £00m digital banking platform to Virgin, which had planned a £00m investment of its own. Not only that, but CYBG’ platform —nlike rival TSB’ —ould seem to work, with nearly 2m customer accounts migrated without a hitch or a headline. Integrating Virgin Money would require only another 100,000 current accounts to be moved.

Still, there is a reason why the market has made CYBG’ sweetened proposal feel like it’ less: as TSB has shown, challenging incumbent banks requires scale but building scale brings execution risk. Both CYBG and Virgin need to take it, though, as the former no longer has an owner to protect its profits from insurance mis-selling costs, and the latter faces pressure on its net interest margins.

Virgin Money shareholders —ncluding Richard Branson’ Virgin Group itself —ay therefore have to accept a Cary Grant-style bid from CYBG. Arguably, the only negotiation where the price heads north will be for continued licensing of the Virgin name.

Milking the Chocolat

If investing in illiquid, high-risk, non-investment grade bonds is not necessarily good for one’ health, then investing in unlisted, untradeable unregulated mini-bonds certainly isn’. Not least because —n lieu of interest —ecent issues have offered investors £00 worth of burritos, £20 worth of chocolate and £0 worth of beer (with a discount on more). Not so much a credit market as an advertisement for Gaviscon.

For the issuers of these instruments, however —he Mexican restaurant chain Chilango, confectioner Hotel Chocolat , and craft beer maker BrewDog —he concept is rather more palatable. They receive the debt finance they need for expansion, in return for refrying a few more beans, dusting a few more truffles, and bottling a bit more pale ale. And, in some cases, they need never make any promises about repaying the cash to their epicurean creditors. Or to those creditors’octors.

So, news that Hotel Chocolat has decided to repay all of the £.4m it borrowed from customers in the form of “hocolate Bonds”eems surprising. Although these bonds quoted an expensive-sounding coupon of 6.72 per cent, it was paid in the form of six boxes of chocolates a year, worth about £0 each but obviously costing far less to produce. Why give up such a cheap and popular form of funding?

Simply because the company has come through its most capital intensive period of growth, says chief executive Angus Thirlwell , and, having completed investment in its factory and new shops, needs only a short-term overdraft facility to see it through typical Christmas cost and revenue seasonalities. As it has all the working capital it needs, paying for more —ven in chocolate —eems like excess.

And that is perhaps the real value of mini-bonds: they allow serious investors to tell when a business has grown up.

Lombard, however, is now considering a £m mini bond issue, with a coupon paid entirely in the form of spare adjectives and bad puns.

matthew.vincent@ft.com

headline: Milking the Chocolat
author: Matthew Vincent
text:
UK COMPANIES

If investing in illiquid, high-risk, noninvestment grade bonds is not necessarily good for one's health, then investing in unlisted, untradeable unregulated mini-bonds certainly isn't. Not least because —n lieu of interest —ecent issues have offered investors £00 worth of burritos, £20 worth of chocolate and £0-worth of beer (with a discount on more). Not so much a credit market as an advertisement for Gaviscon.

For the issuers of these instruments, however —he Mexican restaurant chain Chilango, confectioner Hotel Chocolat, and craft beer maker BrewDog —he concept is rather more palatable. They receive the debt finance they need for expansion, in return for refrying a few more beans, dusting a few more truffles, and bottling a bit more pale ale. And, in some cases, they need never make any promises about repaying the cash to their epicurean creditors. Or to those creditors' doctors.

So, news that Hotel Chocolat has decided to repay all of the £.4m it borrowed from customers in the form of "Chocolate Bonds" seems surprising. As it has all the working capital it needs, paying for more —ven in chocolate —eems like excess.

And that is perhaps the real value of mini-bonds: they allow serious investors to tell when a business has grown up.

headline: Brewdog  to launch craft fest at airport
author: BARRY MORAN
text:
AN independent craft brewer is launching its own festival on a runway next month.

Scottish firm BrewDog has announced its entry into Ireland with the launch of BrewDog's Cinematic Circus, involving big-screen movies, live music, entertainment, artisan food and craft beer, at Dublin's Weston Airport on Saturday, July 7. The festival will feature several artists such as Fallen Lights, The Academic and Niall Horan and will also include Ireland's biggest mobile outdoor HD cinema screen. Brewdog talent scouts will be scouring Dublin City Centre this coming Saturday to source lucky buskers and busking bands to perform gigs around the venue and the best act will perform in support of Fallen Lights.

Tickets are on sale today at €6 each and are available from brewdog.com/circus.

headline: DOG DAYS ARE HERE
author: ARAN BRAZIL
text:
My Round By ARAN BRAZIL

A LITTLE over two weeks ago, Brewdog teased that something big was imminent and hinted that beer fans should keep July 7 free at all costs.

Like many, I assumed the news would be that the company was finally following through on its promise to open a bar in the capital —onfirmed in late 2016 but put on the backburner since. (The company has continued to scout suitable locations, even going so far as to offer a "finder's fee" at one point.) Instead, when the announcement was finally made, there wasn't a hint of news on a trademark industrial chic watering hole for Dublin —Brewdog is planning a "cinematic circus event".

It has booked an entire airfield —he city's lesser known Weston Airport near Lucan —o launch this bonanza of live music, cinema and a craft beer frenzy of 120-plus taps from the likes of YellowBelly, Kildare Brewing Company, Wicklow Wolf and Stone Barrel as well as a huge range of their own. Tickets are €6.

It's already shaping up to be one of the biggest beer events of the year.

That particular July 7 weekend already has quite the selection for craft beer and food enthusiasts. Brewdog share calendar space with the return of GuinnessXMeatopia —he high-end barbecue festival at which world-renowned chefs work their magic over wood and charcoal.

Tickets are €0 (to include four dishes paired with four beers) at guinnessopengate.com/meatopia Not to be outdone is plucky Underdog —ame Street's relatively new but already much-loved craft beer destination —hich is celebrating its first birthday in style. Underdog owner Patrick Delahunty says: "The support we've received from the start has been amazing.

"We've a genuine interest in pouring good quality beer in peak condition, as that's what we love.

"And that's why we have gone down the the route of keeping taps free for rare, niche and seasonal beers from Ireland and abroad". Underdog's birthday isn't a day, but a week (too many beers involved to drink in a single 24-hour period) featuring exclusive-to-Underdog releases from Norway's Lervig, England's Wylam and Irish breweries. See Underdog on Facebook for date announcements. l FOR Brewdog Circus tickets, see www.universe.com/events/brewdogs-cinematic-circustickets-lucan-WDRKXZ

headline: The best craft beers sold in the supermarkets
author: Jane MacQuitty
text:
Not so long ago, craft beer was a geeky, hipster, hairshirt taste trip often worth avoiding. Not any more. This summer has brought with it more wild and wacky, yet absolutely delicious craft brews than ever before and, frankly, the range and quality have bowled me over. Take your pick from an astonishing selection of low-alcohol, dry-hopped, sour, oak-aged, spice and herb, or citrus and tropical fruit-laced beers as everyone from Marks & Spencer to Morrisons launches new summer ranges.

It helps if you understand craft beer speak. Great craft beer, like cask-conditioned real ale, is often bottle-conditioned, or re-fermented in the bottle, and still has its sludgy yeast. Drinkers who are not frightened of flavour skip the option of pouring the beer slowly off its sediment, preferring instead to shake the bottle for a cloudy, yeasty hit. Confusingly, there is no precise technical definition of craft beer. Instead, brewers differentiate booming craft brews from ailing premium bottled ales such as Spitfire and Newcastle Brown Ale by giving these contemporarystyled beers wacky names and labels, plus —nd much more importantly —uirky turbo-charged flavours that keen beer drinkers cannot get enough of.

Provenance is a big thing in the craft beer world, with brewers happy to explain what grains they use in their brews, including wheat, barley, rye and oats, the yeasts selected, plus the hops: citra, simcoe, mosaic and centennial are favourites of mine.The Kernel Brewery in Bermondsey, southeast London, details all this on its labels, with the 11 brewers who work there producing slightly different brews each week. Check out Kernel's richly yeasted, herby 5.2 per cent Pale Ale (Oddbins, 330ml bottle, £.60).

Citrus is big this summer and while Wild Beer's 3.6 per cent Sleeping Lemons (Waitrose, 330ml bottle, £.50) was a salty preserved-lemon sting, I loved the same Somerset brewery's 4.1 per cent Pogo (Waitrose, 330ml can, £.19), a terrific, passion fruit, orange zest and guava wallop. It's hard to choose just one of Beavertown's brilliant beers, but the 6.7 per cent Lupuloid India Pale Ale (Waitrose, 330ml can, £.40; Oddbins, £) is an elegant, smoky, citrus delight. Fruit beers are especially summery, so nab the splendid, coriander and cherry kernel-spiked, 3.5 per cent Belgian Cherry Wheat Beer (Marks & Spencer, 330ml bottle, £.50).

Finally, don't despair if you are off alcohol. Brewdog's new 0.5 per cent alcohol Nanny State (330ml bottle, Sainsbury's, £.25; Waitrose, £.30) is a light, malty, hoppy ale that works.

The Kernel Brewery Table Beer, 2.8 per cent, 500ml bottle

Oddbins, £.80 My favourite loweralcohol beer is this scrumptious, cloudy, bottle-conditioned gem, with gloriously hoppy, blossomy, citrus fruit.

DE14 Flight Suit Orange Pale Ale, 5.1 per cent, 330ml can

Morrisons, £.75 Zingy, fresh mandarin and bitter orange peel-charged pale ale charmer: an unusual, yet thirst-quenching summer swig.

Oude Geuze Boon à'Ancienne, Lambic Ale, 7 per cent, 375ml bottle, Belgium

Waitrose, £.25 Frothy, floral, yeasty almost winey oak-aged and bottle-conditioned, full-bodied, malt and wheat-stashed triumph.

Wine: this week's best buys

2015 Robert Oatley Margaret River Chardonnay, Australia, 12.5 per cent

Sunday Times Wine Club, 03330 142776, £0.99 Fine smoky oak and masses of vibrant, apple, melon and pineapple.

2016 Morgon, La Chanaise, Beaujolais, France, 13 per cent

Waitrose, £1.99 (down from £4.99) Tasty, bold, floral, violet and raspberry-stashed, cru made from 50-year-old gamay.

2017 Rock Angel Rosé Côes de Provence, France, 14 per cent

Handford, 020 7589 6113, £4.95 This is the posh 2017 Provençl pink to buy, with lashings of bold, herby, grapey, hibiscus fruit.

headline: CITY PEOPLE
text:
The feuds, the faces and the farcical Dominic Walsh @walshdominic Business big shot name logan plant age 39 position chief executive, beavertown brewery The craft beer brigade may be divided over Beavertown Brewery's deal with Heineken, but Logan Plant, its founder and chief executive, is adamant that the north London brewer will "remain independent and autonomous".

The former musician, whose father is Robert Plant, the lead singer with Led Zeppelin, said that the Dutch brewer was investing £0 million for a minority stake —stimated at 49 per cent —o enable him to develop Beaverworld, a new brewery and visitor centre, creating 150 jobs and increasing its brewing capacity tenfold to about 90 million pints.

The father-of-two, who brewed his first beer in his kitchen almost seven years ago, said that he had looked at possible private equity backers, but soon realised they were only interested in maximising their returns and were "not in it for the long run or for the love of the product or industry they are investing in".

Mr Plant, who with his wife, Bridget, retains a controlling stake, said that Beavertown's employees would be given share options, adding: "We retain full control of our destiny ... Heineken do not want us to conform or change. They love what we do and want us to do more of it."

guides can count on getting well-engineered badges Being a girl guide has changed a bit since the days when the focus was on domestic skills and camping. Girlguiding, which has half a million members in Britain, has teamed up with Amey, the engineering and infrastructure group, to encourage more girls and young women to consider careers in science, technology, engineering and maths. This will involve "carrying out scientific experiments, creating algorithms for robots, planning video games, learning about binary code and having a go at being a crime scene investigator". However, some things don't change. Next month, Girlguiding will reveal a new set of badges which Amey has helped to design.

double jeopardy accounting There is one sliver of good news for Steve Denison, the former PWC partner who admitted misconduct over the BHS audit last week and agreed to what is in effect a 15-year auditing ban by the Financial Reporting Council. The pressure intensified yesterday when it emerged that PWC has accused Mr Denison of improperly backdating his audit opinion, but at least he won't be struck off. I hear the Institute of Chartered Accountants in England and Wales cannot expel him as a member because of double-jeopardy rules that prevent him being punished twice for the same sins. Bigwigs at the institute are surely hoping he will quietly resign. We'll see.

get out of the kitchen Landlords bleating about the abuse of company voluntary arrangements can always do what the owner of the Jamie's Italian site in Canary Wharf has done. Although it was not on the CVA closure list for the restaurant chain, the landlord has deployed its right to take back the site and re-let it to Brewdog, the Scottish brewer and bar operator. The Jamie's Italian in Guildford is also being remarketed by the landlord but word is the Jamie Oliver Group is still hoping to hang on to it.

headline: We have the right recipe for success
author: Shaf Rasul
text:
Shaf Rasul One in a million shaf@the-sun.co.uk

SCOTLAND'S food and drink industry is something we should be very proud of and I reckon it has the potential to become our most valuable asset.

We are definitely taking the right steps to make it our flagship business sector.

Two elements that should always be at the core of developing any industry are the support of young entrepreneurs and the nurture of good ideas.

And a competition launched by Edinburgh agency Guy & Co made me very aware that people agree with that.

The organisation is encouraging Scottish entrepreneurs and food and drink businesses with new product ideas in development to apply for a 'go-tomarket' prize pot worth £5k. It's a good amount of money and will help give an exciting new product the push to market that it needs.

This is a fantastic concept and I hope more businesses follow suit.

It should generate some healthy rivalry amongst businesses and give start-ups and entrepreneurs a platform for growth.

Imaginative Competition is always healthy in business.

It gets you thinking about what the opposition are doing, showcases best practice and inspires you to do it better.

Industry bodies help inspire and guide too.

Scotland Food & Drink is going to be instrumental in seeing the industry grow and develop over the next decade or so.

Just last year, the organisation launched the Ambition 2030 strategy which aims to double the value of the industry to £0billion within 12 years by growing markets, developing capabilities and focusing on our behaviours.

Yes, it's ambitious but if we reach the target, Scotland will be recognised as a world leader in responsible and profitable growth, and an attractive option for overseas collaborations, partnerships and trade deals.

Scotland Food & Drink also hosts its own annual Excellence Awards and the newly introduced innovation category is a fantastic springboard for new talent.

Every product has to start somewhere.

Some of Scotland's best-loved products are so iconic that it's hard to believe they started from humble beginnings —ust look at A.G. Barr, Tunnock's, BrewDog and Baxters.

Scotland certainly has the potential to be home to more successful brands and it all starts from a great idea.

As I have experienced firsthand, there is always an element of risk in launching a new business venture and it doesn't always pay off.

You think you have great concept but it just doesn't quite work.

But that's it —ometimes the most bizarre ideas can be the biggest successes.

One unique creation I read about recently certainly is a first for Scotland and I'm sure it's a bit too left-field for some.

An imaginative hotelier in Argyll has created a recipe that sees burger patties rolled in a coating of MIDGES. Yes, you read it right. The burgers are rolled in the crushed flies —nd other ingredients —hen placed on a bun and served with chips.

Hotel owner David Keat, the mastermind behind the dish, is convinced it will go down a storm, arguing that eating insects has become more mainstream in recent years and that midges are an endless supply of protein.

I know flexitarianism has become a buzzword recently —ith people opting for plant based meals through the weekand a steak dinner at the weekend —ut midges on my dinner plate? I'm not so sure.

But who knows? This could become a national delicacy.

We certainly have the base ingredient in plentiful supply, with an estimated 69 billion midges appearing over the summer season. It just might work.

After all, who would have thought that eating spiced sheep intestines or a battered chocolate bar would prove so popular? If it's going to help make the food and drink industry the centrepiece of our economy —hich I think it definitely has the potential to be —'m all for it.

£0bn Food & drink industry worth by 2030

£5k prize pot for food & drink entrepreneurs

headline: BREWDOG  PROFITS GO FLAT AFTER BREWERY OPENING
text:
Profits at craft beer firm Brewdog plunged to less than £m last year as it splashed out on opening a brewery in America and a vodka and gin distillery.

The Scottish brewer reported a 73pc drop in profits to £70,000 in the year to December 31, despite sales soaring from £1.9m to £11.5m.

It came as the firm opened its first American brewery in Ohio, as well as its Lone Wolf distillery in the UK.

It is also looking at opening breweries in Asia, with China already one of its biggest export markets.

Brewdog was founded in 2007 and has grown to become one of the most popular craft beers sold in UK supermarkets. It has 46 bars around the world and shipped 344,387 hectolitres of beer last year - equivalent to 1.4m bottles.

Its most popular beer, Punk IPA, raked in £5.4m worth of sales for the business in 2017.

Almost a quarter of the business is now owned by private equity firm TSG Consumer Partners.

©aily Mail

headline: Brewery pink stink
text:
A BREWERY has refused to accept a watchdog's censure for using the phrase "Beer for Girls" on a bottle label.

The Portman Group ruled it might cause under-age girl boozing.

But BrewDog said: "It was a campaign satirising gender stereotypes on International Women's Day. We're not bothered about this ruling."

The Pink IPS brand launched in March. Portman acted after two complaints and claims it would "appeal to U18s".

headline: DUNDEE 48 HOURS IN
author: HELEN OCHYRA
text:
TRAVEL

This Scottish city is the new hip city break destination, says writer HELEN OCHYRA

GET ARTISTIC

Dundee just got cool. Officially. The shiny new V&A opens its doors in September, and it's not only Scotland's first design museum - this waterfront temple is also the first V&A outside London. Its opening ticketed exhibition celebrates ocean liners and life onboard (Vam.ac.uk). Practically next door is the DCA showing the latest art-house cinema releases (Dca.org.uk), while no Dundonian will forgive you if you don't poke your head into the McManus - a gorgeous free art gallery housed in an ornate gothic pile that is Dundee's best-loved building (Mcmanus.co.uk).

HIT THE SAND

A beach in Dundee? OK, so it might not quite be bikini weather, but this is Scotland's sunniest city, and the sands start three miles out of town at Broughty Ferry castle. The fortress was built in 1490, and it's worth climbing up to the observation post for stonking views along the River Tay - you might even spot a dolphin. Afterwards, call in to Mitchell's for the best coffee in town and pick up a picnic of local produce at Clementine of Broughty Ferry for a feast on the sands. Sun not playing ball? Retreat to Visocchi's for an ice-cream sundae, from £.50, and pretend (Visocchis.co.uk).

EAT SPECIAL SALAD

Forget deep-fried Mars bars - Dundee has one hell of a larder. There's Scottish lamb, aged Angus beef and local venison at chic Castlehill in the city centre. Two courses cost £2 (Castlehill restaurant.co.uk). Or head back to Broughty Ferry's The Tayberry for fab Scottish fine dining - think roasted cod with local baby tomatoes. Two courses cost £8 (Tayberryrestaurant.co.uk). For something totally different, cross the bridge to Newport-on-Tay and 2014 MasterChef: The Professionals winner Jamie Scott's The Newport. The tasting menu has St Andrews lobster, Anster cheese and a super-tasty "salad" to convert even the most hardened lettuce loather. Six courses cost £5 (Thenewportrestaurant.co.uk).

SHOP FOR BANGLES

Visit jewellery designer Lorraine Law's boutique to pick up stunning bangles, hammered to a matte finish, from £0 (Lorrainelaw.co.uk). Back in Broughty Ferry, Lulu & M has contemporary brands such as Sofie Schnoor and Orwell + Austen (Luluandm.co.uk), while Tiger Lily has great affordable dresses and jumpsuits (Tigerlilyboutique.co.uk).

DRINK CRAFTY

Scotland does a fine line in craft beers, and two of the biggest brands have bars in Dundee. BrewDog's industrial-chic bar sits inside the imposing stone Royal Exchange and serves Punk IPA and Dead Pony Club alongside inventive seasonal tipples, from £.90 (Brewdog.com). Innis & Gunn's Beer Kitchen has a similar pared-back vibe and boasts beer from its Perthshire brewery, from £.20 (Innisandgunn.com). You'll find a wider choice at Duke's Corner, a high-spirited late-night venue with live music (Dukescorner.co.uk).

KIP BY THE RIVER

You can see the new V&A as well as the River Tay from your window at Malmaison Dundee, a funky six-storey hotel that has romantic rooms. Some have huge roll-top tubs and all have supremely comfortable beds. The bar is a cool spot for cocktails, too. Double rooms cost from £5 (Malmaison.com). F BTW Marmalade was invented in Dundee - along with aspirin and Grand Theft Auto. Plan your trip at Dundee.com.

headline: Brewdog  to pay £2,000 for [...]
text: Brewdog to pay £2,000 for sacking 'blind' man A tribunal has told Brewdog, the Scottish brewing company, to pay £2,000 to a former worker after they sacked him because he was about to be declared blind. The company was accused of making assumptions about James Ross's condition and paying "lip-service" to equality laws. Mr Ross, 47, who has Stargardt disease, told bosses at the Aberdeenshire brewery that his sight had deteriorated and he was awaiting tests. Despite reports from the RNIB and a specialist in visual impairment saying he could work if adjustments were made, Brewdog dismissed him. Mr Ross, of Fraserburgh, won his case for unfair dismissal and disability discrmination.

headline: BREWDOG  TRIBUNAL RAP
text:
TRENDY craft beer producer Brewdog has to pay £2,000 compensation to a former employee who was sacked after telling the company he was about to be declared legally blind.

James Ross, 47, who worked in the Aberdeen brewer's packaging department, suffers with Stargardt disease, which causes progressive vision loss. After he refused to move to a different department last year, claiming the new role would be more difficult with his condition, Brewdog fired him.

Employment judge Nick Hosie described Brewdog's actions as 'astonishing'.

©aily Mail

headline: CRAFT BREWS BLEND MORE THAN [...]
text:
My Round By ARAN BRAZIL

CRAFT BREWS BLEND MORE THAN HOPS COLLABORATIONS are nothing rare in the craft brewing world.

Much as it was in the early days of European brewing, brewers often travel far and wide to learn the specialised techniques of a particular product, such as a Belgian sour ale or a hop forward American IPA.

They also engage with up-andcoming breweries in emerging markets, sharing knowledge and prying open potential markets.

The industry as a whole is pretty open-source —og on to any homebrewing forum and you'll see master brewers giving amateurs (such as myself) advice and often sharing the recipes of their most famous brews.

Famously, Brewdog once printed DIYdog, a book of all their recipes for homebrewers.

As craft beer has moved from niche to mainstream, its potential is being unlocked by more established industries, such as the restaurant trade.

A local brew helps an eatery stand out from the crowd, offering a unique selling point that not everybody has yet.

Last week, Fire restaurant on Dawson Street in Dublin, announced that it has launched its own lager, Fire 1864, brewed on commission by St Mel's Brewing Company in Co Longford.

Billed as a lightly malted, hop-forward lager, it uses a process known as 'first wort hopping' in which aroma hops with fewer bittering properties are added to the kettle before boiling (as opposed to post-boil which is common). Liam Hanlon of St Mel's says: "First wort hopping is a technique that's been around for a long time. The idea is that it imparts a smoother bitterness to the beer.

"We used the aroma hops to FWH the Fire lager and we liked the results —t was finer.

"We chose mittelfruh for the distinctive noble hop aroma, and Fire 1864 is almost a hybrid of the Bavarian and Pilsner styles. It's maltier than a pils and hoppier than a helles ... It's goes well with the meat they serve."

Klaw, the Dublin chain of seafood restaurants, pours a range of beer brewed for them to order by north Dublin-based brewer Hopfully, with input from head chef and owner Niall Sabongi.

Each was chosen for its affinity with seafood and they include KIPA, an IPA, a fine stout called Auntie Arthur, and the aptly named pilsner Shuck N Suck.

Independent off-licences have also been turning to brewers to create something unique for the thirsty craft enthusiast.

Blackrock Cellar and Martin's off-licence, both in Dublin, have teamed up with DOT Brew and Hope Beer respectively, for ownbrand releases that are exclusive to each shop.

Dublin off-licence group Molloy's dropped Covert Operation, it's own collaboration with Wexford's Yellowbelly, brewed to celebrate the 85th anniversary of the family's first pub, the Foxes Covert, in Tallaght.

Brewed with Mosaic and Azacca hops, this 440ml, 5% ABV hazy pale ale is easy-drinking, packed with pineapple, mango and apricot notes with minimal bitterness.

It's currently being offered at three for €0. Previously, the group teamed up with Rascals Brewing to produce it's session pale, All Night Long, during the 2016 UEFA Euros.

headline: £0K HOMEBREWDOG
author: TRACEY BOLES
text:
CSunITY Edited by TRACEY BOLES Tel: 020 7782 4050 e-mail: tracey.boles@thesun.co.uk THE FTSE 100 ROSE 2.21 TO 7558.59 £ BUYS €.12, US $1.27. BRENT CRUDE $71.74

BREWDOG is offering investors the chance to have a craft beer bar built in their home —f they pump £0,000 into the firm.

The offer is part of the brewer's latest round of fundraising, called Equity for Punks. It has raised almost £0million since October.

The three-tap bar will be designed in BrewDog's signature industrial style, complete with a fully stocked beer fridge, and a keg of its headliner beer, Punk IPA.

Co-founder James Watt said: "Having your own craft beer bar at home is the ultimate dream for many beer fans."

headline: Heather cola gives a taste of Scotland
author: Marc Horne
text:
A Scottish cola flavoured with heather extracts is being launched by a former footballer to capitalise on the boom in soft drinks (Marc Horne writes).

Chris Ewing is behind Alba Cola, a "craft soda" made with Highland spring water, which he hopes will become a hit.

Sales of premium soft drinks have soared in the UK in recent years, with Fever-Tree, the mixer maker, reaching a record value of £.5 billion last month.

Mr Ewing hopes that Alba Cola will emulate its success while embracing the irreverent attitude of Brewdog, the Aberdeenshire craft beer company that has become a global giant.

The former Mother well and Stranraer footballer was inspired by the market in France, where more than 30 regional colas, including Paris Cola and Corsica Cola, have sprung up over the past decade.

"I thought it was a cool concept and realised it could work in Scotland," he said.

"In the last few years there has been a real move towards craft, artisanal products, particularly in beers, and now it is moving towards soft drinks.

"We have created a premium product using quality ingredients that has a uniquely Scottish taste and identity. I'm sure the people of Scotland, and the people who love Scotland, will get behind it."

In 2011, Mr Ewing set up the Edusport Academy in Hamilton, which provided talented young French footballers with coaching and language training. Now based in Glasgow, it also caters for rugby players.

The Caledonian cola will have 25 per cent less sugar than its US rivals, will be flavoured with heather and its bottles, which will sell for about £, will be made from recycled materials.

Mr Ewing, 39, insisted that there was a place in the market for it. "In Scotland we love an underdog and a challenger, someone who is going to shake up the market," he said. "Look at what Brewdog have achieved. We are not here to take on Coca-Cola or Pepsi. We are a higher end product that will be in select bars, café and restaurants, rather than in every corner shop."

Fritz-kola, invented by two students in Hamburg in 2002, which uses lemon juice rather than citric acid, has become a global success with young, ethical consumers.

Mr Ewing, whose Glasgow-based venture has been funded by the success of his football and rugby academy, hopes Alba can have a similar appeal. He said: "We are more conscious of what we are actually buying now. We are all taking the time to think about what is going into the things we drink. Having 25 per cent less sugar than the leading colas is a statement of intent. Kids will want to try it and we have a responsibility to them." He added: "Provenance is very important now. We use water from the Cairngorms National Park in the Highlands.

"The Scottish government are all about trying to promote our own produce and that's something we believe in strongly."

The cola, which is being made at the Start-Up Drinks Lab in Port Glasgow, Inverclyde, will be launched next month.

headline: Life in the Doghouse  Brewdog  [...]
text:
news in brief

Life in the Doghouse Brewdog will open its first hotel this week, not in its home country but thousands of miles away in Columbus, Ohio. The Scottish company's 32-bedroom Doghouse venue sits alongside the brewer's facilities in the city and guests will have beer taps in their rooms and a built-in fridge stocked with ales in the shower. More than 500 reservations are said to have been made so far. A second hotel, at its Brewdog's brewery in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, is scheduled to open next year.

headline: Room at the tap..
text:
SCOTS beer firm Brew-Dog has opened a "craft beer hotel" in the US.

Rooms at the Dog-House venue have views over the company's brewery in Columbus, Ohio. All 32 apartments also have beer taps on hand for thirsty punters.

The site was built after a crowdfunding drive raised £33,000 with donors given first chance at booking a night there.

A hotel at BrewDog's HQ in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, opens next year. A spokeswoman said: "We have created an unrivalled experience."

headline: Brewdog  raises the bar after turnover jumps
author: Greig Cameron
text:
A Scottish brewer that has exported its craft beer to the United States has reported a 55 per cent jump in first-half turnover to £8 million.

Brewdog, which has breweries in Scotland and America, said that the increase came from greater contributions from its bar division and rising exports. Revenue from the bar arm rose 92 per cent to about £0 million and the US business earned £ million.

The business, which is based in Ellon, near Aberdeen, was founded by James Watt and Martin Dickie in 2007 and employs 1,200. TSG Consumer Partners, the private equity firm, paid £13 million in April last year to take a 22 per cent stake in Brewdog, which plans to open breweries in Australia and China. Punk IPA is its best-selling craft beer in Britain and it has moved into production of spirits and cider.

Brewdog, which declined to provide profit figures for the first half because they were still being processed, said that the value of overseas sales was up 32 per cent to £1 million. It runs dozens of bars under its own brand and purchased the Draft House group of pubs in March. Continental Europe remained its largest export destination but it reported rising demand across Oceania and Asia. In the UK, partnerships with JD Wetherspoon and Greene King led to 1,200 extra draught points for Punk IPA in pubs. It said it also had wider distribution agreements with Morrisons and Tesco.

headline: CRAFT BREWER TOASTS BOOMING SALES
text:
SCOTTISH craft brewer Brewdog racked up record sales growth as it continues its breakneck expansion into the US.

The beer maker posted a 55pc rise in revenues to £8m during the six months to June 30, as sales from its pubs and bars soared 92pc to £0m.

Brewdog has just opened the world's first craft beer hotel in the US with rooms with draught beer taps and dog-friendly accommodation.

It follows its first brewery across the pond, which opened in Ohio. The US business brought in £m of sales during the first six months of the year. Brewdog's Punk IPA, has been the best-selling craft beer in supermarkets for three years.

The company shot to success after it was founded in 2007 and now has 37 pubs and bars in the UK and 21 across the rest of the world.

Next year it is opening a brewery in China and is preparing to open its first facility in Australia.

Last year it was valued at £bn after San Francisco private equity house TSG Consumer Partners took a 22pc stake and plugged it with £13m.
