headline: TOAST TO  BREWDOG
author: TRACEY BOLES
text:
CSunITY Edited by TRACEY BOLES Tel: 020 7782 4050 e-mail: tracey.boles@thesun.co.uk THE FTSE 100 FELL 11.02 TO 7,563.22 £ BUYS €.11, US $1.28. BRENT CRUDE $74.73

CRAFT brewery BREWDOG is toasting a 55 per cent jump in half-year revenues thanks to rapid expansion of its bar chain and strong demand for its tipples.

The group behind Punk IPA beer reported revenues of £8million for the first six months of 2018, with UK retail sales surging by 83 per cent.

Scotland-based BrewDog said performance had been helped in particular by its fast-growing bar and pub business.

This saw interim revenues almost double after a raft of new openings and its acquisition of the 14-strong DRAFT HOUSE estate.

headline: IT'S THIRST CHOICE FOR BEER LOVERS
author: LISA MINOT
text:
Sun travel

Edited by LISA MINOT Follow on Facebook @TheSunTravelEditor

THE world's first beer hotel has opened at Brewdog's brewery in Ohio, USA.

The DogHouse offers guests the chance to stay the night inside the brewery watching the brewers at work from rooms overlooking the state-of-the-art facility.

Each room features a fully stocked beer fridge of BrewDog's headliner craft beers, seasonal brews and guest favourites handpicked by co-founders James Watt and Martin Dickie, as well as an en-suite bathroom featuring a built-in shower beer fridge.

The front desk has been replaced with a lobby bartender who will check guests in while they enjoy a complimentary beer.

Brewdog also plans to build a hotel a little closer to home, next to its headquarters in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, which is scheduled to open next year.

news

headline: Features; Offers BREWDOG  GOT GOOD BARKING..
author: Shaf Rasul
text:
BEING in the doghouse is usually a bad thing - but for US beer fans it's quite the opposite.

The DogHouse is the name of BrewDog's new hotel in the US, at its brewery in Ohio.

BrewDog has always been a company to do things out of the ordinary and this hotel is no exception, with beer taps in rooms which overlook the brewery.

The craft beer hotel is the result of a $300k crowdfunding initiative.

That might sound like a lot of dosh, but actually it's a drop in the ocean compared to the bigger picture for the BrewDog.

Its revenue has just rocketed by 55 per cent to a whopping £0million according to its latest trading update.

Initiative Not half bad for a company that started out with small scale production at an industrial estate in Fraserburgh.

And it's not the only company making waves in Scottish brewing, even if it is by far the biggest. There are a host of others out there turning heads.

St Andrews Brewing Co recently launched its very own crowdfunding initiative which surpassed expectations, smashing it at 160 per cent of its target.

Then there's Isle of Skye Brewing which sprang up after a jokey conversation in a pub about the lack of good beer - and has since enjoyed decades of success and scores of awards. Meanwhile relative newcomer Fierce Beer in Aberdeen has scooped awards and secured international partnership deals despite being little more than two years old.

I could name so many more. I think it's fair to say whisky is no longer the only amber nectar Scotland seems to be pretty good at.

headline: Technology & Media Video Is on Tap for Brewer --- Scottish craft-beer maker  BrewDog  offers 100 hours of content on drinking culture
author: Benjamin Mullin
text:
A craft-beer company hopes to give new meaning to the term "binge watching."

BrewDog, a Scottish beer company, is offering a streaming service featuring more than 100 hours of video centered on drinking culture, the latest effort by a brand to launch its own media venture.

"The BrewDog Network," available on smartphone apps and online, costs $4.99 a month. Breaking through in a crowded subscription-video market won't be easy.

Executives at BrewDog are hoping the new streaming service will double as a moneymaker and a marketing channel, to help it stand out in a cluttered market for craft beer.

"Ultimately it's both," said Chris Burke, general manager of the BrewDog Network. "It has the potential to be a meaningful revenue stream, but it's also about telling the story of BrewDog."

The BrewDog Network will carry a mix of licensed and original content in which drinking is an element, from food shows to travel series. "The BrewDog Show," featuring the company's founders, will also be available at launch.

The streaming service is the latest in a series of unconventional business moves by the brewer, which has distinguished itself in part by cultivating a roguish image and appealing to a fan base of beer-obsessives. Companies from Casper mattress to Airbnb have tried their hands at media ventures, a form of content marketing they hope will connect with consumers in a way that isn't possible with advertising.

BrewDog allows fans to buy into the company through "Equity for Punks," a shareholder crowdfunding campaign that has attracted 85,000 investors. The company has opened a chain of bars, built a hotel with in-room beer taps and even proposed a quickly aborted plan to allow shareholders to gamble their equity on the roulette wheel.

That marketing pizazz, aided by the craft-beer boom, has helped transform BrewDog from a two-man garage brewery into an international company that now employs about 1,500 workers. BrewDog made a profit of $12 million in 2017 on revenue of more than $140 million, according to a company presentation. Last year, private-equity firm TSG Consumer Partners bought a 23% stake in BrewDog in a deal that valued the company at $1.24 billion.

Niche subscription services can face difficulty winning market share in a streaming marketplace saturated with alternatives, and with many consumers already spending a healthy amount on major services like Netflix, Hulu and HBO Now. CuriosityStream, a nonfiction streaming service, recently lowered prices and added advertising after slower-than-expected subscriber growth. And Seeso, the comedy streaming service from NBCUniversal, closed in 2017 about a year after its launch.

BrewDog is aiming to lure at least 50,000 subscribers in the first six months of the service, which Mr. Burke says will be enough to make it sustainable. The company is also planning to boost revenue for the network by cutting licensing deals with pay-TV providers that sell "skinny bundles" to consumers.

Craft-beer volumes grew about 1.6% in 2017, the slowest growth in a decade, according to trade association the Beer Institute. But direct-to-consumer delivery via taprooms and brewpubs is still growing at a fast clip.

BrewDog, which operates its own bars in the U.S. and U.K., has been insulated from the slowdown, said co-founder James Watt.

headline: Brewery unbottles on-demand video
author: Matthew Moore
text:
First there was Netflix. Then Amazon got into the streaming game with Prime Video. Now there's an unlikely new competitor: Brewdog (Matthew Moore writes).

The Scottish brewery and pub chain launched a $4.99-a-month subscription video service this week hosting an array of booze-related programming, as it seeks to cash in on the streaming boom and diversify its brand.

Shows available to watch include Brown Bag Wine Tasting with William Shatner, in which the 87-year-old Star Trek actor interviews friends and celebrities over a bottle of red. Other programmes on the Brewdog Network include Burgers & Beers, a self-explanatory food series, and a comedy gameshow called Are You Smarter Than a Drunk Person? The on-demand service is being launched with more than 100 hours of exclusive content, Brewdog says. These include a travel series starring the company's founders James Watt and Martin Dickie, who are understood to have made about £00 million by selling a 22 per cent stake in their firm to the US private equity house TSG Consumer Partners.

Brewdog is a self-styled "punk" brewery known for publicity stunts and provocative marketing, but the founders insist their streaming venture is a genuine business move. The pair previously presented a beer programme on the now defunct digital station Esquire and say they decided to launch their own channel after the series was cancelled. "We didn't want to sit around in our underpants, crying into a tub of ice cream," Mr Watt said.

Brewdog is not the first to see potential in "niche" video subscription services that cater for small but ardent fanbases.

There are on-demand services for history buffs, comedy fans or people interested in the royal family.

Crafty investors, page 57

headline: Say cheers as craft beer flows into the mainstream
author: Hugo Cox
text:
? Serious money The home brew industry is burgeoning, with investors keen to enter the land of hop and glory. Hugo Cox explains how

Following in the footsteps of a famous parent is no easy task. For Logan Plant, the son of Robert Plant, the Led Zeppelin frontman, the job got a little easier in June when Beavertown Brewery, the craft beer producer he founded in 2011, netted £0 million in funding from Heineken.

While traditionalists boycotted Beavertown's brews, accusing the company of selling out, the deal was the latest measure of how the UK's craft brewery scene has moved out of its hipster shadows and into the sights of investors. Opportunities have mushroomed. Since 2009 the number of breweries in London has increased twelvefold to 110; over the same period the number in the UK has increased from 696 to 1,732, says the Campaign For Real Ale.

Crowdfunding is the principal route investors use to turn home brew into the land of hop and glory. "BrewDog is the great crowdfunding craft beer success story," says Sarah Bright, the chief executive of Businessagent.com, a crowdfunding data provider. Early supporters of the Aberdeenshire company were offered the chance to cash in up to 15 per cent of their shares last year, which the company said represented a return of 2,800 per cent for those who had bought them in 2010.

Breweries have been a common sight on crowdfunding platforms, but Redemption Brewing presents the only opportunity at present, according to Businessagent.com. The north London craft brewer has raised a third of the £00,000 it is seeking through Crowdcube. Crowdfunding investments can be part of an Enterprise Investment Scheme (EIS) and Seed EIS investments. Benefits include 30 per cent income tax relief for an EIS, 50 per cent for a Seed EIS and exemption from capital gains tax on both.

However, success stories such as BrewDog are the exception. Platforms sometimes publish annual return data, but these often reflect the success of businesses in raising money from investors rather than returning it to them. To realise any profits on crowdfunding investments you'll need a buyout —hose 2010 Brew-Dog investors had to wait until 2017 for Heineken's offer —r a public listing. If you're lucky you'll get a dividend before then, but these are rare. Without a route to cash in your invest-ments, claims about annual returns are theoretical. "It is also worth noting that not all shares are created equally," Ms Bright says.

Early investors in BrewDog have a different share class, based on a different company valuation, than those who invested in 2017.

"If you are going to invest this way, make sure you know where you stand in the pecking order and keep an eye on voting rights," she says. There is also a risk of dilution if the company issues additional shares to raise more money, decreasing the value of those that have been sold already.

With only one craft brewer crowdfunding, EIS and venture capital trust (VCT) funds offer an alternative route into the beer market. "Managed EIS and VCT trust funds have been particularly active in the pub space, as the property assets provide an element of security," says Jason Hollands of Tilney, a financial planner. He recommends the range of VCTs from Puma Investments, which backs the Brewhouse & Kitchen chain of 23 UK microbrewery pubs.

Another route is through the main listed beer and drinks companies. "While edgy craft brands represent a small proportion of overall earnings for companies like Diageo and Heineken, these stocks provide a very accessible way to invest in beer and provide superb free cash flow," Mr Hollands says.

Laith Khalaf, a senior analyst at Hargreaves Lansdown, an investment company, recommends one of the oldest largest European brewers, AB InBev, which owns Budweiser, Corona Extra, Stella Artois and Becks, as well as a range of craft offerings. The stock is yielding 4.25 per cent "but is trading at a price-earnings ratio of 25, making it expensive," he says.

Closer to home, and with a range of craft beers alongside stalwarts such as Abbot Ale and Old Speckled Hen, is the pub firm and brewer Greene King. "It has a great track record of dividend growth: turnover and dividend per share have doubled since 2005," Mr Khalaf says. He has one final warning: be aware that the squeeze on wages is hitting the demand for drinking and eating out, with profits falling at some companies.

headline: Travel tips
author: Ben Clatworthy
text:
Easyjet launches flights to Aqaba

Easyjet is to introduce flights from Gatwick to Aqaba in Jordan. The southern port city, on the Red Sea's Gulf of Aqaba, is a prime spot for divers, who flock to the dive clubs near the Yamanieh coral reef. It is also a gateway to the Wadi Rum desert valley and a couple of hours' drive from the ancient city of Petra. Flights start on November 10, operate weekly on Saturdays, and returns cost from £20.

Crocodile watching in Papua New Guinea

Bamboo Travel, the Asia specialist, has launched a series of trips to Papua New Guinea, with itineraries ranging from 14 to 18 days. Highlights include highland tribal festivals, crocodile watching along the Sepik River, spotting birds of paradise and tours that explore the Pacific island's Second World War history. Two-week trips cost from £,795pp, including flights, 12 nights' B&B, some additional meals and the services of a guide (020 7720 9285, bambootravel.co.uk).

Orient Express opens new route

A quirk in European rail timetabling has enabled the launch of a Venice-Simplon Orient Express rail holiday taking in Venice, Paris and Berlin. The seven-day journey starts in Venice before winding on to the French capital and finally to Berlin, where there's a guided tour of the city's highlights. The six-night holiday costs from £,565pp, including flights, B&B hotels and two nights on board the train (020 7590 0674, luxuryholidaycompany.com).

Spend a night in the Doghouse

Brewdog, the Scottish brewery and pub chain, has opened a Doghouse hotel on the site of its brewery in Columbus, Ohio. The 32 rooms overlook the state-of-the-art brewery and come with beer taps and a built-in beer fridge in the shower. Rooms cost from $162 (£25) a night, and a second Doghouse is planned to open in Ellon, near Aberdeen, next year (brewdog.com).

Ben Clatworthy

headline: Newbie firm aims to be best, not biggest, in Scotland
author: Greig Cameron
text:
From the outside Gilson Gray would appear to be a fairly typical law firm. Its Edinburgh office is located discreetly in a blond sandstone townhouse not far from the Caledonian Hotel. Inside the carpets are plush and the walls are tastefully decorated with paintings.

A hint that things might be a bit different comes with Glen Gilson, the co-founder, who is dressed casually in an open-necked pink shirt, light trousers and dark trainers. The firm adopted a "dress for your day" policy at the start of the year, ending the requirement for business dress at all times.

That suits Gilson Gray as the young newcomer in the Scottish legal world. It launched in 2014 with the claim of being the first new full-service firm to open its doors north of the border in a century. Turnover is running into eight figures and the client list includes names such as KPMG, Cala Homes and Brewdog.

Mr Gilson, 41, says that the aim was always to create a culture that meant the firm was a great place to work rather than to hit specific financial performance targets. That included his mission to break down the gaps between the traditional hierarchies associated with law firms.

"There was an opportunity for a firm to be a bit more human," he says. "Anything can be raised here, and there is no ladder it has to be run up to do so.

"Frankly we wanted to make sure that people, when they got up in the morning, wanted to come and work here. That creates a community feeling and our success is a direct result of that. If you get the people stuff and the client stuff right at the front then the financials will follow."

Having started on day one with several senior hires from around the industry, the firm acquired about 4,000 clients in its very early days. While that may have been a logistical and technical challenge, it gave Gilson Gray an immediate level of traction and credibility.

According to Mr Gilson the firm was running profitably at the end of its first quarter of trading.

"I think that the Scottish business community see us as one of them rather than a necessary evil or something they have to buy," he says.

There have been bumps along the way with Mr Gilson acknowledging that the firm sought help from external consultants about two and a half years in because it had "run out of strategy".

What followed was an evaluation of the original drivers and an exercise in asking staff how they saw the firm.

From that a list of 20 measures was drawn up; only one relates to the financial performance.

Mr Gilson suggests that the combination of those elements, which include issues such as work-life balance and mindfulness, are core to what the firm is trying to achieve and can be independently assessed each year to see what progress is being made.

His ambition is still to be seen as the best firm in Scotland, although he concedes that Gilson Gray does not expect to challenge for the title of the biggest.

"We are not trying to be the biggest turnover in the country. Scale does not necessarily drive towards profitability," he says. "I genuinely think there is a ceiling to real culture.

"We are talking about a finite resource of legal talent in Scotland and within that there is a certain DNA we are looking for. We will not greedily pursue additional turnover in sacrifice of that DNA."

He is also not afraid to criticise the industry, describing it as an often "impenetrable environment" for even the most savvy of consumers. He also believes that the traditional financial models pursued by law firms need to change.

Mr Gilson points to the fact that the partners at his firm are "on the tools", doing work for clients rather than handing it down the chain to more junior lawyers. "People expect lawyers to be able to write pieces of paper but our lawyers have social capability. They know how to communicate and treat another person," he says.

"Clients want to know that the person on the Law Society of Scotland books as the supervising partner is actually doing their work. At the majority of law firms they are not, as it is being delegated so far down the system."

headline: THE DAILY BRIEFING
text:
Electric swoop??French oil and gas giant Total has snapped up French electric car charging network G2mobility, giving it control of around 10,000 charging points amid rising demand for electric cars.

Stores takeover??Poundland is taking over 20 former Poundworld stores, creating 400 jobs. Poundworld collapsed in June, resulting in the closure of all 335 shops and the loss of 5,000 jobs.

Risky UK??Billionaire trader Howard Marks, 72, has said the UK is too risky to invest in due to Brexit. The American founder of Oaktree Capital Management, who made his fortune by snapping up cheap assets in economic downturns, said there could be huge market swings if Britain leaves without a deal.

Jumping jack's??Hundreds of shoppers queued up as Tesco's first new Jack's discount stores opened in Chatteris, Cambridgeshire, and Immingham, Lincolnshire.

Iceland first??Craft brewer Brewdog has opened its first bar in Iceland, and saw sales soar 55pc to £8m in the six months to June 30.

Egypt buy??Oil and gas explorer Soco International is buying Egyptian rival Merlon for £90m, as it looks to build its business in the country.

Second place??Wall Street titan Goldman Sachs has overtaken Citigroup as the world's second-largest investment bank. JP Morgan is in top spot.

Well chuffed??North Sea focused Jersey Oil and Gas is very excited about prospects of its Verbier well, set to be drilled this year.

Pubs bonanza??Sales at pub chain City Pub Group rose 24pc to £0m in the 26 weeks to July 1 after opening nine pubs. It swung to a £31,857 profit from a £8,186 loss.

Finance role??Retirement firm Saga has poached insurance executive James Quin, 49, as its finance chief. He is the chief financial officer at Zurich Insurance Group.

Pension boost??More workers are saving for retirement following auto-enrolment. The Pensions Regulator said 84pc save into a workplace pension, up from 77pc.

©aily Mail

Broccoli and Stilton soup

Serves six

YOU NEED:

25g butter Small onion, diced

450g broccoli

500ml vegetable stock 100g Stilton 150ml single cream

METHOD: Melt the butter in a large saucepan, add the onion and cook gently for five minutes.

Finely chop the broccoli florets and stems, keeping a few florets unchopped, to decorate.

Add all the broccoli to the onion, plus the stock, and bring to the boil for five minutes.

Remove the broccoli florets for garnish, then blend the rest with the stock until very finely chopped.

Crumble the Stilton, making sure you keep a few crumbs to garnish the soup.

Add the Stilton to the soup and whizz briefly. Stir in the cream and heat gently, but do not boil.

Before serving, season to taste. Then pour into bowls and garnish each bowl with broccoli florets and a few crumbs of Stilton. Blackberry bakewell cake

Serves eight

YOU NEED:

150g unsalted butter

150g caster sugar

3 eggs, beaten

1 tsp almond extract

100g ground almonds

80g self-raising flour

200g blackberries

30g flaked almonds

2 tbsp demerara sugar

METHOD: Preheat the oven to 180C/160C fan/gas 4. Grease and line a 20cm round loose-based cake tin with baking parchment.

In a bowl, cream the butter and sugar together until fluffy. Add the eggs, almond extract, ground almonds and flour and mix well.

Spread the mixture evenly in the prepared tin. Scatter blackberries over the top then sprinkle over the flaked almonds and demerara sugar.

Bake in the oven for an hour or until a skewer inserted into the centre comes out clean.

headline: A pint of principle
text:
A TRENDY Scots beer firm axed a project with a US brewer as it reportedly offered free drinks to Donald Trump fans.

BrewDog was to stage events with Atlanta-based ale outfit Scofflaw at six of its pubs in England.

But the Aberdeenshire firm pulled the plug after their American partner offered freebies to supporters of the president. Its founder James Watt tweeted: "We had no idea about the plan Scofflaw have announced.

"We are in no way aligned with their position and will be cancelling all the events and sending the beer back."

headline: Free beer stunt for Trump fans bites  Brewdog
author: Greig Cameron
text:
They are the anarchic, publicity-hungry bad boys of brewing but a campaign offering free beers to British Trump supporters has spectacularly backfired on Brewdog.

The Scottish craft beer company was due to help launch Scofflaw, a US beer maker, in its UK bars but the collaboration collapsed as Scofflaw said it would offer thousands of pounds of free beer to fans of President Trump.

James Watt, co-founder of the selfstyled craft beer punks, took to social media to express his disgust with the idea. Scofflaw, which has its main base in Atlanta, Georgia, has previously worked with Brewdog to create a special edition beer named 2-8-5 Quad IPA.

It had been intended that Scofflaw's products would be stocked in Brewdog's 36 UK bars. A press release publicising promotional events at four of Brewdog's London sites, as well as in Manchester and Leeds, was sent out at about 11am yesterday by Frank, a London-based public relations agency on behalf of Scofflaw.

Free beer was promised for Trump supporters and the founders of Scofflaw had also apparently planned stunts such as using a monster truck to crush beer cans.

About 90 minutes later the press release was recalled as it contained "incorrect information", but by then the details had made it onto social media platforms and a backlash had begun.

Mr Watt said on Twitter: "We had no idea about the Scofflaw press release or the plans they announced today for the events.We [are] in no way aligned with their position and we will of course be cancelling all the events and sending all of the beer back."

Many people suggested that they would no longer drink Brewdog beers or visit its pubs. However, others suggested that the whole thing was a stunt. Mr Watt announced a separate promotion in which anyone announcing that they "support love not hate" could get a free drink in the company's bars. He suggested that a "rogue" element within Frank had been to blame and described the situation as a "mess".

Scofflaw also tweeted that the press release had been put out in error and without their consent.

Frank did not reply yesterday to a request for comment from The Times.

Brewdog, which uses a different PR agency, has built much of its reputation on edgy marketing with stunts such as using taxidermied animals as drinks holders and brewing the strongest beer in the world.

In 2015, Mr Watt published a blog post in which he said that Mr Trump's political ideas were as "misplaced as his hair".

Last year the company launched a beer in protest against Mr Trump's decision to withdraw the US from the Paris climate change agreement. The Make Earth Great Again brew had a label showing a polar bear fighting a robotic version of the president.

Mr Watt, a former captain of a North Sea fishing boat, founded Brewdog in Fraserburgh in 2017 with his old schoolfriend, Martin Dickie.

headline: BREWDOG  BANK BID TO FLOAT
author: TRACEY BOLES
text:
CSunITY Edited by TRACEY BOLES Tel: 020 7782 4050 e-mail: tracey.boles@thesun.co.uk THE FTSE 100 ROSE 4.26 TO 7237.59 £ BUYS €.14, US$1.31. BRENT CRUDE $84.70

CRAFT brewery BREWDOG is set to appoint banks as the Scots firm pumps up for a stock market flotation.

Co-founder James Watt said plans for an initial public offering were still on track for 2020, despite recent wobbles for other flotations.

He has started talking to financial firms including JP MORGAN , GOLDMAN SACHS and JEFFERIES to see which ones to work with on the public offering which would raise money for international expansion.

The independent brewer was valued at £billion last year after selling a 22 per cent stake to private equity.

It is considering a market debut in either the UK or US, although Mr Watt said London was the preference.

Based in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, Brewdog recently launched in Australia, with China set to follow.

headline: BREWERY BANK BID TO FLOAT
author: TRACEY BOLES
text:
Sun CITY

CRAFT brewery BREWDOG is set to appoint banks as it pumps up for a stock market flotation.

Co-founder James Watt said plans for an initial public offering were still on track for 2020, despite recent wobbles for other flotations.

He has started talking to financial firms including JP MORGAN , GOLDMAN SACHS and JEFFERIES to see which ones to work with on the public offering which would raise money for international expansion.

The independent Scottish brewer —alued at £billion last year after selling a 22 per cent stake to private equity —s considering a market debut in either the UK or the US, although Mr Watts said London was the firm's preference.

Brewdog recently launched in Australia, with China set to follow.

headline: Brewdog  denies role in Trump stunt
author: Greig Cameron
text:
The co-founder of Brewdog has insisted that his company no longer has to rely on stunts to sell beer after it was forced to retract a promise to offer free drinks to Donald Trump supporters.

James Watt was speaking at the launch of a new blueprint to grow the business that includes an £1 million investment in a refrigerated warehouse at Eurocentral in North Lanarkshire.

The site will become the company's global distribution hub and is expected to create around 60 jobs when it opens next month.

The beer-maker, founded in 2007, has cultivated an anarchic image with stunts such as serving beers in taxidermied animals and projecting naked images of the founders on to the House of Commons. Last month it suffered a public relations mix-up when collaborating on the UK launch of Scofflaw, a US craft brewer.

Materials promising supporters of Donald Trump free Scofflaw beers if they turned up to Brewdog bars were circulated to media outlets before being hastily recalled.

Mr Watt posted on Twitter to suggest Brewdog had no knowledge of the plan while Scofflaw also distanced themselves from the public relations firm involved. Brewdog's past antics caused some people to consider whether the whole thing had been set up to generate publicity.

Yesterday, Mr Watt, 36, reaffirmed it was not something the company had planned and said: "I don't think we could have dreamt up a stunt like [that]." He added: "When we started we had to shout loudly just to be heard and make an impact. We have done a lot of things that have been on the edge and controversial and challenged how people thought about beer.

"The realisation is that now we have a bit more scale, a bit more of a team and a bit more community we maybe don't need to do those things.

"We have built the platform we need to speak to people about the things we are passionate about. We can do that without necessarily doing the stunt to make the impact."

Mr Watt said the new refrigerated warehouse at Eurocentral will help the company improve its beer quality by storing drinks at a lower temperature.

He added: "We looked at a lot of different locations in the UK but we were very keen to make the investment in Scotland."

Brewer to move from IPA to IPO, page 46

headline: Brewdog  is ready to move from IPA to IPO
author: Greig Cameron
text:
A Scottish-based brewer and bar operator wants to float on the stock market by 2020, but has not decided whether to list in London or New York.

Brewdog is exploring options with its financial advisers and is strengthening its internal systems ahead of a potential initial public offering, according to James Watt, its co-founder. Mr Watt and Martin Dickie founded the business in Fraserburgh in 2007. It has breweries in Scotland and the United States, with construction under way on another in Australia. The company runs 70 bars around the world and made a £.4 million pre-tax profit on revenue of £11.6 million in 2017.

As well as potentially raising cash to fund further expansion, a listing would give existing shareholders an option to exit. TSG Consumer Partners, an American private equity group, paid £13 million for a 23 per cent stake in Brewdog last year, valuing the business at £ billion. The company also has thousands of small retail investors who have bought into the business in crowdfunding initiatives. The latest valued the company at £.7 billion and is due to close next week.

headline: Brewdog  ready to move from IPA to IPO
author: Greig Cameron
text:
A Scottish-based brewer and bar operator wants to float on the stock market by 2020, but has not decided whether to list in London or New York.

Brewdog is exploring options with its financial advisers and is strengthening its internal systems ahead of a potential initial public offering, according to James Watt, its cofounder.

Mr Watt and Martin Dickie founded the business in Fraserburgh in 2007. It has breweries in Scotland and the United States, with construction under way on another in Brisbane, Australia. The company runs 70 bars around the world, employs more than 1,000 people and its Punk IPA is the bestselling craft beer in Britain. Brewdog made a £.4 million pre-tax profit on revenue of £11.6 million in 2017.

Mr Watt, 36, said that a flotation was one of the funding options under consideration. "It is something we are actively looking at and exploring with some banking partners," he said. "We are making preparations for inside our company from a systems, accounting and financial perspective, as well. It is not to say we are going to do it, but we would like to be in a position internally that we could if it was the right strategic choice.

"I think our preference would be to do it in the UK as it is our home, but we haven't discounted doing it in the US."

As well as potentially raising cash to fund further expansion, a listing would give existing shareholders an option to exit. TSG Consumer Partners, an American private equity group, paid £13 million for a 23 per cent stake in Brewdog last year, valuing the business at £ billion. The company also has thousands of small retail investors, which it calls equity punks, who have bought into the business in crowdfunding initiatives.

The latest valued the company at £.7 billion and is due to close next week. It is in line to raise more than £3 million from 46,000 investors, which would mean that Brewdog has raised about £4 million from such schemes and will have more than 90,000 shareholders.

At the time of the TSG deal, Brewdog suggested that people who had been original crowdfunders in 2010 had seen their shares rise in value by 2,765 per cent, while those investing in 2016 could have realised a 177 per cent gain.

However, there has been criticism from investment groups, as small investors were limited to selling a maximum of 15 per cent of their holding, or up to 40 shares. There also have been questions over whether the company's valuation is justified.

Yesterday Mr Watt launched a "blueprint" for the next stage in Brewdog's growth that includes a plan to have almost 120 bars by the end of next year, including outlets with in-house brewing facilities in Manchester, Dublin and Paris. The group also will test a franchise scheme in which equity investors can start their own Brewdog bar. The company is closing in on a deal in China and Mr Watt said it was in talks about opening a brewery in Vietnam.

headline: Need to know
text:

Brewdog is exploring options with its financial advisers and is strengthening its internal systems ahead of a potential initial public offering. Hunterston in North Ayrshire and Longannet, Fife, are being considered for a plant that could provide up to 1,000 jobs. Talgo, which is based in Las Rozas near Madrid, specialises in building lightweight, high-speed trains. Page 47

headline: THE DASTARDLY MR DEEDES
text:
Morgan Stanley's Canary Wharf office has been a hotbed of giddy excitement. Chisel-cheeked Hollywood studmuffin Patrick Dempsey, 52, best known for playing Dr Derek 'McDreamy' Shepherd in hospital drama Grey's Anatomy, popped in to do research this week for a future role. My man on the trading floor reports: 'He wasn't short of females offering their mentoring services. Nor male ones, for that matter.'

Domino's Pizza delivered its first trading update yesterday under chief financial officer David Bauernfeind, after predecessor Rachel Osborne departed in June. Bauernfeind is the fourth head bean-counter in four years to serve under the pizza parlour's not-as-nice-as-he-looks boss David Wild. He's so far been in post for ten days. Who said it wouldn't last?

Why hasn't Amazon founder Jeff Bezos commented on the disappearance of Washington Post journalist Jamal Khashoggi, last seen entering Saudi Arabia's consulate in Turkey a fortnight ago? As proprietor of the Post - and a model one thus far, it should be said - one might have expected him to have at least issued a statement on the matter. Surely Bezos isn't afraid of offending the Saudi Kingdom, where he has business interests? He's always more than happy to irritate President Trump.

Rolls-Royce is moving its HQ in Victoria's Buckingham Gate - minutes from the Palace - to the Guardian's glass-fronted property in King's Cross. The area now also houses Facebook and Google, and is unrecognisable from its previous incarnation as London's most notorious red light district.

Once-mighty US department store Sears' decision to file for bankruptcy raises inevitable questions about its chairman and majority shareholder Edward Lampert. Some accuse the billionaire hedge funder of being out of step with the store's traditionally middle-class clientele. Alarm bells first began ringing among staff when the ex-Goldman trader, 56, began espousing to them the customer service he received in luxury store Hermes while buying £00 shoes.

Craft brewers Brewdog today launches Hop Exchange, a new beer whose price correlates with the movement of the FTSE 100. Something to look forward to during the next meltdown, I suppose.

©aily Mail

headline: EVERYONE knows the saving grace [...]
author: LISA MINOT
text:
Edited by LISA MINOT

lEVERYONE knows the saving grace of long-haul flying is being able to work your way through the on-board drinks menu.

Well, now Aberdeenshire brewer Brewdog is taking in-flight boozing to the next level with its very own airline.

Brewdog Airlines has chartered a Boeing 767 to Columbus, Ohio, for a fournight beer-themed holiday. Those onboard will be the first to sample the brewer's latest beer "designed to taste better at altitude".

The trip leaves Stansted on February 21, 2019 to the USA where beer lovers will stay in The DogHouse Hotel and tour the company's brewery and beer museum plus other bars, restaurants and other breweries.

headline: Brewdog  grabs Diageo expert to head up spirits arm
text:
An executive in charge of some of Diageo's biggest brands has joined Brewdog to head its spirits arm.

David Gates spent 27 years with the world's biggest spirits producer, which included time overseeing Johnnie Walker Scotch whisky, Smirnoff vodka and Captain Morgan rum. Most recently he ran the company's futures division, which monitors innovations and science that can bring benefits to the drinks industry.

Brewdog started Lonewolf, its distilling business, in April last year. It produces gin and vodka and wants to make whisky. James Watt, Brewdog's co-founder, said: "David is as obsessed with quality and flavour as we are.

His proven success makes him a force to be reckoned with in the industry."

Mr Gates, 52, said: "The [chance] to shake up the spirits category with the industry's biggest disruptor was just too good to pass up."

headline: Scotland champions businesses with a cause
author: Mure Dickie in Edinburgh
text:
Ministers encourage entrepreneurs to combine commerce with charity

As an idealistic university graduate, Alan Mahon says he was very disappointed when he failed the tests to join the UK’ overseas aid agency. But he later found a different job he felt could be socially useful: selling beer.

His two-year-old venture Brewgooder, a craft beer brand that promises all profits will go to charities providing clean water, has already funded more than 60 projects serving more than 33,000 people in Malawi, he said.

That makes Mr Mahon one of Scotland’ growing ranks of “ocial entrepreneurs” a class of business people the government is keen to encourage. Two years ago, ministers launched a 10-year national strategy that aimed to establish social enterprises as “ more just, democratic and inclusive way of doing business” promoting the concept in schools and universities.

An official census last year found that the number of social enterprises in Scotland had risen by 8 per cent since 2015 to 5,600 businesses, while the sector’ total trading income had grown 22 per cent to an annual £.74bn.

Alastair Davis, chief executive of Social Investment Scotland, which finances social enterprises, said interest in combining business with social impact is growing among young entrepreneurs.

“eople in that world are becoming more socially minded,”e said.

SIS this year launched a venture fund aimed at individuals who wanted to invest in social enterprise. Scotland was seen as a place where support for the sector was “among] the most advanced, certainly in the developed world” Mr Davis said.

Most social enterprises remain small though —he Social Enterprise Scotland survey found 57 per cent had annual income of less than £00,000 —nd many are happy to remain so. But entrepreneurs such as Mr Mahon are as keen to grow as his more profit-minded peers.

Social enterprise, he said, allowed him to combine a charitable impulse developed during a stint volunteering in Nepal with an entrepreneurial instinct that led him to sell protein bars to school friends at a 50 per cent margin.

Being hired by Josh Littlejohn was a revelation. Mr Littlejohn is a social entrepreneur who has created a chain of sandwich shops and a Scottish Business Awards venture that has hosted high-profile charity dinners with guests such as George Clooney and Bill Clinton to support homeless people.

“ did not realise you could have this business model that did a good thing while building a brand and making something that was cool,”r Mahon said.

Brewgooder has agreed a series of deals with supermarkets to sell its Pilsner-style lager, which is made by beer producer BrewDog at “ero margin” Revenues in the 12 months to last April were about £50,000 and expected to grow 60 per cent again this year.

Hey Girls, a menstrual products company founded in January, is growing even faster. The business, which gives away one pack of product for every one bought by a customer, had generated sales of about £87,000 by July, said Celia Hodson, company founder.

It has won contracts with Asda, Waitrose, the Scottish government and councils across Scotland. “ow we are doing anywhere between £0,000 and £00,000 a month in turnover,”he said.

The focus on the difficulties some women have affording menstrual products is informed by Ms Hodson’ experience as a single parent. This, along with careful product design, creates customer enthusiasm that helps the company challenge the multinationals that dominate the market, she said.

The success of individual projects and the sector in general will depend, though, on maintaining consumer trust, a task that may be made harder by the lack of a generally accepted definition of a social enterprise.

There is agreement that a social enterprise must have a primary social goal and that all or the bulk of profits must go to charitable purposes or be put back into the business, but there are no rules limiting staff salaries.

Still, rewgooder and Hey Girls are unlikely to draw the kind of criticism over high executive pay that has hit big charities.

Mr Mahon says that as managing director, he is the lowest paid of eight staff with a £5,000 annual salary.

Ms Hodson said she had decided on a flat salary of £0,000 for everyone at Hey Girls. “xcept myself —ho doesn’ get paid,”he said.

Still, Brewgooder and Hey Girls are unlikely to draw the kind of criticism over high executive pay that has hit major charities.

Mr Mahon says that as managing director, he is the lowest paid of eight staff with a £5,000 annual salary.

Ms Hodson said she had decided on a flat salary of £0,000 for everyone at Hey Girls. "Except myself —ho doesn't get paid," she said.

'I did not realise you could have this business model that did a good thing while building a brand and making something cool'

headline: Beer industry has big ideas brewing
author: Greig Cameron
text:
The Scottish beer industry has a target to double its collective turnover to £ billion by 2030.

A new strategy outlining the growth potential of the sector is being published today by Scotland Food and Drink, the trade body. The blueprint lays out plans to emphasise the quality of homegrown products, to work more closely with companies in tourism and hospitality and to define a more positive position for beer in society. It is estimated there are more than 130 beer breweries in Scotland, which have a combined turnover of about £00 million and support 8,500 jobs. Along with bigger and long-established brewers, such as Tennent's and Belhaven, there has been a sharp rise in independents over the past decade.

Brewdog has the bestselling craft beer in the UK, with its Punk IPA, but the sector is still dominated by microbreweries and small businesses, with only 10 per cent of companies thought to have sales of more than £ million.

As well as looking to push sales in overseas markets, it is hoped that work can be done to ensure that greater varieties and amounts of Scottish beer are sold at events including music festivals, conferences and sporting occasions.

The 24-page document highlights challenges that need to be addressed in areas including skills, regulation, taxation, exports and environmental sustainability.

Hilary Jones, chairwoman of the Brewing Industry Leadership Group, which has produced the strategy report, said: "Even though we have an unrivalled track record producing great beer and centuries of history behind us, the rising popularity of global craft beer means that Scotland needs to sharpen its game if it is to remain an international leader.

"If you look back to less than two centuries ago, Scotland was one of the few great brewing nations. We were inventive, bringing technologists and chemists into our breweries. We created new beer types and brewed Porters, Stouts, IPAs and small batch beers, added botanicals and flavourings. We exported to the USA, Asia, Australia and Africa.

"Scotland has a precious reputation for brewing quality and there are many opportunities for growth, but we need a new national and unified approach that maximises potential and quality."

£00m Turnover of the sector in Scotland

headline: HEATH RISKS OF BOOM IN STRONG CRAFT BEER
author: ANDY DOLAN
text:
THEY are painstakingly brewed to win over discerning ale lovers - and have been embraced by the middle classes.

But craft beers can be as potent as the ciders or super-strength lagers favoured by street drinkers - and officials fear they could be fuelling alcohol-related health problems.

Waitrose, Sainsbury's and Tesco are among chains selling craft beers and ales with more than 9 per cent alcohol-by-volume. Brewdog's Mr President American Double IPA, at 9.2 per cent, is sold in 300ml bottles at Tesco for £.10. Just one has 3.04 units of alcohol - almost a quarter of the weekly recommended maximum of 14 units.

James Calder, of the Society of Independent Brewers, said Belgian beers, stouts and some IPAs 'have more interesting and complex flavours ... the higher [alcohol content] is simply part of that traditional style of brewing'.

But Andrew Misell, of Alcohol Change, warned: 'If we drink these as we would a weaker beer, we're in danger of drinking a lot more than we intend.'

©aily Mail

headline: Brewer return to bar
text:
NEWS IN BRIEF

Brewdog, the Aberdeenshire craft brewing, distilling and hospitality company, has opened its 80th bar, taking its total opened for the year to 15. The new venue in the Finnish city of Tampere is Brewdog's second in the country, after it launched an outlet in Helsinki in 2014. James Watt, the co-founder of Brewdog, said that Finland was an exciting hub for craft beer innovation and experimentation, and added: "We couldn't be more excited to expand our presence here."

headline: Workplace perks have gone berserk —ut do we care?
author: Pilita Clark
text:
Office workers have always seemed pampered compared with what others endure

One of my first jobs was on a prawn trawler in northern Australia where I was hired as a cook and deckhand.

This was baffling considering I was just out of high school, could barely boil an egg and had never worked on a boat. But I was cheap and more or less willing, until the day the nets dragged up an enormous shark.

It was so heavy that the First Mate, a short man with a terrifying temper, decided the only way to get rid of it was to tie a rope around its thrashing tail so it could be winched up off the deck and lowered back in the water.

After some effort, the creature was finally dangling vertically from its tail over the railings, its nose just inches above the water. As another deckhand was about to cut the rope to free it, the First Mate yelled: “on’ waste the rope. Cut the tail!”

To my horror, the deckhand sawed off the tail, just below the rope. The great fish plunged headfirst into the sea and sank to its certain death, leaving a bloody trail behind as it went.

It is fair to say that workplace relations were not enhanced by this event. But since we were far out at sea, days from land, and everyone from the Captain down was scared of the First Mate, there was nothing to be done about it.

Years later, whenever I was ever yelled at by an editor or aghast at some idiotic mistake I had made at work, I would look back to that day on the boat and think: could be worse.

The lot of the office worker has always seemed a haven compared with what millions of others endure. But I was reminded of how great the gulf between corporate life and blue collar work has grown the other day as I read a new book by Dan Lyons called Lab Rats: Why Modern Work Makes People Miserable.

Mr Lyons is a very funny journalist who thinks greedy tech barons have wrecked office life by cutting wages and dehumanising workers, subjecting them to maddening management fads and job insecurity disguised by ping pong tables and free snacks.

Much of his polemic rings true. I especially liked the section on the “ightmarish hellhole”f open plan offices that are sold as a way to foster collaboration but instead make people grumpy and unproductive, all to save on overheads.

Yet as I got towards the end of the book, where Mr Lyons writes of sensible bosses who treat their workers well, I was struck by another Silicon Valley trend: the lavish perk.

I am sure that in extreme cases the modern office can be a joyless horror, possibly worse than a prawn trawler. But it is useful to remember that in recent years, it has also become a place of once unheard-of largesse.

At the Basecamp software company in Chicago, Mr Lyons reports that workers are not merely offered the usual suite of gym membership, massages and whatnot. For those who stay at least a year, the firm also pays for an annual vacation worth up to $5,000.

I had heard of Facebook’ egg-freezing and gender reassignment benefits, but not this far more useful paid annual holiday. I probably should have.

There has been an “xplosion of diversity”n the types of perks companies are offering workers in recent years, say researchers at the Glassdoor jobs site, and not just in the US.

At UKFast, a British data hosting business, workers get a paid day off on their birthdays, a week’ leave for getting married, plus something called UKFest, an annual festival with live bands, free food and drink that it describes as a “ini-Glastonbury”

In Scotland, the BrewDog craft beer company offers people with a new dog a week’ leave to help settle it in.

Interestingly, it is not clear if they should bother. Exotic perks might spur headlines but Glassdoor research in 2016 showed just three core benefits matter most to workers: health insurance; retirement plans and time off.

A more recent study suggests the number one perk desired by most workers is even simpler: natural light and outdoor views.

I sincerely hope this is true. It means that all those people working outside in fierce conditions, on land or at sea with not a gym membership in sight, might be the real workplace winners after all.

pilita.clark@ft.com

Twitter: @pilitaclark

Letter in response to this column:

Nice offices make for happy, productive staff / From Racheal Cadey, London, UK

headline: CITY PEOPLE
author: Dominic Walsh
text:
The feuds, the faces and the farcical Dominic Walsh @walshdominic

Business big shot

NAME MATTHEW BAYFIELD

AGE 44 POSITION CHIEF EXECUTIVE, PARITY GROUP

Parity Group , a technology consulting and recruitment company based, has appointed Matthew Bayfield as its chief executive as it sets out to expand into selling data advice (Tabby Kinder writes).

Mr Bayfield was named as one of the 50 most influential people in data by Data IQ, the industry's awards, for advising companies including O2, Virgin, Hiscox , Rolls-Royce and Paypal on using data to make business decisions.

The psychology graduate worked as head of data for Ogilvy & Mather , an advertising agency, and started and sold three data strategy and research businesses. Before joining the London-based Parity as managing director, Mr Bayfield led a £ million crowdfunding round as interim boss of Nhouse Commercial, an architectural firm. He joined Parity's senior management team in May last year to expand its consultancy services business.

Parity has moved Alan Rommel , its former head, into the position of chief operating officer, with the executive "having returned the business to profitable growth". Analysts at WH Ireland , a broker, said that Parity, which has a market valuation of £.2 million, would report profit before tax of £50,000 for 2018.

CAFÉS COLOURFUL HISTORY IS TAKING THE PATISSERIE

Amid the carnage at Patisserie Holdings , it has passed almost unremarked that the Patisserie Valerie in Old Compton Street, Soho, was among the 71 café closed by administrators at KPMG . The first caféas opened in 1926 in Frith Street by Madame Valerie, its Belgian founder, but when the premises was destroyed during the Blitz it was re-established in Old Compton Street. At least that's the history the company always peddled. BBC research suggests the founder was probably not called "Valerie", implying this story may not quite match up with the truth. A bit like the company's finances, then.

PROMISES BUILT ON SAND

The housing minister's "more, better, faster" slogan fell flat at the British Property Federation 's investment conference in London. Marnix Elsenaar, head of planning at Addleshaw Goddard , the law firm, described the speech by Kit Malthouse as "an extraordinary blustering performance" as he "boasted that it's never been such a good time to build houses while completely ignoring the massive elephants in the room: Brexit and the appalling rise in homelessness".

CAUGHT ON THE HOPS

When Brewdog sold a stake in the business to an American private equity firm in 2017, the deal valued the Scottish brewer at about £ billion. Then last October it closed a £6 million crowdfunding, boosting its value to £.8 billion. Yet the most recent trades last week on Asset Match, a trading platform, were struck at a price that valued Brewdog at a mere £.1 billion.

Which means that those who invested in the crowdfunding are under water. Or does it? According to James Watt, the company's cofounder, left, "with such a small number of trades it is impossible to get an accurate view on valuation".

headline: Pay packages: benefits focus on homes, loans and wellbeing
author: Alice Hancock
text:
Rewards are a way to help employers stand out when recruiting

When Diya Gera moved to London from her home in India as a graduate recruit in 2017 she spent months looking for somewhere to live. She was not alone.

“ lot of my friends were struggling to find good flats with good facilities for an affordable price,”he says.

In the end it was her employer, professional services firm Deloitte, that threw her a lifeline. In 2015 Deloitte had made a deal with corporate landlord Get Living to reserve flats for a number of its graduates in the former Olympic Village development.

The flats are let at market rates but Deloitte takes care of the form filling and tenants do not have to stump up an initial deposit or agency fees, which can bring the otherwise unaffordable within reach.

In the year before the scheme started, Deloitte ran a survey among its graduate intake in which nearly 90 per cent said they wanted help to find housing. “ stopped looking after [I found out] about this,”ays Ms Gera.

Deloitte partner James Ferguson, who had made it a new year resolution to get the scheme under way, hopes to work with other employers to scale it up. “e could make it available to all our staff,”e says.

The core employee benefits offered by an employer are pensions and life and health insurance. But companies are also paying attention to workers’eeds beyond the pay packet.

“f the economy carries on growing and there are skills shortages, organisations will be looking to see how they can stand out in the labour market,”ays Charles Cotton of the Chartered Institute of Personnel and Development.

One way to stand out is to offer idiosyncratic benefits. They may also reflect the wider values of the organisation and are harder to copy.

At outdoor clothing company Patagonia, some employees are offered three-day weekends to encourage them to get out and about and it has pledged to pay bail for employees if they are arrested during a peaceful environmental protest. At UK-based brewery and pub chain BrewDog, employees with a new dog can take a week’ “uppy leave” Facebook and Apple offer egg freezing for women and free housing for interns, while Netflix offers unlimited holiday.

Less quirky is help with repaying student loans. A number of employers, including Goodman Masson, PwC, and Penguin RandomHouse, have set up schemes to assist graduate employees saddled with student debt. Forbes magazine dubbed student loan repayment “he hottest”mployment benefit of 2018”

The US arm of PwC will ultimately pay $1,200 per year towards associates’tudent debt for six years, which it says will reduce their total loan principal and interest obligations by $10,000 and shorten the repayment period. PwC says around 8,400 employees have taken up the scheme since 2015 and that 7,700 are currently receiving money.

Offering student loan repayment both attracts workers and keeps them loyal. “f people leave within a certain period they have to repay the company, so it’ a good retention device,”ays Mr Cotton.

Other employers have focused on the “ellness”rend. “here used to be a divide between work and home, and people talked about work-life balance,”ays Debra Corey, adviser to benefits platform Reward Gateway and author of books on HR. “ow it’ work-life integration.”

At London law firm Addleshaw Goddard, as well as private medical insurance, the firm offers a £80 cash benefit toward any health or sports club membership, free weekly yoga classes and a subscription to meditation app Headspace.

Despite “orking in a somewhat traditional profession”mployees expect such benefits, says the firm’ reward manager Helen Ford.

Addleshaw is one of a group of financial services and law firms that last year drew up a Mindful Business Charter, setting out best practice for reducing stress at work.

There is little point in offering rewards, however, if staff do not know they exist.

Ben Frost, a consultant at US-based recruitment specialist Korn Ferry, says many companies worry they should be offering more: “ut actually it’ not a reality problem, it’ a communication problem.”

Developments in technology mean that organisations can easily both glean information from employees about preferences and keep them up to date on benefits offerings through platforms and apps.

Reward Gateway offers one such communications platform. “ach company can put their own personality on it and can use it however they want to drive engagement,”ays Ms Corey.

Small companies may not be able to compete with the healthy free food, classes and massages offered by big tech companies in Silicon Valley, but they can make limited budgets work harder by finding out what employees want through engagement surveys and monitoring take-up.

Ms Corey says: “owadays tech is so easy. HR needs to get on that bandwagon.”

headline: GOOD HEALTH! HOW BOOZE-FREE BEER'S BOOMING
author: HANNAH UTTLEY
text:
IT'S just two weeks since the end of dry January.

But figures reveal there are millions of us who are happy to cut out the alcohol all year round - thanks to a string of low and no-booze beers and wines.

Back in the 1980s and 90s alcohol-free drinks such as Kaliber were derided for their high price and awful taste.

But the industry now has its act together - not least because so many millennials are shunning alcohol.

The demand for the beers has in fact helped Heineken post its best sales growth in more than a decade.

The Dutch brewer revealed a 7.7 per cent rise in revenues from its Heineken beer brand as it rolled out its non-alcoholic Heineken 0.0 brand to 38 countries around the world. It saw overall sales rise 5.9 per cent to £3.5billion in 2018.

Rival Carlsberg said it has also seen a 'significant' increase in demand for non-alcoholic lager and confirmed it was putting 'a lot of money' into further investment.

It recorded a 33 per cent jump in sales of alcohol-free beer across Western Europe in 2018, compared with a year earlier.

As well as helping to avoid a thumping headache the next day, alcohol-free beer tends to have fewer calories, making the risk of a beer belly less likely.

A pint of alcohol-free beer will typically come in at around 150 calories, versus 190 for standard lager and as much as 240 for a strong ale.

Alcohol-free drinks are also a good alternative for pregnant women who want to enjoy a drink without the associated risks. Andy Wood, chief executive of Adnams, which makes an alcohol-free version of its Ghost Ship pale ale, said younger customers are leading the way in shunning booze. 'I don't see millennials changing this habit very soon. They are choosing not to drink but still go out to socialise.'

The quality and choice of alcohol-free beer has improved dramatically, Mr Wood added, helping to boost sales. 'We're now producing great tasting beers without the alcohol.'

Many supermarkets now have dedicated alcohol-free sections in their drinks aisles, with options including Budweiser's Prohibition beer and craft beer firm Brewdog's Nanny State hoppy ale.

Low alcohol and alcohol free wines are also on the up, as well as 'Nosecco', an alternative to the Italian sparkling wine.

©aily Mail
