Anumber of employers, including Goodman Masson, PwC, and Penguin RandomHouse,have set up schemes to assistgraduate employees saddledwith student debt. Forbesmagazine dubbed student loanrepayment "the hottest" employment benefit of 2018".

The US arm of PwC will ultimately pay $1,200 per yeartowards associates' studentdebt for six years, which itsays will reduce their totalloan principal .and interest obligations by $10,000 and shorten the repayment period. PwC says around 8,400 employees have taken up the"scheme since 2015 and that7,700 are currently receivingmoney.

Offering student loan repayment both attracts workersand keeps them loyal. "If people leave within a certainperiod they have to repay the company, so it's a good retention device," says Mr Cotton.Other employers have focused on the "wellness"trend. "There used to be adivide between work andhome, and people talked aboutwork-life balance," says Debra Corey, adviser to benefits platform Reward Gateway and author of books on HR. "Now it's work-life integration."

At London law firm Addleshaw Goddard, as well as private medical insurance, thefirm offers a £80 cash benefittoward any health or sportsclub membership, free weeklyyoga classes and a subscription"to meditation app Headspace.

Despite "working in a somewhat traditional profession"employees expect such bene" fits, says the firm's rewardManager Helen Ford.

Addleshaw is one of a group of financial services and lawfirms that last year drew up aMindful Business Charter, setting out best practice for reducing stress at work.

There is little point in offering rewards, however, if staff do not know they exist.

Ben Frost, a consultant at US-based recruitment specialist Korn Ferry, says many companies worry they should beoffering more: "But actuallyit's not a reality problem, it's acommunication problem."

Developmentsin technology mean that organisations caneasily both glean informationfrom employees about preferences and keep them up todate on benefits offeringsthrough platforms and apps.

Reward Gateway offers one such communications platform. "Each company can put their own personality on it andcan use it however they want to drive engagement," says MsCorey.

Small companiesmay not be able to compete with thehealthy free food, classes andmassages offered by big techcompanies in Silicon Valley,but they can make limitedbudgets work harder by finding out what employees wantthrough engagement surveysand monitoring take-up.

Ms Corey says: "Nowadays tech is so easy. HR needs to getonthatbandwagon."

headline: Scottish Tories can challenge the tired Holyrood brands Craig Hoy
text:
Ispend a lot of my time talking to PR people. Relationships are "win-win" and everything "dovetails". Recently the phrase "Challenger Brand" has crowbarred its way into conversation. There is substance behind the slogan and this presents Scottish Conservatives with a huge opportunity. Challenger Brands, such as the online estate agency Purple Bricks, are neither market leaders nor niche players. They have huge goals, take risks and aim to succeed. The Scottish craft beer producer, BrewDog, believes "you can become more like Goliath as long as you continue to think and act like David".

Challenger Brands can be revamped brands which lost their lustre. This fairly describes the Tories before Ruth Davidson became leader.

Challenger Brands need to secure consumer trust. Over decades, the Conservatives lost the trust, or at least the support, of many Scots.

Davidson has deftly won their trust on the Union. It's time to extend this to areas such as health and education. Trust is secured by a product doing what it says on the tin. Challenger Brands have to offer high-quality products which meet consumers' changing needs. In politics, swap "products" to "policies".

Having never been in government at Holyrood, the Conservatives can innovate in a way Labour and the SNP can't. Both are bound by a legacy of squandered opportunities.

The Tories need to blend a centreright, commonsense philosophy with people's priorities. Challenger Brands have to identify consumers underserved by the leading brands —n this case the SNP and Labour.

Davidson's party has started to show policy mettle, such as increased fines for antisocial behaviour. This can underline another important factor: predictability. Successful brands are reliable and consistent. Consumers trust a product that offers positive attributes, such as strong environmental credentials. The Conservatives need to offer equivalent positive voter experiences. Clarity, authenticity and transparency might be good places to start.

Like David against Goliath, the underdog is starting to think about victory. The next decade could be defined by Scottish Conservatives if they approach the 2021 Holyrood elections thinking like a Challenger Brand.

Craig Hoy is a former political journalist and public affairs entrepreneur. He is an approved member of the parliamentary candidates list for the Scottish Conservatives

headline: Camra, Action!
text:
British beer has changed and needs more support

A generation ago British pubs offered a dismal selection of beers. Fizzy lagers and sludgy bitters predominated. Disciples of the Campaign for Real Ale (Camra) fought a lonely crusade against the bland uniformity of the big breweries. Its members were derided as bearded obsessives devoted to a doomed cause. Yet their efforts played a vital role in reviving independent breweries.

Now Camra risks putting itself on the wrong side of the counter. Over the past decade, the beer industry has undergone a renaissance thanks to the emergence of craft beers. This began in America, which faced an even more uninspiring selection of beers, and has since flourished in Britain due to a tax break for small brewers introduced in 2002. Drinkers can choose from a selection of interesting and exotically named new beers. Love Among the Ruins anyone? Or how about a can of Even More Jesus? This should be a cause for celebration, but some members of Camra's old guard are refusing to welcome newcomers. They argue that the craft beer movement flouts Camra's strict definition of "real ale", which states that a beer must have yeast in the barrel while it is being served. This disqualifies many craft brewers who filter the yeast before putting the beer into a keg. Brewdog was excluded from Camra's Great British Beer festival on these grounds.

The organisation needs to update its mission and its image. But last year members narrowly defeated a motion to broaden its remit to campaign on behalf of "all pub-goers" along with other modernising measures. This is baffling. British beer is in the midst of an unprecedented rejuvenation yet it is one that its most important consumer association refuses to embrace. Camra should welcome this success rather than reject it. Let's have more exotically named beers all round!

headline: Trouble brewing as hipsters take on Camra's old peculiars
author: Jack Blackburn
text:
It might seem like small beer to some, but to the warring factions of the Campaign for Real Ale the battle for the organisation's future is anything but.

In one corner of the pub are the longserving members, often caricatured as a hardcore of bearded men in T-shirts who would be an asset to any tug-ofwar team. In the other are the hipster reformers, younger, perhaps cooler and more likely to be female.

The disagreement between them has gone public after a group of seven reformers warned that Camra, as it is abbreviated, was "riddled with accusations of sexism and cronyism" and without change risked becoming a "pensioners' drinking club". In a letter published in this month's What's Brewing, the organisation's newsletter, the members, all in their early forties or younger, said that they were feeling increasingly disenfranchised.

"We need to see a campaign thinking more seriously about the next generation of pubgoers — campaign whose public image is not riddled with accusations of sexism," they wrote.

At the root is a row over the definition of real ale, which Camra insists must be served from a barrel in which it is fermenting and must not be served with "extraneous carbon dioxide". This excludes many craft beers that are served from a keg from which the yeast has been filtered, made by breweries such as Brewdog and Camden Town.

Craft beer has exploded in popularity recently, with 500 million pints from independent breweries sold last year. Thousands of smaller breweries have sprung up, reversing 70 years of consolidation in the industry.

Lucy Cousins, 41, a signatory to the letter, said that conservatism was hurting recruitment at a time when 18 pubs a week were closing. "If we don't start getting younger people interested now, it's basically a time bomb," she said.

Younger members have also questioned some of Camra's imagery, including a poster for its Peterborough Beer Festival in August, which featured a man and woman astride a barrel. While the man was holding a pint of beer the woman had a glass of wine.

Peter Holt, landlord of the Southampton Arms in Kentish Town, north London, and winner of Camra awards, said that excluding craft beers over their storage missed the point. "It's about good beer and shit beer, and it doesn't actually matter whether it's in a cask or a keg." He said that if Camra members had the choice of going to Brewdog or a Greene King pub, they would find the cask beer more bland. "It doesn't make sense. The cask beer they're drinking is the equivalent of the stuff they were campaigning against 40 years ago."

The reformers believe that Camra is betraying its founding ideals. It was set up in the 1970s to fight against lager and bland ales. It published its first Good Beer Guide in 1974, held its first festival in 1977 and has 187,000 members.

Tom Stainer, Camra's chief executive, said he had sympathy for reformers but that it was important to maintain standards. "It's a poor analogy but you wouldn't expect the Cat Protection League to spend much of their resources promoting dog ownership," he said. "We're not saying we won't support craft breweries. We are involved in a lot of industry-wide campaigns."

He said that old-fashioned views of women were a problem in the industry. In 2014 Camra withdrew leaflets featuring blonde models holding pints while dressed in corsets. Two years earlier the House of Commons stopped selling Top Totty, a beer advertised by a woman in a bikini and bunny ears.

"Sadly, there probably are elements of that sexist culture across the beer and pub industry," Mr Stainer said. Leading article, page 27 What makes ale real? Camra defines real ale as "a beer brewed from traditional ingredients ... and matured by secondary fermentation in the container from which it is dispensed".

In layman's terms, this means that there is yeast in the cask from which the beer is served: the beer is live. Camra also prohibits serving it with "extraneous carbon dioxide". This definition was agreed by Camra shortly after it was founded in 1971.

Craft beers may be brewed in a number of ways, but many differ from real ales only in that they filter out the yeast before being put into a keg for serving. They are usually made by smaller breweries, which benefited from tax relief introduced by Gordon Brown as chancellor in 2002.

headline: Sup above the clouds
author: JAMES MOORHEAD
text:
FLY TO BREWDOG'S HOTEL IN OHIO

THE first rule of Flight Club is . . . you DO talk about Flight Club.

Some 200 beer fans had gathered for the inaugural flight of BrewDog Airlines.

The atmosphere fizzed as we sipped craft brews before heading to The DogHouse in Columbus, Ohio.

Since founding BrewDog in 2007, James Watt and Martin Dickie have opened 80 bars and export their beer to 60 countries. For this landmark tasting, they unveiled a new 4.5 per cent IPA designed to peak at altitude, called . . . Flight Club.

James says: "We'd had enough of being served mediocre, lifeless beer on flights. So we decided to show the world how it's done." Beers flowed as we climbed to 35,000ft. Then it was time for Flight Club.

At this altitude, travellers often report dulled senses. But you knew this beer was special - light and crisp with floral and citrus notes. Even the aroma of freshly cut grass . . . or so it said on the tin!

There were so many beer hipsters on board chugging so much beer the plane's overworked toilets had to be closed two hours before touchdown in Columbus, Ohio, calling for heroic levels of bladder control.

The beer scene in Columbus is exploding and more than half of the city's 39 breweries opened in the past five years. That includes The DogHouse, BrewDog's biggest project yet. First up at this brewery-hotel was was a tour and tasting, with a can of its Punk IPA perfect for both enthusiasts and newcomers.

After that, it was time to sit back and enjoy the immersive experience of The Dog-Tap, the hotel's main bar. It serves all of BrewDog's big-hitters —s well as local guest beers.

The DogHouse lets guests drink great beer, eat well, socialise and play classic arcade games. Or test your skills at shuffleboard — bit like curling played on a smooth table covered with salt.

Each of the hotel's 32 rooms is inspired by "Planet Brew-Dog", with free wifi, smart TVs and craft beers in the fridge.

Even the bathrooms have a beer fridge —nd yes, there is nothing better than drinking a beer in the shower.

In the morning, guests are treated to a free breakfast and beer mimosas. BrewDog operates two other bars in Columbus, both accessible on the city's free C-bus service.

Further afield is the funky city of Cincinnati. It has loads of great breweries too, such as local champ Rhinegeist. Drink your way down the Columbus Ale Trail, exploring the area and local culture.

Just pick up an Ale Trail book from any participating Central Ohio brewery. They are not hard to find!

For a break from breweries, you can catch the Columbus Blue Jackets of the National Hockey League. Soak up the big-atmosphere of bigtime US sports |(85) and enjoy an epic bagel! They play at the city's Nationwide Arena, with tickets from around £0. Or for a younger scene, the university district is full of lively bars.

Last orders were called on our trip all too soon. On the flight home, two BrewDog fans from Sweden —obyn and Stefan —ere leaving with more than just happy memories and a hangover.

They had got the BrewDog Airlines logo tattooed on their hands. That should last longer than the sore heads.

JAMES MOORHEAD

GO: COLUMBUS (for a beer or two) GETTING/STAYING THERE: Our BrewDog Airlines trip was priced at £,250pp including return flights, three nights at the 5H Renaissance Columbus Downtown Hotel, one night at The DogHouse and excursions. BrewDog has plans to make its airline a regular fixture. Look out for more trips at brewdog.com.

headline: TSG Consumer expands in Europe after raising $4bn fund
author: Leila Abboud in London
text:
New London office to scout for deals with upstart consumer brands

TSG Consumer Partners, the US private equity group that has backed craft beer maker BrewDog, plans to invest about $1bn in Europe as it hunts for rapidly growing pstart brands that are challenging consumer goods giants.

To drive the European expansion, the San Francisco-based group also said it was opening a London office and has hired Beth Pickens, an investment banker formerly with William Blair & Co who has extensive consumer sector experience. It recently raised $4bn for a ew global fund, taking total assets under management to about $9bn.

“e expect Europe to be a significant area of focus for the new fund,”aid Colin Welch, the partner at TSG who will oversee the new office located in Mayfair.

TSG is one of a handful of specialised private equity funds that are seeking to profit from a period of tumult in the consumer goods sector as online shopping and social media have made it easier than ever to launch new brands. Across a wide array of food and beverage, cosmetics, and household products, challenger brands are taking market share from established players such as Unilever, Nestlé, and L’real.

Another fund with a similar sector-based approach is L Catterton, which has $15bn in assets under management; big generalist private equity funds such as KKR and General Atlantic have also been active in the consumer industry.

Mr Welch said that TSG would be looking for investments in the $100m to $800m range, and was open to taking minority or majority stakes in companies depending on what the founders want.

“e’e looking for European brands that have already demonstrated a certain global portability and are led by isionary entrepreneurs,”e added.

TSG’ expansion in Europe comes after it invested in roughly half a dozen companies in the region, including high-end cycles maker Canyon Bicycles. TSG bought a 22 per cent stake in BrewDog, the Scotland-based beer brewer and pub company, in 2017 for £13m, with about half the proceeds going to early investors who wanted to exit and the rest going to further expansion.

It has also made several bets on the cosmetics sector, which has been particular fertile territory for challenger brands as influencers such as ylie Jenner  nd wyneth Paltrow have become instant tastemakers.

Those include evolution Beauty, a fast-growing London-based maker of cosmetics aimed at price-conscious consumers, as well as HUDA Beauty, which was founded by influential beauty blogger uda Kattan, who has nearly 35 million Instagram followers, on par with pop star Lady Gaga.

“ith direct-to-consumer businesses, you own the customer experience and that can have better profitability than relying on the retail sales channel,”aid Mr Welch.

headline: LCF: WATCHDOG 'IGNORED TRADE IN TOXIC BONDS'
author: JAMES BURTON
text:
REGULATORS knew four years ago about problems with toxic bonds like those sold by collapsed trading firm London Capital & Finance - but failed to take action.

The City watchdog warned in 2015 that so-called mini-bonds are highly risky products where investors' money is not protected, and that many firms selling them rely on dodgy marketing practices which fail to make the dangers clear.

But after saying it had concerns, the Financial Conduct Authority did nothing to make the products safer.

And now, 11,500 savers who pumped their money into mini-bonds through LCF face massive losses.

LCF went bust earlier this year after raking in £37m from customers who were offered returns of 8pc and thought their cash would be invested in a wide range of start-ups.

Administrators revealed the money went to just 12 companies in transactions described as 'highly suspicious'.

The revelations that the FCA could have acted sooner will add to pressure for an independent investigation into what went wrong.

Labour MP John Mann, a member of the Treasury select committee, said: 'The FCA has clearly been asleep at the wheel over mini-bonds.

'If it knew these products were risky years ago, it should have acted then to protect consumers. Tougher regulation could have saved thousands of LCF customers from losing their life savings.'

Mini-bonds are typically issued by small firms which have struggled to get funding from a bank. Investors buy them and are guaranteed regular payments as long as the business continues trading.

The products - which are not regulated by the FCA - have grown in popularity since the financial crisis, with issuances by firms including craft brewer Brewdog and Hotel Chocolat to Lancashire County Cricket Club.

But if a firm goes bust, then the savers who bought the bonds can lose everything - and unlike in a bank account, their money is not protected by the Financial Services Compensation Scheme.

In its 2015 report - which mostly focused on peer-to-peer lending over the internet - the FCA said it was concerned by how mini-bonds are being marketed.

The watchdog said: 'Firms are failing to make clear that mini-bonds are investments that place investors' capital at risk, and are not deposit-based or capital-protected products.

'Mini-bonds are generally not traded, so investors' money is effectively locked in until maturity as the mini-bond cannot be sold on before the end of its term. This should be made clear to prospective investors.'

Nicky Morgan , chairman of the Treasury select committee, has written to the FCA calling for a regulatory failure investigation to determine why more had not been done to protect LCF customers.

The watchdog's board was meeting last night to determine whether to open a probe.

©aily Mail

headline: WATCHDOG IN DOCK OVER COLLAPSE OF SAVINGS FIRM
author: JAMES BURTON
text:
THE City watchdog faces an official inquiry into why it allowed the sale of toxic bonds by failed business London Capital & Finance.

An independent review is being launched into the Financial Conduct Authority's handling of LCF, which went bust earlier this year owing £37m to 11,500 mostly elderly savers.

LCF sold so-called mini-bonds which offered returns as high as 8pc.

Investors were told their cash would be lent to a wide range of promising start-ups. But after the company collapsed, administrators discovered it had gone to just 12 companies in a wave of 'highly suspicious' transactions. LCF investors face a long wait to get their money back - and could only see 20p for every £ they handed over.

Mini-bonds are typically sold to the public by small companies that want to raise cash but struggle to borrow any from the banks.

They are meant to pay investors a regular sum - and have been used by a wide range of legitimate companies including Hotel Chocolat and craft beer maker Brewdog - but are unregulated.

Unlike a bank account, the bonds are not covered by the Financial Services Compensation Scheme, meaning that if the company goes bust investors will be left with nothing.

FCA board members decided to ask the Treasury to order an independent review at a meeting last week. The probe will look at whether existing regulations adequately protect retail buyers of mini-bonds from 'unacceptable levels of harm'. It will also examine the FCA's supervision of LCF.

City minister John Glen said: 'By ordering this investigation, we will better understand the circumstances around the collapse and make sure we are properly protecting those who invest their money in the future.'

The Mail revealed on Friday that the FCA knew the bonds were risky four years ago, but took no action to regulate them.

In a 2015 report focused on the peer-to-peer lending industry, the watchdog said: 'Firms are failing to make clear that mini-bonds are investments that place investors' capital at risk, and are not deposit-based or capital-protected products.'

LCF failed after the FCA stopped it from taking on new customers due to concerns about the way its mini-bonds were marketed. Four people have been arrested by the Serious Fraud Office, and the FCA is investigating a company called Surge Group, which was responsible for the bonds' marketing.

Administrator Smith & Williamson said: 'There are a number of highly suspicious transactions involving a small group of connected people which have led to large sums of the bondholders' money ending up in their personal possession or control.'

Individuals named by S&W include LCF chief executive Andy Thomson, its founder Simon Hume-Kendall, as well as their associates Elten Barker and Spencer Golding.

The administrators have approached all four, asking them to hand over what they received so it can be returned to savers. Hume-Kendall and Thomson have agreed to do this, but S&W is still waiting to hear back from the other two.

©aily Mail

headline: BREWDOG  INVITES PUNKS TO INVEST
author: DAILY MAIL REPORTER
text:
BREWDOG has invited beer fans to invest in the business for the sixth time in just nine years.

The craft brewer's crowdfunding round, which will run for an initial 90 days until July 5, is aimed at raising money for breweries, bars and other projects. The business told existing investors - known as Equity for Punks - over the weekend that it would seek to raise a fresh £m.

If it reaches the targeted amount, it would bring the total raised since its first equity crowdfunding round in 2010 to £4m.

'Since Equity for Punks first began we have been blown away by their enthusiasm,' said founder James Watt. Brewdog said the new cash would go towards several projects, including development of the Stone Brewing Tap Room, a brewery in Berlin.

Watt said the decision to develop the German brewery was linked to Brexit.

'Uncertainty surrounding Brexit is crippling British business,' he tweeted. 'So we took our destiny into our own hands and bought a brewery on the continent.'

©aily Mail

headline: READY FOR TAKE-OFF
author: NIGEL TISDALL
text:
Fancy a long weekend seeing mountain gorillas in the wild? RwandAir is increasing its service from Gatwick to Kigali to five times a week. Specialist Tribes offers an 'express' four-day gorilla safari from £,678 pp, excluding flights.

rwandair.com, tribes.co.uk

Scottish brewer BrewDog has created a bespoke IPA called Speedbird 100 as part of British Airways' centenary celebrations. Specially crafted for high-altitude drinking, the beer will be on board from May 1.

ba.com, brewdog.com

A SWISH Italian hotel, Rome Cavalieri, has appointed a Jet Lag Butler to help guests get over a long flight. Advice is given on the perfect pillow, in-room dining and spa treatment, with an Ultimate Remedy costing £43 for two. Alternatively, just have a couple of Negronis ...

romecavalieri.com

Families passing through Gatwick over Easter can make things easier by checking out the airport's online guide to travelling with children. It covers everything from the location of play zones to where you can warm a baby's bottle.

gatwickairport.com/faqs/families

Thomas Cook Airlines has a new flight from Manchester to Montego Bay every Monday from May 6 to October 28. The Half Moon resort has a two mile-long beachfront, championship golf course, 11 tennis courts and equestrian centre. Double rooms from £99 a night with breakfast.

thomascookairlines.com, halfmoon.com

Oman Air is the greenest airline flying out of Heathrow according to the airport's league table. British Airways and SAS also scored well.

omanair.com, heathrowflyquietandgreen.com

Nigel Tisdall

headline: Upstarts are putting pressure on consumer giants, and it shows
author: Graham Ruddick
text:
According to Warren Buffett, it takes twenty years to build a reputation and only five minutes to ruin it. We should not underestimate, therefore, how entrenched some brands are with consumers and the challenges that they have overcome to get there. Unilever, the owner of Dove soap, Wall's ice cream and Lynx deodorant, can trace its origins back to the 1870s. It has seen off world wars, the launch of own-label products by supermarkets and a takeover bid by a Buffett-backed Kraft Heinz.

However, according to analysts at Barclays last week, established food brands face an unprecedented challenge to their supremacy from small, niche rivals. "The food industry stands at a crossroads where its traditional advantages of scale, big brands and mass marketing have been all but eroded," they said.

The analysts quoted research from Nielsen that shows many Gen Z consumers (those aged under 20) and millennials (21 to 34-year-olds) are prepared to pay a premium for healthier and higher-quality products. They also explained how the internet has revolutionised how brands reach consumers, eroding the reliance on expensive television adverts and physical shops.

It is not only in food where this change is taking place, but also in drinks, personal care and homecare. Brewers are being challenged by fast-growing craft beer brands, while Gillette's share of the razors and blades market for men has fallen from 70 per cent to roughly 50 per cent in eight years because of the rise of subscription services, such as Dollar Shave Club and Harry's.

One glance at the profit margins in the consumer goods sector shows that it is ripe for disruption. Last week Tesco, Britain's biggest supermarket chain, celebrated hitting an operating margin of 3.96 per cent in the second half of its financial year as it published its annual results. In contrast, Unilever, which updates on its trading this week, recorded an operating margin of 24.6 per cent last year.

The chasm between the margins of Tesco and Unilever not only dispels the myth that supermarkets have all the power in their dealings with consumer goods makers, but also suggests that it is retailers that have taken the brunt of the impact from the changes in how we shop.

These margins also have left the door open for new challengers prepared to take a smaller piece of the profit cake. Brewdog, which makes some of the bestselling craft beers in the UK, made a £76,000 loss last year despite its sales rising 55 per cent to £72 million.

It is going to cost the established consumers goods groups to adapt to the changes in the market in terms of marketing, innovation and acquisitions. Unilever bought Dollar Shave Club for $1 billion in 2016. The Barclays analysts believe that big food companies can and are fighting back. They point to them becoming more agile and getting products to market in half the time.

Yet the challenge may just be starting, let alone the fightback. As new brands emerge and grow, so will awareness of them. Many will attract the supermarkets, which can stock them on shelves across the country, turning popular local delicacies into national stars. Crate, a brewery in east London launched in 2012, now has its beers sold in nearly 1,500 Tesco stores and on the retailer's website.

Losers are already beginning to emerge. Shares in Kraft Heinz are half the value they were a year ago. 3G Capital, the private equity firm that backs Kraft Heinz, is better known for cost-cutting than innovation.

Mr Buffett is also a big shareholder in Kraft Heinz. His take on the company was that it was hard to take market share away from Heinz Tomato Ketchup and Philadelphia cream cheese. "I was wrong in a couple of ways on Kraft Heinz," he said recently.

Graham Ruddick is Deputy Business Editor of The Times

headline: BREWDOG 'S UNLEASHED
text:
BREWDOG plans to have 100 bars by the end of the year up from 87 now as it opens outlets with cash raised from beer fans.

The craft brewer, known for Punk IPA, is tapping investors for £million to pay for its ambitious plans which include completing two new breweries overseas.

Potential investors what the firm calls its "equity punks" can put in as little as £5 in exchange for perks such as a lifetime discount.

Over a million pounds has been raised so far, said founder James Watt.

headline: Brewers back consumer shift towards a taste for non-alcoholic beer
author: LEILA ABBOUD
text:
Food & beverage. Healthier living

Producers make big effort to overcome ingrained attitudes about booze-free beverages

Beer drinking is so ingrained in British life that any mention of choosing alcohol-free versions provokes shock and ire. What is the point of drinking beer without alcohol in it, the sceptics ask, while others rail against the supposedly poor taste.

Yet when Anna Brown, a 26-year-old consultant living in London, goes to the pub with colleagues, her drink of choice is St Peter's Without, an alcohol-free lager. "I still get to take part in the fun without compromising on things that are important to me," she said, referring to her desire to cut back on calories and avoid hangovers.

While beer purists may recoil, more people are opting for low- and no-alcohol brews, and Europeans in particular have taken to them.

Alcohol consumption is falling in many western countries as people seek healthier lifestyles. In the UK, nearly a quarter of 16- to 24-year-olds say they do not drink alcohol, up from 19 per cent in 2005, and the increase is greater among 25- to 44-year-olds, according to the government's Office for National Statistics. In the US, alcohol sales by volume have been falling since 2016.

Spotting a growth opportunity in an otherwise mature market, Anheuser-Busch InBev and Heineken, the biggest brewers, have launched dozens of lowand no-alcohol beers in the past three years. With their biggest brands they are creating new versions, such as Becks Blue and Heineken 0.0, and then throwing marketing muscle behind them.

Smaller-scale and craft brewers are also getting into the game: Scotland's BrewDog has had success with its aptly named low-alcohol Nanny State pale ale, while English newcomer Big Drop Brewing Co has a popular stout with only 0.5 per cent alcohol.

Supermarkets in Paris and Amsterdam now have shelves full of no-alcohol beers, which typically have less than 0.5 per cent alcohol by volume (ABV), and their low-alcohol cousins, which have less than 3.5 per cent ABV. In the UK, Ocado and Tesco sell roughly 20 types online.

Sales volumes of low- and no-alcohol beer have risen almost 18 per cent in the past five years in western Europe, according to Euromonitor, and are forecast to climb another 12 per cent by the end of 2022.

Companies are betting that alcoholfree brews will not only lure new customers but also retain beer drinkers who are now consuming less. Another attraction of alcohol-free offerings is that they have one-third to half the calories of traditional beers.

There is a bonus for brewers. Alcoholfree beers tend to be more profitable, because in many countries beer is subject to taxes that are built into the retail price, so removing or reducing the alcohol means less duty is owed, thus improving margins.

Yet many consumers are sceptical. Although alcohol-free beers have existed for decades, surveys found people thought they tasted quite bad, and were merely a default option for those who could not drink genuine alcohol, such as pregnant women or drivers.

In response, Heineken and AB InBev challenged their brewers to make an alcohol-free beer that is also strong on taste.

David De Schutter, AB InBev's head of innovation in Europe, had doubts that it could be done. "You have to turn your brewing process upside down and think of it all from all angles," he said. "Normal beer has over 1,000 aroma components, which gives it a complex taste and feeling in your mouth."

Beer brewing typically relies on four ingredients malt barley, hops, yeast and water that can be combined in myriad ways to create hugely different beers. The yeast feeds on the grain and, via fermentation, produces alcohol.

To make alcohol-free beer, you either have to stop or slow the fermentation process or remove the alcohol after it is formed, usually by heating it because alcohol has a lower boiling point than the rest of the beer. Both approaches can render the beer either too sweet, too acidic or too flat.

Heineken took nearly a year before the company had a zero-alcohol beer that was ready to be launched. Ilaria Lodigiani, Heineken's global director for the low- and no-alcohol division, said the next challenge was convincing people to drink it.

The Dutch brewer's strategy was simple but bold: build huge distribution quickly, and then back the launch with multimedia advertising campaigns.

"Non-alcoholic beers used to be seen as the uncool version of your brand, so they didn't get a lot of marketing dollars," Ms Lodigiani admitted. But for Heineken 0.0, it recommended that countries spent up to 25 per cent of their marketing budgets on the product.

Lastly, Heineken focused more on sampling, for consumers to taste the beer. It targeted people at moments not associated with drinking, such as athletic events and lunch spots. A television ad with the slogan "Now You Can" emphasised that Heineken 0.0 could be enjoyed at any time, by showing people drinking it at the office, in the gym and in a parked car, while onlookers reacted with surprise.

The tactics seem to be working: Heineken 0.0, which was launched in 2017 in 14 markets, has been rolled out to 50 markets globally, including the US last year.

The roll-out helped Heineken achieve a 7.7 per cent increase in sales by volume last year for the group's main brand, its best growth rate in more than a decade. Heineken has about 159 products in its 0.0 range, up from 78 only two years ago, and has launched alcohol-free versions of Birra Moretti, Tiger and Sol.

"I really believe this can be something very big," said Ms Lodigiani.

headline: BA LIFTING BEER TO A BREW LEVEL

HAVING an in-flight beer just got better - with an ale made at 40,000ft.

British Airways, which is celebrating its centenary, has teamed up with craftale firm BrewDog to create a transatlantic IPA.

And with the help of cabin crew, BrewDog bosses Martin Dickie and James Watt created a world first. They started the brewing process on board one of the airline's Boeing 787 Dreamliners, while cruising at 500mph, 40,000ft above Scotland.

Cans of a bespoke IPA, named Speedbird 100 after the airline's call sign, will be available exclusively on board all long-haul and short-haul flights and in selected lounges from Wednesday.

The recipe has been brewed to work perfectly in the air and adapt to the reduction in taste and smell sensitivity at high altitudes.

The Scottish craft brewery will also open its first bar in New York in British Airways' Club lounge at JFK's Terminal 7.

headline: Why artistic TV leaves viewers in the dark
author: Carol Midgley
text:
Do you ever struggle to see the picture on your TV screen because it's too dark, or strain to hear dialogue because characters mumble? Well shut up, philistine. It's not about you. It's about "art".

Many viewers complained they couldn't see much of the action in the Game of Thrones battle scene as the lighting was so farcically dim. My husband, squinting with the TV brightness cranked up, said: "Is it just me or is this mad?" It did seem so.

But it appears we weren't appreciating the artistry. A director has explained that he wanted to "make the storytelling of the lighting evolve with the storytelling of the characters" (any clue? Me neither). Many people, he said, can't tune their TVs properly and anyway it is not always necessary to see what's going on because "it's more about the emotional impact".

Ah. Even if that means you say: "Sod this: let's watch The Chase"? I would have enjoyed The Wire more had I been able to decipher the dialogue (the writer wanted the audience to "work at it"). Ditto the BBC drama SS-GB. However, incoherence is often intentional because it is "naturalistic". If this comes at the expense of being heard, tough: style (and aiming for a Bafta) comes first.

Viewers frequently object to background music obscuring dialogue but it makes naff all difference. A reader told me he wrote to a TV channel to complain about annoying recaps after every ad break during documentaries. He was told that it was "a technique of modern commercial broadcasting" and unlikely to change. So there: pipe down and know your place.

Driven mad Ihave noticed, as a watchdog says, that creeping vegetation is obscuring some motorway signs, causing drivers to make last-ditch manoeuvres to pull off the road. But is the problem really so great that they need redrawing? You get a mile's notice of a junction on motorways and another at half a mile. Sometimes you get ten minutes' warning: "M62, 11 miles, 10 minutes" (a challenge to beat that, obviously).

Why don't people concentrate? I often see drivers wearing earphones or big headphones at the wheel.

What's that about? Don't they have a radio? They could surely play their phone music on speaker. Better would be to "redraw" those patronising "Think!" motorway messages which are more distracting than foliage: Don't drive tired; Is your car ready for winter?; Check your fuel. But nothing takes your mind off the road and messes with the mind like "Sign not in use".

Booze boors Afriend, surveying his house after a party where sat an unopened pack of Foster's lager, apologised in advance for being petty. "The person who brought that," he said, bitterly, "guzzled my Peroni and Brewdog all night."

Ah, yes, alcohol recycling. People who offload their unwanted, sub-par booze at parties then head straight for the good stuff. My friend calls them the "Bring Blossom Hill, drink Mirabeau" brigade. He did sound mean-spirited but I take his point. Another friend jokes that the same unwanted four-pack of Carling has been circulating in south London since about 2011. Like taking the last roast potato, people notice.

Pecking order Pah to the advice that we should stop kissing our pets because it risks spreading superbugs. What's the point in having a cat if you can't kiss it? I kiss both mine at least ten times a day and the dog more. One cat lifts her head regally to receive her kiss; it's one of my favourite things.

Ah, I see: the expert urges us to "avoid kissing pets on the mouth". Well, I don't do tongues, only on the head. Another no-no is allowing them "to lick your mouth and nose". Hmm, sometimes a dog gives you no choice. Where does the expert stand on dogs licking plates and lapping tea from cups, sometimes without you noticing, so you pick one up and carry on sipping? Asking for a friend.

@carolmidgley

headline: CITY PEOPLE
author: Dominic Walsh
text:
The feuds, the faces and the farcical Dominic Walsh @walshdominic

unlike rugby, you really can't wing it in this game Dan Luger, a member of England's 2003 Rugby World Cup-winning squad, is launching a Europefocused equity hedge fund called Monaco Alpha, according to Bloomberg. After retiring from rugby, he entered the world of finance, joining the Monaco-based Tavira Securities, initially in wealth management before moving into sales and trading. Monaco Alpha is part of Tavira's fund with more than $800million of assets under management. Luger, below, said a lot of hedge funds "have lost their way and become far too directional and cumbersome". A bit like some of the forwards he played with during his rugby career, no doubt.

Nick Jones joined the Marks & Spencer graduate scheme in 1995 and rose to be the chain's home director before moving to Asda in 2013. There he was in charge of food, general merchandise and George, the company's clothing brand. At Joules, he replaces Colin Porter, 57, who announced his intention to retire last month having taken over from Tom Joule, 51, the founder, in 2015. He lives in Rutland with his wife and two children.

corporate social responsibility, Brewdog, the Scottish craft brewer, has announced it's funding "a sensory garden" for nervous dogs at a Scottish SPCA animal rescue centre in Aberdeenshire. Makes a change from its usual stream of provocative (and increasingly irritating) publicity stunts involving taxidermied animals, dwarves and super-strength beers.

seeing red is not so bad David Wild, the no-nonsense chief executive of Domino's Pizza, is fighting fires on several fronts, what with disappointing international sales, a dispute with franchisees and a resultant sharp fall in store openings, all of which hit Tuesday's trading update. There is also pressure from some quarters for the 63-year-old to hang up his pizza paddle.

Yet despite everything he was in remarkably chirpy mood. "I'm just back from a holiday in the Caribbean, that must be the reason," he said. Presumably he'd gone a nice colour? "It's very embarrassing. I don't go brown, I just go bright red."

headline: Beer firm dog help
text:
A BEER firm has teamed with an animal charity to build a sensory garden for traumatised dogs.

BrewDog is working with the Scottish SPCA on the facility which aims to give abandoned or abused animals a mental and physical lift.

The space will be packed with dog-friendly elements to stimulate their senses at the rescue centre in Drumoak, Aberdeenshire.

The garden is expected to be completed in June.

Centre manager Graeme Innes said: "We are honoured BrewDog have chosen to do this with us. Adapting to kennel life is difficult for many dogs."

headline: WATCHDOG FACES PROBE OVER SAVINGS FIRM COLLAPSE
author: JAMES BURTON
text:
A FORMER High Court judge will probe the City watchdog over its failure to stop a savings scandal.

Dame Elizabeth Gloster has been asked to examine why the Financial Conduct Authority did not protect customers from collapsed firm London Capital & Finance.

LC&F went bust earlier this year owing £37m to 11,500 customers, and is facing a fraud probe. Money was funnelled to ventures such as holiday developments in Cape Verde - benefiting a group led by Simon Hume-Kendall (pictured top right with wife, Helen), the founder of LC&F's predecessor business, according to administrators.

Critics claim the disaster could have been prevented by the FCA, but it did nothing despite repeated warnings.

The Treasury has asked former High Court and appeals judge Gloster to look into the allegations, and the rules could be tightened up to stop it happening again. She has been given a year to look at what happened.

FCA chairman Charles Randell said: 'This investigation will establish what happened with LC&F and whether further changes are required.

'The FCA will ensure that [Gloster] has all the access and support she needs to conclude her work as quickly as possible.' LC&F customers were told their cash would be invested in a wide range of companies through so-called mini-bonds, offering eye-catching returns as high as 8pc.

But after the company's collapse, it emerged the funds were in fact given to just 12 businesses in a series of transactions which administrators described as 'highly suspicious'.

Mini-bonds - which are not regulated by the FCA - have grown in popularity since the financial crisis and are often used to raise cash by smaller firms which have struggled to get funding from banks. They have been issued entirely legitimately by firms from craft brewer Brewdog and Hotel Chocolat to Lancashire County Cricket Club, and often come with perks such as free beer or chocolate.

But if a business goes bust, the savers who bought the bonds can lose everything. Unlike a conventional bank account, their money is not protected by the Financial Services Compensation Scheme.

Last month, the Mail revealed the FCA knew years ago that there were problems with mini-bonds, but took no action to bring them under control.

Regulators warned in 2015 that the bonds are highly risky products where investors' money is not protected.

At the time the FCA said: 'Firms are failing to make clear that mini-bonds are investments that place investors' capital at risk, and are not deposit-based or capital-protected products.'

LC&F victims welcomed the probe but said it should have started sooner, and were concerned it might last more than a year. A spokesman for the LC&F Bondholders Facebook group said: 'There is no mention about victim compensation resulting from serious regulatory failure, and whether the investigation will determine whether the thresholds are met which would justify an ad hoc compensation scheme being set up.'

Tory MP Nicky Morgan, chairman of the Treasury Select Committee, said: 'This cannot be kicked into the long grass. The FCA, Treasury and Dame Elizabeth must think innovatively about how the investigation can report quickly.'

©aily Mail

headline: Brewery in doghouse  Brewdog  has [...]
text: Brewery in doghouse Brewdog has lost a legal battle over the name of its new hotel chain. The Scottish brewery applied to register the name Brewdog Doghouse as a trademark in the UK, but a London-based firm, Doghouse Distillery, objected. The UK Intellectual Property Office ruled in favour of the London distillery. Neither party commented.

headline: BREWDOG  CAMPAIGN IS GLASSY
author: Shaf Rasul
text:
Shaf Rasul One in a million shaf@the-sun.co.uk

BREWDOG CAMPAIGN IS GLASSY IT MAKES ME LAUGH BREWDOG is up to its usual tricks by pulling pints as well as headlinegrabbing stunts. I had a wee chuckle at its latest gimmick, called Honest To Dog.

Its Equity Punks get to choose how much they pay for a beer, and those who pay less are asked to fill out a feedback card which sees their comments printed online.

Is Brewdog wondering how many will have the brass neck? I suspect the odd few will still take the pint.

The funny bit, however, is that this Scottish firm's stunt can't work on home turf due to minimum pricing legislation.

So it's not quite as cut and dried as naming your price unless it's actually over the minimum price, and you'd have to be barking mad to pay that.

IT MAKES ME LAUGH ME CRY I WAS surprised to see a planning application for a phone mast in Pennan got the thumbs down.

You may know the coastal Aberdeenshire village - and its red phonebox - from that brilliant Scottish movie Local Hero.

Apparently, the application was refused because critics reckoned it would affect the village's charm.

Yet the 8m mast was supposed to improve phone coverage, including for emergency services.

We shouldn't spoil our beautiful landscape, but surely practicalities like getting a phone signal, for emergencies in particular, should take precedence over charm? I'd love to be a fly on the wall in Pennan to hear what locals think of the ruling.

headline: ALE CHAUVINISM! DRINKER REFUSED 'PINK BEER FOR WOMEN' WINS £,000 PAYOUT
author: IZZY FERRIS
text:
Have you heard the one about the man who was refused a 'pink' drink for women at the bar so sued a brewery for sex discrimination?

Dr Thomas Bower, 27, ordered a Pink IPA at a Brewdog pub in Cardiff, which was priced at 20 per cent less than the brewer's signature Punk IPA to highlight the gender pay gap.

Instead he was offered the more expensive drink for £ - as the £ IPA was aimed at women. He was only given the cheaper ale, satirically dubbed 'Beer for Girls', after telling staff he 'identified as a woman'. He complained about his experience in March 2018 to the craft ale brewery, which launched the drink to 'expose sexist marketing techniques', before taking the firm to a small claims court.

Court documents show Brewdog argued that, as Dr Bower claimed he identified as female, he received no different treatment and was able to purchase the Pink IPA.

But the software engineer from Cardiff won his sex discrimination case, landing £,000 in damages. Dr Bower said: '[I] felt forced to lie about my sex in order to receive the product at the lower price.'

Judge Marshall Phillips said: Dr Bower would have felt 'humiliated' and was directly discriminated against. Dr Bower donated the money to charity.

* ITV will no longer commission comedies written by all-male teams to tackle gender inequality. Commissioner Saskia Schuster said the move comes after she realised she was being sent scripts by one female writer for every five male writers.

©aily Mail

headline: Payout for man refused 'female beer'
author: Jonathan Ames
text:
A bearded man who felt forced to "selfidentify" as a woman so that he would be allowed to buy a pink bottle of beer has won £,000 from the brewery.

Thomas Bower, 27, ordered a bottle of Pink IPA at a Brewdog bar in Cardiff. He was told by the bar staff that the pink-labelled ale was for women drinkers only as it had been launched to highlight the gender pay gap.

Mr Bower was instructed to order a bottle of Punk IPA, which at £ was a pound more expensive than the pink version. When he refused, Mr Bower, a software engineer, was forced by the bar staff to say he "identified as a woman" to get the cheaper drink.

"After a bit of a back and forth," said Mr Bower, "I felt forced to identify as a female and was then able to get the drink for £. "I complained to the company about this and they said it wasn't discrimination because the price was part of a national campaign to raise awareness about the gender pay gap."

Mr Bower brought a sex discrimination claim against Brewdog brewery, based in Ellon, northeast Scotland. He has donated the £,000 damages to charities helping women and men.

District judge Marshall Phillips told Cardiff's civil justice centre: "It is clear that in this case the claimant has been directly discriminated against by the defendant because of his sex."

The judge accepted that the only way Mr Bowers could purchase the bottle was to identify as a woman and that doing so would have been humiliating.

Brewdog argued that as Mr Bower identified as female he was able to buy the pink IPA without being forced to lie.

headline: The wine critic's favourite low-alcohol beer and wine
author: Jane MacQuitty
text:
If I had to pick one big trend in the drinks business it would be the rise of lower and no-alcohol wine, beer and even spirits. Some 20 per cent of adults say they are teetotal, with many more cutting back, fuelling demand for decent substitutes. Until recently these were thin on the ground. With few exceptions, wines were baked, jammy horrors created by vacuum distillation, beers lacked vital yeasty, hoppy, thirst-quenching oomph, and spirit substitutes and mixers, with the exception of a Seedlip duo, were an over-priced waste of time. The problem is that by removing alcohol you remove some of the flavour, so drinkable alternatives need to be full of character to compensate.

Beer drinkers have been especially badly served, so say hello to the glorious Ghost Ship 0.5% from Adnams, made by reverse osmosis that filters out the alcohol, leaving the beer's flavours behind. Finer still, and bursting with hops, is BrewDog's Punk AF IPA, which has all the punchy flavour of the flagship 5.6 per cent alcohol Punk IPA but none of the alcohol. "Small beer" (up to 2.8 per cent alcohol) has also benefited from the hop and yeast-obsessed craft beer movement Cool-climate vineyards produce lower-alcohol wine naturally, but the New Zealander responsible for The Doctors' range, Dr John Forrest, has hit on a new technique. He says that by removing the leaves on his Marlborough vines the grapes contain about 60 per cent of the standard sugar content, resulting in wines with about a third less alcohol. See how successfully he has been with his Doctors' Sauvignon Blanc (see best buys, below).

Otherwise try the spinning-cone produced Natureo trio by Torres. Red wines are the most difficult to pull off, but their tasty 2017 Syrah (Waitrose, £.99), fermented and aged in French oak, has sufficient peppery, hibiscus fruit to make going alcohol-free a pleasure rather than a chore.

headline: BrewDog  scrapes the bitcoin barrel
author: Jemima Kelly
text:
Anyone for a pint of “ntergalactic liquid currency”

Remember BrewDog, the Scottish beer-for-people-who-think-they-like-beer company that's managed to raise money from over 110,000 "equity punks" (AKA ordinary and really quite un-punky retail investors) around the world?

Well, it's raising again. Or trying to, anyway. In the company's latest "Equity for Punks" crowdfunding raise -- its sixth such round -- it's aiming to raise another £m, with a "stretch goal" of £0m. The £m would bring the total amount of money BrewDog has raised via crowdfunding to an impressive £4m. (Although it should be noted that it also raised £13m from a somewhat less punky US private equity fund back in 2017.)

The offer had been open between April 6th and July 5th, per its prosectus. But on July 4th, with the £m goal not yet met, the company appears to have suddenly been overcome by an even-more-punky-than-usual urge.

Suddenly, BrewDog decided it was time to accept crypto.

Simulaneously, BrewDog announced it was launching a new beer called Cryptonite: Intergalactic Liquid Currency" -- which anyone buying with crypto would receive six cans of -- and that it was extending the fundraising period until April 2020. The extension, of course, wasn't about not meeting the fundraising goal though. Rather, it was "to celebrate this milestone" of accepting crypto.

In a press release, the company said: This is the natural next step for us as a business. James and Martin started BrewDog to be an alternative to the status quo; to challenge perceptions and revolutionise their industry. The developers, miners and users of cryptocurrency are exactly the same. Joining forces to link the old and new financial systems is a perfect fit.

Status-quo-challenging cryptocurrencies accepted by BrewDog include the three main versions of bitcoin (bitcoin, bitcoin cash, and bitcoin BSV), thinking-bro-coin Ethereum and XRP, the banker-coin once known as Ripple that's trying to replace SWIFT.

Other ways that BrewDog gets people to invest -- or to "join the revolution" -- include referral schemes, a ticket to the annual "#punkAGM", and membership to an "exclusive social network just for equity punks". There is also a "tiered benefits" system depending on your level of investment, and anyone who invests is entered into a draw to become a "BrewDog Millionaire".

So this all makes sense right? I mean, we would have thought the BrewDog-bro-to-crypto-bro venn diagram would show a significant overlap already, but there's surely no harm in trying to make that still more more significant. And crypto does, after all, have a remarkable capacity to get people to part with their cash, with no certainty on future returns. So go on, if you think you're punk enough. Artisanal coins for your artisanal beer.

oeeu eco er e. ur es, co, Italian meatballs (Serves four) YOU NEED: 500g minced beef 2 white onions - one grated, one diced 2 tbsp dried Italian herbs 4 cloves garlic, crushed 1 tsp olive oil 1 tbsp balsamic vinegar 400g chopped tomatoes 400ml vegetable stock 1 sprinkle Parmesan cheese 1 small handful basil leaves METHOD: Preheat the oven to 180C fan/200C/gas 6. Place the beef, the grated onion, 1 tbsp herbs, 2 cloves of garlic, and some salt and pepper into a mixing bowl. Stir together to combine.

Line a baking tray with baking parchment. Shape meatball mix into 12 balls, place on the lined baking tray and cook for 25 minutes. To make the sauce, heat the olive oil in a frying pan. Add the diced onion and sautéor two to three minutes, until soft.

Add two crushed garlic cloves and cook for one minute. Add the balsamic vinegar and reduce for one minute.

Add the chopped tomatoes, 1 tbsp of Italian herbs and vegetable stock. Bring to the boil, reduce the heat and simmer for ten minutes. Add the oven-cooked meatballs to the tomato sauce and cook for five minutes in the pan. Garnish with parmesan cheese and torn basil.

Can be served with gluten-free pasta.

Lime, coria ander and ginger chicken fajitas (Makes six) YOU NEED: 4 chicken breasts, cut in 1cm strips 35g root ginger, peeled and grated 4 tbsp sour cream 6 limes - grated zest and juice of 4 limes, plus 2 limes cut into wedges 2 tbsp mango chutney 2 avocados, sliced 6 tortilla wraps 1 small bunch coriander, chopped METHOD: Place chicken, grated ginger, 2 tbsp of sour cream, grated lime zest and juice into a mixing bowl. Stir well to combine.

Cover and place in the fridge to marinate for 30 minutes. Meanwhile, cut two limes into wedges. Put 2 tbsp mango chutney and rest of sour cream into two separate pots and place them on a serving platter.

Heat a griddle pan or frying pan over a high heat. Griddle the marinated chicken in batches, ensuring the chicken is thoroughly cooked. Place the cooked chicken on to a serving platter.

Just before you are ready to serve, cut the avocado into slices and place on the serving platter. Warm the tortilla wraps and serve with the sliced avocado, sour cream, lime wedges, coriander leaves and chicken.
