headline: BREWDOG 'S BITCOIN BID
text:
Brewdog is seeking to raise £m from the public, using bitcoin.

The craft brewer initially opened the offer between April 6 and July 5 and was accepting pounds. But following a lack of demand it has resorted to the controversial crypto-currency as a way of encouraging investors. The fundraising period has been extended until April 2020. It has also launched a beer called Cryptonite: Intergalactic Liquid Currency, and will give six cans to any investors paying in bitcoin.

Brewdog has already raised £7m from the public through a scheme called Equity for Punks. Participants get shares, although these are almost impossible to sell again as it is not listed.

©aily Mail

headline: Why an iPhone and an iced bun have more in common than expected
author: Philip Rodney
text:
When I saw an article last week headed "Improved Apple Turnover", I mistakenly thought that it was reporting on the convergence of two business success stories Apple, the multinational technology company headquartered in Cupertino, California, and Greggs, the largest UK bakery chain with its head office in the equally wonderful Newcastle upon Tyne.

Both are business megahits and each has just reported positive trading results. Apple unveiled an unexpected gain in sales for the third quarter of the year, despite a dip in sales of the iPhone. And while other operators are closing stores, Greggs reported a 58 per cent rise in first-half profit and is on track to open its 2,000th outlet shortly.

At first blush, there is little that connects an iPhone with an iced finger. Apple products are confidently expensive. Greggs has a strong value proposition. Last year, the average purchaser spent about £50 on an iPhone, while a meal deal at Greggs costs £.15. However, on closer examination their respective business strategies and practices have several common themes. So what are the similarities? The first is that both have reinvented themselves. Greggs has been transformed from a traditional bakery business to a modern "foodon-the-go" brand. Having captured breakfast and lunch trade, it is looking to the evening meals market, experimenting with a dinner menu and extending opening hours in some shops to 11pm. Apple, long criticised for over-dependence on the iPhone in an increasingly saturated market, has diversified, not only with its product range into wearables, home and accessories but also by growing its subscription business by 55 per cent over the past year with a predicted massive increase in 2020 when it launches AppleTV+.

Each is clear about brand and positioning. According to YouGov polling, Greggs is the most popular dining brand in Britain. It has 98 per cent recognition, but, more importantly for a growing business, it's most popular among millennials. It defies the food snobs and projects itself with a sense of humour, even baiting Piers Morgan over his attack on its vegan sausage rolls. Apple is the world's most valuable brand. Its products are unapologetically upmarket, again strongly focused on the younger demographic. There's no tech talk; the adverts don't sell the product's features, but rather the fun and creative experience of owning one.

Whether you like the product or not, there's a commitment at Greggs to quality no artificial colours, flavours, added trans fats or MSG. It adapts to demand. It engages with communities and charities and has a motivated workforce.

There's a part of their respective souls embedded in Greggs' and Apple's products. You know exactly what you're getting, but more than that, each enjoys genuine affection, fandom even, from their respective customer bases.

So how do you create the love for products as diverse as MacBooks and Mac'N'Cheese? A lot of it is common sense. This is far from a comprehensive list, but here goes: ? Customer advocacy is possibly the most powerful way of creating an emotional connection. If someone you know well and respect recommends a product or service, that's likely to have a huge impact. ? Never assume. Learn not only what your customers want, but who they are. Take all that on board. ? Get it right every time. There are 650 flights into Heathrow every day. If 99.5 per cent of them landed safely, that would still be an unsatisfactory outcome. ? Concentrate on the relationship, not merely the transaction. That means keeping in touch.

? Tell them about the company, not just the product. Customers like to be associated with success. ? Reward loyalty. Giving a better or cheaper service to a new customer can disaffect those who support you. ? Build trust be transparent. ? Keep the spark alive. Bring something new to the relationship. As Steve Jobs once said when he thought a product was getting tired: "There's no sex in them any more."

But perhaps the most important behaviour is the one proclaimed by Scotland's own Brewdog. "We are uncompromising. If we don't love it, we don't do it. Ever."

Philip Rodney is the founder of Rimalower Consulting

headline: Brewdog  'uncool' label axe
author: STUART PATTERSON
text:
SCOTS craft beer giant Brewdog axed the design for its new "punk whisky" after it was blasted by fans.

Founder James Watt asked Twitter followers for feedback on the artwork for Young Blood, left.

But it was slated as "truly terrible", "not cool", "as edgy as Songs of Praise" and "like budget beard oil".

James later deleted the post, writing: "Thanks for the feedback!

"We're going to scrap this and start again."

headline: Local knowledge: it can pay to invest in the neighbourhood
author: Hugo Cox
text:
? The guide Putting money into companies in your area can be rewarding and astute, reports Hugo Cox

Environmentalists have long been urging us to think globally but to act locally and it seems investors are following suit.

Appetite for investing in local businesses is growing, with 42 per cent of those with £00,000 or more to invest saying that they would support a local business or entrepreneur who benefits their community, according to a survey by IW Capital, an investment house.

"Investment is becoming increasingly local," says Jenny Tooth, the chief executive of the UK Business Angels Association (UKBAA), which represents roughly 15,000 "angels" highnet-worth individuals who invest directly in private companies. "Brexit is a driver. People are aware that small businesses will struggle most to get funding after we leave the EU. Property is looking weak, the recent Woodford scandal has shown how even listed holdings can be hard to sell. People are saying, 'Why not back local companies?' " she says.

Luke Davis, the chief executive of IW Capital, says: "Private investors are a hugely important source of finance for small companies that are typically turned away by banks and traditional lenders."

Becoming a local investor, however, can be challenging. Opportunities are rare, typically require deep pockets and take time and effort to research. There are three main routes.

? Crowdfunding Luke Lang, a co-founder of Crowdcube, a crowdfunding platform, says that users can opt to be notified when a business located near them launches on the site. "Outside London and the South East, the South West has the most businesses raising finance and the highest amount raised on Crowdcube."

Cornish Lithium, which has exploration rights for lithium and other "battery metals" across a large area of Cornwall, recently raised £.44 million.

When the Scottish brewer Innis and Gunn raised £.4 million on Crowdcube in 2016, 37 per cent of the 1,913 investors were from Scotland. Brewdog has raised £9 million, most recently in September 2018, with 16 per cent of the 5,623 investors based in Scotland.

Davis says: "Crowdfunding is one of the easiest ways to support small businesses, with thousands of opportunities, but can be higher risk."

? VCTs and EISs Venture Capital Trusts (VCTs) and Enterprise Investment Schemes (EISs) offer investment in small companies and are available to sophisticated investors with large portfolios, typically requiring investment of at least £,000.

VCTs are tax-efficient listed funds that invest in early-stage unquoted companies; investors can claim tax relief of 30 per cent on subscriptions to new share issues, which is repayable if the shares are sold within five years. VCTs can offer tax-free dividends and capital gains.

An EIS provides tax relief of up to 62 per cent (in the case of losses) for investments into eligible companies. Advisers such as Seneca or Parkwalk offer EIS funds, which are portfolios of shares in companies that have been chosen by the adviser, which often sits on the companies' boards.

Investment in an EIS-eligible company provides 30 per cent income tax relief (which must be repayed if you sell the stake within three years) and any gains are free from capital gains tax. If losses are made on EIS shares, loss relief can be claimed against either an income tax or a capital gains tax liability. After two years EIS shares qualify for business relief, which means they are exempt from inheritance tax.

Local options include Mercia EIS and Seneca EIS and VCT, which typically invest in companies in the North and the Midlands and have minimum investments of £5,000 (£,000 for Seneca VCT). Par Equity EIS typically invests in technology businesses from Scotland and Northern Ireland and has a minimum investment of £0,000. Parkwalk EIS offers three funds that collect EIS investments associated with UK universities. "The companies come from sectors including AI, machine learning and medical tech," says Moray Wright of Parkwalk.

Alex Davies from Wealth Club, an investment platform for experienced investors, says that choosing a local business can offer benefits. "For many, EIS investment is a leap into the unknown. Therefore, investing in a business that is local can add some comfort. You can walk past and see if there is much activity, you can listen out for local gossip."

? Angel investing Angels typically invest between £0,000 and £0,000 in private companies for three to eight years, taking an active role in advising the company. They will often be part of a syndicate, and many favour EIS-eligible companies because of the tax benefits.

"Syndicates share opportunities. Try to invest alongside people you know well," Tooth says. Hunting in a pack is more efficient: you bring a diverse set of skills from your professional backgrounds. The UKBAA has regional angel hubs in Leeds, Cambridge, Belfast, Manchester, Bristol and London.

Crowdfunding is one of the easiest ways to support small businesses

headline: £M P*SS-UP IN A BREWERY
author: STUART PATTERSON
text:
BOOZY AGM LOSS SHOCK

Beer firm sinks fortune into fan parties

BEER firm Brewdog has lost £million on organising booze-ups for fans, bosses admitted yesterday.

They revealed the bill for putting on wild AGM parties for shareholders had not been properly totted up for nine years.

But rather than rein in costs, the company is set to splash out on making their "Annual General Mayhem" shindigs even bigger.

James Watt, co-founder of the Scots craft beer giants, said they'd now worked out their nine events up till now had lost around £.9million.

He said: "The 2019 AGM cost us £25,000 to put on and we lost £40,000.

"For the first time it was over two days. But even on previous ones we lost around £00,000 a year."

Previously, James thought the bill for each AGM was about £0,000. But he added: "We weren't properly costing the event before.

"It now includes all our staff costs, accommodation, travel and other expenses."

Each of Brewdog's 120,000 "equity punk" backers can apply for two AGM tickets.

April's event in Aberdeen attracted 13,000 revellers.

They were entertained over the weekend by seven bands while paying for a choice of 111 beers at 15 bars.

And other AGMs in Ohio, US, and Berlin, Germany, will set the company back about £00,000.

Brewdog started in Fraserburgh, Aberdeenshire, in 2007 by James and Martin Dickie uses the bashes to reward those who've bought shares to fund expansion. The firm is now valued at £.8billion.

Scottish Sun business expert Shaf Rasul said: "They are building something arguably more important than profit real loyalty."

stuart.patterson@thesun.co.uk

headline: Can I book a timetable for one?
author: MATT BENDORIS
text:
PAIR'S NEW BUS TOUR SERVES UP MEALS ON WHEELS

ht-e YOU wait all day for your lunch on Scotland's new restaurantcum-bus service then three courses arrive at once.

he he in That's what happened when The Scottish Sun jumped on board the Bustourant, which has to be the ultimate in meals on wheels.

le ur Passengers are taken for a spin around Edinburgh's landmarks while dining on the best produce our country has to offer.

ef It's the brainchild of Glasgow chef Martin Wilson, 40, and his Polish partner Aga Napierala, 46, below, who invited the country's No1 newspaper for afternoon tea.

But there are some obvious hazards as we trundle around the capital's potholed and cobbled streets.

Aga warns: "Always keep your drinks in the cup holders to avoid any unwanted spillages there are a lot of bumps on the roads."

I took my seat on the top deck of the ex-Lothian Buses double decker which has been converted into a stylish dining area with a state-ofthe-art kitchen downstairs. And it turns out the Bustourant is just the ticket for special occasions.

Delighted Sarah Starling, 29, from Paisley, was celebrating her engagement to John Campbell from Houston that's Houston, Texas.

And she decided to take in the sights for one last time before she embarks on a new life in the States with her construction boss hubby.

John says: "I've only been on two buses in my life and I don't remember them being like this."

Finance worker Sarah jokes: "I've told him most Scots buses smell of pee, especially the late night ones.

"But this one is absolutely pristine.

It's beautiful. You could live in it."

The bus goes past Holyrood Palace and the Scottish Parliament towards Arthur's Seat then spins to head up the Royal Mile. But the bus is now a tourist attraction in its own right.

We see tourists taking pictures of its giant Highland coo and piper artwork by street artist Rogue One.

But now it's time for lunch and what a lunch.

Aga serves up three tiers of afternoon tea on slate and wooden trays. She says: "It's actually a great work-out serving food on a moving bus. I am now developing muscles I didn't know I had."

I'll need some exercise myself after devouring the row of sarnies, including Lanark blue cheese with pears, watercress and walnuts on sour dough plus egg and Stornoway black pudding. There is also salmon cured in Brewdog Elvis Juice IPA along with Ayrshire ham and wholegrain mustard, coronation chicken and haggis balls all equally delicious.

Then there's a scone and jam and a selection of mini Scottish treats including tablet, fudge, snowball and coconut square.

Preparing such delicate and delicious dishes on the move is all in a day's work for Martin, who was a chef on the Lord of the Glens cruise ship for five years.

He explains: "Lord of the Glens is a flat-bottom boat, which was perfect for canals. But when out on choppy seas, we had to cook while swaying from side to side. So bumping around streets is no problem after facing a Force 9 storm. "I got the idea of turning a bus into a restaurant while working at the Fringe last year. I thought it would be great tourist attraction."

Obviously there are major differences between a bus and a boat. Martin says: "For starters, the bus doesn't have a fuel gauge. So you have to work out how much it will use from the routes you cover.

"It costs £50 to fill up but we're looking to offset our carbon use with a Canadian tree planting firm. You also can't lock the doors, which means you have to a find a safe place to keep it parked up at night.

The orange colour scheme inside is based on the old Glasgow buses Martin used to take to school on Glasgow's south side.

But this is no nostalgia trip, as the couple sank their life savings into the project.

Aga says: "It's never been done before in Scotland so we had to work out everything for ourselves. Such as designing holders for hot tea and a place to put the milk."

The Bustourant will be rolling into Glasgow from September 20 to 22, with plans to tour all major Scots cities. Afternoon tea costs a reasonable £5 per person for the hour-and-a-half journey.

While evening tea, which involves a drive across the scenic Queensferry Crossing, is £0 each for a six-course meal with a main course of braised haunch of venison.

Driver Neil Malcolm, of Musselburgh, East Lothian, insists this is his dream job.

The former Lothian Buses worker says: "Everybody wants to be on this service, whereas normally it's just commuters going to their work or coming home.

"On the Bustourant, people get on with a smile and get off with a smile. I've just got to make sure that I dodge those potholes when they're serving."

Just one last question before my stop where's the little boy's room? Martin beams: "I think everyone recalls with horror the toilets on board the coaches from Scotland to London, where you have to reverse in because they are so small, and the smell would knock you out. So I made sure our loo was nothing like that and had the wow factor."

It certainly has. But here's my top tip for fellow male diners it's probably best to sit down for a No1 to avoid any unwanted spillages. l For more info, see bustourant.com matt.bendoris@the-sun.co.uk

headline: Curve’ cloudy crowdfunding
author: Jamie Powell
text:
How to raise £m without providing financial data.

Curve is a banking app that allows you to collate all of your bank cards onto one smart card. Think a wallet which is not a wallet, but is also kept in your wallet.

Since its launch in April 2018, the fintech product has proved popular, with the company citing over 500,000 users to date, with the hope of reaching one million by the year end.

In aid of its grand plans for bank account unification, Curve raised £3m in Series B funding from names such as Gauss Ventures, Oxford Capital and Connect Ventures in mid July. The valuation —£59m —ut it firmly in the top tier of the most valuable UK fintechs, alongside companies such as Monzo, Starling Bank and TransferWise.

So it was surprising that in August, Curve decided to return to the capital markets. But this time, it wasn’ institutional investors committing cash. But retail punters.

In an echo of Monzo’ crowdfunding last year, Curve announced it was seeking £m from investors via crowdfunding platform Crowdcube. The valuation was the same as investors in the Series B round received.

The marketing rowdfunding went swimmingly. Within 42 minutes of the campaign opening on September 3, Curve managed to raise four times as much as it hoped —es, £m —ccording to Finextra. The round eventually closed with £m committed from 9,591 investors, the company told us.

The story may just sound to you like one of a buzzy product with ardent followers, but the Curve crowdfunding does feel exceptional for one particular reason: disclosure.

Curve’ investment deck for the raise was pretty opaque. The 31 page document, which was shared with FT Alphaville, did not contain one set of financial metrics relating to the company. Indeed, the “inancials”age of the document consisted of a title page, and one slide outlining how it proposed to make money:

There was no discussion of the sort of metrics an investor might want to know, such as customer churn, cash flow, margins or management’ preferred key performance indicators.

There was, however, widely optimistic charts such as this:

And relatively uninformative ones such as this:

(We took a quick look at the American Express figure in the final graph. And there isn’ a UK average monthly spend number in the credit card businesses 2016 annual report. We asked urve where they got the figure from, and it said it used Amex’ average card spend outside of the USA of $13,073 per annum. Which, we think you’l agree, is not quite the same thing.)

We weren’ the only ones to notice a lack of any meaningful financial data. Some of the comments on the Crowdcube page, now inaccessible, voiced similar concerns.

For instance, here’ user balmf with some rather pertinent points:

Curve did a reply to a few of the users who asked about the lack of financials, but the responses were pretty lacklustre:

So we asked Curve why there was no financial disclosures, and it told us:

Financial Regulations in the UK require companies to produce a prospectus where they are raising more than € Million from retail investors. As Curve was below that threshold it was not required to produce a prospectus.

That’ fair enough, after all, why tell anyone more than they need to know? But then again, you’ perhaps expect some level of financial disclosure to guide investors on how the commercial side of the business is performing, or might perform.

Curve told us that investors could look at its ompanies House page or more detail. Unfortunately, the 2018 accounts contains little meaningful financial data, bar a balance sheet which is now 10 months out of date. We do note, however, that its profit and loss reserves doubled last financial year to negative £0.6m which suggested back then at least, profitability was still a long way off.

Curve likes to compare itself to the £bn Monzo, if this particular slide is anything to go by:

But Monzo, for all our questions about its business model, is extremely transparent about its commercials. For instance this year, it took the step of publishing a full annual report, even though it had no real need to.

So perhaps Curve should take a leaf of Monzo’ open book? But then again, as one person on Curve’ crowdfunding page put it, “iven the that crowd investment is nearly 400 per cent over-committed in less than 2 hours nobody seems to care ;-)”

Well, at least for now they don’.

BrewDog scrapes the bitcoin barrel - FT Alphaville

Investors don’ like your company? Try crowdfunding - FT Alphaville

headline: Work harder and feel better by bringing a dog to the office
author: Marc Horne
text:
Workers who are allowed to bring dogs into the office are happier and more productive, an academic has claimed.

In recent years companies including Google, Nestlé and Brewdog, the Aberdeenshire-based beer company, have welcomed pets into the workplace.

Holly Patrick, a lecturer in human resource management at Edinburgh Napier University, claims that the dogs, their owners and their bosses all benefit. "A number of studies have shown that having dogs in the workplace increases the interactions between people which can result in lower levels of stress for employees and higher levels of creativity because people are talking to each other more," she said.

"It also allows people to be able to have dogs. Many rescue shelters won't allow you to adopt a dog if you work full-time unless you can bring the dog with you to work."

Writing on The Conversation, an academic website, she said: "Bringing your dog to work may be seen by employees as part of the reward package offered by their firm, which is not easily replicated by competitors.

"It really helps to build that psychological contract between employers and employees."

Ms Patrick predicted canine companions will become increasingly common in offices, but suggested companies that want to make dogs welcome should consult staff first, draw up strict rules and hold trial days before changing their policies.

Marie-Josénders-Slegers, who studied the relationship between humans and animals at the Dutch Open University, said: "It's definitely good for the work atmosphere to have a dog in the office. An animal at work makes people more motivated they like their work more and they experience less stress."

Nestlé, the multinational food and drink company, recently allowed staff to bring dogs into its York offices.

As well as allowing dogs into its offices and bars, Brewdog offers paid leave to staff with a new puppy or rescue dog.

headline: Stuart McAra
text:
Digital effects producer who was at the forefront of developing computer-generated animation in the film industry

As a boy in Edinburgh Stuart McAra loved trips to the cinema with his father and decided that he wanted to work in films. He went on to play a significant role in computer special effects and captured the imagination of a generation of young filmgoers by illuminating the Great Hall in Harry Potter and the Philosopher's Stone (2001) with dozens of floating candles.

The only child of two civil servants, Stuart Bannerman McAra was born in 1964 and educated at Stewart's Melville and Flora Stevenson primary schools and Broughton High. He was passionate about films and television from an early age, especially the Gerry Anderson programmes Thunderbirds and Captain Scarlet.

He was not particularly academic but got a school magazine up and running. After discovering a video camera in a storeroom at Broughton, he made several silent shorts. There was a brief period selling computers and working as a stagehand with Theatre Workshop in Stockbridge.

A film-making course at Bourneand mouth & Poole College persuaded him that he did not want the stress of being a producer or director. After leaving college, he became a runner at the Computer Film Company (CFC) and worked on basic digital visual effects, such as removing aircraft vapour trails electricity pylons from scenes in period dramas. He was an additional compositor on Braveheart (1995).

McAra excelled at networking. He persuaded CFC to allow him to open an office in California. Jeremy Hall, who worked with McAra there, said: "CFC were the first to build a complete digital pipeline. He then went to LA and was at the forefront of digital work [there]."

Returning to London, he worked with Cinesite Europe and was visual or digital effects producer on Mission: Impossible 2 (2000), Lara Croft: Tomb Raider (2001) and the early Harry Potter films. In the first instalment real candles were suspended on tungsten wires which would be edited out by the digital team above the Hogwarts pupils. The candles burnt through the wires and fell on to the tables below, so the team added them digitally instead.

"The first Harry Potter was instrumental in creating the industry in London," Mr Hall said. "He worked for all the primary companies and was pretty much at the forefront of it all."

McAra particularly enjoyed his time in Mexico on The Legend of Zorro (2005), helping its star, Antonio Banderas, to sort out teething problems with his own computer and ordering ten litres of mescal for the crew at the end of the shoot. He got the order wrong and 100 litres arrived instead.

He worked for several other companies before moving to Singapore after George Lucas's Industrial Light & Magic (ILM) began operations there. He was visual effects producer there on Transformers: Dark of the Moon (2011), Rango (2011), Avengers Assemble (2012) and Star Trek into Darkness (2013).

Away from film McAra was an avid collector of comics and books and a keen cook. A brief marriage ended in divorce. He did not have children and is survived by his partner Natalie Stevens, who also works in the film industry, and by his parents, Robin and Cookie.

After Disney took over ILM, McAra returned to Scotland, where he worked on converting an old school building into a home in Speyside whisky country. He referred to it as the Ranch. He drew enthusiastic crowds at the Moray Science Festival demonstrating how digital effects work, recording children against a green screen and enabling them to see themselves fly through the air in a quidditch match.

Openings for film digital effects work were more limited in Speyside. McAra worked for a while for the Scottish craft beer producer Brewdog, packing cases and driving a forklift truck, hoping briefly that brewing might provide him with a second career. It did not pan out that way and a call from New Zealand led to his relocation to the animation studio Pukeko Pictures and work on a new version of Thunderbirds, this time using digital animation, not puppets.

McAra worked on three series of Thunderbirds are Go, from 2015. He had prostate cancer diagnosed at the end of that year but continued working. In an episode in the second season in 2017 a character was created in his likeness, and McAra voiced it himself.

Stuart McAra, film digital effects producer, was born on December 28, 1964. He died of cancer on September 15, 2019, aged 54

headline: Venture capital and private equity investment is not a savers' panacea
author: Patrick Hosking
text:
When a minister claims that everyone could painlessly enlarge their retirement nest-egg without saving a penny more or taking any extra risk, it seems silly not to listen. More than 18 million people could be missing out, it is suggested, because of a prejudice against a particular asset class.

'' Simon Clarke, the youthful exchequer secretary to the Treasury, last week unveiled a governmentbacked report arguing that modernday pension funds should be investing in venture capital and private equity. Simply by allotting 5 per cent of every defined-contribution pension fund to unlisted shares, total returns eventually would be 7 per cent to 12 per cent higher, the report said. Every pension-saver should have at least a bit of their pot invested in the whizzy, innovative companies that are just not available in the listed world.

The timing of the report was unfortunate, of course. Even as Mr Clarke was enthusing about the rewards from investing in unlisted companies, Britain's most famous and once most admired fund manager was displaying how it can be a bit trickier than it looks. Neil Woodford's woes have been largely down to straying from listed, liquid stocks to unlisted, illiquid ones.

First, there's the liquidity mismatch problem which led to a humiliating ban on redemptions to prevent fire sales of underlying assets. Hundreds of thousands of end-investors remain trapped in his Equity Income Fund.

Then there's the valuation credibility problem. Because there is no market in these unlisted shares, there's no certainty of what they are really worth. Mr Woodford has had to write down the value of four companies in the Patient Capital Investment Trust by £4 million.

And there's the plain picking-ofduff-companies issue. It may be too early to accuse Mr Woodford of that. He needs only one out-and-out winner to offset his dozens of losers. But the evidence is not reassuring. His backing of Industrial Heat, a company developing cold-fusion energy, which scientists think is as likely as a perpetual motion machine, is a case in point. All in all, it is a brave moment for government to suggest that fund managers should be more like Mr Woodford.

The report, by Oliver Wyman, the consultancy, and the British Business Bank, based its conclusion on the fact that unlisted shares have outperformed listed ones for several decades now. Since 1970, the asset class has delivered an 18 per cent per year return after charges, they claim. Listed shares have produced 11 per cent per year, as reflected in the MSCI World equity index.

While traditional defined-benefit schemes sometimes do invest in venture capital and private equity, defined-contribution schemes do not. This is partly because of the 0.75 per cent charges cap on total fees; private equity typically charges a hefty 2 per cent plus 20 per cent of the profits above a hurdle rate. But also because of the liquidity contradiction demonstrated by Mr Woodford.

The report says that even a modest allocation into the asset class would also bring diversification benefits, as well as addressing another difficulty: that the number of listed companies in the West is shrinking. And, as it points out, it is surely perverse that workers in their twenties and thirties who are enthusiastic customers of unlisted unicorns such as Brewdog, Deliveroo, Revolut and the like, aren't able to invest in them, too. The 40-year time horizon of pension savers is perfect for companies that might take years to reach their potential. So what's not to like? Well, that 18 per cent return looks optimistic even allowing for inflation. If sustained over decades, £,000 growing at that rate turns into £50,000 over 40 years, 12 times higher than the amount generated by the 11 per cent of listed shares. If it were that fabulously simple, there would be no pensions crisis.

It is a mighty stretch to assume the next 40 years will replicate the last 40. The huge returns are partly down to leverage and that has worked so well because of decades of falling interest rates and plentiful credit. Inflation helped, too. There may not be those tailwinds in the future.

Moreover, venture capital and private equity has been a minority sport. It is much easier to make strong returns when the supply of capital is relatively modest. If pension funds started investing in the asset class, it would be hit by a tsunami of new money. Defined-contribution schemes are expected to hit £ trillion within ten years. A 5 per cent allocation would require them to find a home for £0 billion. Private equity professionals are finding it hard to find credible investments for the money they already have.

For these bold projections to work, you have to assume that there is a vast supply of entrepreneurs with credible and viable ideas who can't get funding at all. That's a delusion.

According to the Wellcome Trust, one of the smartest private equity investors with a £ billion portfolio, there is an "unprecedented amount of uncalled funds waiting to be called", which it warns will dampen future returns. Another experienced player, 3i, has been ultra-cautious.

The report's authors argue that unlisted shares will continue to outperform listed ones because of an "illiquidity premium" the extra return that investors demand for locking up their capital for five or ten years. The industry also has better access to high-growth companies in technology and life sciences, which tend to stay private for longer and therefore aren't accessible by listed investors.

That may be so. Definedcontribution schemes could develop structures that address the fees and liquidity problems. Some of them may even strike lucky.

But for the vast majority, that 18 per cent projection is misleading fantasy.

Patrick Hosking is Financial Editor of The Times

headline: Brewers tap into huge Chinese tourism boost
author: Marc Horne
text:
The international marketing was carefully targeted to portray Scotland as a country of rugged castles and breathtaking scenery but Chinese visitors, it seems, would rather go to the pub.

Visit Scotland has published a study of Chinese tourists after the number of visitors rocketed in recent years. An estimated 200,000 went through the gates of Edinburgh Castle last year compared with fewer than 1,000 two decades ago.

Sampling Scottish delicacies, including whisky, craft beer, haggis, seafood, gin and ale, was the top attraction, ahead of historical sites and shopping.

Hazel Sellar, Visit Scotland's market manager for China, said that visitors were clamouring to try quality food and drink in surroundings that were quintessentially Scottish.

"Chinese visitors want to live like a local and part of that is going down to your local pub or village inn," she said. "It is part of immersing themselves in local culture. Chinese people are becoming increasingly interested in craft beer, while young women, in particular, are very keen to try the variety of whiskies on offer."

Sales of heavily hopped beer soared in China, which traditionally favours lager, after President Xi was filmed drinking a pint of Greene King IPA at a bar during a state visit to the UK in 2015. It led to the Plough at Cadsden, a 16th-century Buckinghamshire pub, being snapped up by Chinese investors.

Since then a host of craft breweries, including Boxing Cat in Shanghai and Panda Brew and Great Leap Brewing in Beijing, have opened and thrived.

"The Xi phenomenon really opened peoples' eyes to IPA", Michael Jordan, a US-born brewer with Boxing Cat, said.

Brewdog, the Aberdeenshire-based beer company, will open a bar in Shanghai this month and plans to build a brewery to capitalise on the Chinese craft beer boom.

Ms Sellar also revealed that the national dish was a huge hit with the Chinese. "They absolutely love to try haggis, neeps and tatties, the traditional Scottish feast," she said. "They say it is similar to some highly spiced lamb dishes that you can get in China.

"Trying Scottish seafood and shellfish is hugely popular, while eating fish and chips is seen as a classic must-do. China is not traditionally a big cheeseloving nation but increasingly people are keen to try all the different varieties that we offer here."

Chinese visits to Scotland have risen by more than 300 per cent in a decade and almost 90 per cent of tourists were keen to return, citing the scenery, relaxing atmosphere and friendliness. This year Visit Scotland hosted a series of "China Ready" workshops which urged bed and breakfast owners to learn Mandarin and provide chopsticks to capitalise on high-spending visitors.

headline: Burger's a flex vex
text:
BEER firm BrewDog has been mocked for creating a half-vegan, half-meat burger that would "appeal to nobody".

The hybrid is made with beef and plant-based Beyond Meat in green matcha tea buns and topped with vegan cheese.

Bosses said they wanted to create something for flexitarians those cutting down on eating meat without stopping entirely.

But one Twitter user called it "the Theresa May deal of burgers". Another added: "Is it April 1st?"

headline: Heard on the Street Overheard
text:
[Financial Analysis and Commentary]

Who said bonds were boring? Scottish beer brand BrewDog on Tuesday launched a fixed-income offer with a promise to pay investors a 6% yield -- half in cash and half in beer.

Since its foundation in 2007, BrewDog has combined a maverick disregard for high finance with breakneck growth -- no easy combination. It says it is Europe's largest craft brewer and isn't shy about ambitions for world domination.

The company has a history of unconventional stock issues via its own website. Under the marketing banner "equity for punks" it has raised roughly GBP 74 million (about $95 million) over six funding rounds from 125,579 fans who get discounts and access to events in addition to a stake in the company.

Still, BrewDog hasn't been able to raise the kind of money required to build state-of-the-art breweries -- including one opened last year in Columbus, Ohio -- from beer drinkers alone.

In 2017, the company tapped San Francisco private-equity firm TSG Consumer for GBP 213 million.

One problem with BrewDog's punk equity is the lack of a credible aftermarket in the stock. At least the bond sidesteps this problem by offering liquid returns -- in more ways than one.

headline: Festive food that's free from fuss
text:
Mrs Crunch

CATER FOR ALL DIETS WITH THESE BUYS

THERE'S no need to miss out this Christmas - there's a record number of "free-from" foods for those who have to watch what they eat.

From turkey stuffing to chocolate Santas, those wanting to avoid gluten, wheat or dairy have their Christmas wish answered.

Start your shopping with these tasty buys.

Always check packaging for a full list of ingredients.

TEAM UP WITH TURKEY: Morrisons Free From sage & onion stuffing mix is £.70 (gluten, wheat and milk-free)

MAGIC NUMBER: Morrisons Free From chocolate Advent calendar £ (gluten and milk-free)

CRUNCH TIME: No need to miss out on the nibbles with Tesco Free From turkey & stuffing tortilla chips, 150g, £ (wheat and gluten-free).

FESTIVE TIPPLE: Baileys Almande dairy-free almond drink, is down from £0 to £6 at Morrisons

ALE AND HEART Y: Sainsbury's has BrewDog Vagabond gluten-free pale ale, 330ml, for £.80

FESTIVE FLYER: Morrisons Free From robin madeira cake is £.50 (gluten and milk-free)

LET THEM EAT CAKE: Sainsbury's Deliciously Free From iced fruit cake bar, 300g, is £.50 (gluten and milk-free)

LITTLE STARS: Morrisons Free From chocolate brownie star cookies are £.50 (gluten and milk-free)

FESTIVE FLAVOURS: The Co-op's cranberry & orange tiffin bites, five-pack, is £.50 (gluten-free)

BEER FOR ALL: Waitrose has Peroni Nastro Azzurro glutenfree, £.50 for 4 x 330ml

CONTINENTAL CHRISTMAS: Arden & Amici gluten-free panettone, 100g, is £.50 at Waitrose

PERFECT FOR PARTIES: Co-op's Indian-style sharing selection (jackfruit samosas and crispy cauliflower popcorn) and Asian-style sharing selection (vegetable filo candles and crispy vegetable twists) are all gluten-free and suitable for vegans, £.25 or buy two for £ GO CRACKERS: Morrisons Free From savoury cracker selection, 250g, is £.50 (gluten and milk-free)

NICE SLICE: With sultanas, raisins, cherries and dates, Waitrose glutenfree Christmas cake is £

PERFECT PUDS: Made with fruits and cider, Tesco's freefrom Christmas pudding, 400g, is £.75 (free from wheat, milk, nuts and gluten)

It's fine to buy marked-down meat. Either use it that day or freeze it for later.

Gluten-free Christmas cornflake wreath

Makes 15

YOU NEED: 150g gluten-free corn flakes 3 tsp green food colouring 100g mini marshmallows 45g butter 1 bag Skittles

METHOD: Melt the butter in a pan over a low heat and once it has melted, add the marshmallows.

Stir continuously until the marshmallows have fully melted. Next, add in the gel food colouring. Pour out the green marshmallow mix over the cornflakes in a large bowl, making sure you cover the cornflakes evenly.

Line a tray with baking paper and evenly portion out the cornflake mix.

Shape the portions into wreath shapes and top them with red Skittles.

Leave them to set in the fridge before serving.

Recipe: morrisons.com

Gluten-free sausage rolls

Makes 12

YOU NEED: Genius gluten-free puff pastry 6 gluten-free sausages 1 tbsp parsley, finely chopped 1 tsp sage, finely chopped Freshly ground black pepper Grating of nutmeg 1 egg, beaten

METHOD: Preheat over to 200C/180C fan/gas 6. Roll the pastry out to a rectangle approximately 45x30cm.

Take the sausage meat out of the skins and mix with the parsley and sage. Season with freshly ground black pepper and a grating of nutmeg and spread over the pastry.

Brush all edges with a little beaten egg.

Roll the pastry from both ends towards the middle, brushing with the egg as you go.

Cut in to 12 even slices and place on a non-stick baking sheet.

Brush with the egg and bake for 15 minutes. Leave to cool a little and serve.

Recipe: Geniusglutenfree.com

headline: BREWDOG  HOTELS UNLEASHED
author: LISA MINOT
text:
CRAFT beer company BrewDog is launching a city centre hotel chain with a beer fridge in every shower.

Rooms at the BrewDog Kennels start at £5 a night and you can even take your pet pooch with you for free. The first of its UK hotels will open in Union Street in Aberdeen, above the BrewDog Castlegate bar.

Inside the six boutique rooms are showers with a beer fridge inside, stocked with BrewDog cans. Also in the rooms are French-press coffee, vinyl players and guitars. Your pets don't have to miss out - each room has dog beds and treats for your pooch.

And the hotel lets you book from your room some of the excursions BrewDog offers in Aberdeen, from brewery tours to "Beer School". Reservations are now open with availability from November 20. More BrewDog Kennels are planned for Paris, Manchester, Berlin and London. The prototype, in Columbus, Ohio, opened last year.

headline: Got an eagle eye on wing seats Willie?
author: COLAN LAMONT
text:
By COLAN LAMONT WILLIE Rennie swoops in for one of his famous election campaign photo ops yesterday as he gets to grips with flying a golden eagle called Stanley.

The Scottish Lib Dem leader also fed peckish Indian eagle-owl Sage when he turned up at Elite Falconry in Kirkcaldy where he pushed the party's environmental message.

Mr Rennie, whose previous campaign exploits included being interviewed on camera while two pigs had sex behind him, said his interaction with the birds was "wonderful, if slightly terrifying".

Meanwhile, Nicola Sturgeon had her hands full too as she was given a couple of smokies on a visit to meet voters and activists in Arbroath where the Nats hope to topple Tory Kirstene Hair.

The First Minister then headed north to visit beer firm BrewDog in Ellon, Aberdeenshire.

She later tweeted: "Popped into the local and increasingly global company BrewDog as part of my visit to Ellon to support SNP candidate for Gordon, Richard Thomson. Unfortunately my pintpulling skills are a wee bit rusty."

headline: Drink delivery service plans expansion
author: Greig Cameron
text:
A drinks ordering service is hoping to raise £50,000 to allow it to expand into more locations.

Drinkly, which is backed by James Watt, co-founder of Brewdog, the craft brewer, and Stuart Middleton, chief commercial officer at the online travel group Skyscanner, promises to deliver chilled drinks within an hour of customers making an order. It takes a commission and generates further revenue by providing data to drink suppliers on consumer habits.

Founded in 2016, Drinkly has fulfilled more than 14,000 orders for customers in Edinburgh, Glasgow and London. It has also agreed partnerships with brands such as Heineken, Asahi and Innis & Gunn.

The business, which is based in Edinburgh, hopes to raise additional capital through the Seedrs crowdfunding platform in a move that values the business at close to £.5 million. If the crowdfunding were to be successful Drinkly would be able to add more independent retailers to its offerings and expand further in the cities it serves. The company would also like to increase its workforce of five.

John Robertson, 39, the founder and chief executive of Drinkly, pointed out that the global e-commerce drinks market was forecast to be worth £9.6 billion by 2022.

He said: "We have a trusted, tried and tested platform, the team and the tech leaving us well positioned to capitalise on this opportunity and scale up quickly. We plan to take Drinkly to 150 locations by December 2020, becoming the market leader in drinks-on-demand delivery in the UK."

Mr Robertson said that by 2022 he hoped to have 500 retailers in the Britain and overseas using the digital platform.

Mr Watt, 37, who set up Brewdog in 2007, said: "I backed Drinkly because they are doing things differently, using technology to innovate and putting the customer at the forefront of everything they do. Anything that brings the customer closer to fresher Brewdog the better."

headline: Beer ad banned
text:
A BEER advert featuring foul language and put up outside a primary school has been banned.

BrewDog's posters for a non-alcohol beer had the slogan "Sober as a motherfu" with the end of the offensive word disappearing off the side.

But the ad prompted 26 complaints 16 as it was used near the school.

The Advertising Standards Authority said the advert was inappropriate.

headline: A footnote for stars' cobbler
author: patrick kidd
text:
TMS diary@thetimes.co.uk | @timesdiary

Terry de Havilland, the so-called "rock 'n' roll cobbler" who designed shoes for the likes of David Bowie and Cher, had a flamboyant funeral in Brighton after his death last month at 81. His coffin was painted in lurid colours with comic-book words such as ZAP and POW on the side and arrived in a leopardprint hearse. His godson recalled an exhibition of his shoes at Olympia where a podiatrist complained to a TV crew that his high heels would damage women's feet. At this, De Havilland picked up a shoe, turned its sole, with "F*** Me" written on it, to the camera, and declared: "I don't make hiking boots."

Dominic Cummings is a fan of the Nanny State, or at least of a nonalcoholic beer that goes under that name, made by BrewDog. A TMS mole tells me that Boris Johnson's senior adviser necks bottles of the stuff. It seems he has matured from the days when he swigged vodka from the bottle at Russian airports.

hurt feelings Bernie Marsden, guitarist with the rock group Whitesnake, writes in his autobiography about getting drunk in the Hilton with John Hurt. The actor matched the group glass for glass as they sang round the piano into the early hours. Come morning, the musicians went for a recording, leaving Hurt slumped over the bar. The last they heard was Hurt shouting in immaculate theatrical tones: "That's it, you bastards. Go without me. You only have to mime but I will have to act later this day."

Four Royal Marines who plan to row across the Pacific next year tweeted Sir Matthew Pinsent to ask if he had any tips and were delighted to get an immediate reply. "Yes," the four-times Olympic champion, above, replied. "1. Row ten hard strokes. 2. Turn round and ask 'Is that Australia?' If no, go to instruction 1." Simple really.

red flag flying It's the time of year when a bearded man in red gives presents to those who have been good. Alas, Jeremy Corbyn can provide no such treats for his team. Even the lumps of coal have gone Tory. Yet spirits remained high, or at least were being drunk, in the Labour leader's office on Wednesday. At 7pm, a loud and defiant chorus of The Red Flag was heard coming from their Christmas party. Not very festive, though it does at least share a tune with the German carol O Tannenbaum.

My comparison yesterday of Boris Johnson and that 18th-century rogue John Wilkes reminded Steve Pound, the former Labour MP, of Wilkes's cutting reply to the fourth Earl of Sandwich, when he said that he did not know whether the former would die on the gallows or of the pox. "That depends," Wilkes replied, "on whether I embrace your lordship's principles or your mistress."

woman's movement In uncertain times, steps must be taken to protect those we love. Jane Garvey, the Radio 4 Woman's Hour presenter, says she has moved her suffragette tree decoration a few branches so that she can be closer to a guardsman bauble for protection. "Who knows what might happen?" Garvey says. Given the reputation of guardsmen, what indeed?

headline: Merry Xmas to all & hoppy new beers...
author: ARAN BRAZIL
text:
AS the year comes to a close, we naturally tend to look back and reminisce on what was, so next week's column will be my annual Golden Pint awards for the best of 2019. But what about 2020? It's tough to predict what trends, styles and flavours will be hits I'm looking at you suspiciously, Milkshake IPAs and which 'big deals' will fail to live up to their hype.

Here are a couple of beer trends that just may ride a wave of success in the next year, and decade, beyond..

LOW OR NON-ALCOHOLIC (N/A) BEERS For evidence that the lower alcohol movement is already under way, simple look to big beer: major players Heineken, Carlsberg, and Guinness are all pushing their n/a offerings as a sensible alternative to their full-abv siblings.

Still, craft breweries are stepping up too. Brewdog was an early pioneer with its Nanny State n/a ale and recently added Punk AF (alcohol free) to its booze-free line-up.

And Wicklow Wolf is adding two limited edition brews Limelight and Starlight to a stable that already includes Moonlight, its punchy, hoppy n/a ale.

HARD SELTZER As I covered in these pages last month, hard seltzer has taken over the US market and is about to touch down in Ireland.

Alcoholic 'flavoured sparkling water' could be a blessing for craft brewers as it needs no additional specialty equipment and is easier to produce than beer.

While some balk at the thought, it is worth remembering it's gluten-free, low-sugar, low-carb and low-calorie compared to most beers and will appeal to the health conscious.

BARREL BOOM As more distilleries open nationwide, it's only natural that the number of used (or 'exhausted') whiskey barrels will grow.

While they may no longer be suitable for whiskey, they are perfect for beer, so expect a boom in stouts and heavy ales.

Many brewers may also take the opportunity to experiment with lesser-known styles. Saisons, sours, fruit and gruit (herb-and spice-infused) beers are well suited to barrelaging which increases complexity and depth of flavour.

CHAIN PUB BEERS Like them or loathe them, Wetherspoons, the British chain pub, is here to stay.

In 2020 the brand opens its first hotel on Dublin's Camden Street, as well as a couple of new pubs in Waterford, Galway and another new Dublin pub for Grand Canal Dock.

While the chain may be seen as cheap and cheerful (or cheap and nasty, depending on your perspective), it does offer an impressive selection of cask ales, a style completely neglected in most Irish pubs.

headline: Restaurants try new recipe to fund expansion in tough market
author: Alice Hancock
text:
Chains offer free food and high returns to persuade customers to back financing

When Mexican food chain Chilango offered customers the chance to invest in its “urrito bonds”ast year, it promised annual returns of 8 per cent and free food for life.

It was part of a new wave of early stage restaurant businesses that have found creative —nd sometimes risky —ays to finance their expansion at a time when cost pressures and competition in the sector are at all time highs.

But after a period of rapid expansion, last week Chilango filed for a company voluntary arrangement — restructuring process that would allow it to reduce its debts —fter its auditors refused to sign off its accounts.

Its backers now face an unappetising choice: lose 90 per cent of their original investment or take equity in the lossmaking business.

The developments provide a stark lesson for investors that have piled into their favourite restaurant chains —ot just to enjoy the food but in the hope of sharing in the financial returns.

Since 2016, when annual restaurant openings in London peaked, the number of UK food and beverage businesses raising money on equity crowdfunding website Seedrs has almost tripled, reaching 40 this year.

Kirsty Grant, Seedrs’hief investment officer, said crowdfunding was reaching the “oint of mass adoption”

But the growing popularity from private investors has coincided with a difficult period for the restaurant sector following a string of high-profile closures including TV chef Jamie Oliver’ Italian chain. Many other groups —yron and PizzaExpress, for example —re restructuring.

Banks have become more reluctant to lend money to small businesses, and private equity groups that funded a casual dining boom in the mid 2010s have cooled on the industry.

“he headwinds that the sector is facing are only increasing over time so some of the opportunities we might have looked at a couple of years ago and got excited about we probably wouldn’ now,’’aid Tom Makey, an associate director at Gresham House, which has owned the Vietnamese chain Pho since 2012.

Robin Rowland, a partner at private equity firm Trispan and former chief executive of Yo! Sushi, said casual dining no longer produced desirable returns: “n the good old days you could make 20-25 per cent ebitda conversion. The new reality is somewhere between 15-20 because upward cost pressures have not been met by price increases.”

The lack of easy finance has prompted aspiring restaurateurs to turn to angel investors —ealthy individuals that back start-ups —r the general public.

Other chains that have successfully crowdfunded include the vegan brand Vurger, the coffee chain Grind and M Restaurants, which has since merged with steakhouse Gaucho.

The brewery-come-bar chain BrewDog is also part-owned by more than 120,000 investors who have provided £4m over various “quity for punks”unding rounds. In the latest, it offered investors the opportunity to buy shares in cryptocurrency.

Eve Bugler, a former Nando’ executive, raised £.1m from high-net-worth individuals and industry experts for her fledgling kebab chain BabaBoom but had pushback from generalist asset managers and private equity “ecause of the high profile struggles of Byron, Jamie’ and PizzaExpress”

To finance new sites, Ms Bugler crowdfunded this year. She has raised more than £70,000 on Seedrs, offering investors a share of 11.5 per cent of the company. The current round gives BabaBoom, which has two London sites, a valuation of more than £m.

Ms Bugler said there were benefits to crowdfunding beyond raising cash: “s well as getting the investment we need, we also have a range of brand ambassadors, which is really valuable.”

But however enticing the free food, no platform can guarantee that a business is sound.

Sean Cannon began a Kickstarter campaign last year to grow his artisan meat and butchery class business before he received a £80,000 private offer. He said that while he liked having lots of people involved in his business and the promotion that crowdfunding brought, he worried that many retail investors did not understand the risks.

“ worry it will be the PPI [payment protection insurance] of the next 10 years,”e said.

He added that professional investment was still available for those in the food sector with “ good story and a scalable model”

Many in private equity echo this, saying they would consider concepts that are different from the UK’ many Italian or burger chains. Niche businesses such as the wine-tasting bar chain Vagabond, which is backed by private equity house Imbiba, were more likely to receive funds, they added.

Lizzie Ryan, a director at Imbiba, said start-up businesses looking for longer-term finance should be wary of crowdfunding: “t raises expectations very high so it makes it harder for them to raise private equity money after that,”he warned.

headline: Restaurants try fresh recipe to fund growth
author: ALICE HANCOCK
text:
Travel & leisure. Sector downturn

Chains offer free food and high returns to persuade customers to back financing

When Mexican food chain Chilango offered customers the chance to invest in its "burrito bonds" last year, it promised annual returns of 8 per cent and free food for life.

It was part of a new wave of early stage restaurant businesses that have found creative —nd sometimes risky —ays to finance their expansion when cost pressures and competition in the sector are at all-time highs.

But after a period of rapid expansion, this month Chilango filed for a company voluntary arrangement — restructuring process that would allow it to reduce its debts —fter its auditors refused to sign off its accounts.

Its backers now face an unappetising choice: lose 90 per cent of their original investment or take equity in the lossmaking business.

The developments provide a stark lesson for investors that have piled into their favourite restaurant chains —ot just to enjoy the food but in the hope of sharing in the financial returns.

Since 2016, when annual restaurant openings in London peaked, the number of UK food and beverage businesses raising money on equity crowdfunding website Seedrs has almost tripled, reaching 40 this year.

Kirsty Grant, Seedrs' chief investment officer, said crowdfunding was reaching the "point of mass adoption".

But the growing popularity from private investors has coincided with a difficult period for the restaurant sector following a string of high-profile closures including TV chef Jamie Oliver's Italian chain. Many other groups —yron and PizzaExpress, for example —re restructuring.

Banks have become more reluctant to lend money to small businesses, and private equity groups that funded a casual dining boom in the mid 2010s have cooled on the industry.

"The headwinds that the sector is facing are only increasing over time so some of the opportunities we might have looked at a couple of years ago and got excited about we probably wouldn't now,'' said Tom Makey, an associate director at Gresham House, which has owned the Vietnamese chain Pho since 2012. Robin Rowland, a partner at private equity firm TriSpan and former chief executive of YO! Sushi, said casual dining no longer produced desirable returns: "In the good old days you could make 20-25 per cent ebitda (earnings before interest, tax, depreciation and amortisation) conversion. The new reality is somewhere between 15-20 because upward cost pressures have not been met by price increases."

The lack of easy finance has prompted aspiring restaurateurs to turn to angel investors —ealthy individuals who back start-ups —r the general public. Other chains that have successfully crowdfunded include the vegan brand Vurger and the coffee chain Grind.

The brewery-come-bar chain BrewDog is also part-owned by more than 120,000 investors who have provided £4m over various "equity for punks" funding rounds. In the latest, it offered investors the opportunity to buy shares in cryptocurrency.

Eve Bugler, a former Nando's executive, raised £.1m from high-net-worth individuals and industry experts for her fledgling kebab chain BabaBoom but had pushback from generalist asset managers and private equity "because of the high-profile struggles of Byron, Jamie's and PizzaExpress".

To finance new sites, Ms Bugler crowdfunded this year. She has raised more than £70,000 on Seedrs, offering investors a share of 11.5 per cent of the company. The current round gives BabaBoom, which has two London sites, a valuation of more than £m.

Ms Bugler said there were benefits to crowdfunding beyond raising cash: "As well as getting the investment we need, we also have a range of brand ambassadors, which is really valuable." But however enticing the free food, no platform can guarantee that a business is sound.

Sean Cannon began a Kickstarter campaign last year to grow his artisan meat and butchery class business before he received a £80,000 private offer. He said that while he liked having lots of people involved in his business and the promotion that crowdfunding brought, he worried that many retail investors did not understand the risks.

"I worry it will be the PPI [payment protection insurance] of the next 10 years," he said. He added that professional investment was still available for those in the food sector with "a good story and a scalable model".

Many in private equity echo this, saying they would consider concepts that were different from the UK's many Italian or burger chains. Niche businesses such as the wine-tasting bar chain Vagabond, which is backed by Imbiba, the private equity house, were more likely to receive funds, they added.

Lizzie Ryan, a director at Imbiba, said start-up businesses looking for longerterm finance should be wary of crowdfunding: "It raises expectations very high so it makes it harder for them to raise private equity money after that," she warned.

'In the good old days you could make 20-25 per cent ebitda conversion. The new reality is 15-20'

headline: My Round
author: ARAN BRAZIL
text:
Announcing the Golden Pint Awards IT'S that time of year again, so here is part one of my annual Golden Pint Awards, my personal review of all things beer. Best Irish Brewery: DOT brew After releasing a refreshed core range of approachable styles in 440ml cans, DOT went back to focus on what it's best known for wood.

Many favourites returned but it was the flurry of experimental barrel-aged beers that impressed, including a standout 3.2% ABV table beer 'Windfall' a style not often treated to the luxury of barrel ageing. Best New Irish Brewery: The Format It was an incredibly close call this year with the equally impressive Dublinbased Lineman Brewery stepping up with some fantastic debut releases.

Its Astral Grains foreign stout quickly gained a loyal fan base, but The Format just pipped it. Their imperial red ale called 'Things Done Changed' was particularly reminiscent of old-style, hoppy American ambers. Pub/Bar of the Year: The Big Romance, Dublin 1 A cosy and stylish northside haunt that's driven by some of the best vinyl tunes in the city and powered by an eclectic array of rotational taps.

Don't be surprised to find exotic, often bonkers, Scandi ales by the glass next to pints of Guinness.

Best New Pub/Bar: Brewdog, Dublin It may be big, it may have its own in-house brewery, it may have a large range of local craft beers and may be located in an area devoid of craft options but the reason it's my new pub of the year is that, after being announced three years ago, it finally arrived.

The Scottish craft behemoth Brewdog opened the doors to Outpost: Dublin just three weeks ago. The jury is still out as to whether it will have the same impact on the beer landscape it once had but at least it is open.

Independent Retailer of the Year: Craft Central For a second year running this tiny Dublin city centre off-license has continued to impress even increasing its beer range by fitting bigger fridges.

Local and international breweries are well represented with new and limited edition releases often on the shelves upon release.

Online Retailer of the Year: Craftcentral.ie The new online portal for the independent retailer of the year. Constantly updated with new arrivals, deals and sales but, best of all, it offers flatrate, island-wide, next-day delivery.

headline: LET'S DO LUNCH
author: Jane Hamilton
text:
Sunemployment

SAY cheers to lunchbreaks once again.

Research reveals 77 per cent of office workers admit to spending their entire break at their desk, but 57 per cent would welcome a return to the lunchtime pint as a means of switching off.

To help, brewer BrewDog is encouraging Brits to disconnect from work over lunchtime and enjoy an alcohol-free beer for themselves and a colleague. BrewDog co-founder James Watt said: "Most people shovel down a sandwich at their desk. That's not a break, and it's not good for productivity or our mental and physical health.

"This initiative isn't about encouraging people to be unprofessional, it's about reclaiming lunchtime."

headline: BREWDOG 'S BREAKFAST
author: STUART PATTERSON
text:
Elite investor ale sent out as Xmas freebie

nt ee BUNGLING beer giant Brewdog accidentally gave away an exclusive ale reserved for big investors as a Secret Santa freebie.

Shareholders who pumped almost £,000 each into the business were supposed to get limited edition Brettanarchist as a reward. But bottles of the gold-dust tipple unexpectedly arrived at the homes of fans who'd signed up for a festive giveaway. It later et emerged warehouse workers at the firm's base in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, mixed up similar looking boxes of the drinks.

dl Miffed shareholder Gary Joyce said: "Shambles is a massive understatement." Peter Jm Sinclair said: "What a mess." "Overlord" investors who stumped up at least £50 in 2018 were promised exclusive sour beers.

Those putting in £,450 got access to even more rare brews. A new batch of the elite ale, left, should be mailed out this month. Brewdog's Michael Kenyon apologised for the "monumental error". He added: "If you received one of the beers as your Secret Santa, congratulations."

stuart.patterson@the-sun.co.uk

headline: Brewers in doghouse  Brewdog  have [...]
text: Brewers in doghouse Brewdog have lost a legal battle over the name of its new hotel chain. The brewer, based in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, wanted to register "Brewdog Doghouse" as a trademark in the UK. However, the Doghouse Distillery in London objected and the Intellectual Property Office agreed. The new hotels will now be called Brewdog Kennels.

headline: CITY PEOPLE
author: Dominic Walsh
text:
Business big shot

NAME SIMON WALKER AGE 66 POSITION CHAIRMANELECT, TRADE REMEDIES AUTH

Leading the Institute of Directors, Simon Walker banged the drum for British industry as one of its most senior lobbyists (Callum Jones writes). Now he will oversee its protection as chairman of a key post-Brexit government agency.

Mr Walker has filled an array of high-ranking posts in public affairs, from the Queen's communications secretary to corporate affairs director at British Airways during a turbulent spell for the company.

However, Mr Walker, who worked in Downing Street under John Major, has never drifted too far from Whitehall. He has spent recent years on the board of the Department for International Trade.

While at the Institute of Directors, from 2011 to 2016, Mr Walker did not shy away from bashing businesses he felt had fallen short of the mark. Barclays and Sports Direct were among those who faced criticism.

In a statement yesterday, Mr Walker said that he was "very pleased" to be taking up the chairmanship of the Trade Remedies Authority next month. "Maintaining the interests of consumers while ensuring that producers are not handicapped by dumping and other unfair practices is going to be a vital balance," he said.

JIGSAW FALLING INTO PLACE FOR MAN WHO WOULD BE CHIEF

A senior management reshuffle at the world's biggest brewer has set analysts musing on the issue of who might succeed the chief executive. Carlos Brito, who for some reason prefers to go by his surname, does not appear to be leaving AB Inbev any time soon, but the retirement of Felipe Dutra as chief financial officer after almost 30 years with the company could offer a pointer. Dutra's role as head of technology is going to David Almeida in addition to his job as chief strategy officer. Edward Mundy, drinks analyst at Jefferies, sees the changes as "potential succession planning, with Almeida possibly being groomed for chief executive".

MILLENNIAL ANGST

A survey arrives from Virgin Holidays suggesting that social media is raising the bar for the holiday expectations of young Brits. Seven out of ten millennials want to experience ever more lavish holidays, citing must-haves such as business-class flights, luxury spas and sandy beaches with crystal-clear waters. Tell me again how millennials are committed to tackling climate change and saving the planet.

MEN HOLD ALL THE ANSWERS

Management Today tells us that without radical changes it could take nearly 100 years to close the gender gap, but "we cannot —nd will not —ait that long". Its solution? An awards do that's only for men. That's right; the business publication is inviting nominations of men who are "agents of change" on behalf of women folk. You can strike a blow for the sisterhood by entering the non-sexist man in your life by paying the big-hearted folks at MT £50 a pop.

STOCKING UP

It's often said that where there's muck, there's brass. Brewdog, left, the Scottish brewer and bar owner, is offering shares to anyone who returns 50 used beer cans as part of an environmentally friendly manifesto. Hopefully the equity isn't a waste.
