headline: CITY PEOPLE
author: CALLUM JONES
text:
Business big shot

NAME LISA GORDON AGE 53 POSITION CHAIRWOMAN, CENKOS SECURITIES

Lisa Gordon is the second woman to be appointed to chair a UK-listed stockbroker, after Lorna Tilbian at Dowgate Capital (Louisa Clarence-Smith writes). The City high-flier, who has more than 25 years' boardroom experience, is to join Cenkos Securities, the small-cap broker.

Ms Gordon was the youngest woman to be appointed a director of a publicly quoted company when, aged 28 in 1994, she was talent-spotted by Chris Wright, co-founder of Chrysalis Group, the media company.

After completing an English Literature degree, she became an analyst at Greig Middleton, the stockbroker, and County Natwest Securities. Her next stop —or ten years —as Chrysalis, where she was director of corporate development, before she joined several founder-led companies, including Future, another media business. She is a non-executive director of Alpha FX Group, the Aim-listed foreign exchange specialist, and Magic Light Pictures, a film and television production company. She is also non-executive chairwoman of Albert Technologies, an artificial intelligence company.

Outside corporate governance, Ms Gordon enjoys time with her family and taking early morning hikes with her girlfriends.

NEW CHANCELLOR COMES A CROPPER AT THE FIRST FENCE

Last week's upheaval at the Treasury led to chatter that next month's budget would be delayed, pushing back Rishi Sunak's first hurdle as chancellor. Such speculation delighted those who had hated the clash with the Cheltenham Festival. Alas, it appears the new man at No11 is no fan of the horses. Confirming March 11 yesterday, he said his statement would prioritise "levelling up and unleashing the country's potential" —resumably beyond the realms of the Queen Mother Champion Chase at 3.30pm.

A BIT OF SELF-PROMOTION

While a rumoured super-sized business ministry, gobbling up trade, investment and myriad other Whitehall functions, failed to materialise during the reshuffle, Alok Sharma clearly thinks highly of his new department. The new business secretary has wasted no time in updating his biography. After seven months as international development secretary, his website declares he has been "again promoted" to his latest role.

OUT OF PROPORTION

The recent floods have knocked sales of high-margin fishing products at Angling Direct "disproportionally", according to a stock market update. The fact this isn't a word seems to have slipped through the net of Yellow Jersey, the retailer's communication supremos.

TWO FOR THE PRICE OF ONE

Brewdog revealed this month that punters who return 50 cans to Brewdog will be rewarded with one share in the Scottish brewer, but the recycling doesn't stop there. The company announced the scheme all over again yesterday, with the brewer's co-founder James Watt, left, saying that fans of its tinned beer would be doubly rewarded for their good judgment, "when you open it and again when you hand it back to us".

headline: EMPTY PROMISE FROM  BREWDOG
author: tracey boles
text:
BREWDOG fans can exchange their empties for shares under a new initiative from the brewer.

In exchange for 50 empty BrewDog cans, they will be given a voucher for a share in the Scottish brewery.

Each share is worth £5 on paper, the firm says. The offer is restricted to one per person.

The initiative, which is called Cans For Equity, will be live in all European BrewDog bars from 25 February.

It will run until the firm's latest round of crowdfunding closes in April - and is designed to encourage "more sustainable business practices through incentivised recycling".

headline: BREW UP A BARGAIN
author: Captain CRUNCH
text:
Captain CRUNCH

INTERESTED in getting shares in a company but not sure where to start? Independent craft brewer BrewDog has made the process easier by giving away equity in return for empty aluminium BrewDog beer cans.

Anyone can trade in 50 empty Brew-Dog cans in any European BrewDog bar - including its Dublin Outpost - to become an Equity Punk shareholder.

BrewDog co-founder James Watt said: "The BrewDog beer in your fridge can now reward you twice - once when you open it, and again when you hand it back to us."

The Scottish company recently opened its Outpost in Dublin's Grand Canal Docks, featuring a microbrewery, Olympic-sized shuffleboards, outside terrace and heated roof garden.

For more info see brewdog.com.

headline: More than a million jobs at risk in months
author: Philip Aldrick, Ashley Armstrong Greig Cameron
text:
Job losses could top a million in a matter of months as businesses lay off staff and the economy grinds to a halt, analysts and businesses have warned.

The impact on employment from lockdowns and social distancing was likely to be greater and swifter than during the financial crisis, they said. Hotels, restaurants, museums, theatres, events, airlines and trains are on the front line as people stay at home.

Between 2007 and 2011, in the last economic crisis, 1.1 million people lost their jobs. Since then the growth of zero-hour contracts and self-employment has made it easier than ever for businesses to shed staff.

Economists estimate the hit to GDP in the three months to June alone will be worse than the recession of 2008 and 2009 as a whole, when output fell 6 per cent over 15 months.

In Scotland, hundreds of thousands of jobs are at risk with trade organisations pleading for additional government support to avert the looming economic crisis.

Dwindling domestic demand in sectors such as hospitality and leisure is being compounded by a drop in exports as other countries move into lockdown.

The Society of Independent Brewers said yesterday many pubs would run out of cash within a fortnight. Restaurants, gyms, cinemas and retailers are also facing difficult conditions. Food and drink suppliers will soon start to suffer as customers at home and abroad begin to cancel or reduce orders. About 750,000 Scots are employed in leisure, hospitality, retail, wholesale, sports, the arts and other recreation services.

James Withers, the chief executive of Scotland Food and Drink, said the industries he represented employed 120,000 people. "The UK government must now deliver a level of emergency business support, the scale of which we haven't seen before," he said. "That is essential to protect thousands of jobs and ensure we still have Scotland's and the UK's critical business infrastructure after the outbreak has passed." Mr Withers said exports had "fallen off a cliff" and the disruption to airlines was causing further problems in getting goods to market.

PB Devco, which runs nine venues in Aberdeen, said it was closing its sites "until further notice".

Paddy Crerar, the chief executive of Crerar Hotels, said the company had cancelled building projects and refurbishments to try to preserve cash. However, he said that unless state aid was forthcoming the business would be forced into "the difficult and damaging reality of significant staff cuts".

James Watt, the co-founder of Brewdog, said on Twitter that its income had fallen 70 per cent almost overnight. "Every business now faces a grim battle to both survive and protect livelihoods," he said.

Yesterday data showed employment in Scotland rose by 31,000 to 2.7 million for the November to January period. Alf Young, page 39

headline: BREWER TO PRODUCE FREE HAND SANITISER
text:
BEER company Brewdog has announced it will make hand sanitiser at its Scottish distillery in a bid to meet a shortage.

It will give away the product - called Brewgel Punk Sanitiser, in a nod to its Punk IPA beer - for free to people who need it.

Brewdog tweeted a picture of the hand sanitiser alongside the comment: 'Say hello to Punk Sanitiser.

'We are determined to do everything we can to try and help as many people as possible stay safe. It's time to keep it clean.'

James Watt, Brewdog's co-founder and boss, added: 'Hand sanitiser is selling out everywhere. So we have started using our distillery to make Punk Sanitiser.

'We want to do all we can to help everyone get through this difficult time.'

It comes after the company cancelled PunkAGM, its beer and music festival on April 11.

©aily Mail

headline: Gin makers switch to hand sanitiser
author: David Byers
text:
British gin distilleries have started using the drink to create emergency supplies of alcohol-based hand sanitiser —ith the help of the taxman.

Consumers bought 225 per cent more hand sanitisers last month than in February last year, according to Kantar, the market research company.

A growing number of alcohol producers are applying for licences to become hand sanitiser-makers.

HMRC yesterday announced that it would streamline the approval process to become a manufacturer so that permission could be granted within 5 days rather than 40. Getting official approval to manufacture so-called "denatured" alcohol is not essential, but it means approved distilleries will face a zero tax bill on the ingredients.

The British Honey Company, a small Buckinghamshire distillery that makes gin and vodka out of honey, has begun using spare capacity at its distillery in Worminghall to produce hand sanitiser ingredients.

James Watt, the founder of BrewDog, the hipster beer mannot ufacturer, tweeted that it would be "using our distillery to make Punk Sanitiser" and would be giving the product away.

Hand sanitiser ingredients vary, but it is usually made up of extremely high strength alcohol, mixed with tert-Butyl alcohol substances and denatonium benzoate, a chemical compound. Together it is known as denatured alcohol.

LVMH, which owns Louis Vuitton, has switched production at all of its cosmetics and perfume factories to start producing hand sanitiser instead.

headline: Don't panic: here are my seven lessons for surviving the crisis
author: Philip Rodney
text:
Anticipating long-term confinement to barracks as a result of the coronavirus, last Sunday my 89-year-old mother hopped on a bus to the Buchanan Galleries in Glasgow and bought herself a piano. (Disappointingly she arranged to have it delivered, rather than carrying it home herself.) She reckoned that being stuck indoors for three months without social contact would drive her nuts. This was her strategy for confronting Covid-19.

We all need strategies for dealing with the unprecedented challenges we are experiencing. There is no consensus as to how long they will continue for. I've seen estimates of three months, until August and a year and a half. The truth is that nobody knows.

Of course there is a natural initial reaction to feel sorry for ourselves because of the personal inconveniences each of us will suffer. But as everybody's favourite football manager, Jügen Klopp, said in relation to the suspension of the Premier League, where Liverpool have a 25-point lead but still may not be crowned champions: "This really isn't important at all." We have to think constructively about the bigger picture. How do we support those who are vulnerable both from a health and a financial perspective? The way in which we deal with the medical consequences is for the experts to determine. But like my mum, the business community needs strategies for coping with the issue over the next few months.

Usually before writing this column, I thumb through books or go online looking for the wisdom of others. The truth is that there are no analogies for the current circumstances. We need therefore to extrapolate from other situations. For what it's worth, let me try to set out some suggested guidelines based on past experiences.

1. Dealing with crises needs leadership, not just management. Technical support is, of course, important. But someone has to determine the direction of travel, inspire the internal team and give confidence to external stakeholders.

2. As in a fire, one needs to follow the escape route calmly. John Wooden, the famous UCLA basketball coach said: "Be quick, but don't hurry." There is a danger when you act in haste rather than taking stock of the situation. Andrew Bailey, the new governor of the Bank of England, has implored businesses to consider the support available before letting go of staff.

3. Survive. Every other strategic objective temporarily has to take a back seat. Triage what the essentials are. Work out what can wait. Don't abandon these projects, but put them on the shelf. Then concentrate your resources on the principal aim.

4. Pull together a crisis team.

They need to have a wide range of skills, some of which might not be available in the business. For example, in the present situation, I would definitely have someone with a medical qualification who could properly understand and advise on the health data. But they also need to have the right temperament people who think logically and are prepared to make the right and often difficult calls rather than court short-term popularity.

5. Get your communications right, internally and externally. In a fastmoving situation, you need to communicate early and often. Credibility is of key importance. That requires you to be open and honest. Your team expects you to be in control.

6. Can you repurpose your business to address the changed needs? That requires lateral thinking. As an example the Scottish brewery Brewdog, which obviously understands alcohol-based products, has created Brewgel, its punk hand sanitiser.

7. Consider how you can contain costs. Truth is most businesses do have some flab in their processes. For example, statistics suggest that up to half of materials in the building industry are wasted, as is one third of food produced. Apparently only one in nine leads converts to a sale. Efficiencies can be created in all businesses which will benefit you in the longer term.

But much more important than these geeky pieces of advice, I would refer you to the words of Arthur Dent in The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy: "Don't Panic. It's the first helpful or intelligible thing anybody's said to me all day."

Philip Rodney is a consultant, columnist and non-executive director

headline: Firms rallying round those who need aid
author: Shaf Rasul
text:
IN the week since my last column, life as we know it has changed beyond measure.

Only seven days ago, many were still unconvinced by the danger that the coronavirus posed —ot just to our health, but businesses, economy and actual way of life.

The messages coming from Boris Johnson, below, and the experts were all relatively lowkey, all about herd immunity, keeping calm and carrying on, with some vague advice on maybe not going to the pub.

Now, we are living in a completely different world and this is only the beginning.

Tens of thousands of hospitality workers were looking like they were going to lose their jobs as their industry fell off a cliff.

Thanks to the Chancellor's unprecedented intervention on Friday, businesses may be able to minimise some of the damage.

Notwithstanding this, many firms across the board are going to be in serious trouble and some will be lucky to still be here once we are over the worst of this pandemic.

Make no mistake, our governments will need to act —nd act decisively to take control of this nightmare situation as quickly as possible.

What has been heartening, though, is seeing how some businesses and the people who run them have responded to the crisis —ncluding last week's Scottish Sun Bug Buster Heroes Jawad and Asiya Javed, right.

Hand sanitiser instead of gin

The husband and wife shopkeeping team from Stenhousemuir have already given away up to £,000 in aid packages to OAPS and parents of young babies.

And others are stepping up to the plate.

With the schools closed now, children who are normally provided a free breakfast will be missing out, but the Prestoungrange Gothenburg pub in Prestonpans, East Lothian, has filled the void by promising to feed every child that is brought to their door.

Similarly, Glasgow's 1051 GWR are giving away soups, pizza and pasta to anyone who needs it.

The restaurant have set no limits on what can be taken away, with the intention people use it to feed the elderly, vulnerable or isolated, as well as children and essential staff such as NHS workers, of which there is sure to be plenty at the Gartnavel Hospital next door. Responding to a global shortage of hand sanitiser, and I'm sure a rapid downturn in their own output, some smart Scottish drinks companies have pivoted to start making a different kind of alcohol-based product.

Following requests from caregivers in Dundee, Verdant Spirits have now started making their own hand sanitiser instead of gin and are giving it away free to local care homes.

Beer company Brewdog quickly followed their lead, switching from Punk IPAs to Punk Sanitiser and are also pledging to give them away to those most in need.

Glasgow-based PR company Electric Shores normally work in the music and club industry.

But while there are no shows to promote, they have come up with a brilliant plan to try to get iPads and other devices into the hands of the elderly in care homes and the like, so they can video chat with their families while in enforced isolation.

It's a wonderful idea and one I know the Scottish care authorities are keen to get behind.

My favourite has to be the great bunch of lads over at Partick Thistle Football Club —r as they've renamed themselves, Partick Thistle Family Club.

With no games on the horizon (possibly not a bad thing for the poor Jags fans at the moment), the club are calling all their elderly and vulnerable fans and offering help with shopping, collecting prescriptions and more. Even better, it's the actual players who are getting stuck into this worthy task too, not just the regular staff.

These are just a small example of the way Scottish businesses are reacting to the worst crisis the country has seen in generations —nd it's heartening to see.

Obviously the main reason is that people are helping each other, that goes without saying.

But, without being cynical, these businesses are doing themselves no harm in showing the public who they really are and how they have responded when needed.

Consumers have long memories, so when all this is over and done with, we'll all know exactly who was there for us when we needed them —nd who wasn't.

headline: INEOS TO BUILD HAND SANITIZER PLANT IN 10 DAYS
author: MATT OLIVER AND FRANCESCA WASHTELL
text:
CHEMICALS giant Ineos has joined the battle against the coronavirus with a new hand sanitizer factory.

The British firm will help address shortages of sanitizer by building a plant near Middlesbrough in just ten days, which will then pump out 1m bottles per month.

Its move comes as manufacturers and businesses overhaul production and divert resources to tackle shortages of cleaning products and equipment such as ventilators in hospitals.

Other firms turning their hands to producing sanitizer include Scottish beer company Brewdog and the British Honey Company, which have both repurposed their plants.

Manufacturers have also answered the Government's call to produce emergency ventilators for the NHS, with car makers, engineers and medical equipment firms aiming to crank out 5,000 of the devices in the next fortnight.

Airbus, GKN, Siemens, Smiths Group, McLaren and Penlon are among those helping with the effort.

And Burnley-based Lancashire Textiles, which usually makes duvets, pillows and mattresses, is among firms that have switched some production to making face masks.

Foreign firms to have done this include fashion brands Prada, Gucci and Zara.

Professor Stephen Roper, of Warwick Business School, said the changes taking place in many sectors because of the pandemic are reminiscent of the Second World War, when manufacturers were ordered by the Government to switch production to planes and tanks. He added: 'In many cases, what we are seeing is firms adapting as part of survival strategies.'

Ineos said it would provide its sanitizer to hospitals for free. It is Europe's largest producer of the key ingredients needed - isopropyl alcohol and ethanol - at factories in Grangemouth near Edinburgh, and northern Germany.

The company said it expects to build a similar sanitizer factory in Germany after the UK one is completed.

It will produce the cleaning gel for personal use as well and supply it to schools, workplaces, pharmacies and supermarkets.

Sir Jim Ratcliffe (pictured), Ineos's billionaire founder, said: 'Ineos is a company with enormous resources and manufacturing skills.

'If we can find other ways to help in the coronavirus battle, we are absolutely committed to playing our part.'

Mark Swift, a spokesman for industry group Make UK, said firms were also looking at plans to produce hospital beds and oxygen generators.

©aily Mail

headline: BUSINESS FINDS ITS HEART IN FIGHT AGAINST COVID-19
author: FRANCESCA WASHTELL AND MATT OLIVER
text:
BUSINESSES are 'rediscovering their social conscience' by rallying to offer support and to create products to battle the coronavirus pandemic.

Companies have sprung into action at a rate only seen in wartime since cases of the deadly virus began to surge in the UK.

Beer company Brewdog has changed its Scottish distillery to manufacture hand sanitiser, along with the recently listed British Honey Company.

Chemicals group Ineos went a step further, pledging to build a factory in ten days near Middlesbrough that will churn out 1m bottles of the cleaning gel per month. It will provide the sanitiser to the NHS for free, but will also make it available to schools, pharmacies and supermarkets.

And firms that ordinarily build cars and aerospace equipment are designing ventilators desperately needed by the NHS to stop hospitals being overwhelmed. Aerospace group Meggitt, sports car maker McLaren and nuclear submarine maintainer Babcock International are among the companies chipping in.

Science and pharmaceuticals groups have put their expertise to use by designing diagnostic tests and providing research services. A division of biopharmaceutical group Novacyt that is based in Southampton designed one of the first diagnostic tests for the disease, which has already been given approval by the US Food and Drug Administration. And another group listed on AIM, Ergomed, has been providing research services at a hospital in Bergamo, the epicentre of the shocking outbreak in Italy.

Even companies that can't change their production lines or provide scientific resources have made efforts to help emergency workers or vulnerable people wherever they can.

Baker Greggs gave free hot drinks to NHS and other emergency services workers before its stores were shuttered as part of the lockdown.

Oil major BP has been providing free petrol and diesel to emergency services vehicles.

Cosmetics company Lush handed out soaps from stores to women's refuges and food banks, and is arranging for gifts to be given to NHS workers.

©aily Mail

headline: News; Front Page Staff who miss out on holiday can carry it over for two years
author: Steven Swinford
text:
Workers who are unable to take their annual holiday entitlement because of the coronavirus crisis will be able to carry it over into the next two years.

Employees will be given the right to postpone their 28 days of annual leave, including bank holidays, if it is "not reasonably practicable" for them to use it this year because of the pandemic.

NHS workers, supermarket staff, police and delivery drivers are likely to be significant beneficiaries.

Without the change in regulations, which was made yesterday, it was feared that workers would effectively have been penalised for helping the nation get through the crisis. Their employers would also have faced fines if they failed to ensure staff took their statutory entitlement in any one year.

Most workers are entitled to 28 days leave, including bank holidays, every year, which cannot be carried over. People normally lose their holiday allowance if they do not take it.

The new regulations will allow up to four weeks of unused leave to be carried forward. The government said the changes would ensure that all employers affected by Covid-19 had flexibility at a time when granting annual holidays could leave them short-staffed in key industries such as food and healthcare.

Alok Sharma, the business secretary, said: "Whether it is in our hospitals, or our supermarkets, people are working around the clock to help our country deal with the coronavirus pandemic.

"Today's changes will mean these valued employees do not lose out on the annual leave they are entitled to as a result of their efforts, and employers are not penalised."

George Eustice, the environment secretary, said: "From our fields to our supermarkets, we are hugely grateful to the many people working around the clock to keep the nation fed. At this crucial time, relaxing laws on statutory leave will help ensure key workers can continue the important work to keep supplies flowing, but without losing the crucial time off they are entitled to.

"We welcome the measures the food industry is already taking to keep shelves stocked and supply chains resilient, and will continue to support them with their response to coronavirus."

The changes will amend the working time regulations, which apply to almost all employees, including agency workers, those who work irregular hours, and those on zero-hour contracts.

Rishi Sunak, the chancellor, announced that the government would cover the cost of employer national insurance and pension contributions for businesses hit by coronavirus. The move could save businesses an extra £30 a month per employee. ? Companies that have switched their production to help in the UK's fight against coronavirus are being offered free advice to see whether they are entitled to tax credit support. Momentum Group, which specialises in research and development tax credits, will offer claims advice at no charge to those firms stepping up to support others.

Companies that have changed their operations to help include the Scottish brewery Brewdog, which is making hand sanitiser, and the sportswear company O'Neills, which is making scrubs for health and social care trusts in Northern Ireland. "The only way we will beat Covid-19 is by working together and helping each other," said Tom Verner of Momentum Group.

headline: This crisis brings out all our inner entrepreneurs
author: Iain Anderson
text:
This week I was set to host 500 of Scotland's brightest and best business leaders for the annual Entrepreneurial Scotland celebration at Gleneagles. They were going to be inspired by Scots who have founded world-leading enterprises, creating jobs and economic opportunity. But for most of this year we are unlikely to see many mass gatherings to inspire us. Our focus must be on staying safe and ridding ourselves of Covid-19.

None of us, whatever we do, will forget 2020. In its latest analysis the Fraser of Allander Institute tells us: "The large-scale mothballing of our economy in response to the public health emergency is unlike anything we have seen since World War II." In many ways this enemy is so different to previous challenges. My mother, born in the 1930s, told me in one of our daily Facetime calls: "This is like the war and in so many ways unlike the war. At least we were able to go to school in those days."

Although the dislocation to our lives is immense, the contributions some businesses are making will be long remembered. BrewDog, the beer maker, will be thought of as a Scottish enterprise with a global mindset that stepped up, repurposing its business to manufacture and deliver hand sanitiser to the NHS and those most in need. Countless other Scottish businesses, large and small, are doing all they can in this new "war" effort.

History tells us that innovation and wildly creative ideas are often born of disruption. Now is the time for collective strength, action and kindness, to come together to find solutions and support each other.

In business we need brave leaders and we need to rally behind them.

Change-makers need our unwavering support. Leaders are only human; they too are anxious, and they do not have all the answers to this crisis. They will make mistakes like everybody else. But being purpose-led as a business comes into its own right now. Customers and staff need business to reinvent and innovate to survive. Businesses with the service of others as their vision are likely to succeed.

Business leaders need to focus on frequent contact with their teams. We need to ensure that we all maintain employee wellbeing and understand individual circumstances. It is also the time to bring all employees into the business strategy. What a great way to bind everyone together and inspire them to unleash their own ideas and potential.

When we have come through this challenge things will be different. There will be no return to business as usual. Scotland's entrepreneurs have never been more important.

Iain Anderson is executive chairman of Cicero/Amo

headline: 'Making Scotch might not really be essential'
author: Greig Cameron
text:
Scotland's economy secretary has cast doubt on whether whisky distilleries should continue to operate during the Covid-19 crisis.

Fiona Hyslop suggested that making spirits which will not be available for at least three years may not meet the definition of an essential service.

The spirit produced in Scotland must be distilled and stored here for at least three years before it can meet the legal definition of being Scotch.

In practice many distilleries store their liquid for much longer periods with most single malt varieties spending at least ten years in a barrel.

It is not clear what impact a few weeks or months of interruption to production would mean for the industry but it may potentially lead to a shortage of some types of Scotch in the future.

Many distillers were already concerned over new tariffs to the United States and the potential for additional export costs as a result of Brexit.

Ms Hyslop was yesterday quizzed about whether all food and drink companies should be operating in the present climate.

Unions have raised concerns that companies across many sectors are asking employees to come to work and continuing to operate even though they may not be providing essential services. Ms Hyslop pointed to the likes of Tunnock's and Walkers Shortbread as examples of food and drink businesses which have taken the decision to close for "the good of the country".

Speaking at Holyrood she said: "There are those businesses that might be considered part of an essential sector but not all businesses within a sector are essential. There are questions whether, for example, distilling that is not using whisky for three years is particularly something that is essential at this time to supply our supermarkets with.

"We are asking employers themselves to think very carefully about what they do."

Ms Hyslop said the preference from the government was for companies to be closed until it is safe for everybody in them to go back to work.

She added: "We cannot force this but it is a very strong steer."

Scotch whisky distilleries across the country have already closed down their café and visitor centres.

Many drinks firms are using their production facilities to help to manufacture hand sanitiser, either by making it themselves or supplying bulk ethanol to companies which can.

Dewar's, BrewDog, Loch Lomond Distillery, William Grant & Sons and Diageo are among the firms involved in those efforts. The Scotch Whisky Association (SWA) also launched an online portal for its members to offer their services for further production of products for NHS and other frontline staff.

A Scottish government spokesperson said: "In this public health crisis, it is vital that all businesses align fully with the social distancing measures introduced to protect the nation's heath, well-being and economic future. As such we would advise all business premises, sites and attractions to close now unless their activity is essential to the health and welfare of the country.

"These companies as confirmed by the Treasury are eligible for the wage subsidy scheme for furloughed workers. We have published guidance which makes clear that businesses can only continue to operate if they can, by working together with staff and unions, establish ways to keep their employees safe, both in travelling to work and at work.

"We are aware that some distillers are engaging in gearing up to produce alcohol for hand sanitisers, which is clearly essential, and other staff play an important role on care and maintenance of workplaces which can't be closed completely."

A spokesperson from the Scotch Whisky Association said: "The safety of industry staff is always our primary concern.

"Those Scotch Whisky companies which continue to operate are doing so only with significantly scaled-back operations and in rigorous compliance with Scottish government guidance, which says that companies can continue to operate if strict social distancing is in place. "The UK government has also made clear that food and drink production, including alcohol, are essential services and that manufacturing should continue where appropriate safety and social distancing protocols can be put in place."

headline: Thanks, UK. Keep doing the business
author: ALOK SHARMA
text:
SUNDAY ISSUE the MINISTER'S APPEAL TO EVERYONE: STAY CALM AND CARRY ON THE GOOD WORK

OUR country and economy need all our support in tackling the coronavirus pandemic.

This is a shared national effort and workers and businesses, across the country, have answered the call to action.

Workers across all industries and sectors are making a hugely valued and critical contribution to the resilience of our nation.

Whether it is the millions of people working from home, or the army of workers in distribution centres, supermarkets, transport, construction and manufacturing across the country, you are all playing your part in keeping Britain moving, so that as a nation we can support our fantastic NHS workers on the frontline of this pandemic.

Worker safety I want to recognise and pay tribute to those efforts.

We appreciate just how tough the situation is.

The Government has asked people to take unprecedented action by staying at home other than for four exceptions —o buy essentials, for one form of exercise a day, to attend medical appointments, or for work which cannot be done from home.

Safety has always been our number-one priority —nd throughout this crisis, we have followed the scientific and medical advice. The Government has provided guidance on how those who cannot do their work from home can continue to operate safely in the workplace.

I want everyone to be assured that if they are in their workplace, keeping our economy going, they know what they need to do to play their part in reducing the spread of coronavirus.

Many employers are already taking measures to ensure the safety of their workers.

Supermarkets, for instance, have put down two-metre markings on shop floors, installed protective screens for cashiers and are disinfecting stations for trolleys and baskets.

Construction firms have changed shift patterns and numbers on sites to maintain social-distancing rules.

Without the valiant efforts of builders and engineers, who have worked flat out, vital infrastructure such as the NHS Nightingale hospital would not have been built in record time.

Stepping up We have also seen gin distilleries and breweries like Brewdog start developing hand sanitiser to help meet the unprecedented demand at this time, and staff in distribution centres are keeping the public stocked up on essential items.

These are just a few examples of businesses and workers from across the UK that are stepping up to protect our people and economy.

It reinforces the fact that businesses are a force for good as we tackle the coronavirus pandemic.

It is crucial that when we overcome this crisis, as in time we will, the UK's economy is ready to bounce back and businesses are in a good position to move forward.

And all of those who have had to go to work —o stack shelves, to keep phone lines connected, to drive our trains or to build vital infrastructure —eserve the understanding of others as they go about their work.

Instances of abuse being thrown at these people are completely unacceptable —hey are keeping us safe, keeping us fed and keeping us connected.

While our fantastic NHS workers and the wider carer community are rightly at the forefront of our minds, I want to pay tribute to all those who are working to support them by keeping our economy going.

You are doing your bit to deliver for our country.

Times are tough —nd we have harder times ahead of us.

But I know that, together, we will pull through.

headline: The Coronavirus Pandemic: Europe Hopes It Pays to Sustain Wages
author: Jason Douglas in London, Tom Fairless in Frankfurt and Stacy Meichtry in Paris
text:
As job losses ricochet across the U.S., Europe is conducting an unprecedented experiment in navigating the economic fallout from the new coronavirus: Persuade companies to forgo layoffs by subsidizing private-sector wages on a massive scale.

More than one million companies across the continent have signed up for subsidy programs that essentially transform their payrolls into a system for delivering billions of euros in stimulus funds directly to households.

Governments hope the programs will act as a giant pause button for Europe's economy. Under the system, people who go for long stretches without work aren't supposed to miss a single paycheck. Once the confinement ends, workers will have a job waiting and money to spend. Companies can, in theory, pick up more or less where they left off.

The European approach contrasts with that of the U.S., where market forces are allowed to reshuffle capital and labor to a greater degree during downturns. Economists warn the European programs can distort the economy over time by keeping untenable companies alive, locking up capital and preventing staff from switching to better jobs.

In the U.S., Congress has provided $349 billion for a new program that provides loans to companies with fewer than 500 employees to meet payroll for eight weeks and pay some overhead. Those loans will be forgiven if the borrower doesn't lay off any staff. But the bulk of the broader $2 trillion stimulus package that President Trump signed into law aims to cushion the blow of surging job losses rather than prevent them. That includes expanding unemployment insurance and a plan to send households checks of up to $1,200.

James Watt, director of the Scotland-based craft-beer company BrewDog PLC, said 70% of the company's revenue vanished overnight because of plunging wholesale demand and the closure of 98 of its 102 bars world-wide. Most of BrewDog's 200 employees in Columbus, Ohio, had their hours cut to zero without pay, though the company is continuing to fund their health-care coverage and other benefits, and plans to put them back to work when the crisis ends.

In the U.K., however, BrewDog has applied for government grants that cover 80% of each employee's wages for an initial three months, up to a monthly maximum of GBP2,500 ($3,165).The new program is expected to be up and running by the end of the month, but companies are able to furlough workers now on reduced pay and backdate claims to March 1.

Mr. Watt said without the worker-retention program he would have had no option but to lay off as many as 1,000 employees in the U.K.

"The economy is going to need these jobs, this employment, and taxes on the other side," he said.

Whether such programs prevent more widespread layoffs remains to be seen. Consulting firm Oxford Economics doesn't expect record-setting unemployment rates across Europe's major economies this year but forecasts the U.S. will climb to an annual average of 9.8%, beating the 9.6% it averaged in 2010. A report published Wednesday by Ifo, a German think tank, showed the measures would help cap German unemployment at 5.9% this year, compared with 5% last year. That is a modest increase given that German economic output is likely to drop 10% in the three months through June, the sharpest decline since national accounts began in 1970, according to the report.

The only major European economy that has published recent jobs data is Spain, which said 898,822 people had lost their jobs between March 12 and the end of that month, equivalent to almost 5% of the workforce. Spain began to partially subsidize wages for 620,000 workers over roughly the same period, but that figure represents only the fraction of applications the government has managed to process so far.

One drawback is that some subsidy programs are modeled on decades-old furlough schemes that were designed for times when labor markets were less flexible. Traditionally, people who qualify for state aid work at larger companies that can hire people on contracts with heavy job protections. Many Spaniards who lost their jobs in March were on temporary contracts.

Nestor Ortuno, a 46-year-old construction worker, said his temporary contract made him an easy target in recent layoffs. His wife doesn't work, and the couple has three daughters. "I hope I will find something soon. Otherwise I do not know what we are going to do," he said.

The subsidies are also costly. The Institute for Fiscal Studies, a nonpartisan London-based think tank, said the U.K. program could cost around GBP10 billion ($12.3 billion) over three months if only 10% of all employees were covered.

The French government estimates its program will cost more than 20 billion euros ($21.7 billion) while Germany's Federal Labor Ministry estimates a total cost of 10 billion euros. Around 650,000 German companies have signaled in recent weeks that they will put staff on short-time work, more than 20 times the monthly peak during the financial crisis, Germany's labor agency said on Thursday. That is manageable for a government agency that is sitting on around 26 billion euros in reserves.

text:
Captain CRUNCH Need some cash help? write to me: crunch@the-sun.ie or #captcrunch 1RM Thursday, April 30, 2020 ...............

ONLINE TRAINING A new online training platform, Hays Thrive, is offering businesses of all sizes the chance to enroll their employees in courses in remote working, employee wellbeing, and health and safety for free. Courses can be accessed at hayslearning.ie MUSIC Right now, you can enjoy a free three-month Apple Music subscription. With no commitment and the ability to cancel at any time, this is a fantastic deal for anyone who loves listening to music, giving you instant access to more than 60 million tracks.

HISTORY LESSONS Dublin City Council has launched its History on Your Doorstep programme for those who want to expand their knowledge during lockdown. See dublincity.ie/story/ history-your-doorstep

headline: Capitalism is not to blame, it’ our escape route out of this mess
author: Robert Colvile
text:
We have clearly optimised the economy for efficiency rather than resilience

This article is a part of a series in which the FT asks leading commentators and policymakers what to expect from a post-Covid-19 future

The writer is director of the Centre for Policy Studies think-tank

The coronavirus crisis represents a reckoning for capitalism. And it’ not just Jeremy Corbyn saying so. A host of virtuous voices are insisting that any bailouts must separate the corporate sheep and goats, with exemplary punishment for firms that have used such abominations as offshore registration, share buybacks and dividends. Even the FT insists that “ne of the consequences of the pandemic must be a redrawing of the relationship between business and society”

This misreads not just how we got into this mess, but how we can get out of it. Obviously, no one wanted a pandemic. Yet if it had to happen, now is arguably the best time in history. Imagine coping with the last few weeks of lockdown without Zoom, Netflix or Amazon. Imagine how quickly the government’ furlough website would have fallen over without cloud computing behind it. Imagine how much longer it would take to produce a vaccine without the efforts of official bodies, NGOs and pharmaceutical companies cutting the likely development time from years to months.

None of this progress was made in a vacuum. It was possible because the UK is a rich, sophisticated, free-market economy. One in which firms like Barbour can retool to make surgical gowns and scrubs for the National Health Service, or BrewDog can make hand gel. In which AstraZeneca can pile in behind Oxford university to scale up vaccine production. In which we have the wealth and creditworthiness to sustain the economy, albeit at enormous cost, through months in the deep freeze.

The one blessing of this epidemic is that the burden has, so far, fallen largely on countries that are able to cope. This is in sharp contrast to pre-crisis predictions. Economists such as Larry Summers, who warned of the risk of pandemics, universally assumed that the developing world would bear the brunt. A World Bank study in 2013, for example, predicted that a severe pandemic “ould bring shared misery    ith the poor and those in fragile states hit the hardest”

The reason for such forecasts was simple and well-founded. As with the effects of climate change, it is wealth that is the best long-term guarantor of resilience. In recovering from this crisis, it is vital that we remember that lesson. The reason that Rishi Sunak is breaking the bank to preserve the private sector is because the chancellor knows that it is on the private sector that recovery will rest. We will not just be relying on the private sector to mass-produce and mass-market vaccines, tests and Personal Protective Equipment, but to deliver the growth, jobs and tax revenue that pull us out of this hole.

In the wake of the 2008 crisis, Britain benefited not just from an independent monetary policy, but from a far more flexible labour market than many of its European counterparts. The result —oupled with tax cuts on business and employment —as that we put on more jobs between 2010 and 2015 than the rest of the EU put together.

With millions more people now heading for the dole queue, the most valuable thing that ministers can do is not mouth pieties about social contracts but focus on bulldozing any and every obstacle to growth and job creation. That means spending less time pinpointing which businesses are “ood”nd which are “ad” and more on supporting businesses of every kind —n genuinely making Britain the best place to start, grow and run a company.

It’ true that this crisis has things to teach us. We have very clearly optimised the economy —nd indeed the health service—or efficiency rather than resilience. Vast interconnected supply chains are wonderful things but acutely vulnerable to the disappearance of individual components. Construction businesses who have attempted to keep working, with appropriate disinfection and distancing, have found themselves thwarted by the shuttering of builders’erchants, or suppliers of components such as mortar or interior doors.

Yet, at the same time, government command and control does not have a much better record: it is striking that the health systems that coped best with the epidemic, in Germany and South Korea, had a much wider range of provision and much more decentralisation of functions like testing.

In the wake of the corona crash, there will inevitably be sectors that struggle. Making it as easy as possible for workers to move elsewhere has to be a key aim of policy. So does preserving Britain’ small and family businesses, which create the lion’ share of jobs and have long been neglected by Whitehall.

But the lesson of this crisis is not that the free market needs to be fixed. It is that it is still the best tool we have for delivering prosperity. Let’ stop obsessing about what kind of capitalism we want to have after the crisis and be grateful we have it at all.

headline: Capitalism is not to blame, it's our escape route
author: Robert Colvile
text:
T he coronavirus crisis represents a reckoning for capitalism. And it's not just Jeremy Corbyn saying so. A host of virtuous voices are insisting that any bailouts must separate the corporate sheep and goats, with exemplary punishment for firms that have used such abominations as offshore registration, share buybacks and dividends. Even the FT insists that "one of the consequences of the pandemic must be a redrawing of the relationship between business and society".

This misreads not just how we got into this mess, but how we can get out of it. Obviously, no one wanted a pandemic. Yet if it had to happen, now is arguably the best time in history. Imagine coping with the last few weeks of lockdown without Zoom, Netflix or Amazon. Imagine how quickly the government's furlough website would have fallen over without cloud computing behind it. Imagine how much longer it would take to produce a vaccine without the efforts of official bodies, NGOs and pharmaceutical companies cutting the likely development time from years to months.

The writer is director of the Centre for Policy Studies think-tank

headline: Spinal column Melanie Reid
text:
'Low-alcohol beer, YouTube videos, barking at Alexa - my son has turned me into a millennial'

Remember how, when you were little, every so often came the thrilling day when your much older sister or brother came home for a while. In that moment your world exploded with colour and fun and new experiences.

You were entranced by these exotic creatures, students and young adults, speaking knowingly of people and places you knew nothing about. You were in awe of their clothes, their possessions, the way they sat and laughed; the intoxicating sense of youth and freedom and vitality they exuded.

They were so different from your parents (well, mine, anyway). You became their shadow, copying their mannerisms, pretending to understand, yearning to share their magic. Amused, they humoured you.

In one of life's wry inversions, this is where I find myself once again, drinking in the heady air of youth and the wider world, this time from my son. He's back living at home for the foreseeable due to the lockdown, the first time since he left for university 12 years ago. And it's exciting.

It's not that I'm sitting outside his bedroom door stalking him, as I used to do with my brother or sister, because that wouldn't be normal, but I'm definitely a bit of a cling-on, soaking up new ideas, experiences, expressions, learning about another generation's life.

In fact, there's nothing else to do but admit it: wowser, I'm becoming a millennial. My life hasn't been this interesting for years (you mustn't mock; I'm starting from a low base). I'm now a convert to oh, let's begin with alcohol. Millennial drinks are loads nicer than our generation's.

Session beer - designed for a long session; how clever is that? - is unashamedly low alcohol, has all sorts of fruity flavours, and costs not a lot from Aldi. There's also a yummy genre called sour beer - I've fallen for Cosmic Ripple, a red rye raspberry. Plus, truly on a roll, I've had my first BrewDog experience - a hoppy zero-alcohol number with grapefruit undertones called Nanny State. Here's what it says on the label: "Join us and raise a glass to the Revolution. Here's to the gamechangers, the truth seekers and the punks. To those who want to make a difference." Bravely setting aside three decades of cynicism, I drank to enjoy. It was great - and tasted pleasantly alcoholic.

Another way to be effortlessly millennial: believe that Google has the answer to everything. Why bother carrying information in your head? With Google, in 20 seconds, every loose fact, random ponder or recipe for smoked salmon risotto can be nailed. Unsure how to change a car tyre or construct a set of shelves? Go to YouTube. An instruction video on how to do every single damn thing in the world has already been made. You just copy it. (The unfortunate corollary to this, that if it's not on Google it can't exist or be true, is an issue to be tackled another day.) I've also taken on board the millennial approach to Facebook, which is that everyone's still on it but it's pretty hateful and the only people who actually use it are weird uncles. Facebook makes it as hard as possible for you to protect your privacy - but you just google for advice on how to beat them and change your settings.

Then there is my new skill in addressing Alexa. You do it brusquely and matter-of-factly. It's a robot. Don't say "remind me" because then it asks you what you want reminding of. Don't say "alert me" because it doesn't understand. Just bark, "Alexa, timer 10 minutes." Or, "Alexa, play Radio 6." Easy when you know.

In a fantastically mature way, this generation makes fresh salads, refuses to pass personal criticism and upcycles or recycles old stuff instead of hoarding it.

I'm grasping, though, that their key characteristic may be their absolute freedom from terrestrial TV schedules. The concept of framing your life around telly is unfathomable to them. Watching the teatime news bulletin means you require carbon dating. The least likely millennial sentence? "Where's the TV guide?" " On the contrary, their infotainment - instant, portable, on demand - mustfit around their lives. I must try harder at this; it doesn't yet feel liberating. After weeks of millennial studies, I sit an exam later this week: I've got to watch The End of the F***ing World at 11am in the kitchen. And find it funny. Wish me luck. ? n @Mel_ReidTimes Melanie Reid is tetraplegic after breaking her neck and back in a riding accident in April 2010

text:

Page 8 2 Matt Hancock, the health secretary, said that "lavish international holidays" in July and August would probably have to be cancelled to control the spread of coronavirus. Last night, Tui said that it was planning to reintroduce holidays from June 12 while Jet2 will resume its flights and holiday schedule from June 17. Page 10 3 One in three companies that have temporarily shut during the lockdown fear that they may never reopen, according to a survey of thousands of small businesses by the Federation of Small Businesses, Britain's largest employers' group.

 Page 31 4 Petroineos, a company coowned by Sir Jim Ratcliffe which runs the Grangemouth oil refinery, is seeking an emergency government loan reported to be for as much as £00 million. The company is half owned by Ineos, the petrochemicals giant controlled by the billionaire tycoon.
