headline: News; Front Page BRITAIN'S MOST TRUSTED NATIONAL NEWSPAPER
text:
CORONAVIRUS SUMMARY

Global confirmed cases 4.3m 290,597 Global deaths UK confirmed cases 226,463 32,692 UK deaths Extension for furlough The government's wage-subsidy scheme has been extended until the end of October. Rishi Sunak, the chancellor, said he would keep the overall level of the wage subsidy scheme at 80 per cent up to £,500 a month. Instead of reducing the amount that staff will receive, he announced that from August all employers furloughing workers would be required to contribute towards the cost of paying people's salaries, although the amount that they will have to pay has yet to be decided. Page 4 Grim milestone More than 40,000 people in the UK have died from Covid-19, according to data from the Office for National Statistics. It showed that a third of deaths in the week ending May 1 mentioned the virus. It also confirms that the outbreak is no longer centred on London, with the southeast and northwest overtaking the capital. Separate modelling by The Times estimates that the number of fatalities could be as high as 61,000 when deaths caused directly and indirectly by the virus are included. Page 6 COMMENT Russia's oligarchs are digging deep into their pockets to help citizens out of the virus crisis ROGER BOYES, PAGE 22 Scots care home threat The proportion of coronavirus patients dying in Scottish care homes is almost double that of England, according to new figures. The Office for National Statistics said yesterday that 7,903 deaths in English care homes had been linked to the virus by May 1, 25 per cent of all Covid-19 deaths. In Scotland the figure up to May 3 was 1,195, accounting for 43 per cent of virus deaths. Page 7 DAYS Pubs of the future 52 OF UK LOCKDOWN Friends will talk through plastic screens and order beers on their phones under plans being considered to allow a pub chain to open. Brewdog has proposed to adapt its bars for the postpandemic era, but the atmosphere may not be so convivial: queuing for the bar and proffering banknotes will be abolished in favour of German-style table service and drinks ordered and paid for through an app. Page 8 52 DAYS OF UK LOCKDOWN Hols out for summer The new "reality of life" under social-distancing rules means that summer holidays abroad are unlikely this year. The health secretary, Matt Hancock, said that "lavish international holidays" in July and August would probably have to be cancelled to control the spread of coronavirus. It comes as Spain announced that from Friday all incoming travellers would face two weeks of quarantine. Page 11 Firms fear closures A third of companies that have temporarily closed during the lockdown fear that they may never reopen, a survey suggests. The same proportion said they were considering or had made redundancies. The Federation of Small Businesses, which questioned more than 5,000 companies, found that 70 per cent were using the government's furlough scheme. Page 31 Warning to US business American states that ignore White House guidelines and lift restrictions on business activity too early risk turning back the clock and damaging the US economy further, according to the country's top disease expert, Anthony Fauci. He was speaking as President Trump tweeted support for a Tesla car plant that defied lockdown rules to resume production. Page 12 Football's coming home Premier League football clubs are expected to be able to use their own grounds to complete the season after positive talks with the government and the police, who had said that using neutral venues was the only safe way to complete the remaining 92 games. Despite the progress, convincing players that it is safe to return to competitive action remains a significant obstacle. Page 57

headline: News; Front Page Sturgeon denies virus cover-up
author: Mark McLaughlin
text:
Nike delegate infected in FebruaryTransmission thought to be Scotland's earliestFirst minister cites patient privacy

Nicola Sturgeon has denied a cover-up after her government failed to reveal that one of the country's earliest coronavirus outbreaks began at a conference in Edinburgh a month before lockdown.

At least 25 people were infected after attending the event organised by Nike, the sportswear company, at the Hilton Carlton Hotel in the city on February 26 and 27. Eight of those who attended lived in Scotland.

The first minister said that patient confidentiality meant she was not allowed to make the information public. However, the Scottish government believes that the first person to have contracted Covid-19 on Scottish soil was infected at the conference, The Times has learnt.

The Scottish delegate tested positive on March 2 with either the Grampian or Ayrshire health board. Another positive case was identified in the Lothians. The government declined to be more specific, to protect the identities of the delegates.

The patient in Grampian or Ayrshire was only the second case to be confirmed in Scotland, indicating that the virus had been spreading within the country in late February.

The first confirmed case was in Tayside on March 1, when the government said that an infected person had returned from Italy.

The first death was announced on March 13, an elderly patient in the Lothians. Death by coronavirus occurs about three weeks after infection on average. Lockdown was enforced on March 23.

Nicola Sturgeon rejected claims that she had covered up one of Scotland's earliest outbreaks of coronavirus as "complete and utter nonsense".

In other developments: ? Care home residents account for a significantly higher share of coronavirus deaths in Scotland than in England, according to official figures.

? Police officers in Scotland can be tested if they fear an attack has put them at risk of the virus even if they have no symptoms.

? Weddings may only be able to take place with a "very limited number of people" as lockdown begins to ease. ? The pub chain Brewdog has revealed plans to reopen its bars with single-use menus, cashless payment and plastic screens on tables.

? Fifty more deaths of people who had tested positive for Covid-19 were announced yesterday, bringing the total to 1,912.

Ms Sturgeon said that there was a greater risk of identifying the 70 people at the Edinburgh conference and that Tayside was a "big place". The population of Tayside is estimated to be 417,470 while Edinburgh's is 524,930.

She said that the Tayside diagnosis was the first confirmed incident in Scotland but the Nike conference was probably where the virus was introduced in Edinburgh. At her daily briefing she said that all appropriate steps had been taken to protect public health.

Ms Sturgeon strongly denied claims that ministers were guilty of failing to disclose the Edinburgh incident, which emerged on a Disclosure programme called Scotland's Lockdown, broadcast by BBC Scotland on Monday night.

"These are not always straightforward, simple judgments," Ms Sturgeon said. "On the accusation of a cover-up, that is complete and utter nonsense. Why would we have been trying to cover anything up? "We were reporting figures on this. I stood up here every single day to be as open and transparent with you, the public, as possible."

Jeane Freeman, the health secretary, told MSPs that the government had not disclosed where the Tayside patient travelled from in the interests of protecting their identity. She said: "The patient was a resident of Tayside and we Continued on page 2, col 3 continued from page 1 Sturgeon denies cover-up believe that they contracted the virus in a country where the virus was present. We didn't name the country."

The Scottish government's press release on March 1, the day that the first infection was confirmed, said: "The patient is a resident of the Tayside area and has recently travelled from Italy."

Ms Freeman later wrote to Holyrood's presiding officer to apologise for the error and correct the record.

Ian Murray, the shadow Scottish secretary, said: "Nicola Sturgeon has failed to come clean on this cover-up. It is not acceptable to hide behind patient confidentiality. You don't need to identify people to take action on a pandemic."

A Scottish government spokesman said: "All individuals who had attended the conference were contact traced. The close contacts of cases in Scotland were also traced, as confirmed in the news release issued on March 4. All of the eight cases in Scotland associated with the event have been included in the daily updates of positive case numbers and their contacts were traced."

The road to lockdown December 31, 2019 Pneumonia cluster reported in Wuhan, China January 11, 2020 China reports first death January 12, 2020 Covid-19 gene sequenced January 13 First case outside China in Thailand January 24 First case in Europe in France January 30 World Health Organisation declares a global emergency January 31 First UK case in York, with two Chinese nationals. Italy also announces first case February 26 Nike Conference, Carlton Hotel, Edinburgh March 1 First case confirmed in Scotland. A man in Tayside who visited Italy March 2 Foreign Nike delegate tests positive March 3 Scottish Nike delegate tests positive March 4 Scottish Nike delegate traced to Grampian or Ayrshire. One more is later traced in Lothian March 5 First UK death in Berkshire March 11 Scottish government permits 14,000 people to watch the Stereophonics in Glasgow March 13 SPFL overrules the government and cancels Old Firm match at Ibrox March 14 First Scottish death in the Lothians March 23 UK enters lockdown

headline: Shields and spray to keep pubs clean
author: Marc Horne
text:
Pta. de Carave Friends will talk through plastic screens and order beer on their phones under proposals being considered to allow a pub chain to reopen.

Brewdog, the Aberdeenshire company, has announced plans to adapt its bars for the post-pandemic era. The brewery has more than 50 bars in Britain, including 16 in Scotland.

The atmosphere may not be quite as convivial and care-free as before the coronavirus lockdown, however.

Queuing for the bar and proffering banknotes will be a thing of the past, abolished in favour of German-style table service and drinks ordered and paid for through an app similar to that used by the Wetherspoon chain.

B R A Z I Under the proposals customers would be able to request transparent protective shields mounted on their tables. Staff would wear masks and gloves at all times and surfaces would be disinfected every 15 minutes. The numbers of seats and tables would be reduced to allow social distancing. The carefully restricted number of punters would use hand sanitiser stations and disposable menus.

AN Belo The British government ordered bars, pubs and nightclubs to close in March to curb the spread of Covid-19.

David McDowall, chief operating officer for the Brewdog group, said that the company had made detailed plans in the hope that they would help ensure its pubs could reopen when the time is right.

"Although the future for hospitality businesses is incredibly uncertain, we remain optimistic," he said. "We long for the day when our team is back in our bars.

"With that in mind, while we eagerly await official guidance from the government our amazing bar teams have been working on various proposals.

"When we finally get our bars back open again, and our customers visit us to enjoy a welldeserved pint, we will ensure that they do so with a very warm welcome, and in a clean and safe environment."

The proposals have been welcomed by UK Hospitality, the industry body.

Kate Nicholls, its chief executive, said: "This is brilliant and shows the positive steps hospitality is ready to take to reopen at the right time and in the right way to protect our teams and guests and to contribute again to our communities." Fred Sirieix, the restaurant manager and broadcaster, tweeted: "Brilliant! Simple and effective plan. Makes me want to go."

Wetherspoon, the UK's largest pub chain, said last month that it would reopen its 850 sites "in or around June" but gave no detail on its plans. Tim Martin, its chairman, said: "We've made an assumption, just for planning purposes, that pubs might be allowed to open in late June, but we don't know any more than anyone else."

The Scottish government has commissioned academics from the Stirling University to study options for reopening pubs, clubs and restaurants. Kate Forbes, the finance secretary, said last week it was likely to be "summer and beyond".

headline: Half of Scotland's pubs and bars may never open again
author: Marc Horne
text:
The traditional Scottish local pub is in danger of becoming a much-mourned victim of the coronavirus pandemic, industry leaders have warned.

For centuries pubs and bars have been at the heart of communities, surviving world wars, the smoking ban and stringent restrictions on drink driving.

However, the UK government ordered bars, pubs and nightclubs to close in March. More than half of the nation's 3,000 hostelries will never reopen if ministers withdraw financial support and insist on social distancing, trade bodies believe.

"The fight is on to save the Scottish pub," said Paul Waterson, spokesman for the Scottish Licensed Trade Association (SLTA). "It is as serious as that."

The prime minister has said some hospitality venues could reopen in England as early as July 4, on condition that strict social distancing rules are adhered to. Scottish pubs are expected to remain closed for longer and Mr Waterson said a partial reopening would swiftly lead to widespread closures, with small, independent bars being particularly vulnerable.

"With social distancing our members would lose between 50 and 80 per cent of their capacity overnight," he said. "If you have a pub with a capacity of 50 you are talking about maybe 15 people being allowed in. It's not just viable.

"If government help was stopped I think at least half of our bars and pubs would close, maybe more."

Craft beer venues, which have become increasingly popular in recent years, would be among the worst hit. "These are usually small venues which rely on the fact that they have a wide variety of beers," said Mr Waterson. "But with a greatly reduced capacity and through-put the beers will go off.

"The actual product range within pubs is going to really go down, meaning you might have a lager, a beer and a Guinness, but not much else."

The SLTA believes the vast majority of hostelries would be better off remaining closed, with furlough support and grants in place, until a vaccine can be found.

Niamh Fitzgerald, professor of alcohol policy at the University of Stirling, who is leading a Scottish governmentfunded study into the viability of easing lockdown, shared their concerns. "It is really hard to see how this would work," she said. "The worst of both worlds would be that they are allowed to reopen but restrictions are such that they are simply not viable and all support from the government disappears."

The British Beer and Pub Association added: "We believe most pubs wouldn't be able to open and operate with twometre social distancing and those that tried would find it incredibly costly and impractical to do so."

Larger chains, including Wether-spoon, have indicated they do plan to reopen as soon as it is legally possible to do so.

Earlier this week Brewdog, which has more than 50 bars in Britain, including 16 in Scotland, announced plans to adapt its premises for the post-pandemic era.

Staff would wear masks, surfaces would be disinfected every 15 minutes, customers would be able to request transparent table-mounted shields and drinks would be ordered and paid for through a phone app.

The Scottish government said: "We are considering how best to plan for the restart and long-term recovery of Scotland's hospitality industry and we will be inviting the Scottish licensed trade and the hospitality industry to be a key partner in those discussions. We are determined to support businesses for as long as they need it."

headline: Bright minds will keep us out in front
text:
Shaf Rasul One in a million shaf@the-sun.co.uk

LIKE many Scottish business owners, I am turning my attention to the next phase of what post-lockdown commerce may look like. And, with no blueprint for what comes next, we have much to think about.

Businesses emerging from lockdown in Scotland, Wales, Northern Ireland and England are set to do so at different times. And with Scotland still facing the stronger restrictions, we can only plan, be well-coordinated and ready for action when the time is right.

But does this time shift also give Scotland a greater opportunity to adapt and evolve, as we monitor how businesses south of the border open their doors once more? Scotland punches above its weight on many fronts, but its ability to adapt and evolve is one area where it is a heavyweight contender, providing some of the world's greatest entrepreneurs as a result.

Many Scottish tycoons have changed the world.

Steel magnate Andrew Carnegie became one of American enterprise's most-important figures, Pete Cashmore founded Mashable (one of the internet's most-popular news and technology sites) and Sir Brian Souter transformed UK bus and rail operations with his Stagecoach Group.

These individuals are just three great examples showing Scotland's strength as a great place to launch and grow a business and it is our ability to diversify and create new businesses which will be vital.

In fact, with so many Scottish businesses forecast not to return following lockdown, the rate at which we can be brave and adapt, or set up new ones, will be key to our recovery.

We are a small country in size with a population of just over five million, yet over the last five years the creation of companies in Scotland has grown more strongly than the rest of Britain.

In 2015 the number of new start-ups here rose by seven per cent, ahead of the UK average of four per cent.

Scottish start-ups can be championed by some fantastic businesses, such as one of the world's most-successful beer brands, Brewdog, and sunglasses brand Tens, which is exporting all over the globe from its Highland base.

It's also been incredible to see some of our food and drink operators diversifying and using innovative ways to survive the lockdown changing business models, in many cases quite dramatically, in an attempt to keep them afloat.

Glasgow and Edinburghbased Six by Nico restaurants launched its Home by Nico service where a four-course meal is delivered to customers' doors. Meanwhile, I am hearing the farmers' markets in Stirling and Edinburgh have launched online selling portals for customers to order local produce in just a few clicks, which seems to have been very well-received.

Other types of retailers are also proving agile while consumers are unable to shop as they would have previously. Mud Urban Florists, for example, have continued to deliver from their outlets in Edinburgh and Glasgow and have also switched from fresh to dried flowers which have a significantly longer shelf life.

Bellshill-based online retailer Homefresh Foods have also altered their business operations. They've moved from solely supplying restaurants to offering deliveries of vegetable boxes direct to consumers.

While we all continue to adhere to the lockdown measures there are also numerous examples of gyms, make-up artists and hairdressers moving much of their business online to provide "at home" classes, sessions and tips for their customers. The public have welcomed the efforts of these businesses to maintain personal connections, which operators hope will enhance customer loyalty post-lockdown.

I will always be a great ambassador for true entrepreneurial spirit and trust that innovation and diversification is essential for our recovery, but I appreciate the severe pressure many Scottish businesses are under and the challenging road ahead for all of us to move forward from this.

For some, however, diversification has demonstrated that there is a strong market for alternative products and services which can be delivered in a different way.

It's encouraging to see innovation is getting some retail, hospitality and leisure businesses through the worst of the Covid-19 restrictions and if we can channel our fighting spirit to build new business, there may just be a phoenix able to rise from the ashes.

headline: Like prisoners let out of jail, our post-lockdown lives won't be a breeze
author: Philip
text:
With lockdown-lite in prospect I'm starting to think about how I will adapt to reentry into society. For eight weeks I have worn two pairs of trousers (Banana Republic chinos and Fat Face cords, the latter bought in Silverburn last year) plus four T-shirts (VW campervan, Abbey Road Studios, Kraftwerk and my alter ego, George Costanza). That wardrobe will require refreshing, my Unabomber beard will demand shearing and the use of garlic as the principal ingredient of every meal, will call for moderation.

But of even more interest is how the commercial world deals with what hopefully is an interim phase, while a cure for Covid-19 is found.

Darwin's theory of evolution is often misinterpreted. It's not the strongest of the species who survive, nor is it the cleverest. Rather, it is the one who is best able to adapt to the changing environment. That is equally true of business.

In the longer term there will be a new normal. It will be as developed and refined as was our world prepandemic. But in the meantime we need to find practical and imaginative ways of making the transition. There are, of course, precedents for this. Immediately after 9/11 we saw airports operating with ad hoc security checks and city streets lined with improvised concrete barriers, before a higher level of anti-terrorism safety was integrated into our infrastructure.

We are now starting to see how business is making these adaptations. Brewdog has set out its plans for adapting its bars for the post-coronavirus era. They include a contactless app for ordering, staff in masks and gloves, capacity monitoring and table screens. These changes may not be conducive to the most convivial of atmospheres, but between permitting some trading during this period or not, they represent a preferable option. ` Last week I was interviewed for a webinar by Hey Legal. Rather than try to get lawyers to attend evening continuing professional development sessions in stuffy conference rooms after a day's work, they are creating a community through an online subscription model. It is intended to change the mindset from one of attaining your minimum regulatory requirements, to engaging through continuous daily learning as a matter of choice. It's an approach that social distancing has made more relevant.

Street Soccer Scotland is a charity that provides training and personal development for socially disadvantaged groups. Like many other organisations, it has had to postpone a number of vital fund-raising events during the lockdown. The answer has been to organise an at-home gala dinner including a virtual "in conversation" with the Scotland captain Andy Robertson and an appearance by Gary: Tank Commander. Even more enterprising is a menu created by Nick Nairn with an online cooking masterclass delivered the week before, so that one can create the dishes in your own home. It sounds like an improvement from the average Thursday evening spent in a dinner suit eating rubber chicken with a thousand others.

Aside from the practical adaptations that we will be required to make are the psychological and emotional ones. As restrictions have been loosened in other countries, psychologists have observed the emerging phenomenon of postlockdown anxiety. One can understand that. On release from prison it is common to suffer from post-incarceration syndrome. There is a certain security that we have felt being at home with our loved ones. As the lockdown is relaxed we will have to confront the stresses of public transport and the workplace combined with a fear of infection. Having experienced the enormous upheaval in our lives over the past couple of months, we now need to reacclimatise with some of our old regimes, while hopefully retaining the positive lessons that we have learnt.

If, like me you consider Seinfeld one of the best comedy series, you too will have been saddened by the passing of Jerry Stiller a week ago. Stiller played the over-the-top father of the neurotic George Costanza. Whenever he was stressed, he controlled his explosive temper by screaming the mantra "Serenity Now!" Perhaps we should follow his lead.

Philip Rodney is a consultant, columnist and non-executive director

headline: Birthday beer Ian in Rebus bar jaunt
author: ELLIE FORBES
text:
REBUS writer Ian Rankin revealed he celebrated his 60th birthday with a lockdown drink at the fictional cop's No1 boozer.

The author supped a beer outside the closed Oxford Bar in central Edinburgh last month despite fearing police would nab him amid the coronavirus restrictions.

Rankin, who lives in a plush flat in the capital's Lauriston, said: "It was a 330ml can of Brewdog as that was what I had in.

"I took a glass, stuffed both in my pockets and walked to the Oxford, 20 minutes from my flat.

"I stood outside, opened the can, poured it, drank it and then walked home briskly before the police could stop me for being too far from my home."

The 23rd Rebus novel, A Song for the Dark Times, is out in October.

headline: Brewer's libel case over PR stunt rejected
author: Jonathan Ames
text:
Donald Trump supporters are not everyone's cup of tea or pint of beer, which is why a Scottish brewery sued when a PR company linked it with American "rednecks".

A High Court judge has taken a different view and dismissed a libel claim brought by Brewdog in relation to a suggestion that it would offer free beer to backers of the US president.

The company, which is based in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, and brews beers including Punk IPA and Dead Pony Club, told the court that it had been defamed by Frank PR. The agency announced in 2018 that Brewdog would be distributing the US brewer Scofflaw's beer in the UK.

The press release, which was not approved by Brewdog or Scofflaw, announced that "redneck US brewers Scofflaw" had partnered with "badass beer brand Brewdog to launch in the UK" but to get "beered up redneckstyle completely free of charge ... you have to be a Trump supporter".

Brewdog, which says it is known for being critical of the US president, claimed that it was criticised for being associated with Mr Trump as a result of the press release, that people could think the brewer supported his policies and it was therefore "a hypocrite".

The PR company's legal team said that the tone of the press release was light-hearted and recipients would recognise the offer for what it was: a publicity gimmick.

Sitting remotely in the High Court yesterday, Mr Justice Nicol agreed and said that Brewdog's libel case could not proceed. He said that the press release "said nothing about the political philosophy of the claimants".

headline: Pivoting production eases pressure on pandemic-hit governments
author: Nick Huber
text:
Flexibility in supply chains set to bring about fundamental changes

Company supply chains and production lines have long been focused on providing goods promptly and efficiently. Yet over the past few months, the priority for a growing number has been reinvention —roducing different products to help governments cope with the Covid-19 pandemic.

Fashion houses such as Burberry are making non-surgical gowns and masks. Breweries are making hand sanitiser as well as pale ales. Flight attendants with medical training have been seconded to hospitals.

Although pivoting production to alternative goods can be costly, such flexibility may result in fundamental changes —hat lessons can this offer to companies and governments?

The cost of switching varies according to how different the new product is to the old —he bigger the difference the more expensive it will be, experts say.

Keeping some production lines and supply chains open during a pivot may help companies return to normal production quicker because “hey are able to continue running core parts of their business” says James Yearsley, a partner at consultancy Deloitte who specialises in supply chains and procurement.

BrewDog, a Scottish craft beer brewery, is making hand sanitiser for Britain’ NHS and other frontline workers. The sanitiser is produced voluntarily by some of the company’ furloughed bar staff who were working until March —hen the UK government ordered the closure of bars —nd staff in the company’ head office and refinery. Meanwhile, BrewDog has continued to brew beer and expanded its home delivery service.

“ovid-19 has shifted a lot more of our sales direct to consumer,”ays James Watt, BrewDog’ co-founder.

BrewDog runs a chain of bars as well as producing beer, meaning that it will be especially affected by the Covid-19 lockdown. “e, like all other companies, are thinking what the landscape, post-Covid-19, will look like,”ays Mr Watt. “ow long does it take to come back to how things were? Does it ever come back like that? Or does it come back differently? At least for the rest of this year and into next year people are predominantly going to be a bit reluctant to go out to the same extent that they did before.”

For large companies, it is likely to take weeks rather than months for production to return to normal levels, experts reckon.

Rebooting production lines should usually be fairly straightforward, because much of it is computerised, says Bob Sibik, co-founder of Fusion Risk Management, which advises companies on supply chains and business continuity. A programmer can reprogram the “omputer aided design”hat runs most manufacturing machines, such as 3D printers and laser cutters. “t will depend on what [a company] is manufacturing but they can retool in a couple of days,”ays Mr Sibik.

A requirement for “ocial distancing”t work —eeping employees apart to reduce the risk of Covid-19 infections —ill make it hard for employers to manage crowded workplaces. This is likely to mean companies will spend more on technologies that cut the need for human intervention in supply chains, Mr Sibik says.

More automation may result in job cuts, Mr Sibik says. However, workers will still be needed to monitor manufacturing systems, he adds, with staff who understand the technology being best placed to keep their roles.

Covid-19 may even prompt companies to judge supply chains more on their resilience and reliability during a crisis, and less on their ability to minimise surplus stock and their efficiency of delivery. “efore Covid-19, just-in-time demand and no excess stock [in supply chains] was seen as desirable,”ays Mr Yearsley. “n future, maybe excess capacity for supply chains will be seen as desirable because it builds buffers into supply chains to cover changes in demand.”

Governments may also review their supply chains after Covid-19.

“overnments are definitely thinking and worrying about [supply chain problems] a lot, most obviously in medical goods and PPE [personal protective equipment],”ays Geoffrey Gertz, an expert in trade and foreign investment at the Brookings Institution, a US think-tank.

For example, the US government’ $2tn economic stimulus package in response to Covid-19 —nown as the Cares Act —ncludes a call for the National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine to audit the medical product supply chain to identify vulnerabilities, he adds.

Global crises can lead to positive changes, such as encouraging governments and companies to collaborate to better prepare for future shocks, says Creon Butler, research director at Chatham House, a London think-tank. “he advantage of a globally pooled approach is that you share information,”ays Mr Butler. “f you are acting individually as countries or as companies, it is harder to produce the most efficient response.”

headline: Short-sighted beer launched The website [...]
author: Greig Cameron
text:
Short-sighted beer launched The website of a brewery crashed after it launched a new beer poking fun at Dominic Cummings (Greig Cameron writes).

Brewdog gave customers options to name a limited edition drink and Barnard Castle Eye Test Hazy IPA was voted the favourite.

The company in Aberdeenshire has never been shy over its marketing. In the past it projected the naked bodies of its founders on to the Houses of Parliament and served up super-strength drinks in taxidermied animals.

The Barnard Castle brew, named after the UK government adviser's trip to the beauty spot, is a limited, 6 per cent alcohol edition. It is flavoured with pineapple, mango and lime and costs £6.95 for a pack of 12 cans.

James Watt, the co-founder of Brewdog, said it should be ready to be send to customers within two weeks. Profits will go towards the company's production of free sanitiser for the NHS, healthcare charities and key workers. Mr Watt said it had produced more than £ million worth of sanitiser over the past eight weeks.

headline: BREW DO YOU THINK IT'S FOR?
author: Shaf Rasul
text:
Shaf Rasul One in a million shaf@the-sun.co.uk

IT MAKES ME LAUGH YOU didn't need to drive 30 miles to a tourist attraction to have seen this coming, but Scottish craft beer giants BrewDog have once again placed tongue very firmly in cheek with the launch of their new Barnard Castle Eye Test IPA. Clearly a nod to the recent events surrounding Dominic Cummings, the company says the limited edition beer was inspired by "the people".

All proceeds will go towards production of BrewGel, the free sanitiser which they have been creating for NHS workers and healthcare charities.

BrewDog has always shown a sense of humour, and true to form the company describes Barnard Castle Eye Test IPA as a "short-sighted beer for tall stories" which gave me a lockdown laugh.

headline: FOOD LET THE GOOD TIMES BE-GIN
text:
On a plate, on a stick or in a glass - our fave tipple is just the tonic this summer

GIN & LEMON ROAST CHICKEN SERVES 4 Prep time 10 minutes Cooking time 1 hour 50 minutes Cals 868 Sat fat 14g

*11/2-2kg free-range chicken, jointed into 4 portions, or a whole chicken

*1 lemon, cut into quarters

*Crisp salad or spring vegetables, to serve For the glaze

*1 lemon, grated zest and juice

*60ml gin

*100g soft light-brown sugar

*4 juniper berries, crushed

*1 heaped tsp finely chopped rosemary leaves

*50ml olive oil 1 Preheat the oven to 220°/ 200° fan/gas mark 7. 2 Mix together all the ingredients for the glaze and season with salt and pepper. Line a roasting tin with foil (the sugar can make it a bit sticky!) and place the chicken on top.

3 Spread the glaze over the meat, pop the lemon quarters around the chicken and roast in the oven for 35-40 minutes, depending on the size of the chicken pieces - or 1 hour 50 minutes if using a whole chicken - until the skin is crisp and the juices run clear. Check after 20 minutes or so and if the sugar is browning too much, pop a sheet of foil over the top. 4 Once cooked, remove from the oven and leave to rest for 10 minutes before serving with the roasted lemon quarters and a crisp salad or selection of spring vegetables.

GIN & TONIC ICE LOLLIES MAKES 6 Prep time 5 minutes, plus 4 hours freezing Cals 70 Sat fat 0g

*250ml tonic water

*75ml gin

*50g caster sugar

*Sliced strawberries, cucumber slices and fresh mint leaves (or whatever fruits and herbs you like), to garnish You will also need

*6-mould ice lolly tray

*6 wooden ice lolly sticks 1 Start by making the G&T mix. Open your tonic and let the bubbles dissipate. Then combine in a mixing jug or bowl with the gin and sugar. Stir gently and set aside.

2 Fill the lolly tray with your strawberries, mint and cucumber - we like to roughly chop everything and sprinkle a small handful into each lolly mould. Pour over your G&T mix and freeze for at least 4 hours, but preferably overnight. headline: BrewDog  Den fund bid 'worth £60m'
author: JONATHAN COLES
text:
A BREWDOG boss once snubbed by Dragons' Den has revealed the £00,000 he wanted would now be worth £60million.

Co-founder James Watt said the huge return on a 20 per cent stake in the booze firm would have been "by far the best deal" in the show's history.

Watt told how he and partner Martin Dickie, who launched the venture in Fraserburgh, Aberdeenshire, in 2007, had a screen test the following year but were snubbed by BBC producers.

James said the pair "got over the knockback eventually". He added: "We pitched our hearts out before they rejected us. They deemed us not worthy. We were crushed.

"Based on our latest BrewDog valuation, that investment would now be worth almost £60million."

headline: Hard seltzer —ow alcoholic is your water?
text:
It is tipped to be the drink of the summer —t least according to neophyte supermarket buyers, who having tried to push first craft beer, then pink gin on to consumers have now moved on to hard seltzers.

The concept is simple: alcoholic fizzy water. And it is huge in America, with a new brand launched every week and the market expected to be worth $2.5 billion a year by 2021. By the end of this year it is expected to be about a tenth the size of the beer market in America.

The marketing is unequivocally aimed at millennials keen on something healthier than beer. Nearly all claim to be vegan, glutenfree, full of antioxidants and having fewer than 100 calories a can.

Hard seltzers have now just hit the UK, with Tesco, Sainsbury's and Morrisons stocking the US's biggest brand, White Claw. Brewers keen not to miss out have started producing their own versions, with Kopparberg selling a passion fruit hard seltzer, Smirnoff doing a raspberry and rhubarb flavour, and the beer company Brewdog launching Clean & Press: "zero carbs, zero sugars and only 90 calories per can", it claims. Aldi is launching its own brand in a few weeks.

Jaded consumers may think this is just a reboot of the alcopops of the mid-Nineties, drinks that managed to get a generation of teenagers drunk while dancing to the Happy Mondays. These, the manufacturers insist, are a notch above. Are they? Harry Wallop Bodega Bay elderflower, lemon and mint, 250ml, 4 per cent ABV, 72 cal If I had to choose an ice-cold hard seltzer to refresh me after an early evening's game of tennis, I'd probably plump for this one. I'm not really getting elderflower, just generic citrus.

But it's not unpleasant.

I'd still prefer a beer, mind you. *** Sparkl lemon, ginger and chilli, 330ml, 4 per cent ABV, 80 cal This is almost very nice; the ginger is fresh and dry like a good ginger ale. But to limit this to a mere 80 calories in a 330ml bottle it has resorted to using stevia, the alternative sweetener, leaving an annoying aftertaste. ***(( Two Brooks Passion Star, 250ml, 4 per cent ABV, 80 cal This wins points for describing itself as "alcoholic sparkling water" rather than the Americanised "hard seltzer", but it tastes like very watered-down fruit juice.

Without any kick. It's OK. ** Drty raspberry rosé 330ml, 4 per cent ABV, 83 cal This smells fresh and says it is derived from Bulgarian roséxtract. It's all downhill from there. It manages to be watery, but also make you feel as if your teeth are being stripped of their enamel.

** White Claw raspberry, 330ml 4.5 per cent ABV, 95 cal Really? This is the drink that launched a multibillion-dollar brand? It's really not that nice. It's meant to deliver a "surge of pure refreshment", but I think it tastes like the dregs of last night's cocktails, with the ice melted, the guests gone home and the ashtrays overflowing.

*

headline: Dominic Cummings —he mask that’ too big an ask
author: Robert Shrimsley
text:
How you wear your Covid-19 face covering could be the new social divide

It turns out that you can buy a Dominic Cummings face mask. It seems like a good bet. After all, the prime minister’ chief strategist seems to enjoy levels of immunity not available to ordinary mortals. Admittedly, it is immunity from the consequences of his actions rather than from the virus itself, but I think an image of Dom over my face has got to be some sort of deterrent.

The search for face-coverings has taken a disproportionate time in our household, but then again it’ not as if there is somewhere we have to be. A quick browse online will show that you can pretty much buy any kind of wacky mask: Disney characters, works of art and all the Kennedys except for Ludovic. It may seem fun now but, trust me, this is not a joke that is going to last. These are the equivalent of novelty socks except that they are on —nd in —our face.

Nonetheless, I sense a pretty wide social divide on the face masks issue. The smart, sanitary approach is paper masks, but what does that really say about you other than you live near a Boots. Then there are the home-made ones for those who want to master the “’ wearing a sock on my face”ook. Cyclists will already possess a useful anti-pollution mask with just a hint of Darth Vader, though even these come second to the N-95 “e’e taking this seriously”asks. Even so, this year’ must-have mask is a lightly patterned number, elegant but understated, with enough style to show you care but not enough to suggest you care too much.

But as we ready ourselves for a more normality outside, it is life inside that is getting more difficult. The easing of lockdown was meant to make things, well, easier. If anything however, it is intensifying the turmoil.

Like many of those fortunate enough for lockdown to be a nuisance rather than a trauma we have been content to follow official advice and even lag a little behind it. This however has created a fierce domestic divide which pits tremulous boomers against Gen Z and it is running hot. The spawn have internalised the Black Lives Matter movement and all I’ saying is that if there were statues of the adults in our home they would have been boarded up for their own protection by now.

For 12 weeks we coexisted manageably. The spawn were both at home but the lockdown rules were sufficiently strict that the scope for conflict was minimised. They have known ever since we first yelled “tay away from the dog”uring a visit to a country with rabies that their parents took a risk averse approach to killer viruses. What is different is that normally we are worrying about them rather than us. But as lockdown restrictions eased they became more aware of their oppression.

It has been hardest for the boy. Returning from the freedom of university, he has borne with fortitude the burden of being unable to blast out music in the bathroom during his 2am shower. But now the bonds are chafing and forbidden meetings are the field of conflict. His first gambit was to declare that we had already had Covid-19. It is true that my wife lost her sense of smell for some weeks, but if you are stuck in the same house for three months that’ surely nothing more than Darwinism. Anyway without reliable antibody tests we were reluctant to go with the medical opinion of a history student.

His next push was to declare that lockdown was “asically over” No one was observing it any more and we should just chill. This of course was not reassuring. It is not so much that we worry about them going out, more that we worry about them coming back. he girl followed suit soon after. She knew the virus was deadly, but then again, BrewDog.

They haven’ exactly chosen orphandom over a ruined summer so much as it suits them not to accept the premise. They are trying to accommodate our risk aversion. They carry hand wash, try to social distance and never leave home without a Dom Cummings face mask. But the odd tactical flare-up keeps us honest.

It is obviously miserable for them, though not as miserable as the broader economic fate awaiting their generation. No wonder they want to party while they can. Then again it is their fault for having us.

headline: ROBERT SHRIMSLEY THE NATIONAL CONVERSATION
text:
The face mask that's too big an ask It turns out that you can buy a Dominic Cummings face mask. It seems like a good bet. After all, the prime minister's chief strategist seems to enjoy levels of immunity not available to ordinary mortals. Admittedly, it is immunity from the consequences of his actions rather than from the virus itself but I think an image of Dom over my face has got to be some sort of deterrent.

The search for face coverings has taken a disproportionate time in our household but, then again, it's not as if there is somewhere we have to be. A quick browse online will show that you can pretty much buy any kind of wacky mask: Disney characters, works of art and all the Kennedys except for Ludovic. It may seem fun now but, trust me, this is not a joke that is going to last. These are the equivalent of novelty socks except that they are on - and in - your face.

Nonetheless, I sense a pretty wide social divide on the facemask issue. The smart, sanitary approach is paper masks but what does that really say about you other than you live near a Boots? Then there are the home-made ones for those who want to master the "I'm wearing a sock on my face" look. Cyclists will already possess a useful anti-pollution mask with just a hint of Darth Vader, though even these come second to the N95 "we're taking this seriously" masks. Even so, this year's must-have mask is a lightly patterned number, elegant but understated, with enough style to show you care but not enough to suggest you care too much.

But as we ready ourselves for more normality outside, it is life inside that is getting more difficult. The easing of lockdown was meant to make things, well, easier. If anything however, it is intensifying the turmoil.

Like many of those fortunate enough for lockdown to be a nuisance rather than a trauma, we have been content to follow official advice and even lag a little behind it. This however has created a fierce domestic divide which pits tremulous boomers against Gen Z and it is running hot. The spawn have internalised the Black Lives Matter movement and all I'm saying is that if there were statues of the adults in our home they would have been boarded up for their own protection by now.

For 12 weeks we co-existed manageably. The spawn were both at home but the lockdown rules were sufficiently strict that the scope for conflict was minimised. They have known ever since we first yelled "stay away from the dog" during a visit to a country with rabies that their parents took a riskaverse approach to killer viruses. What is different is that normally we are worrying about them rather than us. But as lockdown restrictions eased, they became more aware of their oppression.

It has been hardest for the boy. Returning from the freedom of university, he has borne with fortitude the burden of being unable to blast out music in the bathroom during his 2am shower. But now the bonds are chafing and forbidden meetings are the field of conflict. His first gambit was to declare that we had already had Covid-19. It is true that my wife lost her sense of smell for some weeks, but if you are stuck in the same house for three months that's surely nothing more than Darwinism. Anyway, without reliable antibody tests we were reluctant to go with the medical opinion of a history student.

His next push was to declare that lockdown was "basically over". No one was observing it any more and we should just chill. This of course was not reassuring. It is not so much that we worry about them going out, more that we worry about them coming back. The girl followed suit soon after. She knew the virus was deadly, but then again, BrewDog.

They haven't exactly chosen orphandom over a ruined summer so much as it suits them not to accept the premise. They are trying to accommodate our risk aversion. They carry hand sanitiser, try to social distance and never leave home without a Dom Cummings face mask. But the odd tactical flare-up keeps us honest.

It is obviously miserable for them, though not as miserable as the broader economic fate awaiting their generation. No wonder they want to party while they can. Then again, it is their fault for having us.

robert.shrimsley@ft.com @robertshrimsley

headline: Raise your glass but remember pubs are infection hotspots
author: Chris Smyth
text:
Pubs are "super-spreading" hotspots implicated in hundreds of Covid-19 outbreaks around the world, and drinkers must be careful as they reopen, the chief scientific adviser has warned.

Sir Patrick Vallance said that "there's no question" Britain will see more local outbreaks with the easing of restrictions, as Boris Johnson presented a fivepoint plan to avoid other cities following Leicester into local lockdown. However, Sir Patrick said there was no sign yet that Black Lives Matter protests in recent weeks and the crowding of beaches had led to the country experiencing an increase in infections. "It doesn't mean we won't, but there's been nothing that's been seen yet. And the same is true, actually, in some other countries where outdoor events are taking place."

Pubs in England were permitted to open from 6am today after Downing Street acted to prevent midnight parties last night. The pub chain Brewdog cancelled openings planned for a minute after midnight after ministers took what No 10 described as a "sensible" precaution to delay opening. However, premises will only be able to open at 6am if they have existing licences to do so and many were expected to open for breakfast rather than alcohol sales.

A "rave clause" allowing police to break up gatherings of 30 or more has been included in regulations signed yesterday by Matt Hancock, the health secretary, which came into effect at midnight. Rules limiting outdoor gatherings to six people and indoor ones to members of two households will be guidance rather than legally enforceable.

Mr Johnson has urged people not to "blow it now" by giving up on social distancing. He said that the Leicester lockdown has shown that Britain is "not out of the woods" and that pubs and restaurants will be shut down again if cases surge. "Anyone who flouts social distancing and Covid-secure rules is not only putting us all at risk but letting down those businesses and workers who have done so much to prepare for this new normal," he told the Downing Street press briefing.

"The success of these businesses, the livelihoods of those who rely on them, and ultimately the economic health of the whole country is dependent on every single one of us acting responsibly. We must not let them down." He said his approach would be first to monitor case numbers and local behaviour, then to warn local communities to try to deal with clusters, then to send in more testing capacity to find out where people were catching the virus, and then to close down institutions like schools, hospitals or factories that proved to be sources of infection. Leicester-style local lockdowns would be "a last resort", he said Official figures published yesterday said that the Scientific Advisory Group on Emergencies could not be sure that the R rate, the reproductive rate of the virus, was below one in London. Although this is likely to be the case, as cases are low and officials are not too concerned, Sir Patrick added: "There will be an increase in local outbreaks, there's no question about that."

headline: Best fish and chips in the capital? Yes. Best in Scotland? Probably
text:
The Fishmarket is a joyful collaboration between Edinburgh's kings of the North Sea, says Chitra Ramaswamy

We are in a time of transition from lockdown to something approaching the new normal. What this means shifts like the seas. Some are thrilled to return to restaurants: to raise a glass with someone they love, to order food from a menu, to eat together again. Others are still looking for compromise: the pint ordered from the pub doorway, the takeaway eaten in the park and, perhaps most iconically of all, the fish supper by the sea.

And so I wipe down a rain-spattered bench overlooking the austere splendour of Newhaven Harbour in high summer, then lift the lid on a cardboard box to allow the time-honoured scent of battered haddock, chips and the glossy tang of chippy sauce to be hoisted into the air by the wind that rolls off the Firth of Forth come what may. Some things don't change, thank goodness. Fish and chips, inhaled fast and loose on an ancient harbour front in a drizzle, beneath giant northern skies and the belligerent glare of herring gulls, are as good as ever. No, better.

I ordered our food and drink five hours earlier from the Fishmarket, a collaboration between Edinburgh's kings of the North Sea. Roy Brett, the chef-patron of Ondine, and Gary Welch, the owner of Welch Fishmongers, are a match as divinely ordained as, well, fish and chips. The Fishmarket is running the kind of efficient online collection service you would expect from a restaurant/takeaway, open 9am to 10pm seven days a week, serving the best fish and chips in the capital. Scratch that: it is a contender for the best in Scotland.

We arrive en famille, dog included, at the appointed time: 5.30pm on Friday. Along the old wooden pier, restaurant and fishmonger stand side by side, watching over the boats that bring in the daily catch. In the olden days of 2019 I could regularly be found nipping into Welch's to buy dressed haddock, smoked mackerel, and, if I was really pushing the boat out, six scallops, then grabbing a takeaway fish supper next door. This was Edinburgh at its best: old and new, egalitarian and staggeringly beautiful.

I put on my mask as we approach the pier, where about 20 people are waiting diligently outside for their name to be called kind of like Argos, with salt and sauce. Someone asks me what the difference is between a single and a supper; my first 2020 Edinburgh tourist encounter! The rain comes on again, a smirr as fresh and light as a spa treatment. We collect our giant brown paper bag of goodies, which includes bottles of beer and apple juice for the children, and head for a bench. The other benches, conveniently socially distanced along the harbour front, are taken by folk with the same idea.

What carries good fish and chips on to the crest of greatness is the best batter (and fish, and chips). The Fishmarket's is so light and brisk it barely coats my daughter's haddock: you can actually see the pearly white of the fish through it in places. There is crispness rather than the brittle crunch that really just means greasiness. Everything is outrageously fresh and cooked with the technical precision of a Michelin-starred restaurant. The chips are all sizes and shapes; some are soft and yielding, others little shards of joy. My partner Claire's crispy monkfish is made up of four small blocks of this uniquely meaty and delicately flavoured fish, expertly filleted and deep-fried in that sublime batter. We declare it a disaster; how will we ever go back to haddock? There is also crispy squid, all softness and no bounce, dipped into a punchy Vietnamese sauce that has Ondine written all over it.

And I've gone a bit mad and ordered a lobster supper. At £2 it's simultaneously crazy expensive for fish and chips and astonishingly cheap for lobster. Put it this way: I'm expecting half. What I get is a whole crustacean, generously and sustainably sized, bathed in nothing but herb-spiked garlic butter, with a side of thick, wobbly aioli. The setting could not be more harmonious for such an occasion, in a time when we are all making promises not to take anything in life for granted. The gulls wheel and laugh in the overcast skies. The boats bob in the old harbour. Never have I eaten lobster like this: out of a plastic container, with a wooden fork, in the rain, overlooking the choppy sea whence it came. Never will I forget it.

The Fishmarket, 23A Pier Place, Newhaven, Edinburgh EH6 4LP (0131 552 8262, thefishmarketnewhaven.co.uk)

How it rated Food 9 We ate Total £4.10 Scottish lobster supper Brewdog Nanny State beer Schiehallion beer Garlic mayo sauce Vietnamese dipping sauce Children's haddock, chips and peas Crispy monkfish supper Crispy squid single We drank 2 x apple and pear juice

headline: Seltzers: my top tip for picnics
author: Jane MacQuitty
text:
Have you come across hard seltzers yet? You will soon. This sparkling alcoholic water, made with a spirit, wine, cider or malt base, enhanced by a dash of fruit, is destined to be the drink of the summer.

With about 5 per cent alcohol and a low sugar content, seltzers make a useful, more health-conscious summer slurp, having fewer calories than wine or beer. In lightweight 25cl cans, they are handy for a picnic basket as well.

Spiked seltzer popped up in the US in 2013, but it was last year that sales there rocketed, with new brands launched every week. By 2021 hard seltzer, the fastest-growing drinks category in the US, is expected to be worth $2.5billion a year.

It's surprising, then, how long it has taken hard seltzers to make it to the UK. Last summer Bodega Bay was the first homegrown edition to hit our shelves, and at Morrisons and Sainsbury's sales are 25 per cent ahead of predictions. White Claw, the big American brand, followed, and its best offering, Natural Lime, at 4.5 per cent (33cl can, Morrisons, £; Tesco, £.50), will no doubt clean up here as the mercury rises.

Cider companies have been quick to jump on the bandwagon. Aldi is this month launching the Nordic Wolf quartet of cans in association with the Swedish Cider Company. Kopparberg's 5 per cent 33cl cans are everywhere, and may have beaten them to it, but it's only sweet-tooths who will enjoy them. And I wasn't that taken with Brewdog's Clean & Press 5 per cent hard seltzers; a pity since I love some of its beers.

Of the 40-plus hard seltzers I tasted for this article, the best were citrus-led, with lime and grapefruit the big success stories. The gin and vodka-based ones were cleaner and more refreshing than the beer-derived malt-based versions, which often tasted grubby or too watery.

As the summer holiday months kick off, look out for Served Hard Seltzer Lime, a 4 per cent salty lime-lick hit (25cl can, selfridges.com, £.99), and Mike's Hard Seltzer Lime (33cl can, Tesco and Morrisons, £) —ike a 5 per cent alcoholic version of Rose's lime juice.

The gin-based seltzers to buy include Chase's Seville Orange Seltzer, a bitter orange-peel hit, and Chase's London Dry & Lemon, a tad frothy and weak, but with a delicate lemon finish, both 5 per cent (25cl can, chasedistillery.co.uk, £).

Two Brooks Lime Cooler Alcoholic Sparkling Water, 25cl, 4 per cent twobrooks.co.uk, £.50 This vegan-approved fizzy lime-twist and mint spritzer has a pleasing peppermint finish.

Smirnoff Seltzer Orange & Grapefruit, 25cl, 4.7 per cent Asda, £.70 With a punchy orange and grapefruit burst, it's Smirnoff's best vodka and sparkling water combo.

Chase's Gin Seltzer Pink Grapefruit & Pomelo, 25cl, 5 per cent chasedistillery.co.uk, £ I liked all three of Chase's seltzers, but this one's tangy double grapefruit hit was most refreshing.

Peggy English Roséine Spritzer, 25cl, 5.5 per cent selfridges.com, £.49 Prettily packaged wine-based spritzer with a mint, rose and lavender kick.

2018 Exquisite Collection South Australia Shiraz, 14 per cent Aldi, £.79 Terrific, tub-thumping, creamy cassis and blackberry-sweet shiraz. Barbecued bangers and burgers love this.

2019 Chapel Down Flint Dry, Tenterden, England, 12 per cent Booths, £2.50, down to £.50 tomorrow Chardonnay-dominant, topped up with bacchus — tart, zingy, hedgerowscented triumph.

2017 Taste the Difference Gigondas, France, 14.5 per cent Sainsbury's, £4 (down from £5) Another barbecue-friendly summer red with tannic, inky, bramble and liquorice-scented fruit.

2018 Petit-Chablis, Hautéivien, France, 12.5 per cent leaandsandeman.co.uk, £0.75 The Pommiers' organic chablis are a joy, and even this steely, leafy petit chablis hits the heights.

Hard seltzers make a healthconscious summer slurp, having fewer calories than wine or beer

headline: Apology for not warning virus venues
author: Marc Horne
text:
Marc Horne Health officials in Aberdeen have apologised after they failed to contact venues before naming them as being linked to the city's coronavirus cluster.

NHS Grampian published a list on Wednesday featuring about 30 establishments in the city, including pubs, restaurants, golf clubs and a football social club. However, it failed to inform many of the owners before doing so.

The number of cases linked to the outbreak rose to 79 yesterday, with a further 30 cases under investigation.

The rapid escalation in positive tests led to the city becoming the first area in Scotland to have a local lockdown imposed on it on Wednesday. The Moonfish Cafe, a city centre bistro, was named on the list, but its owner, Brian McLeish, insists that he was not made aware. "No one has been in touch with us," he said. "NHS Grampian has not been in touch and nobody has asked for information.

"I'm not saying they're wrong for imposing the lockdown but we do have all the information ready for them.

"It seems like poor communication." Brewdog's bar in the city's Castlegate was also on the affected list.

The venue manager said: "We have not been contacted by NHS Test and Protect or NHS Grampian regarding any links to the detected cases. We have a record of the guests who have visited our bar and will co-operate with Test and Protect when contacted for this information."

The manager added: "Our priority now is to ensure our team are safe and well. We are taking extra measures to deep-clean the bar."

NHS Grampian said: "We worked hard to communicate directly with those venues in advance of publishing the list and apologise unreservedly to those we did not reach.

"We know this has led to a great deal of anxiety for these businesses, their staff and their customers.

"We are ensuring this communication is taking place and appropriate support is being provided by ourselves and our local authority partners as appropriate."

headline: BrewDog  can help you find new dog
author: GORDON TAIT
text:
TV RICKY'S BID TO AID STRAYS

RICKY Gervais has teamed up with Brew-Dog to help find stray pooches a forever home using cans of beer.

The comedian and animal lover, 59, revealed he'd been in secret talks with the craft brewer to raise cash for animal charities.

Tins of 'Street Dog' which feature pics of homeless pooches are on sale with the profits going to Dogs on the Streets and All Dogs Matter.

Afterlife star Ricky revealed partner Jane Fallon, also 59, came up with the name.

He said: "I've been in secret talks with BrewDog.

"We chose a couple of charities and they decided to dedicate a beer called Street Dog which Jane came up with and all the profits go to homeless dogs and rehoming dogs. "Is that not amazing? What an amazing company they are."

Gervais, responsible for some of Britain's best comedies over the last two decades, said he would be happy if he's remembered for saving pooches. He said: "If that was my only legacy, I'd be happy with that sorting out homelessness for dogs. Dogs make me happy.

"Beer makes me happy. A beer that saves dogs makes me very happy."

BrewDog co-founder James Watt said: "We are always looking for ways to use our business to do good.

"We then decided to take it a step further and not only donate profits but give our pawsome four legged friends space on our cans to help them find forever homes."

lSTREET Dog is available to order in 12 packs of 330ml cans from the BrewDog website now.

gordon.tait@the-sun.co.uk

headline: Coronavirus crisis fuels interest in vertical farming
author: Mure Dickie in Invergowrie and Emiko Terazono in London
text:
Crops grown in 9m tall towers could point the way to the future of food production

The coronavirus pandemic has been a major worry for many British farmers, threatening access to agricultural labour and complicating international supply chains. But for proponents of indoor farming, the crisis has offered an opportunity.

David Farquhar, chief executive of technology developer Intelligent Growth Solutions, says the pandemic has prompted a spike in interest in ‘ertical farms’ where batches of crops can be individually watered, fed and lit using LED lights, allowing them to be grown year-round with minimal labour near their markets, regardless of local soil or weather conditions.

At the company’ demonstration farm in Invergowrie near the Scottish city of Dundee, trays of produce stacked in 9 metre-tall towers are managed remotely from seeding to packaging. Humans only need to enter the towers for occasional maintenance. “ou can run it entirely on robotics    ou probably need to go in once every six months,”r Farquhar said.

This higher productivity will have particular appeal to British farmers, who are facing a steep increase in costs because coronavirus restrictions have affected the arrival of seasonal workers from eastern Europe. Brexit may also make it more difficult to access labour from the bloc when the UK’ transition period expires at the end of the year.

Colin Campbell, chief executive of the James Hutton Institute, a research organisation that hosts the IGS farm and another vertical farming company, Liberty Produce, said the global food supply system had generally worked well during the pandemic. But he added that the crisis had highlighted worries about food safety and the risks of relying on seedlings or produce grown far away from where it is consumed.

“ovid-19 is making a lot of people rethink how we want to grow our food,”e said.

While agritech investors have been pouring millions of dollars into vertical farm start-ups in the US and Europe, Irving Fain, founder of US vertical farming firm Bowery Farming, said the pandemic had accelerated interest around the world. “he simplicity of the supply chain is extremely important,”r Fain said.

IGS sold its first vertical farm in July to an Aberdeenshire venture backed by a founder of craft beer business BrewDog. According to Mr Farquhar the company last year raised £m from three venture capital firms and the Scottish Investment Bank and could build a four-tower farm that would produce more than 80 tonnes of produce a year for around £.5m.

At the IGS facility in Invergowrie, each tray of produce has its own microclimate, with temperature differences of up to 6C between the trays immediately above or below. The growing tower is accessed through an air lock and plastic curtain barrier, removing the need for chemicals to control pests.

Despite the advantages, many vertical farm start-ups have struggled with the high initial investment for building the facilities as well as running costs that include electricity for lighting and ventilation. The sector has been littered with bankruptcies as companies struggling to remain competitive on price have run out of cash.

Niccolo Manzoni of Five Seasons Ventures, a Paris-based agritech venture capital firm, said locally-grown fruit and vegetables appealed to consumers. He added that producers would also welcome technology that could work in regions such as parts of the Middle East, where conventional farming was impossible, or areas in countries such as China that suffer from serious soil contamination.

Nevertheless, Mr Manzoni said the investment case for vertical farms suffered by comparison with open field production. “n an open field, you have the sun for free. In a vertical farm, the electricity bill is very high,”e said. “t only makes sense to operate a [vertical] farm where electricity is dirt cheap or a place where energy is being heavily subsidised.”

But Graeme Warren, chief executive of Aberdeenshire-based Vertegrow, said the company expected the four-tower facility that will be built for it by IGS this year to quickly start paying for itself.

“hen the facility is fully operational and the four towers are operating at capacity, then it should be profitable at that point,”r Warren said. “e wouldn’ be going into this project if we didn’ think we would be cash positive in the not so distant future.”

Because of the relatively high costs of vertical farms, it makes sense to grow high-margin crops rather than commodities like bulk grains or cereals. Martin Dickie, founder of Vertegrow and co-founder of BrewDog, said the new facility would grow leafy greens, baby leaves like pak choi, and herbs such as basil and coriander using only rainwater and without pesticides.

“ith Covid, people are really focused on healthy lifestyles, and focused on more sustainable plant based diets, and they want to make sure what they are putting into their bodies are spray-free,”r Dickie said.

The green credentials of vertical farming are undermined by the need for artificial lighting, particularly if the electricity is generated from fossil fuels. But Prof Campbell said this was offset by the reduced need for transport and a reduction in food waste, because crops were of more uniform quality and supply could be matched closely to demand.

And, he added, “here is likely to be reduced greenhouse gas emissions, because of reduced fertiliser use”

“Vertical farming] appears to be very disruptive in the true sense of the word, because it is making people change their ideas of what the food system should be like.”

This article has been amended to corrected the spelling of Mr Fain’ name

headline: Memorial f-word ad
text:
A BREWERY has come under fire after an advert hoarding saying "f*** you" was placed next to a city's cenotaph.

Councillor Thomas Kerr said families of the fallen heroes could be upset by the BrewDog campaign.

The billboard, at Glasgow's George Square, is part of the firm's bid to reduce carbon footprint, stating: "F*** you CO2."

Mr Kerr said: "The language used clearly has potential to cause offence."

BrewDog, of Ellon, Aberdeenshire, was approached for comment.

headline: Risk of failure is the key to success in tech start-ups Ben Spigel
text:
Anew phase in Scotland's entrepreneurial ecosystem appears to be opening up thanks to the Scottish government accepting suggestions from a businessman who played a central role in one of the country's great tech economy successes.

The Logan Report laid out a road map for supporting the digital economy with the goal of producing more unicorn entrepreneurial firms that grow quickly to be worth more than £ billion. Building globally competitive technology firms is a key part of Scotland's broader economic development efforts, especially now as the Covid pandemic and global decarbonisation efforts will shrink the oil and gas sector and potentially the tourism market as well.

The first tangible outcome of this report, written by Mark Logan, the former chief operating officer with the Skyscanner flight price comparison site, has been the investment of £ million to support five new technology hubs. But do these investments represent a new approach or are they the simply the next iteration of other failed schemes to build a gleaming Silicon Glen? Ironically, if the government wants to succeed at supporting entrepreneurs it must get better at failing. Too often, ambitious plans to support entrepreneurs in new industries have been hamstrung by the need to show high performance by the next election. These programmes try to avoid failures that end up on the front page or are raised in first minister's questions.

But entrepreneurship requires risk, and so does its support. The Logan Report discussed the need for more risk-tolerant policy and it's important that this recommendation is followed. We must be prepared for the failure of the firms these new technology hubs support, and indeed for the hubs themselves to fail. This isn't to say that we should tolerate profligate spending of public money, but instead that the government and support systems need to tolerate risk and accept that the true value of these projects may not be visible for a decade or more.

One of the great shifts this would allow is for the support for technology entrepreneurship to move beyond what we normally consider technology. Some of the biggest recent successes in the Scottish entrepreneurial community have been non-tech firms such as BrewDog and Genius Foods. Because these types of firms require more up-front investment they are riskier, but they also have the potential to create a far larger economic impact. But is the Scottish government willing to take the risk? Ben Spigel is a senior lecturer in entrepreneurship at the University of Edinburgh Business School
