headline: Beer firm Elvis KO
text:
BEER firm BrewDog has lost a legal battle with Elvis Presley's estate over the name of an ale.

It was barred from registering BrewDog Elvis Juice as a European trademark over claims it breached branding rights relating to the late singer.

The EU Intellectual Property Office ruled: "The applicant's submission based on the peaceful coexistence of the marks cannot succeed."

BrewDog won an earlier battle with Elvis Presley Enterprises on UK rights.

Founders Martin Dickie and James Watt changed their names to Elvis to prove it wasn't exclusive.

The Aberdeenshire firm must pay £,068 costs.

headline: Brewdog  loses trademark row with Presley estate over 'Elvis Juice' beer
author: Stuart MacDonald
text:
Brewdog has lost a legal battle with Elvis Presley's estate over the name of one of its bestselling beers.

The company launched its grapefruit and orange flavoured Brewdog Elvis Juice IPA in 2015 and it now has annual sales of £.4 million in the UK alone.

Brewdog was contacted by lawyers from the singer's estate who demanded they change the name of the drink.

The craft brewing company, based in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, won the right to register the name Brewdog Elvis Juice as a trademark in the UK in 2018.

However, it has been denied exclusive rights to the name in Europe, potentially threatening sales on the Continent.

Although Presley died in 1977, his name and likeness have been trademarked by Elvis Presley Enterprises (EPE), which earns millions of dollars every year through a licensing programme that grants the right to manufacture and sell Elvis Presley merchandise worldwide.

EPE objected after Brewdog applied to register the drink name as a trademark for beers to be sold in European countries. The estate said the application overlapped with its registered trademark "Elvis" and that people could mistakenly believe that the beer was endorsed by it.

The EU Intellectual Property Office (EUIPO), which rules on trademark disputes, has found in favour of the Presley estate and said the trademark cannot be registered.

The EUIPO said: "It is clear that the applicant's submission based on the peaceful co-existence of the marks cannot succeed ... No conclusions about a lack of a likelihood of confusion in the market may be drawn from evidence that the applicant markets products bearing the mark in various commercial places."

It added: "The board has found that 'Elvis', which is inherently distinctive, is entirely reproduced in the contested sign and is the most distinctive element therein. There exists a likelihood of confusion." After the row began in 2016, James Watt and Martin Dickie, founders of Brewdog, changed their names by deed poll to Elvis in an effort to prove the name was not exclusive. They insisted that their beer had no connection to the rock-and-roll star and suggested that there ought to be "a little less conversation and more time enjoying our beer".

The latest ruling means that Brewdog may have to alter the name or apply to the Presley estate for official permission to use it, although it can still appeal to the EU's General Court. Brewdog has been ordered to pay £,068 costs in the case. In evidence to the EUIPO, Brewdog said Elvis Juice was the third most popular craft beer in the UK and famous in its own right, adding that the trademarks were "very different" as the drink name contained the additional words "Brewdog" and "Juice".

Mr Watt and Mr Dickie, who were at school together in Aberdeenshire, started Brewdog in 2007 with a bank loan and a grant from the Prince's Trust. The company has been valued at up to £ billion.

Brewdog was approached for comment.

headline: How the Granite City built a reputation for cool charm
text:
Aberdeen is enjoying a renaissance, thanks in part to a £5 million facelift for the city's Victorian art gallery and a thriving festival scene. Marc Horne breathes it all in

The past few months have not been plain sailing for Aberdeen, a city with salt in its veins and granite at its core.

Last month it was dragooned into a strict three-week lockdown but true to its proud maritime heritage, it weathered the storm and is open for business again.

For much of the past century Aberdeen was a thriving holiday resort. The author of a city guidebook from 1950, who clearly did not get the memo that Aberdonians are meant to be happier sounding a foghorn than blowing their own trumpet, wrote: "A poem in granite, a translucent pearl set in platinum, a visit to the silver city with the golden sands will produce a feeling of exhilaration seldom experienced!" Carefree holidaymakers frolicked in the North Sea, but in the 1970s, knotted handkerchiefs were replaced by Texan stetsons as oil was discovered off the coast and it became an unlikely boom town. The arrival of oil men and their generous expense accounts meant tourism was all but forgotten. Now Aberdonians are dusting down the welcome mat again. This time, however, Europe's oil capital is marketing itself as a cultural powerhouse.

At the heart of this renaissance is the city's art gallery (free; www.aagm.co.uk), a grand Victorian treasure house that reopened late last year after a £5 million facelift. The number of items on display has trebled and there are 19 galleries, compared with the previous 11, as well as a stunning new roof terrace.

Highlights include works by Monet, Tracey Emin, Henry Moore, Francis Bacon and Gilbert & George, as well as Edwin Landseer's Flood in the Highlands, a work that fascinated and terrified me when I first saw it as a small boy. I was impressed by the V&A in Dundee, but Aberdeen's gallery is superior in almost every measure. It makes Glasgow's Gallery of Modern Art seem dowdy, neglected and provincial.

The displays continue throughout the city thanks to the success of the street art festival Nuart Aberdeen. On almost every corner the grey granite is adorned with towering images of unicorns, leopards and even marauding gulls. My favourite is Gallus Quines & Deeds Not Words, mosaics on St Nicholas Lane that lionise the city's female luminaries such as Annie Lennox, Emeli Sandénd the suffragette leader Caroline Phillips.

Nuart and Aberdeen's October comedy festival are in Covid hibernation this year, but the city still has festivals celebrating everything from jazz (March 12 to 24; aberdeenjazzfestival.com) and Granite Noir (February 20 to 23; granitenoir.com), to craft beer (February 20 to 24; aberdeencamra.org.uk) and dolphins (April dates and website TBC).

As for Aberdeen's dolphins the harbour shelters the largest bottlenosed dolphins on the planet Torry Battery is the best place to see them. Diners at the Silver Darling seafood restaurant overlooking the harbour (mains about £8; thesilverdarling.co.uk) also often see dolphins and seals from their tables.

A stone skim away is Footdee (pronounced Fittie), an eccentric and charming community of colourful and ramshackle fishermen's cottages. It's also the gateway to the beach with its amusement park and waterfront café serving lurid knickerbocker glories and rowies (aka butteries), a delicacy that has been described as a "roadkill croissant".

For more sophisticated dining, wind your way to Cafe 52 (mains £0; cafe52onthegreen.co.uk), a grotto-like city centre restaurant packed with charm and whimsical art, much of it created by the owner, Steven Bothwell. His 83-year-old mother, "Mrs B", creates each irresistible dessert from local ingredients, many grown in her garden.

History enthusiasts will be beguiled by the warren of medieval cobbles and cloisters, while hopheads will find a host of native craft brew bars including Fierce Beer, Six Degrees North and Brewdog, which launched its global empire in the city and opened six hipster rooms (aka Kennels) in November in Castlegate (B&B doubles from £5; brewdog.com).

In a handsome former university building between the art gallery and a glorious Edwardian theatre, the boutique Sandman Signature hotel pays homage to its host city with images of red squirrels, highland cows and seagulls in its lifts and a showpiece neon art installation stating "Gie's a bosie" (Aberdeen-speak for "give me a cuddle").

Lewis Grassic Gibbon, the author of Sunset Song, once likened life in Aberdeen to "passing one's existence in a refrigerator" but 21st-century visitors will be delighted to find that the writer was almost right: it's very cool indeed. Marc Horne was a guest of Visit Aberdeenshire and Sandman Signature Aberdeen (doubles from £7, B&B; sandmansignature.co.uk)

headline: Pubs with grub still serving in the capital

text:

headline: The home brewing kit that's as easy as a Nespresso
author: Phil Robinson
text:
My first attempt at home brewing was about five years ago, and it didn't end well. The idea was to make fizzy ginger beer, just without the benefit of equipment, a recipe or the right ingredients. I grated a kilo of ginger, scarified some lemons and poked all the mush into a vast mineral water bottle along with half a bag of sugar and enough baker's yeast to clear a drain. After topping it off with water, I set the hooch out on my back deck to mature, then promptly forgot all about it.

Three days later the bottle exploded, cannoning halffermented ginger spew all over next door's newly decorated extension. Their rabbit, whose hutch faced our fence like a soldier on the North Korean border, was collateral damage. The neighbours came home from work to find a seemingly binge-drunk bunny with a thousand-yard stare.

Well that was then. Now a Walthamstow-based start-up called the Greater Good Fresh Brewing Co has created the Pinter, a machine that threatens to simplify home brew in the same way Nespresso simplified coffee: no more nerdy pamphlets, tedious recipes or garages stacked with more equipment than the Unabomber. For £5 the company will send you a bit of kit that does everything from brewing to conditioning and makes ten pints in just seven days. Once the beer is ready, you clip on a tap and pour fresh draft beer direct from your fridge perfect for that post-pub 10pm lockdown drink-up with your pals. (I'm JOKING!) On unpacking the box, the first thing that strikes me is how well designed and simple the set-up is. The clutter I would normally associate with home brewing is absent there are no thermometers, tubes or bottles. The online video instructions are brief and the kit comes with two Pinter Packs, the ingredients you need to make 20 pints of booze. All that I require to finish the job is a sink, some running water and a fridge.

After tipping a small bottle of purifying granules into the Pinter and sloshing it around its internal organs I gave mine an extra rinse because some of the granules didn't appear to dissolve completely, and cleanliness is a vital factor in brewing the machine is prepped with one of a selection of the "fresh presses". These letterbox-size rectangular bottles can be ordered on a monthly delivery service (ten pints for £2!).

Each syrupy press be it cider or beer is the result of the complex process of malting, kilning, milling and mashing by the manufacturer's master brewer (he has an MSc in brewing science). I chose Public House IPA, which smells quite wonderful, like a brewery crossed with miso paste, and chugged the contents of the bottle into the Pinter. After this I added a small bottle of brewer's yeast and ten pints of water. The final step is to give the whole thing a shake. Sadly, doing this with ten pints is physically taxing enough to rule out the Pinter as a Christmas gift for the mother-in-law.

So how good is it? While waiting for my brew (I'm calling it Lockdown 2: Electric Boogaloo) I tried some bottled samples that I'd been sent by Pinter's manufacturer to demonstrate the quality of the range. Beers include Stars and Stripes, an American IPA; Craftwerk, a pilsner; and Fresh Republic, a continental lager.

The result was surprising. Stars and Stripes, an American-style pale ale, is a lively pint that is more than equal to the beer you find in most pubs. It's refreshing and citrusy without the aggressive tropical fruit hit you get from so many try-hard craft IPAs. I generally prefer something a little less cloudy, but this apparently happens if you leave your brew to condition a little longer. I look at my Pinter forlornly: only seven days to go.

After four days I release some pressure from the Pinter, attach the beer tap and make room in my fridge by clearing out a multitude of salad bags. After three more days of fridge conditioning the beer is finally ready to drink; it is dark and hoppy with a luxurious meringuelike head and drinks like Brewdog's Nanny State, just with added booze. Rather satisfactorily, it feels as if I have done nothing at all. The only thing I have to do now is convince the wife and kids to go without fresh food while I work my way through the other nine pints. thegreatergood.co.uk

headline: CROWDFUNDERS IN £40M TIE-UP
text:
TWO of Britain's biggest crowd-funding platforms are set to merge to create a £40m firm.

Crowdcube and Seedrs, which are both loss-making, allow entrepreneurs to raise money from the public by selling shares online.

Brewdog and Monzo bank started out on Crowdcube, while Revolut bank and Chapel Down wine raised money with Seedrs. But experts worry a merger could drive down competition. Oliver Kicks, of RLC Ventures, said: 'It leaves fewer choices for entrepreneurs'.

©aily Mail

headline: Two's company as Crowdcube and rival Seedrs agree to merger
author: James Hurley
text:
Britain's leading equity crowdfunding platforms are set to join forces after Crowdcube agreed a deal to acquire Seedrs.

The merger will allow the two lossmaking services to cut costs and, the companies said, to deliver new services to investors and companies.

Crowdcube and Seedrs pioneered the equity crowdfunding industry, which allows ordinary investors to buy shares in companies in their early stages for as little as £0. Since 2011, £ billion has been invested via campaigns on Crowdcube and Seedrs, with more than 1,500 companies securing funds, including Brewdog, the craft beer business, and Revolut, the financial services start-up that is now valued at more than $5 billion.

The platforms said that their planned merger, which is subject to regulatory approval, would create "one of the world's largest private equity marketplaces".

However, returns for crowdfunding investors generally have been disappointing and both platforms have faced questions about due diligence standards, inaccurate claims and optimistic valuations from fundraising businesses, as well as allegations of poor treatment of investors.

Crowdcube made a pre-tax loss of £.6 million in 2019 and has built up accumulated losses of £3.1 million. In 2018, the most recent year for which accounts are available, Seedrs made a pre-tax loss of £.3 million, with accumulated losses of £5.2 million. Seedrs also has warned that the number of fundraising campaigns going live and the volumes of investments have slowed as a result of the Covid-19 pandemic.

The difficulties that the platforms have had in proving the viability of the model is reflected in their modest valuations, with Crowdcube valued at £4 million and Seedrs at £6 million at their latest fundraising rounds.

The merger will be structured as an acquisition by Crowdcube of all the outstanding share capital of Seedrs Limited via a scheme of arrangement, a deal structure often used in takeovers and company debt restructurings. Existing Crowdcube shareholders will own 60 per cent of the combined company, with the remainder owned by backers of Seedrs.

Darren Westlake, 51, chief executive of Crowdcube, said: "Equity crowdfunding has redefined how many ambitious businesses raise investment and engage with their customers."

Jeff Kelisky, 55, chief executive of Seedrs, said: "We believe that you need to be a player of greater scale to serve companies and the investors who support them."

Mr Kelisky will be chief executive of the combined company and Mr Westlake will be its executive chairman. Rob Murray Brown, 60, a critic of standards in the industry, said that the merger would merely extend the life of the platforms but would not alter the fundamentals. "They are in a sense a reflection of so many of the zombies they help to fund," he said.

One industry insider said that the merger made sense as it may help to stem losses, but it would not on its own tackle problems with the business model, including a lack of quality among fundraising companies.

However, David Brear, 39, chief executive of 11:FS, a financial technology consultancy, said: "My hope would be that this merger could lead to a really interesting secondary market for pre-[flotation] companies."

The transaction is subject to approval by competition and financial regulators, as well as shareholders.

headline: Enjoy a pizza
text:
MARGHERITA PERONI Peroni Nastro Azzurro, £.50 at Tesco for 4 x 330ml bottles "A classic Italian pizza and you don't want to overpower the taste with your beer.

Peroni Nastro Azzurro is crisp and clean. It uses malt as an ingredient that makes the beer taste soft and a bit sweet. The sweetness will contrast perfectly with the saltiness of the cheese on the pizza. If you prefer, the alcohol-free Peroni Libera would work just as well."

ast e er, FLORENTINE ERDINGER WEISSBRAU Erdinger Weissbrau, £.75 for a 500ml bottle at Morrisons "A Florentine pizza is topped with egg and spinach. All the flavours are very delicate. German wheat beer Erdinger is really creamy with a mousse-like froth and there's very little bitterness. The Erdinger will not overpower the delicate flavours of the pizza. It's a beer for people who say they 'don't like beer'."

AMERICAN SPUTNIK PALE ALE North Brewing Sputnik American Pale Ale, £ at Tesco for 4 x 330ml cans "An American pizza is usually covered with Pepperoni. If you pair this with a standard lager, the flavour of the beer will get lost. The Sputnik has tropical fruit and citrus fruit aromas so it won't be drowned out by the meat. Pepperoni is quite fatty but the abundance of hops in the Sputnik will slice right through. A perfect pairing."

MEAT FEAST INNIS & GUNN ORIGINAL Innis & Gunn Original, £.80 at Tesco for one 330ml bottle "This is one of very few beers that will work well with heavy flavoured pizza toppings. It's a brew that is fullbodied and aged in oak barrels, which gives it a lovely smoky flavour. This will complement the smokiness of the meat on your pizza, giving your taste buds a real meaty treat."

SPICY BEEF BREWDOG PUNK IPA BrewDog Punk IPA, £ at Sainsbury's for four 330ml cans "You can really quench the heat of a spicy meat pizza if you choose a beer packed full of citrus and tropical fruits. The citrus hops in the BrewDog Punk IPA cut through the chilli heat of the pizza, cleansing your palate in readiness for the next mouthful."

HAM & MUSHROOM WESTMALLE DUBBEL Westmalle Dubbel, £.20 at Waitrose for a 330ml bottle "Mushrooms have deep, earthy flavours plus you have the saltiness of the ham. Westmalle Dubbel is rich, velvety and packed with fruit cake-like flavours. The earthiness and the saltiness of the ham and mushrooms contrast with this wonderful beer. On your tongue they'll provide a really unique flavour combination."

HAWAIIAN BEAVERTOWN PALE ALE Beavertown Gamma Ray American Pale Ale, £.25 at Sainsbury's for one 330ml can "If you like pineapple on your pizza, you need a really fruity beer to bring out the flavour. This is punchy, tropical and juicy and perfectly complements the sweet and salty combination you find on a Hawaiian pizza."

d" BBQ CHICKEN BROOKLYN LAGER Brooklyn Lager, £.60 at Asda for one 660ml bottle "Brooklyn Lager contains lots of toffee and malt, giving it a sweet flavour. I would describe it as having almost Coca Cola-like flavours, which really complement rich, smoky barbecue foods. Perfect if your favourite pizza has a barbecue sauce on the base."

SEAFOOD HITACHINO NEST WHITE ALE Hitachino Nest White Ale, £.10 at Waitrose for a 330ml bottle "Cut through the oily tuna without overpowering the flavour of prawns by matching this pizza with a spritzy white ale. Hitachino Nest White offers subtle citrus, nutmeg and orange peel notes which act like a squeeze of lemon on the fish. A superb beer that will complement the delicate seafood tastes."

headline: Drink to the success of smart ideas
text:
Shaf Rasul One in a million shaf@the-sun.co.uk

IT has been an interesting few weeks for the Scottish drinks industry.

While it's undoubtedly negative for the on-trade sector with the Scottish Government's ban on selling booze indoors in pubs and restaurants, there has been a lot of positive news from the off-trade industry, with a number of major commercial investments taking place and two in particular caught my eye.

Just last week, I was incredibly heartened to see the Scottish Borders Council approving plans for two whisky distilleries to be built as part of a £0million complex in the region.

The development, which won unanimous approval for a second time after permissions had previously lapsed, will be based at the former Jedforest Hotel, just south of Jedburgh.

It will not only reinvent the site but, according to developers, hopefully create up to 70 full-time jobs in the area.

Proposed work on the new complex is set to be carried out in two phases with a small distillery constructed first, which will boast a visitor centre, while a larger-scale factory capable of producing millions of bottles every year would be completed at a later date.

The transformation of the site into a new state-of-the-art distillery and tourist centre would certainly bring new investment and employment opportunities to the area for many years to come, and this type of economic boost, especially during these difficult times, should be celebrated.

Unemployment figures are undoubtedly set to rise in the coming months. With rural areas in Scotland already feeling the pinch due to a decline in population, as people continue to leave more remote areas and move to cities for work, a project like this will certainly help an already fragile local economy.

I've touched on rural diversification in the past, with farm shops and dining destinations popping up in parts of the country and attracting more visitors to smaller towns and villages who don't normally see much tourist traffic.

Although on a much larger scale, this is another fine example of how these smaller communities can adapt to an ever-changing world and ensure their prosperity for years to come.

It comes as no surprise then that the plans were unanimously voted through, as I am sure that will have been in the back of the councillors' minds when weighing up the submitted proposal.

It's great to see this forwardfor thinking development being proposed, especially during times like these where the Scottish economy (not just in rural locations, but in our big cities too) could take years to recover due to the impact of coronavirus.

It is not just economic performance that Scottish businesses and communities are looking to maximise either, with the news that BrewDog has bought more than 2,000 acres of farmland north of Loch Lomond.

As part of its new Make Earth Great Again plan to improve the company's environmental performance, Brew-Dog will plant one million trees on the site currently used animal grazing and produce one of the largest native woodlands created in the UK for many years.

When the Ellon-based brewer revealed its acquisition of the land a few weeks ago, it promised to dedicate 1,500 acres to native broadleaf trees, and the other 550 acres to peatland restoration, which would act as a natural carbon sink.

It's not the first foray into improving its environmental credentials for the brewing giant, which already employs a number of green measures including capturing CO2 from fermentation and using it to carbonate its beers, recycling brewing waste into dog food and powering production and pubs with wind power. However, with work set to start on the BrewDog Forest early in 2021 and planting to continue for a number of years, it is hoped that their ambitious new site in Scotland will help lower the carbon footprint considerably, reducing carbon emissions from the air.

I applaud the actions of BrewDog.

It is great to see a Scottish company not just paying lip service to its corporate social responsibility and role within society, but actually investing heavily in an innovative idea and new ways of working, which will make a real difference to the communities in which it operates.

headline: LIVING WAGE RISE
text:
MORE than a quarter of a million workers will get a 20p an hour pay rise today as the Living Wage is increased.

They will now get £.50 an hour nationally and £0.85 an hour in London 78p more than the Government minimum wage.

It is paid voluntarily by 7,000 UK employers including Tate and Lyle, Network Rail, Capital One, Brewdog and Everton FC.

The Living Wage Foundation said: "Today's new rates will give a boost to hundreds of thousands of workers including key and essential staff who have kept our economy going."

headline: 'Cook together? Lockdown has rendered us feral'
text:
Hannah Betts Ilove the idea that lockdown is bringing couples together in blissful culinary harmony, all romping about nymphs-andshepherds-style, before chucking a baby lambkin Marie-Antoinettishly on to the fire pit, then serving it with za'atar and yoghurt. It's so Jamie and Jools (Oliver), Joe and Rosie (Wicks), Terry and June.

Back in the real world, in which sanity must be preserved, day jobs clawed on to, my partner and I have gone the opposite way, barely crossing paths unless it's to demand that Terence remove his dirty mask from the worktop or eradicate the trail of soiled crockery, spilt tea and Twix wrappers he leaves in his wake. Think less John and the lovely Lisa, more Roald Dahl's The Twits.

Time was when we used to be cultured, adult, human, eating together with a degree of ceremony, all candles, napery and "How was your day, darling?" Tinkly laughter abounded; ditto political commentary.

We would be washed, dressed, scented, accessorised. One of us had plucked his nose. It was bonding, heartening; we communed. Now we snatch frantic carb hits over laptops, me in pyjamas, Terence in full care-in-the-community guise (pool slides and hole-ridden socks he nicked from a spa).

Lockdown has rendered us not epicures, but feral. Like decrepit teenagers, we eat radically different things at radically different times, never to be part of civilisation again. Rarely do we occupy the same time zone, let alone dining table.

Early bird/bore Tezzer has consumed brekker by 7am, "breakfast pudding" (Coco Pops) by 7.30. Lunch is at 11ish, evening bath with Brewdog aperitifs at 5.30pm, sundry suppers from 7pm, largely beige Co-op wares, followed by Ryvita and Lurpak, plus assorted Kitkatery. Menu-wise, it's the Seventies, only with craft beer and YouTube survival vids.

Meanwhile, in my capacity as a permanently pre-menstrual night owl, I breakfast at about 11am (kefir, fruit and cashews, or peanut butter on sourdough depending on my degree of angst). Lunch is a Ferrero Rocher with a squirt of vitamin D spray. I will then contrive some sort of vast sober, vegetarian supper at 5.30ish, before an evening spent working (that is, on Vestiaire Collective).

Close readers will observe that the only window this gives us is "brunch" that most abhorrent of millennial inventions in which he is knocking back lunch, while I am toying with the day's first meal. This seldom ends well. Terence will be firing on all cylinders, while I mew: "Be quiet/ I can't bear you/ It's over." Occasionally, he will invite me for espressos in his man cave. However, what sane individual wants to spend time with a management consultant, with or without pool slides? The only time he has cooked over the past few months was when we had a pal in our bubble over and Terence concocted a theatrically accomplished roast lunch, or "masculine stunt cookery" as I like to refer to it. Otherwise, it's every man for himself, apart from when I assail with a handheld Hoover, crumb-collecting as if my life depended on it (it does). Brace yourself, but the only time food brings us together is when we discuss the consistency of the dog's stool, aka "going through the motions".

Lockdown-wise, the sole occasion that unites us is bed. By this I refer not to sexual athleticism, but a sort of general domestic collapse that sees Terence quelled by earplugs and mask, me aromatherapy stoned, and the dog in her happy spot between our legs.

Our bed is as muddy as the Somme. It cannot be long before there is war poetry. At least there aren't crumbs.

We snatch frantic carb hits over laptops me in pyjamas

headline: Profits have a hangdog look as costs increase
author: James Hurley
text:
Profitability at Britain's largest craft brewer fell back last year, despite a 25 per cent rise in sales to £15 million, as its fast growth came at a high price.

Brewdog experienced an increase in overheads as it expanded its bar network in Britain and overseas, the Scottish company's latest accounts show. Group pre-tax profit was £.1 million, compared with a pre-tax loss of £00,000 in 2018, but the company only sneaked into the black thanks to its recording of a £4.2 million gain related to an acquisition. Operating profit was down compared with 2018 once the impact of the acquisition was stripped out.

Known for its outrageous publicity stunts and wide range of craft beers, Brewdog was founded in 2007 by James Watt and Martin Dickie. The Scottish company has expanded internationally to become the largest of a new wave of craft brewers. It is part-owned by TSG, a private equity firm.

Its Punk IPA brand is the most popular craft beer in the UK and it has more than 100 bars. The company said that it had shipped 183 million cans of beer last year, while sales at its bars grew by 48 per cent.

In the accounts, Mr Watt said that "slightly reduced" earnings "reflect our high growth rate and our global investments and infrastructure as we now have four major breweries globally".

He said that the business had weathered the Covid-19 storm "better than expected, with very strong grocery and online sales compensating for the temporary closures of our bars".

The accounts, which were signed off before a second lockdown for England was announced, record that the com-pany had enough liquidity to endure a "second pandemic wave that would impact the group" into 2021.

The accounts also show that Brewdog secured a £5 million loan under the government's coronavirus large business interruption loan scheme, which provides financial support for companies hit by the pandemic.

The accounts will raise fresh questions about the £.85 billion valuation the company priced itself at in a recent crowdfunding round, which is far more than comparable businesses.

Brewdog, which has more than 130,000 small investors via its "equity punks" crowdfundings, said that it had made and donated more than 500,000 units of hand sanitiser to the NHS, charities and key workers.

headline: Alsatian's £5k gold  BrewDog  prize find
author: ABBI GARTON
text:
A BEER lover last night joked that his pooch sniffed out a gold Brew-Dog can to win £5,000.

Kenny MacIntosh was confirmed the first winner of the Willy Wonka-style prize after his German Shepherd Maverick inspected his Punk IPA pack. He told how partner Rachel Arnott, 41, then found a can with a gold sticker as she unpacked the booze into the fridge.

Kenny, of Airth, Stirlingshire, yesterday traded the dummy tin for a £5,000 24-carat gold one.

He also bagged £0,000 in shares and a tour with founders James Watt and Martin Dickie at their site in Ellon, Aberdeenshire.

Kenny, 42, said: "Maverick was sniffing around the beer crate and I jokingly said, 'Have you sniffed out a gold can?' "I'm really chuffed." Nine more prize packs are still to be claimed.

headline: Couriers' 'hunt' for £5k gold cans fury
author: OLIVER NORTON
text:
DELIVERY drivers have been accused of tampering with boxes of beer in a bid to find a 24-carat golden can worth £5,000.

BrewDog customers claim couriers from DPD ripped open promotional 12 packs of ale in transit in the hunt for one of 10 Willy Wonka-style prizes.

One punter shared a photo of a torn pack of Punk IPA and wrote: "No attempt whatsoever to hide the blatant tampering of the box."

Another buyer added "Two of the four cases had been opened from both ends and the box was barely sealed."

DPD vowed to investigate. BrewDog also giving winners £0,000 of shares and tours of its brewery in Ellon, Aberdeenshire said it had "increased security across all deliveries".

headline: JET2 TOP FIRM FOR REFUNDS
text:
Edited by LISA MINOT & HEATHER LOWRIE

JET2 and Jet2holidays have been rated the best travel companies for providing refunds.

A survey found 89 per cent of their customers who had a break cancelled due to the pandemic had been given their money back in full.

Airbnb and British Airways Holidays were close behind with 87 per cent.

But when customers were asked about overall experience, including cancellations as well as refunds, the poll revealed a stark divide in how travellers are treated by different firms.

Only eight per cent of Lastminute.com customers said they had got their cash back after their holiday was cancelled, and the online travel retailer was almost at the bottom of the pile for net experience with a score of just -92 per cent.

news

Other wellknown names that fared badly included Jetline (-95 per cent), Opodo (-94 per cent) and Ryanair (-77 per cent).

Love Holidays' net experience score was also low at -68 per cent while Teletext Holidays was -45 per cent.

Travel Counsellors came top with 88 per cent, followed by Trailfinders, which scored 82 per cent.

Third place went to holiday park firm Haven Holidays with 78 per cent, while Hays Travel - the UK's largest independent travel retailer - took fourth with 77 per cent.

Jet2 and Jet2 Holidays were again rated highly, achieving net experience scores of 75 per cent and 76 per cent respectively.

The survey of 40,000 customers was carried out by cash tips website MoneySaving Expert.com.

headline: A QUICK ROUND OF SHOTS
author: CHRISTINE LAVELLE; CHRIS TAYLOR
text:
PLANS TO USE PUBS AS MASS VACCINATION HUBS

Brewers offer venues in Covid fight

Move could 'speed up' jab rollout

LOCKDOWN-hit pubs could be used as mass vaccination centres under plans being considered by Scottish Government officials, it emerged yesterday.

The prospect emerged after beer giant BrewDog offered its 12 Scots bars to the First Minister for free to help speed up the roll-out of vital Covid jabs.

And last night nightclub owner Donald MacLeod told how shuttered dance and music venues could also play a part in the lifesaving effort.

He said: "Any public space lying empty should be considered.

"There are pubs and clubs in every part of the country waiting to be used. The hospitality sector went to great pains to ensure venues were cleaned and sterilised to the highest standards to be ready for the short periods we've been allowed to open."

Mr MacLeod, owner of Glasgow's Garage and Cathouse clubs, added: "We are used to dealing with big volumes of people. Let's not be shy in rolling this out."

BrewDog chief executive James Watt tweeted his proposal to Ms Sturgeon and England's Health Secretary Matt Hancock.

He said: "We would like to offer our closed venues to help with a quick rollout of the vaccine. For free. We refrigerators, separate rooms for vaccinations and an ace team who can help organise. We want to help."

And the First Minister replied: "Thank you. I'll pass this on to our vaccination team."

Last night a spokeswoman for BrewDog ? founded in Fraserburgh, Aberdeenshire, in 2013 by Mr Watt and Martin Dickie ? said the firm was "excited to work with the Government to do all we can to help with a fast and effective vaccine rollout".

It comes as NHS bosses prepare to start giving the first doses of the Oxford/ AstraZeneca jab to Scots aged 80 and over from Monday.

Nats ministers have yet to publish a list of sites for mass vaccination centres. The UK Government announced plans to use sports stadiums and conference centres down south.

And SPFL chiefs previously said footie clubs were "ready, willing and able" to help out by offering all 42 Scots grounds to the NHS.

Yesterday Health Secretary Jeane Freeman said GP surgeries and pharmacies will be used to vaccinate the most elderly while "larger centres" will be used "as we go through into larger groups of people".

She said Scots would receive jabs round the clock, with 2,300 specialist staff expected to administer doses in the evenings and at weekends.

Ms Freeman told the BBC's Good Morning Scotland: "We've got 2,300 registered vaccinators.

"I'm delighted and hugely thankful to qualified NHS staff but also NHS contractors such as pharmacists and dentists and others who have come forward and volunteered to give us shifts in order to be able to do this.

"We want to be able to vaccinate not just during the day but over weekends and into the evening.

"People are being really enthusiastic, positive and helpful in wanting to be part of this major national effort."

However, when asked if her plan to vaccinate everyone over the age of 50 by spring was a "guarantee" or an "ambition", she replied: "It can't be a guarantee because it depends on supplies. But what I do know is that our planning is ready.

"Our modelling and the number of registered vaccinators is there. And, as long as the supplies of both vaccines continue to come through to us, then we are ready to deliver them to all those in that Joint Committee on Vaccinations and Immunisations priority list ? that is everyone over 50 in the spring.

"And we will get there as long as those supplies arrive. That is very good news because that is a group of people that experts have identified as most at risk of serious illness and death from Covid-19.

"It's really important we work as hard as we can to make sure we vaccinate those individuals as quickly as we can."

Around 44,000 doses of the Oxford/AstraZeneca vaccine are expected to arrive in Scotland next week.

The UK Government has ordered 100 million doses ? enough for 50 million people. It is the second jab to be approved in the UK but is cheaper than the Pfizer version to mass produce and can be stored in a regular fridge.

Scots Tory shadow health secretary Donald Cameron praised the "generosity" of the hospitality bosses in offering their venues.

He said: "Using different sites could be handy in speeding up vaccine distribution. But the priority is a thought-through plan, outlining first how vulnerable groups can get it."

Labour's Monica Lennon added: "The swift roll-out of the vaccine must be the Scottish Government's top priority.

"We need centres to be accessible, clean and safe. Offers of help from the business community and others should be welcomed."

christine.lavelle@the-sun.co.uk

headline: News; Front Page JABBY HOUR
author: CHRISTINE LAVELLE; CHRIS TAYLOR
text:
WE'RE SET FOR A LOCK-IN

Nic backs pubs & clubs vaccine rollout plan

SCOTS

SCOTS could soon be heading back to the pub ? to get a Covid jab.

Beer giant BrewDog and nightclub tycoon Donald MacLeod yesterday offered a host of locked-down venues across the country for the vaccine rollout.

They said bars are ideal as they can manage large numbers and have fridges to store the drug.

Nicola Sturgeon pledged: "I'll pass this on to our vaccination team."

Full Story ? Pages 4&5

headline: Pub cure brewing
text:
BREWDOG is in talks with the Government over using its closed pubs as vaccination hubs.

And it will offer anyone who gets the jab a jokily named special ale.

Owner of the Scotlandbased brewer, James Watt, tweeted Scottish First Minister Nicola Sturgeon and Health Secretary Matt Hancock with the offer.

Ms Sturgeon thanked the tycoon, whose boozers are safely shut.

Beer name suggestions include Little Prick, Vaccine Canteen and Jab Lab.

headline: Cheers to eco beer
text:
BEER bosses have been given the go-ahead to turn their lager leftovers into green energy.

BrewDog secured planning permission to build an anaerobic digestion plant to process liquid residue reducing the amount of harmful waste.

The site in Ellon is part of the Aberdeenshire firm's bid to become carbon neutral by 2022.

Co-founder Martin Dickie said: "The planning application is pivotal to significantly reduce our carbon release during the brewing of beer."

headline: Money Mail WILL FTSE FRESH BLOOD REVIVE YOUR RETURNS? AS MOONPIG AND DR MARTENS MAKE THEIR STOCK MARKET DEBUTS...
author: ROBERT JACKMAN
text:
After a quiet few years, the FTSE is set to welcome a wave of fresh blood.Alongside Dr Martens and Moonpig —oth of which joined the FTSE this week — number of multi-billion pound companies are preparing to 'go public'.

This will be the first time these companies will be open to retail investors.

Investors often get excited about IPOs initial public offerings), as they can be a chance to buy into a fast-growing company.

The IPO boom should also deliver a jolt to the wider FTSE, which has been flagging in recent ears.

So should you seize the opportunity to freshen up your portfolio? And can you get in before the crowd?

time for a change?

As many investors will know, the FTSE hasn't had a great run ately.

This is not a reflection of the UK economy. Indeed, many of the weakest performers are large multinationals with no connection to the British High Street.

Oil majors, mining companies and struggling banks have contributed to the FTSE 100's reputation as an outdated index.

To worsen matters, the number of companies joining the stock-market has ground to a halt.

Dynamic new companies —he likes of Pret A Manger, for example —ave instead raised money privately from rich investors.

That's bad news for everyday investors and pension funds, which can't benefit from their success. It is a problem for the FTSE too, which has become less popular with international investors.

But 2021 may prove to be the year that starts to change.

the new kids

The City of London is excited about a number of upcoming IPOs expected to take place this ear.

They include lockdown winner Deliveroo (estimated to be worth £ billion), Cambridge-based cyber experts Darktrace and craft beer pioneers Brewdog.

For Rob Burgeman, investment manager at Brewin Dolphin, the IPO frenzy is good news for the ider market.

'It shows that the animal spirit has returned to the stock-market,' he says. 'People are feeling confident enough in the future to want to float their companies.'

Investors will also be hopeful that the likes of Deliveroo and Brewdog follow the trend of recent IPOs —here share prices often shoot up after listing.

That was certainly the case for Manchester-based retailer The Hut Group, which joined the FTSE last year.

The firm was the biggest IPO in London for seven years, and was accompanied by a large buzz.

Its shares have since risen some 30 pc in four months, rewarding those who got in early.

Dr Martens stocks opened at 22 pc above the IPO price when it floated on Friday, but has slipped 1 pc since. Moonpig opened at 25 pc above, but has since fallen 5 pc.

So how can you get in first this time —nd beat the crowd?

get in first

The IPO process itself is complicated, with the first public shares ypically offered to large institutional investors.

'It's a long-running grumble among private investors that they rarely get to participate in IPOs ahead of flotation,' says Lee Wild from Interactive Investor.

Even so, it's worth checking the website of your investment platform about details of upcoming IPOs —nd signing up for email alerts of future offers.

There are other ways, however, for investors to put some of their money into new companies.

When large investors invest in  rivate (i.e. pre-IPO) companies, they will often include these shares in their retail funds. Edinburgh-based Baillie Gifford, for example, has become well known for investing in pre-IPO companies. Most famously, the investment house was an early backer of Tesla.

Its flagship Scottish Mortgage Investment Trust allows for 30 pc of investors' money to be put into private (non-listed) companies.

This strategy has paid off in recent years, with £0,000 invested five years ago now worth more than £0,000 (before fees).

For retail investors, this has the added benefit of diversification: you're essentially investing in dozens of companies at once.

Risky Buys

But experts warn that —or all the excitement —nvestors should be wary of assuming every new offering is a guaranteed winner.

'Not all IPOs go well, as those who snapped up shares in Aston Martin Lagonda will ruefully tell you,' warns Russ Mould from investment platform AJ Bell. The car-maker issued its first shares back in autumn 2018, at  rice of more than £0,000.

Since then, its shares have plummeted, losing 80 pc of their value in just over two years.

And in the U.S., some high-profile IPOs have fallen short of xpectations.

Ride sharing platform Lyft, a competitor to Uber, has fallen 40 pc since March 2019.

The company had originally been tipped by Baillie Gifford, and was included in its retail funds.

'When it comes to IPOs, investors certainly need to take each deal on its merits and carefully assess each company's competitive position,' says Mr Mould.

Either way, investors can look ahead to an interesting year —nd hopefully a stronger FTSE, too.

moneymail@dailymail.co.uk

©aily Mail

headline: ECO BOOZE TONIC FOR THE PLANET
author: NATASHA CLARK
text:
BEER and spirits are being made more environmentally friendly to help save the planet.

Brewers and distillers are finding innovative ways to cut the harm their industry causes to the environment.

Initiatives include slashing the amount of water used, hydrogenpowered production, recyclable bottles and a refillable rum station.

One green producer has even announced that it has produced England's first carbon-negative gin.

James Grundy, who runs the water-efficient, renewablespowered Small Beer Brew in South London, said: "We're all aware of the pressures being put on our planet. We need to be making a stand."

The drive for greener boozing has been boosted by a £0million government pot, funding 17 eco-friendly projects.

Elsewhere, Brewdog is promising to plant a million trees one for every multipack sold.

And founders of Wildjac distillery, Aster and Chris Sadler, are giving away a packet of seeds with every bottle.

Trade body UK Spirits Alliance hailed the successes but said: "We need the Government continuing to back investment."

headline: WORKER APP IS A TOP TIP
text:
Shaf Rasul One in a million shaf@the-sun.co.uk

A SCOTTISH caféas introduced a cashless tipping system, allowing customers to reward staff while lockdown restrictions are in place.

Kauai UK in Edinburgh is using an innovative online platform called TiPJAR. The software allows workers to receive tips via QR codes and contactless, allowing for direct and transparent transactions which are easily shared among members of staff.

The cafe, which sells healthy brunch and lunch options, is the first to introduce TiPJAR in Scotland.

It is a fantastic idea as it will be a huge help to workers, particularly in the current climate.

The hospitality industry has taken a major hit throughout the pandemic. Most workers have been affected with many experiencing furlough, pay cuts or job losses.

On top of that, regular tips will have diminished due to decreased footfall inside the premises.

Thanks to software like TiPJAR, staff at outlets which can only open for takeaway or delivery can still have access to tips.

It was founded by Brewdog managing director James Brown. He was inspired after witnessing the challenges faced by the industry on staff earnings being reduced alongside the admin pains businesses faced in managing and distributing tips transparently, fairly and within the complex laws that govern the practice.

I encourage Scottish businesses to follow Kauai UK's lead and consider installing a contactless tipping system.

Not only does it allow customers to give staff a cash boost while maintaining social distancing, it could help make sharing tips easier in the future when lockdown restrictions ease.

headline: Crowds have a big say in getting deals
author: Shaf Rasul
text:
MILLIONAIRE Shaf Rasul is our brilliant business columnist. The star of Dragons' Den Online knows the economy and enterprise inside out and can help with YOUR ideas and problems.

Shaf Rasul One in a million shaf@the-sun.co.uk

AS Amazon announced the launch of its new Build It platform, I've been thinking about how crowdfunding may look in the wake of the pandemic.

Build It allows customers to decide which new gadgets will be manufactured by the online retail giant next, by inviting shoppers to pre-order items they are considering for production.

Products which secure enough pre-orders within a 30- day period, and therefore raise enough funds, will be brought to market, and buyers who placed a pre-order will receive a discount on the product as away of thanking them for helping it come to fruition.

The first three items being considered include a smart cuckoo clock, a sticky label printer, and a smart food nutrition scale. While these don't sound particularly groundbreaking to me, I'm sure they'll be well thought through, and customers will be queuing round the block (virtually), to get their orders in.

Amazon says this move reflects the company's ethos of putting the customer at the centre of everything they do, by letting them play a crucial role in deciding which new products are made and sold on the site. They describe the process as 'low risk, high reward, and a whole lot of fun.'

This unique spin on crowdfunding may seem cuttingedge, but the concept has been around for several years, and sites like Kickstarter have allowed entrepreneurs to bring their concepts to market through crowdfunding for over a decade.

Of course, there are other crowdfunding models rewards-based, where investors receive something in return for their investment, equity-based, where investors are given a stake in the business, and donation-based, where no financial return is given, and is often used for charitable causes.

BrewDog is a huge Scottish brand which automatically springs to my mind when crowdfunding is mentioned. The brewers first launched their Equity For Punks scheme in 2010, and there has been a steady stream of announcements from them about further investment opportunities ever since.

I've read about several Scottish businesses turning to crowdfunding in recent months. A mix of start-ups hoping to secure enough cash to get their product to market, and those which have been on the go for some time, but require more funding to grow.

Perth-based Voyager CBD, for example, was founded in November last year and recently smashed its target of raising £75,000 via crowdfunding, generating investments of over £00,000 within just four days.

Creative Scotland also recently launched its Crowdmatch 2021 programme, giving creatives the support they need to launch their own crowdfunding campaigns.

It's certainly encouraging to read stories of Scottish businesses looking for ways to finance exciting new projects, and it shows there is still a strong, entrepreneurial spirit out there in spite of the ongoing economic impact of Covid. Naturally, some may shy away from seeking funding in the current climate, but crowdfunding platform Indiegogo has said its web traffic remains steady despite Covid.

Some of its top-performing campaigns last year included e-bikes and state-of-the-art coffee grinders, reflecting the kind of items we were most interested in as we adapted to life in lockdown.

I think we may begin to see more stories about businesses turning to crowdfunding in 2021, as more Scots take the leap and set up their own businesses, or turn their side hustle into a full-time job.

While the mass job losses people have faced as a result of the pandemic are heartbreaking, the situation has undoubtedly made some people more entrepreneurial, with many going it alone.

As an investor, the beauty of crowdfunding is that you can often invest a small amount of money, which is great for those 'armchair investors' looking to get a slice of the action without spending a fortune.

It also gives consumers the opportunity to support causes they're passionate about and get involved with brands they love. It's not without risk, but I think crowdfunding is definitely here to stay for the foreseeable, and I look forward to seeing what exciting opportunities lie ahead for Scottish businesses going down this path.

headline: The Property Report U.K. Pub Industry Draws U.S. Investors --- Oaktree Capital is latest American firm to invest in sector, undeterred by crisis
author: Ruth Bloomfield
text:
The pandemic hasn't stopped Brits from drinking. But it has been a bad year for the U.K.'s 47,200-strong pub sector, which was battered by three lockdowns, long-term bans on eating and drinking indoors, and restricted operating hours.

Yet even though lockdown restrictions are expected to remain until at least June 21, there are recent signs that this classic British institution is on the path to recovery.

International investors haven't been deterred. Oaktree Capital Management, a Los Angeles real-estate owner, recently said it would invest GBP 200 million, equivalent to $280 million, in the newly formed RedCat Pub Co.

RedCat is led by Rooney Anand, former chief executive of the U.K.'s largest pub firm by number of pubs, Greene King, who said he plans to acquire venues in the affluent south and east of England. Since pubs survived "the Blitz, the Great Plague and the credit crunch," he said, they should withstand the pandemic.

Still, their numbers are dwindling. In 2000, there were just over 60,000 pubs in Britain. That count fell by more than 20%, after the 2007 ban on smoking, higher alcohol prices and the rise in home entertainment hurt the business.

But the Oaktree deal suggests there are still investors who believe in a future for a social ritual that began in the first century, when the Romans invaded Britain and set up stalls selling food and wine.

"The expectation would be that there would be a lot of distress in the market, but in reality it has been quite the reverse," said Stephen Owens, managing director, pubs and restaurants, at the commercial real-estate broker Christie & Co.

Instead, he said, the U.K. government has subsidized payments to furloughed pub workers and cut taxes. This support has enabled most pub landlords to survive. "There has not been a fire-sale situation," Mr. Owens added.

Some American investors can't get past the door. Platinum Equity, another L.A.-based investment firm, last month tried to take over Marston's PLC, a major brewer and operator of around 1,400 pubs. But the company rejected Platinum Equity's GBP 666 million offer, saying that the business was worth more.

After pubs are allowed to reopen in the summer, pent-up demand from Brits long denied a pint or a pub meal will propel the sector back to profitability, a new report by the U.K. investment bank Numis said. In Australia, the report noted, pubs reopened around six months ago and visits had reached pre-pandemic levels by October.

Mr. Owens agrees that 2021 is looking positive for pubs. "We have got more buyers than we have got sellers," he said. "It seems rather counterintuitive, but because of the vaccine rollout there is light at the end of the tunnel.

Before Covid-19, he added, pub closures had started to level off and beer sales were up. "We are having a lot of conversations with a lot of private-equity firms in the U.S. and the U.K.," Mr. Owens said.

American interest in the pub market predates the pandemic. In 2017, TSG Consumer Partners, a San Francisco private-equity firm, acquired a 23% interest in BrewDog, the craft-beer brewer and pub chain based in Scotland, in a deal worth GBP 213 million.

The company now has more than 100 venues in 15 countries. Founder James Watt said last month that it has another 42 sites in the pipeline, including across the Atlantic in Atlanta and Cleveland.

headline: Regulator to block Seedrs and Crowdcube deal
author: James Hurley
text:
The competition regulator is poised to block a proposed merger between Britain's largest equity crowdfunding platforms, throwing the future of the industry into doubt.

The Competition & Markets Authority(CMA) said it was concerned that a tie-up between Seedrs and Crowdcube would "reduce competition and innovation". Both services allow small and medium-sized private companies to sell equity stakes to the public and professional investors via an online platform. They enjoy a combined market share of at least 90 per cent.

Crowdcube agreed to acquire Seedrs in October, a move that was expected to allow the two loss-making services to cut costs and deliver new services to investors and fundraising companies.

However, the competition watchdog said yesterday its provisional findings were that "blocking the merger may be the only way of addressing competition concerns" as it feared a "substantial lessening of competition". Brewdog, the craft beer business, and Revolut, the financial services start-up, are among businesses that have turned to equity crowdfunding to help finance their growth.

Between 2011 and 2020, £ billion was invested via campaigns on Crowdcube and Seedrs. The two were responsible for close to 500 equity raises last year. The CMA said: "A deal between the two could result in UK SMEs and investors losing out as a result of higher fees and less innovation."

It said an independent panel had reviewed evidence including representations from Crowdcube and Seedrs that "should the deal not go ahead, there could be the possibility that one or both firms exit the market or would need to reorientate their business strategy".

Kirstin Baker, chairwoman of the CMA inquiry group, said: "The decision to block any deal is not taken lightly and is made only if there is a real risk of customers losing out."

Seedrs and Crowdcube said that they were disappointed, but that they were in a strong financial position.

Watchdog reaches out to whistleblowers

Whistleblowers are being urged by the Financial

Conduct Authority to report potential wrongdoing as the watchdog battles to overhaul its reputation after a series of scandals that have raised questions about its ability to police firms (Ben Martin writes).

Yesterday, the regulator stepped up its efforts to encourage people working at financial services firms to come forward with information about possible misconduct. It said that it had added to its whistleblowing team and introduced new training for all of its employees to help them handle tip-offs.

It comes just months after the authority was criticised by an independent investigation into its handling of the £37 million London Capital & Finance investment scandal, which found the regulator missed a string of red flags about the now-collapsed firm.

The report in December by Dame Elizabeth Gloster, a retired Court of Appeal judge, catalogued a series of tip-offs that the regulator failed to act upon.

Andrew Bailey, the governor of the Bank of England who led the regulator during the period scrutinised by Gloster, conceded last month that the authority had not possessed a proper system for extracting information it had received.

headline: Colin vs Cuthbert: M&S takes legal action against Aldi’ caterpillar cake
author: Jonathan Eley
text:
Move is latest in series of complaints against the discounter’ products

Marks and Spencer’ “olin the Caterpillar”ake is a children’ birthday staple and the supermarket is in no mood to let rivals crash the party.

M&S is taking legal action against Aldi, saying it has infringed multiple trademarks with its “uthbert the Caterpillar”ake, in the latest in a series of such complaints against the discounter.

Papers filed at the High Court on April 14 state that the design of the cake and its packaging, which closely resembles its Colin product, infringe its trademarks and call on its rival to desist from selling Cuthbert.

The British retailer said “ove and care goes into every M&S product on our shelves”nd it wanted to “rotect Colin, Connie and our reputation for freshness, quality, innovation and value.”

More than 15m Colin cakes have been sold since it was launched in 1990, and the lepidopteran larva —nd its female version Connie —ave been turned into a wider range including cupcakes, trifles, sweets and chocolates.

Intellectual property lawyers said M&S would need to be able to prove that the appearance of Colin was so well known that consumers would recognise it as being an M&S product without any other cues.

“olin is certainly iconic, and it seems highly possible that M&S will be able to show goodwill” said John Coldham, IP partner at law firm Gowling WLG. “o the question will be whether Aldi's product is close enough to cause confusion in the marketplace”

Other supermarkets, including Tesco, Asda and Sainsbury, also produce caterpillar-shaped celebration cakes in green packaging.

Aldi has in the past acknowledged that it seeks to produce own-label products that bear a close enough resemblance to branded equivalents to trigger purchasing decisions.

Its own-label version of Kellog’ Krave cereal is branded Kraze, for instance, while its caramel wafer bars look similar to those that Lanarkshire-based Tunnock’ have been making since 1952.

In 2018, family-owned sausage-maker Heck described the company as a “arasite”or imitating the packaging of its Chicken Italia range. n response, Aldi maintained the packaging was consistent with its own established brand, which was clearly recognisable to customers.

Last year, Aldi launched an “nti-Establishment IPA”learly modelled on Punk IPA, the mainstay product of BrewDog. However the two companies subsequently reached agreement and the brewer now produces “LD IPA”hat is sold in the supermarket.

Aldi suffered a legal defeat in 2019 when make-up brand Charlotte Tilbury used copyright law to argue that the supermarket’ own-label powders were too similar to its own.

The German-owned supermarket also frequently crosses swords with Britain’ advertising watchdog over its pricing claims. In one case in 2019, Tesco successfully argued that an advertisement unfairly compared a bottle of Moet et Chandon champagne to a much cheaper Aldi own-label version in order to skew a price comparison of a “ypical weekly shop”n the discounter’ favour.

Aldi did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

headline: SUMMER LICKED: Nothing says summer [...]
text:
Thursday, May 13, 2021

SUMMER LICKED: Nothing says summer like the sound of icecream vans. Vendors can tune up their vehicles at Halfords, where members of the Ice Cream Alliance get a free five-point check.

ECO-FRIENDLY BATH TIME: Only a fifth of us recycle our shampoo and shower gel containers, with 30,000 tonnes ending up in landfill every year. Superdrug has launched a Doing Good Feels Super campaign to encourage shoppers to check packaging for recyclable options.

BRANCH OUT: Want to tread a greener path? Offset your carbon use from just £.25 a month with Treepoints. The first 100 sign-ups to the carbon offsetting platform at treepoints.green will get a £0 BrewDog beer voucher.

BASKET CASE: Online shopping may have soared in lockdown but we can be commitmentphobes for sealing the deal. More than £9billion worth of goods were left in online baskets and never bought, according to Barclaycard payments.

PRESSING OUR BUT TONS: Giving their handset to a child gives more than eight in ten phone owners the collywobbles. Half are worried the child will drop it and a third that they will delete content, insurers Protect Your Bubble found.

PET PROBLEM: Staffordshire bull terriers are the most commonly stolen dog, according to data interpreted by GoCompare, with 164 reported stolen between 2018 and 2020. Of 26 areas included, West Yorkshire had most dogs taken.

headline: We saw them on balcony.. it looked dangerous
author: GORDON TAIT
text:
DEATH PLUNGE WITNESS' SHOCK

PALS 'SAT ON EDGE' BEFORE TRAGEDY

SHOCKED witnesses told how they saw partygoers sitting on the edge of a penthouse balcony shortly before a reveller plunged 50ft to his death.

The victim in his 20s is believed to have been part of a group enjoying a private bank holiday bash at the flat.

He fell from a third floor area of the building and landed yards from revellers outside the BrewDog bar in Glasgow's Merchant City.

Horrified customers rushed to help the stricken man at 10.15pm on Sunday before paramedics took him to the Queen Elizabeth University Hospital, where he was pronounced dead soon after arriving.

One witness said: "I was sitting on an outdoor terrace opposite with friends and we saw a group of young people sitting on the edge of the balcony in front of us.

"It looked quite dangerous at the time but we didn't know if there was a terrace under them that would prevent a bad injury in case of a fall."

Another said: "The first anybody knew was when they heard a noise. Staff and customers from BrewDog raced to his aid along with others who were passing.

"It was a busy Sunday in the city centre with people leaving bars to head home so a lot of people saw the terrible aftermath."

Detectives spent yesterday inside the third floor flat as they tried to establish the circumstances. Floral tributes were also left at the scene.

It's believed up to 30 people were at the party in the plush pad before cops received a 999 call about a fall from a window.

One witness added yesterday: "The police were still in the flat trying to work out how he fell. There were around 20 to 30 people at the penthouse at the time. They were all removed by police, who turned up after they were called to the tragedy.

"The balcony is strewn with empty bottles and CID have been at the scene all day."

A spokesman for Police Scotland said: "About 10.15pm on Sunday, officers were called to the report of a man having fallen from a window at an address in Glasgow.

"He was taken to the Queen Elizabeth University Hospital by ambulance, however he sadly died.

"Investigations into the circumstances surrounding the death are now ongoing."

gordon.tait@the-sun.co.uk

headline: Heart of gold
author: GORDON TAIT; RUTH WARRANDER

HEARTBROKEN pals paid emotional tributes to a lad with a "heart of gold" who was killed in a 50ft plunge from a penthouse balcony.

Stuart Anderson, 23, fell to his death during a party being held at an exclusive block of flats in Glasgow's Merchant City on Sunday.

The popular footballer and student, from Port Glasgow, Renfrewshire, passed away shortly after arriving at hospital.

As police declared Stuart's death non-suspicious, shattered friends posted hundreds of tributes online to the mate they called Ando.

Last night one pal said: "Ando was a fantastic guy, everybody liked him, nobody had a bad word to say about him. It's so hard to take in.

"My heart goes out to his family, they are all very close."

Ex-teammates at Inverclyde AFC Under 21s issued a statement expressing their grief.
