headline: Former  BrewDog  staff accuse company of ‘ulture of fear’nd misogyny
author: Judith Evans
text:
Craft brewer apologises after more than 60 ex-workers hit out in open letter

Dozens of former staff at the UK’ largest craft brewer, BrewDog, have accused the company of fostering a “ulture of fear”nd misogyny in an open letter.

More than 60 former workers at the Aberdeenshire-based company, using the name “unks with Purpose” said: “he single biggest shared experience of former staff is a residual feeling of fear. Fear to speak out about the atmosphere we were immersed in, and fear of repercussions even after we have left.”

The group, which said it had the support of another 45 anonymous ex-staff, claimed the brewer pursued “rowth at all costs” created a “oxic”ttitude to junior staff and failed to address complaints.

James Watt, BrewDog’ chief executive, apologised and said the company would “isten, learn and act” while adding that many staff had had positive experiences.

The open letter said “ significant number of people have admitted they have suffered mental illness as a result of working at BrewDog” It said the brewer of Punk IPA “ostered a culture within craft beer that deifies founders, and gives weight to sexist and misogynistic brewers who claim to be standing up for free speech”

Founded in 2007, BrewDog has raised more than £0m through equity crowdfunding and has been one of the largest beneficiaries of the UK craft beer boom. In 2017 it announced it had sold a 23 per cent stake to private equity investors TSG Consumer Partners for £00m.

Known for publicity stunts such as bottles made of taxidermy animals, the group reported £75m of net revenues in 2019, the latest year for which accounts are available.

Watt said in a response to the letter on Twitter: “t BrewDog we are focused on building the best business we can, which is why the open letter    as so upsetting, but so important.

“ur focus now is not on contradicting or contesting the details of that letter, but to listen, learn and act.

“s a fast-growing business, we have always tried to do the best by our team —e do have thousands of employees with positive stories to tell as a result. But the tweet we saw last night proves that on many occasions we haven’ got it right,”e said. “ost of all, right now, we are sorry.”

Ben Duckworth, a former BrewDog employee and signatory of the letter, said unhappiness about the company had “eached a critical mass where we thought we’e got to do something about this”

Some current employees support the campaign, he said, adding that the company needed to hire “ew blood to try and change the culture”

The letter follows a digital outcry over working conditions in the broader UK beer industry, spearheaded by social media accounts such as Instagram user British Beer Girl, who posts employees’ccounts of sexism and harassment in the sector.

Employees and ex-employees in a range of sectors have meanwhile used online open letters to level criticism at companies.

Former employees of cosmetics brand Glossier accused the group of poor working conditions and racism in an open letter last year, prompting the company to promise “hange and accountability”

Two years ago thousands of Amazon employees used an open letter to call for the ecommerce group to launch a wide-ranging climate change plan. Amazon said it had an “nwavering”ommitment to sustainability.

headline: 'Toxic'  BrewDog  mauled by ex-staff
text:
Open letter says culture of fear at 'punk' brewer left many burnt out, Gabriella Bennett and Peter Chappell write

They styled themselves as the original sons of anti-establishment anarchy punks dedicated to overthrowing the corporate monoliths of brewing.

James Watt and Martin Dickie, the best friends who founded BrewDog 15 years ago, have cultivated an image of hip chaos and disorder from their HQ in Ellon, Aberdeenshire.

But their cool reputation is looking a little worse for wear.

Former staff have come forward to allege that there was a "culture of fear" in the business and "toxic attitudes" towards junior staff had left many feeling burnt out, afraid and miserable . BrewDog has apologised after a "significant number" of former employees are said to have suffered "mental illness" from working at the craft brewer, signatories to an open letter said.

The letter, from more than 60 former employees, said: "Put bluntly, the single biggest shared experience of former staff is a residual feeling of fear. Fear to speak out about the atmosphere we were immersed in, and fear of repercussions even after we have left."

The group, calling itself Punks with Purpose, details alleged failings and sexist and misogynistic behaviour. "Being treated like a human being was sadly not always a given," it writes.

It has alleged that the business is built on a "cult of personality" around Watt, 38, and Dickie, 39, and that "growth at all costs" was the overarching focus.

Managers allegedly treated staff "however they liked, without repercussions making them feel belittled and/or pressured into working beyond their capacity, and often eventually feeling forced out of the business".

Watt, an Edinburgh University law and economics graduate who was married to the illustrator Johanna Basford before they separated last year, said on Twitter that the letter was "upsetting" but that he would not "contradict or contest" its contents, and would instead "listen, learn and act".

"We are committed to doing better, not just as a reaction to this, but always, and we are going to reach out to our entire team past and present to learn more," he said in a statement. "But most of all, right now, we are sorry."

Punks with Purpose, which claims that many people suffered mental ill- ness from working at BrewDog, says it was inspired to speak out after an outpouring of #MeToo allegations on social media of misconduct and misogyny in the US brewing industry.

Jacob McKean, founder and chief executive of Modern Times Beer, a San Diego brewery, announced last month that he would step down from his role after employees complained about alleged harassment at work.

Brienne Allan, production manager at Notch Brewing in Salem, Massachusetts, asked her followers on Instagram whether they had experienced sexism. Hundreds of women responded.

Siobhan Hewison, a beer writer from Edinburgh who goes by the moniker British Beer Girl, was inspired by Allan to raise awareness that "harassment, prejudice and discrimination is not uncommon in the UK beer scene".

Her posts, sharing allegations sent to her, have included complaints about BrewDog and other breweries.

Founded by Watt and Dickie in their home town of Fraserburgh, Aberdeenshire, in 2007, BrewDog is recognised as having helped to bring craft beer to the mass market. Dickie was Watt's flatmate in Edinburgh but attended Heriot-Watt where he studied distilling.

Watt has spearheaded many publicity stunts, such as serving a 55 per cent proof beer in bottles made from stuffed stoats and squirrels.

BrewDog's canned and bottled beers are stocked in supermarkets and it runs numerous bars in the UK. The company is valued at about $2 billion (£.4 billion), according to the latest estimates from Forbes.

The letter addresses a passage directly to Watt: "It is with you that the responsibility for this rotten culture lies.

Your attitude and actions are at the heart of the way BrewDog is perceived, from both inside and out.

"By valuing growth, speed and action above all else, your company has achieved incredible things, but at the expense of those who delivered your dreams."

headline: BREWDOG  boss James Watt yesterday [...]
author: DAVID IRVINE
text:
LAGER FLOPS

Beer firm's apology after staff 'culture of fear' blast

BREWDOG boss James Watt yesterday said sorry to former workers who accused him of creating a "culture of fear".

The chief exec, 33, issued his grovelling apology after the Scots craft beer firm was blasted for allegedly "harassing and intimidating" staff.

The claims were made in a scathing open letter published online by a group calling itself Punks with Purpose.

It said: "BrewDog was, and is, built on a cult of personality. Growth, at all costs, has always been perceived as the number one focus.

"Put bluntly, the single biggest shared experience of former staff is a residual feeling of fear.

"Fear to speak out about the atmosphere we were immersed in, fear of repercussions even after we left."

The letter called for "a genuine apology from anyone and everyone who has worked for BrewDog and treated people like objects harassing, assaulting, belittling, insulting or gaslighting them."

And it says to Mr Watt, above: "It is with you that the responsibility for this rotten culture lies. Your company has achieved incredible things but at the expense of those who delivered your dreams.

"In the wake of your success are people left burnt out, afraid and miserable. The true culture of BrewDog is, and seemingly always has been, fear."

Mr Watt yesterday called the letter "upsetting" but said his focus now was "to listen, learn and act".

He added: "We have many thousands of employees with positive stories to tell. But on many occasions we haven't got it right.

"We are going to reach out to our entire team past and present to learn more. But most of all, right now, we are sorry."

BrewDog was founded in 2007 in Fraserburgh, Aberdeenshire, by Mr Watt and Martin Dickie, then both 24. It now has more than 1,000 staff and dozens of branded bars in the UK, USA and Europe.

david.irvine@the-sun.co.uk

headline: Brewdog  'so sorry'
text:
BREWDOG bosses apologised after ex-workers accused the firm of creating a "culture of fear".

In an open letter, they called on bewery founder and chief exec James Watt, 33, to say sorry for "harassing and intimidating" workers.

They accused the craft beer company of being built on a "cult of personality" and focusing on the firm's growth at the expense of staff.

Mr Watt said: "We are committed to doing better and we are going to reach out to our entire team past and present to learn more. But most of all, right now, we are sorry."

headline: Cooper: We are into tournament mode now - it's serious
text:
Michael Grant reports from Scotland's camp in England where caution over Covid looms large

There was a box of Walker's shortbread, a bottle of Johnnie Walker Blue Label, cans of Brewdog and Tennent's, a Fifa 21 computer game with bespoke Scottish cover, an expensive set of Bang & Olufsen wireless earphones and a bottle of Irn Bru with the label: 'Good luck. Andy Robertson'.

No, not the conveyor belt round from the Generation Game, this was the presentation box the captain gifted to all of his team-mates when they gathered at the squad's training base near Darlington on Wednesday night. It was a classy reminder that things are different for Scotland right now. As Liam Cooper put it: "We are into tournament mode. You get that sense of seriousness as opposed to last week's trip to Spain, which was a bit more chilled out."

Sophistication and wealth radiate from Rockliffe Hall, the luxury hotel and golf resort where Scotland will be based until the Croatia game on June 22 and, they hope, beyond. But so does caution and hygiene. Any visiting journalists very few are allowed in must submit evidence of a negative Covid-19 test. Hand sanitisers are conspicuous. Those players selected by the SFA to speak to the media it was Cooper and Kevin Nisbet's turn yesterday keep their face masks on for as much of the time as possible. This is no time to contract the virus.

Cooper went down with it in March and it cost him possible caps in that month's World Cup qualifiers. Nor was he asymptomatic. "I'm not ashamed to say I picked up Covid. It was rough the first few days but after that I was pretty fine.

"I wasn't too sure about losing the smell and taste, that was weird. I was trying to eat all sorts to get that back. It was well and truly gone. It was curries, chillies, everything, you name it, vindaloo. There was nothing there! The food here is lovely. I'm just thankful I can taste it.

"We follow the precautions round the place. We still wear masks. If you did contract it now you'd be in serious trouble. That'd be your Euros over."

Manager Steve Clarke and the SFA allowed the players to disperse and spend some precious time with their families between last Sunday night's return from the Luxembourg friendly after a training base in Spain and a game in Portugal and Wednesday's gathering at

Rockliffe Hall. But it was not a case of the players being released into the wild: every one of them was mindful of taking extreme care.

"I don't think the lads got up to much because they knew what was at risk," said Cooper. "I had my family tested before I got back from Spain, just to be cautious. Speaking to a few of the lads, they've done the same.

"I think you'd be stupid to go out there and see people, knowing what's at risk. You've got the chance of a lifetime to go and play for your country in the Euros. The lads are pretty switched on and everyone's PCR tests have come back negative. We're sticking to the precautions. Dr [John] MacLean [the team's senior doctor] has been really unbelievable with us."

When John Fleck joined the group at the hotel, having had to isolate in his Spanish hotel room for ten days after testing positive, he received a round of applause from all the players. "It's hard to come away on international duty and have that club mentality but it really does feel like that here," said Cooper. "The boys get on unbelievably well and the chemistry and team morale is as high as I have ever seen it within the camp."

It might seem that Cooper is guided by two entirely different types of manager, Clarke for Scotland and Marcelo Bielsa for Leeds United. But to him there is no great contrast between the Ayrshireman and the Argentinian. "No, not really. They both have their ideas and ways they want to play. They're both demanding managers and bring out the best in you.

"They leave you to express yourself and it's been great for me. When you've worked with Marcelo you can work with any manager.

"He has taken my career to places I never thought was possible. I have a lot to thank him for. I have to thank Steve as well for showing a lot of faith in me and bringing me into the squad and letting me show what I can do.

"He has that level of respect which all managers need to have, I believe. The group listens to him and takes everything on board that he and his coaching staff are trying to get over to us.

"This year is one I'll probably look back on when I'm retired and think, 'what a year that was'. Although there's been a pandemic I've had an unbelievable year. I'm probably the first person to get promoted from the Championship, play in the Premier League and play in the Euros all in a pandemic. I can always tell my grandkids that."

Craig Brown remembers, pages 4-5 Tournament opener, pages 8-9 England's dark arts, page 10

headline: Large measure of arrogance, hold the nice
text:
Behind the story

When a guy has just struck a £ billion deal, you want the interview to look a little bit different (Mike Wade writes).

"Would you mind pouring some beer over your head?" asked the photographer.

James Watt didn't mind at all. Within minutes, he'd walked to the heart of the BrewDog brewery complex and was emptying not one but two bottles of Punk IPA onto his shaven head. He'd do anything to stand out on a busy news day.

On that occasion in 2017, he was celebrating a deal with TSG Equity which raised the value of his shareholding in the company he co-founded to £50 million.

He didn't get there by being nice. The charity Advocates for Animals was outraged when he chose to package a super strong beer in roadkill; police were called when he rolled a tank into the City of London to unveil a share offering styled "equity for punks".

At his HQ in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, his staff visibly wilted in his presence. On the wall behind his desk, a poster read: "Being reasonable is the most common path to mediocrity."

Watt's wife, the illustrator Johanna Basford, once let slip that to understand the man you must realise "he's not troubled by self-doubt".

His character was seen in Who's the Boss?, a BBC show that opened up a senior management post to the entire BrewDog workforce. With a shortlist down to three, Watt intervened because he didn't think any of the candidates were suitable.

Not for the last time, social media turned on him. "Wow. BrewDog owner is the definition of arrogance," tweeted one critic. "What a dick."

headline: STAFF BLAST 'TOXIC' TRENDY BEER FIRM
author: CHRIS BROOKE
text:
BEER company Brewdog has a 'culture of fear' that has caused mental illness among employees, former staff have claimed.

Some 76 ex-workers have signed an open letter holding founder James Watt responsible for 'toxic attitudes towards junior staff', which has led many to become 'burnt out, afraid and miserable'. Bosses have been accused of using 'lies, hypocrisy and deceit' in PR campaigns and fostering a culture which 'gives weight to sexist and misogynistic' people.

The Scottish-based brewer and pub-chain founded by Mr Watt and Martin Dickie in 2007 has expanded rapidly with beers such as Punk IPA, pictured left, proving popular. The firm has more than 100 bars worldwide and is valued at around £.4billion, according to Forbes.

Yesterday Mr Watt apologised. He said: 'It's hard to hear those comments, but it must have been harder to say them... we will endeavour to honour that effort and courage with the real change it deserves.'

headline: Trouble brewing over 'toxic' culture
text:
Atrendy brewery has been accused of creating a "rotten culture" for its workers that left them feeling burnt out, afraid and miserable (Louisa Clarence-Smith and Dominic Walsh write).

James Watt, the co-founder of Brewdog, apologised yesterday after he was accused of being responsible for a culture of fear at the Scottish company.

An open letter from more than 60 former staff said: "Put bluntly, the single biggest shared experience of former staff is a residual feeling of fear. Fear to speak out about the atmosphere we were immersed in, and fear of repercussions even after we have left."

The letter from the group, calling themselves Punks with Purpose, goes on to detail alleged and unsubstantiated failings ranging from "toxic attitudes towards junior staff" to sexist and misogynistic behaviour, adding: "Being treated like a human being was sadly not always a given." The group, which claims that many people suffered mental illness from working at Brewdog, was set up by former employees of the company, who say they were inspired to speak out following an outpouring of #MeToo allegations on social media of misconduct and misogyny in the US brewing industr y. Jacob McKean, founder and chief executive of Modern Times Beer, a

San Diego brewery, announced last month that he would step down from his role after employees complained about alleged harassment at work.

Brienne Allan, production manager at Notch Brewing in Salem, Massachusetts, asked her followers on Instagram whether they had experienced sexism. Hundreds of women responded.

Siobhan Hewison, a freelance beer writer based in Edinburgh, who goes by the moniker British Beer Girl, was inspired by Allan to raise awareness that "harassment, prejudice and discrimination is not uncommon in the UK beer scene".

Her posts, sharing allegations sent to her, have included complaints about Brewdog and other breweries.

Watt responded to the allegations in the open letter with an apology, insisting: "Our focus now is not on contradicting or contesting the details of that letter, but to listen, learn and act."

headline: BrewDog 's ethical status at risk over allegations of 'rotten culture'
author: ouisa Clarence-Smith
text:
BrewDog is in danger of losing its ethical business certification in the wake of allegations of a toxic work culture.

The Scottish craft brewer has a page on its website dedicated to its "B Corp" certification, a stamp of approval for private companies meeting high standards for impact on workers, customers, community and the environment.

However, B Lab, the New York-based group that administers the certification, is reviewing BrewDog after receiving a complaint from a former employee, who said that the company had failed to conduct its business "as if people matter" and without "causing significant harm through their business practices".

BrewDog was founded in Fraserburgh, Scotland, in 2007 by James Watt and Martin Dickie and now has more than 60 bars in the UK. It has about 145,000 small shareholders through its "Equity for Punks" scheme and is expected to seek a stock market flotation.

The brewer is reeling after an online open letter from more than 60 former staff this week accused it of creating a "rotten culture" that left them feeling burnt-out, afraid and miserable.

Watt, the chief executive, apologised on Thursday, saying that the letter proved "that on many occasions we haven't got it right", despite thousands of employees having positive experiences. A spokeswoman for B Lab told The Times: "Identifying and investigating allegations of misconduct or misrepresentation by B Corps is essential to maintaining the integrity and value of the B Corp certification. This specific case is under review by our standards team and B Lab does not comment publicly on the details of open cases."

About 4,000 companies in 153 industries have B Corp status. They range from Ben & Jerry's, the ice cream brand, to Patagonia, a clothes retailer.

The complaint details how BrewDog had to pay £2,000 in 2018 to a former worker who lost his job at its brewery because of eyesight problems. A year later, it was sued by a customer who said he felt forced by staff at its bar in Cardiff to say he "identified as a woman" to buy a bottle of Pink IPA. BrewDog declined to comment on the B Lab investigation.

headline: THE SHAMING OF ENTERPRISE
author: ALEX BRUMMER CITY EDITOR
text:
Founding a new enterprise is hard. The start-up generation has had a real chance to lift the bar when it comes to looking after the interests of employees.

Similarly, it might have been hoped that Covid-19, the suffering of so many, and the push for environmental, social and governance investing by Blackrock among others, might have produced a real change in boardroom behaviour.

Instead, we have seen unfettered greed, with bosses lobbying for, and accepting, big pay increases when restraint and responsibility are in order.

Among newer enterprises, think about craft brewing. The name itself exhibits a point of difference with the established industrial-scale beerage. There, the conformity of the product and logistics play second fiddle to the quality of hops.

Brewdog is one of the more successful of the craft breed and tapped into respect for what it does by crowdfunding. So it comes as a shock when former workers take to Twitter exposing a record of cutting corners on health and safety and driving staff so hard that it has caused mental illness.

Uber had to be dragged through the UK courts to be persuaded that its drivers in the UK should be offered the same employment rights as payroll employees.

The lack of formal employment conditions for riders was among the factors that led to a stock market flop of Deliveroo. Instead of resetting the dial for workers, some millennial and GenZ firms seem to have feeble grasp of what a company built for all stakeholders might look like.

It doesn't help that bigger firms set a bad example. Dismal conditions in Sports Direct and Amazon warehouses have been widely reported. Tesco fought an equal pay claim for women to the highest court in Europe and lost. Its former chief executive, Dave Lewis, waltzed off with a £.42m pay and bonus package during the pandemic.

Shareholders in rival Morrisons this week handed chief executive Dave Potts and his team bloody noses, with 70pc voting against the group's pay report. It has been a year of executive pay revolts at companies as different as Rio Tinto and publisher Informa.

Supermarkets were among the pandemic heroes, keeping people supplied. That is no excuse for 'wartime' profiteering. Investors at Morrisons were disturbed that the pay committee failed to adjust bonuses to account for windfall income as a result of hospitality and other closures.

Fat cat pay is no accident. It arises as a result of weak remuneration committees, grasping executives with no sense of personal shame and lack of responsibility and poor advice from remuneration experts who are often conflicted. The fight for good governance and better value systems in corporate life has a long way to go.

Trading places

Post-Brexit Britain's trade figures have become a new battleground for those who thought leaving the EU was a dreadful mistake. With each passing month the pattern becomes clearer.

Trade with countries outside the EU is buoyant with imports up 4.9pc since December and exports off just 1.3pc. Trade with Europe is being hit hard.

The UK, by all accounts, is getting the best of the bargain with exports down 5.7pc and imports falling by a whopping 19.1pc.

The figures are distorted by pandemic effects and the relatively slow recovery in the eurozone. In spite of border disputes and EU use of non-tariff barriers, such as intrusive document requests, living outside the EU is not the disaster predicted.

Private grief

At least fund management outsourcer Sanne did not capitulate at the first sound of private equity cannon fire.

By holding firm, chairman Rupert Robson, an HSBC emigre, has elicited five improved offers and only succumbed to talks when the offer from Cinven hit 875p, valuing the group at £.42m. The final outcome will be the same. A valuable UK financial enterprise with good tech vanishing into the private equity black box.

There is a symmetry to Jersey-based Sanne's fate. Before its London listing in 2015, it was owned by private equity outfit Inflexion. Much of its income derives from offering the private equity sector fiduciary, administration and governance services.

Private equity takeovers should never just be about price.

Unless a compelling and ambitious business case can be made, the directors have a duty to kick the offer into touch.

©aily Mail

headline: My hipster man problem
text:
I'm not surprised by craft beer's Me Too moment, says Jane Mulkerrins. The 'woke bros' I dated were toxic

Time was, toxic masculinity was simple to spot. It wore a designer suit, drove a Porsche, touched up waitresses and blew its Wall Street wages on strippers, steaks and cocaine. Then bought its leggy, long-suffering girlfriend a 3k handbag to say sorry.

In 2021, however, the boundaries are blurred. The mainstreaming of man buns, beards and fixed-gear bikes might lead one to assume that a cuddlier, more caring masculine culture has emerged as hipsterdom has taken hold. They cook. They camp. They compost. They compose earnest ballads on the ukulele. They willingly take up paternity leave, wear papooses and churn their own avocado ice cream. But beware: beneath the Breton T-shirt and the manicured twirly moustaches, the hipster may well be hiding just as egregious a set of attitudes.

Last weekend James Watt, cofounder of the craft beer company Brewdog, promised to "listen and learn" from the savage criticism of the company by 60 former employees who alleged "toxic attitudes" and a "culture of fear". In an open letter, the former staff claimed a significant number of them had "suffered mental illness as a result of working at Brewdog", the Scottish brewery and pub chain that employs 2,000 staff. Watt has apologised and said the company is "committed to doing better".

This month Jan Rogers, managing director of the brewery Marble Beers, which owns two pubs in Manchester, resigned over "historical allegations" of alleged bullying and sexual harassment by others at the company. In a statement, she said she wanted to offer a "wholehearted" apology to staff "who suffered unduly". A spokesman for Marble Beers said it would "investigate thoroughly the past culture within the company".

Meanwhile, across the Pond, a "tidal wave of sexual harassment allegations and toxicity in the craft beer industry" has led to multiple firings, according to the American beer site Brewbound. Thousands of stories of abuse and harassment at companies from California to Massachusetts have been uploaded to social media.

If you are surprised that craft beer, with its supposedly progressive, female-friendly persona a far cry from the blokey image of bitter is having its own #MeToo moment, don't be. I recently moved back to London after ten years in Brooklyn, the neighbourhood with the greatest global concentration of hipsters anywhere (sorry, Berlin), and I can assure you, after a decade of dating this demographic which I consider important social anthropology they really are no more evolved than their flashy bro predecessors.

Simon, a former lawyer who had given up the rat race to write a film, would frequently tell me over the course of our four-year fling that "the world would be better if women just ran everything". In reality, he did not really like it when I tried to run even my own writing career, getting angry if I refused his requests to pop over for a spot of afternoon delight and dismissing my deadlines entirely. He would also often invite me over late at night, with the caveat "you can't stay over". To be clear, I was the only one of us getting up the next day to work.

Seth, with his regulation bare ankles and fine line in flannel, volunteered for an organisation tackling far-right extremism, grew his own rocket and radishes, and worshipped his three elderly cats. He would also brutally ghost me for weeks on end, and frequently "forgot" his wallet when we went out for drinks.

My platonic male friends behaved no better. Jared, a self-identifying feminist who came to Washington DC with me for the Women's March, would sit beside me in cabs, scrolling through his catalogue of dating apps at breakneck speed. His tactic, he told me, was to swipe right indiscriminately, "liking" absolutely everyone, "then see what you get and filter out the least hot ones later". It was, he said, "a numbers game". He also disclosed a technique of deliberately putting dates on the back foot in order to undermine their confidence and gain the upper hand, a technique known as "negging".

Christian, meanwhile, played championship frisbee and four acoustic guitars, and had the requisite man bun. But, as I discovered when he invited me up for teriyaki tofu one night, he also held some fairly horrifying opinions about the women he worked with at a trendy start-up.

The main problem with hipster men, apart from the competitive obsessions with bread-baking and Burning Man, is that they are trying so hard. There is no luxuriating in a day-long hangover binge of Dawson's Creek unless, of course, you do it ironically because hipsters don't own TVs. And if they did, they'd only watch documentaries.

So, before you are seduced by that untrammelled ankle or extensive vinyl collection, or microdosing magic mushrooms "for wellness", stop. That brooding balladeer may just be a toxic bro with some beard oil.

Some names have been changed

The men I dated were not any more evolved

headline: BrewDog  to investigate its 'rotten culture'
author: Dominic Walsh
text:
BrewDog has announced plans to launch an independent review into allegations of a "rotten culture" at the controversial Scottish brewer.

James Watt, its co-founder and the main target of a critical open letter from former employees, said that the company would appoint an independent agency to review "our culture and people practices to ensure we can make positive and inclusive change".

He said that BrewDog would reveal the findings of the review before the end of the year internally and externally, while the company would conduct an anonymous staff survey "to paint a comprehensive picture of the BrewDog culture at every level".

The open letter, from a group of former employees calling themselves Punks with Purpose, accused Watt of propagating a culture of fear that had left many former workers "burnt-out, afraid and miserable". It detailed "toxic attitudes towards junior staff", sexist and misogynistic behaviour, and added: "Being treated like a human being was sadly not always a given for those working at BrewDog."

The number of signatories to the open letter has swelled from 60 to more than 300 since it was posted on Twitter last week.

Watt, 38, apologised for the pain caused to them. "I am ultimately responsible for the culture of our business," he said. "The letter that ex-colleagues wrote to us is 100 per cent my fault."

He said that he had "all too often neglected many important people elements" of the business and that some of the mistakes he had made had "detrimentally impacted our culture".

BrewDog, founded in Fraserburgh in 2007, has become famous for its publicity stunts, but Watt admitted that he had got some wrong and promised: "I will not make these mistakes again."

As part of his vow to improve the way BrewDog treats its employees, Watt listed initiatives including exit interviews, the scheduling of delayed salary reviews and the formation of an employee representative group. He also admitted that during the pandemic BrewDog had become "too lean" in some areas of the business and said that he would "put in place a plan to properly resource these areas".

Punks with Purpose said: "While we hope this statement can bring some measure of closure to former staff, we have seen hollow apologies in the past. We are not going away."

headline: Gabriella Bennett The beer boss who left a bad taste in the mouth
text:
BrewDog's investors may call themselves punks, but they're more like members of a cruel, cold-hearted cult

About a decade ago I boarded a train in midsummer heading to Aberdeenshire. I'd been invited by BrewDog's Martin Dickie and James Watt to visit their mega brewery, set amid yellow and green patchwork fields outside Ellon. I was there to learn about the way they ran things, and to find out more I was assigned oneon-one time with Watt. As I looked into his face and began to talk I was struck by his eyes cold, dead, shark eyes.

Here's do-goo

In the years that followed I interviewed Watt on the phone and was met with the same imperious responses as I did that day in Ellon. Other journalists recall seeing his staff "visibly wilting" in his presence, and describe the poster in his office declaring "Being reasonable is the most common path to mediocrity". No big deal, I thought at the time, when he cut me off in the middle of another question. I'd come up against worse.

From day one, Brewgooder money into projects around world, establishing water points supporting economies. intentions beers taste Brewgooder.

Those eyes, though. I couldn't get them out of my head. And here they were again, regarding me coolly through the laptop screen as stories of BrewDog's alleged toxic workplace culture leaked out via a group of former employees. "Some people", the group wrote, dropping a hint that could be heard in Mongolia, had discovered that by creating an anarchist façde you can treat staff as if they were "less than human" behind the scenes.

Who on earth could "some people" be? Surely not the businessmen who initiated legal action against a tiny independent bar that accidentally used the name of BrewDog's gin brand. No way could it be the guys who regularly crowdfund to further their expansion plans despite being worth an estimated £.85 billion.

Although we'll never know either way, it makes sense that two CEOs capable of morally bankrupt decisions such as these could also be capable of fostering a toxic workplace culture. What makes less sense is the slavish devotion with which BrewDog's fans its "punks'' go along with its regime.

In 2007 BrewDog's Equity for Punks was launched, an investment scheme in which aficionados buy shares in the company in return for the kind of extremely generous benefits you'd expect from a hugely successful corporation, such as receiving a single beer on their birthday.

BrewDog claims 120,000 punks have signed up, which has enabled the company to buy electric cars and fund wind power programmes, alongside other similarly virtuesignalling endeavours. These investors may call themselves punks, but in my view cult members would be more appropriate. How else do you sum up a community whose leaders persuade not insubstantial sums from its followers? These punks can afford premium prices, otherwise they wouldn't drink BrewDog's beers in the first place. I don't for a second believe a 10 per cent discount is the driver. This is tribal, pure and simple, the same force that keeps alive football clubs and gossip forums and any corner of the physical or digital worlds where people go to belong, to feel part of something greater than themselves.

There's nothing wrong with tribalism, but in this case it has been co-opted by BrewDog for profit. I hope the punks know that. I hope they know that as stakeholders they are in the unique position to bring about meaningful change for the employees who still work at BrewDog by revoking their membership. The odds say they won't, though, because they worship at the feet of the canniest marketers in the business. And because belonging is an enormously powerful feeling, one that trumps logic and reason.

a real der has poured clean water the

This is not the end for BrewDog, for Watt, or for Dickie, who I have left off my list of character assassinations (for now) because he appears less interested in obsessive self-promotion. Soon the whole sorry tale will be spun into a story of redemption, sold to Netflix as the men who fell from grace for a seven-figure sum. And guess who'll be masterminding the PR makeover the one with the cold, dead eyes. These people only fail up.

and local Noble plus its great too. com

And when they do, let us not forget that this has always been the Watt show, and for now, the plotline is that he's sorry. Really sorry. But also it's sometimes the fault of the workers too, because "there are two sides to every story", as written by BrewDog in a leaked draft open letter. And actually, it's a "little bit demeaning" and "a little bit hurtful" that the brand has been made to look so bad by a bunch of former employees allegedly treated so badly they suffered mental illness.

Why am I humming A Little Bit Alexis, the song from Schitt's Creek written about a character so spoilt, so entitled and so narcissistic they're beyond parody? Sometimes truth is stranger than fiction or at least it is for "some people".

Here's a real do-gooder

From day one, Brewgooder has poured money into clean water projects around the world, establishing water points and supporting local economies. Noble intentions plus its beers taste great too. Brewgooder.com

headline: Lists of top bosses are fine but what about the 50 worst?
author: Pilita Clark
text:
Employees and customers could benefit from knowing more about the least impressive CEOs

Earlier this month, a British chief executive had an email run-in with one of his customers that left the woman gobsmacked.

This man is not a famous grouch, like Ryanair boss Michael O’eary, who once said the idea the customer was always right was “orseshit”

Nor does he make news like James Watt, co-founder of the craft beer group, BrewDog, who last week vowed to listen and learn after dozens of ex-staff wrote an open letter claiming the company was rife with “oxic attitudes”nd had a “otten culture”

His name is James Price and he runs an outfit he founded called Everything Genetic, which is one of the Covid test providers the UK government lists for arriving travellers who need to prove they can safely leave quarantine.

The email dust-up began when a friend of mine who had flown into London from abroad wrote to ask him for a refund because the test results she had ordered from his firm had failed to turn up at the expected time.

Mr Price wrote back to say that as far as he could tell, his company had stuck to its advertised turnaround times and if he paid a refund there would be no test result.

My friend, fearing this meant she would be trapped at home even longer, protested vigorously. She said she needed the result and a refund would encourage customer loyalty, to which Mr Price told her bluntly: “e’ rather have return customers that understand what they purchase.”

He eventually relented when shown what one of his staff had told her about when her results would be ready. Conceding she had been given incorrect customer service information, he offered both the refund and her test results, but it was too late.

My friend, a former senior manager, said she found his attitude so belittling she would never use his firm again.

Mr Price told me later he was sorry for any poor service, including his responses to customer complaints, which would now be handled by others in the company.

But he came to mind last week when an annual list of “op CEOs”ame out, this time from Glassdoor, the career site where employees can post anonymous reviews of their companies.

The list ranked bosses in the US, UK, Canada, France and Germany over the 12 troubled months up to May this year, using an internal rating system that measures the quality, quantity and consistency of reviews.

Among its more notable findings: Microsoft’ Satya Nadella made it on to every country’ list except France, which takes some doing, according to Glassdoor.

Facebook’ Mark Zuckerberg failed to make it to the list of top 100 US chief executives for the first time since the rankings started in 2013, when he was rated number one, which is also quite a feat.

Other CEOs who made it on to three countries’ists were Salesforce’ Marc Benioff; Morgan Stanley’ James Gorman and Airbus’Guillaume Faury.

This is all interesting, as were the reasons employees gave for rating these bosses so highly. They didn’ just provide decent pay, good benefits and career progression, many also got points for offering flexible or remote working.

This is all good to know. Yet the experience of my friend was a reminder that it might also be good to have a reliable list of the worst CEOs. Wouldn’ that offer a more useful guide for potential employees, customers and investors, especially when it comes to smaller, less scrutinised companies? I know a few people have attempted such things over the years, but no one with the heft of Glassdoor. When I asked the site if it had ever considered such a list, a spokesman said it had not. “e prefer to look at best practice and those that are doing well.”

He pointed out CEOs get a Glassdoor rating based on cumulative reviews —s opposed to one for the most recent year —nd these can be instructive.

Ryanair’ O’eary scores just 43 per cent, well below the site’ average CEO rating of 73 per cent. BrewDog’ Watt comes in at just 52 per cent.

That is all very well, but if the site ever comes up with an annual ranking of the worst CEOs, I know I won’ be the only one eager to read it.

pilita.clark@ft.com

Twitter: pilitaclark

headline: BrewDog  try hard to be good boys
author: Shaf Rasul
text:
Shaf Rasul One in a million shaf@the-sun.co.uk

NOW that the dust has somewhat settled following the most recent storm to hit infamous Scottish craft beer company BrewDog, I'm interested to see what kind of lasting impact, if any, the whole affair will have on the firm.

The brand has had its fair share of controversies over the years, but this one has potential to cause the most reputational damage, given the serious nature of the allegations.

In case you didn't get caught up in any of the media storm surrounding BrewDog in recent weeks, it all kicked off with an open letter published on Twitter and signed by 61 former employees of the beer company, which contained some very concerning accusations.

Accusations of a toxic culture of fear, sexism, workplace bullying and health and safety breaches were just some of the most-serious complaints contained in the letter.

Of course, social media went wild, and the post generated thousands of comments. Many claimed they had a negative experiences of working with the brand, while others rushed to BrewDog's defence.

During their short but meteoric rise to fame, BrewDog has been embroiled in controversy (I'm sure you'll remember the Beer for Girls saga), but it was difficult to see how they could bounce back from such serious claims with their usual tonguein-cheek approach.

Public apology They acted fast. A public apology was issued the following day, and co-founder James Watt appeared on numerous TV and radio shows, facing tough questioning and promising to learn from the experience.

I applaud Mr Watt for tackling the situation head on, but I wonder if it's enough.

Has BrewDog's reputation already been tarnished beyond repair, or will people forget about this whole situation in a few months?

There have been inevitable calls to boycott the brand, and I'm sure many will do just that.

Ex-signed letter

But perhaps the reality is that as long as the brewer can demonstrate positive next steps, others will allow the company and its founders to move on by continuing to purchase their favourite tipple.

I'm sure there will undoubtedly be a negative impact, at least in the short-term, as some who have watched the situation unfold will be eager to spend their money elsewhere to disassociate from these allegations.

However, I'm not convinced there will be any serious longterm effects.

What they do over the next few months however will be critical to how they come out the other side.

A matter of days after the workers open online open letter was published online, BrewDog announced the appointment of a new female chairwoman, Blythe Jack, who is supposedly being parachuted in as the brand aims to draw a line in the sand.

While their sales may not be hugely affected, recruitment may be a problem for some time to come.

People won't exactly be queueing up round the block to interview for a role with a company at the centre of this kind of storm, so it's important for them to show they have truly learned from this, and that they are striving to make genuine changes to the workplace culture.

I think they need to keep their heads down for now and work hard on making sure their staff feel listened to and valued before coming out with any kind of big announcement or publicity stunt to try to curry favour with the public.

A gimmicky marketing ploy certainly would not go down well right now.

BrewDog is an amazing Scottish success story, and a great example of a small company which has gone from strength to strength to achieving international success, so it's a real shame to see them come up against this kind of criticism.

However, if these accusations are accurate, then of course it's only right for them to be called out and held to account.

It's impossible not to admire BrewDog's accomplishments, but if it's true that the business was run in a way that made employees so uncomfortable they've felt the need to come together to collectively name and shame their bosses, there's no excuse.

I'm sure BrewDog can, and will, recover from this but it's clear that evidencing a real departure from the claims made in the open letter will be the way to revive its reputation.

I, like many, will certainly be watching with interest to see what comes next from the brand.

will

Ex-workers signed open letter online to

We are committed to doing better, not just as a reaction to this, but always; and we are going to reach out to our entire team past and present to learn more. But most of all, right now, we are sorry.

JAMES WATT Co-founder of Brewdog

headline: BrewDog  'is evicting gamekeepers' to make way for forest
author: Marc Horne
text:
BrewDog has been accused of evicting gamekeepers to make way for an ambitious reforesting project.

The Aberdeenshire brewery and pub chain bought the 9,300-acre Kinrara Estate in the Cairngorms for an estimated £.5 million this year. It plans to create a "lost forest" to offset the carbon produced at its brewery in Ellon.

It is also planning to create an environmentally sustainable hotel, distillery and campsite along with hiking and biking trails.

However, there are claims that gamekeepers, gardeners, farming and domestic staff, who were employed by the previous owner, have been laid off and the properties they lived in put up for sale. The land was used for shooting, fishing and deer stalking; six keepers were employed.

The Scottish Gamekeepers Association raised concerns about their fate. "Any loss of gamekeeping jobs is obviously a big concern for us," a spokesman said. "Local land managers will know that the area to be planted is a key wintering ground for a host of iconic species. There are at least three pairs of nesting eagles in the vicinity that have fed on the deer, grouse and hare food source created over many years through moorland management by gamekeepers. Losing this is not going to enhance biodiversity."

A councillor, speaking under condition of anonymity, told Shooting Times magazine: "In a national park where low-cost housing is at a premium and young people struggle to find homes they can afford, I find myself at a loss as to why a millionaire would put his vanity project above local people for any other reason than greed."

James Watt, the BrewDog cofounder, has said that work on the forest is due to begin in August.

"We purchased a huge chunk of land in the Scottish Highlands where we will embark on the single biggest native woodland establishment and peatland restoration project ever carried out in the UK," he said. "The scale is staggering the BrewDog Lost Forest is bigger than 17 actual countries. We will be planting millions of trees to create a biodiverse broadleaf woodland and ecosystem. Overall the Lost Forest is capable of pulling one million tonnes of carbon dioxide out of our atmosphere."

This month BrewDog announced an independent review into allegations by staff of a "rotten culture" within the firm. Watt, 38, apologised.

BrewDog did not respond to a request for comment about the keepers.

headline: Never mind the beer, here's the punk shareholder rebellion at  BrewDog
author: JUDITH EVANS; ALICE HANCOCK ; MICHAEL O'DWYER
text:
Food & beverage

BrewDog's "equity punks" are in revolt.

A 180,000-strong army of crowdfunding investors helped to fuel the company's growth over more than a decade, turning it into the UK's largest craft brewer. But their loyalty has been shaken after former staff accused Brew- Dog of misogyny, a "toxic" attitude to junior workers and pursuing "growth at all costs".

Some of the small investors the Scottish brewer calls "punks" who have invested more than £0m and been reliable drinkers of its lagers and ales are now concerned that attractive financial terms offered to private equity groups mean they will end up losing money even if BrewDog's value increases.

"I wouldn't have invested at all if I'd known what the culture was within the company," said Andrew O'Neill, a 53-year-old veteran of Belfast's hospitality industry who invested several thousand pounds last year. "The sheen has come off BrewDog for me."

Richard White, a London-based brewer who invested eight years ago, said: "I'm angry and I feel a bit responsible for enabling this ... I feel a bit guilty for swallowing the BrewDog Kool-Aid, because clearly that hasn't been everyone's experience."

The company, seen as one of the biggest success stories of the UK's craft beer boom, has struggled to respond to the criticism. The complaints bring a further challenge after BrewDog's growth slowed in the pandemic, making it harder to justify a high valuation as the company moves towards a planned IPO.

"If not adequately addressed, then the criticisms could grow into a crisis for a consumer-led business like BrewDog," said Dave McCarthy, managing director at consultancy Drinks Adviser.

BrewDog was founded by its current chief executive, James Watt, and Martin Dickie, then aged 24, because they "were bored of the industrially brewed lagers and stuffy ales that dominated the UK beer market".

It now has more than 100 bars in cities including Las Vegas, Tokyo, Shanghai and Brisbane, as well as dozens across the UK and its beers are sold in major supermarkets. It has a valuation pushing close to £bn.

Like many of its rivals, such as Camden

Town Brewery, now owned by Anheuser-Busch InBev, and Scotlandbased Innis & Gunn, BrewDog used crowdfunding to propel its early growth.

It defied critics to deliver strong returns to early investors. Neil Fletcher, an administrator, invested £,450 with his husband in BrewDog's early days and has since sold enough shares to pocket more than £2,500 while retaining the majority of his holding.

Since the claims emerged from former staff, individual investors have berated BrewDog executives on a company-run shareholder forum.

Watt has promised change and an independent review of the company's culture. "I know the events from the last few days have caused a lot of pain for all of our team members and our community and I can only apologise for that," he told shareholders last week.

BrewDog has made Blythe Jack, a managing director at the private equity firm TSG Consumer Partners, which bought a stake in 2017, chair of its board following the claims.

But some investors remain unhappy. While many primarily view their small shareholdings as something like a loyalty card, since shares entitle them to discounts on beer, others especially those with larger holdings want financial returns.

That is made harder by the fact that TSG, which declined to comment for this article, is guaranteed an 18 per cent compounding annual return on its 22 per cent holding in the event of an IPO or sale. This takes priority over other shareholders, according to BrewDog's prospectus, meaning newer investors risk making nothing or losing money even if the company's value rises.

"If BrewDog raise the maximum £0m at the current valuation of just under £bn, investors in the latest UK Equity for Punks crowdfund would only get their money back if the company sells for [about] £.2bn in 2024," said Myrto Lalacos, investment executive at venture capital firm Praetura Ventures.

O'Neill said he had been unaware of the returns contracted to TSG when he invested, although they are set out in

BrewDog's prospectus. "I wouldn't have invested if I had known," he said.

Brewdog's latest crowdfunding round values it at about 10 times net revenues. McCarthy said the valuation was "remarkable" considering that listed brewers such as Heineken and Molson Coors traded at enterprise values of two to four times net revenues.

The brewer made a loss of £.4m in 2020, down from a £.7m profit a year earlier. Its net revenues were up 4.2 per cent to £82m in 2020, having risen by 25 per cent the previous year.

Lauren Carrol, special projects director at BrewDog, said: "Our equity punks community is massively important to BrewDog and we are proud that it continues to grow. There are many public and private channels to ask questions, raise queries or escalate complaints.

"Our prospectus is always clear and we urge all investors to read all of it before making any investment decisions." But the brewer will have to work to regain trust. Lewis Nyman, another investor, said: "I've been on Twitter encouraging people to boycott BrewDog until they manage to steady their own ship and learn to treat their staff right."

headline: FAILS
author: ROBERT McAULAY
text:
LATEST BREWDOG STORM

Beer fan's £5k 'solid gold' can prize is only gold-plated

By ROBERT McAULAY

A BREWDOG competition winner hit out after his 'solid gold can' prize turned out to be gold-plated and worth a fraction of its supposed £5,000 value.

Mark Craig, 32, had hoped to pay for his wedding by flogging the special Punk IPA tin he scooped in a Willy Wonkastyle draw, but was left feeling flat when it arrived.

The beer lover was one of ten punters who got lucky in the competition set up by the multinational firm based in Ellon, Aberdeenshire.

Mark, who works in the booze industry, said: "I wanted to sell the can and contacted BrewDog for any certification they had. The certificate they sent said it was gold-plated but they promoted it as solid gold. When I contacted them they told me the 'solid gold' claim was an error."

Tweets posted by BrewDog when the draw launched in November told entrants they could be one of ten customers to win a 'solid gold' can if they bought cases of Punk IPA.

Bosses told Mark the claim was a typo but insisted the value was due to its rarity, with only 50 produced, and the cost and quality of the cans.

Mark, of Lisburn, Northern Ireland, added: "I ultimately feel disappointed.

"When I won I was ecstatic, believing a solid gold can could contribute towards bills and our wedding, which has been postponed until 2022."

BrewDog last night said it had apologised to Mark for "erroneous use of the phrasing 'solid gold'" but said it stood by the £5,000 valuation.

A spokesman said: "Once the error was flagged, we immediately removed or changed all such mentions.

"The phrasing in question was never included in the terms and conditions of the competition, nor in the wording informing winners of their prize.

"We believe the £5,000 valuation is reasonable based on multiple factors including the price we paid for manufacture and the rarity of the cans."

The latest row comes after Brew- Dog co-founder and chief exec James Watt, 33, apologised following claims earlier this month by 61 ex-workers of a "culture of fear" at the company.

robert.mcaulay@the-sun.co.uk

headline: BrewDog  boss to sell personal assets over conflict of interest fears
author: James Hurley;Louisa Clarence-Smith
text:
The co-founder of BrewDog is selling off personal property assets linked to the craft brewer ahead of a planned flotation amid concerns over potential conflicts of interest.

James Watt told The Times that his remaining property holdings rented to the Scottish brewer were "in the process of being divested" and that three sites he owned and leased to BrewDog had been sold in the past two years. Watt was responding to concerns over related-party transactions worth hundreds of thousands of pounds that the beer business entered into with him in recent years. These have included a bar in Columbus, Ohio, that BrewDog sold to Watt and that he leased back to the business, as well as a business he owns that was paid to charter jets on behalf of the brewer.

BrewDog said all the deals had been conducted at market rates. Watt, 38, strongly denied the suggestion that the arrangements had been set up to extract money from BrewDog, adding that the business had benefited from having him as a landlord, particularly during the pandemic, when 27 months of rent-free holidays had been provided for three locations he owned.

He said all the remaining assets linked to the brewer had been marketed in the past 24 months as the company moved towards "acting and feeling like" a public company.

BrewDog, which was founded in Fraserburgh in 2007, has about 100 bars around the world and about 200,000 small shareholders after a series of crowdfunding rounds.

The company has not set a timeline for a flotation. It is valued at close to £ billion, but its growth has been hit by the pandemic.

It faced scrutiny this month after an online open letter from former staff accused the company of creating a "rotten culture". Watt said that it would launch an independent review into its "culture and people practices".

A spokesman for the brewer said that the related-party deals were "managed by third parties at arm's-length market rates designed to optimise benefit for BrewDog".

Questions for boss over property deals, pages 40-41

headline: GOLDRINGER
author: BEN ARCHIBALD ; ROBERT McAULAY
text:
BrewDog '£5k' can winner alerts watchdog

EXCLUSIVE

A SICKENED competition winner called in watchdogs after a BrewDog "solid gold can" prize turned out to be just gold-plated.

Adam Dean, 30, alerted the Advertising Standards Agency after finding out the special Punk IPA tin he scooped in a draw was worth a fraction of its supposed £5,000 value.

The ASA is looking into the claim ? a day after we told how fellow winner Mark Craig, 32, was gutted by the true worth of the Aberdeenshire-based beer firm's prize. Sales manager Adam said: "I'm just totally disappointed and I want it resolved. I legally entered a competition to win a solid gold can but I've not got that. I asked for shares to make it up to £5,000 and BrewDog basically said no, so I called the ASA." Adam, of Shrewsbury, Shropshire, said the ASA asked for all the emails he's received from the company and told him earlier this month that it will "assess my concerns".

He added: "It's annoying that I've lost out on so much of the money I've been promised.

It'd still have been a cool prize if they hadn't mis-sold it."

An ASA spokesman confirmed: "We received a complaint.

"We're in the process of assessing the complaint to establish whether we need to take action. No investigation has been launched at this stage." We told yesterday, left, how Mark was one of ten punters who got lucky in the competition. Mark, of Lisburn, Northern Ireland, had hoped to flog it to fund his wedding.

BrewDog earlier said it had apologised to Mark for "erroneous use of the phrasing 'solid gold'" but stood by its £5,000 valuation.

text:
1

Burberry is facing questions over its future after Marco Gobbetti, its highly rated Italian boss, was poached by Salvatore Ferragamo, a rival luxury goods group. The Florentine design house is known for its shoes worn by Hollywood stars such as Audrey Hepburn. Pages 9, 33 2

The cryptocurrencies sector shrugged off the crackdown by the Financial Conduct Authority on Binance, one of the world's biggest crypto exchanges, as the price of bitcoin climbed by 7 per cent to $34,445.

headline: Questions for r  BrewDog  boss over property deals
author: James Hurley;Louisa Clarence-Smith
text:
Brewer's founder under renewed pressure after bullying allegations, write James Hurley and Louisa Clarence-Smith

The co-founder and chief executive of BrewDog, Britain's most successful craft beer business, is a self-styled "punk" who has called venture capitalists "toads" and City types "fat cats", while repeatedly criticising the established giants of the brewing industry. Now it is James Watt's own business dealings that are in the spotlight.

After this month's allegations of bullying at the company that Watt, 38, co-founded with Martin Dickie, 38, in Fraserburgh, Scotland in 2007, new questions are adding to their burdens, this time concerning businesses that belong to Watt that secured property worth hundreds of thousands of pounds and other deals with BrewDog.

The arrangements have further engaged the interest of some of the private equity-backed company's 200,000 small shareholders over whether the transactions represented value for money.

Last night Watt told The Times that he was in the process of divesting all his property assets linked to BrewDog, but he insisted that the arrangements had benefited the company as it grew.

BrewDog said that as it "looks ahead to a future IPO, all related-party transactions will be subject to review". The company noted that all the deals had been conducted at market rates.

BrewDog is Britain's largest craft brewer, with about 100 sites around the world and annual sales of £15 million in 2019. The company is renowned for its provocative marketing stunts, from naming a drink after the drugs cocktail that killed River Phoenix and John

Belushi, the Hollywood stars, to chartering a branded helicopter to drop taxidermied "fat" cats on the capital.

This month the business, estimated to be worth nearly £.9 billion, has faced allegations from a group of former employees that it fostered a "culture of fear". Watt apologised to staff and said that the brewer was "committed to doing better".

The business owes its substantial roster of small shareholders to a series of crowdfunding rounds that have seen it raise £0 million.

One of the related-party deals that some of these investors are questioning involves a bar opened by BrewDog in

Columbus, Ohio, in 2018. Disclosures show that BrewDog had owned an interest in the American property where the new bar, BrewDog Franklinton, is located, but had sold it in December 2017 to an entity owned by one of Watt's companies, Ten Tonne Mouse, before the doors were opened.

Under the deal, BrewDog provided

Watt's company with an interest-free loan to purchase the property for $657,816. The loan was repaid in 2018. BrewDog entered into a five-year lease to pay rent to Watt's company of $150,000 a year.

Watt said that the rent had been set independently by a third party and that substantial construction costs had brought his investment to £.75 million.

Ten Tonne Mouse is one of several subsidiaries of a UK company called JBW (77) that Watt owns.

In 2018, BrewDog bought services worth £61,000 from businesses connected to JBW. That dropped to £21,000 in 2019 after Watt sold BrewDog-linked assets "resulting in lower levels of purchases from related parties", accounts show.

Among subsidiaries of JBW, which owed £.7 million to Watt in 2019, is an entity called Jet Pack Pie, which in the three-year period that ended on September 8, 2020, was paid approximately £25,000 annually by BrewDog for flights. Watt said that the arrangement was for the provision of charter flights and that it had ended in 2020.

Watt owns personal property interests via Pie & Mouse, another JBW subsidiary, which as of 2019 held £0.1 million of investment properties and had bank debt secured against Watt's "personal investment portfolios". Its commercial tenants are understood to include Amazon.

BrewDog said that other than Franklinton, the only properties still owned by Watt and used by BrewDog were a house in the United States "used by various members of the BrewDog management team" and three in Britain ? a London flat "paid by the night on market rates" and two bars in Aberdeen "leased on an arm's-length market basis". Watt said that he had sold three other properties rented to BrewDog in the past two years and was in the process of selling the remainder. Two deals had fallen through because of Covid-19, he said.

One crowdfunding shareholder said: "I find it staggering that people can still trust them saying they are punks and a small ethical business when it's quite clear that James is making hundreds of thousands of pounds from the company on top of his salary."

Watt said that BrewDog had benefited from having him as a landlord, particularly during the pandemic when he granted 27 months of zero rent across the three remaining locations he owns, where other landlords generally had provided between three and six months' rent concession. He said that the arrangement was not to extract money from the business but to help it to expand. Despite the divestment plans, he said it was not unusual for public company chief executives to own property assets linked with their businesses.

Last year, Watt said that he and Dickie would forgo their 2020 salaries to help to protect jobs. Watt was paid £77,094 in salary and benefits in 2019.

A BrewDog spokesman said: "Related-party transactions are commonplace in many businesses. As with everything BrewDog does, these transactions are conducted under advice and managed by third parties at arm's-length market rates designed to optimise benefit for BrewDog. All transactions to date have been reviewed by financial advisers and were at market rates in effect at that time."

headline: BREWDOG  SELL-OFF
author: TRACEY BOLES
text:
SUN CITY

BREWDOG'S co-founder is said to be selling off his property assets linked to the firm over fears of a conflict of interest.

It's reported that James Watt's remaining holdings are "being divested" ? and that three sites he owned and leased to the Aberdeenshire brewers were sold in the last two years. It comes ahead of a planned float of the near-£billion firm. Concerns raised over deals worth hundreds of thousands in recent years included a US bar.

BrewDog said the deals were at market rates. Mr Watt denies any wrongdoing and BrewDog was asked to comment.

headline: BrewDog 's not-so-golden ticket reported to adverts watchdog
author: Mike Wade
text:
It was billed as a prize to match the golden ticket in Charlie and the Chocolate Factory.

In the event a "solid gold" can marketed by BrewDog turned out to be a flight of fancy, too.

Mark Craig, 32, was sent the can, purportedly worth £5,000, by the brewer after winning the prize last year.

At a valuation last month he was stunned to learn that the prize contained a "minuscule" amount of gold "thinner than a layer of paint".

When he contacted BrewDog at their headquarters in Aberdeenshire, the company blamed a "typo" for the mix-up and said that the value was based on its rarity. Advertising regulators are reviewing the incident.

An email from its customer service team to Craig reads: "It seems that any reference to 'solid gold' was an error, and all other comms I can find mention 'gold cans', so this typo seems to have slipped through the net."

Literature promoting the competition as recently as February claims that the cans are solid gold.

The email added that the £5,000 valuation was very reasonable and based on "standard retail markup, brand value, rarity and uniqueness".

Craig, from Lisburn, Northern Ireland, said that he believed the can to be worth about £00 and confessed to being confused by BrewDog's behav- iour. His feelings, reported by The Scottish Sun, were shared by Adam Dean, 30, another winner of the golden can.

Dean, a sales manager, was so angry that he contacted the Advertising Standards Authority. "I'm just totally disappointed and want it resolved," Dean, from Shrewsbury, Shropshire, said. "I legally entered a competition to win a solid gold can but I've not got that. I asked for shares to make it up to £5,000 and BrewDog basically said no."

He added: "It's annoying that I've lost out on so much of the money I've been promised. It would still have been a cool prize if they hadn't mis-sold it."

The row is the latest controversy to hit BrewDog, known for its eye-catch- ing PR campaigns, such as its pink "beer for girls" or the launch of a "transgender beer" that drew criticism from Stonewall.

Craig, "a lifelong BrewDog fan", said that his initial excitement had been followed by disappointment. He said: "It's all a bit strange, I've got 20 instances where they refer to solid gold cans. It's deliberate lies. I'm just confused as to why they would deceive people."

As a further reward for finding the gold can, Craig received £0,000 of BrewDog shares, which are not freely tradable unless the company goes ahead with a long-anticipated stock market float that is widely tipped to happen this year.

He also received an invitation to a VIP tour of the company's brewery in Ellon. "I'm now wondering if they'd want me there," he said.

BrewDog said that it had apologised to Craig for "erroneous use of the phrasing 'solid gold' " but stood by its £5,000 valuation.

The brewer was accused three weeks ago of perpetuating a "culture of fear" in the workplace by former staff. James Watt, the chief executive, apologised and pledged to launch an investigation into the claims.

The Advertising Standards Authority said: "We received a complaint. We're in the process of assessing the complaint to establish whether we need to take action. No investigation has been launched at this stage."

headline: IT MAKES ME LAUGH ME CRY
author: Shaf Rasul
text:
1SM Monday,

Shaf Rasul One in a million shaf@the-sun.co.uk

ALL that glitters isn't necessarily gold as beer lovers found out recently after winning a "solid gold can" prize in a competition run by BrewDog.

The Scottish brewer had offered 10 people the chance to find a gold can hidden in a case of its beer, worth £5,000, in a Willy Wonka-style draw.

Winners were left feeling flat after discovering that the cans were gold-plated, and one even reported the firm to the Advertising Standards Authority.

A company spokesperson said the term "solid gold" was a mistake, but they stand by their valuation although an independent jeweller has valued the can at just £00.

Sounds like their latest PR stunt has backfired.

headline: Kitchen workers share harassment claims on social media accounts
author: Gabriella Bennett
text:
Allegations of sexual harassment and manhandling of staff working at restaurants owned by Scotland's leading chef Tom Kitchin emerged when former employees contacted an anonymous social media account.

The Instagram account Hospitality Bullshit was inundated by more than 30 former chefs, managers and waiters after posting a graphic description of a trainee at The Kitchin who claimed to have been deliberately burnt by a colleague.

The account gained thousands of followers as it continued to post anonymous first-hand allegations including unwelcome touching and intimidation at four Kitchin Group restaurants.

Twelve former employees came forward to share their stories with The Times in an investigation published on Saturday.

The Kitchin Group said that it had suspended two senior members of staff while claims were independently investigated. One female chef, who spoke on condition of anonymity, alleged that she was sexually harassed by a senior chef while working at the Kitchin in 2017. "He asked me intimate details about my sex life what positions did I prefer, what things did I like," she claimed.

"He would say 'I wish I could take you into the corner or around the back'."

Separately, the anonymous Twitter account Punks with Purpose posted a number of open letters to the craft brewing giant BrewDog, signed by more than 60 former employees complaining of an alleged toxic workplace culture. In an Instagram post Martin Dickie, the brewery co-founder, wrote: "I would like to wholeheartedly apologise to every single person who has put their name on that list."

Peter Southcott, managing director of the Kitchin Group, said further to the investigation launched into the claims: "We will not hesitate to take whatever action may be necessary."

headline: BREWDOG 'S AD IT ON BOOZE HEALTH CLAIM
author: STUART MacDONALD
text:
Seltzer drink promo ban

A CHEEKY advert for a hard seltzer drink by beer firm Brewdog has been banned over misleading health claims.

The Advertising Standards Authority said the promo for vodka-based Clean & Press cans breached its code by purporting to be nutritious.

The ad, right, was posted on Instagram in January.

It said: "Due to advertising regulations we cannot claim this drink is healthy. Even though Clean & Press is only 90 calories per can, with no carbs or sugar and a little bit of alcohol, this is not a health drink."

Eight people complained about the health benefit claims.

The ASA ruled the 5 per cent booze volume was well above the 1.2 per cent limit for 'low alco- hol' drinks. It said: "We acknowledged that the claims presented information in a tongue-in-cheek manner.

"However, we considered consumers would understand that the product was in fact healthy."

A spokesman for Brewdog, based in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, said: "We've accepted the ASA ruling and have removed the wording."

scottish-sun@thesun.co.uk

headline: BrewDog  forced to delete health claims about its alcoholic water
author: Josie Clarke
text:
An Instagram post by BrewDog has been banned for making misleading claims about an alcoholic drink.

The advertisement for the brand's Clean and Press hard seltzer read: "Due to advertising regulations we cannot claim this drink is healthy. Even though Clean and Press is only 90 calories per can, with no carbs or sugar and a little bit of alcohol, this is not a health drink. If you are looking for a health drink, do not drink Clean and Press."

The Advertising Standards Authority (ASA) received five complaints that the ad implied the drink was healthy and used general health claims that were not permitted for alcoholic drinks.

The ASA ruled that the phrase "a little bit of alcohol" implied that the drink was low-alcohol, an increasingly popular designation, despite its strength of 5 per cent.

BrewDog said the ad, posted in January, was intended to be "tongue-incheek" but the company accepted that it had breached the rules.

Hard seltzers typically contain carbonated water, alcohol and flavourings. They are often marketed as healthier alternatives to beer and wine.

The ASA said "only 90 calories per can" and "no carbs or sugar" were nutrition claims that were not permitted for alcoholic drinks, as was the implication that the product was beneficial to health or related wellbeing. The ASA said that consumers would understand the claim of having "a little bit of alcohol" to mean that the product was low-alcohol when it had an alcohol by volume strength of 5 per cent. It ruled that the advert must not appear again in this form, adding: "We told BrewDog not to make health claims or non-permitted nutrition claims about alcoholic drinks. We also told them not to make permitted nutrition claims for alcoholic drinks if the product did not meet the associated conditions of use for the claim."

BrewDog, which has been valued at up to £ billion, was founded by James Watt and Martin Dickie, who were at school together in Aberdeenshire. They started the company in 2007 with a bank loan and a grant from the Prince's Trust because they could not find anything they wanted to drink.

Last month the brewer was accused of perpetuating a "culture of fear" in the workplace by former staff. Watt apologised and pledged to hold an investigation into the claims. The company was also forced to apologise to a man who won a can of BrewDog beer billed as "solid gold" that turned out to be largely made of brass.

A BrewDog spokesman said: "We have accepted the ASA ruling and have removed the wording in question."

In separate rulings, the ASA banned two Instagram posts by Drty Drinks for making misleading nutrition claims for an alcoholic drink and for encouraging excessive drinking, as well as website posts by the High Water and Whisp Drinks companies for making misleading and non-permitted nutrition claims for alcoholic drinks.

headline: Hard line on hard drink
text: An ad for a "hard seltzer" drink made by BrewDog from vodka, sparkling water and fruit flavours has been banned. The brewer had posted a can of its drink on Instagram with the words: "Only 90 calories per can, with no carbs or sugar and a little bit of alcohol, it is not a health drink." The advertising watchdog recognised the "tongue-in-cheek" approach but disputed the phrase "a little bit of alcohol". The drink was 5 per cent ABV four times the normal definition of low alcohol.
