headline: BrewDog  must change its stripes on Tiger gin brand
author: Stuart MacDonald
text:
BrewDog has lost a legal battle with a small gin-making business after the Scottish beer company tried to launch a rival product.

The Shropshire Gin Company, who make Tiger Gin and Tiger Vodka, took action after the craft brewer attempted to introduce its own range of spirits.

BrewDog, who are based in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, applied to register the brand name "Tiger Strike" as a trademark last year for drinks including gin, vodka and rum.

However, Jeffrey Lawrence, who owns the Shropshire Gin Company, lodged a formal objection and said the name was too similar. He claimed that Tiger Strike would threaten sales of their product as customers would think that it was linked to Tiger Gin.

The UK Intellectual Property Office (UKIPO) has ruled in his favour and said the trademark cannot be registered.

Lawrence, 54, of Telford, Shropshire, was previously granted permission to register Tiger Gin as a trademark after winning a dispute with Heineken, the makers of Tiger beer.

He said: "I appreciate that the UKIPO decision was in my favour. The trademark for Tiger Gin was hard won and is strongly defended."

The Shropshire Gin Company's representatives told the hearing that Tiger Gin had built up a substantial following in the UK since it was launched in 2016 and had won a number of awards.

BrewDog was ordered to pay £,000 towards Lawrence's costs.

In their written ruling, the trademark hearing officer of the intellectual property office said: "I have found all of the applicant's goods to be identical with the opponent's goods. I have found the marks to be visually, aurally and conceptually similar to a medium degree.

"The following factors lead me to conclude that there will be indirect confusion between the marks: the fact that the goods are identical; the goods will be purchased with no more than a medium level of attention; the earlier mark is inherently distinctive to at least a medium degree.

"Consequently, I find that a significant proportion of average consumers will note the differences between the respective marks but conclude that the marks relate to economically linked undertakings, eg 'Tiger Strike' might be perceived as a range or line of drinks under an overarching brand 'Tiger'.

"The opposition has succeeded and the application is refused."

BrewDog, which has been valued at up to £ billion, was founded by James Watt and Martin Dickie, who were at school together in Aberdeenshire. They started the company in 2007 with a bank loan and a grant from the Prince's Trust because they could not find anything they wanted to drink.

Last month, the brewer was accused of perpetuating a "culture of fear" in the workplace by former staff. Watt apologised and promised to launch an investigation into the claims.

The company was also forced to apologise to a man who won a can of BrewDog beer billed as "solid gold", which turned out to be largely made of brass.

A spokesman for BrewDog said: "We made the decision not to progress with the launch of Tiger Strike well before the UK Intellectual Property Office came to its conclusion so the ruling has had no impact on our plans."

headline: Brewdog  gin sling in brand fight loss
author: STUART MacDONALD
text:
BEER giant Brewdog has lost a legal battle with a small distillery.

The firm's bid to register the brand name Tiger Strike for a spirits range was opposed by The Shropshire Gin Company, which makes Tiger Gin and Tiger Vodka.

Bosses claimed Brewdog's name was too similar and would confuse customers.

The UK Intellectual Property Office agreed and refused the application, ordering the brewers to pay £,000 costs.

Brewdog said yesterday it had already decided not to launch the brand. It came as the business reported a £3million pretax loss last year as the pandemic hit their pubs.

Overall revenues rose 10 per cent to £38million while online sales rocketed by 900 per cent.

Co-founder James Watt said the firm, based in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, was "stronger than ever".

headline: Online sales help ales
text:
news in brief

BrewDog, the craft beer business, fell into the red last year after the Covid-19 pandemic forced the closure of its bars around the world, of which there are more than 100. The company made a pre-tax loss of £3.1 million in 2020 compared with a profit of £.1 million in 2019. But James Watt, the co-founder, said the business had enjoyed "the most significant achievement in our short history" by growing revenue by 10 per cent to £38 million, thanks to a surge in online sales.

headline: Good hair day for £00m Den reject
author: Ashley Armstrong
text:
An entrepreneur whose brush was ridiculed by TV's Dragons is joining the super-rich, writes Ashley Armstrong

When Shaun Pulfrey, a former hairdresser, appeared on Dragons' Den, he was sent packing after his hairbrush to detangle knotty hair was branded a "waste of time".

Pulfrey, 59, might have the last laugh, however, after he landed a bigger fortune than some of the dragons, sealing a multimillion-pound deal for his Tangle Teezer business.

The inventor is understood to have made at least £00 million after selling a majority stake in the haircare brand that has won a global fanbase for its ability to solve tangled hair.

Pulfrey, who was born in Grimsby, has struck a deal to sell a stake to Mayfair Equity Partners, a London-based company that recently floated Séaphine, the maternity brand worn by the Duchess of Cambridge, in a £50 million London listing.

He told The Times: "I'm incredibly excited for the future and the new opportunities it will present for our iconic and much-loved brand, including continuing to bring even more new product innovation, which is the heartbeat of the brand."

Terms of the deal have not been disclosed but Tangle Teezer had been linked to a deal in 2016 that valued the entire business at £00 million. The company has grown in the past five years, with sources saying that the deal was "significantly more" than the previous estimates. Companies House says that Pulfrey still owns a stake of more than 75 per cent, putting him in line for a big payday. The company has been a British success story, with more than 80 per cent of its sales generated overseas, selling almost a million brushes in Latin American markets.

The entrepreneur appeared on Dragons' Den in 2007, hoping to raise £0,000 in exchange for a 15 per cent stake in his business. He was flatly turned down as Peter Jones, Duncan Bannatyne, James Caan and Deborah Meaden accused him of being a "waste of time". Jones said that the product was "hair-brained" and Bannatyne said that it made him want to "pull my hair out".

Meaden, who is reported to be worth £0 million, likened the product to a "horse brush". Pulfrey, however, could well be sitting on a fortune almost four times as large as hers.

Meaden tweeted last year that she had made a mistake not backing the haircare brand. "It's good to be wrong when a Biz goes on to make a huge success," she posted. "Never believe anyone is always right."

On the night the show was aired Tangle Teezer's website crashed as customers tried to buy the product. It soon struck a deal to be stocked in Boots stores, the first step on a rapid expansion path. Pulfrey had worked in Vidal Sassoon salons as a colourist before coming up with the idea to detangle wet hair.

Along the way he developed a method to detangle knots by tapping the hair with a brush and a comb which he said loosened the tangle. He went on to research plastic modelling and how to patent products in between his salon shifts. He came up with a two-tiered teeth design that flexes through the hair rather than tugging it.

Pulfrey put £8,000 into the business and bought patents. He turned his twobedroom flat in Brixton, south London, into a warehouse and distribution centre. After being turned down by the dragons he remortgaged his flat to raise £5,000, which kept the company going for six months. The company, which sells hair brushes for £ to £4, is stocked in 75 countries.

Celebrities including Victoria Beckham, Keira Knightly and Emma Watson have all told of their appreciation. Pulfrey disclosed that a Tangle Teezer was used to comb Chewbacca's fur in the recent Star Wars movie.

The company recorded £7.8 million of sales in the year to the end of 2019 and pre-tax profits of £62,000, according to its last available public accounts. Last year the business achieved record sales of £0 million.

Bertie Aykroyd, a partner at Mayfair Equity Partners, said: "Using smart technology and innovative designs, the brand has developed a product portfolio that consumers adore."

Slaying the dragons

Brewdog Beer punks James Watt and Martin Dickie applied to go on Dragons's Den in 2009 to pitch their craft beer business. Brewdog has recently been valued at about $2 billion in a fundraising round. Trunki Robin Law's business was called "worthless" when it appeared on Dragons' Den in 2006. It is now sold in more than 100 countries worldwide and made a record £.5 million sales in 2019.

Hungryhouse Co-founders Shane Lake and Tony Charles had secured £00,000 from James Caan and Duncan Bannatyne in exchange for half of the company but the deal fell through. It went on to be bought for £40 million by rival Just Eat.

Cup-a-Wine Plastic wine cups filled with a single serving of wine and sealed with a foil lid were rejected in 2009. The product was later picked up by Marks & Spencer.

headline: Berkshire brewery accused of bullying and sex harassment
author: Dominic Walsh
text:
A brewery in Berkshire chaired by the founder of the Firkin pub chain has been hit by allegations of bullying and sexual harassment similar to those that sent shockwaves through BrewDog.

A group of present and former employees of West Berkshire Brewery calling themselves WBB Renegades have written an open letter claiming that a survey had thrown up "multiple accounts of bullying, racism, homophobia, sexism, sexual harassment [and] a disregard for staff's physical and mental wellbeing".

The group ran a survey of present and former staff after an article in the Morning Advertiser, the trade magazine, claimed that the make-up of its senior team, which is 70 per cent female, had created a positive culture that was in sharp contrast to the "toxic cultures" at many male-dominated craft brewers, including BrewDog in Scotland.

The feedback had "painted a very different picture to the one in the article", according to the WBB Renegades, with "a significant disconnect between senior management and the rest of the company being reported".

The West Berkshire Brewery, in Yattendon, has been chaired for the past eight years by David Bruce, who also co-founded the City Pub Company and the Capital Pub Company. He has raised more than £7 million to expand the business. Bruce, 73, said he was upset by the claims. "We're not a blokey, boorish, toxic culture. I'm particularly upset and taking it personally as I've always prided myself on being chairman of companies that don't have disgruntled former employees."

He denied the pressure group's claims that the business was not taking the issue seriously. "We are responding. We have undertaken to have an external party to come in and carry out a review right across the company, including the board from me as chairman right down to the drayman and the girl who answers the phone, to find out whether there is any truth in it at all."

In June, BrewDog bosses were accused by a group of former employees calling themselves Punks with Purpose of propagating a culture of fear. The company promised a sweeping review of its culture and operating structure, which is now being enacted, although employees at some other craft brewers have made similar claims, prompting fears of a wider problem in the sector.

headline: BREW MUST BE KIDDING
author: STUART PATTERSON
text:
lAle's 300 mile trip to local Tesco

lStore less than a mile from firm

BREWDOG HQ, ELLON

lDrinks giant's eco brag canned

BREWDOG sends beer destined for a Tesco store minutes from its HQ on a 300-mile road detour despite the firm's eco boasts.

Consignments are driven from the booze giant's brewery in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, to a supermarket distribution centre in Livingston, West Lothian.

Once there they are separated and packed on to lorries, with some products driven 153 miles back to the Tesco superstore just half a mile from Brewdog's base a five-minute walk away.

Bosses at the drinks giant have told of their aim to be the planet's greenest brewery through "radical" action.

But one local said of the near six-hour round trips: "It's been going on for years. Surely if they were serious about being eco friendly this would be looked at. You can see Brewdog from Tesco's front door.

"There are obviously logistical issues with deliveries but why can't they take a radical approach?"

It's understood beer

Boss ... Watt sent to Tesco's hub is part of a huge load that supplies stores nationwide. Earlier this year Brewdog famed for its Punk IPA forked out £.5million on a Highland forest where trees will be planted to offset the carbon it produces.

The firm also uses windgenerated power and two bio-gas fuelled trucks shift beer to a warehouse from its HQ on an industrial estate.

A Brewdog spokesman said: "We are always looking to improve the sustainability of our business, which is why we are taking action.

"We do send our beer to centralised distribution centres, in line with every other producer in the country.

"This is far and away the most efficient way to get our produce to market, and what major retail customers want.

"One-off truck deliveries would have the net effect of actually increasing carbon emissions by adding another truck onto the road."

We told in June how Brewdog co-founder James Watt, 33, apologised after ex-workers claimed the firm had a "culture of fear".

stuart.patterson@thesun.co.uk

headline: BrewDog  agrees tie-up with Japan’ Asahi to boost sales
author: Judith Evans and Alice Hancock in London
text:
Deal comes as UK’ largest craft brewer plans London IPO

BrewDog has established its first international joint venture with Japan’ Asahi as the UK’ largest craft brewer seeks to increase sales ahead of a planned London IPO.

The Scottish group aims to boost sales in Japan sixfold in five years after agreeing the deal with Japan’ largest brewer.

The partnership will market and distribute BrewDog beers such as Punk IPA, Hazy Jane and Elvis Juice, using Asahi’ distribution capability to push up supermarket sales. It could later establish a brewery in Japan, said James Watt, co-founder and chief executive. BrewDog already owns a bar in Tokyo.

The deal comes as BrewDog meets with bankers about a likely London listing, having appointed Rothschild as independent adviser to the IPO process.

“e see [the IPO] as a key part of our future,”att said. The joint venture “s a new type of initiative for us    f this is successful in Japan there might be similar type agreements with other partners in other geographies.”

Ahead of the pandemic, BrewDog had been looking to list in 2020. It has not committed to a revised timeline for the IPO, but is close to appointing lawyers, Watt said.

It aims to list in London but is also considering New York; a sale, rather than an IPO, is not on the cards, he added. BrewDog lists independence, in an industry dominated by big drinks multinationals, as one of its company “eliefs”

The craft brewer, which will own 51 per cent of the venture, is seeking to take a greater share of the Japanese beer market, which was worth $28bn in 2020, according to Euromonitor. The venture will be led by chief operating officer, Daisaku Okuda, an almost 20-year veteran of Asahi Japan.

As it expands internationally, BrewDog has also established a franchise agreement with Ace-Aloha Group in India, which will open up to 50 BrewDog venues there.

BrewDog, which was valued at almost £bn in its latest crowdfunding round, reported net revenues of £82m in 2020, up 4.2 per cent from a year earlier, but swung to a £3.1m pre-tax loss. As well as brewing, it has opened 99 bars and four hotels globally.

BrewDog’ beer sales were set to rise 25 per cent this year, Watt said. “iven all the challenges of last year, this year would be a result we are very happy with,”e added.

Founded in 2007, BrewDog has been the most high-profile group to emerge from the UK’ craft beer boom, raising more than £4m via crowdfunding. It drew attention in its earlier years through publicity stunts such as dropping taxidermy “at cats”rom a helicopter over London.

As well as the upheaval of the pandemic, the company faced criticism this year after a group of former staff accused it of having a misogynistic work culture, a “oxic”ttitude to junior employees, and pursuing “rowth at all costs”

Watt said the criticism was “omething we’e taking extremely seriously” BrewDog has appointed culture advisers Wiser to carry out an independent review, is pushing up salaries, and has set up an ethics hotline as part of a series of changes.

BrewDog also appointed Blythe Jack, managing director at private equity firm TSG Consumer Partners —hich bought a 22 per cent stake in 2017 —s its first chair following the claims. More governance changes would be unveiled ahead of the listing, said Watt.

headline: Brewdog  looks east before flotation plan
author: Dominic Walsh
text:
Brewdog is seeking to put recent allegations of a "rotten culture" behind it after launching a joint venture in Japan with Asahi Breweries as a prelude to an initial public offering (Dominic Walsh writes).

The Scottish brewer, which in June faced claims of sexist behaviour towards female staff, will retain a controlling 51 per cent interest in Brewdog Japan as it rolls out beers such as Punk IPA and Elvis Juice.

The joint venture with the Japanese market leader will be run by Daisaku Okuda, who has spent two decades at Asahi, most recently as general manager of spirits, based in Tokyo. Brewdog beers are available in Japan but the number of outlets where they are sold will increase significantly.

Asahi's distribution network will be used to grow Brewdog sales in Japan sixfold over the next five years.

It comes as Brewdog's co-founder, James Watt, right, seeks to address claims of "toxic attitudes". Watt apologised and promised to make changes. One was the appointment of Blythe Jack, managing director of TSG Consumer Partners which acquired a 22 per cent stake in Brewdog four years ago as its first chairman. Brewdog, valued recently at about £ billion plans a stock market flotation.

headline: BrewDog  names Allan Leighton as chair
author: Alice Hancock in London
text:
Appointment comes after former staff claimed craft brewer operated ‘ulture of fear’

BrewDog, the UK’ largest craft brewer, has appointed City grandee Allan Leighton as its chair three months after former staff accused the company of operating a “ulture of fear”

Leighton, who takes up his role immediately, has been brought in to provide “xperienced counsel on leadership and governance matters”nd will act as a mentor to BrewDog founder and chief executive James Watt, the group said.

Leighton is also chair of retailer The Co-Operative Group and the casual dining chain PizzaExpress. He was previously chief executive of supermarket group Asda and jewellery retailer Pandora, and has also chaired Royal Mail and Wagamama restaurants.

His appointment comes as BrewDog prepares to list on the stock market. Its initial public offering had been planned for 2020 until the pandemic hit.

“his is a very good business but it could be a great business. It has many points of uniqueness,”eighton told the Financial Times.

He likened the brewer to the engineering company Dyson, whose products shook up the traditional vacuum cleaning industry in the 1990s. “BrewDog can become a category all of its own in the same way,”e said.

Watt said: “Leighton] brings immense experience and a phenomenal track record to BrewDog. I have no doubt we will all benefit from his insight and leadership.”

Leighton will replace Blythe Jack, managing director at private equity company TSG Consumer Partners, who was made interim chair earlier this year. She was appointed to oversee a governance review following the accusations of 45 former employees who said in an open letter in June that the “ingle biggest shared experience of former staff is a residual feeling of fear”

TSG bought a 22 per cent stake in the Aberdeenshire-based brewer in 2017.

BrewDog has committed to an internal cultural review run by the advisory company Wiser and has pushed up staff salaries. The former employees said the company had been pursuing “rowth at all costs”

“e have been so high-growth in the past few years and had a difficult time coming through Covid and we haven’ always dealt with that challenge,”att told the FT last month.

Leighton said that BrewDog had dealt “easonably well”ith addressing the issues raised by former staff about its culture. “his is a high growth disruptive entrepreneurial business and when you have those ingredients you have growing pains,”e added.

Founded in 2007, BrewDog grew rapidly amid the UK’ craft beer boom raising more than £4m to date through crowdfunding.

It was valued at almost £bn in its latest crowdfunding round and posted a pre-tax loss of £3.1m for 2020 with net revenues of £82m.

BrewDog said on Monday that 99 of its 104 bars and hotels were now open and operating after lockdowns and that it had opened its third hotel, in Manchester, last month.

It is also planning new bar openings in Germany and the US.

headline: Disruption from UK’ truck driver shortage to deepen, warns logistics boss
author: Harry Dempsey
text:
More goods may fail to reach shops ahead of Christmas shopping season, says Wincanton chief

Disruption from the UK’ truck driver shortage is expected to deepen with more goods failing to reach shelves on the high street, the head of Britain’ largest logistics outsourcer has warned.

James Wroath, chief executive of Wincanton, said the extra strains were likely to hit the haulage industry as demand picks up ahead of the start of the Christmas shopping season on Black Friday at the end of November.

“he number of drivers will take time to increase. That’ not going to change anytime soon. At the moment, we’e in the calm before the storm,”e said. “he concern will be once the Black Friday and Christmas peaks come along.”

The company, which traces its roots back to milk delivery and works with household names including Argos, Heineken and Unilever, has vacancies for about 600 drivers, or 12 per cent of the near 5,000 it employs.

A combination of Brexit, the pandemic, tax reforms and driving test backlogs has left the UK short of an estimated 100,000 hauliers to carry goods to the country’ warehouses and retailers.

As a result, shortages of items from beer and milkshakes to building materials and furniture have emerged, while retailers have started to narrow the range of products they sell.

While noting that demand was difficult to predict this year, Wroath added that businesses most willing to pay more to secure haulage subcontractors would be least affected by the shortages.

“t will depend on what our customers are prepared to pay as the resources are squeezed. Supermarkets will be willing to pay more than most for subcontracting haulage,”e said.

Wincanton has benefited from a 20 per cent surge in revenues in its current financial year, driven by strong ecommerce demand, it said in a trading update. It made £.2bn of revenues in the year to March.

However, the Chippenham-based group is negotiating with customers to pass on extra costs to hire and retain drivers.

Wroath said consumers had been conditioned to think logistics services were free because of cheap next-day delivery offered by the likes of Amazon and grocery deliveries that only cost about £.

His company also acts as the link between hauliers and retailers when it does not have enough of its own drivers to handle the work.

He added that a temporary relaxation in rules around foreign drivers would help with the Christmas rush, although he said the government was unlikely to ease them.

Wincanton also said in its trading update that it had acquired Cygnia Logistics, which works with BrewDog, Moonpig and Molton Brown, for £3.9m.

headline: Primark-owner AB Foods raises profit target
author: Bryce Elder
text:
Plus, Antóio Horta-Osóio’ Credit Suisse power grab and Mohamed El-Erian

Primark owner AB Foods raised its annual profit target even after sales at its flagship retail chain fell short of expectations.

Better profit margins at Primark and an improved performance from the food business, in particular its sugar division, meant adjusted operating profit for the fourth quarter ending mid September ill be ahead of the previous year, AB said. Its guidance excluded the planned repayment of job retention scheme cash.

Inventory delays held back sales at Primark, whose profitability benefited from lower markdowns and abour osts. The sugar division beat expectations on rising prices and Chinese volumes.

Briefly

SThree said that based on strong trading and the timing of the planned increase in investment shifting into 2022, pre-tax profit for the year ending November will be significantly above market expectations. The staffing agency posted a 29 per cent improvement in third quarter net fees.

FirstGroup said that trading in the year to date has been in line with expectations set out at its full-year results in July. Passenger numbers have reached 65 per cent of pre-pandemic levels on average in recent weeks, it said. The transport group also set out a plan to return £00m to shareholders via a tender offer, which follows its sale of the First Student and First Transit divisions in July.

Sir Martin Sorrell’ S4 Capital raised gross profit guidance for the third time this year, with the marketing group now targeting 40 per cent like-for-like growth versus 35 per cent previously. S4’ half-year results showed gross profit up 49 per cent year on year on a like-for-like basis to £36.7m. The rate of growth accelerated to 66 per cent for the second quarter alone, S4 said. The acquisition roll-up reiterated a target to double in size organically over the three years to 2023.

Rolls-Royce and Babcock separately announced that they had agreed to sell their minority stakes in the AirTanker Holdings joint venture to Equitix Investment Management. AirTanker operates a fleet of Voyager ircraft that provide services including air-to-air refuelling to UK Ministry of Defence. Rolls said it had agreed to sell its 23.1 per cent stake in AirTanker for £89m cash, with Babcock’ 15.4 per cent stake sold for £26m.

Heathrow airport said passenger numbers were 71 per cent lower in August compared with pre-pandemic levels. John Holland-Kaye, chief executive, called on the UK government to streamline travel rules and protect international competitiveness. A slower recovery than peers means Heathrow is Europe’ tenth busiest airport, having held the top spot in 2019.

MP Evans, the producer of Indonesian palm oil, said higher prices meant half-year operating profit surged 588 per cent to $41.3m on a 69 per cent increase in revenue to $128m. Volumes of crop processed improved by 28 per cent.

BrewDog, the UK’ largest craft brewer, has appointed City grandee Allan Leighton as its chair three months after former staff accused the company of operating a “ulture of fear” Leighton takes up his role immediately.

Beyond the Square Mile

Credit Suisse’ new chair Antóio Horta-Osóio has taken on more executive duties at the Swiss bank in what insiders describe as a ower grab hat has diluted the authority of chief executive Thomas Gottstein.

Chinese regulators want to reak up Alipay, he 1bn plus-user super app owned by Jack Ma’ Ant Group and create a separate app for the company’ highly profitable loans business, in the most visible restructuring yet of the fintech giant.

A group of European deal advisers led by former Big Four accounting partners has announced a partnership with a US rival to try to cash in on the boom in transatlantic mergers and acquisitions. EOS Deal Advisory, EKEM Partners and AC Christes & Partner, advisory firms based in the UK, France and Germany respectively, will join forces with Virtas Partners, a 30-person business headquartered in Chicago.

Essential comment before you go

Mohamed El-Erian

Disrupted supply chains, high transportation costs, container scarcity and congested ports add up to stagflationary winds for the global economy. The dominant structural theme post the financial crisis —hat of deficient aggregate demand —as given way to frustrating supply rigidities. or companies and policymakers, containing further disruptions is cheaper and easier than having to clean up the damage.

Lex

Gene sequencing specialist Oxford Nanopore, which last week set out flotation plans, is in the shadow of larger competitor Illumina. But as Oxford’ versatile technology can expand the market, investors are likely to view the company as a credible innovator and the float should have no problems getting off the ground.

headline: BREWDOG  BAGS LEIGHTON
text:
Craft beer giant Brewdog has appointed former Asda boss Allan Leighton as chairman in a bid to move past previous criticism of its work culture by employees ahead of a planned stock market listing.

Leighton, a City grandee who serves as chairman of the Co-Op and Pizza Express, will take up the position immediately. His appointment follows a letter from former Brewdog staff in June that accused the company of creating a 'culture of fear'.

The accusations dented investor enthusiasm. However, the group will be hoping a series of apologies and reforms, as well as Leighton's reputation, will help it maintain favour ahead of its IPO, which has been pushed back by the coronavirus pandemic.

Aside from his stint at Asda, the Co-Op and Pizza Express, Leighton's CV also includes serving as chairman of Royal Mail and as chief executive of jewellery group Pandora.

Leighton (pictured) said Brewdog was 'a good business that can become a great business', and that it was simply experiencing 'growing pains'.

The 68-year-old will take over from Blythe Jack, a managing director of private equity firm TSG Consumer Partners, which bought a stake four years ago.

©aily Mail

headline: BUY KETCHUP AND TEA IN REFILLABLE PACKAGING AT TESCO
author: SEAN POULTER
text:
CUSTOMERS will be able to buy products ranging from laundry detergent to ketchup in reusable packaging.

Tesco has signed a deal with brands including Tetley, Persil, Heinz, Coca-Cola and BrewDog to sell items in refillable containers that consumers can return for them to be reused.

The initiative is similar to bottle deposit schemes and the items are cleaned and refilled before going back on the shelves. Shoppers pay a 20p deposit on the purchase, which is repaid when the container is returned to the store.

Tesco will include 88 products as part of a trial in ten stores, including 35 own-brand essentials such as pasta, rice and oil. They will be sold in glass jars and screw-top metal canisters, which can easily be reused. The scheme is run in partnership with the company Loop and is for those trying to tackle plastic waste.

©aily Mail

headline: BrewDog  appoints City grandee Leighton next chair
author: ALICE HANCOCK
text:
Food & beverage

BrewDog, the UK's largest craft brewer, has chosen City grandee Allan Leighton as chair three months after former staff accused the company of operating a "culture of fear".

Leighton, who takes up his role immediately, has been brought in to provide "experienced counsel on leadership and governance matters", and will act as a mentor to BrewDog founder and chief executive James Watt, the group said.

Leighton is also chair of retailer The Co-Operative Group and the casual dining chain PizzaExpress. He was previously chief executive of supermarket group Asda and jewellery retailer Pandora, and has also chaired Royal Mail and Wagamama restaurants.

His appointment comes as BrewDog prepares to list on the stock market. Its initial public offering had been planned for 2020 until the pandemic hit.

"This is a very good business but it could be a great business. It has many points of uniqueness," Leighton told the Financial Times.

He likened the brewer to the engineering company Dyson, whose products shook up the traditional vacuum cleaning industry in the 1990s. "Brew- Dog can become a category all of its own in the same way," he said.

Watt said: "[Leighton] brings immense experience and a phenomenal track record to BrewDog. I have no doubt we will all benefit from his insight and leadership."

Leighton will replace Blythe Jack, managing director at private equity company TSG Consumer Partners, who was made interim chair earlier this year. She was appointed to oversee a governance review following accusations from 45 former employees who said in an open letter in June that the "single biggest shared experience of former staff is a residual feeling of fear".

TSG bought a 22 per cent stake in the Aberdeenshire-based brewer in 2017.

BrewDog has committed to an internal cultural review run by the advisory company Wiser and has pushed up staff salaries. The former employees said the company had been pursuing "growth at all costs".

Leighton said BrewDog had dealt "reasonably well" with addressing the issues raised by former staff about its culture. "This is a high-growth, disruptive, entrepreneurial business, and when you have those ingredients, you have growing pains."

Founded in 2007, BrewDog grew rapidly amid the UK's craft beer boom, raising more than £4m to date through crowdfunding. It was valued at almost £bn in its latest crowdfunding round and posted a pre-tax loss of £3.1m for 2020 with net revenues of £82m.

BrewDog said yesterday that 99 of its 104 bars and hotels were now open and operating after lockdowns and that it had opened its third hotel, in Manchester, last month.

It is also planning new bar openings in Germany and the US.

'This is a very good business but it could be a great business. It has many points of uniqueness'

headline: FIRST GROUP'S £00M BOOST FOR INVESTORS; MARKET REPORT
author: CALUM MUIRHEAD
text:
First Group, one of the UK's largest train and bus operators, has confirmed plans to hand back £00m to shareholders.

In a trading update ahead of its AGM, the Aberdeen-based firm, which runs four rail franchises including Avanti West Coast and around a fifth of local bus services, said it will plan to return as much of the cash as possible by buying back shares, with any remaining sum to be paid through a special dividend. The bumper payout was first announced in July after the company raked in £.3bn from the sale of its US transport and school bus businesses.

First also said the number of passengers riding its buses had reached 65pc of pre-pandemic levels in recent weeks, a figure it expected to rise further as schools and universities returned for the autumn term. It added that overall trading in the year to date had been in line with expectations.

'With a well-capitalised balance sheet and an operating model that will support an attractive dividend for shareholders commencing in 2022, I am confident that First Group is well-placed to deliver sustainable value creation,' said chairman David Martin.

Martin might be optimistic about the company's prospects, but his own were put into sharp focus by the AGM results.

Just over 20pc of votes cast were against his re-election to the board, while board director Warwick Brady had the same pushback. The votes are not binding - but follow a campaign by its largest investor, activist group Coast Capital, which believes the two are ill-suited to the company.

First Group shares rose 3.2pc, or 2.8p, to 89.3p.

The FTSE 100 climbed 0.6pc, or 39.23 points, to 7098.43 during the session, while the FTSE 250 added 0.2pc, or 42.17 points, to 23775.73. The mood seemed positive, however, the outlook was bleaker among professionals in a Deutsche Bank survey, 58pc of whom expected stock markets to fall between 5pc and 10pc before the end of the year, while 10pc predicted the drop could be even bigger.

Metal markets were also in focus as prices of aluminium hit a 13-year high of $3,000 (£,171) a tonne as China tightened output controls on the metal, which is used to make drinks cans and aircraft parts. A military coup in Guinea has also raised concerns about the availability of bauxite, a key ingredient in aluminium production.

Meanwhile, oil prices climbed to their highest levels since late July, with Brent crude rising 0.7pc to more than $73 a barrel as the damage from Hurricane Ida continued to dent US production.

Oil major BP was up 2.3pc, or 6.65p, at 302.3p while rival Shell rose 2.2pc, or 31.8p, to 1456.6p.

Abcam, which supplies research tools to scientists, reported that it had swung to a £0.3m profit in the six months to the end of June from a £6.1m loss in the same period last year as lab activity continued to recover to pre-pandemic levels. However, the shares dropped 3.2pc, or 48p, to 1470p as the company warned foreign exchange headwinds were expected to weigh on revenue growth going forward.

Podcast network Audioboom slumped 3.5pc, or 30p, to 830p after private equity firm All Active Asset Capital withdrew its takeover offer, saying the company's management had 'ignored the wishes of shareholders' by not engaging with the proposal.

Audioboom itself issued an upbeat trading update, saying revenues for 2021 will be significantly ahead of previous expectations.

In the risers, FTSE 250 exhibition organiser Ascential ticked up 0.9pc, or 3.6p to 417.4p as it said it had purchased Onespace, a company that helps brands increase sales online.

Logistics firm Wincanton rose 0.5pc, or 2p, to 384p as it unveiled plans to buy Cygnia Logistics, a provider of supply chain services for the likes of Brewdog, Moonpig and Molton Brown, for £3.9m.

©aily Mail

headline: Former Asda boss steps in at  BrewDog
author: Greig Cameron
text:
Allan Leighton, a former boss of Asda, is to join BrewDog as the company tries to rebuild its reputation after dozens of former staff accused it of being a "toxic" place to work.

Leighton is to head the board at the Aberdeenshire brewer after a career at the sharp end of the corporate world. He has been chairman at Royal Mail, Matalan and the Co-operative Group as well as sitting on the boards of BskyB, Dyson and Selfridges.

The 68-year-old is joining BrewDog, which also runs bars and hotels, as it reviews its practices and tries to prepare for a stock market flotation. The company's Punk IPA is one of the bestselling craft beers in the UK.

A critical open letter from about 60 former staff, dubbed "Punks With Purpose" was published in June. It claimed there was a "culture of fear".

James Watt, 39, the co-founder and chief executive, has already apologised. The company also hired about 100 more workers as it was "under resourced" in certain areas.

Yesterday Watt said he was looking forward to learning from Leighton. Speaking to BBC Scotland, he said: "Pretty much everyone saw [the letter] and it didn't make pleasant or positive reading. All we can do is respond to it in the best way we possibly can."

Blythe Jack, 47, had been chairwoman and will remain on the board.

Watt confirmed that BrewDog's latest crowdfunding round had raised £0.2 million, with much of that being allocated to sustainability and business expansion.

Leighton is in the chair as BrewDog looks to float, page 36

headline: Leighton is in the chair as  BrewDog  looks to float
author: Dominic Walsh; Greig Cameron
text:
BrewDog has taken a further step towards an eventual stock market flotation by naming Allan Leighton as its non-executive chairman.

The former Asda boss's appointment comes only four months after BrewDog appointed Blythe Jack, managing director of TSG Consumer Partners, which acquired a 22 per cent stake in BrewDog four years ago, as its first chairman.

The Scottish brewer said that part of Jack's role when she was appointed was "overseeing the recruitment process for a fully independent chairman in order to ensure the composition of the board is moving towards meeting the requirements of the UK corporate governance code".

It said that she would remain on the board as deputy chairwoman and would continue to oversee the review of BrewDog's corporate culture, which was announced in June in the wake of allegations of sexist and misogynistic behaviour towards female staff and "toxic attitudes towards junior staff".

A group of rebels calling themselves Punks with Purpose has been particularly critical of James Watt, the company's co-founder and chief executive, accusing him of presiding over a culture that left many workers "burnt-out, afraid and miserable".

Watt, 39, has been contrite in his response, insisting that there were "no excuses" for the way in which staff were treated and promising to make big changes. The company said that he would be mentored by Leighton "on leadership and governance matters as the company enters its next phase of growth".

Watt said: "Allan brings a fantastic amount of experience as CEO of larger companies, but also as chairman of some amazing businesses as well. It is going to be fantastic for me personally to have such an experienced business leader acting as a mentor."

Leighton, 68, has one of the most impressive CVs in the City, chairing the Co-operative Group, Element Materials Technology Group and PizzaExpress and being a former chairman of companies including Royal Mail, Entertainment One and Wagamama.

Appointing an independent chairman of his stature will provide a boost to the 14-year-old brewer as it plots a stock market float, probably in London, although it has not ruled out listing in

New York. It was valued at more than £ billion in its most recent crowdfunding round. That funding round, which was completed last week, led to 74,000 investors, including about 68,000 new backers, putting in £0.2 million.

The company, which has a hundred bars and four hotels around the world as well as breweries in Scotland, Ohio, Berlin and Brisbane, has raised a total of almost £00 million through crowdfunding since it was founded by Watt and Martin Dickie, 39, in 2007.

A week ago the company expanded its Asian presence by launching a joint venture in Japan with Asahi Breweries. It will retain a controlling 60 per cent interest in BrewDog Japan as it rolls out beers such as Punk IPA, Hazy Jane and Elvis Juice.

Leighton said that BrewDog had built a remarkable market position in a short space of time and was "a good business that can become a great business''. Referring to its recent problems, he said that it was a "high-growth, very entrepreneurial business" that was experiencing "growing pains ... It was slightly unfortunate, but it's something that can be rectified very quickly."

He confirmed that an initial public offering was under consideration and that Rothschild had been appointed to assess options. Asked whether Brew- Dog could float next year, Leighton said "at the earliest", emphasising that other options would be considered.

On the to-do list

Culture The furore over the "toxic culture" at BrewDog during the appeared to doubts over its but Leighton it as "growing pains". He is used to dealing with change, governance and culture and indicated it was an that would not much to resolve.

Initial public offering The role of chairman in a company eyeing up a stock market listing is key. Sorting out the culture is important to ensure a contented and effective workforce and to preserve BrewDog's record of strong momentum and high growth, which in turn are what the public markets demand to support an IPO.

Image Having positioned itself as a craft brewer with a disdain for so-called industrial brewers, there is a danger that BrewDog could come across as hypocritical and its marketing narrative could start to appear hollow.

Leighton's marketing prowess and experience will be invaluable in avoiding such pitfalls.

headline: Wincanton warns on UK truck driver shortage
author: HARRY DEMPSEY
text:
Transport

Disruption from the UK's truck driver shortage is expected to deepen with more goods failing to reach shelves on the high street, the head of Britain's largest logistics outsourcer has warned.

James Wroath, chief executive of Wincanton, said the extra strains were likely to hit the haulage industry as demand picked up ahead of the start of the Christmas shopping season on Black Friday at the end of November.

He said: "The number of drivers will take time to increase. That's not going to change anytime soon. At the moment, we're in the calm before the storm. The concern will be once the Black Friday and Christmas peaks come along."

The company, which traces its roots back to milk delivery and works with household names including Argos, Heineken and Unilever, has vacancies for about 600 drivers, or 12 per cent of the nearly 5,000 people it employs.

A combination of Brexit, the pandemic, tax reforms and driving test backlogs has left the UK short of an estimated 100,000 hauliers to carry goods to the country's warehouses and retailers.

As a result, shortages of items from beer and milkshakes to building materials and furniture have emerged, and retailers have started to narrow the range of products they sell.

While noting that demand was difficult to predict this year, Wroath added that businesses that were most willing to pay more to secure haulage subcontractors would be the least affected by the shortages.

"It will depend on what our customers are prepared to pay as the resources are squeezed. Supermarkets will be willing to pay more than most for subcontracting haulage," he said.

Wincanton had benefited from a 20 per cent surge in revenues in its current financial year, driven by strong ecommerce demand, it said in a trading update. It made £.2bn of revenues in the year to March.

However, the Chippenham-based group is negotiating with customers to pass on extra costs to hire and retain drivers.

Wroath said that consumers had been conditioned to think logistics services were free because of cheap next-day delivery offered by the likes of Amazon and grocery deliveries that only cost about £.

His company also acts as the link between hauliers and retailers when it does not have enough of its own drivers to handle the work.

He said a temporary relaxation in rules around foreign drivers would help with the Christmas rush, although he said the government was unlikely to ease them.

Wincanton also said in its trading update that it had acquired Cygnia Logistics, which works with BrewDog, Moonpig and Molton Brown, for £3.9m.

headline: DON'T CLAIM THESE DRINKS ARE HEALTHY, PRODUCERS WARNED
author: SEAN POULTER
text:
DRINKS giants have been warned against trying to cash in on the boom in 'alcoholic water' by suggesting it is a healthy or diet option.

The craze for these 'hard seltzers' has come from the US and British drinks brands and supermarkets have created their own versions, which are designed to appeal to women.

But watchdogs are concerned that firms are trying to make health, wellness and diet claims about the drinks, which are typically a mix of sparkling water and alcohol at 4-6 per cent.

And health experts are worried that they can look like soft drinks.

Dr Richard Piper, chief executive of campaign group Alcohol Change UK, said: 'We do have a concern about the similarity in packaging of some hard seltzers and their "soft" equivalents. These products can look particularly attractive to under-age drinkers and is yet further evidence that products containing alcohol need clearer and more prominent health warnings, to protect and inform us all.

'The advertising rules state that alcoholic drinks cannot be presented as a healthy option or make any weight-control claims.' The Advertising Standards Authority (ASA) has issued guidance to manufacturers after upholding complaints about the marketing of several hard seltzers.

It said: 'Hard seltzers have taken off this year, and drinks manufacturers are piling in to bring us their own unique twist.

'Ads for hard seltzer drinks have often associated them with a modern, conscious consumer who cares about what they put into their bodies. So marketers should be especially cautious not to make unauthorised health or nutrition claims about hard seltzers, or to mislead about the amount of alcohol they contain.'

Earlier this year, the ASA upheld complaints about several products, including a drink called Whisp.

Its website described it as a 'refreshing, low calorie, lightly alcoholic sparkling water', which was the 'perfect accomplice to a balanced lifestyle'.

A firm called High-Water was criticised by the ASA for marketing its hard seltzers on the basis they contain under 100 calories per can.

Social media posts for one brand called DRTY were banned because they carried health and diet hashtags, including # lowcalorie, #nosugardiet, #zerosugar, #keto, #ketodiet, #carbfree, and #nocarbs.

BrewDog was censured by the ASA after it used its marketing to mock the fact that it is not officially allowed to describe its product - Clean & Press Hard Seltzer - as healthy.

On Instagram, it wrote: 'Even though Clean & Press is only 90 calories per can, with no carbs or sugar and a little bit of alcohol, this is not a health drink. If you are looking for a health drink, do not drink Clean & Press.'

©aily Mail

headline: Happy staff = success in any industry
author: Shaf Rasul
text:
1SM Monday, September

Shaf Rasul One in a million shaf@the-sun.co.uk

YOU'LL recall the backlash that Brewdog faced following 70-plus staff members signing an open letter to the company, criticising the business and its employment practices.

The PR disaster unfolded in June, but it's only now that co-founder James Watt has spoken out on the issues faced by the Aberdeenshire brewery firm.

Yes, he publicly apologised at the time, but many questioned whether this would be enough to save his and the brand's reputation, with accusations of bullying, a culture of fear and a toxic workplace.

Last week, James spoke to Good Morning Scotland to address the changes they've put in place since the allegations came to light and, perhaps wisely, he put his hands up and admitted to mistakes on his part.

He acknowledged that Brewdog has not always been the best employer, nor has he been the best people manager, and I have to say I think it's at least commendable that he admits his part in the issue.

I'm not making excuses for him as I can see how employees have been negatively affected but it can be hard to consistently get things right within a leadership position.

Brewdog's meteoric rise to become one of the most wellknown Scottish breweries came around rather quickly, possibly forcing him into a role he wasn't equipped to deal with so soon.

COMPLAINTS

However, the company has hired former Asda chief executive Allan Leighton to step in as a non-executive chairman and mentor, so that they can learn from past mistakes and ensure it never happens again.

The brand has also promised a three per cent salary increase for all employees, along with a structural review of areas in the business that are believed to be under-resourced.

I hope that these changes show current employees that there will be positive change.

This is just one example of how people management is so important to a company.

As an entrepreneur I know that people are the heart of any business. If your employees don't feel valued and they are not treated respectfully then it will ultimately result in complaints, people leaving and, if problems continue to go unanswered, it can result in a media storm.

Similarly, when I was reading the accusations around telly chef Tom Kitchin and his restaurant business, I was gobsmacked.

No matter what industry you are working in, the bullying and harassment that is alleged to have taken place is unacceptable whether it's from bosses or from other colleagues. Following these accusations against Kitchin's business (which are being investigated by an outside team of HR specialists), others from within the hospitality sector claim to have suffered at the hands of former bosses of various restaurants, both physically and mentally.

Right now, when the sector is in desperate need of a recruitment boost, allegations of this nature are certainly damaging to the reputation of the industry in general.

With so many brilliant opportunities available across Scotland in hospitality and customer-facing jobs, many people will be put off after reading those allegations.

It's a real shame as I know just how excellent and rewarding a career in the hospitality industry can be.

What it highlights for me is the power of a team coming together to stand up for something and the importance of good people management. This should be a warning to other businesses. They should be looking at their structure and leadership teams and contemplating if they need to make amends, before the next media storm is about them.

Everyone deserves to have a positive workplace, and employees should be determined to work hard and put in the dedication and time, however we should also strive to create a work/life balance which allows us to enjoy our work, feel valued and supported, whilst also maintaining a social life.

This is what brands should be aiming for, especially following the pandemic.

The time is now to create working practices to ensure that employees are fully motivated and have great morale.

COMPLAINTS

headline: 'Green lairds' urged to share their land
author: Jeremy Watson
text:
Scotland is on the brink of a North Sea oil-type boom with businesses looking to invest millions in Highland estates to meet their climate change objectives.

The land rush is being led by companies such as Standard Life and Brewdog, which are seeking to offset their carbon emissions with their purchases. They will also increase the value of the land and boost profits by taking advantage of generous government grants for initiatives such as tree planting and peat restoration.

However, the Scottish Land Commission (SLC), a government agency that promotes land reform, is warning that the new breed of "green lairds" risk perpetuating an old model of private land ownership. The commission is welcoming the boom in interest in the "natural capital" of land, but it wants profits shared more equally between owners and local communities.

Some politicians fear company purchases of estates will stop local communities from being able to mount bids when land comes up for sale.

Hamish Trench, the SLC's chief executive will tell a Land Connections conference today that the investment in land has the potential to bring significant change to Scotland's environment and drive a transition to a net-zero economy. However, there are risks to address so that the market works in the public interest.

"There is an opportunity for a winwin, a transformation that benefits nature and people," Trench will say. "There is no question that this new investment will be much needed in delivering the land use outcomes Scotland is looking for, but there are questions to consider about unintended consequences and about how emerging value is used fairly and productively."

"Some of the early community land acquisitions like Eigg and Knoydart were established with collaborative, partnership governance arrangements across sectors," Trench said. "It seems time to revisit these ideas now in establishing land ownership structures that are accountable, resilient and deliver on net zero."

Standard Life Investments Property Income Trust recently spent £.5 million buying 1,447 hectares in the Cairngorm National Park to be used as part of the company's carbon strategy. Ellon-based Brewdog has bought more than 3,600 hectares in the Cairngorms to reduce its carbon footprint Rhoda Grant, Labour MSP for the Highlands & Islands, wants tighter regulation. She said: "The emergence of the green lairds shines a light on the inadequacy of our land laws, and it also shines a light on how we subsidise the creation of private wealth from owning land when we could be building community wealth instead."

Jeremy Leggett, a landowner who made millions from solar panels and is a former director of Greenpeace, suggested widespread shared ownership of estates as the way forward. This month he is planning to sell one of his two recently purchased rewilding estates, Beldorney near Huntly in Aberdeenshire, to a new "mass ownership" company, for as little as £0 per share.

? Nature conservation workers have accused environmental charities of paying "slave wages", claiming employees get burnt out by long hours and cannot afford to buy homes in Twitter posts highlighted by the Scottish Greenkeepers' Association, which says many of charities exploit demoralised workers while sitting on multimillionpound financial reserves. One environmentalist and bat ecologist wrote that "the field of conservation in my opinion is still reserved for upper classes unless you are ok to live extremely simply".

Scottish LINK, which represents environmental charities, said: "Members are committed to paying their staff a fair wage while aiming to ensure all staff and volunteers are in productive and satisfying jobs that enable them to build experience and enjoyment."

headline: Three cheers for going green from bottoms up
text:
Melanie Reid Notebook

In homage to an important conference coming shortly to a city near me, I'm going more green. Although as individuals we can only do what we can do, I find it's a great way to cheer myself up.

It's not smugness. It's excitement about clever ideas being turned into viable businesses. I'm swithering, for instance, over whether my first order of Who Gives a Crap toilet rolls should be 100 per cent recycled paper (most manufacturers fell softwoods) or bamboo. Bamboo for the world's bottoms could be an eco game-changer (and pleasantly soft, apparently). It is grass, grows lightning fast and requires little water. Right now it's shipped from China, but would grow here too. Besides, Who Gives a Crap donates half its profits to build toilets and sanitation in the developing world. Too much to hope its products will be in the hotel bathrooms of Cop26 delegates.

I also like the sound of Loop, a spin-off from the recycling multinational TerraCycle. Loop does away with single-use products, like shampoo bottles, by running a circular system of refillable containers between brands and retailers. Last month it went mainstream. Ten Tesco stores in England started selling household goods in containers that are returned (or collected by Loop from doorsteps) and reused. Brands such as Fever-Tree, BrewDog, Carex, Alberto Balsam, Coca-Cola, Ecover, Heinz, Persil, Quaker Oats and Tetley are participating. Plus lots of Tesco own-brand stuff.

Roll on the scheme going nationwide. The time is right. It's hard to pear

An appeal to amateur fruit growers. Can you do anything with an old conference pear tree that produces fruit as hard as wood? Apart from chainsaw it. I have a peculiar love of hard pears, but these are genuinely inedible. Even cooking doesn't soften them. Message from pod

On the Isle of Harris last month, driving alongside Luskentyre beach, friends pointed out to me a rather bedraggled local curiosity. It was a pod from Castaway, the millennium BBC series that predated Big Brother, arguably pioneered reality TV, and launched the career of one Ben Fogle. The pod was pretty derelict. Someone had retrieved it from nearby Taransay, the island where 36 random men, women and children volunteered to live for a year and be filmed creating a self-sufficient community.

As well as being a cultural relic, the pod also struck me as an uncanny blueprint for a million similar eco-huts now being built on every hillside for holidaymakers hungry to reconnect with nature. There was no blue heritage plaque on this one for Fogle. By coincidence, though, tomorrow he launches a four-part BBC series Scotland's Sacred Islands, a personal pilgrimage around the Hebrides and north to Shetland. Wiser and more thoughtful than the fruity young posh-boy of 2000, he told the Herald the spirituality of the islands now reminds him of the conservationist John Muir's words: "I'd rather be in the mountains thinking of God, than in church thinking about the mountains." Automatically unhelpful

Alingering curse from Covid is that lots of petrol stations installed pre-pay card systems. Many drivers struggle with them, predominantly older and less computer-savvy ones. At our local garage the trauma isn't lack of fuel there's a civilised £0 limit and zero fighting but the dreaded card machine. My husband and several friends are so scared of it they go elsewhere. I'm reminded of the filling station in Bearsden that years after automation kept a pump attendant especially to cater for the many local elderly drivers. The marriage of youth and technology can, without intending to be unkind, be a discriminatory force.

@mel_reidtimes

headline: Wincanton races ahead by steering clear of crisis
author: Jessica Newman
text:
Jessica Newman Market report

The disruption caused by the UK's lorry driver shortage has wreaked havoc among many businesses, especially those in the haulage industry. But for Wincanton, Britain's largest logistics outsourcer, the crisis looks more like a bump in the road. The company enjoyed its best singleday rally in nearly nine months as shares rose 24p, or 7.3 per cent, to 355p after it told investors it remained on track to deliver full-year profit in line with expectations.

In a trading update before its interim results, the Chippenhambased company said revenues continued to grow in the first half of the year, including contributions from all four of the group's sectors, which are digital and fulfilment; grocery and consumer; general merchandise; and public and industrial.

Wincanton, whose customers include Sainsbury's, WH Smith and Tesco, also said its pipeline of new contracts and extensions of existing agreements "remains encouraging". The group has recently completed the £3.9 million acquisition of Cygnia Logistics, an ecommerce and e-fulfilment provider which works with the likes of Moonpig and BrewDog, and has secured a five-year extension to its contract with BAE Systems, which will see Wincanton provide transport, warehousing and inspection services to the aerospace and arms company.

The company said it was "working closely" with customers to mitigate the impact of the driver shortages. Last month James Wroath, chief executive, said it was negotiating with customers to pass on extra costs to hire and retain drivers. The company added that the recent fuel shortages did not affect its profitability.

Reversing Monday's slide, the FTSE 100 rallied 66.09 points, or 0.9 per cent, to 7,077.1 and the FTSE 250 index recouped 75.73 points, or 0.3 per cent, to 22,730.65 Heavyweight banking stocks buoyed London's premier index after positive notes from analysts. Barclays rose 7½, or 3.9 per cent, to 194¾ while Lloyds Banking Group added 1¾, or 3.9 per cent, to 46½. Analysts at Credit Suisse said recent weakness in the Lloyds share price was a "buying opportunity" and were also upbeat about Barclays' third-quarter update, increasing its target price to 220p and reiterating an "outperform" rating.

The UK's automotive industry took a hit after the Society of Motor Manufacturers, the industry's trade body, said new car registrations slumped 35 per cent compared with the same month last year its lowest levels in September for more than two decades. Aston Martin Lagonda, London's only listed carmaker, shed 33p, or 1.9 per cent, to £7.51 while Melrose, the engineering group dropped 1¾, or 1.1 per cent, to 169p as it also warned that its automotive business was being hit by customers delaying orders due to the shortage of computer chips.

Investors continued to steer clear of The Hut Group, which slid for a ninth straight trading session. THG closed down by a further 21¾, or 4.9 per cent, to 418½ following a 10 per cent slump last Friday that knocked the stock below the 500p listing price set last year.

Despite a warning about continuing supply issues for its business, investors tucked into Greggs after the baker raised its full-year profit expectations. The company closed up 319p, or 11.1 per cent, to £1.92 its dearest price on record.

Elsewhere, Darktrace, the cybersecurity specialist, saw its shares rise 50p, or 6.3 per cent, to 840½, after buyers flocked to the stock following last week's sell-off.

headline: Restaurant staff must be served well
author: Shaf Rasul
text:
Shaf Rasul One in a million shaf@the-sun.co.uk

AS we all know, hospitality businesses have been hit hard over the last 17 months and the pressure faced by the industry doesn't seem to be letting up any time soon.

Supply chain issues, vaccine passports and recruitment have been hot topics across the media since lockdowns began and the effect on hospitality professionals has been detrimental to not only morale, but the mental health of these frontline workers.

Owners of businesses within this industry must begin to focus on these issues and take steps to look after their teams, especially if they intend to expand, and survive, the next few months. I touched on this two weeks ago when discussing the ongoing PR challenges of both Brewdog and Tom Kitchin.

Which is why it was so refreshing to see Buzzworks Holdings make the positive decision to introduce a Wellbeing

Day and close all thirteen of its bars and restaurants across Scotland last week.

The unusual move from the independent hospitality business allowed over 500 team members to enjoy outdoor activities designed to boost their wellbeing and mental health and meet up with colleagues. These included archery, crazy golf and cycling around Millport. All activities were decided by local, on the ground restaurant teams, with the only rule being that it had to make people feel great.

The Buzzworks brand has created a strong community spirit and passionately supports the areas it operates in. So much so, that due to its wellbeing initiatives and local charity work, it was awarded the newlycreated Catering in Scotland Excellence Wellbeing in Hospitality Award back in Summer 2021.

One of the initiatives that won this honour was popular Ayrshire restaurant on Scotland's West Coast, Lido Troon, raising a whopping £0,000 during lockdown with the introduction of a pop-up café

Half the proceeds went towards funding the Wellbeing Day with the remaining amount going to industry charity, Hospitality Action, to support the greater hospitality family across Scotland and beyond.

As tourism and hospitality are essentially the backbone of Scotland's economy, I am encouraged to see companies taking these steps to improve the industry's reputation and commit to the changes they are implementing. Buzzworks has already set aside £0,000 towards its 2022 Wellbeing Day and I hope other companies follow the lead in the dedication to their teams.

Stories of American-based companies closing for a week and giving employees paid time off, such as Nike and the popular dating site, Bumble, have been cropping up over the last few months but Buzzworks appears to be one of, if not the first in the UK, to take this approach.

Will this be a new trend going forward and how beneficial is this for businesses in general? On one hand, staff shortages are already causing smaller independent restaurant to reduce their opening days.

One of Glasgow's best res- taurants, The Gannet, announced that, from September, it will only be opening Thursday to Sunday due to a lack of team members and chefs to cover the rest of the week, however, there was also a mental health aspect to the decision.

Owners Peter McKenna and Ivan Stein, pictured inset, who are trained chefs themselves, were keen to ensure the team had a good work life balance and by reducing the opening days, they could ensure that this was still the case going forward.

On the other hand, will incentives such as mental health and wellbeing days encourage people to consider the service industry as a lifelong career, rather than a stop gap during the holidays or while studying? Higher wages are the obvious answer to enticing people to the industry, but this comes with a cost of higher menu prices and, would it really be enough to compensate workers for dealing with the stressful environment?

Coincidently, October is Mental Health Month with Mental Health Day being observed yesterday and there are some fantastic resources available for managers and team leaders on how best to support workplace mental wellbeing.

Let's also remember to #BeKindtoHospitality and that, without the patience, understanding and kindness of the general public, our famous Scottish hospitality could very soon be gone forever.

headline: Cautious  BrewDog  puts listing on ice
author: James Hurley; Dominic Walsh
text:
The UK's largest craft brewer has indicated that it will delay a planned £.1 billion flotation amid turbulence in the hospitality industry.

James Watt, BrewDog's chief executive and co-founder, said a listing might not take place until 2023 after advisers and banks were said to have recommended that a delay would be prudent given pandemic-related issues facing the hospitality industry.

The company has appointed Allan Leighton, the former Asda boss, as nonexecutive chairman, heightening speculation that a flotation could be imminent. However, in an interview with the The Daily Telegraph, Watt, 39, appeared to cool these expectations. "Could it be sometime in 2022? Maybe. 23? Maybe."

He denied the delay was connected to accusations from a group of former employees that the business had fostered a "culture of fear", telling the newspaper: "That happened after we made the decision to push things back."

Instead, investor nervousness over the prospects for hospitality after the pandemic is said to be to blame, with the industry facing uncertain demand, staff shortages, supply chain disruption and inflationary pressures. Leighton told The Times last month that 2022 was "the earliest" the company might float and other options would be considered.

BrewDog has about 100 sites around the world and had annual sales of £38 million in 2020. It was co-founded by Watt and Martin Dickie, 39, in 2007.

In April BrewDog said it had hired Rothschild to advise on the float after Covid-19 pushed back plans for a possible 2020 listing.

The Times revealed in June that Watt was divesting personal property assets that BrewDog had paid him hundreds of thousands of pounds to use. The firm said that all related-party transactions were subject to review as it "looks ahead to a future IPO". Watt said the arrangements benefited the firm as it grew.

The Watt interview threatened to reignite controversy over allegations of bullying earlier this year from a group of former employees. Watt had earlier apologised to staff and said that the brewer was "committed to doing better". In an open letter from former employees in the summer, Watt was accused of propagating a culture of fear that had left many former workers "burnt out, afraid and miserable".

He told the Telegraph that the brewer "should have been clearer about the high performance culture" and suggested there was a "mismatch of expectations" among certain employees who wanted the "dynamism" of a highgrowth company but the "steady state, perks and benefits" of a more established employer.

Watt and BrewDog were approached for comment.

headline: Need to know
text:
1 The price of beer is predicted to increase by 30p a pint even before the chancellor considers any increases in duty in the budget. More than eight in ten pubs say they have or are about to raise prices because of increased costs including higher wages and the rising energy charges, plus supply shortages.

Page 2

Page 8

Retirement packages for public sector workers have left the country with a £.4 trillion liability. The national debt is £.2 trillion.

Page 24
