headline: Solid gold claim leaves  BrewDog  winners flat
author: Stuart MacDonald
text:
BrewDog has been criticised by advertising watchdogs for a competition where customers won beer cans wrongly billed as being made of "solid gold".

The craft brewer launched two promotions where 15 of the cans were hidden inside beer multipacks. It said each was worth £5,000.

However, the cans were only gold-plated and contained a tiny amount of the precious metal.

The Advertising Standards Authority investigated after 25 people complained that the prize was not made from solid gold.

BrewDog told the authority that the "solid gold" claim had been made in error in three social media posts and that it had publicly apologised for it. However the company, based in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, stood by the estimate that the winning cans were worth £5,000.

The company responded to the ruling by announcing a new competition giving people the chance to win one of ten diamond-encrusted gold-plated cans. Winners will have the option of claiming a can worth £5,000, or a cash alternative.

James Watt, co-founder of BrewDog, said: "We hold our hands up, we got the first gold can campaign wrong. Whilst we still stand by the valuation, we made mistakes which we've learnt from."

The new contest would be "a short campaign over ten days, and we'll contact a new winner each day to let them know they've won".

The advertising watchdog has banned the initial adverts, posted in November 2020 and February this year, for breaching the advertising code by making misleading claims.

The authority said: "We considered a general audience was unlikely to be aware of the price of gold, how that would translate into the price of a gold can, and whether that was inconsistent with the valuation as stated in the ad.

"However, we understood the prize consisted of 24-carat gold-plated replica cans. Therefore, because the ads stated that the prize included a 'solid gold' can when that was not the case, we concluded the ads were misleading.

"We considered that because the awarded prize was not the same as that described in the ads, the promotion caused unnecessary disappointment to participants and therefore breached the code. We told BrewDog not to state or imply that consumers would receive a solid gold can when that was not the case."

The authority added that it had told BrewDog "to conduct their promotions equitably and fairly, and to avoid causing unnecessary disappointment".

Mark Craig, from Lisburn, Northern Ireland, was among those who snapped up cases of Punk IPA to find one of the gold versions inside.

Craig, 32, who had hoped to fund his wedding by selling the gold, discovered the can was only plated with the metal after asking for a certificate from Brew- Dog. Speaking in June, he said: "I ultimately feel disappointed. When I won I was ecstatic, believing a solid gold can could contribute towards bills and our wedding, which has been postponed until 2022."

BrewDog said that the promotional cans were gold-plated and were manufactured by the goldsmiths Thomas Lyte using materials of the highest quality. The company provided a certificate from the manufacturer to confirm the cans were plated in 24-carat gold.

BrewDog said it had amended the posts as soon as that error was noticed. However, it explained that it was unable to amend tweets and therefore rather than delete the tweet, it ensured that subsequent tweets dropped the word "solid", and all references thereafter were to "gold cans".

The company claimed that a 330ml can, made with the equivalent 330ml of pure gold, would have a gold value of about $500,000 at the current gold price of $1,800 per ounce.

It could not see "that any reasonable consumer who entered the competition would assume they were going to win over half a million dollars of gold". The company said it had never been provided with a valuation which contradicted its estimated value.

BrewDog, which has been valued at up to £ billion, was founded in 2007 by James Watt and Martin Dickie, who met at school in Aberdeenshire.

headline: BEER SO SORRY.. HERE'S THE £5K
author: STUART PATTERSON
text:
A BREWDOG boss yesterday vowed to personally pay £5,000 each to winners of beer cans wrongly billed as solid gold.

Chief executive James Watt confirmed he will forego two and a half years of his salary to give a total of £20,000 to 28 raging IPA fans.

The firm, based in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, was slammed by ad watchdogs as the cans in last November's giveaway turned out to be goldplated and worth a fraction of their supposed value.

We told yesterday, right, how bosses launched a new competition ? threatening saying was of insists: this time offering punters 25 diamond-encrusted gold cans or the cash alternative of £5,000 each.

Confirming his cash offer, Mr Watt said yesterday: "In fairness, we should have done this earlier."

Mark Craig, 33, who landed a gold can, has been contacted by Brewdog about the cash windfall along with his fellow winners. He said: "Why didn't he get in touch with the winners before he announced this online? But I am delightedbecause it's worked out well for me."

headline: Pawternity leave is a step too far in the pandemic pet boom
author: Pilita Clark
text:
Demands for time off to be with lockdown pets is jarring when millions still lack paid parental leave

A third of UK workers would consider changing jobs rather than work with an unvaccinated colleague, a survey claimed last week.

But what if that colleague also had claws? And a tendency to drool, nip and swipe your unguarded lunch?

If you thought the push to bring dogs to work was annoying before Covid-19, brace yourself. The explosion of pandemic pet ownership is set to hit the workplace in ways that once would have seemed unimaginable.

Let me say here that I am petless but deeply pro-pet. If I lived in a larger home in London, I would have joined the 3.2m Brits who have acquired a pet since lockdown —referably a dog or cat and ideally one of each.

Pre-pandemic, I thoroughly approved of the pet-friendly policies at places such as Ben & Jerry’ and Amazon, whose Seattle headquarters staff worked with as many as 7,000 dogs.

For the past 18 months, I have enviously watched homebound colleagues brushing cats away from keyboards on Zoom calls. Now, as offices start to reopen, it is perfectly understandable that hordes of new pet-owners are suffering a surge of separation anxiety about abandoning their furred companions.

Considering how tight the labour market is in many places, it is equally understandable that reams of employers have been planning a pet-tastic office reopening.

At least half of 500 executive-level managers in the US plan to start allowing pets in the workplace once offices reopen, a vet hospital group survey found this year. Most said they were doing this to meet employee requests and a large chunk were aiming to lure staff back to the office.

For people like me, this is excellent news.

But what if you’e allergic, or dislike the general concept of sharing an office with beings of limited conversation, foul breath and uneven bowel control?

No wonder one British pet care business has just launched “etiquette” a service designed to help employers go dog-friendly without annoying the dogless.

Frankly, I am not sure a business needs its advice on “stablishing a dinner and play time that does not interrupt others” But the general idea is sensible.

As I was reading the PR guff for Petiquette though, I noticed this: the Pets at Home petcare business that developed it also offers its own employees “ETernity leave” Staff are allowed a day off when they get a new pet to help the creature settle in.

Such work perks are not new. BrewDog, the Scottish-based craft beer group, has allowed its staff to take a week of fully paid leave when adopting a new puppy or rescue dog since the start of 2017.

This did not stop dozens of former BrewDog staff writing an open letter this year claiming the company had a “otten culture”nd “oxic attitudes”

But the idea of pawternity leave has taken off during the pandemic as pet ownership has boomed.

The other week, Roger Wade, chief executive of the British pop-up food and retail business Boxpark, posted a social media poll asking people what they thought of an employee who had asked him for paternity leave to look after a new puppy.

My finger hovered uncertainly on the keyboard when I went to cast a vote. The more men who take paternity leave, the better. This month’ to-do in the US over the leave that Transportation Secretary, Pete Buttigieg, took to care for his newly arrived twins was as unpleasant as it was predictable.

I also understand why anyone with a new dog wants time off, and why it may make sense at a company offering lots of benefits and, crucially, generous paid parental leave, for humans.

But far too many businesses do not, especially in the US, the only wealthy nation that does not guarantee paid leave for new parents. As of last year, just 21 per cent of US workers had access to paid family leave from employers.

Thinking of statistics like that, I voted with the 61 per cent who were against Wade giving his employee puppy paternity leave. In a fairer world, it might be reasonable but for now, it’ just a pawprint too far.

pilita.clark@ft.com

Twitter: @pilitaclark

headline: FISHY AND CHIPS
author: SARAH RIDLEY ;BEN O'DRISCOLL
text:
RECORD BREAKER?

Eyebrows raised at 18-inch Spoons fry

A SHOCKED pubgoer announced that he'd found the world's longest chip ? and was then accused of doctoring his dinner.

Sam Mumford, 29, posed for a snap with the extra-lanky carb measuring more than a foot-and-a-half long. The deep-fried freak towered over the pint of beer accompanying his chicken burger at his local Wetherspoons.

The hospitality worker could not wait to show it off on the pub chain's Facebook fans' page.

He said: "Forty-two chips served tonight with a chicken burger, along with the world's longest chip ? the size of a pint of Brewdog."

Some praised him for his "utterly massive length" but others accused him of cheating.

One follower commented: "That's clearly two chips held together, Sam. Do you think we were born yesterday?"

But Sam hit back with another snap of the 47cm fry, adding: "For the haters in the comments section, here's verification of my magnificent chip ? 18.5in of pure goodness."

However, it failed to satisfy the sceptics, with one saying: "Send over an adjudicator."

Wetherspoons said it could not verify the length of the chip served last Friday at The Black Boy in Newtown, Powys.

But spokesman Eddie Gershon said: "It is a whopper of a chip. It's great Sam took a photo for posterity. Hopefully he enjoyed eating it too."

In June, eight-year-old Ethan Emms, of Stamford, Lincs, found the world's longest chip ? at 22cm ? in a bag of frozen fries. Sam has been contacted for comment.

WETHERSPOONS is selling a pint of Ruddles Best for 99p throughout November.

sarah.ridley@the-sun.co.uk

headline: Rewilding lairds 'ignore local needs'
author: Jeremy Watson
text:
The takeover of Highland estates by companies aiming to profit from rewilding land is simply a "modernised version of landlordism", the SNP president Mike Russell has said.

The new wave of "green lairds" will take money out of the area, ignore the rights of resident communities and perpetuate huge inequalities, he said.

Russell, a former cabinet minister, was speaking out after the launch on Friday of Real Wild Estates (RWE), which aims to identify land for corporate investors to buy and restore while generating financial returns.

RWE, based in Somerset, says the UK is one of the world's most naturedepleted countries and it is aims to unlock private capital to accelerate rewilding. But some land reform groups have suggested that corporate investment in Scottish country estates is more aimed at exploiting government subsidies for environmental initiatives than helping local communities to reverse centuries of private ownership.

Companies which buy land can benefit from potentially lucrative subsidies for storing carbon by planting trees and restoring peatlands.

RWE is backed financially by the L'Oreal Group, the cosmetics giant that runs its own nature regeneration fund. RWE said there was a "growing recognition" that investments in naturebased solutions to climate change offer attractive, long-term returns. It aims to become a "trusted partner" of institutional investors and asset managers globally in investing in natural capital and biodiversity.

Its launch last week was attended by Stanley Johnson, the prime minister's father, who has written several books on environmental concerns.

Russell, the former Argyll MSP who left frontline politics following the Scottish election earlier this year, tweeted: "Simply a modernised version of landlordism which takes out of the Highlands the resources that should be applied there, ignores the rights of resident individuals and communities and perpetuates huge inequalities." He added: "The climate crisis demands real, not cosmetic, change."

Alastair McIntosh, a Highlands ecologist wrote: "We don't want this in Scotland unless it is from, with and for local communities. That's what modern Scottish land reform is all about."

One Twitter user said: "Thanks but no thanks. Absentee profit driven landowners is completely wrong for Scotland. Even more so that your profits essentially come from selling carbon credits. And L'Oreal backing it? No way."

Another added: "The green welly bri- gade. The problem pretending to be the solution. This is just the latest manifestation of the clearances."

RWE said it hoped to work on at least 100,000 acres of land over the next decade, providing a monetary return for investors over 30 years and boosting the number of local jobs.

Benedict Macdonald, the head of nature restoration at the company, said: "There has long been a gap in the UK, between the deep love of wildlife in this country and the deep capital we have, as one of the richest countries in the world, to do something about restoring the natural world. Fusing nature with business is therefore an imperative. We will work with landowners, and investors acquiring land, to deliver significantly better returns on ecological restoration than conventional land uses. In doing so, we hope to significantly increase rural jobs on larger holdings, especially in areas like the Highlands."

Recent company acquisitions of Scottish estates included Standard Life Investments Property Income Trust's £.5million purchase of 1,447 hectares in the Cairngorm National Park under its carbon strategy. Ellon-based Brewdog bought 3,600 hectares in the Cairngorms to reduce its carbon footprint.

headline: The Times hotel guide DogHouse Manchester
text:
Food ????? Location ????? Rooms ????? {{{{{

What's the story? The innovative Scottish beer company BrewDog has opened northern England's first craft beer hotel, which is also Manchester's first carbon-neutral hotel. BrewDog is Europe's largest craft brewer and the emphasis is on letting the good times roll, which means guitars, turntables and vinyl in the 18 rooms, and lots of beer in the bar, on the rooftop, in rooms and even in the showers.

What do we like? Beer is central, but it's all playful and creative, rather than blokey and boorish. The welcome drink, a choice from the bar's 30 craft offerings, certainly beats cucumber water.

There's a very "Manchester" industrial feel to the interiors. Dimly lit corridors are brightened with jars of free pick'n'mix, and spacious bedrooms, named after BrewDog headliner beers such as Hazy Jane and Punk IPA, are painted dark grey, with red-brick walls and exposed concrete ceilings. Red neon signs hang over the beds, and the record player comes with a stack of vinyl by Joy Division, Elbow and other Manchester artists.

Fridges are packed with beer and cider, with BrewDog's rum, vodka and gin on shelves. Pay £50 extra for the TopDog package and you get your own in-room beer tap and a shower beer fridge, as well as breakfast (you'll need it) and a beer-school tasting session. Guest happiness (hoppiness, they'd say) is helped along by friendly, on-the-ball staff who also know their beer, and novel offerings such as free hot dogs (vegan or meat) available 24/7.

The carbon-negative beer company has wrapped the hotel's exterior in a "living wall" of 26,000 plants, and motivates guests to reduce laundry with beer-token rewards for reusing towels. On the rooftop the taco hut has ten craft beers on tap and heaters and blankets and the main downstairs bar and restaurant is buzzing. The menu, which lists dishes' carbon emissions, focuses on burgers, bowls and wings, served at tall tables, sofas and booths. Children, vegans and veggies are catered for, breakfasts are substantial enough to soak up the previous night's indulgences, and the restaurant doubles as a daytime workspace.

What's near by? You're in central Manchester. The Arndale shopping centre is two minutes' walk away, as is the more interesting Northern Quarter, with street art, café, bars and shops, including vintage clothing and two Oldham Street institutions Piccadilly Records, which supplies DogHouse's vinyl, and Afflecks, an eclectic mix of boutiques selling punk T-shirts, vegan cosmetics, tattoos and Japanese anime. For other forms of worship, Manchester Cathedral, the National Football Museum and Manchester Art Gallery are all within a ten-minute walk. To go further afield, borrow one of the hotel's Brompton bicycles.

Graeme Green

Graeme Green was a guest of DogHouse Manchester. Room-only doubles from £45; mains from £2 (brewdog.com)

headline: SportO SUPER SCOTLAND KEEP THE GOOD TIMES GOING; SCOTLAND 2 DENMARK 0
author: CRAIG HOPE
text:
THE good news: Scotland are in the form of their lives and will be seeded for the World Cup play-offs. The bad news: those matches are four months away and Italy or Portugal could yet lie between them and a place in Qatar.

But let us enjoy this brave and brilliant performance for just a moment, a sixth straight victory thanks to John Souttar's first-half header and Che Adams' late strike ending the 100 per cent record of group winners Denmark.

Steve Clarke's side are getting better with each round of fixtures and saved their best for last.

Next up are March's play-offs, for which they are now guaranteed a home semi-final against an unseeded nation. Another performance like this in front of a sold-out Hampden Park and you would fancy them to advance to the final, where another one-off match awaits.

But the fate of the balls during next Friday's draw in Zurich is perhaps just as significant as those kicked during qualification, especially when a fair wind could, theoretically, leave North Macedonia and Finland between them and a first World Cup in 24 years.

There is also another far less attractive scenario. Serbia's Aleksandar Mitrovic was the toast of Scotland last year when his missed penalty sent the Tartan Army on their way to Euro 2020. Those same fans were chucking their BrewDog at the TV on Sunday as the striker's 90th-minute winner in Portugal sealed qualification at the expense of Cristiano Ronaldo and Co.

Then came news on full time here that European champions Italy had dropped into the play-offs.

For Scotland to be drawn in the same pathway as either of those big guns would, like air escaping a balloon, leave a nation deflated

But boy, in this mood they should not fear anyone. A chorus of 'Super John McGinn' was rattling around Hampden as the Aston Villa midfielder measured a corner on 35 minutes. Adrenalin pumping, McGinn's delivery looked way over-cooked. Scotland, in fact, were just coming to the boil and, from the by-line, Liam Cooper rose to return a header on to the brow of Souttar to power in from six yards.

And a deserved victory was sealed four minutes from time when Adams finished with a confidence befitting his team's wonderful display.

©aily Mail

headline: Rewilding project lets nature find its own way
author: Jeremy Watson
text:
Not everyone is happy about the changing Highland landscape, writes Jeremy Watson

The Highland glen looked serene in the early winter sun but transformation is everywhere. Progress may be glacially slow but the scattered groups of Scots pine saplings inching towards the sky are just one sign that rewilding is taking place.

For Rachael Iveson-Brown, an ecologist who leads mountain bike safaris in the Cairngorms National Park to help explain the benefits of natural regeneration, the positives are clearly visible.

In previous years, on this expanse of land on the vast Invercauld estate, the young trees would not have stood a chance, grazed flat by red deer. Fences to exclude the voracious herbivores, as well as recent culls of the animals to reduce their numbers, are now bringing rewards.

"Areas like this should be mainly wooded landscapes with Scots pines, rowan and birch, which would have been home to a variety of native species," Iveson-Brown, who runs the Wild Discovery wildlife travel company, said.

Large tracts, however, have been managed for generations as a "monoculture" that has favoured the deer and grouse shot for sport.

"Landowners have to make a living," Iveson-Brown said, "but we need to find a way forward that allows them to do that while allowing the natural habitat here to thrive."

For its supporters, particularly at a time of climate emergency, rewilding, a form of large-scale conservation that leaves landscapes to regenerate through natural processes, is the answer. The aim is to allow habitats such as natural woodlands, heaths, and native wildlife to flourish. Land management for grouse shooting and deer stalking is reduced, drainage removed from peat bogs and new trees allowed to grow without being eaten by grazing animals. Green tourism booms with an attendant boost for jobs and local communities.

It's not a new idea. Anders Holch Povlsen, a Danish retail billionaire, has been buying up hundreds of thousands of acres of the Highlands many previously run as sporting estates since the Victorian era for more than a decade to pursue his rewilding dream.

In September the Trees for Life charity announced it was to work with the owners of 500,000 acres stretching from Loch Ness to the northwest coast to regenerate a huge natural forest over the next 30 years.

Rewilding is gathering pace due to wider recognition that natural environments have their own value in helping to save the planet from global warming. The UK and Scottish governments are offering subsidies to owners who commit to mass tree plantings and restoring peat bogs, which both store carbon.

Corporate money is flooding into the

Highlands. The Standard Life Investments Property Income Trust and the drinks company BrewDog have recently bought small estates to reduce their overall carbon footprint.

Some Highland politicians who want rewilding to incorporate, not exclude, local communities in new forms of land ownership are unhappy that corporate acquisitions will only drive land prices out of reach of the people who live there. Rhoda Grant, a Labour MSP, said: "There is a real concern about what is being called the 'green lairds'. Rewilding should bring more community ownership of land so that everyone benefits, not just a few."

The multimillion-pound community buyout in March this year of 5,000 acres of the Duke of Buccleuch's Langholm estate in the Borders is seen as one way forward. The new Tarras Valley Nature Reserve was created to manage the land for tourism and nature conservation, with a spin-off of more local jobs.

Richard Bunting, of the Scottish Rewilding Alliance, said: "Community buyouts can be a positive way for local people to help restore life to the land as well as helping to fix climate breakdown." Attitudes in some areas are polarising, however, with some traditional sporting estate owners fearing that the concept of rewilding is political cover for ending sports shooting of grouse and deer. Scottish Land & Estates, whose members own about two thirds of Scotland's private land, says many owners are already pursuing rewilding initiatives but will continue to rely on country sports to provide a steady income.

headline: Rewilding projects lead to growth in 'green lairds'
author: Jeremy Watson
text:
The movement to rewild large areas of the countryside is giving rise to a growing number of "green lairds" as corporate cash flows into the projects, politicians have said.

Rewilding, a form of large-scale conservation that leaves landscapes to regenerate through natural processes, is increasingly recognised as a way in which landowners can help to tackle climate change.

However, there are concerns in rural areas such as the Highlands that the process can exclude the local community, with rewilding often the preserve of wealthy landowners or commercial enterprises wishing to offset their carbon emissions. MSPs fear that corporate acquisitions will drive land prices out of the reach of local people.

Anders Holch Povlsen, the Danish retail billionaire, has been buying up hundreds of thousands of acres of the Highlands many previously run as sporting estates since the Victorian era and rewilding them.

In September the Trees for Life charity announced it was to work with owners of 500,000 acres stretching from Loch Ness to the northwest coast to regenerate a huge forest.

The UK and Scottish governments are offering valuable subsidies to owners who commit to mass tree plantings and restoration of peat bogs.

In the Highlands the Standard Life Investments Property Income Trust and the drinks company Brewdog have recently bought small estates to reduce their overall carbon footprint.

Rhoda Grant, a Labour MSP, said: "There is a real concern about the 'green lairds'. Rewilding should bring more community ownership of land so that everyone benefits, not just a few."

The multimillion-pound community buy-out in March of 5,000 acres of the Duke of Buccleuch's Langholm Estate in the Borders is viewed as one way forward. Tarras Valley Nature Reserve was created to manage the land for tourism and nature conservation, with a spin-off of more local jobs.

The royal family were criticised this week as hypocritical by Nick Kempe, an environmental campaigner, for managing land for grouse shooting on their Balmoral estate while extolling the virtues of conservation.

Scottish Land & Estates, whose members own about two thirds of Scotland's private land, insists many owners already pursue rewilding initiatives but will continue to rely on country sports to provide a steady income. Sarah-Jane Laing, its chief executive, said: "Our members already run their estates in a balanced and integrated way."

headline: Bully row brewery faces administration [CORRECTED]
author: Dominic Walsh
text:
This article is the subject of a complaint.

The Berkshire brewery at the centre of allegations of bullying and sexual harassment is understood to be on the brink of falling into administration.

West Berkshire Brewery, which makes beers including Good Old Boy bitter and Renegade lager, is believed to have appointed Grant Thornton to find a buyer and has put the firm on standby to step in as administrators.

It has emerged that Tom Lucas, 41, was removed from his post as managing director in September, four weeks after a report in The Times highlighted an open letter from a group of current and former employees making a number of allegations of improper behaviour.

Although the letter did not identify the perpetrators, it claimed that a survey had elicited allegations of "multiple accounts of bullying, racism, homo- phobia, sexism, sexual harassment [and] a disregard for staff's physical and mental wellbeing".

The pressure group, calling itself WBB Renegades, claimed that the brewery, which also has three pubs, ignored a request for an independent review of company culture and senior management behaviour, promising only an internal review.

Lucas, who was appointed managing director three years ago, worked at the brewery for 13 years, initially as an accountant then finance director. Since he left, he has run his own business, GFT Consultancy. According to WBB Renegades and two other sources familiar with events at the brewery, Lucas was sacked after the internal inquiry found he had behaved improperly towards some members of staff.

The brewery in Yattendon, which was founded in 1995, has been chaired for the past eight years by David Bruce, who founded the Firkin brew-pubs chain and co-founded the City Pub Company and Capital Pub Company.

Bruce, 73, who did not return calls from The Times, raised more than £7 million to expand the business from more than 800 small shareholders, and has talked about the possibility of a flotation. Industry rumours suggest he may be considering making a bid to buy it back out of administration.

The West Berkshire is not the first craft brewer to face accusations of sexual harassment and bullying. In June, BrewDog's bosses were accused of propagating a culture of fear. It has since initiated a sweeping review of its culture and operating structure.

Lucas declined to answer questions about the circumstances surrounding his exit, while Grant Thornton also refused to comment.

headline: Lot of fizz in non-alcohol drink sales
author: Shaf Rasul
text:
One in a million shaf@the-sun.co.uk

AS many Scots across the nation gear up for the Christmas season, the festive spirit and the preparations it brings are in full effect.

High streets enjoyed the increased rush of Christmas shopping spending and hospitality venues were relishing the return of the annual office party in whatever capacity it is.

Businesses were adapting to the "new" Christmas but, with the cautious government guidance about limited numbers over the next few weeks, people are opting for a much softer approach to their Christmas celebrations as the non-alcohol or "adult soft drink" sector is booming.

With more alcohol-free substitutes and "nosecco" pouring across the break, many Scots are aiming not to have a hangover come Boxing Day.

This rise in buying tee-total tipples has long outgrown its usual Dry January boom because of the change in health habits during lockdown.

UK sales of non-alcohol and adult soft drinks rose by 18.5 per cent this year, bringing over £14million to the nation's economy.

This massive surge includes a 64.1 per cent rise in sales of low and non-alcohol wines, a 24 per cent rise in alcohol-free beers and a cracking 120 per cent rise in non-boozy spirits.

Scottish businesses and business owners are taking note, adding it to their lists and checking it twice.

Edinburgh-based brewer Days has recently bubbled to prominence and raised £million via investments from other independent businesses in the food and drink sector.

These taste tycoons include Propercorn founder Ryan Kohn, Mindful Chef founder Giles Humphries and former Camden Town managing director Adam Keary, who also joins the company.

Originally launched during lockdown in October 2020 by childhood pals Mike Gammell and Duncan Keith, the Days duo have since poured into hospitality venues with a clear focus on wellbeing of their customers and are on track to be available at 500 UK venues by the end of January.

It's boosted by the demand that comes with sober starters in Dry January.

In a sector Mr Gammell described as one which has even "more opportunities" than its boozier big brother, it's clear that there's plenty of happy days left for this growing brewing beast.

Another non-alcoholic beverage business which is set to make waves this Christmas is the fellow Edinburgh-based Jump Ship Brewing, a non-al- cohol beer brand who have recently put the city on the map with their Yardarm lager being celebrated as the best alcohol-free lager in the world at the prestigious World Beer Awards back in September.

This capital success story founded by entrepreneur Sonja Mitchell got themselves an extra Christmas present in the form of a £0,000 investment from a high growth potential business funding competition organised by Scottish EDGE, an accumulation of Scottish business giants from businesses such as Brewdog, Stagecoach and the Hunter Foundation.

These Scottish business leaders aren't the only ones looking to get involved with the funding of this lucrative corner of the drinks market however with Scotland's richest man Anders Povlsen also popping the cork for a smaller Fife non-alcoholic drinks brand this Christmas.

The private landowner and shareholder in fashion retailer ASOS, Mr Povlsen has recently announced that he will be financially backing Feragaia, a Fife-based brand known for their alcohol-free spirits with a remarkable £.5million investment into the group.

Founded back in 2019, Feragaia describe this year's conclusive gift as a 'landmark' moment for their business, with the investment helping them to further grow the brand and continue their fastgrowing success story to new heights.

Speaking of highs, the company also have a commitment to restoring parts of the Highlands to their former natural splendour via the investment, so this business is wholesome in so many more ways than just the New Year's health kick.

It's refreshing to see so many success stories pouring out of this Scottish sector and it's this entrepreneurial spirit that's clearly the fizz the industry needs and something that we need to keep an eye on.

Either way, it's looking like the Scottish non-alcohol sector is set to pop the cork to a very merry Christmas indeed.

headline: Brewer draws line under culture row
author: Dominic Walsh
text:
Senior managers at BrewDog have sought to draw a line under allegations of a "toxic culture" after completing an in-depth review of the way the business is run.

In June the Scottish brewer, which is mulling a possible flotation, was hit by allegations of sexist and misogynistic behaviour towards female staff and "toxic attitudes towards junior staff".

A group of rebels calling themselves Punks with Purpose was particularly critical of James Watt, the company's co-founder and chief executive, accusing him in an open letter of presiding over a culture that left many workers "burnt out, afraid and miserable".

After the completion of the review, Allan Leighton, 68, the newly appoint- ed chairman, and Blythe Jack, 47, the deputy chairwoman, issued a joint message to its 2,300 staff last night. It said: "Having read the review, we don't subscribe to the characterisation of the company set out in the open letter and we know from having spoken to numerous crew members, neither do many of you. But it is also clear that BrewDog has made mistakes and the key now is to learn from them, address them and be better."

The review included an online survey of almost 1,000 present and former employees and one-on-one interviews with more than 400. Other issues addressed were an under-resourced HR function and a lack of career progression and mental health provision. The firm also has hired 600 extra staff.

The duo said that while it had been a challenging period for BrewDog, there was "incredible momentum inside the business today" and in 2021 it had grown by about 25 per cent year-onyear, helped by 16 bar openings.

"Strong brands provoke strong emotions," they added. "We have not always got it right and, at times, this had had a negative impact on our culture. We draw a line under that today."

Watt, 39, announced the launch of measures including a workplace code, a 3 per cent salary rise and an independently run ethics hotline. He "fully accepted the findings of the review", adding: "I accept I haven't always looked after our people as well as I should have done."

He said that he would work with Leighton, his mentor, to "be the best leader I can be".

headline: 2022 SET TO BE ANOTHER BUMPER YEAR AS A HOST OF BIG NAME FIRMS WEIGH UP BLOCKBUSTER LISTINGS
author: FRANCESCA WASHTELL
text:
THE UK is in line for another booming year of floats as an array of big name firms consider listing in London.

The 'raw material is there' for a stellar year on the stock market, according to analysts.

Private equity giant CVC, digital bank Monzo and Virgin Atlantic are among the groups that could go public this year.

More than 120 companies joined the London Stock Exchange last year, raising the most money since before the financial crisis.

The LSE saw its first float of the year yesterday as movie make-up trailer company Facilities by ADF enjoyed a bumper debut.

Shares in the group rose 9pc to 54.5p - indicating traders are still keen to invest in newly floated companies. Facilities by ADF provides production sites with wardrobe rooms, accommodation and toilets in trailers.

Founded in 1992, it now has a fleet of more than 500 trucks and its clients include Apple, Sky, Disney, Netflix and the BBC.

It raised £8.4m in its listing which chairman John Richards said would help the firm 'meet increasing demand'.

The UK saw the number of companies coming to market slow in 2015, when the looming Brexit referendum created uncertainty for businesses.

When Britain left the EU in January 2020, analysts expected a rise in floats - but this was kiboshed by the pandemic.

Last year saw a frenzy of listings which in London included huge successes such as Darktrace, Bridgepoint and Dr Martens, as well as flops from Deliveroo, Pension Bee and Victorian Plumbing.

The UK was behind only the US and Greater China for the number of floats. And the LSE raised more money - £6.8bn - than exchanges in Amsterdam and Paris combined. Dan Nickols, of Jupiter Asset Management, said 'the raw material is definitely there' for this success to continue. 'Our team looks at many would-be float candidates and - qualitatively - we have seen many exciting businesses across a range of sectors,' he said.

Last month it was reported that private equity group CVC had hired Goldman Sachs to explore a stock market listing that could value it at £1bn - though it was not clear if it would choose London. British semiconductor designer Imagination Technologies has also brought in advisers to help it weigh up a UK or US listing, while the Barclay family was said to be mulling a £bn float of online fashion retailer Very Group, which sells clothing lines designed by actress Michelle Keegan (pictured left).

Rumours have also swirled that Virgin Atlantic could join the stock market after its finances were wiped out by the pandemic.

The chief executive of online bank Starling, Anne Boden, said she has set her sights on floating as soon as this year, while the bosses of rival Monzo have signalled a listing is on the cards.

The Issa brothers were reportedly mulling a £0bn float of their EG Group petrol forecourt business and analysts are keen to see if Brewdog revives its plans to go public after it delayed a £.1bn float in October. Susannah Streeter of Hargreaves Lansdown said: 'Although the pipeline for 2022 is reasonably strong, 2021 will be a hard act to follow.'

©aily Mail

headline: SINCE YOU ASK...
text:
When a privately-owned company sells its shares for the first time through a stock exchange, it is said to be floating, or listing.

Older readers will remember the floats of the privatised utility companies, back in the 1980s and 1990s.

These days, people increasingly use the American term 'to IPO'.

IPO stands for Initial Public Offering, but the noun has been turned into a verb.

The company hires lawyers and banks who assemble a prospectus with detailed information about the business and the number of shares to be sold to raise capital.

The banks will also underwrite the float. These professionals earn handsome fees. Of course they do.

The UK is set for a boom year for floats in 2022, with companies like fashion retailer Very, craft beer maker Brewdog and Starling Bank forming a queue.

The price of the shares is set close to the time when the company is set to make its debut on the exchange.

It is partly determined by the anticipated level of demand: if this is high, the float may be oversubscribed and applicants for shares will have their allocations cut back. If the float is under-subscribed - because of a nervous mood in the markets, say - allocations will be met in full. The bank that has underwritten the float will buy any surplus.

Some floats are warmly received, sending the shares soaring. Others are met with indifference or hostility.

Despite the lauded expertise of advisers, there is no fail-safe way to gauge the markets' sentiment in advance. Two UK companies were among those that got off to a great start in 2021. Darktrace, the Cambridge cyber security company, floated in April. Its shares, offered at 250p, rose to 330p. Moonpig, the greeting card company, also fared well, jumping from 350p to 440p on the first day.

Sometimes, but not always. The risks are high and there can be a lot of hype. A float may flop because of doubts about the company's management or prospects.

The March 2021 float of food delivery service Deliveroo was dubbed 'the worst IPO in London's history'. The shares, offered at 390p, slumped by 26pc on the first day, a debacle blamed on a list of factors. They now stand at 197.15p.

Er, no, not always. Small investors are excluded from many floats which are open only to financial institutions. As a result, small investors miss out on the profits that can be made in early trading. All very unfair. It is short-sighted too. In many businesses, offering shares to customers could create a loyal and stable shareholder base, along with a great deal of goodwill.

PrimaryBid - whose app makes it easier to apply for shares in a float - is one of the organisations pressing for reform. Richard Wilson, chief executive of Interactive Investor, argues for legislation requiring all companies to reserve a chunk of shares for small investors. Why should a float be called an IPO if the public are shut out?

©aily Mail

headline: BEER v BEEB
author: STUART PATTERSON
text:
Brewdog boss sue threat over TV docu

A RAGING BrewDog boss is threatening to sue the Beeb over a new documentary.

James Watt, 34, said yesterday the telly probe will be an "attack" on the Scots beer giant.

He claimed it will feature sacked ex-staff with an axe to grind and use info from illegally hacked emails.

BBC Scotland's The Truth About BrewDog is due to be broadcast on Monday. It comes after former workers alleged last year there was a "culture of fear" at the firm, based in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, and a "cult of personality" around its co-founders Mr Watt and Martin Dickie.

The claims have now been investigated by reporter Mark Daly and his Disclosure team. Mr Watt hit out: "BBC sent us a list of 30 points they intend to attack us on. We are responding legally.

"I suffered a number of hacking attempts last year into my personal email accounts. BBC appears to have acquired personal information that is entirely confidential.

"It's clearly a concern the BBC has been passed information that may well have come from that illegal hacking or was otherwise illegally obtained." Mr Watt went on: "We also know false information has been given to the BBC which, if broadcast, would be highly defamatory."

He insisted the company would "not hesitate" to protect itself.

The chief exec claimed some of those interviewed were axed for offences such as being drunk or theft.

A BBC spokesman said: "Our programme will be fair, balanced and accurate. Mr Watt has been asked repeatedly to participate but has refused so far. The offer still stands."

stuart.patterson @the-sun.co.uk

headline: Beer boss is accused of intimidating staff
author: Greig Cameron
text:
The boss of BrewDog has been accused of intimidating former employees before a documentary that investigates claims about a "toxic" work culture is broadcast next week.

James Watt, co-founder of the craft beer-maker in Aberdeenshire, alleged that BBC programme-makers have received "highly defamatory" information after his email account was hacked.

Watt, 39, took to an internal forum for investors to suggest that sources for the show could regret co-operating. "All of this is very, very likely to end up in court," he said.

Watt suggested that the BBC was likely to have told sources that their identity would remain confidential but that "can never be guaranteed".

In a separate post shared widely on social media, he says the company is "very concerned about the BBC's sources" because many people it had spoken to left for disciplinary reasons and had "an obvious axe to grind".

Some were dismissed for theft, poor performance, being intoxicated, abusing others, health and safety breaches and endangering colleagues, Watt said.

He added: "For the BBC to use a source who was dismissed, for instance, for theft and not cite that fact is neither fair nor responsible journalism."

Watt has been fighting to restore the company's reputation after dozens of staff, under the collective name of Punks with Purpose, signed an open letter in June alleging "toxic attitudes towards junior staff" and a culture of fear that had left many "burnt out, afraid and miserable".

Bryan Simpson, of Unite Hospitality, said: "Perhaps if Mr Watt had spent more time and resources over the last six months trying to resolve the issues instead of trying to discredit whistleblowers then there wouldn't have been a need for workers to speak out as part of the BBC documentary."

In a statement released yesterday Watt said hackers had made attempts to access his personal email last year. He said: "The BBC appears to have acquired personal information that is entirely confidential and it's clearly a concern that [it] has been passed information that may well have come from that illegal hacking, or information that was otherwise illegally obtained.

"We also know false information has been given to the BBC, which, if broadcast, would be highly defamatory."

Watt has previously described appearing on the BBC show Who's the Boss? as one of his biggest mistakes and said it was "a complete hatchet job".

Watt and his friend Martin Dickie, also 39, started BrewDog in 2007. Disclosure: The Truth About BrewDog is being shown on BBC1 on Monday.

headline: BEER BOSS 'MADE FEMALE STAFF FEEL UNCOMFORTABLE'
author: ELLIOT MULLIGAN
text:
THE boss of craft beer maker BrewDog has been accused of inappropriate behaviour towards women and abuse of power in the workplace.

More than 15 ex-staff have spoken out against chief executive James Watt with some claiming he made female bartenders feel 'uncomfortable' and 'powerless'.

They were even advised by colleagues on how to avoid unwelcome attention from Mr Watt, 38, according to an investigation by BBC Scotland's Disclosure programme.

The Scottish company has more than 100 bars and 2,000 staff worldwide. It is understood to be worth around £billion.

Katelynn Ising, who worked at a BrewDog bar in Ohio, US, said female staff would dress down when they knew Mr Watt would be in their bars. She said: 'We would make a point to warn new girls... like, "Hey, just so you know, James Watt's coming to town. Just kind of, like, leave after your shift, don't really hang around [and] don't always do your hair and make-up that day, like don't catch his attention".'

Other former staff claim Mr Watt was witnessed by staff kissing an intoxicated customer on a roof terrace at another US bar.

Mr Watt has said the allegations are false and denies behaving inappropriately.

A lawyer for Mr Watt said that 'the only party that Mr Watt could have been present with' on the roof terrace 'has confirmed ... absolutely no sexual encounter took place. She has also confirmed that far from being intoxicated, she drove herself home.'

Mr Watt's lawyer added: 'At no time has Mr Watt given unwelcome attention to any female bartender.'

BrewDog chairman Allan Leighton said Mr Watt had told him the BBC claims were inaccurate and based on misinformation. He said: 'James has committed to making improvements to his management style.'

Last year nearly 300 former and current BrewDog staff signed a letter accusing Mr Watt of presiding over a toxic culture of fear. Mr Leighton said that since then BrewDog had 'implemented a wide-ranging action plan to address the issues raised'.

©aily Mail

headline: BrewDog  boss 'inappropriate' to women
author: Ali Mitib
text:
The chief executive of BrewDog has been accused of abusing his power in the workplace by behaving inappropriately with female employees.

Several members of staff working in the craft brewer's US outlets said that they had seen James Watt give private late-night tours to female customers and his conduct often made female bartenders feel "uncomfortable".

More than 15 former BrewDog employees have spoken to BBC's Disclosure programme alleging inappropriate behaviour by Watt.

The accusations come less than a year after dozens of former staff signed an open letter alleging a "culture of fear" at the beer firm with a "toxic attitude" to junior employees.

BrewDog is based at Ellon, Aberdeenshire, but has expanded across the UK, Europe and into the US. Katelynn

Ising, a former employee at DogTap, BrewDog's flagship bar and brewery in Canal Winchester, Ohio, told Disclosure that female staff would dress down to avoid unwelcome attention from Watt, 39.

She said: "We would make a point to warn new girls like, 'Hey, just so you know, James Watt's coming to town.

"'Just, kind of, leave after your shift, don't really hang around [and] don't always do your hair and makeup that day, like don't catch his attention'."

Ising also said she saw Watt take intoxicated female customers in their twenties on a private tour of the brewery. Watt's lawyer denied this allegation, saying that the Scot regularly took male and female friends as well as customers on evening tours of the brewery, and that they were not intoxicated. The lawyer added that a claim of such behaviour made in 2021 had been "fully investigated" and "not substantiated", warranting no further action from BrewDog HR.

Two staff claim to have seen Watt have a sexual encounter on a roof terrace. Nathan Quick, the duty manager at Brewdog's Franklinton bar, said: "He was on the rooftop kissing with his hand up the shirt of this young lady."

Watt's lawyer denied that the sexual encounter occurred.

The documentary, The Truth about BrewDog, also reported that male members of staff were aware of Watt's behaviour and took steps to protect female colleagues when he was in the bar.

Dylan Gray, the manager of a Brew- Dog bar in Columbus, Ohio, said: "I would schedule certain female staff around him so they would not be there. I would schedule more men at nights when he was there. I would sit behind the bar with female staff so they wouldn't feel uncomfortable."

BrewDog was founded in 2007 by

Watt and his friend Martin Dickie. The company owns more than 100 bars and employs 2,000 people worldwide.

Last year, BrewDog delayed a £ billion flotation citing pandemic disruption to the hospitality industry.

Watt declined to be interviewed for the documentary.

BrewDog's chairman Allan Leighton said he had been given assurances by Watt that the BBC's claims were inaccurate and based on misinformation. "James has committed to making improvements to his management style," he said. "Since publication of the open letter in 2021, the board has overseen a major independent review into our culture and we have implemented a wide-ranging action plan to address the issues raised."

Last week Watt was accused of intimidating former staff who were due to appear in the documentary. Posting on an internal forum for investors, the chief executive suggested that sources for the show could regret co-operating. He said: "All of this is very, very likely to end up in court."

Watt has also attempted to cast doubt on the employees who spoke to BBC Scotland's Disclosure team, writing a separate post widely shared on social media that the company was "very concerned" because many people it had spoken to had left for disciplinary reasons and had "an obvious axe to grind".

headline: Brewdog  boss accused of abusing his workplace power over women
author: Nadeem Badshah
text:
The chief executive of BrewDog, the Scottish craft beer company, has been accused by former staff of abusing his power in the workplace and making female bartenders feel "uncomfortable" and "powerless".

A BBC documentary screened last night alleged that James Watt was seen by BrewDog USA staff kissing an intoxicated customer on a roof terrace bar. It was also claimed female bartenders were advised how to avoid unwelcome attention from Watt and managers would attempt to schedule some female staff to be off work during his visits.

The programme, The Truth about BrewDog, also alleged that one bartender said she felt "powerless" to prevent unwelcome attention from Watt and he flirted with a staff member before taking her to the roof of a Brew- Dog building in view of other workers.

Lawyers for Watt, 39, said the allegations were false and he denied behaving inappropriately. He has been fighting to restore the company's reputation after dozens of staff, under the collective name of Punks with Purpose, signed an open letter in June alleging "toxic attitudes towards junior staff" and a culture of fear that had left many "burnt out, afraid and miserable".

Katelynn Ising, who worked in Dog- Tap, BrewDog's main bar and brewery in Ohio, said female staff would dress down when they knew Watt would be visiting the bars.

She said: "We would make a point to warn new girls like, 'Hey, just so you know, James Watt's coming to town. Just, kind of, leave after your shift, don't really hang around [and] don't always do your hair and makeup that day, like don't catch his attention'."

Jackie English, a former duty manager for the company, said Watt's behaviour made her feel "uncomfortable".

She said: "There were three separate nights ... [he'd] come into the building with a girl, a young female, you know, cute little blondes, and he would take them into the brewery ... to give them a tour or whatever."

English added: "You're showing your employees that you can do whatever you want because of who you are and who your name is. That's how it felt a lot of times."

Watt's lawyer told the BBC this allegation was false. "Mr Watt regularly takes both women and men, friends and customers on evening tours of the brewery," the lawyer said. "It is not true to say that those who accompany him are intoxicated. The claim that he did, was made by an employee in June 2021. It was fully investigated the claims were not substantiated no further action was warranted by BrewDog USA HR."

Nathan Quick, who was employed as a duty manager in BrewDog's Franklinton, Ohio, bar until last year, is among two former staff who say they witnessed Watt having a sexual encounter in the roof terrace bar. Watt's lawyer said: "At no time did Mr Watt have a sexual encounter on the roof terrace of the Franklinton bar. We hold a statement from the only party that Mr Watt could have been present with at the bar.

She has confirmed in her statement that Mr Watt and her did visit the roof briefly, but absolutely no sexual encounter took place."

Watt and his friend Martin Dickie, also 39, founded BrewDog in Aberdeenshire in 2007.

The BBC Disclosure programme made no allegations of criminal behaviour with women.

headline: Pubs gear up for bumper weekend as UK lifts Covid restrictions
author: Alice Hancock in London
text:
Bar chains expect rush of sales as people spend long-awaited January pay packets

Pub groups are gearing up for a weekend of bumper sales as the UK’ remaining Covid-19 restrictions are lifted and consumers receive their long-awaited January pay packets.

Marston’, Mitchells & Butlers and BrewDog are among the pub operators who say they are expecting one of the best weekends’ales since the arrival in late November of the Omicron coronavirus variant, which prompted all the UK nations to impose restrictions of varying levels.

“estrictions should fully lift this week and it’ pay week    ’ cautiously optimistic that it will be bigger than the normal uplift on January pay weekend,”aid Andrew Andrea, Marston’ chief executive.

“ormally at the end of January there is a bit of ‘ahey, thank God we’e paid’moment], so we’e hoping for that again,”aid Phil Urban, chief executive of Mitchells & Butlers. “ think we would all be disappointed if we don’ get an uplift next weekend.”

The last weekend of January has traditionally offered a boost to pub sales after a fallow month when people tighten their belts after more extravagant spending at Christmas.

Urban said M&B would normally expect an uplift in sales of at least 3 per cent at the end of January compared to a normal Friday and Saturday. Industry tracker CGA said that pub sales in the final week of January in 2019 were 8 per cent higher than during the rest of the month.

The first payday of the year will coincide with the lifting of Covid-19 measures across the UK. Prime Minister Boris Johnson announced last week that England’ Plan B measures, which include guidance to work from home and mask-wearing on public transport, would be removed on Wednesday.

In Wales, nightclubs will be able to reopen and limits on group sizes and social distancing will be lifted for hospitality venues on Friday.

James Watt, managing director of brewer and bar chain BrewDog, said the company was expecting a particularly “ig weekend”n Scotland, where remaining restrictions were dropped on Monday. Bookings were around “0 per cent more than the week prior” he said.

Clive Watson, executive chair of City Pub Group, said he expected to build on “umper”rading last weekend when sales at the 46-strong pub company were 30 per cent up from the previous week.

Oakman Inns reported its sales were up 12 per cent last weekend compared to 2019 thanks to growing consumer confidence, with a notable uptick in drinks purchased at the bar.

Watson added that he expected events such as the Queen’ Platinum Jubilee and the Six Nations rugby tournament next month to provide further boosts for business. “ookings are really flooding in for [the Six Nations],”e said. “ think people are going to go tonto.”

Pub operators have warned, however, that returning customers could face higher prices as rising inflation and energy costs bite. Some establishments have already raised their prices and expect to do so again in April when the VAT rate increases back to pre-Covid levels.

“e have got huge issues at the moment, difficulties in the supply chain, record levels of debt and increased across the board costs,”aid Emma McClarkin, chief executive of the British Beer and Pub Association.

“t’ difficult when you want to win back consumers, they don’ want to see you putting prices up.”

headline: BREW turn
author: STUART PATTERSON
text:
'PUNK' ROCKED

Fury at founder's secret £00K Heineken shares

BREWDOG co-founder James Watt admitted he ploughed £00,000 into Heineken after slating the Dutch giant.

The under-fire Punk IPA boss, 33, has fostered an anti-corporate ethos around his brand and blasted "bland industrial beers".

But behind the scenes he secretly tried to persuade Heineken to set up a distribution deal with his brewery based in Ellon, Aberdeenshire.

He bought at least £00,000 of shares in what he says was a goodwill gesture.

Watt admitted: "Would I do it again? Probably not."

The 2017 shares transaction was exposed in BBC documentary The Truth About BrewDog.

In an online post for angry shareholders, Watt said: "Whilst we do not want to be owned by big beer, we are open to working with big beer on distribution. We were having discussions with Heineken about a distribution deal for certain territories.

"It was my show of good faith we could work together.

"The deal did not work out and I sold the shares, which I only held briefly."

Watt and partner Martin Dickie filmed themselves blowing up Heineken as a PR stunt for the firm, now worth £billion.

In a 2015 blog, Watt slammed big brewers and vowed to "live and die by what we believe in."

Shareholders last night called for him to quit, with one saying: "Everything he claimed to stand for was a lie. His position is untenable."

The Disclosure piece also accused him of inappropriate behaviour and abuse of power.

Watt threatened legal action, claiming the BBC used false information and material from hacked emails.

stuart.patterson@the-sun.co.uk

headline: Brewing boss is sorry
text: The chief executive of BrewDog, James Watt, has apologised to anyone who felt uncomfortable because of his behaviour. Watt's comments came after a BBC documentary which accused him of an abuse of power in the workplace. Watt criticised "false rumours and misinformation" and added that "false claims" in the programme were upsetting and damaging. However, Watt also said that he "truly apologised" to anyone he made feel uncomfortable.

headline: Pubs prepare for bumper weekend sales

text:

headline: GONE TO THE DOGS
author: Zara Janjua
text:
Zara Janjua

IT really grinds my chops when people say thing like "fake it till you make it".

We are living in an era of authenticity we can keep it real, am I right?

"The magic of TV" was a phrase used with a wink and a nod when secrets were unveiled behind the scenes fights staged for drama, stunts and scams orchestrated to grab headlines and boost audience figures.

But those days are coming to an end, and that may also be true for BrewDog whose marketing stunts were put under the microscope by Mark Daly, pictured, this week in BBC's Disclosure The Truth About BrewDog From "faked" employee of the month campaigns to Elvis juice name change stunts, the magician CEOs were unmasked as masters of illusions.

BrewDog's image is punky, youthful, dynamic and groundbreaking but allegations of inappropriate behaviour by CEO James Watt reveals the same old story with many powerful men in business - perpetual abuse of power.

He denies all the allegations but said in a statement: "I am trying to be a better leader, and to be far more mindful of the impact I have on our team when

I am on site." It seems that Brew- Dog's "proceed until apprehended" attitude has got them this far but they can't stay cloudy like their IPA. It's time for some transparency.

headline: Carbon dioxide agreement staves off food crisis in Britain
author: Emiko Terazono, Judith Evans, Alice Hancock and Harry Dempsey
text:
Meat industry expresses relief as fertiliser group and industrial gas companies reach deal

Britain’ biggest carbon dioxide producer and its three industrial gas customers have struck a deal to secure upply that ends weeks of uncertainty and averts a crisis n the country’ meat, beer and soft drinks sectors.

The agreement enables US fertiliser group CF Industries to continue to supply industrial gas companies BOC, Nippon Gases and Air Liquide even while global gas prices remain high, said the Department for Business, Energy and Industrial Strategy on Tuesday. “t means key sectors, including food processing and nuclear power, are ensured supplies of CO2,”t added.

CF Industries said its plant in Billingham, north-east England, “emains operational and continues to sell CO2 on a contractual and spot basis” Air Liquide, one of the UK’ three largest distributors of the gas, said: “e’e committed to do our best to supply the UK market with CO2.”

The new deal comes after contracts between CF and the three industrial gas companies signed last October expired at the end of January.

CF shut its two UK plants last September, as surging natural gas prices made the production of fertiliser uneconomical. But it also cut off a large portion of the UK’ supply of CO2, a byproduct of the fertiliser manufacturing process, prompting warnings of chaos, especially in the meat industry which uses the gas for slaughtering livestock. The gas is also used in food and drink production and packaging.

Production at CF’ Billingham plant resumed after the government stepped in to offer a three-week subsidy and brokered new contracts between CF and the industrial gas groups.

Sections of the food industry expressed relief that a deal had been reached. “t bears out [the government’] decision to step in in October, which meant this deal had time to be negotiated. This gives our industry more time to find other sources of CO2,”aid Nick Allen, chief executive of the British Meat Processors Association.

CO2 prices had roughly quadrupled since shortages began last year and would contribute to overall cost inflation, though they were a relatively small component of the total for meat producers, said the BMPA.

Richard Griffiths, chief executive of the British Poultry Council, said: “ don’ know whether there’ a duration attached to this agreement, but anything that guarantees the supply is good news. No increase in cost is welcome, but given the context this is the best outcome we could have hoped for.”

The crisis sparked concern among some meat producers that the industry was too reliant on CF for CO2 supplies. Since last September, however, more companies were looking for alternative sources, cutting CF’ share of the market to 30 per cent from about 60 per cent, said Allen.

He added that higher CO2 prices meant more businesses, including other fertiliser makers, were now selling the gas rather than releasing it into the atmosphere.

Emma McClarkin, chief executive of the British Beer and Pub Association, said that brewers “rgently”eeded to see the details of the agreement “o understand the impact on our sector and the longer-term sustainability of CO2 supply”

Some brewers have switched to alternative suppliers. St Austell Brewery in Cornwall is buying from BioCarbonics, which generates CO2 from corn being broken down through anaerobic digestion and accounts for about 4 per cent of the UK market. BrewDog has invested in building a CO2 recovery plant at its brewery in Ellon, Scotland.

Grant Pearson, commercial director at Ensus, which makes bioethanol from corn and supplies about 30 per cent of the UK’ CO2, said there had also been a switch to alternatives. “ome downstream users have switched to nitrogen instead of carbon dioxide, depending on availability and price. There will be some lowering of demand.”

headline: M&S and Aldi make peace in Colin and Cuthbert caterpillar cake war
author: Ian Johnston
text:
German discounter says product will return after retailers agree confidential settlement

Marks and Spencer and Aldi have called a truce to their caterpillar cake war, after the high street stalwart settled its lawsuit with the German discount supermarket.

M&S had last year launched legal action against Aldi over the design and packaging of its “uthbert the Caterpillar”ake, saying Aldi’ version copied its own “olin the Caterpillar”roduct and infringed trademarks.

The two parties have reached a confidential agreement. Although the terms have not been made public, M&S said on Tuesday: “he objective of the claim was to protect the IP in our Colin the Caterpillar cake and we are very pleased with the outcome.”

M&S had called on Aldi to stop selling Cuthbert, but the budget supermarket group implied that the settlement was not the end for its insect-themed sponge roll cake. Aldi said: “uthbert is free and looking forward to seeing all his fans again very soon!”

Cuthbert was withdrawn after the legal claim was first filed at the High Court in April 2021, but Aldi later released a limited edition version of the cake in May, with proceeds from sales going to cancer charities.

The claim was settled on November 5 and a consent order was signed off on January 27, resolving the case.

“olin the Caterpillar”as been on sale at M&S since 1990 and more than 15m Colin cakes have been sold, M&S has expanded Colin and female version Connie into a wider range including cupcakes, trifles, sweets and chocolates.

Other supermarkets, including Tesco, Asda and J Sainsbury have also released named caterpillar-shaped cakes in green packaging but Aldi is the only one to have been subject to legal action.

In December, M&S also launched a legal claim against Aldi over the sale of an illuminated gin bottle with gold flakes through it, alleging that it copied its “ight Up”hristmas Gin.

The German company has said it makes products that are “asily identifiable for customers without seeking to copy”ut several brands have taken issue with its own-label products. Sausage-maker Heck, for instance, has branded it a “arasite”or imitating the packaging of its Chicken Italia range.

In 2020, Aldi released its take on BrewDog’ Punk IPA product —n “nti-Establishment IPA” The two companies subsequently reached agreement and the brewer now produces “LD IPA”hat is sold in the supermarket.

Make-up producer Charlotte Tilbury successfully took copyright action against Aldi in 2019 for selling cosmetics similar to its line of products.

The outcome of the Colin case was first reported by legal publication Law360.

headline: M&S and Aldi settle caterpillar cake dispute
author: IAN JOHNSTON
text:
Retail

Marks and Spencer and Aldi have called a truce to their caterpillar cake war, after the high street stalwart settled its lawsuit with the German discount supermarket.

M&S had last year launched legal action against Aldi over the design and packaging of its "Cuthbert the Caterpillar" cake, saying Aldi's version copied its own "Colin the Caterpillar" product and infringed trademarks.

The two parties have reached a confidential agreement, news of which was first reported by Law360. Although the terms have not been made public, M&S said yesterday: "The objective of the claim was to protect the IP in our Colin the Caterpillar cake and we are very pleased with the outcome."

M&S had called on Aldi to stop selling Cuthbert, but the budget supermarket group implied the settlement was not the end for its insect-themed sponge roll cake. Aldi said: "Cuthbert is free and looking forward to seeing all his fans again very soon!"

Cuthbert the Caterpillar was withdrawn after the legal claim was first filed at the High Court in April 2021, but Aldi later released a limited edition version of the cake in May, with proceeds from sales going to cancer charities.

The claim was settled on November 5 and a consent order was signed off on January 27, resolving the case.

Colin the Caterpillar has been on sale at M&S since 1990, with more than 15mn units sold. M&S has since expanded Colin and female counterpart Connie into a range including cupcakes, trifles, sweets and chocolates. Other supermarkets, including Tesco, Asda and J Sainsbury have also released named caterpillar-shaped cakes in green packaging, but Aldi is alone in having been subject to legal action.

In December, M&S launched a legal claim against Aldi over the sale of an illuminated gin bottle with gold flakes through it, alleging it copied its "Light Up" Christmas Gin.

The German supermarket has said it makes products that are "easily identifiable for customers without seeking to copy", but several brands have taken issue with its own-label products.

Sausage manufacturer Heck branded it a "parasite" for imitating the packaging of its Chicken Italia range.

In 2020, Aldi released its take on BrewDog's Punk IPA product, an "Anti- Establishment IPA". The two companies subsequently reached agreement and the brewer now produces "ALD IPA", which is sold in Aldi stores.

headline: BrewDog  fired me day after I said my father was dying
author: Ali Mitib
text:
After spending her first day sampling an array of craft beers at BrewDog's flagship bar in Aberdeen, Janine Molineux felt like she had finally found the "most amazing job in the world".

She saw the self-styled "punk" brewery as a far cry from any company she had ever worked for and could not wait to join its "rock'n'roll" finance team and work under James Watt and Martin Dickie, the co-founders. But as Molineux settled into BrewDog's head office in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, on her first day in November 2017, she claims she was issued with a stark warning: "If you see James Watt walk down those stairs, don't make eye contact with him."

BrewDog has been mired in controversy after dozens of staff signed an open letter in June last year alleging a "culture of fear" and "toxic attitudes towards junior staff" at the company.

Last month former employees told the BBC's Disclosure programme that Watt abused his power in the workplace and behaved inappropriately with female employees and customers. The BBC made no allegations of criminal behaviour by Watt, 39, against women. He has denied wrongdoing or inappropriate behaviour.

Molineux, 40, said she was sacked by BrewDog's boss in January 2018, a day after telling him her father had terminal cancer.

Following the publication of the last year's open letter, she shared her experience with Dickie, who "profusely" apologised for the way she was "treated and exited by Brewdog", adding that it was "not close to the standards we talk about and set ourselves". The accountant said she told Watt about her father's diagnosis in a replyall to an email he had sent which she felt was directed at her performance in a monthly sales meeting, in which she was berated after BrewDog failed to meet forecasts.

She claims that the meeting was the "lowest point" in her professional life. She left it in tears after Watt said she had put the forecasts together and asked her a series of "random" questions. Her manager later told her: "That's just James," she said.

Watt followed up the meeting with a group email a few days later, which suggested the company was more concerned with an employee's performance on a "bad day" rather than a "good day". She replied, informing him of her father's diagnosis, and demonstrating her love for the company by attaching a photo of her celebrating the beer's launch in Australia. She claims that Watt replied: "I hope your dad is OK."

Her contract was terminated the next day. She said the company "reluctantly" paid her threemonth notice period in March 2018 after she threatened legal action, leaving her without income for two months. Her father died in June 2018.

She said: "James Watt is vile. He had zero empathy. If I knew someone [was] having a bit of a bad time and their dad was dying of cancer, I wouldn't fire them."

A spokesman for BrewDog said: "Ms Molineux was on a fixed-term contract which was brought to an end early, a decision taken by her line manager due to performance issues, totally independent of our CEO, as emails and other records show. She was paid her full contractual entitlement on leaving."

headline: BrewDog  boss fury at 'starer' TV claim
author: STUART PATTERSON
text:
BREWDOG boss James Watt hit back at a claim he stared at his staff by saying he was probably just working out how to improve customer service.

The brand's co-founder denied "starer" allegations made by an anonymous bartender in an explosive BBC investigation.

He said the Disclosure documentary which aired claims that he abused his power at the beer giant based in Ellon, Aberdeenshire was "based on untruths" and a "malicious hatchet job".

Watt, 33, told shareholders: "All our bar teams are very familiar with me sitting at the bar, lost in thought, observing service, thinking about how to improve customer experience. I utterly refute the characterisation of me."

A BBC spokesperson said: "We do not accept his characterisation of the programme and we stand by the journalism."

headline: Union urges cut in funds for  BrewDog
author: Ali Mitib
text:
Ministers must cut funding to Brew- Dog after claims of a "culture of fear" at the brewer and pub chain, the trade union Unite has said.

It called on the Scottish government to end investment in the company until it commits itself to "transformative improvements".

BrewDog has faced controversy since June when dozens of staff signed a letter alleging a culture of fear and "toxic attitudes towards junior staff". Yesterday The Times re- vealed that employees stayed quiet over working conditions, bullying or claims of sexual misconduct out of fear for their jobs.

Last year the company was awarded up to £50,000 to help it decarbonise. In 2015 it received a £.5 million grant to create 130 jobs and was hailed by John Swinney, the deputy first minister, as a "remarkable Scottish success story".

Bryan Simpson, of Unite, said: "This is hardly the kind of employer the Scottish government should be supporting with public funds."

The Scottish government said: "We are firmly committed to promoting fair working practices."

BrewDog said that as part of an independent review last year, it had made "considerable investments" in its employees.

headline: Former  BrewDog  staff start online register for anonymous complaints
author: Ali Mitib
text:
Former BrewDog employees have launched an anonymous complaints platform amid claims of a "culture of fear" at the brewer and bar chain.

Punks with Purpose, the group that last year published an open letter alleging toxic work attitudes towards junior staff at BrewDog, has created an independent registration site to allow former and current staff to voice any issues. BrewDog has been embroiled in controversy since June when the open letter was signed by dozens of staff.

On Monday, The Times revealed that employees did not report issues about working conditions, bullying, or claims of sexual misconduct out of fear of losing their jobs.

Following the publication of the letter, BrewDog identified and implemented measures to address the issues raised, including an independent review of the company's culture.

Punks with Purpose said: "Undoubtedly some progress has been made, but by centring themselves in the process and engaging with former staff only on their own terms, BrewDog have skirted past several areas of concern that we have repeatedly raised with them."

The group said that an independent review that had been commissioned "lacks crucial contextual detail, glosses over some really serious issues, and was ultimately overseen by BrewDog's board, which does not instil confidence in its impartiality".

A spokesman for BrewDog said that the company was committed to being a "great employer" and added that there was "work to be done but we are listening and taking action".

It said it had invested in mental health provision and career progression, as well as creating staff representative groups and launching a workplace code.

"If any of our colleagues feel they are unable to raise concerns directly with their line manager they can call an independently managed ethics hotline," a company official said.
