headline: Estate row is a blot on the landscape
author: Shaf Rasul
text:
SCOTLAND has the oldest system of land registration in the world.

The set-up is at worst unequal and elite and, at best, a way into a unique wilderness of untapped potential.

Striking the right balance between old ways and modern methods is proving difficult.

The iconic image of tweed and tartan-clad lairds are straight off a shortbread tin.

The Scottish Government has been keen to try and modernise the rules to change the ways Scotland's land is owned, managed, and governed ? but who is making use of the reforms, and is it going to make any positive change?

The value of Scotland's natural assets is splitting opinion.

Established industries like tourism and game shooting are increasingly rubbing up against new businesses who promote ecological credentials via forestry restoration and rewilding ? many of whom are newcomers to a sector which has existed for centuries.

This friction is causing tension. And the question of who actually owns our land in the first place is adding to that.

The Scottish Government believes that 57 per cent of rural land in Scotland is in private hands. It may not be any great surprise that Scottish Greens are front and centre of calls to overhaul Scottish land ownership.

Land reform campaigner Andy Wightman MSP says half of the country's rural land is owned by 432 people.

The most common perception is that most of this elite group have inherited their estates and have used it for traditional blood sports like deerstalking and pheasant shooting.

However, that's a view which may be stuck in the past.

More than a million acres of public forestry is managed by Forestry and Land Scotland.

It is said to generate around £95million for the Scottish economy every year through timber and tourism enterprises.

There are also a great many foreign business people who simply love the lifestyle the Highlands can offer.

Danish retail magnate Anders Povlsen owns 12 estates and more than 220,000 acres of land across Scotland ? more than the Queen and the Church of Scotland combined.

Valued at over £20million, Povlsen has a 200-year rewilding plan to allow native woodland and species to regenerate and flourish.

While many would claim that rewilding is important, it is not without controversy.

Scottish-based beer manufacturer BrewDog has recently bought land in the Cairngorms, where it says it will plant over a million trees. The PR spin is undoubtedly borne out of the corporate responsibility obligations that big businesses use to market themselves as ethical.

BrewDog says its planting plans will cancel out the company's carbon emissions and then some ? by removing twice as much carbon from our atmosphere as the company emits.

They also applied for £.2million in public money to help convert the 9,300 acres at Kinrara Estate into woodland.

The company's woodland ambitions come at another cost. They have lodged an application with NatureScot for a licence to control the wildlife population from digging or eating the saplings that will be planted. Wildlife has blatant disregard for borders and boundaries, so the hare and deer, which threaten BrewDog's Lost Forest project, risk being culled if non-lethal methods to control their numbers fail.

It seems a counter-intuitive approach given the aim is an ecological pursuit. However it proves how circuitous the rewilding arguments are.

Gamekeepers who have worked the land for centuries say their role involves just that ? managing the wildlife populations at tolerable levels to allow the local ecology to thrive.

Only their method has been deemed similarly unpalatable by city dwelling environmentalists who don't like the idea of animals being shot for sport.

Maybe more needs to be done by our public bodies to give greater powers of decision-making to rural communities and to raise awareness of the realities of how our land is managed, rather than focusing on the moral arguments around who owns it?

The friction won't go away. There must be a way for modern business to be able to sensitively exploit our natural assets, stimulate the economy, create jobs and make profits ? but in a way which respects the existing structures and industries which many communities rely on and for which our countryside is famous.

headline: Brewdog  chief complains to Ofcom over 'grossly false' documentary
author: Greig Cameron
text:
The co-founder of Brewdog has formally complained to the broadcast regulator over a BBC Scotland documentary about the company.

James Watt has sent a lengthy list of grievances to Ofcom, the watchdog, detailing what he feels are factual inaccuracies in the programme The Truth About Brewdog. Those include allegations about Watt's personal investments and private financial information as well as his treatment of staff and use of private air travel.

Watt, 39, has previously suggested some of the information made public could only have been obtained through the illegal hacking of his email. The complaint details concerns over the BBC's sources, describing many of them as ex-employees with an "axe to grind".

Watt has also made a formal legal complaint to the BBC, stating the show was inaccurate, misleading and defamatory. The documentary was shown in January and repeated last month.

Brewdog was founded by Watt and his friend Martin Dickie in 2007 and it has grown into one of Scotland's most successful independent brewers. Its headquarters and main brewery are in Aberdeenshire and it has breweries in the US and Australia. It also has thousands of small investors or "equity punks" through crowdfunding. Watt said this year that plans for a stock market flotation, which would probably value the business at £ billion, are being worked on. But its reputation has been under scrutiny since June after dozens of staff, using the collective name of Punks with Purpose, signed an open letter alleging a culture of fear.

Watt instigated an internal review of operations while also bringing in Allan Leighton, the former Asda boss, as chairman. Watt said: "The grossly false picture painted by this documentary is simply not true. These are deeply hurtful and damaging lies based on the claims of a very small group of ex-employees working directly with the BBC, and I will fight to put the record straight."

BBC Scotland said: "The documentary was fair and balanced and included responses from Brewdog's lawyer. Mr Watt declined our offer to be interviewed for the programme."

headline: How Newcastle became winners
author: MARTIN HARDY
text:
Having tasted victory in just one of their first 20 matches of the season, Newcastle won their fifth game in six matches with a 2-1 triumph over Brighton. Their eight-game unbeaten run has lifted them seven points clear of the relegation zone and their new owners are tentatively making plans for next season as a Premier League club. These are five things that have helped Newcastle turn their season around.

Burn baby Burn On Saturday evening, in the Brewdog pub on the way to Newcastle's Quayside, Newcastle defender Dan Burn ended up in the company of a group of Brighton supporters. "Said he was sad to leave us but the situation was right for everyone," @Charlie_Gresty wrote on Twitter. "He chatted to us for longer than he needed to. The nicest guy ever."

That would be an opinion shared by Newcastle supporters now. The £2 million Newcastle paid the club they beat on Saturday is starting look like a bargain.

Burn has been a colossus since he moved back home, to the north east. Howe referenced his leadership qualities. On field he is happy to bring the ball down and play, as well as offering a commanding presence in a defence not noted for such dominance earlier in the season. With Burn, Newcastle are no longer leaky.

Smells like Team Spirit There were 46 of them, kit men, masseurs, physios, coaching staff, and of course, the Newcastle players, even those out of favour, in another team photo to celebrate victory. Ever since Newcastle's first win of the season against Burnley at the start of December, this has been the way.

"It is something we do to foster the enjoyment of winning," said Howe. "That sounds silly but you have to enjoy these moments because they are rare.

"It is just part of the culture we are trying to create here. We are in it together, players, staff, injured players, fit players, subs, everyone plays a huge role."

Saving Ryan There was a goal, an assist and a kick in the head for Ryan Fraser (from Pascal Gross). Three months ago he couldn't even get a game at

Newcastle. Last week he was nominated for the Premier League player of the month for February.

When he was homesick after moving to Bournemouth from Aberdeen at the age of 19, Howe took Fraser out for meals. He sorted out his diet, told him to stop eating pizzas, made him into a Premier League player.

That relationship has been rekindled. Fraser, below, was a player with injury, doubt and no direction before being reunited with his former manager. Now he has brought pace, energy and spirit to the side. He has started the last ten games, of which Newcastle have won five and drawn three and his goal in the 12th minute on Saturday set Newcastle on their way to victory.

He only cost £0 million The transformation of Joelinton has been one of the truly remarkable stories of this season. He was voted Newcastle's player of the month for February by the club's supporters.

There were times during his Newcastle career where that would have seemed implausible. He was jeered by the travelling support following an FA Cup draw at Rochdale at the start of 2020. What a difference two years makes.

In launching an admirable defence of the 25-year-old recently, Sean Longstaff revealed that in training team-mates always want the Brazilian on their side, because he never loses. That was interesting because it highlighted a combativeness and desire that Howe has nurtured to the point it is now one of the key elements of his team.

Newcastle, United "I said before the home crowd can be a huge and positive influence on the players but they can also be a negative one and I think probably, if I'm being honest, that was the situation before."

Those were the words of Graham Potter on another raucous St James' Park crowd. Whatever Newcastle has right now, nothing feels more important than the unity amongst its supporters. They have waited 14 years to show their passion for the club without the spectre of Mike Ashley, the former owner, and it provides a powerful platform for the team to perform, and, as was the case on Saturday, find inspiration to hold off Brighton.

headline: BrewDog  chief hires private investigators to tackle 'smears'
author: Greig Cameron
text:
The boss of the brewing and bar company BrewDog claims he has been the victim of a "co-ordinated criminal campaign" and hired private detectives to gather evidence of it.

James Watt used Integritas Investigative Solutions to look into his concerns about the damage being done to his personal reputation and that of the company he co-founded. The Scottish businessman suggested he had been subject to defamation, blackmail, fraud, malicious communication and harassment over the past two years.

BrewDog has been under pressure since a group of former staff, known as Punks with Purpose, published an open letter last year complaining about the culture fostered under Watt.

Watt, 39, apologised and started a widespread internal review while a new chairman, Allan Leighton, a former Asda chief executive, was brought in to oversee changes.

A BBC Scotland documentary broadcast this year made further allegations about practices at the brewer and Watt's behaviour. Watt has already complained to the BBC and Ofcom, the broadcasting regulator. He has also suggested some of the information made public could only have been obtained through the illegal hacking of his email.

BrewDog was launched by Watt and his friend Martin Dickie in 2007 and now has breweries in Scotland, Australia and the United States plus a portfolio of bars and hotels. The company has been exploring a possible stock exchange listing, expected to value the business at about £ billion.

The claims about private detectives being hired were first published in The Guardian. In response, Watt wrote on LinkedIn: "I have been subject to a two year-long co-ordinated criminal campaign of online harassment, defamation, blackmail, significant fraud, and malicious communications ... We subsequently engaged the services of digital investigative specialists to seek to identify the source of these damaging and false allegations and identify the individuals who defrauded me."

Watt added that a criminal prosecution for serious fraud and malicious communication had begun in London while civil proceedings at the Court of Session in Edinburgh are also under way. He said there was a separate complaint which had been made to police against a founder of the Punk with Purpose group for blackmail.

Watt said: "Criticism of our business is fine and often warranted I fully accept that. Colluding in illegal activity to damage me or the business is not."

headline: BrewDog  in row with HR adviser
author: Jessica Newman
text:
A row has erupted between BrewDog and a human resources consultancy that offered to improve relations between the beer brand and its staff.

Allan Leighton, BrewDog's chairman and the former boss of Asda, has accused Hand & Heart of "amplifying attacks" on its management team and has declined to take part in a proposed reconciliation programme.

In a letter to Kate Bailey, Hand & Heart's managing director, Leighton said he was concerned about a platform that had been set up for BrewDog workers, claiming it was "encouraging participants to submit malicious content ... The unavoidable impression is that of H&H charging the company to extinguish a fire it is fuelling itself."

BrewDog was founded in 2007 by James Watt and Martin Dickie and is based in Ellon, Aberdeenshire. As well as sellings its beer, which includes Punk IPA, it operates more than 100 bars and employs 2,000 people worldwide. Its treatment of staff came into focus after more than 100 former employees published an open letter last June criticising the culture of BrewDog. The BBC also broadcast a Disclosure documentary about the company.

Watt apologised after the complaint by former staff, who went by the name Punks with Purpose. An internal review was launched, with Leighton brought in to oversee changes.

Hand & Heart, which is based in Berlin, has set up a platform on its website where it says that BrewDog workers can independently register their experiences with the company "to assist Punks with Purpose in their core mission of tackling BrewDog's cultural issues". The consultancy had not been formally hired by BrewDog.

The letter from Leighton was first reported by The Mail on Sunday. In it he also raises concerns about paying Hand & Heart "significant" fees to manage a reconciliation programme.

Bailey said: "BrewDog issued a letter to me and my company that was filled with malicious, unfounded accusations designed to discredit me and my work. I published two lengthy and evidence-based factual rebuttals on my website. The letter and its dissemination, in my professional opinion as an experienced workplace consultant, is a failure of leadership and corporate governance who seemingly cannot comprehend the paper trail of facts that easily refute their claims, or how that would reflect on their company in the future.

"For the sake of BrewDog's workforce and the many people exhausted by this situation, I do wish BrewDog could focus on the individuals who have alleged being harmed in their workplaces and make it right with them, and growing into a thriving global company instead of spending their time attacking me and my company on false grounds.'' BrewDog confirmed the letter had been sent but did not comment further.

headline: BREWDOG 'S DONUT BEER TO TAKE HOMER
author: ISAAC CROWSON;WILL BARKER
text:
NEW ALE'S THE HOLEY GRAIL

BREWDOG is launching Homer Simpson's perfect beer flavoured with artisan doughnuts.

The quirky craft tipple jam-packed at six per cent alcohol is said to have a "definite taste" of raspberry preserve and chocolate.

The IPA is a collaboration between the brewing giant based in Ellon, Aberdeenshire and doughnut start-up Project D.

I'll be d'oh nuts about this beer

The firm's director Jacob Watts told The Sun: "It's obviously on the sweet side. But anyone who likes our doughnuts is going to absolutely love a pint of Project D beer.

"Obviously beer and doughnuts are both yeastbased, so it really is a match made in heaven. But it's a limited edition and won't be around for long."

Around 15,000 of the bright pink cans go on sale this month, available via BrewDog's monthly beer club.

Derby-based Project D's hand-decorated range has been served at London Fashion Week, on British Airways flights and they even had an order from Windsor Castle.

Celeb fans include Olympic swimming champ Adam Peaty and the Love Island cast. Jacob added: "We are so pleased to be collaborating with BrewDog. It's amazing to see our branding on the side of a can."

headline: Planting trees in Scotland will not fix climate change
author: John Gapper
text:
Companies and investors should create fewer northern plantations and restore more tropical forests

Trees are lovely things. Sturdy and elegant, with branches that provide a canopy of shade in summer, and often a colourful leaf display in autumn. Not only that, but they suck carbon dioxide from the atmosphere and store it in their trunks. Who could object to a tree?

Even climate change sceptics who bridle at having to drive smaller cars, or live in colder homes, to curb global warming have a soft spot for a forest. Ninety per cent of US adults support the idea of planting 1tn trees around the world to absorb emissions, according to a poll by Pew Research Centre.

But some farmers are wary of trees. The Scottish Land Commission found this week that farmland prices in Scotland increased 31 per cent last year as companies, institutions, investors and charities snapped up estates to plant trees for carbon capture. Crofters who have farmed the land for centuries face being squeezed out by “reen lairds”

BrewDog, the Scottish brewer, is among corporate investors in forestry and last year ran a “uy one, get one tree”romotion for multipacks of beer. Trees provide cover for many environmental promises, from carbon offsets on airline flights to green finance.

The world suffers from the loss of trees. Nearly half of them have been felled since the start of human civilisation, and 15bn more are chopped down every year, according to one study. The loss of biodiversity in tropical rainforests has caused great harm.

But when offered a simple, universally popular remedy to a complex problem, beware. Not only are plantations becoming rivals to traditional farms, but planting one variety of tree in Scotland or the US is a poor substitute for retaining others elsewhere.

“he trees being cut down are beautiful tropical forests that are ecologically diverse and the ones being planted are monocultural pine trees,”ays Tom Crowther, a professor of ecology at ETH Zurich university. Unlike the people who chop them down, trees and forests are not created equal.

The trillion tree target in the Pew poll derives from Crowther’ research into global biodiversity. He and others calculated that the world holds 3tn trees and there is room for nearly 1bn more hectares of canopy cover, providing “n opportunity of climate change mitigation through global tree restoration”

From this came the World Economic Forum’ 1t.org initiative, which has enlisted 30 companies promising to conserve, restore and grow 3.6bn trees in more than 60 countries. Apple will protect 11,000 hectares of Colombian mangrove forest, while Nestlé invests in forest conservation and restoration in Côe d’voire and Ghana.

Note the emphasis on conserving and restoring, which tends to get lost in the rush to plant trees. Pew fell into this trap, asking respondents whether they supported “lanting about a trillion trees” The more important elements were lost in translation.

Tree planting has its role in combating global warming, but Pew is not alone in placing too much weight on it. One reason for all the planting in the UK is that the government has created targets and offered incentives. One study found that almost half of countries’nvironment pledges to boost forests involve commercial tree plantations.

It also suits companies that crave simple metrics. Planting trees in the northern hemisphere is easier and cheaper than reforming a complex supply chain to protect forests. It can be quantified to investors and marketed to consumers as a quick ecological win.

The trouble is, it does much less good than preservation and restoration of what exists already. Plantations that are harvested every couple of decades release carbon back into the air, and lack of variety makes them more vulnerable to disease. A tropical forest is more diverse and harder to replicate, but is ultimately stronger.

In ecological terms, the optimal strategy would be to restore to health all the forests and peatlands that have been degraded, and let nature take its course. “he trillion tree target is not about planting that many trees. It is about building a world in which they naturally regrow,”ays Crowther.

That is a complex message to impart in a world where slogans that cut through are most effective. Crowther has himself faced criticism from other scientists for his “atchy findings” Companies are starting to take it more seriously —he WEF initiative is aimed correctly —ut there needs to be deeper involvement in the right locations for the effort to succeed.

Instead of disruptive attempts to mass manufacture trees, portfolio investment is required in projects that also reward local people. Crowther cites one entrepreneur who sells coffee in Zurich that is grown in forest glades in Ethiopia, rather than by cutting down trees to clear land. The environment benefits and the coffee tastes better.

It is in salutary contrast to Scotland, where farmland prices are being driven up by tree planting. The lesson, not only for governments and companies but for consumers, is to examine carefully any simple woodland fix. The right trees must grow in the right forest.

john.gapper@ft.com

headline: BrewDog  chief to hand £00mn of his personal stake to staff
author: Alice Hancock
text:
Employees in line for payouts of up to £20,000 as brewer looks to move on from claims of ‘oxic’ulture

BrewDog staff are in line for potential payouts of up to £20,000 after its chief executive pledged to share with them £00mn of his personal stake in the UK’ largest craft brewer.

James Watt said on Tuesday that he would donate a fifth of his 24 per cent stake in the company to an employee trust, which would grant share options to BrewDog’ 750 salaried staff over the next four years.

The move comes as the brewer works to repair relations with staff after claims of a “oxic”ork culture exploded last year.

Each employee will receive the same share award, which BrewDog said could amount to £20,000 per employee based on the company’ most recent fundraising round of £.8bn in 2018.

Bar staff paid by the hour, who make up the remainder of BrewDog’ 2,250 employees, will receive half of the profits made in its 111 bars every six months in a separate incentive scheme.

“e wanted to do some radical, different things that would help build the company long term,”att said. “his is very much about ownership, about building a new type of company and about giving back.”

It has been a turbulent year for BrewDog and for Watt, who was accused in a letter signed by almost 300 former employees of creating “ residual feeling of fear”n the company and a misogynistic culture. At the same time, Watt was personally subject to a £00,000 fraud case that is now being heard in the Scottish courts.

BrewDog has slowed its progress towards a long-planned stock market listing. Watt, who hinted in January that a listing could come as soon as this year, said on Tuesday that an IPO was now a “edium as opposed to short term plan”

Since the allegations by the former staff, known as “unks with Purpose” the Aberdeenshire-based brewer has made efforts to reconcile itself with its staff and improve its public image.

In September, BrewDog appointed City grandee Allan Leighton as chair and personal mentor to Watt. In December, it pledged to expand its human resources department, reintroduce quarterly career discussions and employ 600 more staff to ease “ottlenecks”ollowing an independent review.

“ave we always been a perfect company to work for? I can accept that we haven’. Are there things that we can do better? Yes, and we have put a plan in place to do that,”att said.

But Bryan Simpson, hospitality organiser for the union Unite, said that while the share options would be “elcome news for the minority”ho receive them, the move “ill not resolve the toxic culture cultivated and imposed from the very top of the organisation”

BrewDog, which was founded in 2007, grew rapidly due to its unusual publicity stunts and crowdfunding campaigns. It plans to open 22 more bars this year and increase overall headcount to 3,000.

Watt admitted that sharing half of bar profits was “ leap into the unknown”iven current cost pressures on the business.

The cost of aluminium cans has doubled since last year, while malted barley was “ignificantly up”he Scottish founder said. The brewer has also been stung by the recent swings in exchange rate as it sources 95 per cent of its hops in the US.

headline: Beer money at  Brewdog
text:
BREWDOG staff could receive shares in the firm worth £20,000 each as the brewer seeks to repair relations with employees.

The Scottish craft brewery, which also runs 111 bars, wants to mend fences after co-founders James Watt and Martin Dickie were accused last year of creating a 'culture of fear'.

BrewDog, which is weighing up a stock-market listing, commissioned an independent review of its management, and committed itself to leadership training, gave staff a pay rise and introduced a whistleblowing hotline.

Yesterday, it said Watt would give away 5pc of the business, worth almost £00m, to 750 staff. It will see Watt's stake drop from 24.2pc to 19.2pc.

The payout will be distributed evenly among all staff and see each employee being paid £0,000 a year in shares over four years.

It also said half the profit it makes in each of its bars will be shared among its remaining 1,500 team members being paid hourly.

headline: BrewDog  boss gives away £00m of his shares to staff
author: Greig Cameron
text:
Hundreds of workers will be handed a stake in the Scottish drinks firm Brew- Dog after its co-founder unveiled plans to give away £00 million of his shares.

James Watt will move more than 3.7 million shares to an employee benefit trust. Salaried staff will then be offered free stock options over the next four years, starting from June. Brew- Dog estimates about 750 workers are eligible and they are in line to receive shares worth up to £20,000 based on present valuations. The company, which has its headquarters in Aberdeenshire, employs about 2,200 people.

Bar workers will also receive up to 50 per cent of the profit from the area they work in every six months. Brewdog said it was likely to increase the pay for those employees by £,000-£,000 a year.

Watt is not the first Scottish business leader to make a large gesture. Sir Tom

Hunter, the founder of Sports Division, is estimated to have donated at least £38 million to charitable ventures. In 2019 it emerged Sir Brian Souter, the founder of the Stagecoach bus company, had transferred £09 million of shares from his investment firm to fund the Souter Charitable Trust.

The reputation of Watt, 39, and BrewDog was damaged last year after a group of former staff, who called themselves Punks with Purpose, published an open letter accusing him of overseeing a toxic workplace. Watt apologised and promised changes. A BBC Scotland documentary this year made further allegations about Watt's behaviour, which he has denied.

Watt said the decision to reduce his stake from 24.2 per cent to 19.2 per cent was his own, adding: "This is something I wanted to do for our people." BrewDog staff set for £00m shares, page 34

headline: Brewdog  staff set for £00m shares
author: Greig Cameron
text:
The boss of Brewdog is to give £00 million of shares to staff and hopes to lead the business for years to come.

James Watt, right, intends to donate a 5 per cent stake over the next four years to salaried workers at the firm.

The company said that 750 of its 2,200 people were eligible for the scheme, which could mean that each receives shares valued at about £20,000.

In addition, Brewdog said that it was re-shaping its bar and hotel rewards scheme by promising that 50 per cent of profit from those areas will go to the staff who work in them.

Watt, 39, said: "I think both things make us a significantly better company going forward and will help us to realise our long-term ambitions to make a dent in the beer market globally."

Watt's reputation was damaged by an open letter published by former employees last year that claimed he oversaw a toxic workplace culture.

Brewdog brought in Allan Leighton, the former Asda boss, as chairman and set up various new initiatives to improve the environment.

Brewdog was formed by Watt and his friend Martin Dickie in 2008 and has risen to become one of Scotland's biggest independent drinks companies. It has breweries in Scotland, Australia, Germany and the United States as well as bars around the world.

More than 3.7 million shares will be transferred to an employee benefit trust, which is equivalent to 5 per cent of the business. 1.25 per cent of that will be distributed evenly to salaried staff annually in the next four years.

Workers will be given share options although in some countries, such as the United States, they might be offered cash settlements.

Watt's equity stake will reduce from 24.2 per cent to 19.2 per cent as a result.

He said: "This was my initiative. It was something after 15 years working really closely with our team it was something I wanted to do."

An initial public offering (IPO) of the business has long been mooted but Watt said the market backdrop meant it was a "medium term" goal.

The announcement came as Brewdog set out a blueprint for investment to mark its 15th anniversary, including expanding its Aberdeenshire headquarters, completing a whisky distillery and opening 25 new bars this year.

Watt said that the transfer of his shares should not be seen as him looking to exit the business.

headline: HEIRS OF THE DOG
author: SIMON READ
text:
e-mail: simon.read@thesun.co.uk

CSunITY

THE FTSE 100 ROSE 16.78 TO 7,561.33 £ BUYS €.19, US$1.25. BRENT CRUDE $105.98

The Scottish brewer founded by James Watt and Martin Dickie, above has been hit by bullying claims from ex-employees. But Mr Watt said he will hand over nearly a fifth of his shares to loyal staff.

headline: Share around  BrewDog  chief to give staff up to £20,000
text:
BrewDog staff are in line for potential payouts of up to £20,000 after its chief executive pledged to share with them £00mn of his personal stake in the UK's largest craft brewer.

James Watt said yesterday he would donate a fifth of his 24 per cent stake to an employee trust, which would grant share options to BrewDog's 750 salaried staff over four years. The move comes as the Aberdeenshire-based brewer repairs relations after claims of a "toxic" work culture emerged last year.

Each employee will receive the same share award, which BrewDog said could amount to £20,000 per person based on the company's most recent fundraising round of £.8bn in 2018.

Hourly paid bar staff, who make up the rest of BrewDog's 2,250 workers, will receive half of the profits made in its 111 bars every six months in a separate incentive scheme.

"We wanted to do some radical, different things that would help build the company long term," Watt said. "This is very much about ... building a new type of company and about giving back."

It has been a turbulent year for BrewDog and for Watt, who was accused in a letter signed by almost 300 former employees of creating "a residual feeling of fear" in the company and a misogynistic culture. Watt was also subject to a £00,000 fraud case now being heard in Scottish courts.

BrewDog has slowed its progress towards a stock market listing. Watt, who hinted in January a listing could come this year, said yesterday an IPO was now a "medium as opposed to short-term plan". Alice Hancock

headline: Rewilding is taking over our countryside
author: Magnus Linklater
text:
Buy-ups by green lairds do not always benefit locals or those who have made the acres beautiful

The fox quartered the field with all the cunning of its breed. Newborn lambs, sensing danger, raced towards their mothers, who stood facing the enemy, ready to offer protection. A ewe can guard one lamb against an attack, but not two, so twins are always vulnerable and the fox knows that. Instinctively he headed up the field, separating the singles from the twins, and then moved in for the kill. By the end of the week, three lambs had gone. We saw the blood on the ground.

This is the kind of scene you never get on Countryfile. It is nasty, brutish, and harrowing for the shepherd. The fox has to be stopped, because he will return every day as long as there are spring lambs. Our local shepherd, a young woman highly skilled at her job, needed help. She turned to the gamekeeper, who was there within the hour and shot the fox that night.

Lovers of the countryside don't like to hear this kind of story and I don't blame them. There is nothing pretty about it. All the talk these days is about rewilding the landscape, saving the environment and returning the land to nature. Across Scotland thousands of acres are being re-forested, or allowed to return to the wild a policy encouraged by government on the ground that trees and peatland are part of our armoury against climate change: the more trees planted and peat restored, the more carbon captured.

This has inspired a new species of landowner, more interested in turning farmland into forestry, or grouse moors into wilderness, than in tilling the soil or stalking deer. Thus it is that Anders Povlsen, the

Danish billionaire, has overtaken the Duke of Buccleuch as Scotland's largest landowner, and though he appears to break every rule in the SNP handbook property magnate, foreign national, buying up thousands of acres of Scottish land no word of criticism is voiced, presumably because he neither shoots nor fishes, so far as I know, and is therefore not a member of the landed gentry.

He is not alone. A company called Highlands Rewilding has announced a scheme for "mass-ownership nature-recovery" aimed at a "broader base of co-ownership" of Scottish land. Starting with the Bunloit estate on the north shores of Loch Ness, owned by Jeremy Leggett, it has drawn investment of £.5 million from 50 shareholders to buy an additional 1,600 acres of ground, which will be turned over to rewilding. The 5,500 Kildrummy estate, now owned by an American property owner and his wife, is doing the same, calling shooting and stalking "an abuse of the land". These so-called "green lairds" have almost doubled the price of land in the last few years and they include some decidedly idiosyncratic names. Oxford University, for instance, has bought the 7,400 acre Urlar estate that was once a famous grouse moor. Brewdog, the beer company, now owns 2,000 acres of farmland north of Loch Ness. Julia Hands, a Guernsey tax exile, has spent £7 million buying almost 30,000 acres in Scotland for planting trees.

The emerging carbon market means much of the rewilded land can be turned into a money-spinner. Commercial companies value wild land, whether trees or peatland, for its potential to capture emissions and therefore fulfil their own commitments to carbon capture.

We will hear more and more of such phrases as "natural capital", whereby land is assessed not just for its potential for agriculture, more for its contribution to reducing carbon emissions. And nothing wrong with that, you may say. Except, as Andy Wightman, the land campaigner, points out "anyone from anywhere can buy as much land as they like". As he said recently, there is "a level of incomprehension that important assets, which impact upon local economies and the environment, should be just sold to the highest bidder no questions asked".

Mairi McAllan, the minister for environment and land reform, agrees: "The test should always be, are the people who live and work in this area benefiting from it?"

Scotland's land, she says, should "absolutely" be used to mitigate climate change but in a way that people support. What is certain is that, for all their credentials as green campaigners, the new landowners have little interest in the needs of farmers, or their traditional partners the gamekeepers. Nevertheless, it is they who have formed the landscape that most country-lovers cherish.

They are criticised for burning heather, but if they did not do so those much-loved purple hills would be nothing but rank overgrowth, highly vulnerable to wildfires. Gamekeepers get paid to do this by NatureScot, the environment agency. They are also funded to control predators foxes and crows that would otherwise greatly reduce the number and variety of wild birds, such as the sadly vanishing curlew and plover.

Mary Colwell, author of Curlew Moon, concedes that the places where the curlew flourish most tend to be grouse moors, because it is there that gamekeepers control the predators that raid their nests.

This is a battle for the heart of the countryside. There is a balance to be struck between the need to save the planet and the responsibility to protect those who live and work on the land. To the list of endangered species we may soon need to add farmers and their protectors the gamekeepers.

It is gamekeepers and farmers who have formed the landscape

headline: Let summer be-gin
text:
WHICH COOLING TIPPLE IS JUST THE TONIC?

THE sun has got its hat on, sip-sip-sip hooray - time for a cooling gin and tonic.

But which gin gets the biggest grin?

The tipple may scream summer like cricket fans with hankies on their heads, the jingle of a Mr Whippy van, sunburn on the prom, stawberry picking and ... rainy Bank Holidays.

But there are now so many flavours it is easy to feel all mixered up.

THEA JACOBS helps you gen up on your gin as she tries the various tastes - someone has to - and marks each one out of five.

LEMON & LIME Brewdog Cactus And Lime (Tesco, £5)

IS cactus a flavour we have been crying out for? Hmm. I can smell the lime, the dominant taste here. But I'm unsure where the cactus comes in, other than with the name. Sorry, this doesn't spike my interest.

2

Hortus Lemon And Lime (Lidl, £4.99)

SWEET and sherberty as lemonade but with a delightful kick. The smell and taste is reminiscent of Sprite. Very easy to drink. In fact, once this is mixed with tonic, you may forget it is boozy at all. Risky.

Sipsmith Lemon Drizzle (Tesco, £8.50)

4 DRINKING this is a piece of cake. On its own there is a sweet, lemon taste and lovely, refreshing smell. But when mixed, it blended too much into the lemonade and taste was lost - making it hard to justify the price.

Greyson's Sicilian Lemon (Aldi, £2.99)

3 CHEAP as chips but not as tasty. Strong lemon flavour when you first sip it but within seconds this gives way to the sting of cheap alcohol that could be used to strip paint. You would want a lot of tonic to make this one work.

1

Malfy Blood Orange (Tesco, £8)

ORANGE SMELLS bloody great, does this one, and has a strong dark-orange colour when mixed. Unmistakable bloodorange taste and doesn't feel artificially flavoured. Lovely and delicate gin but a little on the pricey side.

4

Sipsmith Orange (Waitrose, £2.50)

VERY subtle. The orange compliments the botanicals and doesn't overpower them. But more generic citrus than properly orangey. Barely needed a mixer, so if you are a fan of straight gin, this is for you.

Adnams Orange And Sea Buckthorn (online, £1.99)

3 LITTLE orange flavour, but the smell of seaweed, transports me to the beach. The sea buckthorn is the overpowering taste. But there's a reason we don't drink seawater. For the price, I would expect nicer.

Greyson's Mediterranean Orange (Aldi, £2.99)

1 PROPER hit of orange with all the zing and zest you would expect. Like a slightly boozy orange juice. Nothing artifical about the taste or smell, and leaves a lovely, fruity flavour in the mouth. Topnotch drink at such a low price.

5

Sipsmith Strawberry Smash (Tesco, £8.50)

BERRIES NOT a whiff of strawberry, no matter how hard I search. It's like straight gin. Very subtle, it feels light and summery. Not very flavoured compared to the rest but does at least. refresh you on a summer day.

2

Whitley Neill Raspberry (Asda. £5)

SMELLS saccarine, like raspberry sweets rather than the real berry. I had been hoping for a tart hit but this is just overwhelmingly sugary. If you have a sweet tooth you will love this, but it is one of the least subtle flavours.

Blackfriars Raspberry And Rhubarb (Sainsbury's, £5)

3 MUCH tarter than the other raspberry gins, the rhubarb gives it a bitter kick. Feels like I am drinking raspberry and rhubarb crumble, as if someone bottled that summer pudding, and I have no complaints.

Warner's Raspberry (Waitrose, £0)

5 DOESN'T really smell like raspberry to me but I am blown away by the fabulously succulent taste. It is as if they have crushed real berries right into my glass. Nice balance of sweet and sour, like a really good raspberry jam.

4

Gordon's Tropical Passionfruit (Sainsbury's, £7)

TROPICAL SMELLS like a poor man's porn star martini compared to the best of the other tipples on this page. Not the strongest flavour at first, but leaves a sweet passionfruit aftertaste. For the price, a decent drink.

4

Chase Pink Grapefruit And Pomelo (Waitrose, £8)

GRAPEFRUIT aroma explodes out of the bottle. A rather bitter drink, like the fruit it is based on, and with a strong alcoholic aftertaste. I've never been a big fan of grapefruit and this does nothing to combat that.

Blackfriars Mango And Pineapple (Sainsbury's, £5)

3 I HAD high hopes of this Sainsbury's Taste The Difference gin, as it smells like a tropical holiday. But I couldn't taste pineapple or mango and it was bitter. More of a wet weekend than a tropical getaway.

Bathtub Gin Grapefruit and Rosemary (online, 36.95)

2 SO much rosemary from the smell that I would be tempted to slosh it over roast lamb. Sadly, it looks like sparkling wee when in the glass. Tastes better than it smells and looks, but doesn't really work for me.

3

headline: margate. rocks
text:
KENT TOWN HAS SUN, SEA, SAND AND ... HIPSTERS

OFTEN dubbed Shoreditchon-Sea, after the trendy London postcode, Kent's arty seaside town of Margate is on the up, with new bars, restaurants and attractions.

It also has a huge golden sandy beach to tempt you.

Here, KARA GODFREY offers up the ten best reasons to visit ... CLASSIC SIGHT: Entering Margate, you cannot miss the Ferris Wheel of Dreamland, one of the UK's oldest theme parks. Opening again from today, it is free to enter, with rides bought by the ticket and arcades to spend the pennies in. It also has live acts this summer including Craig David, All Saints and De La Soul. See dreamland.co.uk TOP TOUR: Pick up a free town tour map from the Visitor Information Centre, and enjoy a self-guided trip around town. With 27 spots to explore, you'll visit the Old Town, home to quaint shops and restaurants, the Turner Contemporary art gallery and the harbour's Shell Lady statue. See visitkent.co.uk CULTURAL FIX: The arrival of local girl and artist Tracey Emin's Turner gallery was the starting point for a renaissance in the town's cultural fortunes. There is currently an exhibition of her work at the town's Carl Freedman gallery. And there's more modern art to enjoy at the striking Turner Contemporary. Or catch a live show at the Winter Gardens. For comedy lovers, there is always something on, too. Previous acts include Ed Gamble and Nish Kumar, and there are upcoming shows by Jason Manford. Music fans can enjoy talent such as Texas. See margate-live.com FUN NEIGHBOURHOOD: Margate's

Old Town is a popular haunt for tourists, but locals reckon it is the Cliftonville area that has the bestkept secrets. Start the day with a coffee and cake from Skinny Dip, do some vinyl shopping at Ghost Papa and have a drink at one of the many new bars such as Daisy for a cardamom Espresso Martini, or Stingray for locally brewed beer. THE WOW MOMENT: Weird and wonderful, Margate's Shell Grotto was discovered in 1835, but the Grade I listed building's purpose and age remain unknown even now.

Made up of a whopping 4.6million shells, the grotto is open to the public and is well worth a visit. See shellgrotto.co.uk.

NEW FOR 2022: Test your nerves at the newly opened Margate Leisure Centre where you can hurl an axe at a target with Black Axe Throwing Co, or try petanque a form of boules with Klak. Finish off with a drink at the bar and grab a burger and fries from on-site Radburger. FANTASTIC FEAST: Enjoy great food with a brilliant view at the Waverley House restaurant, complete with a roof terrace overlooking the beach. The seasonal menu

LOCAL ... artist Tracey changes often but you can expect dishes such as freshly caught prawns with aioli or pan-fried seabass, and finish off with a delicious carameland-chocolate mousse. See waverleyhousemargate.co.uk ONE MORE TREAT: Grab your wetsuit and head to one of the two lidos in Margate. The main one, Margate Lido, is nearer the station, but Walpole Bay Tidal Lido has the UK's largest tidal pool. Go for a swim then head to the tiny wooden sauna a few steps from the beach, run by local skincare brand Haeckels. GREAT OUTDOORS: If you have had enough of sunbathing on the beach, weather permitting hit Margate Sup and Surf, hire a board and take to the waves. Classes range from beginner to expert but best is the Sunset Sup Club to watch the sun set. See margatesupandsurf.co.uk THE GRAND HOTEL: Go for showbiz opulence at the fab Albion Rooms, owned by rockers The Libertines. Opened in 2020, each room has vintage furnishings and an Apple Beats sound system, with moody decor suited to the grunge vibe. The kitchen is loved by locals for its Sunday Roast, but visit on a Monday for a burger and a pint for a tenner. There is also cafecum-bar The Arcady Coffee House, and The Waste Land bar which hosts live music, comedy and as it's named after a TS Eliot poem poetry. It boasts two exclusive drinks BrewDog's Wasteland IPA and The Libertines' Gunga Gin.

GO: MARGATE

GETTING THERE: Southeastern run frequent high-speed trains from London St Pancras, Victoria, London Bridge or Charing Cross to Margate, taking around 90minutes. See southeasternrailway.co.uk STAYING THERE: One night B&B at The Albion Rooms is from £36per night, based on two sharing. See thealbionrooms.com MORE INFO: See visitthanet.co.uk/see-and-do/margate.

headline: Martin Sorrell’ S4 Capital looks to bolster audit committee after results delay
author: Patricia Nilsson
text:
Advertising start-up says it has already made changes in financial control, risk and governance

S4 Capital is looking for a new chair of its audit committee, after Sir Martin Sorrell’ advertising start-up was forced to delay the publication of its annual results earlier this year.

In an update on Monday morning, the London-listed company said it had already made changes up to board level in areas such as financial control, risk and governance.

Rupert Faure Walker, who has led S4’ audit committee since the business was established in 2018, will remain a member of the committee but it will have a new chair, the company said. The veteran financier was one of the first contacts Sorrell tapped for the board of his new venture.

The company also said that sales in the three months ending March had reached £06.8mn, up 40 per cent on a pro forma basis. It was helped by two new “hopper”lients, its name for those that bring in more than $20mn a year in revenue.

It also won new contracts with companies such as BrewDog, TikTok and Booking.com.

Sorrell, who was ousted from WPP in 2018 after building the business into one of the world’ biggest advertising empires, continues to pursue aggressive growth through acquisition at S4.

Two weeks ago, the company struck its 30th deal in just under four years when it bought Los Angeles-based tech services company TheoremOne. It was S4’ first acquisition since it issued a notice in March saying its auditor had not been able to sign off on its results just hours before they were due to be published.

The TheoremOne deal was structured as a 50:50 split in cash and S4 shares, an approach the business has taken with many of its previous deals, which some analysts had argued would be harder given the recent plunge in S4 stock.

The company’ share price was up 4 per cent on Monday morning, but remains more than a third lower than before it delayed its results.

Analysts at Jefferies noted that S4 had “ot seen a slowdown in client activity”nd argued that now that the company had published its results “nvestors can refocus on a compelling story”

headline: S4 Capital rings changes in auditing
author: Louisa Clarence-Smith
text:
Sir Martin Sorrell's S4 Capital has said it is looking for a new audit committee chairman after "unacceptable delays" to the publication of its annual results.

The London-listed digital advertising company released its 2021 results this month following two delays after external auditors at PwC required more time. The uncertainty over the second delay in late March led to a shares sell-off.

S4 Capital said yesterday that it was seeking a new chairman of its audit committee. Rupert Faure Walker, 74, the non-executive director who leads it, will remain on the committee. The company said it was also strengthening its financial reporting procedures to try to avoid further reporting delays.

S4 was founded in 2018 shortly after Sorrell, 77, left WPP, the FTSE 100 group that in three decades he had turned from a wire baskets maker into one of the world's biggest advertising groups. His new company focuses on digital, the fastest-growing advertising sector. It has grown quickly to emply more than 7,500 people in 33 countries.

In a trading update, S4 said that likefor-like sales in the three months to the end of March were up 41 per cent to £07 million. Its new clients included Brewdog, Tiktok and Diageo. S4 expects robust demand for digital advertising to continue despite forecasts of slowing economic growth.

"New business activity remains frenetic and the pipeline is above the level at this time last year, with considerable current pitch and land and expand opportunities," Sorrell, its executive chairman, said.

Jessica Pok, an analyst at Peel Hunt, the broker, said: "S4 Capital has seen no change in the spend of its client base and has added two [big clients]. Even if global marketing spend does reduce in the near term, we believe S4 Capital's client mix provides resilience."

Shares in S4 Capital rose 12½, 4.3 per cent, to 297¾, but remain about 48 per cent down since the start of the year.
