headline: Brewdog  takes big bite out of its losses
author: Cameron Scottish
text:
Brewdog, the brewer and pubs group, has narrowed its annual loss after its revenue grew by 21 per cent.

The company's annual results show that revenue, including duty, was at £86 million in 2021, up from £38 million in the previous year, helping to narrow its pre-tax loss to £.4 million, down from a £2.5 million loss in 2020.

Brewdog's market share in the UK grew in spite of Covid restrictions and lockdowns across last year, with beer volumes increasing by 20 per cent to 962,000 hectolitres one hectolitre is 100 litres with the greater scale leading to an improvement in gross margins.

Brewdog was founded in 2007 by friends James Watt and Martin Dickie. It has its headquarters and main brewery in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, as well as other production sites in Columbus,

Ohio, Brisbane and Berlin. The company has more than 2,300 staff and operates about 100 bars across the world as well as some boutique hotels.

Brewdog's reputation was under intense scrutiny last year when a group of former employees published an open letter claiming a toxic culture. Watt apologised and instigated a review that resulted in Allan Leighton, the former Asda boss, being brought in as chairman.

Court actions have been launched by Watt, 39, who claims he has been the victim of a campaign to damage him and the company. Complaints have also been lodged with Ofcom, the broadcast regulator, over a BBC Scotland documentary that looked into Brewdog.

Watt announced this month that he would gift 5 per cent of his own equity in the business to staff over the next four years. That portion of his stake is valued at about £00 million.

The company said the most recent loss was a direct result of the impact on its retail arm during the pandemic and increasing investment across the business. It hired more than 800 extra staff in the year as well as spending £3 million on new venues and £7 million on marketing.

Watt, its chief executive, described it as "another year of huge progress", and added: "Though challenges remained, not least the continued closure of much of our bar estate during the year, we delivered significant growth across the business."

The 2022 financial year is said to have started strongly, with the bar division delivering record revenue and returning to profit as a result of fewer Covid rules. The company is expanding its Ellon headquarters, working on a Scotch whisky distillery and planning to open several new bars this year.

headline: UK sales of low-alcohol and no-alcohol beers almost double in 5 years
author: Judith Evans
text:
Alternative versions of mainstream brands help drive uptake

UK sales of low-alcohol and no-alcohol beers have almost doubled in five years as weaker versions of global brands such as Heineken and Budweiser helped convert drinkers from carbonated soft drinks.

Drinkers in the UK bought $454mn of alcohol-free and low-alcohol brews in 2021, up from $240mn in 2016, according to research group IWSR, as they tapped into lower drinking rates among younger generations and fresh marketing drives by global brewers.

This included $184mn of “lcohol-free”eers, which have 0.5 per cent ABV (alcohol by volume) or less, a figure more than triple the $52mn of these products sold five years earlier, even though the overall beer market shrank.

Trevor Stirling, analyst at Bernstein, said the country had become “ne of the hotspots”or growth in low-alcohol and no-alcohol brewing.

“t’ an adult soft drink rather than a substitute for alcohol    he acceptance of the products is probably higher than I was expecting,”tirling said.

UK innovations such as Brewdog’ Nanny State, launched in 2011, and Adnams’host Ship, launched in 2012, helped the UK market gain a head start, he said.

Fernando Tennenbaum, chief financial officer of Anheuser-Busch InBev, said the world’ largest brewer was recording double-digit annual growth in low-alcohol and no-alcohol beers, with softer versions of brands including Budweiser, Corona, Becks and Leffe on sale in the UK.

“ntil five years ago no one was investing seriously in it [low and no alcohol],”e said. “e learned that it is better to have extensions of mainstream brands as opposed to developing new brands.”

The technology is evolving, he said, with brewers now extracting alcohol from finished beer rather than halting the process midway. Low-alcohol and no-alcohol brews account for 3.1 per cent of the UK’ beer market, IWSR said, compared with 2.7 per cent globally.

Heineken’ launch of the 0.0 version of its flagship brand in 2017 was a pivotal moment, analysts said, together with its decision to use the alcohol-free version as sponsor for the Uefa Europa League and Formula One.

Emily Neill, head of research at IWSR, said promoting low-alcohol drinks was partly a commercial decision.

“hat you’e seen in markets such as the UK and US is consumers becoming much more conscious of their health    here’ a higher proportion of younger consumers who don’ drink at all or would like to moderate their consumption,”he said.

“he other point is    t’ a push from the companies to meet their ESG [environmental, social and governance] targets, to actually do something about the issues around responsible drinking.”

Alcohol-free beers are often more profitable because they do not incur excise duty, said Stirling, although Neill said higher production and storage costs could cut into that advantage.

AB InBev said six years ago that it would aim for low-alcohol and no-alcohol beers to make up a fifth of sales by 2025, a target it admits it is unlikely to meet, with about 6 per cent of sales currently from the products. But Tennenbaum said the target had served to “end a signal inside the company”

Neill said IWSR expected low-alcohol drinks to remain an “nteresting niche”kin to speciality beers, rather than a large chunk of the market.

Additional reporting by Alice Hancock

headline: Zero hour Alcohol-free beer sales on the increase
text:
UK sales of low-alcohol and no-alcohol beers have almost doubled in five years as weaker versions of brands such as Heineken and Budweiser helped convert drinkers from soft drinks.

Drinkers in the UK bought $454mn of alcohol-free and low-alcohol brews in 2021, up from $240mn in 2016, according to research group IWSR, as they tapped into lower drinking rates among younger generations and fresh marketing drives by global brewers.

This included $184mn of "alcoholfree" beers, which have 0.5 per cent ABV (alcohol by volume) or less, a figure more than triple the $52mn of these products sold five years earlier, even though the overall beer market shrank.

Trevor Stirling, analyst at Bernstein, said the country had become "one of the hotspots" for growth in low-alcohol and no-alcohol brewing.

"It's an adult soft drink rather than a substitute for alcohol ... The acceptance of the products is probably higher than I was expecting," he said.

UK innovations such as Brewdog's Nanny State, launched in 2011, and

Adnams' Ghost Ship, launched in 2012, had helped the UK market gain a head start, he said.

Fernando Tennenbaum, chief financial officer of Anheuser-Busch InBev, said the largest brewer was recording double-digit annual growth in low-alcohol and no-alcohol beers, with softer versions of brands including Budweiser, Corona, Becks and Leffe on sale in the UK.

"Until five years ago, no one was investing seriously in [low and noalcohol]," he said. "We learned that it is better to have extensions of mainstream brands as opposed to developing new brands."

The technology was evolving, he said, with brewers extracting alcohol from finished beer rather than halting the process midway. Low-alcohol and no-alcohol brews accounted for 3.1 per cent of the UK's beer market, IWSR said, compared with 2.7 per cent globally.

Heineken's launch of the 0.0 version of its flagship brand in 2017 was pivotal, analysts said, together with its decision to use the alcohol-free version as sponsor for the Uefa Europa League and Formula One.

Emily Neill, head of research at IWSR, said promoting low-alcohol drinks was partly a commercial decision.

"What you've seen in markets such as the UK and US is consumers becoming much more conscious of their health ... There's a higher proportion of younger consumers who don't drink at all or would like to moderate their consumption."

Alcohol-free beers were often more profitable because they did not incur excise duty, said Stirling, although Neill said higher production and storage costs could cut into that advantage.

AB InBev said six years ago that it would aim for low-alcohol and noalcohol beers to make up a fifth of sales by 2025, a target it admitted it was unlikely to meet, with about 6 per cent of sales from the products.

But Tennenbaum said the target had served to "send a signal inside the company".

Additional reporting by Alice Hancock

headline: BrewDog  is thirsty for new direction
author: Mike Wade
text:
The world's number one independent beer brand is changing after a year of controversy, Mike Wade writes

At an entrance to BrewDog's gin production line, a curly-haired guy in a hi-vis jacket marked "Mental Health First Aider" steps aside to allow a guided tour to pass by.

The curly bloke is among more than 100 employees who have recently taken a mental health training course, "a big thing for our team", according to Martin Dickie, one of Brewdog's co-founders.

"It's related to where the world is now and having a more responsible understanding of other people's struggles in life," Dickie, 39, said. "I don't think it's related to [our] business practice."

Perhaps not, but the fact that Dickie himself rather than James Watt, his friend and fellow co-founder, has just hosted a two-hour tour of BrewDog's Ellon headquarters, north of Aberdeen, seems significant.

Watt, 39, has often been the public face of the company throughout its helter-skelter growth, from start-up with two men and a dog in Fraserburgh in 2007, to BrewDog's position as the world's No 1 independent beer brand.

Watt was the brains behind its most outrageous promotional stunts such as a beer packaged in roadkill and it was the same man who penned a uniquely aggressive self-help guide, Business for Punks, whose chapter headings include: "Being reasonable is for ambitionless wimps".

Last year, disastrously, it was Watt at the centre of a whirlwind of accusations of a toxic and sexist workplace culture, catalysed when Punks with Purpose, a group of 300 former and current workers, signed a letter complaining of a policy of "growth at all costs". In a subsequent BBC documentary he was the subject of allegations hotly denied of predatory behaviour.

There has been a year of "self-reflection" at BrewDog, Dickie says, with the appointment of Allan Leighton as nonexecutive chairman and the commissioning of an independent review into its workplace culture.

The review, by the Wiser consultancy, said that some current employees felt "uncomfortable speaking up and challenging" behaviour, "partly related to James [Watt's] previous demanding leadership style and the way decisions were made in previous eras". As a result a new workplace code is in place among other things empowering employee representative groups.

His guided tour ends at Watt's office, which looks a little different than it did five years ago. Then BrewDog had just struck a £13 million deal with the American investor TSG, and the chief executive was at his peak of uncompromising powers. On the wall, behind his head, was a sign reading: "Being reasonable is the most common path to mediocrity."

Now the sign is gone, and rather than eyeball his visitors across a giant desk, Watt greets them from a couch, a man apparently chastened by the recent past. The author who wrote "We tore up the business rule book and did things on our own terms" now has a mentor, in the shape of his new chairman.

That doesn't mean he has completely changed but he has been prepared to "work through the things that I can get better at", he says. "I think with that help we can help a better company, while keeping the edge, which is important because if you tune that out then you [lose] intensity."

He won't comment on matters awaiting adjudication: he has a complaint under consideration by Ofcom against the BBC documentary and there are actions pending against people he alleges have harassed and defrauded him.

Does he blame himself at least in part for the problems which have befallen the company? "I can't blame anyone but myself," he says. So if someone told him he had been too abrasive? "I would accept that," he says. And have they told him? "A few times." Yes, he adds, he has been described as part of the problem at the company.

Watt goes on: "It was never my intention at all. I was just so focused on building the business, there have been times I haven't focused on what does this mean for the team and how hard should we push this thing?"

The focus now, he said, is on sustainability and on "our people".

headline: Punk brewer is growing up
text:
BrewDog has received planning permission to plant 1.1 million trees at its Lost Forest, the 9,308-acre former Kinrara estate, near Aviemore. It's part of a programme to maintain the company as carbon negative. BrewDog appointed a director of sustainability, Sarah Warman, last year.

At its Ellon HQ a new £2 million green gas plant, including an anaerobic digester, will help the company to recycle most of the 200 million litres of waste water produced every year in the beer-making process, as well as generating bio-methane to power the brewery's boilers.

An employee ownership programme will distribute 5 per cent of the company evenly among 750 salaried staff over the next four years. Each BrewDog bar shares half of its profits with its crew.

The ambition is to grow from 16th most popular beer brand globally into one of the world's top five. That, James Watt says, was their vision from the start.

headline: Why beer in your gut could beat disease
author: Rhys Blakely
text:
A daily bottle of lager may boost the range of bacteria and stave off chronic conditions, writes Rhys Blakely

It was Thomas Jefferson who argued that "beer, if drunk in moderation, softens the temper, cheers the spirit and promotes health".

He appears to have been on to something, as a new study suggests a daily bottle of lager could help men avoid chronic diseases by boosting the range of bacteria in their guts. Researchers found men who drank one 330ml bottle of alcoholic or non-alcoholic lager a day for a month had a more diverse set of gut microbes. They believe this could help cut the risk of chronic conditions, including heart disease and diabetes.

"One beer per day, with or without alcohol, can be used as a strategy to improve our microbiota, as part of a wellbalanced diet," said Professor Ana Faria of the University of Lisbon, one of the authors of the study. "But we need to be careful with the results so we don't pass on the message that the more the better."

The findings, published yesterday in the Journal of Agricultural and Food Chemistry, may help to explain other studies, which have suggested that modest doses of beer are linked to a lower risk of cardiovascular disease. Faria and her colleagues believe that compounds found in lager known as polyphenols, which were equally abundant in the non-alcoholic and alcoholic types tested by the researchers, are likely to be involved.

The study involved 19 healthy men aged between 23 and 58, who were randomly divided into two groups. They drank a small bottle of either alcoholic (5.2 per cent) or non-alcoholic lager with dinner for four weeks.

They were told to not change their physical activity levels and to maintain their dietary habits. The researchers found that the participants'

weight, body mass index and tests for heart health did not change during the study, but at the end of the four-week period, both groups had greater bacterial diversity in their gut microbiome and higher levels of alkaline phosphatase in their faeces, indicating improved intestinal health.

Trillions of bacteria are found in the digestive tracts of humans, and directly impact the wellbeing of their host. Previous studies have shown that when more types of micro-organisms are present, people tend to have a lower chance of developing metabolic diseases, such as obesity and type 2 diabetes, irritable bowel syndrome and auto-immune diseases. The researchers behind the new study had also previously found a link between decreased bacterial diversity and an increased risk of severe Covid. "We now have strong evidence that good, healthy and diverse microbiota is part of good health," Faria said.

Other tactics likely to boost gut microbe diversity include eating vegetables and fruit which also contain polyphenols as well as whole grains.

Faria added: "This effect was independent of the alcohol content of beer so beer without alcohol can have the same effect without the effect of ethanol, making it an appealing choice." She and her colleagues now hope to confirm the findings with a larger study. BrewDog thirsts for new path, page 18

headline: Boss: I'd go bust for planet save bid
author: STUART PATTERSON
text:
BREWDOG boss James Watt has told how he's willing to risk the beer giant going bust in his bid to save the planet.

The tycoon, 39, revealed accountants were baffled by his plans to plant more than a million trees to soak up carbon at his new £million estate.

And he admitted the eco project will likely leave him out of pocket.

But he insisted: "It has nothing at all to do with making money. It's going to lose a significant amount of money for us.

"It's OK for this business to fail, it's not OK for this planet to fail."

Mr Watt has bought the former Kinrara Estate near Aviemore, Invernessshire, which covers more than 9,300 acres.

He's going to transform it into the Lost Forest and restore native trees, including oak and birch.

And he'll consult locals on the move, which he hopes will attract visitors to the area.

headline: BREWDOG  founder James Watt, determined [...]
author: Ephraim Hardcastle
text: BREWDOG founder James Watt, determined to make his craft beer business a £billion company in 2017, asked his assistant to electronically transfer an investment cheque for 'tens of millions' from his lawyer to his bank account. 'For some reason, there was a typo in the account numbers,' he recalls. 'The sort code sent it to a bank in St Petersburg. The money was lost for four days.' Is it any surprise that after retrieving the dosh James took himself off to an American wood for five days of intensive therapy?

headline: BrewDog  boss seeks autism diagnosis after bullying claim
author: Peter Chappell
text:
The chief executive of BrewDog, who was accused by hundreds of staff of bullying and misconduct, has said he is seeing a specialist to explore whether he has autism.

James Watt, 38, said he had spoken to a medical professional after he was described in The Times in April as a "cold-eyed" person who struggled to "express empathy or read social cues".

Watt was accused by BrewDog staff of abusing his power in the workplace in an open letter published last year. Watt apologised at the time and promised change.

In an interview with the businessman Steven Bartlett, Watt admitted that he had been "too intense and demanding" and pushed people "too far", but said the behaviour had been well-intentioned and caused by his "high standards".

Watt told Bartlett's Diary of a CEO podcast: "I think I might have some kind of light-level autism in the mix that would explain some of the social cue thing, some of the mindset thing, and some of the awkwardness as well.

"Because of that exact quote and I was chatting with my doctor, and she's like, 'I've thought that for a while, James, quite possibly'. So I'm working with a specialist at the moment to see if there's a diagnosis there or not."

Watt said that he had had "100 per cent good intentions" towards his staff, despite the company's culture having been labelled "rotten". "It's completely fair to say, at times in the journey I have been too intense," he said.

"I have been too demanding, that I have set standards for the team which I would set for myself, and for a lot of the team members that is unattainable.

"I just pushed for such high standards, unrealistic deadlines it's because I was so focused on 'let's build the thing, let's create more jobs, let's deliver more value for our customers'. " BrewDog has been under pressure since a group of former staff members, known as Punks with Purpose, published the open letter complaining about the culture fostered under Watt.

BrewDog was formed by Watt and his friend Martin Dickie in 2008, and has risen to become one of Scotland's biggest independent drinks companies. It has breweries in Australia, Germany and the US, and bars around the world.

A number of female bartenders said they had felt "uncomfortable" and "powerless" when Watt visited their workplaces. They were advised by colleagues on how to avoid unwelcome attention from him, according to an investigation by Disclosure on BBC Scotland. Watt denied the allegations.

Watt described the Punks with Purpose letter as "so upsetting, but so important". He said: "Our focus now is not on contradicting or contesting the details but to listen, learn and act."

It was reported this year that Watt was prosecuting a woman he said had given false information about people making "malicious" comments online.

Watt said in May that he intended to give £00 million of shares to staff and hoped to lead the business for years to come. He said he would donate a 5 per cent stake over four years to salaried workers. The company said that 750 of its 2,200 staff were eligible, which may mean that each receives shares valued at about £20,000. Watt's stake will fall from 24.2 per cent to 19.2 per cent.

headline: Brewdog  boss 'too hard' on his staff
author: COLAN LAMONT
text:
BREWDOG boss James Watt admitted he pushed staff too far after he was accused over a "toxic" work culture.

The co-founder of the craft beer giants said: "It's fair to say at times in the journey I've been too intense, I have been too demanding. That I set standards for the team that I'd set for myself.

"And for a lot of the team members that is unattainable. I did push people too far."

Watt, 39, told Dragons' Den star Steven Bartlett on his Diary of a CEO podcast: "We had to do things that were provocative, and sometimes we crossed that edge."

A BBC documentary claimed Watt abused his power at the firm he set up in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, in 2007 with pal Martin Dickie, 39. Last year more than 100 employees signed a letter claiming their mental health had suffered.

headline: Need to know
text:
1

The executive in charge of easyJet's day-to-day operations has left the budget airline amid growing anger about thousands of flight cancellations as airports were hit by more travel disruption. Peter Bellew resigned as chief operating officer after two and a half years "to pursue other business opportunities", the airline said. Page 10 2

James Watt, the chief executive of Britain's craft beer firm BrewDog, has admitted being "too intense and demanding" to employees after a public relations disaster in which hundreds of employees signed an open letter accusing him of bullying and misconduct. Page 21 3

UK gas prices hit their highest level in three months as concerns rose about supply cuts from Russia and planned strikes in Norway offshore workers that threaten an already tight energy market. Page 33 4

Germany, Europe's largest economy, recorded its first monthly trade deficit since 1991 as companies were hit by the rising costs of imports, including energy, and weaker demand for their products. Page 33 5

A furore about the use of pronouns on staff badges at Halifax has failed to result in a customer exodus, The Times has learnt. Halifax said on social media that customers who disagreed with its policy of allowing employees to display their chosen pronouns were free to close their accounts. Page 33 6

AO World has attempted to reassure the stock market about its financial health after investors were spooked by the decision by Atradius, a credit insurer, to cut cover for some suppliers of the online white goods retailer.

Spirax-Sarco Engineering, a 7

FTSE 100 engineering company. has entered into exclusive talks over a £25.5 million acquisition of Vulcanis Group, a European industrial electric heating group.

8

Schools are failing students by not adequately equipping them with the skills they need for work, according to a group of 250 entrepreneurs. A joint letter from business leaders shown to The Times said: "Too many young people are leaving school without the entrepreneurial skills necessary to succeed." Page 36 9

A £5 billion bid for UK Power Networks, Britain's biggest electricity distribution company, by a group led by New York-based KKR and including Macquarie Group of Australia, has collapsed after the owner, a division of Li Ka-shing's business empire, raised the sale price at the last minute. Page 38 10

Rolls-Royce, the engineering company, has shortlisted six sites for a factory to build its proposed small nuclear reactors. The sites are Richmond in North Yorkshire, Sunderland, Deeside in Wales, Ferrybridge in West Yorkshire, Stallingborough in Lincolnshire, and Carlisle. Page 39

headline: Hit show throws pottery into limelight
author: Dominic Walsh; Ben Ellery
text:
A ceramics brand founded in a Derbyshire village is on sale for £0m, write Dominic Walsh and Ben Ellery

When Joseph Jager founded a pottery in the Derbyshire village of Denby in 1809, he can have had little idea that one day his ceramics would be highly prized in Asia.

More than 200 years later, however, his company has appeared in a blockbuster South Korean series and has gone on sale, with a valuation in the region of £0 million, driven by its status across the region.

The pottery company, named after the village where it was founded, still occupies its original factory. Its owner is seeking a buyer that will continue its success, driven by demand from countries such as South Korea, as well as the United States and China. Sales outside the UK account for more than 50 per cent of its production.

The brand is so desirable abroad that it has expanded its range of bowls to attract the Korean market. Items include nesting pots, straight-sided rice and deep ramen noodle bowls, small round dishes and soju cups in which to serve Korean alcohol.

The company was so popular that its Halo tea/coffee cup featured in an episode of Squid Game, the surprise lockdown hit last year. More than 110 million people revelled in its highly stylised scenes of violence.

The drama revolved around 456 people, suffering financial hardship and risking their lives to play a series of deadly games, lured by the opportunity to become wealthy.

Denby has an Instagram page devoted to its South Korean fans, where 19,000 followers can view photographs of the homeware in the midst of native dishes.

Sebastian Lazell, 58, a former Unilever boss who is now the company's chief executive, once said: "We resisted the temptation to offshore [our production] and now our 'Made in England' label is a key part of our appeal overseas."

Denby has opened a store in Gangnam, the district in Seoul made famous by the 2012 pop song Gangnam Style that referred to an affluent way of life.

Lazell said: "There are 55 million people in South Korea and they love the premium casual look of our glazes because formal ornate wear is out of fashion."

The company has tweaked its range to suit international demand. "They lay the table very differently in Korea, so almost 40 per cent of our production over there is bowls," Lazell said.

He said that in India, where many affluent customers have housekeepers, Denby's products were known as "maid-proof".

It has not all been plain sailing for the company, however. In 2009 it was rescued from administration by

Hilco Capital, a British investment firm and turnaround specialist that has helped companies including Homebase, Habitat and HMV.

After a £0 million buyout, the business now has a turnover of about £0 million and underlying earnings of £.5 million.

Denby, which is famed for its coloured glaze, makes all its stoneware from clay sourced within a 20-mile radius of the village.

The factory was founded after a seam of clay was uncovered at the site three years earlier during the construction of a road between Derby and Alfreton, 15 miles to the north.

The workshop, which produces more than six million pieces of pottery a year, was featured in Gregg Wallace's Inside the Factory on BBC Two. The site has undergone significant investment in order to expand from stoneware to porcelain.

Vikki Irvine, senior brand manager at Denby, said: "People in Korea love Denby because the quality is great. Our collections are popular with younger generations in Korea, who like table-setting with beautifully crafted, British-made ceramics.

"It's also a move away from the traditional tableware their parents owned, as we see a shift in the Korean culture for the younger generation to have their own identity. We find that clean colours and pastel-toned ceramics perform well in Korea."

Shopping for success Certain British brands have enjoyed popular success in South Korea, including cosmetics, beers and supermarkets.

Boots is considered one of the most notable to have entered the Korean market recently, according to the British Chambers of Commerce. It signed a franchise deal with the Emart hypermarket chain in July 2016 and months later the first Drugstore Boots opened in Starfield Mall in Gangnamt, Seoul. Both Emart and Boots were said to be eyeing the country's booming cosmetics market.

Brewdog, Scotland's largest independent brewer, also has managed to penetrate the South Korean market after arriving in December 2013.

Waitrose sells 160 products from its Waitrose Seriously, Love Life and Essential ranges at a lavish international food market in the Myeongdong shopping district in Seoul, having signed a deal with Shinsegae Group in 2012 with the aim of breaking into the country's ready-meal market. The partnership was regarded as a good fit for Waitrose.

headline: My guide to light, low-alcohol session beers
author: Jane MacQuitty
text:
Summer's the time to reach for a session beer. They are brewed specifically to be refreshing thirst-quenchers and not so alcoholic as to knock you out. It's irritating that there is no strict definition, but the idea was for brewers to create a sessionable swig that you could have several of in one session (hence the name) and not feel worse for wear. Unlike the majority of beers that weigh in at 5 per cent-plus alcohol, session beers can go as low as 2.5 per cent, though most hover around 4 per cent. A few of the session pale ales and India pale ales (IPAs) creep above 5 per cent, but they have been brewed to be full of fresh, fruity, floral, hoppy summer flavours, without the bitterness of most ales, and that makes them a happy exception.

The craft beer movement started in the US, but nobody could accuse British brewers of failing to embrace the trend. So, time to chill out with the host of New England session-inspired IPAs, handcrafted beers from different breweries that for once are available on supermarket shelves. It's just the likes of

The Kernel Brewery's Table Beer, brewed fresh every week with an everchanging range of hops, that you'll have to hunt for ? and I promise it's worth it.

Newcomers to the style might dismiss this summer's cloudy, unfiltered beers as out of condition, but persevere: pale gold, lower-alcohol session beers, with zingy, refreshing flavours that explode in your mouth, are just what parched palates need. Hazy Jane is a guava-scented version (see best buy), while Brewdog v Northern Monk The Vermont Sessions New England IPA is a 5.2 per cent, frothy, intensely hoppy, stone-fruited charmer (440ml can, Morrisons, £). If you prefer less alcohol, try Beavertown's Nanobot Super Session IPA, 2.8 per cent (Waitrose, £.70), with delicate yet delicious barley, wheat and oat charm, plus a dab of citrus and mango. Brewdog's Lightspeed Hazy IPA, 4 per cent, is another exotic twist on the theme with peachy, apricot kernel-charged pizzazz, and exclusive to Tesco (4 x 330ml cans £.50). Aldi's beer buyer has risen to the challenge too and the Sharp Tongue Grapefruit Pale Ale (4 per cent, 440ml can, 69p) is, as the name suggests, an invigorating, sweet-sour grapefruit gem.

This week's best supermarket buys

The Kernel Brewery Table Beer, 3 per cent, 50cl bottle noblegreen wines.co.uk, £ An old pale ale favourite from a famous brewery with tangy pineapple and citrus-hopped flavour.

The Original Small Beer Session Pale, 2.5 per cent, 350ml bottle Waitrose, £.20 With its rich malty scent and equally flavoursome malty palate, few will notice it's low in alcohol.

Beavertown Neck Oil Session IPA, 4.3 per cent, 330ml can Sainsbury's, £ Wacky name and label but it's one of summer's best session beers, a richly fruited, floral gem.

Brewdog Hazy Jane Guava, 5 per cent, 330ml can Morrisons, £.35 A tropical, exotic, yeasty twist on Brewdog's uber-popular New England IPA-style beer.

2018 Finest The Trilogy Malbec, Argentina, 14 per cent Tesco, £3 The Catena family turn high-altitude malbec, aged in old oak, into a magical, punchy, curranty mouthful.

2020 Louis Jadot Bourgogne Pinot Noir, France, 13 per cent Morrisons, £5 A delicious, plump, fleshy, gamey pinot noir from Louis Jadot, one of the great Beaune merchant houses.

Laurent-Perrier La Cuvé Brut Champagne, France, 12 per cent Sainsbury's, £2, down from £2 Go on, get £0 off a top chardonnay-led bubbly with racy citrus blossom and lemon-twist fruit.

2021 La Vieille Ferme Blanc, France, 12.5 per cent Waitrose, £.50, down from £.50 The Perrins probably sell more roséhan their zingy, stone-fruited white but it's the better wine.

Session beers are brewed to be full of fresh, fruity summer flavours, and lower in alcohol

headline: BREW'S QUEUES
author: STUART PATTERSON
text:
Problems Dog beer bash

A BOOZE-UP run by beer giant BrewDog turned to chaos as fans queued for hours for food and drink.

Around 10,000 punters flocked to the Annual General Mayhem event in Aberdeen.

Many complained of waiting 90 minutes in the heat for a beer. And one claimed they queued for four hours for a pizza. A raging customer said: "They're a brewery and they can't arrange a p*ss-up. It was an absolute shambles."

Shareholders paid £0 for vouchers in exchange for a digital code to buy ale. But a plan to only accept them and plastic backfired when card machines failed, forcing stalls to shut.

BrewDog apologised after Saturday's Hazelhead Park bash adding: "There are big lessons to be learned."

headline: Brewers fear that a recession will burst the craft beer bubble
author: Constance Kampfner
text:
The thirst for artisanal drinks may not be sustainable as the cost of living soars, writes Constance Kampfner

Another 200 craft breweries opened for business last year, raising fears that the booming sector could go bust if drinkers trade down to cheaper alternatives when the soaring cost of living hits them in the pocket.

There are currently more than 2,400 breweries operating in Britain according to figures from HM Revenue & Customs, as entrepreneurs continue in their quest to create the next BrewDog or Beavertown, two companies that now register tens of millions of pounds a year in sales.

However, as rising inflation and sky-high energy costs force people to reduce their outgoings, many in the industry are questioning whether demand for pricey artisanal beer can be sustained. Wiper and True, a craft brewery based in Bristol, said that the cost of living crisis was "very understandably" hitting sales.

"Craft beer is a luxury item for most people, and with the concerns we're all facing around winter bills, it's only natural that people are thinking twice about buying a £-plus can," a spokeswoman said.

She added, however, that while the company had noticed that sales of beer to drink at home had slowed, Wiper and True's two taprooms had remained "consistently busy".

"People remain keen to enjoy inperson experiences, particularly after Covid-19,"

she said. The brewery, founded in 2012, opened its second taproom only last month which has so far been exceeding sales forecasts.

Not all brewers have been so lucky. When Kelham Island in Sheffield announced in May that it would cease trading after 32 years, it became the fourth brewery to close in a month.

The Society of Independent Brewers (Siba) estimates that between 40 and 60 breweries have closed so far this year, while a further 160 were lost dur- ing the pandemic. It has always been difficult for small independent brands to compete with giants such as Budweiser or Carling, whose beers often sell at half the price. Now with the combination of supply chain problems, rising staffing costs, and consumers tightening their belts and choosing cheaper products, the problem is only being magnified.

Some craft beer brands have gained significant market share after being acquired by brewing majors, such as Camden Town Brewery, which was bought by AB InBev in 2015, or Brixton Brewery, which was taken over by Heineken last year, allowing them to benefit from large distribution networks. This has in turn made it more difficult for smaller firms to gain a foothold in the market.

But even breweries backed by bigger players are being squeezed out. London Fields, owned by Carlsberg, closed last year, while Lion, the Australian brewing giant, announced last week that it was selling both Fourpure and Magic Rock breweries only a few years after buying them.

"People have been talking about the market being oversaturated for many years but now especially it has the feel of a market that can't sustain everyone that's in it," Tom Stainer, chief executive of the consumer organisation Campaign for Real Ale, said. "There's increased competition for a dwindling consumer base." He warned that while new breweries "are relatively easy to set up, that doesn't mean it's easy to make a living out of them".

"Many breweries are on a knife-edge when it comes to margins," Stainer said.

There are signs that entrepreneurs may be becoming more hesitant about making their own beer.

While the total number of start-up breweries has continued to rise over recent years, growth is slowing. The 2021-22 financial year was the third consecutive year of slowing growth, with the number of breweries rising by 9 per cent, having ballooned by nearly 18 per cent in 2018-19.

Business owners are desperate, Siba says, for certainty. In last year's budget, Rishi Sunak announced a 5 per cent reduction in the duty paid on draught beer in containers of 40 litres or more. The group fears the policy could be dropped amid the current political turmoil and is campaigning for the next Tory leader to put it into action by February next year.

It is also calling on the scheme to be extended to include containers under 40 litres, amid concern that independent brewers, which normally use 20 or 30-litre kegs, are set to miss out on the relief.

headline: Fancy an evening out? Take the tube
author: Greig Cameron
text:
BrewDog, the controversial Scottish brewery, has unveiled what it claims is the largest pub in the UK, complete with a podcast studio, florist, bowling alley and metal slide (writes Greig Cameron).

Over two floors and 26,000 sq ft at Waterloo station in London, the venue will also have an on-site brewery, cocktail bar, coffee shop, dining rooms and work spaces alongside a retro ice cream truck selling gelato-filled doughnuts.

James Watt, the chief executive of BrewDog, said the bar would have a capacity of 1,775 people and described it as "the most exciting project" he had ever worked on and "by far the biggest location we have ever opened".

The Royal Victoria, in Ramsgate, Kent, was thought to be the largest pub in the UK, with about 11,000 sq ft.

BrewDog was founded in 2007 by James Watt and Martin Dickie and makes beers including Punk IPA, Elvis Juice and Hazy Jane. It has its headquarters and main brewery in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, as well as other production sites in Columbus, Ohio, in the US, Brisbane, Australia, and Berlin, Germany.

It has more than 2,300 staff and operates more than 100 bars around the world as well as some boutique hotels.

The company's reputation was called into question last year when a group of former employees published an open letter describing a toxic culture.

Watt apologised and instigated a review, which resulted in Allan Leighton, the former Asda boss, being brought in as chairman.

Court actions have been launched by Watt, who claims he has been the victim of an orchestrated campaign to damage him and the company.

Complaints have also been lodged with Ofcom, the broadcast regulator, over a BBC Scotland documentary which looked into the culture at BrewDog.

In May, Watt announced he would give 5 per cent of his own equity in the business to staff. That portion of his stake is valued at about £00 million. The company also announced a profit-sharing scheme for its bar workers.

In 2021, BrewDog made a pre-tax loss of £.4 million on revenue of £86 million.

headline: BRUISEDOG!
author: STUART PATTERSON
text:
25MPH BIKE SMASH DRAMA

Beer boss left bloodied after snapping collarbone twice in 'fun' ride mishap

BREWDOG boss James Watt smashed his collarbone after a "fun" bike ride went badly wrong.

The chief exec, 35, suffered a nasty fall while touring a forest the beer giant now owns.

He had assured his shareholders, known as 'equity punks', their cycle round woods near

Aviemore, Inverness-shire, would not be "intense or challenging".

But some were forced to quit because of the tough terrain and an ambulance was called to treat James, 37, after his scary 25mph tumble.

He said yesterday: "I double broke my collarbone while mountain biking with 50 equity punks at the Lost Forest. It was definitely more challenging than we'd thought."

James posted X-rays of his shattered bone plus pics of him covered in blood and in hospital with his arm in a sling.

He'd been helping to plant the first of more than a million trees at the former Kinrara estate.

Brewdog, based in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, paid £.8million for the site last year. James founded the company in 2007 with Martin Dickie, 39.

stuart.patterson@the-sun.co.uk

headline: The revolution turns ugly for beauty brand
author: Russell Hotten
text:
"I think we can build a global beauty business to challenge the big guys," Adam Minto told the media ahead of the flotation of his cosmetics business Revolution Beauty. A year on and an 85 per cent slump in the share price later, things look very different.

The business that Minto and his co-founder Thomas Allsworth hoped would become a "mini-L'Orél" is suspending trading in its shares after failing to finish an audit of its maiden set of annual financial results on time.

"The company aims to complete its audit and publish its annual report within a matter of weeks of this announcement, post which trading in the company's ordinary shares is expected to recommence," Revolution said.

The beauty and skincare retailer had already warned this month that auditors had "raised certain accounting issues with management" that could have a serious impact on profits. These issues concern stock levels, bad debts and the impact on revenues but beyond this there are no more details.

Revolution Beauty sells cosmetics via online platforms and in about 15,000 stores worldwide, including the Debenhams website owned by Boohoo, and Superdrug and Boots. Formed in 2013, its target audience is 16 to 35-yearolds. Its float on the junior Aim stock market in July last year valued the business at about £00 million. The shares, priced at 160p each, touched 170p in the first couple of weeks of trading but have been sliding since. The company is now worth about £4 million.

Minto, 52, and Allsworth, 56, each sold about £5.6 million worth of shares at the flotation. TSG Consumer Partners, the US private equity firm that invested in the business in 2017, sold the bulk of its holding for about £58.3 million. Since then investors have had little to cheer about, including Soros Fund Management, a 6.46 per cent shareholder since the listing. Minto and Allsworth still own 15.77 per cent each.

Although the share price had been drifting lower this year, the decline accelerated from May, despite a bullish trading update. That same month Revolution's chief financial officer resigned, and in June TSG sold what was left of its stake. It was an announcement in early August that seriously spooked investors. A warning about "the unprecedented macroeconomic backdrop" and delay in publishing the final results from August 4 to August 30 led to a two-thirds fall in the share price.

Revolution Beauty's statement tried to throw a comfort blanket around shareholders: "Management is not aware of any material issues that have been raised by the auditors during the audit process to date." That changed in an August 11 statement when the company said the auditors, BDO, "have subsequently raised certain accounting issues with management regarding the audit and these could have a material impact on the results for FY22".

A week later it said the new August 30 date was unlikely to be met, something confirmed yesterday alongside the shares' suspension. An assurance yesterday that it would take a "matter of weeks" after which the shares would restart trading seems to have underpinned the collapsed share price. They closed down 2.29 per cent at 17¼ yesterday. "There's a view that this is as bad as it gets," a retail analyst said. "You could argue that yesterday's statement suggests all parties have got a grip."

Is it possible that some investors feel the share price has hit the bottom? On August 17 it was disclosed that Boohoo had built a 12.8 per cent stake. It paid about 17p a share. "Would they [Boohoo] have done that if they thought Revolution was going to be a horror show?" the analyst asked. That's small comfort, however, to investors who just 13 months ago thought Revolution Beauty was worth 160p a piece.

Analysis

Timing is ever ything, and for TSG Consumer Partners its investment acumen over Revolution Beauty was spot on. The US private equity firm got in at the bottom and out at the top (Russell Hotten writes). Well, sort of.

The company, which has about $20 billion in assets, specialises in consumer businesses, with investments in a coffee chain, household cleaning, yoga studios and Brewdog, the UK's craft beer company that seems permanently tipped for a stock market listing.

TSG backed Revolution in 2017, describing the UK company as a pioneer of the "fast beauty"

industry. In the years before the listing, revenues tripled, said TSG, as the two companies worked to expand Revolution's presence in the US.

At the listing in 2021 TSG cashed out, selling the majority of its shares for 160p, and making more than £58 million. It was a good move. George Soros's fund and Jupiter Asset

Management, which bought at the listing, are among those to lose out.

Now it's Boohoo's turn to see if it can make some money. The online retailer built a 12.8 per cent stake in Revolution Beauty in August. Boohoo bought after Revolution first hinted it might delay its results, but before yesterday's suspension.

The investment, said an analyst, is the first Boohoo has made directly in a quoted company. "Normally they like to pick up assets after companies have folded Debenhams website, for instance," he said.

Boohoo already works closely with Revolution Beauty, said another source: "They know each other very well. I reckon it's a case that the [Revolution] price has got so low they just thought it worth an investment."

He added: "Revolution still generates revenues. Despite the immediate problems, it operates in a sector that is growing fast." And what about a full bid? "I just can't see it. It's just not the sort of thing [Boohoo] do."

headline: News; Front Page John Lewis to give seasonal staff free food
author: Andrew Ellson
text:
The John Lewis Partnership has said it will give free meals to staff for three months covering the festive period to help with the rising cost of living.

The retail group, which also operates Waitrose supermarkets, yesterday said all its permanent staff and temporary workers would get free food from October 3 to January 6. The news coincides with a drive to hire 10,000 seasonal staff in its shops and supply chain.

The brewer Brewdog announced the closure of six of its bars. It said: "With rapidly increasing costs, including spiralling energy bills, these bars were a substantial distance from being viable to operate." All staff will move to other Brewdog locations.

A restaurant in the Scottish Borders and a pub in Dorset became among the first to shut their doors because of unaffordable energy prices. David Haetzman, 49, owner of the Firebrick Brasserie in Lauder, near Edinburgh, said that he was "distraught" to close, adding that the restaurant's energy bills had shot up from £,000 a month to nearly £,000. In Dorset, the Fontmell pub and bed and breakfast announced that it was closing "with immediate effect" because of soaring costs. A message posted on its website said that it stopped trading after its annual energy bills increased by £8,000.

A food bank also revealed that users were requesting "non-cook food" because energy prices mean that they "can't afford to put the oven on". Reporting a 70 per cent rise in users, Ian Oulton, a trustee of West Cheshire Foodbank, said: "More and more [people] are turning down fresh veg because they can't afford to cook it. What happens when it gets colder?" Energy bills crisis, pages 6-7

headline: Shutting time for new pub worker
author: STUART PATTERSON
text:
BEER firm Brewdog gave a new worker a start at one of their pubs just days before they shut it.

Staff at their bar in Peterhead which opened in March 2019 were told week it is closing because it's not making enough money.

A shareholder at the brewers said: "What a disaster. My heart really goes out to the new start.

"The pub was small but well liked by locals. There's a fair bit of anger at it being axed."

Brewdog's bars MD James Brown said all staff, including the new employee, have been offered jobs at another venue, adding that the company is "fulfilling its contractual obligations".

The craft ale giants revealed they are also closing their Hop and Anchor bar in Aberdeen, Hop Hub in Motherwell and three in London.

headline: TEA POTTY
author: ASHLEY ARMSTRONG
text:
FTSE 100

busin ss ashley.armstrong@the-sun.co.uk @AArmstrong_says

£ €.16 $1.15 OIL $92.67 Brent Crude barrel Petrol (PPL): 170p Diesel (PPL): 183p

DOWN 135.65 TO

7,148.50

£5,000 power hike is threat to historic parlour

ENERGY CRISIS 1

THE energy crisis may force a 125-year-old tea room to shut for the winter making it the latest small business to fall victim to the emergency.

Owner Charles Bunker says his supplier DRAX is charging an extra £5,000 for electricity next year taking the bill from £3,000 to £08,000.

The standing charge alone of the ORCHARD TEA

GARDEN in Grantchester, Cambs which must be paid even if everything is switched off will jump from £.25 a month to £08. The historic tea room, immortalised by poet Rupert Brooke, has been visited by King George VI and Prince Charles.

Mr Bunker said: "It's complete and utter unsustainable madness. The tea garden's survived two world wars but will be killed by the incompetence of the Treasury and the Bank of England. I can't afford this bill."

Mr Bunker told The Sun: "It's a scandal. The hospitality industry, which has been a golden goose for the Government, is now deep inside the abattoir and the knife is poised."

Small firms are facing rocketing bills as fixed energy contracts come to an end. And businesses are not cushioned from a price cap like households. More than half of small firms, which make up 99 per cent of UK businesses, reckon bills will force them to shut down.

Mr Bunker's woes come as fresh findings from CORNWALL ANALYSTS reckon that while "the hospitality sector is not as energy intensive as transport and manufacturing, it is still above the average and also one of the hardest hit areas of the economy following the pandemic with many closures".

Official figures yesterday showed that small firms are already suffering a 10 per cent fall in sales last month the biggest monthly fall since April 2020.

It means that any post-lockdown recovery has started to fade, just as they face the huge bill increase.

Scots beer giant BREWDOG confirmed it had shut six bars across the UK due to "spiralling energy bills".

Among the closures were the Hop Hub Motherwell as well as bars in

Peterhead and Aberdeen this week.

And a restaurant boss in Lauder, Berwickshire, was left devastated after he had to shut because of rising costs.

David Haetzman, 49, was forced to shut the family-run FIREBRICK BRASSERIE. It came after the restaurant's power bills quadrupled to £,000. He said: "We're distraught."

An arcade in Skegness, Lincs, OASIS AMUSEMENT, is facing a £50,000 increase in energy bills to £00,000.

The level of UK manufacturing output is also back to pandemic lows. And input costs are 22 per cent higher, according to PMI data.

headline: Last orders for pubs? 2 in 3 'may shut without bills help'
author: Sean Poulter
text:
UP TO two thirds of pubs may shut this winter unless help is urgently given to tackle rising energy costs, a business chief warned yesterday.

Small businesses - unlike householders - have no cap on energy bills and many are being hit with huge increases, in some cases eight times higher.

This is affecting firms across the high street, while schools, care homes, hospitals and other vital public services also face being crippled by vast bills.

Ruby McGregor-Smith, head of the British Chambers of Commerce (BCC), warned: 'At the moment we're talking about up to two thirds of pubs may have to shut their doors.

'We cannot be in a situation where more and more businesses are shutting down because of costs that are absolutely outside their control. They cannot get support in any other way, it's not as if they can borrow the money.' Baroness McGregor-Smith continued: 'They've come out of Covid. They've already got loans. They've already got higher costs from national insurance. There still isn't a reduction on VAT.

'They absolutely have got to deal with the rising cost of inflation as well... This is too much, they have to have support now.'

TV chef and the holder of two Michelin stars Tom Kerridge shone a light on the crisis in the hospitality trade, saying energy bills at one of his pubs have gone from £0,000 a year to £20,000.

He said many people are considering 'mothballing' their businesses through the winter because they cannot afford to remain open.

'The numbers are so ridiculous and ludicrous that no wonder so many businesses are closing and talking of closing,' he said.

The BCC has forecast that the UK will be in recession before the end of the year. Baroness McGregor-Smith, a Conservative peer, told BBC Radio 4's Today programme that measures suggested by Chancellor Nadhim Zahawi, including reductions in VAT and business rates for retail and hospitality, is the 'right territory' to help people this winter.

But she said: 'I think unless there is immediate support, we will see many businesses close their doors this winter.'

Her warning came as craft brewing company Brewdog said it was closing six outlets - in London, Motherwell near Glasgow, Aberdeen, and Peterhead in Aberdeenshire - amid soaring energy bills that mean they are no longer financially viable.

headline: Brewdog  bike boss in oxygen fall 'cure'
author: STUART PATTERSON
text:
BREWDOG boss James Watt is using an oxygen chamber to recover from a mountain bike accident.

The co-founder of the beer giant shattered his collarbone in a fall during a ride with fans.

He has now told of his recovery plan after an operation to have a sixinch titanium steel plate inserted by surgeons.

Watt, 37, said: "I'm spending two hours per day in a hyperbaric oxygen chamber.

"I'm going to use an infrared sauna daily and my ice bath to try and help speed up the healing process."

Watt said being laid up may help him complete a delayed project.

He added: "It might just give me the chance to finish my new book."

We told last month how he came a cropper while giving shareholders a tour of the forest the firm has bought near Aviemore, Inverness-shire.

headline: Sorrell raises spirits at S4 despite ballooning losses
author: John-Paul Ford Rojas
text:
SIR Martin Sorrell cheered investors in his beleaguered digital advertising firm S4 Capital as he said prospects were 'relatively bright' despite war in Europe and surging inflation.

Shares rose 12.5pc, or 17.9p, to 161.4p even as it reported operating losses ballooning more than fourfold to £5.4m for the first half of the year.

The figures showed revenues continuing to grow, up 59.8pc to £46.4m, and that two 'whopper' clients had been signed up.

Sorrell founded S4 Capital in 2018 after three decades leading adland giant WPP. It now employs more than 9,000 people. It was forced to delay its annual results this year, in a move that was described by the tycoon as 'unacceptable and embarrassing', and warned of lower profits in July as hiring costs surged.

Its woes have left shares 75pc lower for the year to date.

But it has now put the brakes on its hiring spree, and signalled a shift in focus away from buying up smaller companies to maximising value from its existing business.

Client wins in the first half included Adobe, Brewdog, Tiktok, Diageo and online firm Booking.com as well as the US account of an unnamed large consumer goods business.

Sorrell sounded an optimistic note. He acknowledged 'many significant challenges in areas such as climate change, a lengthy war on continental Europe, rising inflation and interest rates, energy shortages, fractious US/China and Western/ Russia relationships and with Iran'.

But he said despite this 'the prospects for digital advertising and transformation remain relatively bright, whilst traditional media languish'.

'There is evidence that demand accelerates during periods of economic uncertainty as we saw with Covid in 2020, when we performed strongly,' Sorrell added.

He admitted the first-half profit performance was 'disappointing' as cost growth ran ahead of sales but said S4 was acting to correct it.

The 'whopper' clients - those with annual revenues of more than $20m (£7.6m) - now total eight, while five more were 'trending' towards that status as it targets a total of 20.

headline: A year of turmoil for beer tycoon
author: DOUGLAS WALKER
text:
JAMES Watt founded BrewDog with business partner Martin Dickie in 2007 and their first bar in Aberdeen launched two years later.

In June last year, over 100 employees signed an open letter claiming their mental health had suffered due to a "culture of fear" and a "toxic attitude" at the company, based in nearby Ellon. In the same month it emerged Mr Watt was selling off his assets connected to BrewDog amid conflict of interest fears.

In January, BBC's Disclosure interviewed former workers who said they found it difficult working for the company.

Afterwards, Mr Watt called the programme an "attack" on his business and threatened legal action.

In February, he denied claims he stared at staff and said he could've been "lost in thought" while observing bar service.

Then, in March, he reported the BBC to Ofcom, saying the documentary about his company was a "hatchet job" of "hurtful lies."

It also emerged that Mr Watt hired private investigators and he revealed it was to save his £.6billion business from "trolls".

In July, he admitted he'd been "too intense" running the firm and pushed folk too far. Last month, he broke his collarbone in a mountain bike fall.

headline: HARASSED DEFRAUDED & DEFAMED
author: DOUGLAS WALKER
text:
AFTER 'VICIOUS' TROLL ABUSE

£00K SCAM

lBrewery chief's web lies torment

lGrasping ex vowed to destroy him

BEER tycoon James Watt has told of his torment after he was "harassed, defrauded and defamed" during his ex-girlfriend's bitter smear campaign.

The BrewDog co-founder, 40, was conned out of £00,000 by twisted Emili Ziem, 29, in a scam that threatened to ruin his reputation and his business.

Ziem shamelessly pretended to help James nail the trolls behind a vendetta which accused him of harassing women and criminality.

But in reality she was responsible for spreading the vicious lies herself from a false social media account.

Last night after the Court of Session in Edinburgh ordered Ziem to repay James plus cough up £00,000 in damages he said: "I have been the subject of a vicious and relentless campaign of abuse, primarily by troll accounts using Instagram to post and share appalling lies about me often direct to family and friends. Lies that were then amplified all over the internet.

"It has taken a significant personal toll and been a massive distraction. I needed to do all I could to bring the campaign to an end.

"At the outset I want to say I didn't go to court lightly.

"But I have been harassed, defrauded and defamed, and it has deeply affected me, my family and my business. I had no choice."

Plotting Ziem first had contact with James in 2020 on Instagram.

The couple met up several times while messaging regularly on Whats- App. But in May 2021, many of James's friends on the site were sent slurs about him from an account in the name of Laura Keller.

The direct messages said he'd "misled and harassed" women.

He was accused of "abusing his position" with them. Some claims even alleged criminality, which he strongly denied.

James said he ignored the false accusations in the hope they'd stop.

But later that month, Ziem got in touch to say she'd met one of his exes at a party, who had claimed a group of women were conspiring to "bring him down".

As the messages continued, Ziem vowed to find out who was behind the bid to blacken his name.

She said she would befriend people she believed could be involved with a fee of £5,000 to hand over their name. She eventually claimed to have identified four culprits.

James said: "Ms Ziem said she could help identify the people responsible and asked for payment to do so. I was desperate to stop this horrific abuse, so I paid up."

But after parting with four lots of £5,000 in Bitcoin, James grew suspicious and drafted in a private detective, who exposed Ziem as being behind the Keller account.

The tycoon said: "The court found she was one of the perpetrators and operated an extremely active troll account as part of a network looking to 'take me down' her words.

"She gave me knowingly false information with the sole intention of deceiving me into agreeing to pay her.

"She was, in fact, a key part of a network involved in a campaign to do as much damage to me and my business as possible." PI Michael

Roberts, who specialises in digital forensic work, was paid £36,000 to help nail Ziem by sending the Laura Keller account a DM including a "TripWire" link to identify where the message was opened.

It led to Edinburgh Science Triangle, close to where she then worked.

A second message was later sent from South Queensferry, West Lothian, where Ziem stayed at the time. It was established that the broadband was registered under her estranged husband's name. Crucially he no longer lived there but she still did. James's claim states: "The TripWire links could only be accessed by someone with the log in details to the LK account.

"That having been done from the defender's home and separately from an unidentified location close to her then place of work is susceptible to no other reasonable explanation."

Documents add that James has suffered, "considerable distress and anxiety". Earlier this month, Lord Brailsford ruled Ziem, who now lives in Norway, conducted the scam.

James who spent £05,000 on other forensic expenses and £72,000 on court costs vowed to give the £00,000 he paid Ziem to charity after she was ordered to return it.

The fraudster previously contributed to BBC Scotland documentary The

Truth About BrewDog, which claimed James abused his power at the firm he set up in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, in 2007 with pal Martin Dickie, 39.

The BBC wiped some claims from the programme, which is still online. James later admitted pushing staff "too far". Ziem wouldn't comment.

douglas.walker@the-sun.co.uk

HOW PLOT UNFOLDED lMr Watt and Ziem first meet on social media in 2020. lPosing as 'Laura Keller', she starts making false claims about him online from May 2021.

lZiem later says she's trying to befriend trolls abusing Mr Watt to catch them out but wants £5k per case. lMr Watt hires a cyber detective after becoming suspicious. lJudge rules Ziem orchestrated the smear campaign and is ordered to pay £00,000 damages.

She said she could help identify people spreading lies about me JAMES WATT

headline: News; Front Page BREWDOG  BOSS CONNED IN £00K SMEAR PLOT
author: DOUGLAS WALKER
text:
WATT TARGETED BY EX-LOVER

Brazen scammer spread web lies to get cash

BREWDOG boss James Watt was scammed out of £00,000 by his ex in a vicious smear campaign.

Emili Ziem, 29, hid behind a fake Instagram account to spread lies about the Scots beer tycoon, 40.

She then conned him out of four £5,000 payments to unmask his 'trolls'. James said last night: "I've suffered considerable distress."

Full Story Pages Four and Five

headline: BrewDog  boss scammed out of £00,000 by ex-girlfriend
author: Poppy Koronka
text:
The chief executive of BrewDog was scammed out of £00,000 by a former girlfriend who masterminded a vicious online smear campaign to ruin his reputation, a judge has ruled.

James Watt, a co-founder of the brewery, had been targeted with defamatory attacks by online trolls and agreed to pay Emili Ziem £5,000 to unmask the people harassing him.

Investigations later revealed that one of the people spreading lies and misinformation was Ziem herself, with whom Watt had a brief relationship.

Watt, 40, told the Court of Session in Edinburgh that he "suffered considerable distress and anxiety" as a result of the fraud.

"For the past two years I have been the subject of a vicious and relentless campaign of abuse, primarily by troll accounts using Instagram to post and share appalling lies about me, often direct to family and friends lies that were then amplified all over the internet," he said.

Ziem, 29, had set up fake social media pages that would spread lies about Watt, the court ruled.

Ziem and Watt first made contact on Instagram in 2020 and met several times in person. According to The Scottish Sun, the pair had a relationship.

In May 2021, several of Watt's friends received messages from an account under the name of Laura Keller, which claimed that Watt had "misled" women. Some messages accused Watt of criminality, which he denied.

Ziem called Watt that month to tell him that she had bumped into one of his former girlfriends at a party, who said that a group of women were planning to "bring down" Watt with the social media pages.

Ziem told Watt she was trying to find out the culprit by befriending her current suspect. She offered to discover who was behind the pages for Watt for the sum of £5,000 for each page.

"I was desperate to stop this horrific abuse, so I paid up," Watt said. After paying Ziem four times, Watt became suspicious and employed a private detective, Michael Roberts, to find the owner of the accounts.

Roberts was able to show that Ziem was behind the scam through so-called tripwire links, which proved the messages were sent from her workplace and home in Edinburgh. The ruling also stated that Ziem should pay Watts the £00,000 expenses he incurred while trying to uncover the fraud.

Lord Brailsford issued a judgment this month ruling in Watt's favour.

It said: "The contract or other arrangement by which the pursuer agreed to transfer to the defender the bitcoin assets was induced by fraudulent misrepresentation made by the defender."

Watt has asked for Ziem to be banned from transferring the bitcoin sums to third parties in an attempt to "defeat his claim".

Watt said Ziem was "a key part of a network involved in a campaign to do as much damage to me and my business as possible, spreading false and defamatory information with the objective of destroying me personally and hurting our business".

Ziem did not comment on the court case.

headline: BrewDog  chief's ex-partner conned him out of £00,000
author: Poppy Koronka
text:
The chief executive of BrewDog was scammed out of £00,000 by a former girlfriend who masterminded an online smear campaign to ruin his reputation, a judge has ruled.

James Watt, co-founder of the brewery, agreed to pay Emili Ziem, 29, £5,000 to unmask the individuals who were harassing him.

Investigations later showed one of those spreading lies and misinformation was Ziem herself, with whom Watt had a brief relationship.

Watt, 40, told the Court of Session in Edinburgh that he "suffered considerable distress and anxiety" as a result.

"For the past two years I have been the subject of a vicious and relentless campaign of abuse, primarily by troll accounts using Instagram to post and share appalling lies about me, often direct to family and friends lies that were then amplified all over the internet," he said. Ziem and Watt first made contact on Instagram in 2020 and met several times in person. The Scottish Sun said the pair had a relationship.

In May 2021, several of Watt's friends received messages from an account under the name of Laura Keller, which claimed that Watt had "misled" women. Some messages accused Watt of criminality, which he denied.

Ziem called Watt and said a group of women were planning to "bring down" Watt with social media pages. She had set up the pages that would spread lies about Watt, the court ruled. She offered to discover who was behind the pages for Watt for payment.

After paying Ziem four times, Watt became suspicious and employed a private detective, Michael Roberts, to find the owner of the accounts. He was able to show that Ziem was behind the scam.

Lord Brailsford issued a judgment this month ruling in Watt's favour.

The ruling stated that Ziem should pay Watt the expenses he incurred while attempting to get to the bottom of the fraud, a total of £00,000.

Ziem did not comment on the case.

headline: BrewDog  scammer has Punked all the cash
author: DOUGLAS WALKER
text:
lCROOK CROOK SPENT £00k IN 5 MONTHS lSPLURGED ON SHOPPING & TRIPS

FRAUDSTER Emili Ziem blew the £00,000 she scammed from BrewDog tycoon James Watt in just five months.

The internet troll, 29, told her husband and pals she'd inherited the fortune in an elderly relative's will.

But we can reveal the mum frittered the huge sum away on shopping and trips away.

We told this week how Ziem hid behind a fake Instagram account to smear the Scots beer boss, 40, then conned him out of four £5,000 payments to unmask his "tormentors".

A source said: "When she got the cash from James she had to come up with an explanation to tell her husband.

"Emili said she'd been left it in her great granny's will. She then ditched her job in Edinburgh and lived off it.

"But when James realised he'd been done she knew he'd come looking for it. She transferred it to a relative who gave her a £,000-a-week 'allowance'.

"It soon ran out after she went shopping mad and took herself away to hotels. The last money she got from James was in July but by January there was nothing left."

She then conned him out of four £5,000 payments to unmask his "t'trolls'. James said last night: "I'vesufferedconsiderabledistress."'FullStoryPagesFourandFive

BrewDog BOSS CONNED IN £00K SMEAR PLOT

Swindled James and, left, scheming internettrollEmili

Ziem first had contact with James whose firm is famed for its Punk IPA beer in 2020 on Instagram. It's understood she said she'd split from her hubby, who she's now separated from.

Brazenscammerspreadwebliestogetcash

Plot ... our story

The pair began dating but in May 2021, many of James's pals on the site were sent slurs about him from an account in the name of Laura Keller.

The direct messages said he'd "misled and harassed" women.

He was accused of "abusing his position" with them. The vendetta even alleged criminality, which he strongly denied.

Ziem later claimed she'd met one of his exes at a party, who had claimed a group was conspiring to "bring him down".

The crook said she'd befriend women she believed could be involved with a fee of £5,000 to hand over their name. She eventually claimed to have identified four culprits.

But suspicious James drafted in a private detective, who exposed Ziem, who now lives in Norway, as being behind the troll account. This month the Court of Session in Edinburgh ruled she conducted the scam.

Lord Brailsford ordered her to repay James plus £00,000 in expenses. But it's unlikely he'll ever see any of the cash.

James set up BrewDog in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, in 2007 with pal Martin Dickie, 39.

headline: Telly Dom's kind words
text:
JAMES has been inundated with messages of support including from telly prankster Dom Jolly.

The BrewDog boss's online post following our story has also had 10,000 likes from colleagues and fans of his beer.

Trigger Happy star Jolly, 54, right, told him: "Onwards and upwards."

Supporter Phil James wrote: "Keep inspiring others." Michelle Niziol said: "Horrendous. The truth always comes out in the end."

headline: World News: Economic Woes Close U.K. Businesses
author: Trefor Moss
text:
LONDON -- Thousands of businesses across the U.K. are buckling under rampant inflation and flagging consumer demand, stemming from the war in Ukraine.

The conflict, which has boosted prices for energy and many other goods, threatens to send parts of Europe into recession this winter. Scores of factories across the continent have throttled back production because soaring natural-gas prices have made production unprofitable.

Governments have tried to support companies by capping energy bills in some places. Germany and France have nationalized two big utilities weighed down by soaring prices.

In the U.K., a wave of business closures is washing over the country amid the economic pressure. Trapped between surging costs and weakening customer demand, cafes, bakeries, breweries, bookstores and pubs have been closing.

More than a quarter of a million U.K. companies stopped trading in the first half of this year -- 16% more than in the first half of 2021 and 40% more than in the first six months of prepandemic 2019, according to the Office of National Statistics. It was the largest number recorded in a six-month period, the ONS said.

Data on business closures in the July-to-September quarter won't be released until later this month, but many analysts expect the failure rate to have accelerated.

Most of those closures are small businesses. The ONS numbers don't break down the size of companies shutting. But 95% of the U.K.'s 5.6 million companies in 2021 had nine employees or fewer, according to the U.K. Department for Business, Energy and Industrial Strategy.

Numbers are difficult to compare across borders, but bankruptcies -- a subset of business closures generally -- haven't increased notably in much of the rest of the continent, eurozone data show. In France, which has reported numbers similar to those in the U.K., 183,530 businesses closed in the first six months of the year, up roughly a third compared with the first half of 2021, according to data from French commercial courts.

U.K. businesses recently have been put under fresh strain closer to home. A surprisingly large tax-cutting package announced by the government last month roiled markets, further clouding the outlook for executives and business owners.

British consumers have stopped buying many nonessentials, executives say. Anxious customers are delaying big-ticket purchases, such as cars, furniture and costly vacations.

Consumer confidence in the U.K. fell in September to the weakest level since data company GfK started tracking it in 1974. "Consumers are buckling under the pressure of the U.K.'s growing cost-of-living crisis," said Joe Staton, the company's client-strategy director. Consumer confidence in the European Union also hit a low, the European Commission said last week.

Businesses were hurting before the Russia-Ukraine war. The pandemic strained many enterprises, though government financial support was a lifeline for some. After Covid-19 restrictions were lifted, soaring demand and hobbled supply lines started to stoke prices. The war, however, has put that inflationary pressure into overdrive in the U.K.

"Trade is just too uncertain," Glasgow-based bookshop Outwith Books said when announcing its closure last month. In Leeds, brewery Nomadic Beers said it was unable to continue because of the "increasing costs of everything from utilities to ingredients." The Station Masters, a fish-and-chip shop in the northern English town of Bingley, said it was being forced to shut by rising food costs and energy bills.

Other companies are taking significant steps to hunker down. BrewDog PLC said last month that it was shutting six U.K. pubs because of rising costs. "If nothing happens, the U.K. looks set to lose half of its pubs and bars," James Watt, the brewer's chief executive, wrote in a LinkedIn post.

Those still operating face the prospect of even-weaker consumer demand, as the Bank of England raises interest rates to combat inflation. That threatens to cut into household finances by increasing mortgage repayments and credit-card bills.

Having battled through the pandemic, Eve Sleep PLC Chief Executive Cheryl Calverley was planning on a strong recovery in 2022. The bedding company was forecasting sales of its mattresses to rise by a third from the previous year.

"Then Russia invaded Ukraine," said Ms. Calverley, "and everything went off a cliff." Costs, chiefly for components for products such as mattresses and bed frames, were already climbing last year. After the start of the war, energy costs rose, too.

Ms. Calverley said her customers have been facing soaring bills, so they have stopped spending money on other things. Eve Sleep's sales declined 16% in the first half of 2022. The company cut staff from 60 to 30. Cash has dwindled to GBP 1 million, equivalent to $1.1 million, at the end of August, down from GBP 5.2 million at the end of June 2021. She is seeking a buyer or investor.

"The middle class has lost the confidence to spend," Ms. Calverley added.
