headline: VIVA LAGERS VEGAS!
author: IONA BROWNLIE
text:
BREWDOG'S FLASH SIN CITY BAR

LAGER lovers will wish they were beer when BrewDog opens this spectacular rooftop bar in Las Vegas.

The Scots firm launches its glitzy new venue in the heart of Sin City on December 2.

And punters can win a £million tab valid at any BrewDog pub for 20 years.

Stunning artist's images of the Aberdeenshire company's "biggest, boldest and most ambitious" project yet show an outdoor area across from the MGM Park Casino on Paradise Strip. The bar will have 96 taps of beer, including exclusive onsite brews and guest ales.

Along with its traditional lobster, steaks and burgers, it will serve up haggis and deep-fried Mars bars.

BrewDog co-founder James Watt said: "Nothing tops Vegas, but our rooftop brewpub and urban oasis is our most epic location yet.

"Las Vegas is a city where everything is turned up to 11, and now it has the ultimate craft beer experience, one that is carbon negative."

iona.brownlie@the-sun.co.uk

headline: Dry away day goof
text:
A FOOTIE fan stocked up for a six-hour 120-mileround coach trip to watch his team but took alcoholfree booze by mistake.

Sunderland fan Allan Crago, 62, was looking forward to seeing them play Blackburn Rovers and to his BrewDog Punk IPA.

He realised his mistake just after setting off. Gutted Allan said: "I saw the funny side of it."

A Leeds fan quipped online: "Imagine watching Sunderland sober."

And BrewDog chipped in: "In fairness, mate, it only says alcohol-free about a dozen times on the box."

Sunderland lost 2-0.

headline: NOT SUP FOR CUP.
author: JENNIFER JONES
text:
BEER giant BrewDog kicks off over corruption and human rights abuse claims in Qatar by declaring themselves anti-sponsors of the World Cup.

The firm got the boot in by calling the location of the tourney a World F*Cup on giant posters.

Other billboards rebranded the beautiful game as the 'beautiful shame'.

Meanwhile, boss James Watt vowed to donate all net profits from the sale of Lost Lager during the competition to charities working in Qatar.

He said: "To be clear we love football we just don't love corruption and abuse.

"So join us. Let's raise a glass to the players. To the fans. To free speech."

headline: My degrees are from university of long life
author: PETERBOROUGH
text:
RECENTLY, I had a novel experience in the form of a handwritten letter from an old colleague from way back. It was typical catching-up how are you, any news or gossip? so I wrote back (after a trip to the stationers to buy a writing pad) in similar fashion and signed it off David Willey AF AMD. A few days later, I received a further letter from him, telling me that he had spent far too many frustrating hours at his local library trying to discover what the letters stood for, but he just couldn't find any information and would I please explain forthwith. Knowing that I'd never been blessed with high academic prowess, he was curious to find out what qualifications or awards I had somehow managed to acquire over the years.

I decided to come clean and write back with an explanation of the mystery, which read as follows: Dear George, I'm afraid you have been subjected to one of our old family jokes and hope you won't be offended by the wildgoose chase I inadvertently sent you on. The letters after my name are simply the abbreviations of my long-term medical conditions! AF Atrial Fibrillation; AMD Age-Related Macular Degeneration. I've become a man of letters!

David Willey, Nuthall, Notts.

Out Of The Mouths Of Babes

I WAS explaining to my four-year-old granddaughter, Eleanor, that I was making draught excluders. She jumped up and ran to her mum in the kitchen, shouting: 'Mummy, Mummy, Nana is making giraffe excluders to stop the giraffes getting under the door.'

Lesley Brunskill, Saltash, Cornwall.

Sign language

SPACED OUT: Maybe the sign on this bicycle rack is referring to model cars? Seen at a local church by Gary Vaughan of Southport, Merseyside.

One Line Philosophers ? THE Met Office throws caution to the wind with its forecasts.

Warren Brown, Falmouth, Cornwall. ? OPENING a can of worms is sure to delight all the birds! Raj Thein, Isleworth, Middlesex.

Picture that!

BREWDOG, SIR? Further to the dog waiting to be served at the bar (Peterborough), was he hoping to be served by Mason in my local pub?

Melvyn Cousins, Ware, Herts.

The PM's Prayer Our PM in Parliament, Sunak is your name. You'd better up your game If (like Hancock) you want fame.

Or your Cabinet will go And you too will be dough In Westminster, as in your constituencies. Give us today (and next week) our daily bread.

Forgive us our sins, as we forgive those of your MPs. Go to COP27 if you must For in you we trust, To get something out of it just for once.

For we are in your hands And Britain understands You can't please all of the people all of the time. But try to please some, Then the next election will be won For ever and ever. Amen.

Mrs M. Tovey, Swansea.

Limerick The climate change conference begins In Egypt to miss it's a sin. Not wishing to roam, Rishi tried to stay home, But from COP out was forced to COP in.

Paul A. Freeman, Abu Dhabi.

TECHNOLOGY I'm fed up with technology; Yes, right to my back teeth! Just shove it in its coffin and Stick on the top a wreath. Make sure it knows who killed it.

For a hero I would be For all the old age pensioners Who tear their hair, like me. Except, of course, the bald ones.

I wonder what they'd do When all your work's deleted, And you know it wasn't you. What's wrong with good old paper?

Do you shout out, 'Save the trees!'

I'm sure that a few hundred Would lay down their lives to please.

Well, maybe not a good idea.

But I reuse my sheets And print upon the other side, Or vent my ire in tweets. You wonder why this grand tirade? Well, I have just been hacked! They took away important things I had, alas, not backed. And lost me all of one weekend. And time is precious now, I could have done without it, But I sorted it somehow. I know the world's a better place When everything goes right.

But when you lose the blooming thing, It's like an uphill fight.

Ruth Twyman Lockyer, Yarmouth, Isle of Wight.

¦END your contributions to Peterborough, Daily Mail Letters, 2 Derry Street, London W8 5TT

headline: Wetherspoons axes more pubs as cost of living takes its toll
author: Russell Hotten
text:
JD Wetherspoon is planning to offload more pubs, amid slowing sales and "substantially higher" costs for staff, food and repairs.

The company said it was disposing of 39 outlets, adding seven to the list this week, on top of five that it had sold recently.

Most of the closures are near existing Wetherspoon pubs, but it comes as inflation and a tightening of consumer spending pile more pressure on the hospitality sector.

In a trading update yesterday, the 847-strong pubs chain said business had been "broadly" in line with expectations, but it added that October had been a slower month. Interest costs on borrowing were likely to be £0 million higher in the present financial year, it said.

Last month Tim Martin, 67, chief executive, said the hospitality industry was still struggling to get customers back into bars after they had grown used to cheap supermarket beer during pandemic lockdowns.

Other "threats" to the company were soaring inflation, rising wages and the cost of living squeeze. "Those caveats aside, in the absence of further lockdowns or restrictions, the company remains cautiously optimistic about future prospects," Martin said. "Sales have improved since the ending of restrictions in the early part of this calendar year and are considerably above the same period in the last financial year."

The group recorded a 1.1 per cent drop in like-for-like sales in the five weeks to November 6 compared with pre-pandemic trading in 2019, having risen by 1.5 per cent in the previous nine weeks. Set against a year ago, sales rose by 10.1 per cent in the first nine weeks of its financial year and were 8.9 per cent higher in the past five weeks.

The company said it had terminated most of its interest rate swaps, receiving £69.4 million after costs, leaving it with net debt as of last week of £45 million, down from £92 million on July 31. In annual results published last month, Wetherspoon said its sales were more than £.7 billion in the year to the end of July, up from £73 million previously.

Wetherspoon cut annual underlying pre-tax losses from £67 million to £0.4 million, although it made a profit of £32 million before the pandemic.

The pubs group was founded by Martin in 1979 on the site of a former betting shop in Muswell Hill, north London. It was floated in 1992 with 44 pubs and at its peak in 2015 the estate had 951 outlets in the UK and Ireland.

Analysts said Wetherspoon would be looking to the football World Cup followed by Christmas trading to give it a lift in sales. "These are a big few weeks for hospitality businesses," said one, adding that much would depend on whether people stayed at home or "were in the office going for after-work drinks".

Wetherspoon shares, which have fallen by more than 50 per cent this year, were down a further 30¾, or 6.3 per cent, at 457½ yesterday.

Brewdog man jumps ship One of BrewDog's top executives is leaving to run Stonegate, the pubs and bars group behind the Slug and Lettuce and Popworld chains (Russell Hotten writes).

David McDowall will join the 4,500-site chain in February, with Simon Longbottom, Stonegate's chief executive, standing down and saying yesterday that he wanted to "pursue interests abroad".

Longbottom will remain a shareholder.

It is thought that Longbottom, 51, intends to spend more time with his family and travel, possibly in Australia. He spent eight years at the private company, helping to turn it into Britain's biggest pub chain, which also owns Be At One.

McDowall, 44, Brewdog's president and chief operating officer, leaves after an unsettled period for the company, which is based at Ellon, Scotland. After achieving rapid growth and accruing a big fanbase through its offbeat marketing strategy, James Watt, BrewDog's chief executive, was accused by staff of fostering a culture of fear. He apologised last year.

BrewDog was also thought to be planning a stock market listing, although that plan appears to have cooled for the moment.

headline: I avoided Porsche's float. It seems destined to flop
author: David Brenchley
text:
Get rich slowly

Investing in a company when it first launches on the stock market is exciting. It gives you the hope that you are in at the beginning and are backing the next big thing.

The carmaker Porsche floated on the German stock exchange five weeks ago, on September 29, and shares are up 21 per cent since.

Over the same period shares in Ferrari (floated in 2015) have gained 7.6 per cent.

Aston Martin Lagonda, the prolific supplier of cars to the fictional agent James Bond, by contrast, is 78 per cent lower than at the start of the year while the company struggles with logistical issues such as supply chain problems and delivery delays.

While Porsche has had success on the stock market so far, if the evidence of most recent flotations, or initial public offerings (IPOs), is any guide we could yet see its shares go into reverse.

For retail investors like you and me, it's not easy to take part in IPOs. Usually, it's much cheaper for companies to offer them only to institutional firms such as asset managers and pension funds. We typically only get to invest on the secondary market, after shares have started trading.

ATLA

Some investment platforms have started to change this. PrimaryBid has been allowing retail investors to buy into selected IPOs for a few years. Back in June the platforms Hargreaves Lansdown and AJ Bell said that they would launch a service to allow more retail investors to take part in IPOs. Unlike PrimaryBid, this new platform, hosted by Peel Hunt, would be open to those of us with a stocks and shares Isa.

This expanded choice for investors is a positive development, but I would caution against getting too excited. IPOs tend to do badly.

Investing in newly floated companies is a notoriously difficult thing to get right. Most of the time you will lose money. Almost half of the 1,205 companies that came to the market in 2020 and 2021 have suffered stock price falls of more than 50 per cent, according to the research firm Edison Group.

That's not surprising. Stock markets have been falling throughout this year and it's the less profitable, more technology-focused firms that have taken the brunt of the selling. These companies have dominated the IPO market recently.

The e-commerce firm THG (The Hut Group) is a member of an elite group of new IPOs. It is one of 94 companies whose shares have sunk more than 90 per cent. In THG's case, the stock has gone from 796p to 67p in 23 months.

The Renaissance IPO exchange traded fund, which uses a computer algorithm to track the share prices of the largest and most liquid new IPOs on Wall Street, is down 54 per cent in 2022 versus a 20.6 per cent decline from the S&P 500.

The figures are no better over longer timeframes. Since the ETF was launched in 2013 it has produced returns of 43.7 per cent. The S&P 500 has returned 167 per cent.

A bear market has led the number of IPOs to plummet. In the first nine months of 2022 there were 992 IPOs, which raised a total of $146 billion globally. In the first nine months of 2021, there were 1,773 IPOs, raising $341.7 billion, according to the consultancy EY.

There are some interesting companies that have said they would like to float ByteDance, the Chinese owner of the social media sharing site TikTok, the digital bank Monzo and the beer maker Brewdog, to name a few. TikTok has almost 31 million users a day, and about 20 per cent of all internet users across the globe have the app. The challenger bank Monzo has won customers thanks to its reputation for good customer service and its easy-to-use app. Profits are up 90 per cent to £70 million in the year to June. Brewdog, which started life in Glasgow, now has a global empire and a turnover of £86 million.

Nonetheless I would be reluctant to take a punt on any of them. I'm not a beer drinker, so even if Brewdog offered me discounts I'm not sure I'd be tempted.

Even if I did believe in these businesses for the long term, it seems that greater gains are to be made by holding off for a while. Overhyped companies often disappoint on their first day of trading only to bounce back later down the line.

I would rather trust a fund manager to do the work. The most likely to back a newly listed stock among the funds that I invest in is the BlackRock Throgmorton investment trust, which invests in medium and small sized UK firms. I'll leave the digging to the manager, Dan Whitestone.

Another option is through the IFSL RC Brown UK Primary Opportunities fund, which invests in newly listed firms and also buys shares from companies looking to raise extra cash by offering cheap shares to institutional investors. Performance has been poor this year, down 20 per cent versus the FTSE all-share's 3.8 per cent decline, but since its 1996 launch the fund's 538 per cent gain has beaten the index's 425 per cent comfortably.

Investors who put their faith in Porsche have so far been well rewarded, but the odds are that its performance will soon hit the brakes.

I'll pass on similar highpowered IPOs for now.

headline: BREW HYPOCRITES
author: CHRIS TAYLOR
text:
Firm's anti-Qatar ads..while doing biz in China

BEER giant BrewDog has been branded "hypocritical" for blasting the Qatar World Cup while doing business in China.

Human rights groups said bosses' slating of football tournament organisers is "nothing but a PR stunt".

Pointing out the Scots firm has a pub in Shanghai and plans to open a brewery in the country,

Mark Sabah, of the Committee for Freedom in Hong Kong Foundation, hit out yesterday: "It's completely hypocritical.

"If they're going to be calling out human rights abuses in Qatar, they should be doing the same in China.

"If they want to make a statement, they should shut down their facilities in China for the duration of the tournament. Otherwise it's just virtue signalling"

BrewDog founded in 2007 by James Watt and Martin Dickie, both 40, in Ellon, Aberdeenshire announced itself as a World Cup "anti-sponsor" this week.

It said the competition should never have been given to the Middle Eastern state due to its record of corruption and human rights abuses. And it branded the tournament the "beautiful shame" on billboards.

There is a craft beer boom in China, one of Brewdog's biggest markets. But the ruling Communist

Party has come under fire for persecuting and jailing opponents of its regime.

A BrewDog spokesman said last night: "There are issues in every country, but this corrupt World F*Cup is only taking place in one, Qatar.

"What's interesting is that brands like Adidas, Visa, and Kia are shelling out millions to endorse the tournament and all it stands for, and no one seems to have noticed or care."

chris.taylor@thesun.co.uk

headline: TOP DOG IN VEGAS
text:
SCOTS beer-maker BrewDog is taking on the bright lights of Vegas.

The brand has just opened its newest bar there - and it is its largest yet, spread across two floors and overlooking the MGM New York-New York hotel.

Boozers visiting at its launch two days ago were automatically entered into prize draw to win a $1 million bar tab that can be redeemed at any BrewDog bar around the world over the next 20 years.

On top of its 96 taps, the newest BrewDog features an extravagant food menu with dishes like lobster mac 'n' cheese, oak-fired chicken and Flying Scotsman burger.

There's also a ten-barrel brewhouse on site, which produces the strongest beer on the Strip, The Haus Always Wins.

BrewDog boss, James Watt, said: "With nearly 100 taps of craft beer and killer views, this oasis in the desert is the biggest BrewDog Bar we have ever opened.

"The $1 million bar tab will offer a lifetime of incredible beer and food."

headline: A BEER FACED LIE
author: ISAAC CROWSON
text:
BrewDog fruit ale ad ban for '1 of 5 a day' claim

AN ad for fruit-flavoured beer has been banned for claiming to be "one of your five a day".

Craft giant BrewDog made the comparison while promoting its beers including Lost in Guava and Hazy Jane Passionfruit.

An email to customers was headed "one of your five a day".

The Scots brewer also boasted "we've got all your fruit needs covered" with the beers which included Pineapple Punch.

The Advertising Standards Authority probed a single complaint that it was misleading as the beers were not equivalent to portions of fruit or veg.

BrewDog said the claim was tongue in cheek and reflected its "playful marketing style". The brewer, based in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, added the email was sent only to existing customers who had opted to receive them.

But the watchdog ruled the advert broke its code and has banned it.

A spokesman said: "The ASA acknowledged that the subject heading 'one of your five a day' might be interpreted by some consumers as a humorous nod to the fruit flavoured beers featured in the body of the email."

However, it said, as the claim referred to government advice on health and well-being, consumers "would not expect" advertisers to include such claims unless they were true.

It said the ad would, in general, leave the public unsure if fruit in booze "counted" as one of the advised "five a day".

isaac.crowson@the-sun.co.uk

headline: Alcohol-free beer brand toasts £0m fundraising
author: James Hurley
text:
An alcohol-free beer business is toasting a lucrative start to "dry January" after it raised £0 million to help to finance its plan to capitalise on healthier drinking habits.

The funding for Lucky Saint, said to be the largest for an alcohol-free beer brand in Europe, was led by Beringea, a venture capital firm, and JamJar Investments, set up by the founders of Innocent Drinks, the smoothie maker.

The London-based Lucky Saint is one of the companies seeking to profit from the rapid growth in Britain's "low and no" segment of the drinks industry. The reduced or zero-alcohol sector is expected to be worth more than £50 million by next year. Established in 2018, it is already the UK's largest independent alcohol-free beer brand and its drinks are available in 5,000 pubs, bars and restaurants, as well as in leading supermarkets.

Other investors in the funding round include Jonathan Warburton, chairman of Warburtons, the baking group, and Will Greenwood, a member of England's Rugby World Cup-winning team in 2003.

Luke Boase, 41, founder of Lucky Saint, said he wanted to bring a "much-maligned proposition into the 21st century. We've all apologised for not drinking. You've almost had to order alcohol-free beer under your breath."

Lucky Saint is produced in a German brewery that removes alcohol from finished beer, a technique that Boase said helps to preserve flavour.

This year a report commissioned by Lucky Saint suggested that one in three pub visits do not involve alcohol as more people moderate their drinking, and the business is expecting bumper trade this month as consumers take up January's abstinence trend. It is forecasting 25 per cent year-onyear growth in people giving up alcohol for the month, bringing estimated participation up to about ten million Britons.

"Beer in general is in decline, Boase said. "Alcohol-free is the only part that's in growth, reflecting changing attitudes towards moderation. Now it's perfectly acceptable to have an alcohol-free beer in the pub. Four years ago, that wasn't the case." He said the new funding would help the company to speed up the availability of its beer on draught in pubs and would finance a planned European expansion.

Lucky Saint faces stiff competition. Established brands including Guinness, BrewDog, Adnams, Beavertown and Heineken all offer low or alcoholfree beers.

headline: My top alcohol-free wines and beers Jane MacQuitty's pick
text:
Giving dry January a go this year? You are not alone. Much as I loathe the "mindful drinking" and "sober curious" movements, almost nine million of us are planning to take a month off this year. And the good news for those cutting back is that the quality of low and no-alcohol beer and wine has never been better, with dozens of new options for drinkers to try.

Decent alcohol-free beer, care of lazy yeasts, flavour-boosting hops and the like, is still a much better bet than de-alcoholised wine. The latter has improved, but the high levels of residual sugar is off-putting, with most zeroalcohol whites weighing in at 38g per litre and reds about 46g compared to just traces of sugar in most every-day dry whites and reds. Discerning drinkers will have a happier time with sparkling wines, which, due to the fizz and higher levels of acidity masking the sugar, make a more convincing substitute.

One of the most exciting new options for wine drinkers is Three Spirit's Blurred Vines duo, which really do give you some of the flavours and joy of the real thing thanks to a mix of unusual teas and fermented fruits, albeit at a price. Sharp (see below) is the white wine alternative and, served cold, its zingy spritz makes a good aperitif, while Spark's pale pink, equally delicate spritz delivers more of a black tea, red chilli and redcurrant wallop (thewinesociety.com, £6).

If it's full-on pink fizz you want, Freixenet's 0.0% Alcohol Free Sparkling RoséSainsbury's, £) delivers pleasant, peppery, grapey fruit. The best musky, grape-stashed white I've found is still Bodegas Torres's 2021 Natureo Muscat (Waitrose, £.72 down from £.30), and while zero-alcohol reds are still the hardest style to pull off, Torres's 2020 Natureo Garnacha-Syrah (see below) is as good as it gets.

Of the dozens of zero-alcohol beers I tasted, new favourites include BrewDog's Elvis Juice, an unusual, spicy, grapefruitinfused IPA (4x330ml; Tesco and Sainsbury's, £.50), and Big Drop's Galactic Milk Stout, a malty, chocolatey gem (330ml; Morrisons, £.50). But don't miss the Edinburgh brewer Days' brilliant new duo; the Pale Ale (see below) is my favourite, but the Days Lager (330ml; daysbrewing.com, £.08), is a fragrant, hoppy winner.

Best no-alcohol wines

Blurred Vines Sharp, Three Spirit England, 0 per cent thewinesociety.com, £6 Splash out on the best white wine alternative, bursting with herby, peppery, green-tea bite.

Zerozecco Sparkling White Alcohol Free Germany, 0 per cent Aldi, £.79 Britain's best-value zero fizz overflows with elegant, grapey, elderflower-scented fruit. 2020 Natureo De-Alcoholised Garnacha-Syrah, Spain, 0 per cent Sainsbury's, £ This bright, dusky zero-alcohol red with a juicy, hibiscus-scented palate is one of the few that works.

Freixenet 0.0% Alcohol Free Sparkling Spain, 0 per cent Tesco, £.50 Convincing, de-alcoholised cava with just enough refreshing, sparky, lime pickle pizzazz to pop up here.

Best no-alcohol beers

Days Pale Ale Scotland, 0 per cent Tesco, £ (330ml bottle) Brilliantly brewed, vegan-friendly, deliciously hoppy, floral pale ale made from three different hops and malted barley.

BrewDog Hazy Jane Alcohol Free New England IPA, Scotland, 0.5 per cent Waitrose, £ (4x330ml cans) Glorious, fuller-flavoured, tropically fruited classic New England India Pale Ale without the alcohol.

Big Drop Brewing Co Uptime Craft Lager England, 0.5 per cent bigdropbrew.com, £4 (12x330ml bottles) De-alcoholised lager can be a yawn, but Big Drop's is a crisp, herby, faintly spicy, citrussy hit.

Guinness 0.0% Draught Alcohol Free Stout Ireland, 0 per cent Morrisons, £ (4x440ml cans) Amazingly authentic stout with almost the same nutty, creamy, roasted-barley whack as the real McCoy.

Quality has never been better, with dozens of new options for drinkers to try

headline: Brewdog  boss pays £00,000 for gold gaffe
author: Nadeem Badshah
text:
The boss of BrewDog has paid out nearly £00,000 to winners of a promotion that offered the chance to find a solid gold can hidden in cases.

Some winners complained to the Advertising Standards Authority after they discovered that the cans were not solid gold but gold-plated. The ASA upheld the complaints in October 2021 and said three adverts were misleading.

James Watt, co-founder and chief executive of the Scottish brewery, said that he had "falsely thought" the cans were solid gold. He said he contacted all 50 gold can winners to offer them the "full cash amount" as an alternative to the prize. "All in all, it ended up costing me around £70,000 well over two and a half years' salary," he added.

Watt said that he had "misunderstood" how the cans were made and made a "silly mistake" in initial tweets that the cans were "solid gold cans".

"Those were three very expensive mistaken tweets that I sent out in my enthusiasm for our new campaign," the entrepreneur wrote in a lengthy post on the website LinkedIn.

The ASA said that it had received 25 complaints about three social media adverts stating the prize was made from "solid gold". A 330ml can made of gold would have been worth about £63,000 at the time. In his post, Watt defended valuing each can at £5,000.

headline: Brewdog 's £00k error
text:
BREWDOG has paid out almost £00,000 to winners of its misleading 'solid gold' beer can competition.

In a 2021 promotion, the beer maker hid 50 cans made of what it claimed was solid gold for customers to find, which chief executive James Watt said was 'inspired by Willy Wonka'. Each winner would keep the can and receive £5,000 worth of Brewdog shares. But the cans were gold plated, which is far less valuable. The UK advertising watchdog, the Advertising Standards Authority (ASA), banned the ad for being misleading.

Watt has revealed that he has paid out £70,000 in total to the original can winners.

headline: Gold tins of beer a flat loser for boss
author: ALICE WALKER
text:
BREWDOG boss James Watt has told how a "misunderstanding" over a golden can giveaway cost him almost £00,000. The craft beer tycoon, 40, claimed he was trying to be Willy Wonka when in 2020 he launched the promo and hid "solid gold" tins in multipacks.

But winners were upset when they discovered they were only goldplated and worth a fraction of the pure metal.

After he was rapped by the Advertising Standards Agency James offered a cash alternative to punters from his own pocket.

He said: "It got pretty grim, I had to make things right without the business being punished for my error.

"It ended up costing me around £70,000."

James said he still has 40 of the Ellon, Aberdeenshire-based firm's golden cans, adding: "Not sure what to do with them. All ideas welcome."

headline: Golden cans give  Brewdog  boss £00,000 hangover
author: Nadeem Badshah
text:
The boss of Brewdog has paid out nearly £00,000 to winners of the company's misleading promotion that offered people the chance to find a solid gold can hidden in cases.

Some winners complained to the Advertising Standards Authority after they discovered the cans were not solid gold, but were gold-plated instead. The ASA upheld the complaints in October 2021 and said that three adverts were misleading.

James Watt, cofounder and chief executive of the Scottish brewer, acknowledged that he made "some costly mistakes" and had "falsely thought" the cans were made from solid gold.

He said that he contacted all 50 gold can winners to offer them the "full cash amount" as an alternative to the prize if they were unhappy".

"All in all, it ended up costing me around £70,000 well over two and a half years' salary," he added.

Watt said that he "misunderstood the process of how they were made" and made a "silly mistake" by telling customers in initial promotional tweets that the cans were "solid gold cans".

"Those were three very expensive mistaken tweets that I sent out in my enthusiasm for our new campaign," Watt wrote on the website LinkedIn on Saturday. "The Gold Can saga was headline news.

We were made to look dishonest and disingenuous and we took a real hammering online and in the press. Deservedly so. My initial tweets had been misleading and we deserved the flak," he added.

In his post the Brewdog boss also disclosed that he now owned 40 of the gold cans.

The ASA said that it received 25 complaints about three social media adverts stating its can prize was made from "solid gold" relating to the prize's authenticity, with some questioning how much the can was worth after Brewdog claimed that it was valued at £5,000.

The ASA said that Brewdog told investigators that a single 330ml can, made with the equivalent 330ml of pure gold, would have a gold value of about £63,000 at the time in October 2021.

However, the regulator concluded that a general audience was unlikely to be aware of the price of gold and "how that would translate into the price of a gold can, and whether that was inconsistent with the valuation as stated in the ad". Watt reiterated in his LinkedIn post that the "valuation of £5,000 per can was accurate".

Brewdog has faced criticism previously for its marketing campaigns and work environment.

In June 2021, Watt promised an independent review after criticism from former staff of working conditions.

A letter from ex-workers posted on Twitter alleged that a "significant number" of former staff had "suffered mental illness as a result of working at Brewdog".

It made a numerous allegations including that the company fostered a culture where staff were afraid to speak out about concerns.

headline: TRAVEL'S FUNNY FINDS
author: Shaf Rasul
text:
IT MAKES ME LAUGH

SOMETHING that gave me a laugh this week was hearing about all the weird and wonderful things people have left behind at budget hotel chain Travelodge.

Items included a 6ft Japanese temple in Glasgow, a shortbread castle in Stirling and a jar labelled "Loch Ness fresh air" in Inverness.

More worrying was the fact electronic devices and business papers were among the most common things left in 2022, which will cause confidentiality concerns.

An unnamed politician forgot his speech, and a company director left their resignation letter!

Password-protect your devices and double check your belongings, so you never lose more than your toothbrush!

IT MAKES ME LAUGH ME CRY

BOSSES at Scottish brew company Brewdog must be feeling light-headed after their 2021 "gold can" promotion fell foul of advertising standards.

The promo gave beer drinkers the chance to win an alleged "solid gold" can, said to be worth £5k. But some customers felt mislead when they realised it was only gold-plated.

Complaints made to the Advertising Standards Authority were upheld, meaning Brewdog had to pay out around £70,000 - well over double CEO James Watt's salary.

Watt admitted a "silly mistake" was made.

Remember the golden rule - make sure your advertising doesn't risk misleading customers.

headline: VIVA LASH VEGAS
author: STUART PATTERSON
text:
BEER FIRM BASH BOMBSHELL

Punter wins $1m Brewdog bar tab in Sin City, buys a $27,000 round, then is turfed out... for being too drunk

BREWDOG gave a punter a $1million bar tab at their glitzy new Las Vegas venue then showed him the door for being drunk.

Bosses revealed merry Jeffrey Appleby, 36, was "encouraged to have an early night" after splurging $27,000 of his prize on rounds for everyone.

The Aberdeenshire beer firm had raffled the £00,000 tab to spend on beer, food and hotels over 20 years to celebrate the opening of their Sin City rooftop bar.

But after winner Jeffrey shoved a wheelbarrow of booze through the pub and sank some ale, staff escorted him off the premises appearing the worse for wear.

An insider said: "I was told the guy had been drinking before he even turned up. "Everyone was chanting his name and it looked like he was having a blast.

"He was definitely making the most of his big moment. "Who wouldn't? He's become a legend now."

American air force veteran Jeffrey leapt on the bar with joy as he was handed an oversized cheque by Brew- Dog's co-founder James Watt. Over 1,000 guests turned up for the grand opening last month and had their wristband numbers put in a draw. It's understood the terms and conditions stated the winner could only get drinks in for the crowd on prize night. After that, it's banned.

Jeffrey agreed to leave the posh bar without fuss. He's returned several times since.

A source revealed: "There are no issues between him and BrewDog. He's been back four times and even had a big group of friends in for a party on New Year's Eve."

BrewDog told how the twostorey pub is doing a roaring trade and has quickly become one of their topgrossing venues.

They say more than 40,000 guests have come through the doors and sampled more than 60,000 ales.

Deep-fried Mars Bars are on the menu, along with a wagyu burger that costs £20.

A BrewDog spokesman said: "We were delighted with our grand opening party, with over 1,000 guests enjoying the evening.

"Our $1million bar tab winner delighted the crowd by buying the bar a few rounds of beer, and after this he was encouraged to have an early night.

"He left with his two friends and was accompanied by two senior managers. "We look forward to seeing him back in one of our bars in the near future to use the remainder of his prize.

"It is valid over a 20-year period, therefore encouraging sensible consumption."

BrewDog was founded in 2007 by James and pal Martin Dickie, who made their first beers in a garage.

It has become one of the world's top booze brands.

stuart.patterson@the-sun.co.uk

headline: ADHD of Scottish brewery top dog
author: STUART MacDONALD
text:
BREWDOG boss James Watt says he has been diagnosed with autism and attention deficit hyperactivity disorder.

The chief executive of the craft beer firm said he sought specialist help after he was described as "semi-autistic" amid allegations of poor behaviour towards staff.

James, 40, said his conditions make it difficult to read social cues and express empathy.

But he said he will continue to work with medical experts to develop coping strategies to lead the Aberdeenshire-based company.

He said: "I am still in the early stages of my journey here and I intend to continue sharing thoughts and insights that may help other neurodiverse business leaders."

The Scottish firm faced criticism for its advertising and work environment and has since overhauled its culture.

headline: Beer exec in ADHD diagnosis
author: STUART MacDONALD
text:
BREWDOG boss James Watt revealed he has been diagnosed with autism and ADHD at the age of 40.

The craft beer firm chief executive said he sought specialist help after he was described as "semi-autistic" following allegations of poor behaviour towards staff.

James told how his conditions make it difficult to read social cues and express empathy.

But he vowed he will continue to work with medical experts to develop coping strategies to lead the Aberdeenshire-based company.

He said: "Understanding the condition means I can build practises into my daily life which help me better manage occasions, settings and experiences that I may find more challenging."

The firm faced criticism for its work environment and has since overhauled its culture.

headline: Boss of brewer with 'toxic culture' diagnosed with autism and ADHD
author: Stuart MacDonald
text:
James Watt, the BrewDog chief executive accused by former staff of inappropriate behaviour in the workplace, has said he has been diagnosed with autism.

The co-founder of the craft beer firm said he sought specialist help after he was described as "semi-autistic" in the wake of allegations that he presided over a "toxic" work culture.

Watt, 40, has now been diagnosed with attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) which causes people to be restless and impulsive and Asperger syndrome, a form of autism.

He said the conditions made it difficult for him to read social cues and express empathy.

Watt said he would continue to work with medical experts to develop coping strategies to allow him to manage his conditions while still leading the Aberdeenshire-based company.

"As a CEO and business leader I am far from alone in being on the autism spectrum Elon Musk has Asperger's," he said. "I am still in the early stages of my journey here and I intend to continue sharing thoughts and insights that may help other neurodiverse business leaders and entrepreneurs on their journeys too.

"I am confident that working with fantastic specialists I can work within the confines of ASD and ADHD to continue to improve how I approach the leadership of BrewDog as well as how I approach life overall."

Watt said he had always thought of himself as an "odd fish" who was uncomfortable in social settings and preferred being alone.

He put it down to having an analytical brain and did not seek help until last year, when he decided to get assessed.

Watt has been fighting to restore the company's reputation after dozens of staff, under the collective name of Punks with Purpose, signed an open letter in June 2021 alleging "toxic attitudes towards junior staff" and a culture of fear that had left many "burnt out, afraid and miserable".

Following his diagnosis, Watt said: "I have always struggled with anything emotional and I am terrible with social cues. I have always needed significant amounts of time by myself to be able to function well as a human.

"I would often be more terrified in simple social settings than I would be facing huge storms in my days on the high seas of the north Atlantic.

"I have always accepted these character imperfections and viewed them as a kind of trad-off with other characteristics my analytical ability, my work ethic and my ability to focus on tasks like a demented motherf***er.

"'It's just how I am' is pretty much what I always thought."

BrewDog, which is valued at more than £ billion, was founded by Watt and Martin Dickie, who were at school together. They started the firm in 2007 with a bank loan and a grant from the Prince's Trust.

Since it faced criticism it has sought to overhaul its culture by introducing a shared profit scheme, three companywide employee representative groups, a diversity forum, a confidential ethics hotline, and increasing investment in learning and development. ? The singer Lewis Capaldi has trialled a new device to help control tics caused by his Tourette syndrome. Capaldi, 26, visited Nottingham University to try Neupulse, a watch-like device that delivers electrical stimulation to nerves. Capaldi, who is touring the UK, told the team he was impressed by the effects of the gadget, which suppressed head and neck tics.

headline: According to the test, I've got ADHD too. Ludicrous!
author: Max Pemberton
text:
Y

OU'D be forgiven for thinking that we're in the grip of an ADHD epidemic. It seems that barely a day goes past without some celebrity revealing their diagnosis. Clearly, ADHD is the latest 'must-have' mental health condition.

Brewdog boss James Watt disclosed last week that he has the condition. Former Premier League footballer Jermaine Pennant announced the week before that he had been given the label. They join the likes of Johnny Vegas, Ant McPartlin, Justin Timberlake, Sue Perkins and Olivia Attwood, who have also talked of being diagnosed.

While I don't dispute these diagnoses, I have to say that, after 20 years of seeing literally no one with ADHD in my clinic, I now come across it all the time.

The one thing these patients have in common? They've paid someone for a diagnosis.

Proponents of this new craze in adult ADHD argue that the sudden explosion in cases is thanks to increased awareness, meaning that more people are coming forward to get tested.

I'm afraid that in many cases I just don't buy it.

Time and time again, I've seen adults claim to have ADHD to explain away the fact that they can't concentrate, or focus at work when what they actually need is to get off their phones and learn to tolerate frustration, boredom and tedium in life.

A whole industry has sprung up in diagnosing people with ADHD. It's very profitable for psychiatrists and psychologists and there's a stream of people who will pay handsomely to get a diagnosis.

Assessments can cost anything from £00 to several thousand; and then there's the ongoing treatment, which often includes private prescriptions for medication (only a psychiatrist can prescribe and they typically charge £50 or more per hour) and ongoing specialist therapy costing £50 an hour upwards.

We are also seeing more and more people who are selfdiagnosing using online tests. It's something that is worrying a lot of psychiatrists, who feel that the tests being used ask vague or leading questions. Before writing this, I did a test myself. And guess what? If the results are to be believed, I too have ADHD. It's ludicrous.

Who doesn't get distracted when there's activity or music playing around them? (One of the questions.) Lots of people find their tasks at work are boring or repetitive (another question). The result is that the term is becoming meaningless.

Over the past few years, the criteria for meeting a diagnosis for lots of mental health problems, including ADHD, has been widened. It's called 'diagnosis creep', and means that more people fall within the threshold for being classed as having the condition.

tycoon Johnson, reported spending $trying to reverse process. Reading schedule I was tedium of his miserable. What growing old Someone him: we live it

Many have pointed the finger of blame at pressures from the pharmaceutical industry, since the more people with an illness, the more people who need medication.

But does this actually matter?

I think it does matter, for a number of reasons. Many point to the fact that childhood ADHD is an established condition. But they fail to appreciate that even this remains controversial amongst doctors, and research suggests it's being wildly over-diagnosed.

It is undoubtedly a complex condition, with genetics, social factors and environment playing a part. Many of us view it as a problem with the family, rather than the child. The World Health Organisation states that a diagnosis of ADHD can represent family dysfunction rather than a problem with the child.

Many worry that it's easy to whack a label on a child, and medicalise their behaviour, rather than accept there might be a problem with parenting that needs to be addressed.

Bryan 45, was week as 2million a year the ageing about his struck by the routine. It seemed happened to gracefully? should tell one life, well!

And rather than looking at an adult's life and their struggles, you label it all with a diagnosis that removes the incentive to look at what could usefully be changed. Labelling can have a serious effect on how people view themselves and on the effort they make to change behaviours. A diagnosis means that people too often feel they no longer need to take responsibility for their actions, or make an effort to change.

There's something reassuring in being able to say that your behaviour is the result of a neurological condition like

ADHD. It also means that if anyone criticises you, you then have the protection of a medical diagnosis as defence.

A few years ago, we had a host of celebrities claiming to have bipolar disorder. This seems to have fallen out of fashion now in favour of ADHD.

NUMBER of highprofile

A doctors including Sir Simon Wessely, former president of the Royal College of Psychiatrists, and Dr Iona Heath, former president of the Royal College of General Practitioners, have spoken out about how doctors must resist the drive to over-diagnose patients.

Yet mental health has been weaponised, and many fear opprobrium will be heaped on them if they question the validity of a contentious diagnosis.

I fear that giving people a label traps them. It tells them there's nothing they can do to change.

Research suggests many of those being diagnosed do have a genuine problem but it's not ADHD. They have a host of things from emotional issues and complex psychological problems to substance misuse.

Ignoring their real problems and putting a convenient label on it is really not the answer.

headline: News; Front Page Advertising ban will destroy our industry, drinks giants warn
author: David McCann
text:
Some of the largest whisky and beer companies have united to demand that a proposed ban on alcohol advertising is scrapped. They say the move will "destroy Scotland's drinks industry".

Companies including Budweiser Brewing Group, Lanson Champagne, Diageo, Whyte & Mackay and Ten- nent's Lager have signed an open letter to Holyrood ministers to urge them to abandon the plan, which at its most extreme would stop alcohol branding or advertising in public spaces or online.

The new policy would affect pubs, restaurants, delicatessens, off-licences, wine shops and single malt specialists.

The letter marks a new front in the campaign against the restrictions, which could also spell the end of drink companies' sponsorship of sport and live events such as festivals.

The Scottish government's consultation about the proposals is due to conclude next month.

Distilleries and brewery shops risk being barred from selling branded merchandise to visitors, and drinks branding would have to be removed from pub umbrellas and glassware. More than 100 drinks firms from across Scotland have signed the letter, including Belhaven, Brewdog, Chivas Brothers/Pernod Ricard, the Macallan and the Molson Coors Beverage Company.

Distilleries from Orkney to Annandale have backed the plea, along with breweries from Lewis to Braemar.

Ministers are also considering a ban on outdoor advertising of alcohol, including on vehicles, and forbidding adverts in newspapers and magazines. The companies fear a "blanket ban on alcohol advertising and sponsorship" in Scotland.

They insist that this "could not have come at a worse time for our sector, and the many thousands we employ", with Continued on page 2 continued from page 1 Drinks advertising ban the drinks industry having "suffered hard through the Covid years", while the cost of living crisis "threatens the very existence" of some businesses.

The companies say there is no evidence to justify the move to ban advertising and marketing. They state: "Restricting the ability to promote and market products responsibly will remove a vital route to market and go against the Scottish government's vision to double the turnover of the food and drink sector by 2030.

"A further unintended consequence would be the blocking of a key source of vital funds to Scotland's sports and arts and culture sectors." The sector employs 88,700 people in Scotland, and contributes £.1 billion a year to the economy. The letter describes whisky, beers and gins produced in Scotland as being "iconic exports which in turn drive our economy at home".

The companies insist they "recognise and share in the Scottish government's determination to reduce harmful consumption of alcohol", adding that there will be "further workable steps" the sector can take to help.

They argue, however: "These proposals will not serve to achieve this and do not address the root cause of why someone might come to have a harmful relationship with alcohol".

More than two million people visited Scottish distilleries in 2019. It is unclear how attractions such as the Johnnie

Walker Experience in Edinburgh could continue if a ban was implemented.

A Scottish government spokesman said: "Alcohol-related harm is one of the most pressing public health challenges. An average of 700 people are hospitalised and 24 people die each week from illnesses caused by drinking alcohol."

Maree Todd, the public health minister, is to meet "key stakeholders" to "hear directly" their concerns.

The spokesman added: "[Todd] has been clear that there is clear evidence that adverts which glamorise drinking can encourage young people to drink alcohol and have a detrimental impact on those in recovery. [We are] determined to tackle Scotland's problematic relationship with alcohol."

headline: BrewDog  to expand in China after Budweiser tie-up
author: Judith Evans
text:
Maker of Punk IPA says it wants to take advantage of Chinese drinkers’rowing interest in craft beer

Scottish craft beer group BrewDog will begin brewing Punk IPA and other beers in China and will open more bars in the country after forming a joint venture with Budweiser China, the largest international brewer in the market.

The privately owned UK craft beer maker, which has been seeking a fresh start after it postponed a planned initial public offering and faced claims of a toxic culture from staff, said it wanted to tap into a new but growing craft beer market.

It was also shifting its expansion away from its home market of the UK because of the impact of inflation on brewing and hospitality venues there, said BrewDog chief executive James Watt.

“e don’ want to be owned by ‘ig beer’ut we do want to become a global beer business,”aid Watt. “ost of our focus in putting our capital to work is international. The environment is very challenging in the UK.”

BrewDog’ Punk IPA, Hazy Jane and Elvis Juice will be produced at Bud China’ newly opened Putian craft brewery in Fujian province under the deal, while it aims to open nine more BrewDog-branded bars to add to one already open in Shanghai.

It follows a similar tie-up in 2021 with Japan’ Asahi, under which BrewDog said it had since doubled sales in the country. Bud China is an arm of Bud Apac, the listed Asian brewer majority-owned by the world’ largest brewer AB InBev.

BrewDog previously had a smaller China partnership with distributor Top Shelf and opened a bar in Shanghai in 2020. The new deal aimed to take advantage of an emerging thirst for craft beer in China, said Watt.

The group said production of craft brews in China had reached 6.5mn hectolitres by 2020, though that was dwarfed by more than 341mn hectolitres of beer produced overall in China that year, according to hops supplier BarthHaas. Local craft brewers include Jing-A and Pandabrew.

BrewDog made a pre-tax loss of £.4mn in 2021, the most recent year for which accounts are available, on net revenues of £19mn.

The brewer, in which private equity firm TSG owns a stake, had planned to list in London in 2020 but postponed the planned IPO as Covid-19 affected hospitality venues and roiled markets.

Watt said the company now had no imminent plans to list but would look to put a timeline in place at the end of 2023.

BrewDog also faced criticism in 2021 when a group of former staff accused the brewer of a “ulture of fear” It made a series of changes in the wake of the allegations, including installing retail heavyweight Allan Leighton as chair, but has also said some of the claims were overblown.

headline: 2023 IS NOW CHINESE YEAR OF THE  BREWDOG
author: HOLLY WILLIAMS
text:
Firm's major deal boost

BREWDOG is to tap into the Chinese market after striking a major deal with beer giant Budweiser.

The Scots firm said the tie-up will see its craft ales in the Asian state from March.

Its beers such as Punk IPA and

Elvis Juice will be made at Budweiser China's Putian Craft Brewery in Fujian province.

BrewDog, based in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, also plans to open a string of new bars in the country by 2026, adding to its one Chinese venue in Shanghai and 110-strong network overseas. China currently accounts for less than one per cent of BrewDog's overall sales, Founder James Watt said: "This is a transformational partnership which is going to bring BrewDog to every corner of the world's biggest beer market, from a bespoke craft brewery which will help ensure the quality of our beers."

BrewDog will also look to Bud's sales and distribution network to help it expand in the region.

The Chinese deal follows its partnership in Japan with another brewer in September 2021.

One of its ads was banned last year over a claim its fruit-flavoured beers constitute "one of your five a day". Chiefs called the statement "tongue in cheek".

headline: BrewDog  thinks big with China venture
author: Dominic Walsh
text:
The co-founder of BrewDog has long been a critic of the global brewers, once describing his company as "a beacon of non-conformity in an increasingly monotone corporate desert" and insisting he would rather "shoot myself in the head" than sell his business to one of the big boys. But it seems James Watt has allowed his head to rule his heart, having joined the industry's big guns by setting up a joint venture in China with the world's biggest brewer.

BrewDog, which still calls itself an "independent craft brewer", said it was "set for major expansion in the world's biggest beer market" after sealing a partnership with Budweiser China, part of AB InBev.

The deal will enable the Scottish brewer to access Budweiser's sales and distribution network, giving a leg-up to sales of brands such as Punk IPA, Hazy Jane and Elvis Juice throughout China, starting from next month.

An affiliate of Budweiser will give BrewDog an entré to South Korea, too, with options to work together in other Asian markets.

The company's beers in China will be produced locally at a dedicated craft brewery, which the company said would enable it to reduce its carbon dioxide output and logistics costs from importing its wares from Europe.

BrewDog, which also has a tie-up in Japan with Asahi, said it planned to open new bars in China by 2026 to add to its existing venue in Shanghai.

Watt, 40, described the Budweiser joint venture as a "genuinely transformational partnership" a far cry from his previous views. When Beavertown Brewery in north London sold a 49 per cent stake to Heineken, Watt suggested that such takeovers of craft brewers were "horrifically bad for the beer, for the consumer and for the industry overall". And when AB InBev swallowed Camden Town brewery three years earlier, he declared he would no longer stock its beers in BrewDog bars.

The former deep-sea fisherman started BrewDog with Martin Dickie, 40, in 2007. BrewDog has undergone huge expansion in the past 15 years to become a multinational operation employing more than 2,600 people globally, including 250-plus at its base in Ellon, Aberdeenshire.

In addition to its bars, it has five hotels and breweries in the United States, Germany and Australia. Watt has said he wants BrewDog to become one of the world's five most valuable beer brands in the next five years.

headline: Brewdog  seals Budweiser tie up to put Punk IPA on map in China
author: Emily Hawkins
text:
BREWDOG is expanding across China after a deal with Budweiser.

The Scottish craft beer company said the tie-up with its rival brewer will see brands such as Punk IPA, Hazy Jane and Elvis Juice launch across China from next month.

Its beers will be brewed at Budweiser China's Putian Craft Brewery in Fujian province.

Brewdog, which is based in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, also plans to open several bars in China by 2026, adding to its one Chinese venue in Shanghai and its existing 110-strong overseas network of sites.

China accounts for less than 1pc of Brewdog's overall sales, but the group is hoping the deal will allow it to ramp up sales and tap into what it believes is the world's biggest producer and consumer of beer. It hopes to tap into a frenzy for beer in China just as its zero-Covid policy has been scrapped, with consumers keen to enjoy post-lockdown freedoms.

Brewdog is known for its quirky marketing campaigns and outspoken founder and chief executive James Watt once said he would prefer to 'shoot myself in the head' than sell out to a brewing behemoth.

Hailing the deal with Budweiser, Watt said the partnership meant Brewdog would reach 'every corner' of China, the world's second largest economy.

Production of craft beer in the country has grown ten times over the past decade, according to Budweiser China.

Brewdog, meanwhile, is attempting to shake off a string of controversies. Negative headlines hit Brewdog after current and former workers accused it in the summer of 2021 of perpetuating a toxic workplace culture. But bosses insisted they had put in place a 'wide ranging action plan' to tackle the issues.

Brewdog has also been criticised in the past for several misjudged marketing stunts.

After several 'volatile' years, Brewdog's public perception in the UK had been 'tainted' by the scandals, Danni Hewson, head of financial analysis at AJ Bell, said.

headline: Culture shock
author: Alex Brummer
text:
THE reputation of 'craft' beer concern Brewdog already has been stained by allegations of bullying, and the company launched a £m plan to change its culture. It is now planning to expand production of key brands in China. It wants to tap into the world's biggest beer market.

Forming a strategic partnership with Budweiser in China looks to be a crass error. It should have given weight to Beijing's oppression of the Uyghurs, the crackdown in Hong Kong and military threats against Taiwan before hopping aboard.

headline: I couldn't organise a p*** up..
author: STUART PATTERSON
text:
BOOZE FIRM BOSS'S ADMISSION

BREWDOG CANS BASH FOR FANS

BREWDOG bosses have called time on their annual parties for fans ? and admitted they're bad at organising booze-ups.

Co-founder James Watt said the wild bashes lost the craft beer firm £million a year.

And as it cuts costs, he's canned the so-called Annual General Mayhems in Aberdeen, angering some of its 220,000 shareholders.

James, 40, said: "The AGMs are always very expensive to host.

"Big events are not our core business and more often than not we have to pull in agency staff.

"This then means we often end up delivering suboptimal experiences to our community." Last year's AGM was the first held outdoors saw chaotic scenes when the 10,000 attendees were left queuing for hours for food and drink.

Fans forked out for food, drink and merch but James said the events always cost a fortune to run.

The Elvis Juice firm, based in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, is now to stage scaled-down get-togethers at its bars around the world. It follows operating losses of £.5million last year.

James added: "Hospitality businesses are fighting to protect jobs due to unprecedented inflation.

"To lose £million on an event when we could do something which helped support our bars would have been imprudent."

One unimpressed punter said: "The AGM was the only reason to be a shareholder."

Another added: "They're forgetting their roots and all the support they've had from Aberdeen."

headline: Pride at Chinese fake pub
text:
FLATTERED James revealed he refused to sue a knock-off pub, insisting it was a compliment.

The pub popped up complete with logos in an undisclosed spot in China.

BrewDog boss James said on Instagram: "It's not every day someone pays you the compliment of copying what you do.

"I mean, I'll admit we were surprised when we saw a picture of the bar in our image, but mainly we were peculiarly proud.

"There's something that says 'you've made it' like a weird replica of your craft beer brand in the world's biggest country."

We told how BrewDog is teaming up with beer giant Budweiser to officially enter the Chinese market next month.

headline: Scotland’ whisky and beer makers alarmed by mooted dvertising ban
author: Lukanyo Mnyanda in Edinburgh
text:
Drinks sector faces similar restrictions as tobacco under proposals designed to tackle country’ alcohol-related problems

Scott Laing, one of the many investors enticed into whisky production by a boom in demand, finished building the Ardnahoe Distillery in Scotland four years ago.

Now, he is concerned that he may not be able to drum up interest in his first batch of liquor because of a mooted ban on alcohol advertising in the country.

“f we start selling [the whisky] in the next couple of years, we wouldn’ be able to promote it in any way within Scotland, which is a bizarre state of affairs,”aid Laing, who runs the distillery on Islay with his brother and father, and invested £2.5mn to erect it.

Scottish officials have given the industry until March 9 to respond to a consultation on potential restrictions meant to help tackle the country’ “eep, longstanding and troubled relationship with alcohol”

Beer, wine and spirits could be consigned to near the back of the shops or placed in closed cupboards, similar to tobacco products, to reduce consumption.

Other proposed measures, some of which will need Westminster’ approval, include a ban on advertising in public places, as well as prohibiting the sale of alcohol-branded sports merchandise, including sponsorship on replica kits.

The looming crackdown on advertising has been met with staunch criticism from drinks companies and retailers, which argue that the Scottish government risks stigmatising an industry that employs about 89,000 people and is an important source of investment and tourism in rural areas.

More than 100 companies wrote to departing first minister Nicola Sturgeon this month calling for the regulations to be scrapped, warning they would “estroy”he industry.

“ didn’ hear any criticism at the time of the investment we were making into a Scottish rural community,”aid Laing.

James Watt, chief executive of Scottish craft beer group BrewDog, said: “oes society at large need to evolve and improve its relationship with alcohol? Absolutely, it does. Is the answer to that to ban advertising of alcohol in Scotland? No, I think that’ detrimental to Scotland.”

He added: “ think a lot of the sponsorships and advertising money [from alcohol] fund important grassroots work in sports, in the community, which is at risk. And Scotland is well-known internationally as the home of whisky.”

Meanwhile, brewer Innis & Gunn, which was founded by Dougal Sharp in 2003, was unimpressed to see its branded fleece blankets named in the government’ documents among the type of products that could soon be barred.

“ find it surprising that our blankets could be talked about as a source of harm,”he entrepreneur said, adding that the industry was tightly regulated and had invested in various campaigns to reduce problem drinking.

“ lack of branding for some of the most iconic brands that Scotland has ever produced    ou can’ imagine that this would make Scotland appealing to an international audience,”e said.

The pushback comes after the whisky industry defied a weakening economy in Scotland and benefited from a surge in demand overseas that pushed exports up 37 per cent to a record £.2bn in 2022. Growth was driven by strong sales in Taiwan, Singapore, India and China, the Scotch Whisky Association said.

The response has become emblematic f the strained relationship between the Scottish government and companies, with many business leaders saying their iews are often ignored by ministers.

Businesses have also criticised Scotland for failing to conduct an analysis of the economic impact such changes would have on the food and drinks sector in the country.

Ewan MacDonald-Russell, deputy head of the Scottish Retail Consortium, said: “e have a lot of proposals here, none of which have been costed.”

The restrictions should be “s comprehensive as possible”o be effective, according to the consultation documents, raising concerns among industry observers that ministers have already made up their mind on their severity.

An average of 700 people are hospitalised and 24 people die each week from illnesses caused by drinking alcohol, according to official figures.

“obody in government is telling people not to drink alcohol at all, this is about responsible consumption,”turgeon said this month. “dvertising that glamorises alcohol can lead to overconsumption.”

Restrictions on alcohol advertising could also cut income for the arts, including Edinburgh’ renowned Fringe festival. Its sponsors include Johnnie Walker Princes Street, a visitor centre opened by drinks giant Diageo as part of a £85mn investment in Scottish tourism.

Shona McCarthy, chief executive of the Fringe Society, said while she supported the Scottish government’ health aims, the proposals came at a “articularly dangerous”ime for a sector that is facing a “leak”uture owing to a weak economy and cuts to public funding for the arts. The Fringe’ partners were “esponsible and ethical” she added.

“f you are going to take away those avenues to support the art and cultural output of a nation that bigs itself up on its arts and cultural identity, then at the very least we need to be having a conversation about what is the alternative,”cCarthy said. “t feels like all the doors are closing.”

A Diageo spokesperson said the proposed restrictions would “o little to support those in need of help, while significantly impacting vital sectors, such as hospitality, tourism and sports”

The Scottish government argued that the ban on tobacco showed that alternative sponsors could be found.

A spokesperson said: “e are consulting on views on the most appropriate next steps in reducing alcohol-related harm, no decisions have been taken on scope or on the type of restrictions that might be taken forward in future.”

Additional reporting by Judith Evans

headline: Equity crowdfunding: consider the risks
author: Rafe Uddin
text:
NEED TO KNOW The growth of online platforms has made this a low-cost way to invest in private companies but it is not for the faint-hearted, reports Rafe Uddin

Buying shares in a company through equity funding ranks among the riskier ways of investing. The businesses tend to be new and the regulatory protections very limited.

But it's a low-cost way of betting on start-ups, as long as you don't get carried away.

Equity crowdfunding platforms hope for a rally this year as the market turmoil has limited many new companies' access to loans and private capital, their main funding sources.

If fundraisers are keen for cash, investors might get better deals. But bear in mind there are tough economic times ahead and private equity experts have withdrawn for a reason.

What is equity crowdfunding? Retail investors have long sought to access private markets, due to the prospect of above average returns. But the main entry routes into individual companies require large investments, often £00,000 or more.

Equity crowdfunding investors typically put £,500 to £,000 into a single business, and sometimes much less.

The market has swelled since the early 2010s, after the 2008 global financial crisis hit mainstream sources of capital.

Hundreds of millions are invested by UK investors each year, with Crowdcube and Seedrs raising £24mn in 2021. Popular sectors are fintech and consumer goods.

Challenger bank Monzo and craft beer company BrewDog have raised multiple times on crowdfunding platforms. Where can I invest? Equity crowdfunding has grown in the UK with the emergence of two marketleading platforms, Crowdcube and Seedrs, which together have 90 per cent of the market.

Investors are charged upfront between 1 and 2.49 per cent, while platforms take anywhere up to 7.5 per cent of profits from a successful exit. Fundraising companies pay between 6 and 8 per cent of the money raised by the platform. Am I a typical investor?

Most investors are their late thirties and early forties, but older people too are active.

Alayne Perrott, a retired climate scientist, who has invested in 60 crowdfunding companies, has supported the likes of Salisbury-based Small Robot Co and Scotland's Orbital Marine Power.

Many crowdfunding investors are enthusiastic about climate tech. Perrott says: "I'm determined to translate my deep anxiety about the global climate into action."

Can I lose my money? Start-ups are speculative. Nearly a fifth of companies which raised money on a crowdfunding platform since 2011 have folded, says Beauhurst, a research group.

Success stories include investment app Nutmeg, acquired by JPMorgan for double its value in 2021 only two years after it had raised funds on Crowdcube.

But if a company does well it will need more finance normally from bigger investors. So the interests of crowdfunders can be forgotten.

How much risk is right for me? Don't even think about crowdfunding if you have other more important financial bases to cover. "There's a lot of boxes to tick off before [equity crowdfunding] comes into the picture," says Byrnes, such as paying down debt.

The Financial Conduct Authority advises investors to stake no more than 10 per cent of net investable assets in unlisted companies.

Crowdfunding platforms say they verify claims by companies raising funds against the FCA's "fair, clear and not misleading" guidelines. But disgruntled investors have claimed companies can be overvalued.

Disaffected consumers can raise complaints with the Financial Ombudsman but only where they think there were shortcomings in how an investment was explained.

Crowdcube says it fact checks every pitch against the FCA's guidelines, significantly discounts sales projections and undertakes credit and bankruptcy checks on company directors. But it does not approve or review pitch decks, updates or company forum discussions.

Seedrs chief executive Jeff Kelisky says the company has a team of 30 focused on due diligence, though it cautions it does not require evidence for "aspirational statements" nor take a view on whether ambitions will be realised.

Are there tax benefits? UK residents can benefit from 30 per cent tax relief on crowdfunding investments eligible under the government's Enterprise Investment Scheme. Up to £mn each year must be held for at least three years. Individuals can "carry back" their investments, treating all or part of an investment as if it were purchased in the previous tax year.

Investors can also claim loss relief if a company enters administration or is sold for less than its initial valuation.

headline: BrewDog  James in Dragons' Den rival
author: STUART MacDONALD
text:
BREWDOG boss James Watt is launching a rival to telly's Dragons' Den after being rejected by the BBC show twice.

The craft beer firm's chief executive recently missed out on becoming a Dragon on the series assessing new businesses.

It came after BrewDog unsuccessfully applied for investment on the programme in 2008.

The Aberdeenshirebased drinks giant, started in 2007 with a bank loan and a Prince's Trust grant, is now valued at over £.5billion.

Mr Watt, 40, is now launching a Dragons' Den alternative to help new entrepreneurs seeking start-up finance.

He said: "Some of you may be familiar with the ill-fated story of Brew- Dog and Dragons' Den. "Spurred on by rejection, we came up with a plan to grow and involve our fans as investors."

author: Jane Hamilton

The Scots beer mogul will invest £million of his own money in three firms and will offer the public a chance to go in alongside him.

The competition winners will get a day with James to test their adaptability, including pulling pints at the firm's Camden bar in central London.
