headline: BrewDog  'roof' fail
text:
BREWDOG bosses have lost a battle to build an outdoor canopy at one of their hotels.

The beer firm wanted to install it on a firstfloor terrace at the Doghouse hotel in Edinburgh.

But the council said it would have a "harmful impact" on the architectural character and setting of the listed building.

The Aberdeenshire brewery appealed to the Scottish Government but the refusal was upheld.

headline: Anyone for pickleball?
text:
SIX SURPRISING NEW SUMMER TRENDS

THIS summer's hottest trend is set to be pickleball.

It's not the latest spicy food fad but one of the world's fast-growing sports and, as we revealed this week, Robbie Williams and wife Ayda Field are both big fans of the game that is huge in the US.

It is predicted to hit our shores along with beers for dogs and long-sleeved bikinis.

LAURA STOTT reveals what else we will be doing this summer ... PICKLEBALL THE racquet sport a combination of tennis, badminton and table tennis played over a short net is getting huge.

Suitable for all ages, courts are popping up all over the country. It can be played indoors or out, using lightweight paddle bats and a perforated ball.

Visit Pickleballengland.org for info and your nearest venue.

CHEESY ESPRESSO MARTINIS THE favourite after-dinner cocktail has been given a taste makeover.

Drinks expert Helena Nicklin, from Amazon Prime's hit show The Three Drinkers, says flavoured twists on the coffee tipple will be big.

Variations include fruity Banana-tinis, hazelnut-latte Martinis or, for cheese lovers, a Parmesan Espresso Martini, garnished with a grating of fromage. SUNSET EYESHADOW A SUNSET painted on to your upper lids using a palette of reds, oranges, pinks and golds is the hot make-up look, according to cosmetic giants Clarins.

Celebs including model Cara Delevingne, left, and actors Charlize Theron and Selena Gomez all love the sunset look.

DOGGY BEERS THAT "hair of the dog" pint in the beer garden won't be for you ... it'll be for your mutt.

Brewers have produced a range of canine craft ales, with Bottom Sniffer Dog Beer and Brewski's Brewdog cans sure to get your dog drooling.

But remember the beers are designed especially for our four-legged friends and are all zero per cent ABV, so don't be tempted to share your alcoholic drink with your pooch.

BARBIE BLOOMS HOT pink will be the colour for summer thanks to Margot Robbie's Barbie movie, which will be in cinemas in July.

Pretty pink bouquets are the floral fashion taking hold right now, according to florists Bloom and Wild.

Expect to see hot, pastel and vibrant pink stalks including tulips, roses, hydrangeas, peonies and stocks filling vases and at weddings.

LONG-SLEEVE SWIMSUITS FORGET flashing the flesh, this summer's beach trend is more about covering up.

Sporty, surfing-inspired long-sleeved swimsuits and long-sleeved crop tops paired with bikini bottoms will be the seaside look to be seen in. Eva Longoria and Kim Kardashian, right, have embraced the covered up style but watch out for some interesting tan lines.

headline: Crowdfunder investigates rental start-up over 'fraud'
author: Tom Saunders
text:
A crowdfunding platform that raised millions for the drinks company Brewdog is planning legal action against a Scottish father and son it claims may have misled investors with "sophisticated forms of fraud".

Crowdcube said it had conducted an investigation after claims of discrepancies emerged into how Student Rents, a letting portal headed by Edinburghbased Grant MacCusker and his son, Jamie Stewart, portrayed itself during a fundraiser on the platform.

UKTN, the technology industry website, found that three of the five employees listed on the Crowdcube pitch for Student Rents did not appear to be real people. Images portraying the senior staff were traced back to stock photography available online, while companies on their career history said there was no record of the individuals having worked there.

Student Rents described itself as the "Uber of student rental" and aimed to bring letting agents with property together with students looking for accommodation through one online site.

Crowdcube said: "We are confident that this was an isolated incident involving sophisticated forms of fraud and have every confidence in our due diligence processes, which we regularly review. We appreciate the trust that our investors place in us and will continue to work tirelessly to maintain that trust."

The Student Rents page on Crowdcube stated the company raised £66,000 from 179 investors. However, Crowdcube is planning to take legal action to return £3,172 from 170 investors. According to Crowdcube, this is because Student Rents said it had directly raised £43,000 outside the platform. Crowdcube then attributed the independently raised money towards the total funding round on its platform, which allowed Student Rents to meet its fundraising goal and access the money pledged by investors. Crowdcube has stated it "subsequently emerged that at least some of this extra investment was fabricated" and that this will "form part of the legal case against Student Rents".

MacCusker recently resigned as a director of Lettinglord, the parent company of Student Rents, but documents at Companies House still have him as the majority shareholder.

The legal action against Student Rents comes just a few weeks after MacCusker, 38, claimed he had sold a separate firm, called Letting Cloud, to Airbnb. Airbnb disputed the sale, indicated it had never heard of the company and said that quotes from its employees had been fabricated. Law firms which MacCusker claimed to be working with subsequently said they had no relationship with him.

Stewart, a 2022 finalist for Young Entrepreneur of the Year in Scotland at the Great British Entrepreneur Awards was named as the chief executive of Student Rents last month. He said: "We are working with Crowdcube and all investors are being refunded like Crowdcube has said. We don't appreciate media outlets making misleading headlines on this when the issue at hand is currently being resolved." Mac- Cusker was approached for comment.

headline: Losing Michael and David was tragic. I think about them all the them.
author: JACK KEENE
text:
SAYS WARREN GREATREX

REMEMBER the name Bill Baxter ... so good they've named a beer after him. And given what Warren Greatrex has been through over the last few years, it's fair to say the popular trainer deserved to sink a few cold ones after the horse's Aintree heroics.

Now, if you even have a passing interest in jumps racing then Greatrex is a name you will should be familiar with.

He broke through at the highest possible level when Cole Harden sprung a shock in the World Hurdle at Cheltenham in 2015.

And he continued to swim with the big fishes for a few years, thanks to top-notchers like La Bague Au Roi, One Track Mind and Emitom.

But, as so often the case in this great but unforgiving game, a steep rise can lead to a brutal and sudden fall.

Even the crispest of pints can turn warm and flat eventually and more trainers than you could shake a stick at have experienced it.

Some come out the other side ... some don't.

And, when tragedy strikes away from the racecourse, the job becomes even more difficult.

In the space of seven months, Greatrex's yard was rocked by the deaths of Michael Pitt and David Thompson, two well-liked members of stable staff who took their own lives.

Greatrex, his family and his staff took a long time to get over it and he still thinks about the young men, who were just 19 and 25, every day.

He said: "It was absolutely tragic. Something none of us want to go through again.

"Strangely, I think about both of them and what happened more now than ever before because, at the time, I was busy worrying about everyone else.

"You had to be strong for the families of Michael and David.

"Of course we also have a lot of young staff in the yard who lost two of their mates.

"It was a very tough and emotional time and it still is.

"I take my hat off to my staff and we are a stronger unit now. They could easily have thought, 'we aren't having winners, we have lost two of our mates, I'm out of here'.

"But we all stuck together, I'm thankful to them. Added to that the horses were never right for one reason or another.

BEER WE can of Baxter "Everything was thrown at us but we're still here."

In memory of his lads, Greatrex has become involved with James' Place a charity which runs centres across the country helping to prevent suicide.

And after a torrid time, Bill Baxter's brilliant win over the Grand National fences was even more meanigful to everyone.

It had been far too long between drinks for a trainer of Greatrex's ability and he has now developed such a taste for it he is even preparing his kids' pony for a crack at the Shetland Grand National (yes, really).

It's not just 'Norman' the Shetland who has lofty targets, Topham winner Bill will keep some hot company next season.

Mind you, Greatrex will have to catch him first.

The grey ran his trainer ragged when he tried to collar him for a photograph in his paddock earlier this week.

He performed several drops of the shoulder, the type that would make Lionel Messi proud.

"He is a character, alright. I ride him out myself every day so I have got very attached to him," Greatrex grinned.

Despite his rapid rise through the ranks, Bill Baxter is still only rated 141 a nice mark for a race like the old Hennessy. He said: "Yes, I'm thinking about the Newbury race early doors.

"It is lovely to have a horse where you can look at races like that again. He'll be a right player in that race off that mark."

GO of Bill brew

Greatrex continued: "I can't tell you how much warmth people have shown since . I'm an emotional guy, so it got to me. "One of my old friends Hannah Bickett had some custommade BrewDog beers sent over which I think is one of the best presents I've ever had!

"I tagged BrewDog on Twitter too you never know, they might want to make a deal!

"No doubt, all the Grade 1 wins meant a hell of a lot but, after the last few years, Bill's win was just on another level.

"When you've been down you need to enjoy the big days and, believe me, we enjoyed it."

Talk about bottle, Greatrex and his staff are not short of it.

"Strangely, I think about both of them and what happened more now than ever before because, at the time, I was busy worrying about everyone else.

"You had to be strong for the families of Michael and David.

"Of course we also have a lot of young staff in the yard who lost two of their mates.

"It was a very tough and emotional time and it still is.

"I take my hat off to my staff and we are a stronger unit now. They could easily have thought, 'we aren't having winners, we have lost two of our mates, I'm out of here'.

"But we all stuck together, I'm thankful to them. Added to that the horses were never right for one reason or another.

BEER WE can of Baxter "Everything was thrown at us but we're still here."

In memory of his lads, Greatrex has become involved with James' Place a charity which runs centres across the country helping to prevent suicide.

And after a torrid time, Bill Baxter's brilliant win over the Grand National fences was even more meanigful to everyone.

It had been far too long between drinks for a trainer of Greatrex's ability and he has now developed such a taste for it he is even preparing his kids' pony for a crack at the Shetland Grand National (yes, really).

It's not just 'Norman' the Shetland who has lofty targets, Topham winner Bill will keep some hot company next season.

Mind you, Greatrex will have to catch him first.

The grey ran his trainer ragged when he tried to collar him for a photograph in his paddock earlier this week.

He performed several drops of the shoulder, the type that would make Lionel Messi proud.

"He is a character, alright. I ride him out myself every day so I have got very attached to him," Greatrex grinned.

Despite his rapid rise through the ranks, Bill Baxter is still only rated 141 a nice mark for a race like the old Hennessy. He said: "Yes, I'm thinking about the Newbury race early doors.

"It is lovely to have a horse where you can look at races like that again. He'll be a right player in that race off that mark."

GO of Bill brew

Greatrex continued: "I can't tell you how much warmth people have shown since . I'm an emotional guy, so it got to me. "One of my old friends Hannah Bickett had some custommade BrewDog beers sent over which I think is one of the best presents I've ever had!

"I tagged BrewDog on Twitter too you never know, they might want to make a deal!

"No doubt, all the Grade 1 wins meant a hell of a lot but, after the last few years, Bill's win was just on another level.

"When you've been down you need to enjoy the big days and, believe me, we enjoyed it."

Talk about bottle, Greatrex and his staff are not short of it.

headline: PLONKERS
text:
1RM Saturday, April 29, 2023 ............... 43

PADDY POWER MEDIA PRESENTS

FROM THE HORSE'S MOUTH podfollow.com/horsesmouth

ARSENAL fans have been left crying into their beer after their title dreams were shattered by Manchester City and our latest Paddy Power stunt didn't help their mood.

City's comprehensive 4-1 win against the Gunners on Wednesday night means Pep Guardiola's side are now 1-18 to win the Premier League title, with Mikel Arteta's men drifting to 9-1.

And just in case the Arsenal faithful needed a reminder that they have blown it after being top of the table for most of the season, we dropped by Emirates Stadium to give them a cheeky reminder We toasted the Gunners bottling the title by plonking a giant bottle of plonk outside the ground.

The 18ft inflatable bottle of 'Brut-al' champagne poked fun at their surrender of a commanding eight-point lead, joking that the title of 'Biggest bottlers in North

London' may well sit with Arteta's men ... at least for now.

To complete this story, we teamed up with leading brewers BrewDog to create a limited edition run of beer, launching bespoke bottles of IPA (It's Proper Arsenal).

Cruel stuff, but hopefully it lightened the mood a little for the suffering Arsenal fans as we wait to see if City will be popping corks to celebrate completing the Treble.

They are 4-11 to beat cross-town rivals

United in the FA Cup final and 4-6 to finally win the Champions League. In the league, Fulham are 11-1 to beat them tomorrow.

Erling Haaland is one goal away from 50 in his debut season for City and he is 2-1 to score first at Craven Cottage, while we are offering 4-1 on Kevin De Bruyne assisting the Norwegian for a goal.

We couldn't find any of

Manchester United's 1999 Treblewinning side to comment on City maybe emulating that feat. They must be in hiding so we turned to ex-Old Trafford striker Louis Saha for a reason City might miss out.

And reflecting on their Champions League near-misses in recent years, the Frenchman said: "Their manager tends to make mistakes in the final few weeks. You judge a team when the heat is on.

"The job is to maintain the philosophy and the power that they deliver in each game."

headline: BrewDog  flies high
text:
BREWDOG is to expand into airports and train stations across the UK.

The Scots craft beer firm has partnered with food service company SSP Group and will open its first bar at London Gatwick in December.

BrewDog co-founder James Watt said: "This will bring new craft beer experiences to thousands of travellers."

Jonathan Pollard, of Gatwick said: "BrewDog is a fantastic brand. I'm certain it will be well received by passengers."

BrewDog, based in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, this year announced a partnership with Bud China to open bars in the world's biggest market.

headline: Upper Crust owner rises as travellers boost profits
author: John Abiona
text:
SHARES in Upper Crust owner SSP rose after it swung back into profit and cashed in on a return of passengers jetting off in the skies and hopping on trains.

The FTSE 250 group, which also owns Millie's Cookies, made a profit of £5.8m in the six months to the end of March, having reported a £.3m loss during the same period a year ago.

And revenues of £.3bn were 64.1pc higher than 12 months earlier, the firm said.

SSP hailed strong demand in North America, where sales were ahead of pre-pandemic levels, while the UK's recovery was hindered by rail strikes.

The airline sector was brought to a standstill during the pandemic as strict Covid measures took a toll on the industry.

But with SSP encouraged by the pace of recovery in passengers travelling once again, it now expects its sales and profits for the year to the end of September to be at the top end of forecasts.

The group added that sales in the first six weeks of the second half of its financial year were up 11pc on 2019 levels as holidaymakers prepare to jet off for summer.

The company also teamed up with Brewdog to bring the craft brewer's pubs to railway stations and airports across the UK this year, starting with Gatwick. Shares gained 3.1pc, or 8.2p, to 272.4p.

The FTSE 100 fell 0.1pc, or 8.04 points, to 7762.95 and the FTSE250 lost 0.3pc, or 65.03 points, to 19208.31.

Blue-chip property landlords rallied amid hopes of a recovery in property values.

British Land gained 2.6pc, or 9.3p, to 365.7p, Land Securities rose 0.8pc, or 4.8p, to 636.2p, Segro added 1.3pc, or 10.2p, to 825.2p and Hammerson grew by 2.3pc, or 0.58p, to 26.02p.

RS Group took a hit after it warned that trading has been weaker since April.

The electronics distributor's shares fell 7pc, or 59.6p, to 793.2p.

Analysts at Jefferies said private equity predators could now target the company after its chief executive, finance boss, chief operating officer and head of North America all left in under a year.

Retail stocks sank into the red, with B&M losing 5pc, or 24.3p, to 465.7p, Next down 1.3pc, or 86p, to 6554p and Frasers Group slipping 4.2pc, or 31.5p, to 717p.

Liberum analyst Joachim Klement said traders are worried about inflation remaining higher for longer despite expected falls.

Dowlais, the engineering group that was spun out of Melrose (down 0.5pc, or 2.6p, to 477.9p) last month, cheered after its revenue rose 9pc to £.9bn in the first four months of the year.

While sales in its automotive division increased by 11pc, revenue was flat year-on-year in the power metallurgy business. Despite this, the company reiterated its forecasts for 2023. Shares plunged 7.4pc, or 10.6p, to 132.9p.

News of water regulator Ofwat's investigation into South West Water over leaks and usage data saw shares in the utility firm's owner Pennon slide 2.7pc, or 22p, to 800p.

Drax is planning to push into the US market as it looks to take advantage of President Biden's tax breaks for green energy.

The power generation group -

which said the US had created a 'supportive investment environment' following the Inflation Reduction Act - revealed it was aiming to build two power plants in the southern US which it estimated would cost £.2bn and begin commercial operations in 2030. Shares gained 2.2pc, or 13.4p, to 635p.

Cranswick, one of Britain's major food producers, reported higher annual profit and revenue despite taking a hit from industry-wide labour shortages. Shares rose 5.4pc, or 170p, to 3310p.

headline: BrewDog  is bullish after loss of £6m
author: STUART PATTERSON
text:
BEER giant BrewDog lost £6million last year, its bosses revealed yesterday.

The firm blamed soaring inflation and energy bills plus investments in new bars and £0million spent on their HQ in Ellon, Aberdeenshire.

The last time the craft ale company reported a profit was back in 2019.

And accounts up to the end of 2022 show they got an extension until 2026 on a £6million Covid loan due to be paid back in full this year.

But CEO James Watt insisted their success will continue after revenues hit £20million and are up 20 per cent this year.

He said: "We have an unshakeable confidence in the future of BrewDog.

"Over the past 16 years our business has grown from two men and a dog to the world's biggest craft brewer by revenue.

"It's a trend we believe is destined to continue."

headline: Ale whiff for fellas' armpints
author: RICHARD MORIARTY
text:
DRINKERS can now smoother themselves in the smell of a pint with a beer-scented deodorant.

BrewDog has teamed up with personal care brand Fussy to create the roll-on fragrance ahead of Sunday's Father's Day.

It has notes of hops and tropical fruits similar to the brewery's popular Punk IPA ale.

BrewDog's chief marketing officer Lauren Carrol said: "For years, people have been in turmoil over what to buy Dad on his special day shall I get him smellies?

"Shall we just treat him to his favourite tipple? I say, why not do both.

"This has been a real fun one. It's insane how amazing the tropical aroma is that comes from our first ever beer-odorant. It smells just like the real thing."

The £5 product is available from supermarkets.

headline: Stuck for a Father's Day gift?
author: Carol Midgley
text:
It's Father's Day on Sunday and for years cards and "gift ideas" for men have been execrable, focusing on four tired themes: beer, sport, flatulence and personal hygiene. Hands up the lucky dad who has never received a card saying "World's Best Farter!", a joke that never starts being funny.

Now things are getting worse. BrewDog has combined two themes to make "beer deodorant". As in a £5 deodorant that smells like you've swilled beer under your armpits. Why would anyone want this? I suppose if you'd had a few secret drinks after work you could always tell your spouse: "No, it's not my breath, luv, it's my deodorant."

Given that it's not 1973, I doubt it. Still, it's a better idea than that of Gwyneth Paltrow, who suggested, incredibly, that dads might like a £77 "hands-free vibrator for prostate stimulation" on Goop. Yes, really. What are you waiting for kids? A gift guaranteed to bring tears to his eyes.

headline: No-alcohol beers to say yes to
author: HAYLEY MINN
text:
AS SALES OF 0% TIPPLES TOP DRY JANUARY...

DRINKERS are taking the hangover out of having a beer, with sales of low and noalcohol tipples 25 per cent higher last month than in Dry January.

Drinks expert JOE WADSACK tested the alcohol-free versions of some of DRY BAR our favourite beers in the market to find the best of the bevs.

Here he rates them out of ten on taste and how similar they are to the original.

tester Joe

Budweiser 4 x 330ml, £.25

IF Budweiser wasn't tasteless enough, Bud Zero is even lighter on flavour.

To be fair, making something this neutral is quite difficult, and it is still thirst-quenching and mildly malty.

TASTE 3 SIMILARITY 8

Leffe Blonde 6x250ml, £.50

THE original Leffe Blonde is a big beer with quite a sweet, bold flavour.

The zero per cent version has some nice Belgian beer notes but you do miss the alcohol and richness quite a lot here.

TASTE 4 SIMILARITY 6

Asahi 4x330ml, £.50

WOW! This absolutely blew me away.

Asahi has managed to nail the creamy texture of lager, and this version is delicious.

You could easily sit at your desk at work drinking these all day - and there's no alcohol, so no worries!

TASTE 8 SIMILARITY 10 2nd place

Adnams Ghost Ship 500ml, £.40

AS an Adnams fan, I must say this is rather disappointing. There's too much hop, which means it tastes very unbalanced and bitter, and it doesn't remind me of Ghost Ship at all. It really tastes alcohol-free.

TASTE 5 SIMILARITY 4

San Miguel 4 x 330ml, £.75

A PERFECTLY acceptable alternative if you don't fancy booze.

This really tastes like San Miguel.

It has got that malty, round flavour of the original, which I really enjoyed.

TASTE 7 SIMILARITY 8

Brewdog Punk IPA 4x330ml, £ I MUST say this is a very good New World IPA.

It is well-balanced, doesn't taste too bitter and you can really taste the cascades with Citra hops.

Overall, it's pleasant, but just slightly not as robust as the original. TASTE 8 SIMILARITY 7

Heineken 6 x 330ml, £.45

I DO not like this as much as the alcoholic version.

It's lacking the hoppy definition of Heineken, and is a bit too wishywashy for my liking.

You can definitely taste that the beer is missing with this beverage.

TASTE 4 SIMILARITY 5

Erdinger 500ml, £.60

ERDINGER is my beer of choice, and I'm delighted to say the alcohol-free version is just as delicious.

I reckon if I'd had three or four pints, and was then offered a non-alcoholic one, I don't think I would be able to tell the difference.

TASTE 9 SIMILARITY 10

Winner

headline: BrewDog  bosses going large with global plans to expand
author: Greig Cameron
text:
BrewDog, the Scottish beer and pub chain, is aiming to almost triple the number of outlets it has around the globe by the end of the decade.

The company hopes to have 300 bars running under its banner by 2030, generating revenue of £ billion. The plans will create thousands of new jobs.

Some of the expansion will come from focusing on bigger sites following the model of the 27,000 sq ft BrewDog in Waterloo, London, and the 30,000 sq ft bar in Las Vegas. Both venues are said to be trading far ahead of expectations.

BrewDog expects bar revenue in 2023 to grow by about £6 million, equivalent to a 25 per cent year-on-year rise. Las Vegas, which opened in December 2022, and Waterloo, which began trading in August last year, will be responsible for £0 million of the growth, the company said.

BrewDog also intends to step up its presence in locations such as airports and railway stations, and expand its franchise programme, including a partnership with SSP Group, which runs food and beverage outlets in travel hubs. An opening at London Gatwick is pencilled in for December this year.

India has been identified as a country with the potential for growth, with an ambition to open 25 bars there over the next five years.

BrewDog was founded in Aberdeenshire in 2007 by James Watt and Martin Dickie. It still has its headquarters in Ellon and also runs breweries in Germany, the United States and Australia. The firm employs more than 2,600 people, has about 120 bars and hotels around the world and exports its beer to more than 60 countries. Its total revenue in 2022 was £21 million.

A first US bar franchise, in Denver, Colorado, is to open later this year with outlets in the UK, India, Italy, Netherlands, Thailand, South Korea and China also in the pipeline.

James Brown, the chief executive of BrewDog bars, said the success of Waterloo and Las Vegas had led to landlords offering large spaces in developments. He said: "We have been inundated because of what we have done in terms of customer experience and footfall with those locations."

Brown confirmed that the expansion would also involve further boutique hotels from smaller-scale five-bedroom units to ones with 25 or more rooms.

BrewDog said its recent pub openings in London Waterloo and Las Vegas had added £0million of revenues alone. It claims the London bar is the capital's biggest, with 60 taps of draught beer.

The business hopes to open 25 new bars in India over the next five years, and more in the UK, Italy, Netherlands, Australia, USA and Thailand. Boss James Watt said: "Fifteen years ago, we opened our first venue in Aberdeen and since then we have opened over 100 across the globe." Investing in high footfall locations was key, he said. headline: BREWDOG  ALE'S DOUBLE TROUBLE
author: stuart patterson
text:
THE boss of a tiny brewDog would "squash" her brand after the Scots giant launched a similarly named ale.

Sonja Mitchell started selling alcoholfree Shore Leave lager at her firm Jump Ship Brewing last year.

She told how she was left stunned when Aberdeenshirebased BrewDog launched a beer with the same title, using the slogan: "It's time to jump ship."

Edinburgh-based Sonja said: "It's disappointing. To use that line is really squash- ing our brand. As a small business it's really uncomfortable." Sonja, who has two staff, had trademarked Jump Ship but failed to do so for Shore Leave.

The similarity emerged when BrewDog co-founder James Watt shared drink promo material. But he said the slogan was just a "quick mock-up".

BrewDog said: "We were unaware of Jump Ship Brewing. When it was drawn to our attention we quickly decided to drop the line 'Time to Jump Ship'. It will be replaced with 'Rock the Boat'."

headline: Now that's a nice job hopportunity
author: DANIELLE MASTERSON
text:
BEST GIG IN THE WORLD.. PROBABLY

ALDI WANT BEER TASTER

ALDI is on the hunt for an "Official Beer Taster" with a "genuine" beer passion to review its new booze range.

And the unpaid taster will help decide what beers hit the shelves in Ireland this autumn.

Budding beer testers are asked to send a few words on why they would be perfect for the un-beer-lievable role, their social media links and their favourite Aldi beer.

The lucky applicant will receive a curated selection of 12 beers in exchange for their brew-tally honest feedback around each beer's taste, flavour, and appearance.

ALDI WANT

The beer taster will also take part in a photoshoot in late August.

John Curtin, Group Buying Director at Aldi Ireland, said: "We are looking for someone with a genuine passion for beer to sample and review Aldi's range of craft beer.

"Ireland has a rich brewing culture so we cannot wait to hear from beer enthusiasts from across the country.

"The winner's feedback will help us curate and grow our range in line with consumer tastes.

"We've seen the price of a pint steadily increase across Ireland over the past number of years and at Aldi we pride ourselves on offering great quality beers, both Irish and international, at a fraction of the price."

Hopefuls, or hop-fuls, have until August 21 to apply. The winner will be notified by August 25 with samples to follow for review.

BEER TASTER

Some of Aldi's popular beers include Dundalk Bay Brewery's award-winning Sailor Sam Hazy IPA, Sligo's Whistle Mountain IPA, Roadworks Pale Ale, left, and Brown Bear Double IPA.

From August 3, Aldi will also be launching a new collaboration with Brewdog.

To apply for the Official Beer Taster role, send an email to beertaster@aldi.ie with your name, proof of age and a 150- word explanation as to why you should be chosen for the role. lFurther details can be found at www.aldi.ie/beer-taster-competition.

headline: The critic's top 12 low-alcohol drinks
author: Jane MacQuitty
text:
Wine Por do Sol. Vinho Verde Portugal, 9 per cent Tesco, £.25 Portugal's sparky vinho verde makes a good spritzy summer white, but few clock in as low as this lively, leafy, off-dry, grapefruit-juicy 9 per center.

2020 The Doctors' Pinot Noir New Zealand, 9.5 per cent gerrardseel.co.uk, £5.99 Hats off to the Forrest family and their meaty mocha and tobacco leaf-scented savoury pinot noir.

Moscato Roséoldova, 9 per cent, Morrisons, £ Moldova's luscious, grapey, musky vegan-approved pink muscat makes a brilliant base for a summer cup, or try it sploshed over summer berries.

2022 Domaine Tariquet L'Impréu France, 9.5 per cent thewinesociety.com, £.75 Light, lemony dry Gascon white from one of the best southwest producers, Grassa, mostly ugni blanc but zhuzhed up with a zingy dash of riesling.

2021 Dr Loosen Üziger Wüzgarten Riesling Kabinett Mosel, Germany, 8 per cent Waitrose, £5.99 Ernie Loosen's superior Kabinettlevel off-dry riesling from the Wüzgarten's steep slopes bursts with crunchy green apple and citrus-zest fruit.

2022 Canti Liberty Asti Italy, 8 per cent, Sainsbury's, £ Don't turn your nose up at Italy's sweet Asti. The better bottles including Canti's strong, intensely fruity, hot-house grape-scented 2022 served ice-cold, make a great pudding wine.

Beer The Kernel Brewery Table Beer 3 per cent, thekernelbrewery.com £.25 (500ml bottle) A scrumptious, bright, hoppy, yeasty, floral beer, good with or without food, and brewed fresh every week with a changing rota of hops.

The Original Small Beer Session Pale 2.5 per cent, Waitrose £.20 (350ml bottle) Maltier than you would expect from a session beer, but that whack of rich flavour means you won't notice the low alcohol.

Beavertown Nanobot Super Session IPA 2.8 per cent, Majestic £.40 (4 x 330ml cans) Wacky can with a wacky but easy-swigging light, hazy session beer within, made from barley, wheat and oats with a blast of citrus and mango.

Shandy Shack Rhubarb Lager 2.5 per cent, Sainsbury's £.75 (330ml can This faintly pink, rhubarb and custard-styled 2.5 per center is surprisingly summery and refreshing.

Brewdog Lightspeed Hazy IPA 4 per cent, Tesco £.50 (4 x 330ml cans) Another favourite cloudy, exotic summer beer with brilliant, explode in your mouth, peachy, apricot kernel, exotic fruit pizzazz.

Rekorderlig Botanicals Rhubarb-Lemon-Mint Pear Cider 4 per cent, botiga.co.uk £.60 (330ml bottle) A combination of pear cider, spiked with rhubarb, lemon and mint, makes for a sweet, summery Swedish swig.

My four easy ways to cut your units Make a spritzer even with red wine Cutting your favourite ordinary white, pink or red wine with sparkling water and ice cubes is an easy way to cut your alcohol consumption. Step up the flavour by adding a squirt of fresh lemon or lime juice, plus a wedge of the same fruit. Orange and grapefruit work just as well, along with fresh cucumber in a white wine spritzer, or sliced strawberry in a glass of fizzy rosé Fresh herbs pump up the flavour another notch, with borage and mint the best summery options.

No need to finish the bottle here's how I save wine There's no need to fret over half-finished good bottles simply invest in some empty glass half-bottles (you can get them at Ikea and Amazon) and decant what's left over into one or both. Stopper with a cork and keep in the fridge, or somewhere cool and dark; the contents will stay fresh for a good three days.

Invest in a sparkling wine stopper Forget that old chestnut of dropping a silver teaspoon into the neck of your sparkling wine bottle. The only sure-fire way of keeping the bubbles in your bottle is to invest in a sparkling wine stopper. The rubber inside cheaper models tends to perish so invest in a superior version, such as The Wine Society's £.95 edition.

Swap champagne for English fizz Does good low-alcohol champagne exist? No! Most weigh in at about 12-12.5 per cent alcohol. New world, champagne method sparklers are more alcoholic still, at about 13.5 per cent. If you want bubbles with less alcohol, switch to English sparkling wine or prosecco (both about 11 per cent).

Three-low alcohol cocktails (half the units)

By the mixologist Ryan Chetiyawardana, aka Mr Lyan

The cobbler (1 unit, 6% abv) Ingredients 60ml fortified wine (eg Tio Pepe fino sherry or Noilly Prat Ambréermouth) 1 slice lemon 1 slice orange 1 slice pink grapefruit 1 cube pineapple 5ml passion fruit syrup 1 dash absinthe Raspberries and mint, to garnish

Method Muddle the fruits together in the bottom of a shaker, add the other ingredients, shake with ice, decant into a sling or wine glass, add more ice and stir. Cap with crushed ice, then garnish with raspberries and a sprig of mint.

Elderflower radler (1 unit, 3% abv) Ingredients 1 tsp lemon juice 1 tsp lime juice 15ml elderflower cordial 10ml tequila Lager or pale ale Cucumber, to garnish

Method Shake the ingredients together with ice, then strain into a highball glass. Top with cold lager, or a light pale ale, and garnish with a long strip of cucumber.

East 8 Spritz (1.5 units, 8% abv) Ingredients 50ml Aperol 50ml pineapple-infused white wine 15ml passion fruit syrup Soda water or sparkling wine Orange wedge, pineapple slice and rosemary sprig, to garnish

Method Infuse 500ml of dry white wine with fresh pineapple in the fridge for 4 hours. Pour the Aperol, wine and syrup over cubed ice in a wine glass and top with soda or sparkling wine. Garnish with the orange, pineapple and rosemary. (mrlyan.com)

headline: Unhappy Hour: Pub chain charges more at busy times
author: City Reporter
text:
BRITAIN'S biggest pub chain is charging punters more for drinks when its bars are busier.

Stonegate, which owns Be At One, Yates and Slug and Lettuce, said the 'dynamic pricing' scheme will help to cover extra costs at times of higher demand in its outlets.

The scheme has been rolled out at around 800 Stonegate sites across Britain, with customers forking out more for drinks at peak times including during sports events and at weekends.

The price increases are around 20p more for a pint of beer at busy weekends at some pubs, according to The Daily Telegraph. Stonegate, which runs more than 4,500 licensed venues, said that the price rises help to cover 'increased cost demands' that come with a packed venue - including washing more glasses and additional door security measures.

A spokesman for the firm added 'dynamic pricing' also means the pub company can offer drinkers 'happy hours, 2 for 1 cocktails, and discounts on food and drink'.

Some customers have hit out at the strategy on social media. 'At a time of rising cost pressures, this "dynamic pricing" smacks of ripping customers off,' one disgruntled consumer wrote on X, formerly known as Twitter. The cost of a pint has already increased for most pubgoers since the Russian invasion of Ukraine, which saw food and drink prices surge as a result.

Last month Scottish brewer BrewDog introduced a pricing gimmick at its bars, promising to reduce the price of its cold beer as temperatures increase.

headline: Brew love with Toff

headline: You just can't top ham and a good bitter
text:
SUN FOODIE MATCHES PIZZA WITH BEERS

BEER and pizza are so popular that Monday is a national day dedicated to the combination.

But if you're not putting much thought into which ale you match to which slice, foodie and Blur bassist Alex James reckons you're missing a trick.

Get it right, and your choice of tipple could bring out the flavours of your dough toppings and make even the most controversial options, such as pineapple, a dream on the taste buds.

Here is his guide

TOMATO pair with NEWCASTLE BROWN ALE

THE passata on the pizza base gives plenty of the natural savouriness and zip that tomatoes deliver. But a few slices of beef tomato can add a very pleasant texture to your mouthful.

If you're going to load up on tomatoes, try a brown ale, maybe Newcastle Brown in honour of Eddie Howe's boys giving a thumping to Mbappénd Paris St-Germain in footie's Champions League this week.

Bon appéit!

HAM pair with OLD HOOKY BITTER

HAM is a great companion to so many drinks, it works with anything from sweet lemonade to the driest Champagne and even a hot cuppa.

A pint of bitter and a ham sandwich is all I need from a pub lunch, so I would go with a local bitter alongside a ham pizza.

Many like their bitter warm, but I'd go with hot pizza and an ice-cold beer.

I love the hot/cold sensation I can feel on the tongue.

PINEAPPLE pair with ST OMER SHANDY BOTTLES

I LOVE pineapple on pizza, not only because it annoys Italians so much but because it just goes to show how many directions you can take the basic slice.

I'd ramp up the sweetness even further with a classic beer and lemonade mix a shandy on the rocks. Or you could go for a harmonic counterpoint with something crazy like a barley wine. This is the great thing about beer and pizza it shouldn't work, but it does.

SWEETCORN pair with CAMDEN PILS

I NEVER like to be without a stash of tinned or frozen corn, but it's in season right now and when it's fresh it's one of the great delicacies.

If you can find them with their husks still on just bake them as is in a hot oven (200C) for 30 mins then discard the skins, strip off the kernels with a sharp knife and you've got a world of sprinkled deliciousness at your fingertips.

A Pilsner lager works magnificently with a pizza enhanced by just-baked sweetcorn sprinkles.

PEPPERONI pair with CORONA AND LIME CHUNK

THE signature dish of New York City, pepperoni pizza is available on every other street corner in Mahattan for two or three bucks a slice.

The cheap and cheerful classic has benefitted from a high-end makeover in recent years. A wood-fired crispy crust slice with salad leaves sprinkled on to soak up the juices is a thing of wonder.

Whether you're going high end or high street, you'll want something zingy to cut through the wonderful oiliness of the salami. A light lager with a wedge of lime in the neck, Sol or Corona-style, will do the job. Tuck in and you're basically on a mini-break, not just a lunch break.

ONIONS pair with THEAKSTON DARK MILD

A MARVELLOUS and versatile ingredient, the humble onion delivers mighty amounts of heart and savouriness to any dish.

I'd be tempted to try a glass of mild with an onionheavy pizza.

The caramel nuttiness of the classic brew will set your onions off a treat.

CHILLIS pair with HAWKSTONE LAGER

THE heat of chilli adds a whole extra dimension to pizza. Everyone has their own preference for how much heat so I tend to let them help themselves from a bowl of chopped fresh chillis on the table.

Even just a slice or two will cut right through the oiliness of the cheese and bring the zing to the doughiest of mouthfuls.

Sprinkle 'em on and have plenty of lager ready whatever you'd have with a spicy Indian.

Hawkstone would suit me.

MUSHROOMS pair with GUINNESS

WE either love or hate them, so perhaps surprisingly, mushrooms were voted Brits' favourite pizza topping of all in a recent survey.

If you're a fungi fan, try sprucing up a basic, shop-bought margherita with all your favourites, sliced and tossed in a dash of vegetable oil chestnut, portabello, anything goes.

The earthy savouriness of mushrooms connects well with rich, dark stouts such as Guinness. If you serve it ice-cold in a glass that's been kept in a freezer for an hour or two, it'll really hit the spot.

EGG pair with BREWDOG PUNK IPA

PERHAPS even more divisive than mushrooms and more controversial than pineapple chunks, I love an egg on a pizza.

Two or three quails eggs added halfway through cooking works really well.

I particularly like Coca- Cola with Fiorentina (egg and spinach) pizza and Coke's got crazy amounts of sugar and hints of vanilla and cinnamon that really shouldn't fly, but it just works. I'd lean towards something with a bit of bite, such as an India Pale Ale.

FISH pair with HEINEKEN

TUNA and, classically, anchovies are the seafood toppings of choice. Salty fish and cheese shouldn't work but they do, and this is all part of the magic of pizza being able to bring farreaching flavours together.

If you make your own pizzas, and everyone should, a pizza base strewn with anchovy fillets is wonderful. Fish and cheese is a powerful mouthful, so I'd go for something light a classic lager that will stand back and let the flavours do the talking.

headline: Buy your pint with a Tesco Clubcard
author: LAUREN KELLY
text:
A PUB chain is letting punters pay for pints with a Tesco Clubcard.

BrewDog knocks £ off a drink for every 50p worth of points. That means drinkers can buy a £ pint with 300 points.

Pubs run by the Scots craft brewer are the only bars or restaurants offering booze and food as part of a Clubcard reward partnership.

Founder James Watt said: "With an existing customer base of over 21million active Clubcard members, we're delighted to be the only restaurant partner to offer pints in return for points.

"Everyone's feeling the cost of living squeeze so if we can help people with the cost of our beer, where possible, you can bet your pint on it that we will."

Some BrewDog boozers are excluded from the deal, including all those in Edinburgh from December 13-31.

BrewDog has become the world's 19th most valuable beer brand, ahead of giants such as Stella Artois.

headline: YOU McNUGGETS
author: STUART PATTERSON
text:
£M DRIVE-THRU IS BINNED

Council blasts burger chain's 'woeful' plans

CHEESED-off officials have binned a bid for a McDonald's drivethru slamming the burger giant's "woeful" proposals.

Planners and roads chiefs flagged a host of gripes, ruling the £million design was too grand with insufficient parking.

The row came after McDonald's earmarked empty land near beer giant BrewDog's HQ in Ellon, Aberdeenshire.

Local fast food fans had backed the application as the next nearest McDonald's is 13 miles away in Aberdeen.

But officials said the site wasn't big enough while 31 parking bays were needed rather than the 18 proposed.

They also blasted "woefully inadequate" plans to have lorries drop off supplies via the main car park three times a week, saying it could see queues spill on to nearby roads, risking traffic chaos.

And council experts also slammed McDonald's comparisons with similar developments in London.

Planners said: "Standard practice in the use of trip comparison data is to exclude London sites as not being representative for other parts of the country.

"None of the study parameters was scoped in advance with Aberdeenshire Council.

"The Ellon site would have insufficient parking."

McDonald's pulled their bid before it could be put before councillors but have vowed to return with revamped plans.

The chain's Andrew Crewther said bosses had been "extremely encouraged by positive feedback". He added: "We look forward to presenting these revised proposals."

stuart.patterson@thesun.co.uk

headline: Joys from the black stuff
author: HELENA NICKLIN
text:
WE TEST PRETENDERS TO GUINNESS CROWN

GUINNESS has long been the go-to brand for stout sippers, but now the famous Dublin ale is facing competition as younger drinkers turn to different dark tipples. Here, drinks expert HELENA NICKLIN gives marks out of five as she tests the latest contenders to the throne.

Guinness Original Extra £.50, 500ml, Sainsbury's

GUINNESS Original from the bottle is a very different animal to the dreamy, draft style you get in cans and from the tap. A bottled Guinness is crisper, has bolder bubbles and delivers a yummy, yeasty fruitiness. Great for food pairing. Lively and lovely.

5

Kirkstall Tiramisu £, 440ml, Asda

THIS dessert-inspired tiramisu-themed tipple has a frothy head but smells less like the espresso and chocolate you'd expect and more like a green pepper. There's a bit of dusty coffee bean and a random note of amaretto, but no chocolate. The balance is just off. 1

Brewdog Black Heart £.85, 440ml, Tesco

POP open the contemporary can and the ring pull sound is satisfying and the pour super silky. With a decent head that's creamy, not bubbly, it's a little lighter than a pub-poured pint. It's elegant though - and there's a subtle cocoa bean nose and bitter kick when you glug.

4

Vocation Double Tonka £.50, 440ml, Tesco

THE tonka-themed ale tastes nothing like you might expect. This stout is intensely strong and a little strange. Less about latte and vanilla beans and more about gingerbread, dried cherry and burnt raisins soaked in brandy. And at an 8 per cent abv, it's extremely boozy.

3

Velvet £.50, 330ml, Asda

ASDA'S own stout is on the rustic side. The pour is more like a cola and the frothy head disappears quickly. It smells good, with an old-school malty, dark chocolate vibe. In the mouth, it's spritzy but rich and the tastes are concentrated. However, the flavours don't linger.

2

Northern Monk Pumpkin £, 440ml, Tesco

A PORTER is a heavily hopped brown malt drink. Not exactly a natural nose with its strong cinnamon and rose petal appeal but there's real pumpkin flavour and aroma too. Thankfully, it's more subtle than it smells. Not one for stout purists but a worthy limited edition.

3

Black Sheep Milk £.50, 500ml, Morrisons

MILK stouts are roasted malts topped up with lactose, which gives them a soothing sweetness. This sip is creamy and cosy with the balance bang on - from the moreish, malted milk notes to the subtle bitterness at the end. Immensely refreshing and drinkable, it's fabulous.

5

Kakawa Chocolate £.85, 440ml, Lidl

HAS A decent head that hangs around a good while. It smells like a bar of fruit and nut chocolate, although a little less sweet. With a tiny touch of creaminess and a good, long length of flavour, this silky ale is a well-priced decent drop and worth a try for something different.

4

headline: Beer company's legal battle with  BrewDog
author: Greig Cameron
text:
A small Scottish brewery is taking BrewDog to court in an intellectual property dispute over an allegedly stolen beer name.

Jump Ship Brewing has taken the company to the Intellectual Property Enterprise Court in London and wants to get its larger rival to stop selling an ale called Shore Leave.

Edinburgh-based Jump Ship, a nonalcoholic beer specialist founded in 2019, had launched a rhubarb and ginger pale ale under the Shore Leave moniker in June last year. It followed that up with a separate flavour, chuckleberry sour, in May this year.

Then it emerged BrewDog had filed a trademark for Shore Leave in the spring of this year and James Watt, its co-founder, shared can designs on social media. There was also a mockedup advert with a slogan, "It's time to jump ship".

At the time BrewDog said it had been unaware of Jump Ship and subsequently dropped the phrase from any marketing of its Shore Leave pale ale.

That product was released in September this year, with BrewDog also selling T-shirts, mugs and beanie hats with its branding.

Sonja Mitchell, the founder of Jump Ship, said cease and desist letters were sent to BrewDog at the end of August but there had been no response so she decided to take further action. The claim at the court is seeking an injunction to stop BrewDog from selling the Shore Leave beer on the basis that the public could be deceived into thinking there is a commercial connection with the Jump Ship product.

BrewDog was started in Fraserburgh by Watt and his school friend Martin Dickie in 2007. It now runs more than 100 bars and had revenue of £21 million in its most recent financial year.

Its most famous beers are Punk Ipa, Elvis Juice, Dead Pony Club and Hazy Jane, while it also has a spirits range including Lone Wolf gin, Abstrakt vodka and Duo rum.

It has been involved in intellectual property disputes before, including with the estate of Elvis Presley regarding Elvis Juice and the family-run Lone Wolf pub in Birmingham.

Mitchell said she was "incredibly disappointed" to have to resort to legal means to try to resolve the situation but felt she "had to take a stand".

A spokesman for BrewDog said: "As the registered owner of the Shore Leave trademark in the UK, we are obviously surprised at this action, but also disappointed.

"Ms Mitchell might have challenged our trademark application months ago, as is standard practice, but chose not to." The spokesman added: "We've sought to settle this matter amicably and offered a range of fantastic collab ideas which would have had a huge boost for Ms Mitchell's brewery."

headline: Ale see  BrewDog  in court on name
author: STUART PATTERSON
text:
THE boss of a tiny alcohol-free drinks firm is taking legal action against BrewDog over its beer of the same name.

Sonja Mitchell wants the Intellectual Property Enterprise Court to order the Aberdeenshire brewery to change the name of its tipple Shore Leave.

We told how she began selling her zero-booze version at her firm Jump Ship Brewing last year.

The Edinburgh-based mum was riled when BrewDog later launched its ale. Sonja, who didn't trademark Shore Leave, said: "I am disappointed that despite contacting BrewDog, I am forced down the legal route.

"We have put considerable investment into the Shore Leave series. We can't afford to lose that."

BrewDog said: "As the registered owner of the Shore Mark trademark in the UK, we've sought to settle this amicably."

headline: Be your own Chancellor
author: HARRIET COOKE
text:
MINIBUDGET SPECIAL

SIX TIPS THAT COULD SAVE YOU £5k

Tax

CHANCELLOR Jeremy Hunt's Autumn Statement has delivered a New Year boost for millions.

He trimmed National Insurance, raised state pension payments and froze duties on booze and fuel.

But many will feel no better off.

The overall tax burden is on course for a postwar record, and prices in shops continue to rise. To balance the books you will need to be your own Chancellor.

Mortgages Debts

HARRIET COOKE has six tips - to save you more than £5,000.

TTAaXx

SAVING: £,256

A TWO percentage point reduction in National Insurance means a worker on the average salary of £5,000 will be £50 a year better off from January 6. From April, self-employed workers will save an average £50.

But income tax thresholds are frozen until 2028. So millions more workers will pay tax on more of their earnings. You can soften the blow by maxing out your tax relief. If you earn less than £2,570, marriage allowance lets you transfer up to £,260 of tax-free personal allowance to your spouse. It's worth up to £52 a year.

Mortgages Debts

Claim for this tax year and each one back to April 2019 to net £,256. Most parents qualify for up to £,000 a year for each child under 12 to help with childcare costs. Claim both childcare costs and marriage allowance at gov.uk.

FUEL SAVING: £00 Fuel

DRIVERS spend an average £,225 a year on fuel, reckons finance website NimbleFins.

Around a third of that goes on fuel duty, now on hold at 52.95p per litre until next March.

Do not pay more at the pumps than you have to. By filling up at a supermarket instead of on the motorway you can save around £3 a go for a small car, based on the latest RAC fuel-tracker findings.

The app PetrolPrices helps you find cheaper fuel near you, and claims to save motorists more than £00 a year on petrol and diesel.

Benets

BENEFITS SAVING: £,500

ADULTS on Universal Credit will receive an extra £70 from next April, while the state pension will rise by £00 a year, to £1,541.90.

Fuel

Check what benefits you are entitled to using a calculator like the one at turn2us.org.uk. OAPs may qualify for pension credit, which is worth an average £7 a week or £,500 a year. Some 880,000 households are eligible for this but fail to claim.

Pension credit also makes you eligible for a free TV licence and help with heating. Michael

Clarke, a benefits expert at charity Turn2Us, says: "A single pensioner getting by on less than £01.05 a week, or a couple with under £06.85 a week, could be eligible even if they're homeowners with savings of up to £0,000."

BOOZE SAVING: £12

PUB-goers can raise a glass to celebrate after alcohol duty was frozen until August 2024 - in a victory for The Sun's Save Our Sups campaign. But you could toast the changes for free. Tesco Clubcard holders get discounts on drinks and food in nearly all BrewDog UK branches, with £ off your bill for every 50 points spent.

For 300 points, you could enjoy a £ pint (you'd probably get two for £ outside the South East!). Get a free spritz cocktail by downloading the All Bar One app, or a bottle of Rekorderlig cider with the Nicholson's pub app. Virgin Media and O2 customers can get two free drinks every Thursday at O'Neill's, Ember Inns or Sizzling pubs in England and Wales from 5pm to 8pm via the Priority app. By keeping a lookout for offers such as these, you could get a free drink each week and save around £12 a year.

Tax

Tax Mortgages DEBTS Savings BeneFuel

Andrew Montlake, of broker Coreco, says: "If you are on a fixed rate mortgage deal that's coming to an end, you're likely to face a payment shock. Don't let your mortgage roll on to your lender's standard rate. Speak to a professional mortgage adviser to find the best new deal."

It means repayments of £4,115 per year on a £50,000 mortgage.
