headline: FUN CALENDARS.. .. BUT DRAM PRICEY
author: CHRIS TAYLOR
text:
BOOZE, biscuits and chocs are among Advent calendar treats Scots can enjoy in the countdown to Christmas.

The festive staples provide a chance to lap up local produce on each of the first 25 days of December.

But fans of our national drink will have to dig deep for the offering from the Really Good Whisky Company priced at a sobering £95.

It features 25 classic nips that include a 55-year-old Invergordon 1965 XOP plus a Glenfarclas 26, 25-year-old Glengoyne and three-decade aged Bunnahabhain.

The firm's brand manager Dani Malka said: "They were each selected by our team of experts to provide the ultimate experience for any whisky connoisseur."

Cosmetics maker Arran has released a £0 calendar that has around £50 worth of their fragrances plus scented candles and soaps.

Heaviest gift is a 10kg box of real ale cans sold by beer giants BrewDog for £9.95.

The Aberdeenshire brewery's selection includes the seasonal tipples Mistletoe Mafia lager, Santa Paws IPA and North Pole pale ale.

Those with a sweet tooth can pick up a hand-crafted Christmas tree for £9.99 which you can stuff with your favourite treats. The design has spaces for 24 Tunnock's

Caramel Wafers plus a Tea Cake for the big day.

Or for £2 you can take your time scoffing 25 delicicious sweets made by hand at family-run Chocolates of Glenshiel in Inverness-shire.

There are alcohol-free and vegan varieties of the chocs, all featuring local ingredients.

Or, for £0.50, Paper Tiger has a plainer version with scenes such as Santa at Edinburgh's Scott Monument.

A VisitScotland spokesman said: "Advent calendars such as these are a fun way to celebrate the festive season, support Scots businesses and enjoy a wee taste of our country's great produce."

headline: Protect your brand or end up canned
author: Shaf Rasul
text:
1SM Monday, November

Shaf Rasul One in a million shaf@the-sun.co.uk

ENTREPRENEURSHIP is often the lifeblood of us Scots.

We have a rich history of innovation and success, taking the green shoots of an idea and building it into a fullyfledged, profitable product or service.

We are resilient and determined, so in the challenging and uncertain world of entrepreneurship, persistence and the ability to overcome any obstacles are crucial if we are to succeed.

Take it from someone who has been there. You can be sailing along nicely with a brilliant plan, only to find yourself in a storm where you begin to question yourself on whether it's worth it at all.

I was reminded of this due to a story that appeared last week regarding a small independent brewery, Jump Ship Brewing, which has launched a legal challenge against industry behemoth BrewDog over a product name dispute.

The small craft brewery is Scotland's only woman-owned, non-alcoholic brewing company, run by Sonja Mitchell, and boasting a core range of four beers, from lager to IPA.

I love a brand with a great story behind it, and the brewing company is no different, with Sonja being a keen sailor, beer-lover and mother of three deciding to "jump ship" from her marketing job to launch the brewery in 2019, due to enjoying a beer, but not the associated hangover.

Avoiding confusion As I've often touched on within my column, the importance of a brand name is crucial. It's shorthand for the value, quality, and reputation associated with the brand so protecting that brand is essential if you are to be successful.

In a crowded market especially such as alcohol, which is flooded with products a unique and well-crafted brand name helps distinguish yourself from your competitors and sets the brand apart, avoiding confusion from consumers.

The Scottish non-alcoholic brewery launched Shore Leave beer in mid-2022, with a second version, a berry-fermented sour beer, released earlier this year.

However, the Jump Ship Brewing owner claims that she came across Brew- Dog's intention to introduce a beer named

Shore Leave with the tagline, "It's time to jump ship" in July 2023. Mitchell asserted that BrewDog's choice of the name Shore Leave for its new ale posed a potential risk of confusion among distributors and consumers. This confusion, she argued, could lead to financial harm to the Jump Ship brand.

Following Mitchell's request, BrewDog abandoned the jump ship tagline but was unwilling to alter the beer's name.

Despite her direct communication with BrewDog, Mitchell found it necessary to pursue legal action to protect the substantial investments made by her and her team in the development, branding, packaging and distribution of the Shore Leave

BREWERY BOSS ... Sonja Mitchell series of beers. Mitchell emphasised the importance of safeguarding these investments, stating: "We cannot afford to lose that."

Protecting your brand name is essential for small businesses to differentiate themselves, attract customers, and achieve sustainable growth. By safeguarding your brand, small businesses can ensure legal protection, foster consumer trust, and help the long-term success of their brand in the marketplace.

Unfortunately, it appears that Jump Ship Brewing did not challenge the trademark when BrewDog applied for the Shore Leave name in March 2023, after the first Jump Ship Shore Leave beer was released, but before its second release in

May 2023. With no opposition to the trademarking raised at the time, the mark was registered in June 2023 to BrewDog a fact they were quick out the traps to point out when questioned on the legal action.

BrewDog stated it was "surprised" with the legal action, pointing to its ownership of the Shore Leave trademark in relation to beer in the UK.

It will be interesting to see how this pans out in the coming months and whether Sonja Mitchell remains the course.

But for entrepreneurs, it serves as a timely reminder that trademarking intellectual property is an essential, proactive and strategic step to secure your business identity, reputation and investment.

headline: AIR OF THE DOG
author: JORDAN TENNANT
text:
BREWDOG'S James Watt checks in to his new airport boozer hoping it will be a runway success.

The tycoon, 39, launched the bar at Gatwick with reality star girlfriend Georgia Toffolo, 29. They also posed for photo-booth snaps, above.

It's the air-craft beer revolution.

headline: I'LL DRIVE BREW
author: RUTH WARRANDER
text:
Beer boss & Toff's 90-mile Mazda trip as jet diverted

BREWDOG boss James Watt drove posh lover Georgia Toffolo to his home in a rental Mazda.

The beer tycoon, 41, had to hire the budget motor at Inverness Airport after high winds diverted the pair's flight north to Aberdeen.

But I'm a Celebrity winner Toff, 29, had plenty of fun on the 90-mile road trip to her boyfriend's Aberdeenshire pad. She told her 1.7million Instagram followers: "Good news is, we're in Scotland, bad news is we are nowhere near where James lives, we're in Inverness.

"But we've got a naughty little rental car look at you, a Mazda. Looking good darling."

She then poked fun at Watt for "not knowing how to drive a manual" car and causing her to gag after "burning out the clutch". The couple, who started dating in the summer, stopped off at luxury clothing outfitters Johnstons of Elgin.

But they left with just a takeaway lunch of quiche, salad and a scone, which Toff rated as "Ten out of ten".

And after three hours they finally made it to Watts' home, near the Brewdog HQ in Ellon, Aberdeenshire. We told last month how the businessman met his girlfriend's family while on a trip to Exeter, Devon.

Watt treated them to burgers and booze at one of his firm's pubs before he and Toff jetted off to the Maldives for a holiday. He is thought to have offered his expertise to help her launch pet food brand Wild Pack. The multi-millionaire divorcee has two daughters.

ruth.warrander@the-sun.co.uk

headline: The 'healthy' zero-alcohol beers packed with sugar
author: Andy Dolan
text:
FOR anyone who's been nursing a sore head over the past few days, the thought of low-alcohol beer may be a more enticing one than usual.

However, while sales of the drinks have been booming among increasingly health-conscious consumers, a survey by the Mail has discovered that they might still leave a sour taste in the mouth as far as their 'benefits' are concerned.

For while low-alcohol and zero-alcohol drinks typically contain less than half the calories of their full-bodied versions, some contain up to ten times the sugar.

Our study found that, while a regular can of beer such as BrewDog IPA contains negligible amounts of sugar, alcohol-free versions from the same brewery can have 6g per 330ml can or bottle - the equivalent of a teaspoon and a half of sugar.

Old Speckled Hen Low Alcohol, meanwhile, contains 2g of sugar per 100ml, compared with just 0.2g in its regular equivalent. Faye Thompson, a nutritional therapist, said: 'Reducing alcohol is great, but the pay-off in switching to non-alcoholic beer is the higher sugar content.

'Sugar is the real culprit, not fat, when it comes to weight gain.'

Ms Thompson, of Lift Nutrition in Manchester, said drinkers should check the labels for sugar content. Adnams Ghost Ship 0.5 per cent, for example contains just 0.1g of sugar per 100ml, she said, while

Big Drop Paradiso has less than 0.5g. Brewers said the varying sugar contents were the result of the different processes used to produce low alcohol beer.

Johnny Clayton, of Big Drop, said: 'In a full strength beer, the alcohol is formed by the yeast eating the sugar extracted from the malted barley. This turns into alcohol via fermentation. With some low alcohol beers brands an excess of sugar does not get converted and remains after fermentation. We do it differently.'

headline: Coming to movie screens beer you
author: JOHN GLOVER
text:
BREWDOG boss James Watt last night revealed plans for a movie charting the firm's rise.

The tycoon, 41, shared the script online for the flick titled 'Underdogs: The Rise of BrewDog'.

The images start with co-founder Martin Dickie, 42, hillclimbing with The Proclaimers' 500 Miles as background music.

Watt dating ex-I'm A Celeb champ Georgia Toffolo, 29 said: "It has been so exciting to work with such talented people on the script of this amazing new movie.

"Since Martin and I started BrewDog, both just aged 24, the journey has been remarkable high highs, low lows, failures, successes, and a healthy dose of controversy along the way."

The drinks empire, based in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, runs more than 100 bars around the world.

headline: The Times hotel guide Forty-Seven Manchester
text:
Rooms and suites 7/10 Food and drink 8/10 What else does it offer? N/a Where is it? 9/10 Overall 8/10

Kro Hospitality already owns the popular Velvet hotel, which is surrounded by the bars and clubs of Manchester's Village district and wholeheartedly embraces the area's 24-hour party vibe. Velvet's new sibling, on the other hand, takes the polar opposite attitude to hospitality. Housed in a characterful grade II listed 19th-century shipping warehouse, Forty-Seven offers low-key, grown-up luxe with the emphasis on a good night's sleep. The bedrooms have been designed to elevate slumber to an art form you're practically guaranteed 47 winks. Rooms and suites The hotel worked with Kerry Davies, also known as the Sleep Fixer, to create for its 32 bedrooms a moodboard that is conducive to sleeping well. That means crisp white bed linen, a gently curved velvet headboard, a touch panel for mood lighting, blackout blinds and a colour palette of bottle greens, midnight blues and burnished golds which cranks up the cosiness. Ceilings are the subject of particular attention, painted in shades from putty to pewter grey for a more soothing sight before lights out. The rooms are compact, with the bed taking up most of the available square footage. A shelving unit has an integrated clothes rail, tea and coffee-making tray and space to store luggage. Cute touches include a Bluetooth speaker next to the bed to listen to those all-important sleep podcasts. Suites have baths, otherwise the galley-style shower rooms are poky, though L'Occitane amenities made from verbena, one of nature's sedatives, provide a touch of glamour. Food and drink The restaurant, Asha's, sparkles with gold chandeliers and purple velvet curved banquettes as rich and warming as the excellent dishes it serves. Try chicken tikka masala and lamb vindaloo, perfectly spiced and moreish, especially when eaten alongside quirky sides such as cheesy chicken tikka makhani fries. Finish with a drink in the basement bar, Peterman, named after the Mancunian slang term for a Victorian safe-breaker. It has safe fronts embedded in the walls, "diamond" lights scattered across the ceiling and cocktails named after a fictitious gang of thieves, including Naughty Nick of Northenden, a mix of rum, maraschino liqueur, coconut and apple juice, topped with vanilla foam (£2). Breakfast brings an Indian twist, such as masala omelette, or you can stick with a more conventional fry-up, albeit with turkey bacon and chicken sausages. What else does it offer?

There is no gym or spa but Manchester is right on your doorstep.

Where is it? Peter Street is home to lively bars such as Peaky Blinders and Brewdog, while Manchester Art Gallery and the Opera House Manchester are nearby. The Northern Quarter's indie shops, street art and restaurants and Canal Street's party scene are within a 15-minute walk. Susan d'Arcy

Susan d'Arcy was a guest of Forty-Seven, which has B&B doubles from £99; mains from £3.25 (fortyseven manchester.co.uk)

headline: BrewDog  drops pledge to pay all staff the UK living wage
author: Delphine Strauss in London
text:
Move by country’ largest craft beer brewer points to broader cooling of wage pressures across economy

BrewDog, the UK’ biggest craft beer brewer, has abandoned its pledge to pay all staff the voluntary living wage in a move that points to a broader cooling of wage pressures across the economy.

The company said in a letter to staff —ublished on Wednesday by the trade union Unite —hat new staff members would be hired at the statutory minimum wage rate, which stands at £0.42 an hour and is set to rise to £1.44 in April, rather than the higher rates set by the Living Wage Foundation that are paid at present.

Pay rates for existing staff outside London will rise from £0.90 to £1.44 in April, while the pay of employees in London will be frozen at £1.95. This is the rate set by the Living Wage Foundation, a charity that accredits employers who meet its standards on pay, to reflect the capital’ higher living costs.

The decision is likely to deal a further blow to BrewDog’ reputation as an employer. Chief executive James Watt was forced to issue a public apology in 2021 after accusations from former staff that he had fostered a “oxic”orkplace culture.

But it could also point to a broader easing of pay pressures across the UK economy, after a year in which wages have grown at a record pace in nominal terms but have still barely outpaced inflation.

Bank of England rate-setters are watching developments on pay intently, because they believe high inflation will be more persistent —nd interest rates will need to stay high for longer —f wages continue to rise rapidly and companies prove able to pass on the cost to consumers.

Ministers are, meanwhile, keen for living standards to rise in the run-up to the general election expected this year, and chancellor Jeremy Hunt has mandated a 9.8 per cent increase in the statutory minimum wage from April.

The BoE is worried that this rise in pay for low earners could complicate its job of returning inflation to the 2 per cent target, if it leads employers to lift salaries for staff higher up the pay scale as well.

BrewDog’ example suggests that at least some companies are finding it both harder, and less necessary, to boost wages —ith redundancies on the rise and labour shortages easing.

Some economists have speculated that employers who pay the voluntary living wage at present may feel less pressure than in recent years to compete on salaries for scarce staff, especially as the gap between the voluntary living wage and the statutory rate has narrowed over time.

However, supermarket J Sainsbury, which already pays well above the statutory rate, said last week that it would raise its minimum hourly wage by 9.1 per cent from March — move that could spur similar increases from rivals.

Next said in a trading update last week that it expected wage inflation to add £0mn to its costs in the year ahead, although the retailer noted it did not expect to raise selling prices as a result, given other cost savings and falling factory gate prices.

Bryan Simpson, lead organiser for the hospitality sector at Unite, said it was “utrageous”o withdraw the living wage pledge “uring the most acute cost of living crisis in a generation”

BrewDog, which in a 2019 blog described becoming an accredited Living Wage Employer as “ne of the best decisions we ever made” said in its letter that “ard decisions”ere necessary to restore profitability and financial stability after a year of “mmense challenges”

The company said staff outside London would still receive a 4.95 per cent pay increase, with those in the capital paid 4.5 per cent above the statutory minimum.

It maintains that its overall benefits package, which includes paid sabbaticals for long-serving staff and a week’ leave for employees with a new pet, remains more generous than the industry average.

headline: Bitter taste  BrewDog  scraps living wage pledge
text:
BrewDog, the UK's biggest craft brewer, has abandoned its pledge to pay all staff the voluntary living wage in a move that points to a broader cooling of wage pressures.

It said in a letter to staff published yesterday by union Unite that new staff would be hired at the statutory minimum wage, which is £0.42 an hour and is set to rise to £1.44 in April, rather than higher rates set by the Living Wage Foundation charity that are paid at present.

Pay rates for existing staff outside London will rise from £0.90 to £1.44 in April, while the pay of employees in London will be frozen at £1.95. This is the rate set by the charity to reflect the capital's higher living costs.

The decision is likely to deal a further blow to BrewDog's reputation as an employer. Boss James Watt was forced to issue a public apology in 2021 after accusations from former staff that he had fostered a "toxic" culture.

It could point to an easing of pay pressures after a year in which wages have grown at a record pace in nominal terms but have barely outpaced inflation. Bank of England rate-setters are watching developments intently; they believe high inflation will be more persistent and rates will need to stay high for longer if wages continue to rise rapidly and companies prove able to pass on the cost to consumers.

Ministers are keen for living standards to rise in the run-up to the election, and chancellor Jeremy Hunt has mandated a 9.8 per cent rise in the statutory minimum wage from April. The BoE is worried that this could complicate its job of returning inflation to the 2 per cent target.

BrewDog's example suggests that some companies are finding it harder, and less necessary, to boost wages with redundancies on the rise and labour shortages easing.

Some economists have speculated that employers who pay the voluntary living wage at present may feel less pressure to compete on salaries for scarce staff, especially as the gap between the voluntary living wage and the statutory rate has narrowed.

However, J Sainsbury, which pays well above the statutory rate, said last week that it would raise its minimum hourly wage 9.1 per cent from March.

Bryan Simpson, lead organiser for the hospitality sector at Unite, said it was "outrageous" to withdraw the living wage pledge "during the most acute cost of living crisis in a generation".

BrewDog, which in 2019 described becoming a Living Wage Employer as "one of the best decisions we ever made", said in its letter that "hard decisions" were necessary to restore profitability and financial stability. Delphine Strauss

headline: BrewDog  backlash on living wage
author: Maya Sall
text:
BrewDog is facing a challenge from its staff after the brewer and pub chain said it would be employing new workers on the legal minimum wage instead of the higher voluntary rate.

The Unite union said the move was "outrageous" in a cost of living crisis.

BrewDog is the second leisure firm this year to blame the increase to the national living wage for its troubles.

Nearly three million low-paid workers will receive a rise of almost 10 per cent after Jeremy Hunt, the chancellor, lifted the national living wage to £1.44 an hour. Hunt's decision came after the higher cost of living led to household budgets coming under pressure.

Bryan Simpson, from Unite, said: "BrewDog has been paying the real living wage since 2015. To withdraw it during the most acute cost of living crisis in a generation is outrageous."

The Scottish brewer said that from April 1 existing staff working outside London would have their hourly rate increased from £0.90 to £1.44, rather than to the £2 recommended by the Living Wage Foundation. It said in a letter to staff: "Even with this strong performance over Christmas ... there is no hiding from the fact that in 2023 we made a loss and ... to reduce spending, we still need to find more ways to get this business back to profitability."

James Watt, 41, its chief executive, defended the pay decision on LinkedIn, saying that "with the Real Living Wage increasing by an additional 10 per cent in April we could not implement this".

The entrepreneur, who was pictured late last year in the Maldives with his partner, the TV personality Georgia Toffolo, 29, added: "Hospitality is going through its toughest period in living memory. There were more closures and job losses last year than ever before."

Last week, Revolution Bars Group said it was shutting one in ten of its venues to reduce losses as a result of the new pay bands, while UK Hospitality, the industry body, has warned of the impact of the pay rise on the sector.

headline: BrewDog  drops living wage pledge
text:
COMPANIES

BrewDog, the UK's biggest craft beer brewer, has abandoned its pledge to pay all staff the voluntary living wage in a move that points to a broader cooling of wage pressures across the economy.

The company said in a letter to staff published by the trade union Unite that new staff members would be hired at the statutory minimum wage rate, which stands at £0.42 an hour and is set to rise to £1.44 in April, rather than the higher rates set by the Living Wage Foundation that are paid at present. Delphine Strauss

headline: 17M PINTS PULLED IN 'DRY JAN'
author: ALISTAIR GRANT
text:
BEERS CHEERS

Pubs' roaring 2024 trade

THIRSTY Scots have already downed 17million pints of beer in 2024 as drinkers snub Dry January.

And pub owners are toasting their best start to a year this decade, with takings last week up three per cent on 2023.

Sales were also around five per cent above figures for January 2020, before the Covid pandemic. Analysis suggests eight million pints had been bought in bars up to the end of last week while a further nine million pints were downed at home.

The stats were derived from Scotland's populationbased share of British Beer and Pub Association consumption trends.

And they reveal many punters swerved the option of staying alcohol-free for the first month of the year.

Last night Lauren Carrol, of Scots-based booze chain BrewDog, said: "Some people do rebel and have a Sup up very wet January." And UKHospitality chief exec Kate Nicholls admitted the operators of Britain's 40,000 pubs will be "relieved to have had such a positive start to 2024".

She added: "The latest sales figures are great news during what has traditionally been a quiet trading period.

"It shows how pubs are broadening their offer to successfully cater for all customers." Analysts CGA said: "Drinks sales have started the year in a positive fashion."

And Emma McClarkin, boss of the British Beer and Pub Association, said: "We are providing the best service to customers, no matter whether their January is dry or not.

"The sector remains fragile, with high energy bills and supply costs. But with government action on alcohol duty, business rates and VAT, 2024 can be the start of a new era of growth for Britain's pubs."

scottish-sun@the-sun.co.uk booze sales

headline: Eagle ale can's 'not enticing for teens'
author: ALICE WALKER
text:
A CARTOON bird used to promote a BrewDog beer is too aggressive to appeal to children, the alcohol watchdog ruled.

The drinks giant released Wingman IPA last year with the can featuring an eagle with tattoos, aviator goggles and a military jacket.

It prompted a complaint to booze regulator The Portman Group that it would entice teens.

But the Aberdeenshire firm said the unfriendly raptor was frowning to put youngsters off.

The Portman Group complaints head Rachel Childs said: "It's absolutely vital that producers take care to ensure their alcohol products don't have particular appeal to under-18s.

"While the packaging was cartoon-like, it was clear from the overall impression that the product did not have a particular appeal to children."

headline: UK advertising watchdog turns to AI to help police ‘odgy’reen claims
author: Kenza Bryan in London
text:
Automated tools in marketing could amplify unverified climate buzzwords for products, says head of ASA

The UK’ advertising watchdog will use artificial intelligence tools to boost the number of ads it scrutinises, in a crackdown on “odgy claims”bout environmental issues that are expected to proliferate due to the rise of generative AI.

Advertising regulators around the world have to tackle “he scale, the volume and the pace of change”rought by targeted and AI-generated marketing, according to Guy Parker, chief executive of the Advertising Standards Authority.

Automated tools could amplify unverified marketing buzzwords, raising some “ricky questions about where accountability lies” said Parker in an interview with the Financial Times. Advertisers “an’ abdicate responsibility by saying, sorry, the AI did that” he warned.

Although a decision on a promotion will ultimately be made by humans, the ASA plans to scan 10mn adverts for wrongdoing in 2024, using its own AI tool, compared to 3mn last year and just tens of thousands in 2022.

Parker’ comments come at a time artificial intelligence is already changing marketing. Google has started using AI to create adverts on its search page based on a company’ website and keywords it deems relevant. WPP, the London-listed advertising group, plans to spend £50mn on AI development this year.

A particular risk was that this type of tool could draw on popular phrases to create “odgy claims”n the environment, Parker said.

The ASA, which is independent of the UK government and primarily funded by an industry levy, has a data and science budget worth about five per cent of its £1mn financing for 2024.

It will check that phrases like “ecyclable” “ompostable”nd “arbon neutral”ome with sufficient context and caveats attached, while also scanning for ads that promote activities such as gambling and vaping to minors.

Google said it was “ommitted to applying generative AI responsibly” with each ad subject to “trict”olicies. Advertisers have to approve ads created using generative AI before these are shown to consumers.

In the past year, the ASA has pivoted to an aggressive clampdown on green ads by polluters including Shell, Equinor and Lufthansa. Earlier this week, it banned emissions claims made by carmakers BMW and MG as well as claims on pollution from London’ transport authorities.

Unlike tobacco or gambling, there are few restrictions on ads by big polluters, so the regulator’ strategy has typically focused on instances of misleading by omission.

In December, for example, the ASA took issue with a suggestion by Norway’ Equinor that wind farms, oil and gas, and carbon capture play a balanced role in its energy mix, when most of the company’ revenues still comes from oil and gas.

One claim that raises alarm bells is “arbon neutral” Companies that use carbon offsets to justify this type of green claim are “ind of cheating”n the eyes of consumers, Parker said. The ASA has recently barred claims by drinks company BrewDog and shirts retailer Charles Tyrwhitt to be “arbon negative”r “arbon neutral”

“eople are confused, and they’e not very happy when they find out that carbon neutral can be substantially based on carbon offsetting” said Parker.

Offsets, theoretically representing one tonne of carbon emissions removed from the atmosphere, have come under fire for the techniques used to verify the emissions claims. EU lawmakers last month approved a ban on some corporate marketing claims reliant on carbon credits.

Companies should use precise language and appropriate caveats, Parker said. “e don’ see it as a binary choice between greenwashing and greenhushing,”e said.

In reference to recent claims made by Lufthansa and other airlines based on offsets and cleaner fuel, he noted: “t the moment, I cannot imagine how a major airline could justify ‘ustainable’lying.”

Under Parker’ watch as chief executive since 2009, the ASA has won high-profile battles over accuracy and fairness in advertising, including against government bodies like the Home Office and consumer goods companies such as Danone and Coca-Cola.

The ASA decisions are typically heavily contested. Campaigners argue for example that it has not gone far enough on tackling greenwashing, in part because it has to rely on government regulators such as the Financial Conduct Authority to enforce its decisions, leading to similar versions of banned ads continuing to appear in public.

In contrast, Geraint Lloyd-Taylor, a partner at London-based law firm Lewis Silkin, argued the ASA’ approach was “ometimes too extreme”nd could discourage clean energy investments in the UK because of a sense companies had to “chieve perfection”efore they could promote their green credentials in marketing.

“ociety changes, we’e got a culture war, we’e got changing attitudes on the environment    nd we have to intermediate,”aid Parker.

He called for companies generally to be more “umble”n the claims they make in advertising.

Consumers were “ bit suspicious of the big bold, ‘ood for the planet’ype claims. They smell greenwashing when they see those claims.”

headline: BrewDog  mixed up in row over 'knock-off' design for tequila bottle
author: Max Kendix
text:
BrewDog is at risk of court action after two independent distillers claimed that its new tequila product bears a striking resemblance to their brands.

The Scottish drinks company launched the Casa Rayos tequila yesterday, but Muckle Brig, a rival based in Edinburgh, says that BrewDog's bottle is a "knock-off" of its Lind & Lime gin, which won an industry award for its design in 2019.

Ian Stirling, co-founder of Muckle Brig, which also operates the Port of Leith Distillery, said he was considering legal action, despite "extortionate" fees. "I was just scanning through Instagram and I saw a post from one of the presenters on Channel 4's Sunday Brunch, and I was delighted because I saw Lind & Lime was featured on the show," Stirling said. "But then I looked closer and saw it was some sort of knock-off.

"We've seen knock-offs before from around the world, including Thailand and Australia, and I thought it was one of these. But then I discovered to my real shock that it was BrewDog, another Scottish company, that had created a product so like our own."

Launching the Casa Rayos drink,

Steven Kersley, the BrewDog master distiller, said: "We've long looked on with jealousy at the burgeoning tequila category, and with growing frustration, because we couldn't participate by creating some agave magic at our distillery in Scotland."

El Rayo tequila, based in London, has also questioned the branding, claiming BrewDog has appropriated the term "rayo". "El rayo" means lightning and legend has it that tequila was discovered by the indigenous people of Mexico when a lightning bolt sent by the gods struck an agave plant, cooking and splitting it open, releasing its sap.

Jack Vereker, El Rayo's co-founder, said: "Creating an independent spirit is hard enough, but with tactics like these from massive companies, it makes our job even harder."

BrewDog dismissed the accusations, saying: "The Casa Rayos bottle design is based on the colour and aesthetic form of the blue weber agave plant from which our tequila is made. The painted as opposed to clear glass colour, bottle shape and contour, glass text, label size and placement, fonts and colours, and embossed design are all obviously different. And Casa Rayos is a superpremium tequila, not a gin."

headline: It's home Brew as telly Toff moves in

text:
GEORGIA Toffolo has moved in with her Brewdog millionaire boyfriend James Watt.

The Made in Chelsea star, 29, announced the news to fans on Instagram last night.

She posted a snap of the Scots beer tycoon, 41, carrying her in his arms ahead of Valentine's Day.

Toff said: "Happiest when with YOU.

"Which is encouraging as @brewdogjames just asked me to move in! We basically live together anyway but I'm excited to make it official."

She added: "Some of the outfits I've worn in between our houses in London have been extraordinary.

"Last week I wore cowboy boots with my PJ's and a flat cap to hide the shame."

I'm A Celeb favourite Toff found love with James last year when they met on a blind date.

headline: Beeb in clear on  BrewDog  boss 'lies'
author: ZOE PHILLIPS
text:
BREWDOG has had its complaint over a BBC documentary accusing its boss James Watt of inappropriate behaviour rejected by Ofcom.

The broadcasting watchdog said Disclosure programme, The Truth About BrewDog, had not presented, disregarded or omitted facts unfairly.

Mr Watt, 41, who co-founded the Aberdeenshire beer giant, had accused it of "hurtful and damaging lies".

In the show, US-based ex-employees made claims about his behaviour towards female staff.

The firm also said it infringed his right to privacy. But Ofcom said that did not outweigh the BBC's right to freedom of expression, also justified by public interest.

BrewDog said: "Ofcom's ruling concerns the BBC reporting processes, not the 'truth' it reported.

"We disagree the BBC took 'reasonable care'."

headline: BrewDog  documentary 'was fair'
author: Max Kendix
text:
A television documentary that highlighted allegations of inappropriate behaviour by a co-founder of the drinks company BrewDog was not unfair and did not breach broadcasting rules, a regulator has found.

James Watt and the company made more than 20 complaints about a BBC Scotland documentary, The Truth about BrewDog, aired in January 2022. Former BrewDog employees spoke to BBC Scotland's Disclosure programme alleging inappropriate behaviour by Watt.

In June 2021, dozens of staff, under the group name Punks with Purpose, signed an open letter alleging "toxic attitudes towards junior staff" and a culture of fear that had left many "burnt out, afraid and miserable".

Founded by Watt and his friend Martin Dickie in 2007, Brewdog has grown into one of Scotland's most successful independent brewers. Its headquarters and main brewery are in Aberdeenshire and it has breweries in the US and Australia.

In a 66-page decision, Ofcom ruled that "material facts were not presented, disregarded or omitted in a way that was unfair to the complainants", that BrewDog "had an appropriate and timely opportunity to respond to the allegations made" and that its response was fairly reflected.

The watchdog also concluded that Watt's expectation of privacy was outweighed by the BBC's right to freedom of expression and the public interest.

Ofcom does not rule on the accuracy of statements made in a programme, but focuses on whether broadcasters take reasonable care to be fair to complainants in presenting material facts.

Watt, 41, who is now dating Georgia Toffolo, a former star of the Made in Chelsea reality TV show, had said he was "very concerned about the BBC's sources" because many people it had spoken to left for disciplinary reasons and had "an obvious axe to grind".

Some were dismissed for theft, poor performance, intoxication, abuse of others, health and safety breaches and endangering colleagues, Watt said.

Before Ofcom can address complaints about the standards of BBC programming, they must first be submitted to the broadcaster. Issues concerning fairness and privacy can bypass this step and be taken directly to Ofcom.

Watt told The Sunday Times in April 2022: "I fully accept that I've been too intense, too demanding as a manager At times I miss the social cues that would enable me to kind of review that situation and then maybe don't course correct. I can understand why people felt the way they did."

Mark Daly, the presenter, said the ruling was a "vindication of the BBC's journalism". The hour-long programme has reappeared to view on BBC iPlayer.

It is unclear whether BrewDog will take legal action against the BBC, but it is understood that there are outstanding complaints not part of the Ofcom ruling. The BBC has published several subsequent articles repeating the allegations, as well as a six-part podcast.

A spokesman for the company said it disagreed with Ofcom's findings that the BBC had taken "reasonable care" and insisted that the documentary contained "numerous inaccuracies and misrepresentations". He added: "Since this programme aired, BrewDog has created nearly 1,000 jobs, opened many bars in some of the toughest trading conditions ever and been included in The Sunday Times Best Places to Work as well as named a top employer by the Top Employer Institute."

headline: Georgia: I admire brew-tiful Scotland
author: JORDAN TENNANT
text:
REALITY TV's Georgia Toffolo says her romance with BrewDog boss James Watt has seen her "fall in love" with Scotland.

The Made in Chelsea star, 29, grew up in Devon and spent much of her live in London.

And she admitted she "couldn't see myself spending enough time up here" to make the couple's relationship work.

But the I'm A Celebrity favourite added: "Now I ask James to ensure we're up here even more.

"Every day, I fall increasingly in love with this breathtaking place.

"The weather can be intense, but beautiful too!

"I have grown immune to turbulence, as it is now unnerving when landing at Aberdeen without being savagely thrown around the plane."

Toff has been dating Scots beer tycoon James, 39, since last year and the pair recently enjoyed a trip to the Maldives.

author: LANA CLEMENTS
text:

headline: Stout shows its strength as the fastest growing beer in Britain
author: Andrew Ellson
text:
St Patrick's Day may have been and gone but "the black stuff" is still being drunk in ever-growing quantities, according to Britain's biggest supermarket. Tesco says that stout has become so popular that it is now the fastest-growing beer variety in the UK, with sales rising by 35 per cent over the past year.

And it is not just Guinness that everyone is drinking. Brewers such as Brewdog, Wychwood and St Austell are also helping to drive interest by adding stouts to their ranges.

Brewdog's stout, Black Heart, which was brought onto the market in January last year, is now Tesco's second most popular after Guinness. The Grocer magazine reported in February that sales of Black Heart had reached almost 10 per cent those of Guinness.

Rather than being a new trend, the demand for stout is a revival of an old one. Irish stout, including Guinness, dominated the British beer market for more than 100 years, but towards the end of the 20th century its popularity declined because of the arrival of lagers from the Continent.

Despite stout's association with Ireland, it is an English invention, originating in late 17th or early 18th century London as porter, a brown ale. The drink got its name because it was popular with London street market workers. The strongest variety was known as stout porter, eventually shortened to stout. The drink was exported to Ireland and inspired a Dublin brewer, Arthur Guinness, to create his own version in 1759.

During the First World War, restric- tions on making roasted malts were enforced in Britain, allowing Guinness to increase its market share. The British stout industry never recovered, with just Mackeson remaining as the major brewer. The Guinness brand is now owned by the London-based Diageo.

Tesco believes that growth in demand for stout is being driven by two main factors: young hipsters' interest in craft brewing and the invention last year of the Guinness Nitrosurge, a device that can be put on top of special Guinness cans and is intended to replicate the quality of a pub-drawn pint.

Christian Clark, a buyer of stout for Tesco, said: "Guinness has created major demand thanks to its gamechanging Nitrosurge device, which has finally given fans a pub-quality version of the drink to have at home.

"But there has also been a general rediscovery of traditional British beer tastes brought about by the craft beer boom. In the last few years, a younger audience of drinkers has emerged who are looking for beers with great character and exceptional flavour to challenge their taste buds."

Clark said Tesco now stocked 15 different stouts, "something which would have been unthinkable five years ago". The introduction of the alcohol-free Guinness 0.0 had also boosted the market, he added.

Sales have been growing fast in pubs too, with Guinness accounting for one in nine pints poured, making it the most popular beer brand on tap.

However, the black stuff still has a long way to go before it threatens the pre-eminence of lager, which accounts for about 70 per cent of beer sales in pubs, according to GCA, the research company. The top ten bestselling beers in supermarkets are all lagers, with Stella Artois the most popular.

headline: 5 of the best
text:
Real ale pubs Peter Irvine

Blue Blazer, Edinburgh Opposite Doubletree Hilton. No frills, no pretensions, just wooden fixtures and fittings and all sorts in this fine howff that carries a huge range of real ales. Regularly voted pub of the year. Extraordinary rum and gin list, and then there are the malts. Much more soul than its competitors nearby (facebook.com/ blueblazeredin).

BrewDog, Glasgow Opposite Kelvingrove Art Gallery and Museum in a prominent Argyle Street corner site. The Glasgow BrewDog of the fast proliferating pack, the flag bearers of the craft beer revolution. All its cute and trashily named beers present, including the not-sostrong ones. Food, games, music. Seven days till 1am Monday and Tuesday, midnight. (brewdog.com)

The Lade Inn, Callander At Kilmahog, on the western approach to town, at the start of road into the Trossachs (junction of A821/A84), this is a good place to stop. The inn brews its own (Waylade, Ladeback, Ladeout). The ale shop adjacent has more than 200 of Scotland's finest (noon-6pm). There's a big pub-food operation although reports vary (theladeinn.com).

The Cobbles Inn, Kelso Popular local pub-grub (more grub than pub) restaurant on Bowmont Street. Luca and Stuart run a tight, friendly ship and care about their (mostly local) suppliers. Everything here is homemade: the bread, the chips, even the beer (Tempest Ales) and the ice cream. Bar and restaurant menu (evenings). Always a good atmosphere (cobbleskelso.co.uk).

Duke's Corner, Dundee All that the connoisseur drinker wants is covered here on Brown Street, including food. A strong beer list on the taps and on the shelf selected Scottish, Tempest, Fyne ales; many Belgians. Rotating real ale taps, including from 71 Brewing in Dundee (dukescorner.co.uk).

Taken from Scotland the Best by Peter Irvine (HarperCollins £7.99)

headline: BrewDog  forest bid goes flat
author: STUART PATTERSON
text:
HALF TREES DEAD

BEER giant BrewDog's bid to plant a million trees has gone stale after hot weather killed around 130,000 of them.

The booze firm blamed "extreme" conditions for the huge die-off at their Highland forest.

Co-founders James Watt, 41, and Martin Dickie, 42, spent £.8million buying the 9,300-acre Kinrara estate in Inverness-shire.

And the company got £90,000 of public cash to plant saplings at the site's Lost Forest.

But campaigner Nick Kempe discovered more than half of its Scots pines about 92,000 and 42,000 oaks had perished, plus thousands of birch trees. He called the project "disastrous".

A BrewDog spokesman said: "Extreme weather conditions resulted in a higher than expected failure on a plan with Scottish Woodlands to replace those that didn't make it." Scottish Forestry said: "The owner is required to replant the failed sections as a contract condition."

headline: LOST FOREST 'DIED'
text:
BREWDOG boss James Watt has broken his silence on his Lost Forest "disaster".

The co-founder of the beer giant revealed at least half of the 500,000 trees he planted last year have died at the Kinrara Estate, near Aviemore.

He blamed extreme weather and hit back at critics of the project, which got £90,000 public cash. He said: "Projects of this scale are always a challenge.

You know some saplings won't survive." The dead trees were revealed in a freedom of information request by campaigner Nick Kempe. He said: "The planting has been a disaster for the area and for humans who used to enjoy it."

headline: PIE DEAL'S BAKED-IN SUCCESS
author: Shaf Rasul
text:
Shaf Rasul One in a million shaf@the-sun.co.uk

IT MAKES ME LAUGH...

ME LAUGH... THAT "recipe for success", the magic formula that has all the ingredients to benefit your business, may be a myth but an Inverclyde butcher has taken the idea literally.

McCaskie's owner Nigel Ovens, above, has recently purchased the brand and intellectual property of the legendary Angus business, James Pirie & Son, following the retirement of Alan and Norma Pirie.

The deal interestingly includes the secret recipe for its five-times World Champion Scotch Pie, and its generationold secret recipe book.

McCaskie's isn't looking to change the recipes, so it is good news for fans.

IT MAKES ME CRY... IT'S almost a given now that multi-national businesses need to pledge to reduce their carbon footprint, due to consumer conscience.

However, it's been a challenging week for bad-news magnets BrewDog, whose Lost Forest plot to plant more trees to offset their carbon footprint has had a disastrous start.

The beer giant has revealed that half of the saplings - almost 250,000 - it planted in Aviemore have died in its first year due to extreme weather conditions.

It just goes to show that you may have the best idea, but if it isn't foolproof and weatherproof, it may not grow to be a success.

headline: BrewDog  co-founder steps down as chief executive after 17 years
author: Madeleine Speed
text:
James Watt turned brewer into UK’ biggest craft beer brand but was accused of ‘oxic’orkplace culture

BrewDog’ chief executive, who turned the brewer he co-founded into the UK’ biggest craft beer brand but was accused of overseeing a “oxic”orkplace culture, has stepped down after 17 years.

James Watt, who started the brand alongside Martin Dickie in Aberdeenshire, Scotland, at the age of 24, will be replaced by the company’ chief operating officer, James Arrow.

Watt said in a LinkedIn post on Wednesday that he would stay on as a board member and keep his shares in the company.

“uring my time at the helm of BrewDog, there have been highs and lows, up and downs, crazy successes and incredibly hard challenges,”att wrote in the post.

BrewDog’ chair and former Asda boss Allan Leighton said Watt had informed him of his intention to step back from the business last year.

“e subsequently put in place a succession plan by recruiting James Arrow as COO and strengthening the wider senior leadership team,”eighton said in a statement, adding that Arrow was a “roven leader”

BrewDog, now the UK’ biggest craft beer brewer, was one of the greatest success stories of the sector’ boom, raising more than £0mn through an army of small crowdfunding investors —ho the company called “quity punks”

However, in 2021 a group of former staff condemned the company’ “ulture of fear”nd misogyny in an open letter. The 60 signatories claimed the brewer pursued “rowth at all costs” fostered a “oxic”ttitude to junior staff and failed to address complaints.

Watt initially pushed back against the claims before acknowledging changes were needed.

“ think it’ 100 per cent fair to say that in the hyper high-growth years, from 2015 to 2018, we could have done more to look after our people,”e said in a 2022 interview with the FT. “ apologise to team members who we let down.”

This January the brewer received another blow to its reputation as an employer when it abandoned a pledge to pay all staff the voluntary living wage.

New staff members will be hired at the statutory minimum wage rate, rather than the higher rates set by the Living Wage Foundation that junior recruits are currently paid.

In a letter to staff at the time, the brewer said “ard decisions”ere necessary to restore profitability after a year of “mmense challenges”

BEER BOSS BID TO BE A FOOTIE BOSS

BEER boss James Watt has quit as BrewDog chief executive after 17 years and joked he wants to be a football manager.

He said he would now take on a boardroom role as "captain and co-founder".

Watt, 40, revealed he would make time for travelling, adventures and reconnecting with friends, new ventures and becoming a football manager before adding: "One of these is a lie."

It comes amid rumours he is on a list of names considering a takeover of 29-year-old reality TV star girlfriend Georgia Toffolo's local footie club, Torquay United.

Watt, who set up the Aberdeenshire company in 2007 with Martin Dickie, 40, will retain his 21 per cent BrewDog shareholding.

He said: "During my time at the helm, there have been ups and downs, crazy successes and incredibly hard challenges. When I look back, my overwhelming feeling is one of gratitude."

In 2021, ex-staff claimed there was a "culture of fear" at the company and, earlier this year, Ofcom rejected a BrewDog complaint over a TV documentary accusing Watt of inappropriate behaviour.

headline: BrewDog  boss calls time after claims by staff
author: Helen Cahill
text:
James Watt, chief executive of Brew- Dog, has said he is stepping down from the top job 17 years after he co-founded the brewing and pub group business.

The company has been beset by controversies in recent years, including accusations from former staff of a "toxic" working environment and criticism over wages.

Watt, whose girlfriend is the former Made In Chelsea star Georgia Toffolo, will be replaced by James Arrow, the chief operating officer, but will remain with the group as a non-executive director and continue to advise on strategy. He will take on the newly created non-executive role of "captain and co-founder" and he retains his 21 per cent shareholding.

In a LinkedIn post, Watt said: "In my new role I will remain as a board member, a director and I will also be a part-time strategic adviser to the business and our leadership team."

Watt started BrewDog from a garage in Fraserburgh, Aberdeenshire, with his school friend Martin Dickie. The entrepreneurs used tongue-in-cheek advertising campaigns to capitalise on the rising public demand for craft beer, and the business now operates 129 bars, with breweries in Scotland, Germany, Australia and Columbus, Ohio.

Watt's tenure has been marred by controversy in recent years, with the company being accused by former workers in an open letter in 2021 of having a "culture of fear" within the business, with "toxic attitudes" towards junior staff. Watt apologised to staff, and has insisted the group has made changes since the open letter was published. In January the company also faced a backlash after revealing it would no longer hire staff on the real living wage, instead paying the lower legal minimum wage. The TUC has called on Arrow, as the company's new boss, to pay its workers the higher wage.

Watt said on announcing his departure that he was "grateful for the tough times too, for the learnings they provided, the resolve they instilled and the perspective they offered".

He said he planned to take time off to travel, spend time with family and friends and work more on his other business interests. Watt has been investing in start-up companies and last year led the Next Unicorn competition, sharing out £ million of funding between five companies.

Allan Leighton, Brewdog's chairman, said: "James Watt, alongside Martin Dickie, created this great business from a garage in Fraserburgh. Few have accomplished what he has. From very humble beginnings, under his leadership BrewDog has grown to become the world's leading craft brewer, employing 2,530 people across its head office, four breweries and over 120 bars. I am especially pleased he will continue to offer his insight, creative genius and energy to the board."

BrewDog's publicity stunts and marketing campaigns also drew criticism from the Advertising Standards Authority, which banned a number of its advertisements for making misleading claims. In one incident BrewDog offered customers the chance to find a "solid gold" can hidden in its cases of beer, but one winner complained when he discovered it was made largely of brass, with gold plating. Watt paid out almost £00,000 to disappointed winners of the promotion, which he said made the brand look dishonest and disingenuous. The beer company, which is based in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, is now expanding internationally with openings in India, Italy, the Netherlands, Australia, Thailand and Denver, Colorado. The company has taken on hundreds of staff and has revealed plans to open another 200 bars by 2030.

headline: Self-confidence..and me
author: Anna Clarke
text:
I'm A Celebrity! winner GEORGIA TOFFOLO, 29, on embracing her imperfections and the power of love

In some ways, I don't think I'm half as confident as I pretend to be. When I was younger, it was all about faking it until you made it, but as I've got older, I know myself better. Growing up by the sea in Devon, I found my sense of confidence through sport. I had a really healthy, happy childhood. My mum Nicky [now 58], got me into tennis - she plays five times a week - so I spent a lot of my childhood playing at a club in Torquay and competing in tournaments, which boosted my confidence. When you start winning, you think you must be alright.

At 13, I felt a real shift, following two hellish terms at an all-girls' school. I was left out of things, girls were unkind to me and I hated every minute of it. Although I can't recall a lot of that time - perhaps I was a little traumatised - I do remember the general nastiness. I would beg my parents to let me stay off. Eventually, I was moved to a new school, where I thrived, but that earlier experience taught me that when someone is being treated badly, you should say something.

W 'I was living life while Made In hen I turned 14, I began struggling with cystic acne, which had a huge impact on me. By the time I was 18 and moving to London, I was still struggling. On the surface, my move to Fulham looked exciting. And it was - I was filming for Made In Chelsea - but behind the scenes, I was suffering terribly with my skin. Filming was difficult, as you can't hide severe acne. On days when my skin was bad, I'd get spots across my back and all down my neck, and I still do. Sometimes I'd cry or get snappy with the cameramen, and tell them to film me head-on, rather than from the side, hoping I could hide my skin better.

I was living a double life. On the surface, I was a confident, outgoing person on TV, but internally, my self-esteem was so low. At the time, I lived with it, but looking back, it tainted the whole experience.

People can underestimate how debilitating having bad skin can be. Going on I'm A Celebrity... Get Me Out Of Here! in 2017 changed my life, but I almost pulled out of doing the show entirely when I learned I couldn't bring my foundation with me and instead it would need to be earned as a luxury item. I had begged ITV to let me do the show, but the night before heading into the jungle, I burst into tears and told the executive producers: "I'm so sorry, I just can't do it." Luckily, they relented - I had 10 years' worth of doctor's notes, so my foundation was considered a medical exemption and allowed in. It was a huge relief. Even so, while I was in the jungle, my skin became a talking point back home. Trolls would comment: "Pizza face," or: "Look at that scarring."

a double filming Chelsea'

When I came out, I did a This Morning segment where I took my make-up off and repeated the nasty things that had been said about my skin online. It was liberating and a big turning point for me.

After meeting my boyfriend James [Watt, 40, BrewDog co-founder] last year, I feel so confident in the way that I carry myself. When you've got a good person beside you, it feels like you can weather any storm. We work together as a little team to navigate any problems.

Looking back, I'm amazed at where I am now, and I wish I hadn't spent the past 15 years worrying that none of this might happen. When it comes to my skin, it's been a long road. It wasn't just a bout of teenage acne, it's my reality. I still feel self-conscious, but it's not as debilitating as it was. I've learned to live with it.

Being honest about it on social media has kick-started a conversation. I still get messages from young people thanking me for talking so openly about acne. I hope it's helped them, because it's certainly helped me.

Georgia is an ambassador for Vital Proteins. For more information, visit Vitalproteins.co.uk.

'I was living a double life while filming Made In Chelsea'

headline: Editorial; Opinion, Columns Fancy a stay at The Fur Seasons?
text:
TOP DOGGIE-FRIENDLY HOTELS

FOR many of us, our dogs are considered one of the family and they are treated as such in our homes.

But when we go on holiday, we often have to leave our furry friends behind in the care of pet sitters or kennels.

Ireland is slowly becoming more pet friendly, with pubs such as Jack's Bar Restaurant Garden in Kells, Brewdog in Dublin and Crew Brewing Co in Limerick now allowing dogs to join their owners for a pint.

But pet lovers now want more shops, restaurants and bars to follow suit.

Here, dog mum NICOLA BARDON has found some staycations for you and your pooch to enjoy together.

THE ARDILAUN

GALWAY is quickly earning a name for itself as one of the country's most pet-friendly locations.

At the Ardilaun Hotel and Leisure Club, dogs are welcome in its Garden Rooms, which have a patio area so they can easily access the gardens for walkies or toilet time.

Dogs are allowed in most areas of the hotel except the restaurants and bar. If you do want to eat with your furry friend, the Ardilaun has an outdoor area, which has a covered section in case the Irish summer makes an appearance.

THE TWELVE

MANY dog lovers will have heard of this stylish hotel just 20 minutes from Galway's centre.

Dogs receive a welcome bag filled with treats, poo bags and a soft toy at check-in.

There are three pet suites, which have a dog bed, map with the perfect walks marked and a menu.

Dogs are welcome in every area of the hotel at no extra fee and you are free to leave them in the room while you go out.

DUNMORE HOUSE

IF you are headed south, check into the Dunmore House Hotel in Clonakilty, Cork, where it is free of charge to bring up to three pets.

Dogs are welcome in all areas of the hotel, except the Michelin-recommended Adrift restaurant. But they can join you for breakfast in the lounge instead.

There are five pet-friendly groundfloor rooms, each with a balcony overlooking the iconic Wild Atlantic Way and walk-in showers.

You will also find doggy treats around the hotel.

BALLINADEE BUS

FANCY a holiday that's a bit different? The Ballinadee Bus in Cork is a chance to stay in a former double decker that has been converted into a homestay for up to six people.

As seen on Dermot Bannon's Super Small Spaces, there are two buses with wifi, air conditioning, a hot tub and hammocks. Or you can pitch a tent in the campsite.

Ballinadee Bus even has its own rescue farm, with donkeys, alpacas, cows and pigs. You can also enjoy the views with a drink at the outdoor bar or coffee shop.

OCEAN SANDS HOTEL

IN Sligo, you can find the pet-loving beachside hotel.

There are eight dog-friendly rooms available, as well as self-catering apartments with a patio on the ground floor. Rooms are as close as possible to the main door for your four-legged pal's night-time toilet trips.

Dogs are allowed to join you in the lobby area at designated pet tables.

Ocean Sands Hotel welcomes up to two dogs per room, but you have to bring your own lead and dog bed. Book on the phone to guarantee a dog room.

GLASSON LAKEHOUSE

THE Glasson Lakehouse in Co Westmeath has views of Athlone and gorgeous grounds to explore with your pet to tire them out before a cocktail or pint on the outdoor terrace at Tom's Bar, or in the lobby.

The exclusive Doggy Rooms come with everything you need, including blankets, water bowls, tasty treats and plenty of space for them to have fun. Dogs are allowed in all areas of the hotel except Bonnie's restaurant.

Meanwhile, front desk can recommend dogfriendly places nearby and provide maps, trails and picnic spots.

headline: Hell yes, sign Labour's business support letter
author: Rutherford Hall
text:
Critical Comms

Messages from the archive of Rutherford Hall, critical communications strategist WhatsApp to Stephen: I've just had Jane from LOTO (Labour leader's office) on the line. She's organising a business letter for Starmer and wants me to help rustle up a few of our clients. She even asked me to sign but I've said I prefer to stay behind the scenes. But we probably need to find a couple of names.

--------------------- From: Rutherford@monkwellstrategy.com To: PHILKW@LKANDW.co.uk Phil. It's definitely flattering that Labour has approached you to sign their business leaders letter. My general advice is "hell yes" to backing the winning horse. But just a few things to bear in mind.

Number 1. Unless you are a dyed in the wool supporter which our previous efforts to get you in with Liz Truss would suggest you aren't then make sure this is a business decision, which you can align with what you do, and square your shareholders.

Number 2. Who else is on the list? If the other top business leaders are Michelle Mone, Frank Hester, that bloke from BrewDog or anyone who runs a water company then give it a swerve.

Number 3. Are you prominent on the list of names? You want to be in the top 5, one of the ones who actually gets noted. A good deed is not a good deed if no one sees you doing it. But you also want Premier League names only. You don't belong with the small fry. Don't sign anything that doesn't make you look good.

Number 4: The other side will want to discredit the list so are you OK with some extra scrutiny?

And 5: You have to be happy with at least a bit of the letter. And not just the punctuation. You have to be able to say you believe at least a few of the words in between.

Best, Rutherford Find me on Strava, KoM Sydenham Hill, PR Al Jubailah/Bawdah Loop 42 mins. ---------------------WhatsApp to PhilK: No it needs to be more than prepositions as well. Actual sentiments.

WhatsApp to PhilK: Sorry Phil I've literally never heard of half these people. You look like the only decent name. Some are retired. You want to be on the letter that the white papers say features "captains of industry". This is the lance corporals of industry. WhatsApp to Stephen: Whose brilliant idea was it to hold our summer party on the night of the election?

WhatsApp to Stephen: Yes, I know it was my idea but we have to move it. Otherwise it will just be clients, retiring MPs and journalists killing time till the exit poll. We need to reschedule for the week after. All the new ministers will rock up. The clients will love it. We'll even get a big mention in the Politico email. Just make sure we don't clash with the big leftie organisations like the New Statesman, Labour Together or the BBC.

WhatsApp to LOTOJane: Jane, sorry to hear about Phil saying no. I'm surprised because he's privately very enthusiastic about Keir. Let me talk to him.

WhatsApp to Stephen: Aargh. Jane says Keir is relying on us to find some names. In other words, we're in Siberia unless we help.

WhatsApp to list 1: Hi, just a heads-up to a few close clients who might be interested. Keir Starmer's team are looking for people to sign a supportive business letter. This election is as near as you get to a sure thing and there's big brownie points available. Let me know if you are interested.

WhatsApp to LOTOJane: Great news, I've got a few clients ready to sign up including some major business bosses David at Fracarys and John from BigEnergy

WhatsApp to LOTOJane: Well yes, there's a teensy fossil fuel dimension to their work. But this is an economy letter not an environment one and anyway they are very forward-looking about the green transition WhatsApp to BigEnergy John: We're on. Now remember, you are very excited by clean energy and the green transition. Maybe keep mum with your views on Ed Miliband and don't mention the renewed focus on the North Sea fields till after the election. WhatsApp to BigEnergy John: I know you aren't aligned with their policy but tell your board this is the best way to influence decisions. And you like most of the letter.

WhatsApp to BigEnergy John: Well, you like the punctuation. WhatsApp to LOTOJane: That's great and I'm sure they will pull in some other big names. I've got Phil K to agree too.

WhatsApp to LOTOJane: Not at all. I'm glad Keir is pleased and please tell him I was happy to help. Look forward to seeing you all at our summer party. Will keep the champagne on ice. Messages recovered by Robert Shrimsley

headline: Glow on, you can do it
text:
sunsavers.co.uk Instagram: @sunsavers Facebook.com/thesunsavers

EARN YOUR TANNING SEASON BRONZE MEDAL

LOVE Island is back on our screens and the cast are looking tan-tastic.

Want to bronze without breaking the bank? Use these budget tanning tricks for a flawless glow.

SUPER DUPE: Avoid splashing out on top tanning brands with a trip to B&M.

If you want a tanning mist, the Tantalize brand, at £.50, is a brilliant dupe for St Tropez's Purity, which is £2.10 at Amazon.

Prefer the original? Home Bargains is selling St Tropez tanning serum, RRP £5, for just £.99. In stores only.

THROW THE TOWEL IN: Prepping skin is crucial for a streakfree tan. Exfoliation is a key but instead of spending money on mitts or scrubs, repurpose an old towel. The rough texture is perfect for sloughing off dead cells, leaving skin ready to absorb the tanning product evenly.

Rub the towel gently over your skin in circular motions, focusing on rough areas like elbows, knees, and ankles. SOCK IT TO 'EM: No tanning mitt? No problem. Pop a couple of old clean socks on your hand and use them to buff in your tan instead. Wash your hands thoroughly after applying. THAT'S HANDY: Areas of dry skin, like elbows, knees and ankles, can absorb more tanning product, resulting in dark patches.

To avoid this, apply hand cream to these places before tanning. The cream creates a barrier to prevent the tanning product clinging to dry skin, ensuring a natural-looking tan. This easy trick helps you achieve a seamless glow.

REMOVAL SERVICE: If you end up with streaks or dark patches, don't fork out for a tanning remover.

BREWDOG games course,

Instead, gently rub the area with a cotton pad soaked in lemon juice or leftover make-up remover. This should lighten the area.

Then apply more tanning lotion if needed.

GLOW FOR IT: For a flawless Love Island-inspired glow, mix sparkly eyeshadow into body cream and rub it into your skin.
