headline: Ex- BrewDog  boss seeks 'high-quality humans' for new venture
author: Lizzie Roberts
text:
The former BrewDog boss James Watt has issued a call for "high-quality humans" to apply for his next business venture.

The trawler captain turned hipster beer magnate, who announced last month that he was stepping down from the top job 17 years after he co-founded the brewing and pub group, announced his new project in a LinkedIn post.

Watt, 41, wrote: "High Quality Humans Apply Here", accompanied by a rocket ship emoji. The business, Social Tip, will be "in the brand building space", focusing on "building community and rewarding everyday people for genuine brand advocacy and love", Watt said.

"I am building a new business! I will share the full details with all of you very soon and we are launching this summer," he added.

The firm already has eight members of staff on board, but is now seeking a US general manager as well as two UKbased graduates.

"The person needs to be an entrepreneurial self-starter and ideally someone with a keen interest in marketing and online marketing, and someone who has partnered with brands to help them deliver results.

"A history of working with brands and an extensive network of brand contacts is key here," the post said.

The ideal candidate needs to be "experienced and ambitious ... who likes being in the trenches too" and will be rewarded with an equity package.

Two "extraordinary" graduates are also being sought by Social Tip to work directly alongside Watt and the sales team.

Watt, who started BrewDog from a garage in Fraserburgh, Aberdeenshire, with his school friend Martin Dickie, first told the board last year that he wanted to step away to focus on other projects.

BrewDog said at the time that Watt would take on a new non-executive role of "captain and co-founder". He retains a 21 per cent shareholding in the firm.

"I love the business, I love the people, I love what we do. But I always wanted to build multiple businesses. The people I look to, most of them have built multiple amazing businesses," he told The Sunday Times last month, adding that the thrill of building the business had worn off and he was ready to move away from the day-to-day demands of the job.

headline: Cheers to you, Dad
author: Ben McFarland ; Tom Sandham
text:
THINKING DRINKERS' LIQUID GIFT GUIDE

IF you're opting for the easy win with a drinks gift for Father's Day, don't stray too far from his standards - after all, if it's not broke, don't ask Dad to fix it.

A bottle of beer, dram of whisky or gin and tonic will always work, and if you can open the wallet a little wider, you can swap his staple sips for something special. Here are our suggestions ... lThe award-winning Thinking Drinkers are touring the UK with their comedy tasting shows. See thinkingdrinkers.com.

Lager and ale THANKS to the craft beer revolution dads can now pick a different pint according to weather or their mood.

Most supermarkets have mix 'n' match deals or you can just grab a four-pack of a favourite lager or some ales for under a tenner.

LAGER SAINSBURY'S has a great deal on Birra Moretti with 12 bottles of the premium Italian lager at £3 for Nectar card holders.

But if the sun's out, dads will be at the bbq, and nothing fits his non-burger flipping hand better than a 25cl French beer.

Chill them and these crisp lagers are perfect to pair with burnt sausages, with ten of Morrisons' Biere Continentale Bottles at £.60 and 10 Saint Bertin Lager in Lidl at £.

ALE THE original craft beer and the national drink of Britain, you'll find decent ale in all supermarkets. Tesco has offers for Clubcard holders on multipacks of the delicious tropical IPA from Tiny Rebel Clwb priced at £ for a pack of four as well as Vocation Brewery's Life and Death IPA four packs for £.75.

Meanwhile, at Asda a four-pack of Greene King India Pale Ale is down to £.50 and a delicious dozen BrewDog Wingman Session IPA cans is just £2.

CRAFT BEAVERTOWN Neck Oil is the craft beer of choice in the boozer these days and Morrisons has reduced this fruity and flavoursome session IPA to £5.50 for ten.

In Aldi, the Harviestoun Brewery Schiehallion Craft Lager is £.85 for a 500ml bottle.

Cider

IT'S supposed to be the summer, and cider is enjoying a welcome comeback. It's not just about the apples with cider these days, there's a whole world of flavours out there.

At Sainsbury's Nectar card holders can pick up a Kopperberg cider variety pack ? with raspberry and Blackcurrant and tropical fruit options ? for £.

Whisky NOTHING suits your old man better than being left alone to sip in peace, and a single malt remains a nailed-on dad pleaser.

Asda has an incredible price of £0 for the Tamnavulin Double Cask, which is a lovely, easy drinking single malt from Speyside, sweet but with a bit of ginger spice.

For fans of blended Scotch, you'll find the premium Johnnie Walker Black Label on offer right now.

The Tesco Clubcard price is £1, which is similar to the cost of the Red Label, with the Black Label blending some rarer single malts.

American whiskey IF your dad loves a Jack Daniel's & coke, then upgrade to Wild Turkey, at £5.99 on Amazon it's worth gobbling up.

This

Kentucky Straight Bourbon was stunt biker Evel Knievel's favourite, and has been matured to deliver a warm vanilla flavour.

Tasty either neat or in an Old Fashioned cocktail.

Rum

AGED sipping rums are winning over whisky fans, so if your dad likes a sweeter spirit then Mount Gay Eclipse is a steal at £0 with a Clubcard at Tesco.

Made in Barbados, the original home of rum, it can be sipped neat or elevates any rum & Coke.

Or for a post-Sunday lunch talking point, the Venezuelan rum Diplomatico Mantuano brings some sophistication to sipping. Aged under tropical conditions, it's on special at £4.45 on Master of Malt and is best served with a chunk of chocolate.

If he prefers something spiced, then Sailor Jerry's is the real deal. The flavours of ginger, vanilla and Christmas cake are courtesy of a recipe dreamt up by US Navy man and tattoo artist Norman "Sailor Jerry" Collins.

Norman is now drinking in the big bar in the sky, but his own artwork still graces the bottle's label. It is £6 on Amazon.

Gin CUT through all the fancy flavoured gins and deliver dad a true classic with Plymouth.

It's a gin with plenty of juniper flavour so it's marvellous in a martini and glorious in a G&T, and you can pick up a special-edition bottle from Amazon for £8.98.

headline: BECOME AN INFLUENCER WITH  BREWDOG  FOUNDER
text:
BREWDOG'S founder is launching an influencer app with the help of his TV personality girlfriend.

James Watt quit as chief executive of the craft beer giant a month ago. And yesterday he announced plans for Social Tip, an app developed alongside former Made In Chelsea star Georgia Toffolo that will allow consumers to become influencers.

Those who post on social media about brands they like will be rewarded with money.

Watt, 42, who is still an adviser and non-executive director at BrewDog, discussed the app's development with influencer

Toffolo, 29. Social Tip will be launched and become available for download in the summer.

Watt will run it alongside Thomas Matecki, former chief technology officer of fintech firm Zilch, and Georgia Lee, ex-managing director of food delivery firm Supper.

He said: 'We will be marketing ourselves from day one, so people see the opportunity to make money from their posts, but companies will also be wanting their customers to be aware of this, aware of the opportunity to share their experience while profiting from it.'

headline: Brewdog  founder to launch social influencer app
author: Greig Cameron
text:
The former Brewdog boss James Watt is launching a new venture which aims to pay people for their social media posts and has signed up his influencer girlfriend's dog food brand as a customer.

Last month he stepped down from running the brewer he co-founded. He believes his Social Tip app can bring "authenticity" to the digital marketing world.

Watt, 41, said the business is in the final phases of testing and he hopes to have it fully available in the next four to six weeks. The app works when someone buys something from one of its partner brands. Depending on the reach and quality of their subsequent social media posts they can get up to 10 per cent of the initial purchase price back. Social Tip makes money by taking a small commission from the companies it works with.

Watt said he was careful to ensure he was doing "something that was a little bit different from Brewdog" but used lessons he learnt while building the brewer. That included seeing the power of fostering a customer community and the value of peer-to-peer recommendations.

Watt pointed out social media influencers are gifted products or paid to promote them but promised Social Tip is different.

He said: "It is increasingly difficult for brands to connect across platforms. Authenticity is really hard to find but this is something which will be a genuine recommendation. I'm really passionate about doing something that puts money back into the pockets of people across the UK and further afield.

"Give me a choice between paying thousands of pounds to a social media personality to fake-love my brand or rewarding masses of genuine, loyal fans, and it's no contest."

A number of brands have signed up, including Puregym, Azio Beauty, P&Co, the clothing firm, the soft drinks maker Trip, and Brewdog.

Also on board is Wild Pack, a raw pet food provider started by the influencer and entrepreneur Georgia Toffolo, 29, who has been Watt's partner since last year. Watt joked that it had led to "some difficult conversations around the dinner table" given his platform hopes to reduce the importance of online influencers but acknowledged it was great to have her support.

Watt has self-funded the business to date and estimates he has spent about £ million on it.

It has a team of eight with its headquarters in London and will initially operate in the UK and United States.

Watt is bringing in external investors, including a deal with Hatch, a tech investment fund, but expects to retain a stake of about 80 per cent.

He co-founded Brewdog in 2007 and built it into one of Scotland's largest independent drinks companies with bars and hotels around the world.

He announced last month he would be stepping down as its chief executive.

Watt had faced allegations in 2021 of creating a toxic workplace culture and then was accused in a BBC documentary of inappropriate behaviour.

At Watt's behest Brewdog overhauled its internal practices and brought in Allan Leighton, the former Asda boss, as its chairman. In an exclusive interview with The Times last month Watt denied many of the charges made against him but acknowledged that at times he was "too intense".

headline: Business; Opinion, Columns IT'S BEER TO PEER..
author: ASHLEY ARMSTRONG
text:
busin ss By ASHLEY ARMSTRONG ashley.armstrong@the-sun.co.uk @AArmstrong_says

BREWDOG founder James Watt has launched a venture to allow regular customers to become influencers and get paid for positive posts on social media.

Mr Watt, who dates I'm a Celebrity... winner Georgia Toffolo, has invested £million in Social Tip.

The app platform has already teamed up with brands including HUEL and pub chain SLUG & LETTUCE. For each post on it, the average Instagram user, with 700 to 1,000 followers, would receive from £ to £0 with Social Tip taking a 25 per cent commission from the brands.

Mr Watt said the initiative builds on the power of "genuine peer-to-peer recommendations".

The Scot stepped back as CEO of craft beer giant Brewdog only last month, and said of his quickfire switch to a new venture: "I just love building things." And he has already sought advice from former Made in Chelsea star Georgia, an influencer with nearly 3million followers.

headline: BrewDog  boss: My dad has cancer
author: SIAN MAHER
text:
BREWDOG boss James Watt has revealed his dad has pancreatic cancer.

The booze giants' cofounder, 42, said the diagnosis three weeks ago had hit his father Jim and the rest of the family "like a sledgehammer".

He said on social media: "Up until three weeks ago he was working fulltime on his lobster fishing boat, hauling 300 lobster pots every day.

"I still can't wrap my head around the fact he went from exceptionally strong, fit and healthy to facing such an arduous health challenge in such a short space of time."

Watt told how one of his closest pals, BrewDog sales director Dan Burton, 40, died of the same illness in January.

The tycoon, who last month stepped back as CEO, also posted a photo of him with his dad and ex-reality TV star partner Georgia Toffolo, 29.

headline: Can online reporting stop workplace microaggressions?
author: Emma Jacobs
text:
Staff at law firm Hogan Lovells can now log harassment on a tech platform —ut some suggest they talk about their problems instead

Law firm Hogan Lovells has launched a new way to improve relations in the workplace. If staff experience unacceptable behaviour like someone taking credit for their work, or microaggressions such as bias related to age, gender or race, they can now log it online, discreetly flagging the problem to management.

The firm introduced the tool after noticing employee “eluctance to call out seemingly ‘inor incidents’ which may nonetheless contribute to someone feeling excluded or marginalised” Penny Angell, UK managing partner, says.

Critics accused the firm of policing staff and stoking a culture of fear.

Rosie Turner, co-founder of InChorus, the platform used by Hogan Lovells and other employers from a range of sectors, denies it is “he weaponisation of the woke” She describes the tool as a “moke alarm”hat exposes problems before they become severe.

“f you look back at Uber, BrewDog, Ted Baker,”he says, “hat you always see is that those big behaviours don’ happen in a vacuum.”ll three companies were publicly accused by workers of perpetuating toxic cultures.

Turner makes the case for a mechanism allowing employees to flag problems that is more proactive than annual engagement surveys and less drastic than formally reporting grievances. “here’ little between these two extremes. It’ not about policing behaviours, it’ about detecting friction points.”

Reporting on such platforms is anonymous, and InChorus and similar providers do not allow staff to blame or identify individuals, which Turner says would make the mechanisms “ulnerable to misuse” The microaggressions or bad behaviour staff might report include assuming an older colleague is technologically challenged, or bullying or belittling behaviour.

After the murder of George Floyd in 2020, employers stepped up diversity initiatives to advance staff from under-represented backgrounds. But subsequently, after the US Supreme Court ruled last June against race-conscious university admissions, corporate programmes have come under attack, including by prominent figures such as Elon Musk and hedge fund billionaire Bill Ackman.

In the UK, stronger worker protections due to come into force in October will place a duty on employers to take reasonable steps to prevent misconduct. Law firm Travers Smith, for example, has updated its policy on staff reporting concerns about conduct, alongside a training programme for managers.

Turner says ideally organisations would want people to speak up in-person when they experience problems. But “hat overlooks power dynamics. You can’ demand psychological safety.”ather than seeing such tools as punitive, employers should use them to collect data and understand friction between co-workers. It might help identify training, or encourage those with differences of opinion, say between generations, to understand each other’ perspectives. “his stuff can feel very binary,”ays Turner. “ctually it’ often not that. There’ a messy bit in the middle.”

Tech platforms that collect complaints can improve understanding, says David D’ouza, director of profession at the CIPD, the human resources body. However, they risk creating a “ulture of reporting rather than direct dialogue. Organisations need to clearly define what should be reported through these channels, while promoting direct and timely conversations between colleagues.”

Ethan Burris, professor of management at the McCombs School of Business at the University of Texas at Austin, says these reporting channels can also “end signals that it is not safe to speak up face to face”

If leadership is funnelling people to use anonymous avenues to tell the truth, then it may suggest that it is risky to speak out. “hile anonymous hotlines are important for HR to capture instances of discrimination, harassment and unethical behaviour, if that is the primary or only mechanism to hear from employees, it is a strong signal that employees should be cautious telling the truth about any issue.”

Another problem can be that such systems rob leadership of the ability of to follow up. “nonymity, while important for protecting the identity of the employee, greatly hinders leaders’bility to    ind out what is really going on,”ays Burris. It would be too extreme, he says, to claim that anonymous systems are harmful for employees. “hey absolutely have their place    but, they need to be complemented with other avenues for employees to relay their feedback and ideas, [leaders need to signal] that employee input is valued and impactful for changing company policy and routines.”

The most important thing for ensuring that microaggression reporting is more than a fig leaf? Employers need to take concrete actions in response to what they hear, says Burris. “bsent these, employees rightfully feel that their voice goes into the ether, or worse, that there will be severe consequences for telling the truth.”

Letter in response to this article:

Bosses should encourage dissent, not anonymous complaints  rom Vera Cherepanova, Founding Partner, Studio Etica compliance consultancy, Malibu, CA, US

Law firm Travers Smith, for example, has updated its policy on staff reporting concerns about conduct and launched a training programme for managers.

Turner says ideally organisations would want people to speak up in-person when they experience problems. But "that overlooks power dynamics. You can't demand psychological safety."

"They absolutely have their place ... but they need to be complemented with other avenues for employees to relay their feedback and ideas."

headline: BrewDog  US blow as losses hit £0m
author: STUART PATTERSON
text:
BEER giant BrewDog is losing a fortune in the US, a new report reveals.

Over the past two years the Scots-based firm has posted operating losses of £0millon in the States.

That's despite bosses at the parent HQ in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, sending more than £3million to America since 2015.

Documents show its US revenue rose last year by £million to £8million.

But operating losses hit £.9million following a £0.8million loss in 2022.

Management blamed "several factors", adding: "These include a general slowdown in the craft beer market and rising costs due to inflation."

Execs have now cut the amount of products they make and the number of states they flog booze in.

BrewDog sell a million cans of beer a day, including Punk IPA. Cofounder James Watt stood down as CEO in May.

headline: BrewDog  battered by £3m US blow
author: STUART PATTERSON
text:

headline: Carbon target abandoned by  BrewDog
author: Sian Bradley
text:
BrewDog has abandoned its pledge to be a carbon-negative company after it emerged last month that half of the trees in its Scottish plantation had died.

The brewery, estimated last year to be valued at $2 billion, said that buying credits to offset its carbon footprint was too expensive and therefore it would not reapply for the status when it expires in November.

Carbon credits fund projects such as forestry management and tree planting. Scottish companies can purchase these through government-backed schemes to offset their own emissions.

BrewDog previously claimed that it was a carbon-negative brewery through a combination of credit purchasing schemes and planting its own forest in the Scottish Highlands.

The Lost Forest, the site bought by the company in 2020 at Kinrara, near Aviemore, was supposedly capable of absorbing more than 500,000 tonnes of carbon dioxide each year. Of the 500,000 trees planted at the site last year, at least 250,000 have died.

The brewery said that it intended to continue to fund the forest, with support from other agencies.

James Arrow, chief executive, said in an email to employees that while the company was "proud to become the first carbon-negative beer company back in 2020", the carbon credit market had become "very distorted" and economically unsustainable. Carbon credits have been criticised for overpromisbeing ing their benefits in removing harmful gases from the atmosphere.

The carbon-negative claims that appear on every BrewDog beer bottle and in their 100-plus bars will disappear over the next few months, although the certification will remain in place until November.

In December last year the brewery was criticised by the Advertising Standards Authority for failing to substantiate claims about the carbon-negative credentials of its beer in an advert.

Brewdog has now said that the benefits of the cheap offsetting schemes flooding the market are "highly questionable, and maybe even nonexistent". The email to staff said: "After regular review of our carbon-negative status, and in close consultation with our scientific adviser Professor Mike Berners- Lee at Small World Consulting, we have decided to step away from buying carbon credits and double down on our emissions reduction strategy and investment in the Lost Forest. This way, we can be certain about what we're investing in, and what the carbon benefit is."

Some campaigners have praised

BrewDog's retreat from its policy of buying carbon credits, which has been called greenwashing, but warn that it will create "severe investor doubt" about buying land for restoration projects.

Dave Morris, a campaigner with the Parkswatch Scotland blog, said: "It reflects growing worldwide concern that the carbon credit market is deeply flawed and not going to deliver the claimed benefits." Unite the Union, which represents Brewdog workers, accused the employer of abandoning its morals. Bryan Simpson, leader organiser, said that it appeared "to have given up on the ethical business that their whole brand is built on".

When Brewdog bought the 9,300- acre plot in the Scottish Highlands in 2020 it broadcast plans to undertake one of the "largest ever" reforestation projects in the UK.

James Watt, who stepped aside as chief executive in May, told The Sunday Times last month that its reputation would have suffered less had it taken that "£0 million and spent it on hookers and coke in Vegas". He added: "Or if we'd given ourselves a £0 million dividend and put it in our pocket. It's easy for people to criticise this at a distance."

Brewdog, which received nearly £00,000 for the project, attributed the loss of trees to an unseasonably hot summer and said it was working with Scottish Woodlands, the forestry management company, to replace them.

Morris said he did not believe that the company's decision to leave the carbon credit scheme would have no effect on the forest. "The BrewDog statement is so explicit about what is wrong about the carbon credit market that it will send shockwaves throughout the whole investment industry. They are all going to think twice about buying land in Scotland for forestry purposes and carbon credits in future," he said.

Brewdog said that it would use the money usually spent on carbon credits to reduce emissions in its operations and supply chain.

It argued that the costs of high-quality, properly verified carbon credit schemes were so astronomical that a carbon negative claim could only be sustained "at the expense of their own sustainability initiatives". Arrow said: "Our passion for the planet remains. This move ensures we are focused on the things that really matter."

headline: Indy IPA is UK No1
text:
AN independent brewery's IPA has been voted the best beer in the UK.

Steady Rolling Man, by DEYA, topped the Top 50 UK Beer Awards from organisers We Are Beer.

The pale ale, made in Cheltenham, beat brands such as Camden Hells and BrewDog's Hazy Jane.

And three no or lowalcohol drinks also featured in the Top 50.

DEYA's Theo Freyne said: "It is the first beer we brewed and remains our most important."

headline: Workers 'entitled'
text:
FORMER BrewDog boss James Watt has branded British workers "entitled".

The beer giant's co-founder, 42 has been criticised for a "toxic culture" for staff at the firm.

But he blamed young workers who have never had to struggle in a harsh economic climate.

Watt said: "I think there's an element of the work ethic in the UK.

"The levels of entitlement are higher because the current generation haven't lived through a tough recession."

headline: BrewDog  founder plans tribute to father
author: Sian Bradley
text:
The co-founder of BrewDog has announced plans to open a lobster restaurant in tribute to his father, who is battling pancreatic cancer.

James Watt, who stepped down as chief executive of the brewery this year, shared on social media that he is considering keeping his father's boat, the Serene, and using all the lobster caught for a restaurant he would call Jim's Lobster Shack.

He said it would be a way to pay homage to his father, who spent decades fishing in the North Atlantic.

"We would serve a super limited menu of my dad's favourite things, focusing on fresh lobsters caught by his boat," Watt wrote on LinkedIn.

His father is receiving end of life care at the Roxburghe House hospice in

Aberdeen after being diagnosed with stage 4 pancreatic cancer in June.

Watt said the diagnosis hit the family like a "sledgehammer" and that his father had been working full-time on his boat, hauling 300 lobster pots every single day, weeks before he was admitted to the hospice.

"Lobster fishing is incredibly hard and manual work, and the North Atlantic is an infamously dangerous and - difficult place to work my dad was fitter and stronger than I am," he said. That strength had been "forged through hard graft in the face of unforgiving northerly gales".

He added: "It would seem almost impossible that [my father] would go from exceptionally healthy to quite so sick in such a short period of time and it has been heartbreaking to watch him decline more and more with each passing day." Watt shared memories of school holidays stowed away on the boat, watching his father catch lobsters with his beloved dog Jura by his side. He has now proposed the business idea on social media, alongside concept images for the restaurant logo and photos of his beaming dad sharing his catch.

A percentage of the profits from Jim's Lobster Shack would be donated to charities supporting pancreatic cancer research, Watt said. He confirmed that the restaurant would be in the northeast of Scotland and he also entertained a commenter's idea to brew a new beer named after his father.

After 17 years at the helm of Brew- Dog, Watt stepped down as chief executive in May. He said he would use his newfound free time to travel, spend time with family and launch a new venture of his own.

headline: BrewDog  James in salute to ill father
author: ALICE WALKER
text:
BREWDOG'S co-founder is set to open a lobster shack to honour his terminally ill fisherman dad.

James Watt, 42, vowed to keep the family's trawler Serene in use so its daily catch can stock the proposed venture.

The tycoon outlining plans for Jim's Lobster Shack revealed: "We would serve a superlimited menu of my dad's favourite things, focusing on fresh lobsters caught by his boat."

He previously told how his father's June pancreatic cancer diagnosis hit loved ones like a "sledgehammer." Jim is now receiving end-of-life care at Roxburghe House Hospice in Aberdeen.

Watt, whose partner is reality star Georgia Toffolo, 29, said: "It has been heartbreaking."

headline: TV Toff's 'different' Scots life
author: ALICE WALKER
text:
I'M A Celebrity winner Georgia Toffolo has told of her "different way of life" in Scotland.

The reality star, 29, splits her time between London and Aberdeenshire with beer tycoon partner James Watt.

She assumed the northeast would be just like her native Devon when she moved up here.

But the Made In Chelsea favourite revealed: "It couldn't be more different. The coast is so rugged. The ports are similar but it's just a different way of life."

She hopes to showcase BrewDog co-founder James's local patch through her TV work.

Toff, who was crowned queen of the jungle in 2017, added: "I've had some really lovely requests about doing programmes that show off the beauty of Scotland.

"It's a bit of a slower way of life."

headline: BrewDog  founder announces father's death from cancer
author: Marc Horne
text:
The founder of the global brewer Brew- Dog has announced the death of his father, who was diagnosed with cancer only six weeks ago.

James Watt confirmed that Jim Watt, a North Sea fisherman, died of stage four pancreatic cancer.

The Aberdeenshire-based entrepreneur broke the news about the death of his father, 69, on social media.

He had previously written movingly about the impending death of the man he said had previously been "completely bulletproof".

Watt, 42, wrote: "Only six weeks from his diagnosis my dad has tragically lost his battle with pancreatic cancer and is now at peace."

He shared a number of images, writing: "This photo shows one of the few highlights of the last few weeks when we briefly left the hospital in London for duck pancakes at the Dorchester."

Watt also posted a picture of his father working at sea and another when he was holding his hand.

His partner, Georgia Toffolo, the model and former Made in Chelsea star, wrote: "I don't think my James has slept in weeks; every second counts and he's ensured he's been with his dad.

"It isn't my usual content, but I always want to be honest. If anyone has been through what we have over the past six weeks, then you'll know how horrendous it is. James has been astonishingly brilliant throughout this, and his dad has been even more so."

Toffolo, 29, added: "I've struggled to see my partner deal with such a scary diagnosis. Sometimes, James will look at me, and I can see the physical pain he is in while he's pretending he's OK.

"I've tried to do everything in my power to support and love, but the most challenging thing is that no one can change the realities of this situation, no matter how hard you want to."

Watt recently stood down as chief executive of BrewDog, which employs 2,600 people and has 120 bars around the world, but continues to be involved with the Ellon-based firm.

He recently wrote in The Sunday

Times about his father, who spent his final weeks at Roxburghe House hospice in Aberdeen.

"I have always been extremely close with my father, and we have been lucky to share a very special bond. From the age of six, I would spend all my school holidays stowed away in his fishing boat and we have pretty much been joined at the hip ever since I entered the world," he said.

"My father captained a ship in the north Atlantic for almost half a century. He remains by far the best leader I have ever seen.

"So much of my approach to life and leadership comes from what my father taught me and the incredibly high standards he set for me."

Watt added: "One of the many enduring life lessons that my dad has instilled in me is that it is only when things are incredibly difficult and when someone is facing severe adversity that you really see their true character."

He vowed to keep the family's trawler, Serene, in operation and will open a restaurant in his native northeast called Jim's Lobster Shack in his memory.

"We will serve a super limited menu of my dad's favourite things, focusing on fresh lobsters caught by his boat," he said.

headline: BrewDog  founder loses dad to cancer
author: JORDAN TENNANT
text:
BREWDOG boss James Watt has announced the death of his dad Jim just six weeks after a cancer diagnosis.

Jim was given the devastating news in June that he had stage four pancreatic cancer.

Scots beer tycoon James, 41, shared a pic of the pair online yesterday and wrote: "Only six weeks from his diagnosis my dad has tragically lost his battle with cancer and is now at peace.

"This photo shows one of the few highlights of the last few weeks when we briefly left hospital in London for duck pancakes at the Dorchester."

James recently revealed in a heartbreaking post that he felt his dad "did not even have a fighting chance". The tycoon lost a pal to the same type of cancer earlier this year.

James plans to open a lobster shack to honour his father's fishing career.

headline: Allsopp's pub on site where Trump 'got into trouble'
author: Richard Eden
text:
edenconfidential

FIRST Kirstie Allsopp's cousin Jamie revived the family's brewery, which had been defunct for 62 years.

Now, I hear that the former hedge fund boss is opening a pub with the same name as one used by the Allsopps in the 19th century.

And, intriguingly, it's on the site of a bar linked to a Donald Trump scandal.

The Blue Stoops in west London will be in the building which previously housed the Kensington Wine Rooms.

'It has been a lifelong ambition of mine to revive my family's lost beers and I'm delighted to open our first pub in 90 years,' says Jamie Allsopp.

He does not say if he will retain the blue plaque above the door. The light-hearted 'Wine Rooms Heritage' notice, inset, reads: 'Donald Trump got into trouble here when a chat over wine turned to Russian hacking.' It refers to the 2016 meeting between

Trump's thenadviser George Papadopoulos and Australia's former high commissioner, Alexander Downer.

At the get-together, Papadopoulos is said to have boasted that Russia had political dirt on Hillary Clinton, who lost the US presidential election to Trump months later. Downer passed on what he'd been told to Australian officials in Canberra, who later informed Washington of the revelations after Wikileaks released confidential emails from Clinton. It led to allegations of collusion between Russia and Trump's campaign, and the subsequent Mueller Report.

Eton-educated Allsopp, who has two children with his exwife, model Jacquetta Wheeler, both pictured, gave up a career in finance in 2021 to become a brewer. He bought back the Allsopp trademarks from Carlsberg and BrewDog and turned on the taps at Samuel Allsopp and Sons, founded in 1730. '[Location, Location, Location presenter] Kirstie will be getting a special discount code, so she can buy beer at cost,' he told me at the time.

headline: Wethers with 'Loo
text:
JD Wetherspoon is to open a pub at a major railway station after a £.8million investment.

The Lion & The Unicorn will open next week at London's Waterloo.

Its interior is said to be inspired by The Lion And Unicorn Pavilion, a popular attraction on the nearby South Bank at the Festival of Britain in 1951.

Brewdog opened its flagship pub then Britain's largest at the station in 2022.

headline: A brewsing encounter with the world's strongest beer
author: TOM BRYDEN
text:
IT'S £5 A BOTTLE, SCOTTISH, AN ASTONISHING 75% PROOF AND CARRIES A WARNING LABEL SAYING YOU CAN'T DRINK MORE THAN 35ml

IT has such a high alcohol by volume rating, it carries a DANGER warning on its spout.

But this is no rum or absinthe this is Beithir Fire, a £5 bottle of BEER.

With a dizzying ABV rating of 75 per cent, it will come as little surprise that it holds the title of World's Strongest Beer and it is brewed in Britain.

"A few people who have tried it said their tongue went numb," said Andrew Chapman, CEO of Edinburgh's 88 Brewery, which created the drink.

"People say they can track it all the way through their system they can feel exactly where it is inside them."

The name Beithir comes from a dragon in Scottish folklore, hinting at its fiery nature.

Around the top of each bottle is a bright-yellow label advising drinkers not to neck more than 35ml in one sitting just under a double shot.

And this caution is no joke a single 330ml bottle of the brew contains 24.75 UNITS of alcohol.

By comparison, the NHS recommends adults drink no more than 14 units a week, and a bottle of five-per-cent Heineken contains just 1.7 units.

The yellow label also reveals the brew's best-before date is in 100 YEARS, making it the beer with the longest shelf-life in the world.

Secret gets out Andrew is confident the drink will remain the world's strongest for a while.

He said: "We looked at a higher ABV when we were creating it, and 75 per cent seemed to be the maximum limit we could get to without it really becoming quite painful.

"We've also got the issue with shipping at 75 per cent, effectively, it's dangerous goods. So if we go higher, the shipping becomes expensive."

But just how is a drink this strong still classed as a beer?

"We brew a beer to the highest alcohol point we can, which is about 20 per cent," Andrew said. "After that it's distilled, going through a sixfilter process three times to remove sugars and impurities.

"People could argue it's a spirit and when it comes to paying UK duty, that's what it's classed as."

Sales of the drink have rocketed by 89 per cent in recent months, and more bottles of the brew have been shipped in the first eight months of 2024 than in 2023 and 2022 combined.

Andrew said: "Beithir Fire was created for a particular market. But, as with most products, the secret gets out and people in the UK and America started talking about it."

He also said UK drinkers tend to take heed of the warning label, but punters in the US see it as a challenge.

"I've watched videos of people in the US trying to drink a whole bottle and it hasn't always ended well," he said.

If you are tempted to pair it with food, Andrew says: "It goes well with anything that's stronger cheeses, meats, as if you were having a whisky."

Just keep a fire extinguisher handy!

'IT'S LIKE SWALLOWING A WASP'

SUN man Thomas survived taking a swig and offered his verdict ... 6

FROM lagers to IPAs, I can happily say that I'm a fan of most beers and I'd never hesitate trying something new if it's put in front of me.

But I can confidently say this is the first beer I've ever had that had a bright-yellow label looped round the neck of the bottle, warning you not to drink more than a double shot's worth.

I will admit: I was nervous. Having tried absinthe and other high-proof spirits, I knew that drinks this strong were not for the faint of heart.

To try it is one thing, but to enjoy it? There was only one way to find out.

Pouring out the amber liquid, there's no immediate smell that hits you.

But then I held it up to my nose.

Immediately, my nostrils felt like someone had stuck a match up them.

It was sharp, strong and didn't exactly give me any reassurance that the dragonthemed branding was just for show.

Bearing in mind that this wasn't a beer you swig, I took a medium-sized sip, and let the fire hit me.

For a moment, nothing. Then it hit all at once. I felt like I'd swallowed a wasp. It stung, it burned, and it forced my face into all sorts of strange positions as I felt it go down inside.

But then this feeling went, and I was left able to focus on the pleasant, hoppy, almost whisky-like flavour it left.

My verdict? I'm a fan - and honestly, I think it's about as enjoyable a 75-per-cent ABV concoction could be.

Drinking it is not a wholly pleasant experience, as colleagues who tried it like a normal beer found out.

But overall, what it takes in pain, it gives back in pleasure.

Now time for a few more sips 7

headline: Brewery logo ban on beer glasses 'is a crackpot idea'
author: Marc Horne
text:
Proposals to ban pubs from serving pints in branded glasses as a means of reducing alcohol-related deaths have been derided as "crackpot" by a former publican.

Earlier this week Neil Gray, the Scottish health secretary, told MSPs that officials would examine the possibility of introducing significant curbs on alcohol marketing in Scotland.

If approved, the restrictions would pave the way for publicans being stopped from serving beers in glasses marked with brands like Tennents, Guinness and BrewDog. Bar staff would also be prevented from wearing T-shirts supplied by breweries with their logos.

Gray's announcement came after it was revealed that alcohol-related deaths in Scotland were at a 15-year high. There were 1,277 fatalities attributed to alcohol-specific causes last year, an increase of one death from 2022.

The Scottish Hospitality Group, which represents the owners of pubs, bars and restaurants, called for urgent talks over the proposals. "We would urge Neil Gray to consult efficiently and urgently with us in regard to this," it said on X.

David Henry, a former publican, said: "This is another crackpot idea by people who have no clue how the world works. The problem is not pubs or bars, but drinking at home."

Emma McClarkin of the Scottish Beer & Pub Association, said: "It is surprising that the Scottish government has decided to revisit this area, especially after the previous proposals were rightly withdrawn after the consultation analysis showed overwhelming opposition."

Gray insisted that action was needed to reduce alcohol-related deaths.

headline: UK surge in low-alcohol beer sales tops other countries
author: Amy Borrett and Madeleine Speed in London
text:
Post-Brexit overhaul of alcohol duty and shifting lifestyles fuel boom in low-ABV drinks

UK sales of low-alcohol beer increased more than in any other market in 2023 thanks to a post-Brexit overhaul of alcohol duties, as brewers took advantage of lower rates by launching new or revamped products.

Sales of low-strength beer with less than 3.5 per cent alcohol by volume doubled from 650,000 hectolitres in 2022 to close to 1.3mn hectolitres in 2023 after years of flat or declining performance, according to data provider IWSR.

The UK market for low-alcohol beer expanded more than any other in volume terms, growing twice as much as the next-highest country, Venezuela, where sales increased by 325,000 hectolitres last year. Romania and Japan were ranked joint third in the survey of 160 countries.

The UK is now the eighth-largest global market for sales in this category of drink, up from 13th in 2022, IWSR told the Financial Times.

Demand for low-alcohol or alcohol-free beer has soared in recent years. The world’ largest brewers, such as Guinness and Heineken, have all launched zero-alcohol alternatives of their big brands in response to the growing trend for moderation among health-conscious consumers.

Analysts said the low-alcohol beer market had taken off largely because of the post-Brexit changes to the alcohol duty system introduced last August, which mean products with less than 3.5 per cent ABV are taxed at a lower rate than stronger drinks.

Under the new system, brewers must pay £1.01 a litre of pure alcohol between 3.5 per cent and 8.5 per cent ABV, while beers below this threshold are charged £.27 a litre. There are no duties on products below 1.2 per cent ABV.

IWSR senior market analyst Patrick Fisher said the UK had the largest-growing low-alcohol beer market largely owing to brand owners reformulating existing brands to take them below the 3.5 per cent ABV threshold. Some sellers have also launched fresh ower-strength products.

“rowth rates for the category are expected to normalise in the years ahead,”e added, with ales volumes of low-alcohol beer forecast to remain broadly flat between 2024 and 2028.

A number of products have been reformulated in response to the change, including Carlsberg’ Danish Pilsner and John Smith’ Extra Smooth bitter. BrewDog, the UK’ biggest craft brewer, launched a 3.4 per cent ABV lager called Cold Beer earlier this year.

The British Beer and Pub Association said brewers had “nthusiastically responded”o changing consumer tastes.

“uty freezes and cuts and certainty around these gives brewers the confidence and ability to innovate and invest in low-strength and alcohol-free products,”t added.

Weaker beers are surging in popularity as consumers look for ways to avoid drinking excessively when socialising and be able to drive home, with 39 per cent of those aged 18-24 not drinking alcohol at all, according to YouGov polling data.

Meanwhile, the share of pubs serving on-draught “o- and low-”ptions has risen fourfold since 2019, BBPA research showed.

Meanwhile, UK alcohol consumption declined 2 per cent last year, according to IWSR.

Lucky Saint, the UK’ largest dedicated alcohol-free brand, said its products, once reserved for drivers, midweek drinkers and Dry January, were now bought consistently throughout the week and year round.

“e’e seeing the rise of ‘ebra striping’ this practice of people alternating between alcoholic and alcohol-free drinks within a single visit to the pub,”aid Luke Boase, Lucky Saint’ founder.

headline: Editorial; Opinion, Columns Cheers for the beers
author: MATT BENDORIS
text:
WE WERE BREW A HOME WIN IN UK SALES CHART...

TWO Scots lagers have made it into the top 10 best selling beers in UK supermarkets for the first time as drinkers enjoy the taste of Brit brews.

Latest figures released by research giants Nielsen Data showed that

Edinburgh-based company Innis & Gunn's Lager Beer was the sixth biggest brand across the nation, with Aberdeenshire's BrewDog snapping at their heels in seventh place.

It comes on the back of the recent "beer olympics" where both Innis & Gunn and Jeremy Clarkson's Hawkstone Lager won gold awards.

Chief Features Writer MATT BENDORIS looks at consumers' growing thirst for home-brewed beers.

ENTREPRENEUR Dougal Sharp doesn't own a farm or a fleet of supercars like Jeremy Clarkson but he does share an award with the TV presenter after both their lagers won gold at the recent "beer olympics".

Edinburgh-born Dougal came out tops with his Innis & Gunn Lager Beer along with Clarkson's Hawkstone Lager at the World Beer Awards.

But the Scot believes their success shows that drinkers have a real thirst for quality home-grown brews that one day, he hopes, will overtake the industry's drinks giants.

He says: "I was so pleased to see Clarkson win an award, because he's new to it all.

"But the beer that he's making is really, really good. I've tried it and I liked it a lot.

"The point is that we need to spend a lot of time and money on getting the best out of our raw materials then think really carefully about how we make the best flavour for drinkers.

"But it just shows that if you've got a good product then the consumer will get right behind products like Innis & Gunn and Hawkstone."

Watching the former Top Gear host make a splash in the drinks industry takes Dougal back to when he launched his Innis & Gunn brewery in Perth in 2003.

Back then he concentrated on the international market, but saw a seachange in 2013 with a growing demand for home beers on tap.

Dougal, 52, explained: "We decided to launch a lager on the back of pubs in Scotland saying to us, 'Can you not brew a lager for us? Because all we have home-grown is Tennents'.

"So we launched this draft product and not long afterwards

I went into this bar and everyone was drinking pints of our lager.

"I could tell because it was served in our iconic glassware, which became something of a collector's item.

"Put it this way, I think in the first month we put 70,000 glasses into pubs and 70,000 glasses got nicked from pubs."

The major overseas brewers still dominate the UK drinks market, but with BrewDog based in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, in the top 10 and Joker IPA made by Williams brewery in Alloa making it into the Top 20 with sales of £.86million, Dougal believes they are going in the right direction.

He says: "I think one of the things that disappointed me about the craft beer movement was that a lot of the beers that came out were quite hard to drink.

"Whereas you now have this new wave of lagers in the marketplace where there's a consistency about them.

"People know they're going to be good, and you'll be able to have more than one pint, and it isn't going to break the bank in the process."

He adds: "There are lots of great brewers in Scotland and I think that the thing that typifies the beer industry is the goodwill towards your competitor we want all Scottish brewers to be successful.

"But we're up against really stiff global competition from companies with massive marketing budgets, huge spending power, and the ability to control distribution, which can make it very difficult for a brand like Innis & Gunn to develop.

World Beer Awards "With a great beer and a great story, people will get behind you as Jeremy Clarkson has seen for himself."

And Dougal believes with The Sun columnist's pulling power thanks to the success of his global hit Clarkson's Farm on Amazon Prime they will continue to grow in stature as people change their drinking habits.

This helped Innis & Gunn lager sales increase by 5.8 per cent in the last year.

He says: "I hope this is the start of a movement. I think Jeremy Clarkson is showing it can be done and like ourselves, you can make a lager to be proud of.

"We have made two of the best beers in the United Kingdom don't take our word for it, that's what the World Beer Awards has said.

"And in the process we've beaten all these famous lagers from around the world.

"I think that's really important because it shows that not only can we make the best beers, but people really will choose them over a big overseas brand if they are given the opportunity."

headline: All hail the lighter supermarket ale
text:
WE TEST BREWS TO ENJOY AT HOME

BREWERY hops are finally getting hip.

Ale - usually associated with bushy-bearded blokes - is enjoying a trendy renaissance, with one in three women also enjoying a regular sup.

If you're alecurious but wary of the dark and sinisterlooking "real" stuff, or simply don't know where to start with this acquired taste tipple, you can give it a go with lighter - and cheaper - versions from the supermarkets before hitting the pub for the real deal.

Here, HELENA NICKLIN, above, offers her selection of top newbie at-home ales.

Harbour Session IPA, Lidl £.85, 440ml, 4% ABV AN exclusive Lidl brew, a can of Harbour Session, which is decked out in Creme Egg colours, feels like an IPA "lite" so it's perhaps not a bad one to begin with if you're new to this category. IPA purists might well say it's a bit too thin rather than hoppy - but they can continue propping up the bar with their pint of cask while you enjoy this at home as it's cheap and very easy to glug. Looks fun with its bright colours so would make a great party ale. Worth a try even if it is not what you end up sipping in the pub.

2/5

Oakham Citra, Morrisons £, 500ml, 4.6% ABV OAKHAM was the first UK brewery to produce a beer with 100% citra hops. These are a fairly new type used for their fruity, citrus and floral aromatics and flavours, and known for adding vibrancy to ales. If you can get past the quirky label then glugging this very light gold will offer you bright tropical fruit and candied lemon peel flavours. It's hip and hoppy with lots going on so worth giving it a go.

My top tip for anyone switching tipples from a glass of white vino is that if you're into sauvignon blanc, you'll love this.

3/5

Fuller's Honey Dew, Sainsbury's £,25, 500ml, 5% ABV BLONDE ale is even lighter than amber and the clue is in the name as it generally has a gorgeous, light gold hue. Such styles tend to be crisp and pretty easy-drinking with light, biscuity notes. If you've never had ale before, something like this would be a great place to start. This organic tipple is made with real honey, malt and hops. It has a touch of baked apple fruitiness and a subtle nectar note without being sweet or too intense. Delicious cracked open on its own any time, and lovely paired with salty crisps.

3/5

BrewDog Punk IPA, Sainsbury's £.25 (Nectar), 330ml x 4, 5.4% ABV

IF you've tried pale ale before and enjoyed its light, bright hoppiness but want a bit more of that flavour, then India pale ale is the way to go. The Aberdeenshire firm's version is a go-to for many, and for a good reason - it gets the balance right between citrus and hops, and has a moreish earthy note on the finish. An easy-to-drink classic that would be a great intro to IPAs. It's well-priced - more so now as it is on offer - so if it isn't for you then you won't break the bank. Drink it chilled with snacks.

3/5

Black Sheep Ale, Tesco £, 500ml, 4.4% ABV

WHILE you currently can't get real dark and savoury cask ale to glug at home - unless you have your own pub, that is - an amber ale like this, with its less hoppy, more carameltoffee notes, is a great place to start to get a feel for the darker stuff. Whether you are a newbie to this type of drink or just want to try some different options in the comfort of your own home then this classic Yorkshire tipple is rich, balanced and smooth, with a Malteser-like, malty sweetness. It's awesome with a meaty pie. Trendy bottle too.

5/5

Saltaire Blonde Ale, Morrisons £.25, 500ml, 4% ABV SOME ingredients really do have the best names and Saltaire Ale says it is made with Bohemian saaz hops, which feels fitting given its slightly quirky profile. Light, refreshing and soft, there's a gentle, spicy maltiness to this well-priced blonde, which hails from Yorkshire. A lower ABV makes it an easy-drinking choice - there's lots of flavour here without being heavy. It's definitely a top at-home brew to try if you are just starting out on your ale journey.

4/5

Adnams Ghost Ship Ale, Asda £, 500ml, 4.5% ABV IF it's the vibrant, citrusy notes you love in a pale ale then spooky sipper Ghost Ship, with its powerful twist of lemon and lime, should be right up your street. Made with a mix of British and American barley and rye, it's a little hoppy and has elderflower hints along with biscuity notes. Apparently inspired by smugglers and our haunted coastlines, it's a great thirst-slayer - and with its spectral label it would definitely be a fabulous choice if you're getting the beers in for Halloween.

4/5

William Bros Birds & Bees, Aldi £.65, 500ml, 4.3% ABV NOT yet sure about the bitter hops of pale ales and IPAs? Then a golden ale might be the best go-to for you. This affordable offering from Alloa gives you a lot of bang for your buck - and its slightly lower ABV than some other options might be appealing for certain sippers, too. More about ripe, tropical fruit flavours and delicate florals, golden ales like this one have a real summer party vibe. This is a great one for spreading a little sunshine into a soggy autumn day. Or sipping outside in the summer.

3/5

headline: BrewDog  founder attacks tax rise plan
author: Sian Bradley
text:
The founder of BrewDog has criticised the government over plans to raise capital gains tax ahead of a long-awaited stock market float of the brewer that could trigger a substantial payout.

In a posting addressed to Sir Keir Starmer and Rachel Reeves on Linked- In, the business-focused social media platform, James Watt, 42, the former chief executive of BrewDog, said raising capital gains tax in the budget this month "will destroy entrepreneurial spirit in the UK and in turn severely damage our economy".

Watt, who co-founded the craft beer brewer in 2007 with Martin Dickie, also 42, warned that entrepreneurs will "simply leave the UK to build businesses and create jobs elsewhere".

"If we lose our brightest and best entrepreneurs, which we undoubtedly will if we significantly increase capital gains tax, we deal a hammer blow to the UK economy and the overall prosperity of every single UK family," he said.

"Increasing capital gains tax will do far more damage to our economy than any gains we may experience from increasing this tax."

Watt, who stepped down from his role of chief executive at BrewDog in May, handing over to James Arrow, chief operating officer, made the comments as the Scottish brewer considers a stock market float, which Watt previously claimed could put the company's value at £.8 billion.

No formal initial public offering has begun yet and other options, including a private equity sale, are also being considered. In an interview with Bloomberg last year, Watt, who holds a 21 per cent stake in BrewDog, said the brewer was looking at a possible stock market listing in London or New York.

Watt did not rule out an acquisition by a big brewer such as Heineken or Carlsberg. He said that it was "not part of the plan" but added: "Everyone's always got a price".

Watt faced allegations in an open letter from former employees in 2021 for having a "culture of fear" within the business, including "toxic attitudes" towards junior staff. He apologised to staff and said the group made changes after the open letter was published.

He also faced criticism in January after revealing that BrewDog would no longer hire new staff on the "real" minimum wage compared with the less generous legal minimum wage.

In an interview with The Times this year, Watt denied many of the charges made against him but acknowledged that at times he was "too intense".

Brewdog overhauled its internal practices and brought in Allan Leighton, the former Asda boss, as its chairman.

headline: £3M LAGER DROPS
author: STUART PATTERSON
text:
BrewDog's 1yr losses

BREWDOG lost a record £3million last year, "woefully late" accounts reveal.

The troubled beer giant's deficit comes after it posted a £4million shortfall in 2022.

BrewDog, founded by pals James Watt and Martin Dickie in a garage in Fraserburgh, Aberdeenshire, was months late in unveiling the latest figures. And furious shareholders last night hit out at the "horrendous" results.

One said: "No wonder this report was woefully late. Absolute car crash."

Another blasted: "This makes for pretty horrendous reading and trying to paint it as anything else is just fantasy."

But new CEO James Arrow said the company would return to profit next year.

He said: "The higher group pre-tax loss was driven by one off impairment costs related to historic acquisitions and restructuring costs."

The last time BrewDog hit profit was 2019. The company employs 2,618 staff across the world.

It owes the government £5million for a Covid loan.

Mr Arrow apologised for the late report, saying it was down to "more complexity that we had to navigate".

headline: BrewDog 's 'tasteless' Chandler lager ad
author: CHRIS TAYLOR
text:
BEER FIRM CAMPAIGN RAP

TRAGIC FRIENDS STAR CATCHPHRASE ROW

EXCLUSIVE by CHRIS TAYLOR BREWDOG was last night branded "tasteless" for ripping off tragic Matthew Perry to flog its Friends 30th anniversary ale.

The beer giant is using the late star's catchphrase, "Could I be any more thirsty?" in promoting its special edition cans of Lost lager.

Perry, who played Chandler Bing in the smash-hit sitcom, was a recovering alcoholic when he died at 54 from a ketamine-related drowning a year ago tomorrow.

Fraser Hoggan, chief executive of Aberdeen-based charity Alcohol and Drugs Action, said: "Given Mr Perry's well documented relationship with alcohol and, sadly, his subsequent death, which was also substance-related, this feels extremely insensitive and tasteless.

"It would be our hope that Brew- Dog may reflect on this and take steps to move away from this advertising campaign.

"Their slogans must demonstrate responsibility and respect to those within the recovery community, their families and their friends."" BrewDog's cans are emblazoned with Friends artwork. The promo includes Chandler's other lines such as, "Could I be any more excited?"

Perry battled drink and drug addiction and attended at least 6,000 Alcoholics Anonymous meetings.

Andy Barr, of digital PR agency 10yetis, said: "As with all BrewDog campaigns, they always flirt with controversy as they know that this will spread the message far wider than a 'safe' marketing campaign.

"This time could be a step too far though.

"Given Perry's previous battles with alcoholism, this feels in very poor taste. I can't imagine the brand reputation team were consulted."

The actor's parents set up the Matthew Perry Foundation to help people struggling with addiction.

BrewDog, founded by pals James Watt and Martin Dickie in a garage in Fraserburgh, Aberdeenshire, in 2007, declared record losses of £3million last week.

The company and the Matthew Perry Foundation were both approached for comment.

chris.taylor@thesun.co.uk

headline: Happy hour for Toff as  Brewdog  boss proposes
author: Jason Chester
text:
MADE In Chelsea star Georgia Toffolo yesterday announced her engagement to a millionaire brewery boss, after he proposed to her in Greece.

James Watt, who is understood to be worth £62million, popped the question last Thursday, while on a trip to the Greek islands to celebrate Miss Toffolo's 30th birthday.

A source told the Mail: 'He asked her to marry him in front of their families in Greece. After the proposal they went to a big party at the Peligoni club and ended up dancing on tables to Abba.' Miss Toffolo, who starred in E4's Made

In Chelsea, has been dating the founder of the BrewDog brewery and pub chain since last summer, and they now live together in London.

Showing off her diamond ring on Instagram, Miss Toffolo wrote: 'James just asked me to marry him. The easiest, quickest yes to being your wife.'

Mr Watt, 42, stepped down as BrewDog's chief executive this year after the firm was accused by former workers of having a 'culture of fear' in 2021. It is Miss Toffolo's second engagement, after her relationship with Nigel Farage's former righthand man George Cottrell.

'The easiest, quickest yes'

headline: TOFF SAYS I BREW
author: LUCY MURGATROYD
text:
STAR SHOWS OFF DIAMOND

Celeb winner to wed

By LUCY MURGATROYD I'M A Celebrity winner Georgia Toffolo is raising a glass after getting engaged to beer boss James Watt.

She revealed BrewDog founder James, 41, proposed in front of her family on holiday in Greece.

They were there to celebrate her 30th birthday last week.

"Toff" shared photographs of her diamond ring with her 1.8 million Instagram followers yesterday and said: "James asked me to marry him.

"The best moment of my life so far. I never knew I could feel happiness like this. The easiest, quickest yes to being your wife."

Georgia added: "Our love story is my favourite. How we managed to find each other out of billions of people out there will be my greatest blessing.

"I love you forever and the magical life we're building."

Georgia and James met last year after being set up on a blind date by mutual friends.

Earlier this year Georgia, who found fame on Made In Chelsea in 2014 and won I'm A Celeb in 2017, moved in with James.

It came as he announced he was stepping down from Aberdeenshire firm BrewDog after 17 years at the helm.

James, who is reported to be worth around £60million, is remaining on the firm's board.

Georgia moved north of the border to be with James and they now split their time between here and London.

lucy.murgatroyd@the-sun.co.uk

headline: Toff's ring is £00k sparkler
author: ALICE WALKER
text:
THE engagement ring that beer tycoon James Watt gave to Georgia Toffolo cost £00,000, an expert has revealed.

The reality TV star, 30, shared pics online of her diamond-encrusted gold band after BrewDog cofounder Watt, 41, popped the question in Greece.

She described his proposal as the "best moment of my life so far".

Laura Taylor, of Lorel Diamonds, said: "Based on the size and quality of the stones, I'd estimate its value to be £00,000.

She also described the three-stone ring as "distinctive" and "elegant".

Watt, worth around £60million, and 2017 I'm a Celebrity winner Taylor are now back home in Aberdeenshire.

headline: TOFF POSTS HAPPY BREWS
author: Zara Janjua
text:
1SM Sunday, November 3, 2024 ............... 17

Zara Janjua zara.janjua@the-sun.co.uk or follow on Twitter @TVZaraJ

EVERYONE has that one friend who is forever single and childfree.

The one you can never imagine growing up and settling down because they live terminally jangled and carefree lives.

I had put my friend in that category and that is where I thought she would stay, only this week she became a mum and I realised that it was actually me who was the forever-single friend. Talk about a plot twist.

I'd like to have a moment for all the friends who have succumb to motherhood and coupledom.

I'm A Celebrity winner Georgia Toffolo recently announced on social media that she is engaged to BrewDog boss James Watt.

She wrote: "Our love story is my favourite. How we managed to find each other out of billions of people out there will always be my greatest blessing".

These days it really does feel like a miracle, so I won't grudge a smug "I said yes" Insta post.

It's always lovely watching friends move into new life stages, to see them embrace new responsibilities, grow and blah, blah, blah, but Shakespeare said journeys end in lovers' meeting. So, by default, adventures begin with single people, right?

More people are choosing to be single now than ever before - there's even a whole singles movement being driven by the likes of events companies like Pip Social, who organise nights out, ceilidhs and activities for singles.

It's not about dating, but about connecting with people with similar lifestyles and goals.

I think we are long overdue a single revolution.

headline: Unhappy hour ... pint up by £ after 10pm
text:
A PUB has been accused of taking 'diabolical liberties' for charging £ extra for beer after 10pm.

Pints at O'Neill's in Wardour Street, central London, cost up to £.40 under the move.

The branch charges £.40 for a pint of Brewdog IPA before 10pm, and £.40 after. A bottle of Budweiser jumps from £.05 to £.05.

Even tonic water surges 46 per cent from £.15 to £.15.

Consumer champion Scott Dixon told The Telegraph: 'The hospitality industry needs to rethink their business model instead of inventing new ways to rip people off.'

Outraged punters took to social media, with one writing: 'It's a diabolical liberty.' Parent company Mitchells & Butlers said: 'Temporary price increases tend to reflect the need to offset additional costs such as at times when door security is required.'

headline: Unhappy hour
text:
Letters

NO HOUR will be a happy one in a pub extorting £.40 for a pint of Brewdog before 10pm (Mail), even if it is considered a craft beer.

And personally I would never drink in a pub that requires door security, so I would resent having that cost factored into the price of my pint.

SALLY BROWN, Basingstoke, Hants

headline: BrewDog  shuts Mumbai bars in blow to India expansion plans
author: Chris Kay in Mumbai
text:
Supply problems and regulatory issues disrupt UK craft-beer maker’ aim to open 100 outlets in the country

BrewDog , the UK’ biggest craft beer brand, has closed its two flagship Mumbai bars, in a blow to its plans to scale up in the country and tap the growing number of drinkers.

The BrewDog bar in Bandra West, a suburb that is home to many Bollywood stars and is known for its liberal nightlife, as well as its bar in the office district of Lower Parel, have been closed since the summer.

The company’ logos have also been taken down from the locked-up properties, according to checks by the Financial Times . BrewDog now has only two bars open in India, having first set up there in 2021.

Pratekk Chturvedi, a director of Aloha nternational Brewpub which operates the BrewDog India franchise, told the FT that the landlords had wanted the bars to close and that the franchise operator had filed legal cases to reclaim “usiness losses that we are facing” BrewDog declined to comment.

Even before the closures, BrewDog had struggled with obtaining consistent beer supplies in India’ financial capital. Chturvedi, who is also chief operating officer of BrewDog India, said that the brewer had also faced issues with the department that regulates and taxes alcohol in Mumbai’ home state of Maharashtra.

“umbai was an extremely important market for us, it is ground zero, it is where we began,”hturvedi said. “ am fairly certain that in the coming time, maybe in the next, say, three to four quarters, we will find a new location and start up in [Mumbai] again.”

BrewDog ’ Mumbai setback illustrates the difficulties faced by alcoholic drinks companies in India, even as drinking has become more acceptable in a country where it has typically been frowned upon and consumption heavily regulated.

In April, a brewery owned by Heineken’ Indian business, United Breweries —hich is the country’ biggest beermaker and the producer of Kingfisher lager —as raided by authorities in the southern state of Karnataka.

United Breweries ’hief executive Vivek Gupta told the FT that “xcessive”egulatory action and the patchwork of different rules across India’ states and territories, some of which prohibit the sale of alcohol, were hurting sales in a “riority market”

The closures are also a setback to BrewDog ’ plans for overseas expansion. Only last year, it announced plans to open 100 bars in India over the next decade.

Chturvedi said the Mumbai closures were only a blip in BrewDog ’ India plans. He added that the local franchise was operationally profitable and would focus on the country’ north where it had two bars in the cities of Gurugram and Amritsar.

“e are looking at aggressive expansion in the coming year    e are looking at one opening every quarter,”hturvedi said, adding that a new bar would open in Chandigarh in the coming weeks.

“ndia is a booming craft beer market,”e said. “t is a very lucrative, very profitable market.”

James Watt , BrewDog ’ founder, stepped down as chief executive in May after he was accused of overseeing a “oxic”orkplace culture. In its most recent results, the company reported an almost doubling of losses to £9.2mn in 2023.
