headline: What's love got to Brew with it
author: COLAN LAMONT
text:
BEER TYCOON'S TAX 'DILEMMA'

WED DELAY RISK

By COLAN LAMONT BEER tycoon James Watt is thinking about delaying his marriage to reality TV star Georgia Toffolo ? to give himself a tax break.

The multi-millionaire founder of Aberdeenshire-based BrewDog has shown his love for the I'm A Celebrity winner by investing in her dog food business.

But the entrepreneur, 41, is having second thoughts about tying the knot after his accountant warned it could cost him. He gave Georgia's WiLD PACK venture a glowing testimonial in a LinkedIn post. But he added: "My tax advisor just dropped a bombshell on me the other day: If I marry Georgia within three years of investing, I become a 'connected person' under HMRC rules, and I lose the EIS tax relief."

He asked his followers to vote on two options, either: "Love always wins ? take the tax relief hit" or "True love can wait for three years ? secure the relief".

The reduction could be worth up to £million to Watt, reportedly worth more than £00million.

He popped the question to Made in Chelsea star Georgia, 30, on a luxury Greek holiday in October.

colan.lamont@the-sun.co.uk

headline: EZ THE DADDY
author: ELLIE HENMAN ; HOWELL DAVIES ; JACK HARDWICK
text:
George baby joy brings smile back to troubled singer's face

IT'S going to be a very special Christmas for GEORGE EZRA - as he has become a dad for the first time.

The Shotgun singer, who is loved up with a new girlfriend, celebrated the new arrival this month.

A friend told me: "George is ecstatic about becoming a dad.

"It's been such an exciting time for him and this Christmas is going to be very memorable for him.

"George is in a great place and is enjoying being a hands-on dad."

The family favourite whose last album Gold Rush Kid became his third No1 record in June 2022 was seen for the first time with his newborn and girlfriend during a day out in Hertford.

My exclusive picture shows George carrying the baby before heading over to a supermarket to pick up supplies namely nappies and a few tins of Brewdog lager.

It's great to see him back on top form.

Last year he had a tough time after being diagnosed with acute vertigo, which forced him to cancel live performances. George had been on the road promoting the Gold Rush Kid record but told his fans he had to stop playing after feeling so unwell.

He went on to take a proper break to recover and broke his social media silence this summer to celebrate the tenth anniversary of his debut album, Wanted On Voyage.

Since then, George has been spotted out and about by some fans, including enjoying a few pints in Norfolk at the Brancaster Staithe Sea Shanty Festival in September.

Those close to the singer said he was planning to extend his break into the new year before getting back to music.

They said: "George took time out to focus on his health and now he's become a dad, he wants to enjoy every moment of that experience.

"He is focusing on what is really important in his life and that is his partner and their baby."

Congratulations, George. You're in for one hell of an adventure.

headline: Can bank make offsetting carbon emissions a growth industry?
author: Patrick Hosking
text:
"Atom Bank has been a bit of a pioneer. The challenger bank was one of the first deposit-takers to launch with no branches at all. It was also the first substantial UK employer to put its staff on a four-day week with no loss of pay. Now it has blazed a different trail: it's bought itself a wood.

Ten hectares (25 acres) newly planted with oak, rowan, willow, birch, sycamore, field maple and hawthorn, to be exact. For now, the 35,000 trees are tiny, no more than saplings, their shoots just starting to emerge from the plastic guards that protect them from nibbling voles.

But in time, they should grow into a mature wood, one that Atom reckons will absorb 7,000 tonnes of carbon, which is almost exactly how much it calculates it has emitted in its first ten years as a business.

"We've been responsible for putting it into the atmosphere," says Atom's co-founder Ed Twiddy. "We should suck it out again." The plot cost £40,000 and the aim is to own it in perpetuity, he says.

It will take decades for the trees to grow sufficiently to absorb the carbon already emitted by Atom and its employees in commuting and that doesn't include its "financed emissions", the carbon attributed to banks because of their loans to polluting borrowers. But it is a great start.

There's a pleasing circularity about its choice of site. The wood is above what used to be West Chevington opencast coal mine, a few miles north of Morpeth in Northumberland and not so far from the Atom offices in Durham.

West Chevington was one of a handful of gigantic craters scraped out of the earth to feed Britain's vast appetite for coal. The neighbouring Butterwell pit was said to be the biggest man-made hole in the ground in Europe. The pits, great engineering feats in their time but terrible lifeless moonscapes too, have been filled in and returned to farmland and, in this case, a small wood in embryo. The fauna, hares and deer are already returning.

The places that played a part in Britain's then unwitting role in contributing to global warming decades ago are now in a small way part of the solution. Many companies have supported tree-planting projects as a way of trying to burnish their green credentials and claim carbon credits to offset their emissions; but almost always they use third-party scheme developers.

One big one is the Woodland Trust charity, which plants trees and manages woodland for companies including Aviva, Lloyds Bank, Pets At Home, St James's Place, Card Factory and Taylor's of Harrogate.

But Atom is rare in putting the wood on its own balance sheet and taking much more responsibility for it in-house. That approach hasn't always been successful. BrewDog, the brewing company, tried it and more than half the 500,000 trees it planted near Aviemore in Scotland died after a prolonged dry spell.

BrewDog's co-founder James Watt declared it "the purest, most altruistic thing we've ever done" but still got widely criticised. "If you're looking purely through a reputational lens, we'd have been better if we'd taken that £0 million [the cost of the project] and spent it on hookers and coke in Vegas," he told The Sunday Times.

Owning a wood is a challenge and a responsibility. "You can't just buy a piece of land, plant it and forget about it," says Martin Taylor of the

Woodland Trust. The land has to be pre-registered with the Woodland carbon code, which polices the carbon credits system in the UK. Choice of species is key to maximise carbon absorption and natural diversity. So is the tending and nurturing and thinning of the wood in its first 20 years before it gets fully established.

Twiddy is confident Atom has done all the right things. A former Treasury official whose job included dealing with what was left of Northern Rock at the time of the banking crisis, he is a quietly persuasive proselytiser for companies to do more in slowing climate change.

He also has big ambitions to expand in woodland, aiming to buy another 100 hectares this financial year and 1,000 by the end of the decade. Atom's latest annual carbon report, published this month, is a model of honesty, clarity and realism compared with the muddle and obfuscation of too many companies.

Huge scepticism surrounds the claims of companies when it comes to their net-zero ambitions, even when it comes to tree planting. The biggest is that they are claiming credit for future carbon absorption they cannot guarantee. The trees have to grow unmolested for a century. An auditor allowing that kind of optimism in financial accounting would be struck off.

Greenwashing remains common, especially among banks. Only last week Lloyds was rebuked by the Advertising Standards Authority and had an advert that misled people about the extent of its environmental claims banned. HSBC was rapped in the same way two years ago.

But there is something about an actual physical wood that dispels the doubts while doing its normal job of housing warblers and worms and soothing the soul. Britain has one of the lowest proportions of woodland in Europe at 13 per cent, while the government target to plant 30,000 hectares a year has proved horribly optimistic. We're running at about 10,000 a year.

Thousands of us will don wellies today to go on a traditional Boxing Day walk, often through ancient woodland. It would be nice to think one or two business chiefs will be inspired to consider planting a wood as an alternative use of spare cash.

It sure beats another boring share buyback. Patrick Hosking is Financial Editor of The Times

headline: BREW CHIEF'S WORK STRIFE GAFFE
author: STUART PATTERSON ; ZOE PHILLIPS
text:
JOB BALANCE ROW

Fury at 'smug' Watt

BREWDOG multi-millionaire James Watt was slated last night for suggesting people don't need a work-life balance.

The Scots beer boss, 42, revealed in an online video that he "doesn't believe" in the concept.

Alongside TV star fiancé Georgia Toffolo, 30, he added: "We believe in work-life integration. The whole concept of work-life balance was invented by people who hate the work they do. When you find work that brings you meaning, you don't want to stop."

But he was accused of failing to appreciate the struggles of hard-working millions.

On viewer replied: "Say this into the face of someone in the NHS." Another fumed: "So smug." A third hit out: "What a privilege not to be stuck in a job you hate."

The craft lager entrepreneur fired back: "People have free will. I have worked a host of low paid jobs and always worked extremely hard."

He later suggested his views were not aimed at ordinary people, adding: "Most of my content is aimed at entrepreneurs. I am just outlining what has worked for me."

Ex-employees complained in 2021 of a "toxic" culture at his Aberdeenshire-based firm. Mr Watt, who quit as CEO last May, was asked for comment.

headline: BrewDog  co-founder berates idle Britons
author: Seren Hughes
text:
The co-founder of the beer company BrewDog has said Britain is one of the "least work-oriented countries in the world" and called for an end to the obsession with "work-life balance".

In a video on social media, James Watt said he and his fiancé, Georgia Toffolo, a social media influencer known for her time on Made in Chelsea, believed in "work-life integration". He added: "The whole concept of work-life balance was invented by people who hate what they do. So if you love what you do, you don't need work-life balance. You need work-life integration."

Watt, 42, said he had deleted his original post on Instagram after comments "crossed the line from debate to personal abuse". He has now linked what he sees as Britain's low work ethic to the backlash he received.

In a new post, he claimed "people are saying they'd like to murder me with a hammer" and asked: "What does it say about our society when a post extolling the virtues of hard work gets met with this kind of furious backlash?"

Watt highlighted studies including one from the Policy Institute at King's College London in 2023 which found that of 24 nationalities, Britons were least likely to say work was important in their life and among the least likely to say work should always come first.

"As a nation, we love to joke about the French being lazy but the reality is that our output per hour is 13 per cent lower than theirs," he wrote, referring to an Office for National Statistics comparison of productivity in the G7 which found the UK's output per hour in 2021 was lower than that of France, Germany and the United States.

"And I've heard countless international leaders say the UK's work ethic doesn't stack up against other nations, especially the US," he added.

Watt said: "This isn't to say nobody in the UK works hard I know that millions of you do and are relentlessly grafting every day. And it's also not to say that people have to adopt my personal philosophy on work-life integration. But ... since when did it become mainstream to hurl vile abuse at somebody sharing their approach to hard work? If we can't have a civil conversation about work ethic without descending into personal attacks, how can we expect to compete on the global stage?"

Watt stepped down as BrewDog's chief executive last year, saying he planned to travel and spend more time with family.

In 2021 former employees accused the company of having a "culture of fear" with "toxic attitudes" to junior staff. Watt apologised and insisted the group had made changes.

headline: I saw 101 Dalmatians at the cinema... and that was just in the audience
author: BEN ENDLEY
text:
PUPS ALLOWED AT MOVIES AND IN CHURCHES

By BEN ENDLEY WHILE the country may be going to the dogs, our fourlegged friends have never had it so good.

Pet lovers are not content with just a walk round the park they're taking them shopping, out for dinner and even to the cinema.

Even churches and cathedrals now cater for the 13.5million pooches across the country by welcoming paws in pews for regular services as well as special animal ceremonies and "blessings".

A new survey by the National Churches Trust has shown that 63 per cent of 285 UK churches polled consider themselves dog friendly and Rev Elizabeth Foot said: "I am always surprised when I see people waiting outside with their dogs and I say come and join us. We even have dog biscuits and if anybody wants their pets to have a blessing I am more than happy to do that.

"We have even had dog bridesmaids in weddings."

Chris Naughton took his dog Layla to church in Didsbury, Manchester, after she battled cancer and had chemotherapy.

Designer Chris said: "I was surprised when I heard about it and was very surprised that so many animals turned up.

NOAH'S Layla "Layla loved it and seemed relaxed as she was blessed by the vicar too."

Cinemas across the country are also holding special screenings where owners can take dogs.

Chain Curzon, which has 16 nationwide, and Picturehouse with 25, show films with slightly brighter lights and duller sounds to help pups adapt. As dogs don't get a seat they go free and at Curzon's weekly "Dog Day Afternoon" when free cans of Butcher's dog food are given out while they watch the latest releases, such as erotic thriller Babygirl.

BARK service

Dogs are generally welcome in pubs with some chains such as BrewDog even offering a dogfriendly menu and "beer".

Gareth Redhead from the Saracens Head in Halsall, Lancs, said: "Many of our customers come in with their dogs, we have treats and sausages for them. We'd have much less trade if we weren't dog friendly so it makes good business sense for shops and cinemas to welcome them." Even upmarket restaurant chains, such as Argentinian steakhouse Gaucho, welcome dogs and some branches host a monthly "Doggy Saturday" with a free three-course doggy menu for accompanying pets.

Laid-back chains Bill's and Costa Coffee allow dogs as long as they stay off the furniture and some serve a coffee-free "puppuccino".

TV chef Gordon Ramsay even accepts small dogs at 11 of his London restaurants while five locations across London and Edinburgh accept dogs of all sizes.

While dogs are also welcome at John Lewis, Apple and Lush.

Research has found going dog friendly can boost businesses by between 20 and 50 per cent. DogFriendly Awards general manager James Waters said: "It's not just nice to have dogs, it's good for business." But a dog behaviour expert has warned pooches may not be enjoying the visits as much as you do.

James Carroll, a clinical animal behaviourist at dogbehaviourist manchester.com said: "Taking your dog everywhere might seem like the best option for them and you, but in reality most dogs aren't having as much fun as you might think.

"So train your dog to settle and relax and make sure they're not more stressed in busy public spaces than they would be if they were left at home."

BEER FOUNDER IN £M NEW BUSINESS SHOW TO RIVAL BBC DRAGONS' DEN

BREWDOG'S James Watt is launching a £million reality TV show after twice being rejected by business programme Dragons' Den.

He told The Sun his fledgling firm was turned down as a competitor in 2009 when he hoped to go on the show and attract a £00,000 investment.

A few years later he auditioned to be one of the Dragons ? but was spurned after a BBC screen test. He said: "I'm fed up with the tired format of reality TV business shows relying on worn-out tropes and stale stereotypes of entrepreneurs for comedy value."

His show House Of Unicorns will be produced by Sony and Whisper Productions and will offer the largest TV cash prize in UK history.

Ten business are being sought to battle for the cash ? £million is being put up by James, with the other million coming from investor community Founders Capital.

Viewers will vote on which business is handed £million, with the other million decided by James.

But fans can also invest in the winning businesses.

If a network is not found the show will be broadcast on YouTube.

James, 42, said: "I'm obsessed with business and love helping founders."

Since stepping down as chief executive of Brewdog last summer, he has invested in 20 businesses.

The Scot hopes the show this summer will encourage firms to end up as Unicorns, defined as being valued at $1million.

headline: BrewDog  founder's £m hunt for unicorns
text:
Aco-founder of the BrewDog craft beer company has announced a new reality TV show in which entrepreneurs will compete for £ million, the largest cash prize in UK television history.

James Watt, who stepped down as chief executive last year, has partnered with Sony and Whisper Productions for the show, House of Unicorns, which is expected to air this year.

He said: "I've always been so disillusioned and frankly fed-up with the tired format of reality TV business shows relying on worn-out tropes and stale stereotypes of entrepreneurs for comedy value, which are well past their sell-by date."

According to the show's website, a successful company is "no longer just about innovation, technology, or even scale it's about fame, it's about building a brand that captivates the hearts and minds of an audience".

Watt, who created BrewDog 17 years ago in a garage in Aberdeenshire with his school friend Martin Dickie, said he wanted to double the amount of "unicorns" coming out of the UK.

A unicorn is a start-up company valued at $1 billion or more. Only 86 of the UK's 400,000 start-ups have achieved this status.

As lead judge on the show, Watt will decide who will win £ million of his own money. Viewers will decide who gets the other half of the prize pot, put up by Founders Capital, and will be able to invest in the businesses themselves.

The former chief executive said there would be "trials, tears and triumphs" as entrepreneurs and business leaders compete over six weeks. Although the show will follow a similar format to Dragons' Den, the "delivery and results are supercharged", according to the website. It adds: "[Dragons' Den] is about pitching all sorts of ideas for entertainment. House of Unicorns is an investment for growth and access to our millions of audiences."

Watt claimed to have received death threats over a social media post last week in which he said that he and his fiancé, Georgia Toffolo, who starred in Made in Chelsea and runs a dog food company, believed people should end their obsession with a work-life balance. "The whole concept of work-life balance was invented by people who hate what they do. So if you love what you do you don't need work-life balance, you need work-life integration," he said in the now-deleted video.

Watt quit as BrewDog chief executive last May. His tenure was marred by controversy. In an open letter in 2021 former workers accused the firm of having a "culture of fear" and "toxic attitudes" towards junior staff.

headline: Brewster millions
author: ABIGAIL HYMAS
text:
BEER CHIEF'S REALITY SHOW

BIGGEST-EVER TV JACKPOT

BREWDOG tycoon James Watt yesterday revealed he is launching a reality TV show with a £million cash prize after two knockbacks by Dragons' Den.

The Scots craft beer boss had hoped to pitch his fledgling firm for a £00,000 investment in 2008 but was "crushed" to be rejected after a screen test.

Years later he auditioned to be one of the Dragons but was also knocked back by BBC producers. Now he will offer UK telly's largest-ever cash prize in his series House of Unicorns.

Watt, 42, said: "I'm fed up with the tired format of reality TV business shows relying on stale stereotypes of entrepreneurs for comedy value."

Ten businesses are being sought to battle for the cash, with £million put up by Watt and the rest from the investor community Founders Capital.

Viewers will vote on who is handed £million, with the fate of the other half of the prize decided by Watt.

Fans can also invest in winning companies. The show is set to be produced this summer by Sony and Whisper Productions. If a network is not found, it will go out on YouTube.

Watt has invested in 20 firms since stepping down last summer as chief executive of BrewDog, based in Ellon, Aberdeenshire. A 'Unicorn' is a privately-owned start-up company valued at over $1billion (£10,000).

scottish-sun@the-sun.co.uk

headline: It's never been so hard to start a business, says  BrewDog  founder
author: Daily Mail Reporter
text:
BREWDOG founder James Watt says there 'has never been a more difficult time to start a business in the UK', blaming the Chancellor's Budget for making entrepreneurs struggle.

He pointed to bosses 'leaving to run businesses overseas', saying: 'I don't think the autumn Budget was helpful.'

It came as the Scottish businessman, whose comments about work-life balance sparked controversy in recent weeks, announced plans for a reality show with the largest cash prize in UK TV history.

Mr Watt is in search of entrepreneurs to take part in House Of Unicorns, offering them the chance to win £million.

He said he hopes to 'double the amount' of UK unicorns (firms valued at £billion or more) through the show but believes Labour's policies have made founding a business in the UK harder.

He added: 'It's never been a more difficult time to start a business in the UK. There are still an amazing batch of entrepreneurs and opportunities in the UK but I think the environment at the minute does mean that some need that extra support to really grow to their full potential.'

Rachel Reeves has faced heavy fire for her Budget, which will add £5billion to bosses' National Insurance contributions.

The estimated number of job vacancies decreased by 24,000 to 812,000 in the three months to December last year, according to the Office for National Statistics, suggesting employers are deciding not to hire.

Mr Watt, who co-founded the craft beer giant BrewDog in Aberdeenshire in 2007, will invest £million of his own cash into the show's winning business, with another £million from Founders Capital - Europe's largest founder investor community.

Mr Watt added that he was 'disillusioned' with reality TV business programmes that rely on 'worn-out tropes' and 'stale stereotypes' of entrepreneurs.

Referring to work-life balance recently, the businessman triggered a backlash when he remarked: 'You don't need work-life balance, you need work-life integration.'

headline: 'Show up' demand to staff is slammed
author: CONOR MATCHETT
text:
A UNION chief has blasted restaurants and cafes who stayed open during Storm Eowyn.

Bryan Simpson, lead organiser at Unite Hospitality, hit out at firms who made staff turn up amid the danger-to-life red weather warning.

He posted on X: "Workers being told if they don't come in they will lose pay and even their jobs ... profit before people in the clearest possible terms.

"There are f*****g cafes and restaurants boasting they are open. The NHS has stopped all non-essential treatment and these money grabbing w*****s expect baristas to risk life and limb?"

Pub chain BrewDog was shamed for telling staff to turn up from 6pm an hour after the red alert ends with Unite calling it "awful".

headline: ‘everance’hows we long to leave work behind
author: Emma Jacobs
text:
The hit drama takes the post-pandemic dilemma of work-life balance —r blurring —o a dystopian extreme

Do you fantasise about leaving your work problems at the end of the day? No more dwelling over a thoughtless comment from a colleague, returning to emails after dinner, or fretting over a knotty problem while trying to sleep. How about being unencumbered by personal problems between nine and five? No worries about ageing parents, anxious teens, or a grumpy spouse, allowing complete focus on finishing that report or clinching that deal.

In the post-pandemic blur of hybrid working, this seems like a fever dream. It is also the premise of Apple TV+’ dystopian workplace satire Severance. Fictional employees of a mysterious corporation, Lumon, undergo a procedure to split their consciousness into two: the Innie (work self) and the Outie (personal self). The central character, Mark, takes this unusual condition of employment to escape the relentless grief that follows the death of his wife.

Fans and critics have welcomed the show’ return after a three-year hiatus. The FT described it as “uperlative” And with return-to-office mandates stiffening, it has laid bare one of the most pressing dilemmas facing workers and employers: do we embrace the blur of work and life, or push for clearer boundaries between the two?

Some workers crave the latter. Many years ago, I found myself flatlined by misery, a bit like Severance’ Mark, but then I’ sit at the FT’ online newsdesk and think about nothing but rolling news. It sounds a bit sad but it was also a kind of magic.

Others are adept at creating a distinction, like the male escort I once interviewed, whose clients were men but dated women in his private life. He drew parallels with military personnel who would fight in the front lines and return home to cuddle their children. In a previous workplace, one colleague remains memorable for two distinct personalities. At work, he was buttoned up and sensible; outside, garrulous and fun. If you tried to continue conversations from the night before in the office, he would shut down. I soon learned to separate his work persona from his good-time one. People like these, who thrive on setting life apart from employment, make nonsense of the philosophy of bringing your authentic self to work.

The Victorian era normalised work-life separation, points out Sam Waterman, assistant professor of English at Northeastern University. As industrialisation cleaved the workplace from home, a domestic sphere overseen by women became a romanticised space for men to retreat and restore themselves.

This split fuelled the concept of the ideal worker, available full-time, with few distractions, from their 20s until retirement. According to Joan C Williams, former director of the Center for WorkLife Law, this functioned “airly well through the 1960s, until women began entering the formal workforce in greater numbers” During the pandemic the illusion of separate spheres dissolved as we saw children on Zoom, and staff found they rather liked putting the washing on between calls.

In truth, individual work-life balance now often boils down to personality type, whether you are a so-called integrator or segmentor. Some people like an office uniform distinct from weekend wear; others opt for smart-casual, suitable for both meetings and Saturday brunch. Possibly this changes throughout a lifetime.

Work-life integration is often touted by work zealots, whose work is not just a salary but also their hobby —nd sometimes their entire personality. “ think the whole concept of work-life balance was invented by people who hate the work that they do,”James Watt, co-founder of beermaker BrewDog, recently posted on Instagram. “f you love what you do, you don’ need work-life balance, you need work-life integration.”his from a man whose employees had alleged a toxic workplace, accusing him of a “ult of personality”nd pursuing “rowth at all costs” The post was taken down.

Over a decade ago, carmaker VW announced it would shut down emails outside office hours, prompting some to complain about a lack of flexibility. Some people like to pick up their kids from school, then log back on after they are tucked up in bed.

It may be, as Waterman has argued, that Severance, with its impossible separation between home and work, speaks to a yearning for a sharper boundary. That the procedure is sinister, deployed by a mysterious corporation, and prone to glitches, may show it is neither flawless nor desirable.

headline: Lord Sugar's not sweet
author: STUART MacDONALD
text:
HUNTER SLAMS 'DINO' TYCOON

.he's saur

'BERATES' TV BIZ HOPEFULS

TYCOON Sir Tom Hunter branded Lord Sugar a "dinosaur" and The Apprentice a "comedy" . The entrepreneur, 63, also claimed the telly boardroom chief's "You're Fired" catchphrase now belongs in the past.

Sir Tom, who is one of

Scotland's richest people, suggested the BBC show mogul's aggressive style sets a bad example to bosses.

The Sports Division founder admitted: "I actually don't like Alan Sugar and The Apprentice.

"Alan Sugar is a dinosaur who just wants to berate people. It's more a comedy show than a business show."

Lord Sugar has been showing the door to wannabe business magnates since 2005, when the UK series launched in the wake of the success of the US version starring Donald Trump.

He returned last week for the 19th series with advisers Karren Brady and Tim Campbell as 18 competitors battle for their £50,000 investment and mentorship.

But Sir Tom who sold his retail business to JJB Sports in 1998 for £90million argued the smash-hit programme doesn't show the realities of how to "attract and retain the best talent".

Ayrshire-born Sir Tom has vowed to give most of his fortune to charity and also coaches young business leaders through his foundation.

He said: "It's all about the people. How can you recruit a team that make up for, frankly, your inadequacies?

"You don't want to build a team where people look exactly like you, because that's a disaster. Getting the culture right and getting the best talent and retaining them is the secret sauce."

Sir Tom instead backed a rival reality show being launched by his craft beer tycoon mate James Watt.

The BrewDog co-founder is behind House of Unicorns, due to air later this year.

Entrepreneurs will compete for £million the largest prize in UK TV history. Ten businesses are being sought to battle for the cash, of which £million is being put up by Watt.

Viewers will decide which is handed half the cash, with the other payout then decided by Watt.

On his Go Radio Business Show podcast, Sir Tom said: "James is a good pal of mine. He knows what he is doing. He spoke at our conference and was very honest about the ups and downs of his business.

"It's his money he's putting in so I'm interested to see what comes out of it."

headline: Toff: Our love gets quarterly reviews
author: ZOE PHILLIPS
text:
lPAIR'S RELATIONSHIP CHATS lHELPS TO 'NIP ISSUES IN BUD'

GEORGIA Toffolo has revealed she and BrewDog tycoon James Watt carry out "quarterly reviews" of their relationship.

The reality TV star, 30, said the business-like approach to love helps the engaged couple "nip tiny issues in the bud".

And she insisted she and James, 42, come away from their "state of the nation" updates with greater appreciation for each other.

Georgia told her 427,000 TikTok followers: "My fiancénd I do a quarterly review. People might think it's a bit weird but we think it's really normal.

"The first thing is, we tell each other what we love about each other. Number two, we talk about things that we love about our relationship."

She said the "savage" part of the process was to look at areas for improvement.

The 2017 I'm a Celebrity winner explained: "One thing that comes up on James's part is budgeting I need to stop spending so much.

"I feel if we don't address little things, like, 'I don't like your lip balm', they can turn into bigger, major things that actually could affect your relationship. I highly recommend it."

Reacting to the video, one poster on X joked: "I've seen too many relationships end over a disdain for one's lip balm." Another said: "Running a relationship like a business treats connection as something to manage, not nurture."

Dad-of-two James co-founded BrewDog in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, in 2007. He popped the question to Toff during a Greek holiday last October. They first met on a blind date organised by mutual pals.

zoe.phillips@thesun.co.uk

headline: BrewDog  founder looks to mimic Musk with Shadow Doge scheme
author: Lizzie Roberts
text:
James Watt, the co-founder of Brew- Dog, has launched a "Shadow Doge", mirroring Elon Musk's so-called department of government efficiency in the US, because he is "sick of how broken our government is".

Watt, 42, who stepped down from his role at the Aberdeenshire-based craft beer brewer last year, announced the project in a LinkedIn post, vowing to "actually do something" saying it was "time to find a solution". Shadow Doge, which Watt described as the "unofficial, underground cousin of America's Department of Government Efficiency", will take an entrepreneurial approach to addressing wasteful public spending.

The initiative will use freedom of information requests to uncover inefficiencies, establish an anonymous whistleblowing hotline for public sector workers and publish monthly reports detailing potential cost-saving measures. Watt also plans to release a "league table" of the worst-offending public bodies when it comes to wasting taxpayer money.

"The UK government will spend £.276 trillion this year," Watt wrote. "If we save just 1 per cent, we could fix our budget deficit. If we save 10 per cent, we could build the strongest economy in the world."

He highlighted the £49 million the UK government spent annually on government PR as a "good place to start".

The government, he said, had "two very difficult options": raise taxes, which would "drive more job creators out of the UK", or cut spending, making our "already failing public services even worse".

Instead, his Shadow Doge will "show there is a better way". He added: "We show that if our government can become significantly more frugal with how it spends our money that will represent a far better solution than further increasing taxes, which just leads to job losses and economic pain for everyone." Watt criticised both the Labour government and the preceding 14 years of Conservative leadership for their handling of the economy. He maintained that his initiative was non-partisan, adding: "This isn't about party politics. I remain agnostic."

He said: "The more badly spent tax money we can help identify and save, the more we can invest in the things that are most valuable to us: job creation, education and our NHS.

"No more complaining. It's time to find a solution.

"Together, as a collective, we can save our country and help build a better future for everyone."

His suggestion comes after Michael Gove, the former Conservative minister, launched the Spectator Project Against Frivolous Funding (Spaff) and Jason Brown, a former Conservative special adviser, started the Waste Files for the Guido Fawkes website.

Musk, the world's richest man, who founded the electric car company Tesla, has been tasked with cutting US government jobs and other spending. His department has begun making people redundant, with termination emails reported to have been sent to federal employees at several agencies, including the education department, the small business administration, the consumer financial protection bureau, and the general services administration. Watt's decision to step down from BrewDog in May last year came after years of controversy surrounding his leadership style.

Last month Watt announced a television reality show that will feature entrepreneurs competing for £ million the largest cash prize in UK TV history.

headline: Editorial; Opinion, Columns How to keep your relationship in tip Toff condition
author: LYNN KERNAN
text:
EXPERT BACKS TV STAR'S ROMANCE REVIEW

FORMER Made In Chelsea star Georgia Toffolo stunned fans after revealing she and BrewDog tycoon fiancéJames Watt do quarterly reviews of their relationship.

The telly favourite turned romance author said the pair sit down every three months to say what they love about each other and what they love about the relationship.

But in what Toff describes as the "savage" section, they address improvement areas.

Sex and relationship coach Gemma Nice says she also has regular reviews with her other half. Gemma, right, said: "A quarterly review is great to keep the relationship alive and allows you both to say it how it is. It's like a company doing its quarterly review to make sure things are staying in check.

It also allows you to appreciate each other."

She added: "It's so healthy to keep tabs on where you both are within the relationship every three months.

"My husband and I usually have a coffee date every Friday morning and discuss things within our relationship, like any worries.

"I also get my clients to do this as it's a great way to see how the relationship is going, where it can be improved, how the communication, trust, intimacy, sex life is going. It allows you to be open and honest with each other." When the expert gets her clients to check in with each other, she says it is vital to turn off devices and listen.

She added: "Do not interrupt each other, and really take on board each other's concerns.

I get my clients to sometimes sit back-to-back so you are physically touching each other. The other person cannot talk until instructed. This works really well when there is conflict."

Gemma, who has been married for 25 years and has two kids, says there are real benefits to regularly communicating, as it stops small problems from growing into insurmountable issues.

The love guru said: "The positives are you can keep an eye on where you are in the relationship and, if anything is starting to go astray, intervene early and sort it out before it escalates.

"It allows you to be fully open and honest, which then moves into better intimacy and a better sex life."

Heaping praise on your loved one and your partnership can boost their confidence, she insists.

It also deepens the bond. Gemma said: "It gives them a sense of purpose and selfesteem. It also strengthens their confidence as well as the relationship, meaning both parties benefit.

"It improves relationship satisfaction because you are both communicating your needs to each other, making it easier for the relationship to progress and stay healthy." And Gemma said quarterly reviews more often found in an office setting are a perfect example of why relationships need work.

She said: "A lot of people get stuck in a rut and don't want to fix things. This is where the relationship starts to dwindle. You have to work at a relationship.

"You wouldn't not put fuel in your car and expect it to run. It's the same principle, you need to put the work in to get better benefits."

But opening up to your partner can also have a down side, if one is not fully on board with the idea.

Gemma said: "You could be seen to be over-analysing and being critical of the relationship, passing the blame. This might be seen as constantly looking for things to bring up at the quarterly meeting.

"It can also bring up smaller issues which might cause tension if not dealt with correctly."

Despite this, the love coach still says it is worth the risk as, like Toff and her soonto-be husband, it helps you come out stronger.

Gemma added: "You need to go about it in a non-judgmental way, which means using 'I' statements.

"For example, 'I really appreciate the hard work you are putting into our relationship' instead of using 'you' statement 'You make me feel sad when you don't put the work in'. It really reframes how you communicate with each other.

"By doing these kinds of things it makes the communication, trust and intimacy stronger in all areas of the relationship."

headline: PLAN WON'T STOP SPLIT
text:
1SM Sunday, February

Zara Janjua zara.janjua@the-sun.co.uk or follow on Twitter @TVZaraJ

PEOPLE in long-term relationships tend to preach about the "work" they have put in over the years as though they're angling for a bonus.

If you want to be in it for the long haul, maybe there's something to be said for getting down to business - and by that I mean, holding quarterly reviews.

Georgia Toffolo and BrewDog's James Watt swear by their system of sit-down check-ins every three months.

Who says romance is dead?

In a TikTok video they shared their advice, which included a breakdown of the agenda: Item one, appreciate the good. Item two, address the not-so-good.

It's apparently an open, honest approach that keeps the small stuff from spiralling into bigger issues.

It's a nice, pragmatic idea for a couple still arguably in the start-up stages of romance but how on earth will that work out for them in the long-term?

Actress Isla Fisher said her split from Ali G star Sacha Baron Cohen was the most difficult thing she's been through.

They were together for more than 20 years, married for 14 and have three children.

Could the relationship have been saved by goal setting, clear communication and accountability?

Love is messy, emotional, and unpredictable, and no amount of strategising can change that.

But that's just my feedback, which they are welcome to store in a bin-shaped filing cabinet.

headline: I BREW!
author: STUART PATTERSON; CHRIS TAYLOR ; HARRY WILLIAMSON
text:
James& Georgia's surprise wedding

BREWDOG founder James Watt and reality TV star Georgia Toffolo secretly tied the knot yesterday.

The beer tycoon, 42, wore overalls, cable-knit jumper and woolly hat as he and bride Toff, 30, celebrated on the water in a tribute to his late fisherman dad Jim.

Guests received just 48 hours' notice of the surprise nuptials where the couple's spaniel, Monty, acted as ring bearer.

James's two daughters from his previous marriage to illustrator Johanna Basford were bridesmaids in Gardenstown, Aberdeenshire.

Georgia married in a fur dress with a Bardot neckline while James wore a dark suit and matching tie before switching to his overalls.

The celebrations came seven months after James's 68-year-old father died six weeks after being diagnosed with pancreatic cancer.

Last night, a source told The Scottish Sun on Sunday: "James and Toff looked so happy.

"They've been through some tough times and deserve some joy.

"They could have got married anywhere in the world, so it is extra special they did it here.

"It's so touching they gave a nod to his dad.

"He'd be so proud.

They are a beautiful couple and they deserve a long, happy marriage."

The couple revealed they had "eloped" after a ceremony in a church in the coastal village where fewer than 600 people live. Continuing the nautical theme, they shared the coordinates of the spot where they got hitched.

It is believed they partied with pals in a village pub over sausage rolls and beer before heading to BrewDog's Dogtap venue in nearby Ellon for an evening bash.

They were met with whoops from staff as they arrived while James was wearing a silver suit.

Former Prime Minister Boris Johnson's dad Stanley Georgia's former I'm A Celebrity campmate was among the guests.

The rest of the bar was filled with families having meals.

One local said: "You would have had no idea there was a celebrity wedding going on apart from the two security guards out front.

"It was all pretty low-key and normal." The newlyweds also announced plans for a honeymoon in Scotland with another bash planned in London on their return.

Toff posted online: "James and I got married today.

"1st March will forever be the best day of my life the day I became your wife.

"To our family and friends who aren't with us, we will celebrate with you soonest. I can't believe it."

James, from Fraserburgh, started dating Made In Chelsea star Toff after they were introduced by a pal two years ago.

The 2017 Queen of the Jungle swapped London for the north east to be with him and they announced their engagement last October.

He proposed while on holiday in Greece to mark her birthday.

stuart.patterson@the-sun.co.uk

headline: TOFF'S A MRS
text:
I'M A Celeb winner Georgia Toffolo and Brewdog founder James Watt pose on a lobster boat after eloping.

They got hitched yesterday Aberdeenshire, then kissed, below, with Georgia, 30, in a fur-lined dress and James in fisherman's trousers and beanie hat.

She said guests got 48 hours' notice, adding: "1st March will forever be the best day of my life".

headline: Workers are going public about bad bosses
author: Oliver Balch
text:
Employees frustrated with complaints processes are joining forces and taking grievances to social media

Between a tightening labour market, return-to-office mandates and bosses calling for more “asculine”eadership, the balance of power seems to be shifting against workers.

Some are turning to collective action as a visible way to clap back. Recent examples include public petitions started by staff at WPP and JPMorgan to protest against tighter rules on office attendance. Group action has also been used to expose poor treatment of women —uch as at beer brand BrewDog —nd decry employers’tance on political issues such as Israel’ assault on Gaza.

Research shows a trend of increased willingness from staff to call out what they regard as unfair treatment. Polling by the Institute of Business Ethics last year found 64 per cent of employees were willing to speak up at work —p from 57 per cent in 2021.

“hat we’e seeing is a shift from individual employees calling the whistleblowing line and hoping someone does something about it, to a lot more collective action and a lot more use of tools like social media and going to the media,”aid Alison Taylor, NYU Stern School of Business professor and author of Higher Ground.

The rise of encrypted messaging platforms such as Telegram and Signal has facilitated this kind of direct action, Taylor added.

In a 2023 survey by law firm Herbert Smith Freehills, 59 per cent of employers said they expected employee activism to increase, with 37 per cent saying accessible channels such as social media platforms would be a big trigger.

Much of the last year has proved them correct: employers from Delta Air Lines to Meta have been hit with staff action over Gaza, while trends such as “uit-Tok” when employees post videos of themselves being fired, exposed poor treatment.

Collective staff action has also helped achieve change in the past. An online petition by female employees at Ted Baker highlighting the retailer’ “ell documented”ulture of harassment, contributed to the resignation of former chief executive Ray Kelvin, for example.

Caroline Herzig, a former team manager at the UK fashion brand, said a perception that the in-house complaints procedure was “ompromised”eant she and many colleagues felt impotent before going public.

The petition sparked an internal investigation and Kelvin resigned. Herzig credits the success to the security offered by collective action. “f you’e just one person in a huge organisation, you figure anything you say will just land on deaf ears,”he said. “ut collectively you can really make a change.”

The public approach can work because it hits companies where it hurts, targeting their reputation, said Amy Lockwood, executive director of Organise, which provides technology and support to help staff run petitions and other actions, including the Ted Baker campaign.

Organise encourages employees to pursue internal complaints procedures first. But workers are often left with no choice but to go public, said Lockwood.

US campaigner Robby Starbuck, who has been targeting employers for pursuing a woke agenda, thinks the ability of staff to easily film bad practice and upload it to social media promises greater accountability for bosses “ho step out of line” He said he regularly received such “itizen journalism” although has not made it public due to verification concerns. “ut if [people] are willing to send them to me,”e said, “ don’ see why they wouldn’ share them with someone else.”

However, workers in some sectors, such as US tech, say there has been a crackdown on activism following the inauguration of Donald Trump. Without institutional support from unions, or other authoritative backers outside the company, staff might find their efforts have limited impact.

“on’ waste time on it. I don’ care how many people sign that fucking petition,”as JPMorgan chief executive Jamie Dimon’ response to staff protesting against a rule that they must work in-person five days a week, where capacity allows, according to a recording of a town-hall meeting reviewed by Reuters. The bank has declined to comment and its policy remains in place.

Some companies have adopted less public strategies to try to keep staff quiet, from promising independent investigations to suppressing dissent with non-disclosure agreements.

headline: Toff chills out on honeymoon
author: Richard Eden
text:
edenconfidential

GEORGIA Toffolo's honeymoon period is proving to be a chilly one.

The Made in Chelsea star, 30, was with her BrewDog founder husband James Watt, 42, at the Fife Arms hotel in Aberdeenshire.

'We went... wild swimming in the icy cold river,' says Toff. Her husband says: 'I maybe enjoyed it more than Georgia - not sure if this counts as torturing my new wife.'

Cold water will hold no fear for Toff, who won I'm a Celebrity in 2017.

headline: On your bike Ythan Estuary loop
text:
A varied, scenic loop about ten miles north of Aberdeen with fine scenery, brilliant bird life and a 16th-century castle.

Starting in Ellon, head out of town on Castle Road (A920), the A948, then Lintmill Brae, which curves along the line of the Ythan Estuary. Riding though rolling farmland you will reach Forvie National Nature Reserve after about 45 minutes of fairly gentle pedalling.

Once in the reserve, be sure to follow the way-marked trails because the routes change seasonally so as not to harm wildlife. Keep your eyes peeled for eider ducks and terns, which breed on the reserve, and don't miss the one-mile detour south from Newburgh village to Newburgh Seal Beach an incredible dunescape that's home to a 400-strong seal colony. You could stop off for a quick bite at Trellis Coffee Shop in Newburgh if time allows before continuing the loop back to Ellon on quiet country lanes via the ruins of Knockhall Castle.

Don't miss BrewDog brewery, Ellon The brewery has a museum and taproom with bar food and 60 brew taps.

Route notes Start/finish 18 miles; moderate; 2-3 hours Suitable for All bikes on mostly paved surfaces

headline: News; Front Page Scrap WFH and ban school expulsions to cut crime, says expert
author: Mark McLaughlin
text:
A leading crimefighter has called for working from home to be scrapped and school exclusions to be banned, amid fears that isolated Scots are being corrupted by malign online influences.

Karyn McCluskey described school exclusion as "an infection" that breeds criminality, because it is often the first time children are picked up by the criminal justice system.

The expert in violence reduction is also worried by the switch from face-toface meetings to video conferencing, allowing people switch off their cameras or disengage from the conversation.

McCluskey is a former police analyst who co-founded the Scottish Violence Reduction Unit, the globally renowned team credited with reforming Scotland's reputation as "the murder capital of Europe" 20 years ago.

She is now chief executive of Community Justice Scotland, a non-departmental body of the Scottish government which aims to reduce reoffending.

Speaking on the Education Scotland podcast, McCluskey said: "I look at some environments where people are now working completely remotely. I disagree with that. Human beings are born connected. I need to look at your eyes and all your body movements to see how we're communicating.

"Am I connecting with you? Can we speak the uncomfortable truths among friends? It's all right for us to disagree but can we disagree well?

"I don't know how you do that in an online world, in an online space, where people are switching off their cameras, they're distracted by phones, so I quite like getting together with people."

An investigation by The Times last month into the attitudes of people born around the turn of the century, known as Generation Z, revealed only one in ten young people wanted to work in an Continued on page 2 continued from page 1 'Scrap WFH to cut crime'

full time and they admitted they were lazier than their parents.

Some companies are scaling back home-working policies over concerns they are cutting productivity.

James Watt, co-founder of BrewDog, said Britain was one of the "least workoriented countries in the world" as he suggested that people should end their obsession with "work-life balance".

The Scottish government said hybrid working, which allows civil servants to alternate between home and office, "is here to stay" after staff got used to working from home during the pandemic.

On school exclusions, McCluskey admitted that her father, the head of a school biology department, thought a zero-exclusion policy was a "hard ask" for teachers.

"I realise it's a tricky thing when I'm asking teachers to do zero exclusion," she said. "I'm always saying, 'One more kid you keep in school is one less kid I'll have in jail', because they get picked up by the police on the outside.

"Then they get touched by the justice system and it becomes like an infection, it becomes life long and life-limiting."

She criticised a taxi driver who described one Scottish school she visited as "where the scum kids go" and said this was indicative of some adults' poor understanding of the challenges of educating difficult children.

Temporary exclusions increased in 2023 for the first time in 15 years, rising almost 5 percentage points to 16.6 per 1,000 pupils, but this was well below the peak of 63.5 in 2007. Total expulsion is almost non-existent.

Education Scotland said that exclusion "should only be used as a last resort and should be proportionate".

McCluskey warned that technology risked making people more antisocial and exposed them to malign influences like Andrew Tate, the right-wing social media influencer accused of rape and human trafficking in the UK and charges abroad, which he denies.

"There are such toxic influences," she said. "We could we could talk about Andrew Tate all day and about that toxic masculinity, and how that is nudging young people's behaviour in some ways are quite abhorrent."

headline: Business; Opinion, Columns Latest  BrewDog  boss quits
text:
news in brief

James Arrow has quit as chief executive of BrewDog after less than a year in the role, triggering a shake-up at the Scottish brewer's management. The company said Arrow, who took over the top job from the co-founder James Watt last May, was stepping down "for personal reasons". He will be replaced by James Taylor, the brewer's chief financial officer, who has held senior roles at Mayborn, the childcare company, and GHD, the hair accessories manufacturer. BrewDog was founded in 2007 and operates over 120 bars, hotels and venues.

headline: Asda boss warns of no quick fix at troubled grocer
author: Jessica Clark
text:
THE boss of Asda has warned there is no quick fix at the crisis-hit supermarket as sales continue to fall after its private equity takeover four years ago.

Executive chairman Allan Leighton said profits will be 'materially' lower this year as he prepares to plough millions of pounds into a turnaround plan.

The profit warning sent shares in listed rivals sharply lower with Tesco down 8.7pc and Sainsbury's off by 7.8pc.

Despite Asda's troubles, Leighton insisted the grocer is in no rush to fill the chief executive position - which has been vacant since 2021 - as he set out a rescue strategy.

US retail giant Walmart sold Asda to the billionaire Issa brothers and private equity firm TDR Capital for £.8bn four years ago.

The supermarket chain was saddled with billions of pounds of debt and has seen its market share eroded. And it risks losing its spot as the UK's third-biggest grocer to German discounter Aldi.

Retail veteran Leighton, 71, was parachuted in last year to replace Stuart Rose, who had taken on day-to-day running of the chain after co-owner Mohsin Issa stepped down.

He was Asda chief executive between 1996 and 2001 - intro- ducing its Rollback and Asda Price initiatives and overseeing its sale to Walmart for £.7bn in 1999. He went on to lead the boards of Co-op, Royal Mail and Brewdog.

Asda sales last year were 'disappointing', Leighton admitted yesterday, as the grocer reported revenue dipped 0.8pc to £1.7bn in 2024. The supermarket is yet to hire a chief executive and there is no formal recruitment process underway. Leighton dismissed talk that Asda has been unable to fill the role because nobody wants to take on the job.

He said he could have a new boss 'tomorrow', adding that the company has been approached by 'five or six' people interested in the role.

Admitting there are no easy answers to the difficulties, he said: 'While regaining customers' trust will take time, we will undertake a substantive and well backed programme of investment in price, availability and the shopping experience to deliver this.'

The company's adjusted earnings before tax and other costs, and after rent, came in at £.14bn for the past year - up 5.8pc on 2023.

Asda said it ended the year with net debt of £.8bn, and about £00,000 cash on its balance sheet.

'Profits will be lower this year'

headline: BLAGGER TOPS
author: STUART PATTERSON
text:
CHEEKY BREWDOG INTRUDER

Watt hails fan who bluffed way into his office

A CHEEKY fan blagged his way into tycoon James Watt's office with a lifesize cutout of himself and the BrewDog founder.

Liam Collins, 35, flew to Ellon, Aberdeenshire, from London a bid to attract investment for his business coaching firm.

And he was praised for his brazen stunt by beer magnate Watt, 52, who wrote: "I think this might be the craziest pitch I've ever seen."

James wore a BrewDog T-shirt before convincing staff he had the green light to go into restricted areas at the company's HQ. He left details about his outfit, Coaching Masters, on the boss's desk with magazines, beer mats and a 10ft banner of him with his hero plus a note that said: "For James Only."

We told in January how Watt planned to launch a £million reality TV show to rival Dragons' Den, where wannabe entrepreneurs would make their pitches for cash.

Liam posted a video of his brewery invasion online.

In it, he said: "This is a message of love and appreciation for James Watt.

"We don't do things like everyone else. You are the investor that we want." Watt admitted he was impressed by the "guerilla" effort.

He said: "Liam snuck past security wearing a BrewDog tee and convinced them that I had asked him to leave his pitch on my desk.

"Absolutely mental. So, yeah, they've got my attention."

His new wife, reality TV star Georgia Toffolo, 30, also revealed she was bowled over. She said: "OMG, this is extraordinary. Wow even I struggle to get in. Love to see it. Bravo."

Liam replied: "Get yourself a BrewDog T-shirt and the struggle will be over. You're awesome Georgia." Watt is aiming to sit down with Liam and his business partner Lewis Taylor, 34, to hear their plans in person.

He said: "It takes some serious guts to pull off something like that.

"It's exactly the route I took 18 years ago to take BrewDog from a tiny brewery operating from a tiny shed in Scotland to a billion-dollar global brand. I'm looking forward to meeting with the guys soon."

But Watt revealed he has taken steps to stop others getting the same idea.

He posted: "Fair warning. Anyone else thinking about pulling a stunt like this we've upped our security measures."

stuart.patterson@the-sun.co.uk

headline: Watt and Elon are two peas in a pod
author: Richard Eden
text:
edenconfidential

BREWDOG co-founder James Watt, who married I'm A Celebrity winner Georgia Toffolo last month, has an estimated fortune of £50million.

And while he's some way from catching up with Elon Musk, who is listed by Forbes as the world's richest billionaire, Watt, 42, claims they share the same attitudes to work.

Writing online, he cites Musk's employee guidelines: 'Excessive meetings are the blight of companies - walk out when it's obvious you aren't adding value. Pick common sense as your guide. If a rule is ridiculous, change it.'

Perhaps he could also take a leaf from the book of his new wife, 30, who showed her resilience when she was covered in cockroaches in the jungle.

headline: Birthdays today
text:
Vice-Admiral Sir Charles Montgomery, pictured, president, Royal Naval Benevolent Trust (2013-23), 70; Sir Alan Ayckbourn, playwright, Absurd Person Singular (1973), 86; Jackson Carlaw, Scottish Conservative and Unionist Party MSP for Eastwood, 66; Tulsi Gabbard, US director of national intelligence, 44; Georgia Hall, golfer, winner of the Women's British Open (2018), 29; Herbie Hancock, jazz pianist, bandleader, 85; Damian Hopley, rugby union player for London Wasps (1992-98) and England (1995), founder and chief executive (1998-2022), Rugby Players' Association, 55; Matt Lawton, journalist, chief sports correspondent, The Times, 55; Allan Leighton, chairman, PizzaExpress and BrewDog, 72; Baroness (Gillian) Merron, minister for patient safety, women's health and mental health, 66; Chi Onwurah, Labour MP for Newcastle upon Tyne Central and West, 60; Lord (George) Robertson of Port Ellen, secretary-general of Nato (1999-2003), 79; Saoirse Ronan, actress, Blitz (2024), 31; Prof Wendy

Savage, obstetrician and gynaecologist, president, Doctors for Choice UK, 90; David Schwimmer, chief executive, London Stock Exchange Group, 56; Mark Thompson, set and costume designer, winner of five Olivier awards, 68; Jacob Zuma, president of South Africa (2009-18), 83.

headline: Stonegate expecting a return to growth
author: Jessica Newman
text:
The chief executive of Britain's biggest pub operator has said it is heading towards "profitable growth" this year after losses narrowed in 2024 (Jessica Newman writes).

Stonegate, which operates 4,370 sites in the UK, most of which are unbranded, made a pre-tax loss of £14 million in the 53 weeks ending September 29, from £57 million the previous year, as revenues edged up to £.7 billion, according to accounts filed at Companies House.

Stonegate, which is domiciled in the Cayman Islands and ultimately owned by TDR

Capital, the private equity firm, said the group delivered "a highly respectable performance" despite the macroeconomic environment and cost of living crisis continuing to affect the business.

Over the period, the company disposed of 70 underperforming trading sites, plus five non-licensed and two non-trading properties, generating £8 million.

David McDowall, chief executive of Stonegate, said: "As a result of the continued progress on our strategic plan, we are well positioned to maintain our trajectory of profitable growth in 2025."

McDowall, 46, a former Brewdog executive who replaced Simon Longbottom at the start of 2023, added that trading had improved in the second quarter despite tough weather conditions and persistent cost pressures.

Stonegate became the country's largest pubs business when it completed a deal to buy Ei Group, formerly Enterprise Inns, for £ billion in 2020. As well as its unbranded sites it also runs the Popworld, Slug & Lettuce and Be At One brands. As part of the merger with Ei Group, Stonegate took on additional debts of £.7 billion.

The company has been a TDR investment since 2010, when it was created via the acquisition of 333 pubs from Mitchells & Butlers.

Last summer a refinancing of Stonegate's £ billion debt load involved TDR, which also owns Asda, injecting £50 million into the business.

headline: Toff's oxygen therapy is a breath of fresh air
author: Lily Jobson
text:
LIFE'S clearly a gas for Georgia Toffolo - specifically oxygen, in this case.

The I'm A Celebrity winner and Made In Chelsea star posted a video of herself in a mask in a hyperbaric oxygen chamber.

The cubicle - championed by her new husband, BrewDog founder James Watt, 42, and described by Ms Toffolo, 34, as 'my fave biohacking tool' - boosts oxygen take-up by providing purified gas in above-normal air pressure.

Used to aid recovery from injury, it is popular as a wellness therapy. Ms Toffolo certainly seemed well. Wearing a black sheep jumper identical to one once worn by Princess Diana, she couldn't stop giggling.

headline: £M LAGER FLOPS
author: STUART PATTERSON
text:
BrewDog suffer 'dire' loss in US amid recovery bid

BEER giant BrewDog has lost a £.7million fortune in the US.

Insiders slammed the "dire" figures after bosses slashed sales across America.

Chiefs blamed a slow-down in the craft beer market.

But one shareholder said: "This is not pretty at all. The situation is pretty dire."

Accounts reveal the Scots company poured £.2million into their US operation last year.

Chiefs insisted revenues of £8million were up two per cent on 2023. But their brewery in Columbus, Ohio, was running at 30 per cent capacity.

The firm has slashed the number of states they sell to from 33 to 14. And it is making drinks for other US companies as part of a recovery bid dubbed Operation

Slingshot with contract brewing up 544 per cent.

Boss James Taylor last night insisted the US operation had profits of £76,000.

He insisted: "It's not a disappointing result at all. It's not vast but we've returned it back to profit. There is still much to do and a huge opportunity."

Accounts also revealed BrewDog US chief John Graham stood down at the start of the year after less than two years in the role. Mr

Taylor said: "We plan to continue evaluating our sales distribution and focusing on more profitable markets."

We told last year how BrewDog posted a £3million loss in 2023. The firm was founded by pals James Watt and Martin Dickie in a garage in Fraserburgh, Aberdeenshire, in 2007.

headline: Don't cheque the table, Sir Jim
author: EMILY-JANE HEAP
text:
BIGGEST BILLIONAIRE LOSER

FOOTIE CHIEF RICH LIST DIP

Fall A

By EMILY-JANE HEAP PETROCHEMICALS tycoon Sir Jim Ratcliffe has plunged down the table of Britain's mega-rich as his team continue to slump too.

The Manchester United partowner, whose Ineos firm owns the Grangemouth refinery, topped The Sunday Times Rich List in 2018 but is now its biggest loser as his fortune fell almost £.5billion.

Yet Ratcliffe, whose company bought a 27.7 per cent stake in the Red Devils last February, still has £7billion.

Meanwhile 29-year-old singer Dua Lipa soared up the charts to become the youngest on the list. She raked in £5million over the year to take her total to an estimated £15million.

Ed Sheeran, 34, was crowned the richest young musician in the 40 Under 40 list, with £70million. Harry Styles' fortune was put at £25million while Adele's stood at £70million.

25m 10m

Sir Andy Murray, 38, was 35th wealthiest overall and third richest sportsman on £10million.

Only Masters champion golfer Rory McIlroy, 36, with £60million, and boxer Anthony Joshua, 35, on £95million, were ahead of the two-time Wimbledon winner.

Sir Andy has boosted his fortune by £0million this year through property investments and the success of his luxury Cromlix hotel, near his home town of Dunblane, Perthshire.

He earned £0million in prize cash during his twodecade tennis career before retiring last August.

Harry Potter star Daniel Radcliffe is still enjoying a successful spell with an estimated £00million. I'm A Celebrity star Georgia Toffolo, 30, was said to be worth £25million after marrying Scots Brewdog co-founder James Watt in March.

JIM

King Charles ranked jointly with ex-PM Rishi Sunak and his wife Akshata Murty at £40million.

David and Victoria Beckham hit the half a billion mark for the first time.

James Bond producers Barbara Broccoli and Michael G Wilson were valued at £50million thanks to handing control of the franchise to Amazon MGM Studios.

US chat show host Ellen DeGeneres made her debut on the list at £63million, following her move to the Cotswolds in the wake of Donald's Trumps election victory.

The richest on the list, for the fourth year running, is investor Gopi Hinduja and his family with a combined wealth of £5billion.

Overall, the rich list reveals the largest fall in the number of UK billionaires in the guide's 37-year history.

The number dipped from a peak of 177 in 2022 to 156 this year. In total 350 individuals and families named hold combined wealth of £72.8billion three per cent down on last year.

List compiler Robert Watts blamed it, in part, on Chancellor Rachel Reeves' October Budget.

He said: "Young tech entrepreneurs and those running centuries-old family firms are warning of serious consequences to a range of tax changes."

However, the Top 100 still managed to collectively give £.7billion to charity last year.

Musical legend Sir Elton John, 78, donated an estimated £6.5million to arts, HIV and humanitarian charities.

Harry Potter author JK Rowling, 59, handed over £8million from her £45million fortune to charities for women and disadvantaged children.

headline: Fashion tycoon still top of rich list with £bn boost in wealth
author: Lizzie Roberts
text:
Scotland's largest landowner has increased his wealth by almost £ billion in a year, the Sunday Times Rich List 2025 has revealed, while the TV star Georgia Toffolo makes her debut following her marriage in March to James Watt, the BrewDog co-founder.

Anders Holch Povlsen is ranked the richest person in Scotland for the fourth year running.

The fashion tycoon topped the list after seeing his fortune grow by £74 million over the past year, and he is now worth £.7 billion. He is the chief executive of Bestseller, the clothing retailer founded by his father, Troels Holch Povlsen, in 1975. The Danish billionaire is also the largest shareholder in Asos, the online fashion retailer.

Toffolo topped the Scottish underforties list with a fortune totalling £25 million. She and Watt were married in an intimate ceremony in the Aberdeenshire coastal village of Gardenstown. Toffolo and Watt have documented their whirlwind romance on social media, participating in a viral online challenge last year in which couples tease one another while taking on the role of cop and suspect. In one swipe, Watt mocked his fiancé, saying: "Suspect says she doesn't have a type but she chooses from the Sunday Times Rich List."

Responding to questions this year that she was "with James for his money", Toffolo shared a screenshot of the couple's joint bank account that showed a balance of £. In a video replying to the question, she said: "Going off the state of our joint bank account, on balance I would say probably not."

Glenn Gordon, the whisky tycoon, remains, with his family, in second place, his net worth having increased by £79 million in a year to just under £.4 billion. Sir Ian Wood and family are third with £.9 billion, having seen their fortune increase by £ million.

Lady Philomena Clark and family, owners of the car retailer Arnold Clark, rose one place into fourth spot, their fortune having increased by £5 million to just over £.6 billion.

JK Rowling, the Harry Potter author, was eighth on this year's list. Her fortune remains the same at £45 million.

This year saw the largest fall in the number of billionaires in the Rich List's 37-year history, with 156 this year compared with a peak of 177 in 2022.

Robert Watts, who compiled the decades-old list this year, said the pattern was "changing".

"Our billionaire count is down and the combined wealth of those who feature in our research is falling," he said.

"We are also finding fewer of the world's super-rich are coming to live in the UK. This year we were also struck by the strength of criticism for Rachel Reeves's Treasury.

"We expected the abolition of nondom status would anger affluent people from overseas. But homegrown young tech entrepreneurs and those running centuries-old family firms are also warning of serious consequences to a range of tax changes unveiled in last October's budget.

"Our research continues to find a wide variety of selfmade entrepreneurs building fortunes, not just from artificial intelligence, video games and new technologies but also mundane, everyday items such as makeup, radiators and jogging bottoms.

"We know many of our readers find these people and their stories inspiring especially the many who had tough starts or setbacks to their lives and careers." The true cause of inequality in the UK, Fraser Nelson, page 21 See The Sunday Times magazine for the full list or visit thetimes.com/sundaytimes-rich-list

headline: King's now twice as wealthy as the late Queen
author: Rebecca English
text:
THE King's personal fortune has increased by £0 million to £40 million in the past year - making him twice as wealthy as his late mother Queen Elizabeth, according to the latest Sunday Times Rich List.

The monarch, 76, ranks joint 238th in the list of the UK's 350 wealthiest people and families, up 20 places from 258th in 2024, the researchers said.

Charles - who yesterday saw Prince William installed as Great Master of the Order of the Bath at Westminster Abbey - is said to be £40million richer than David and Victoria Beckham, who are reported to be worth £00million, making the former England captain Britain's richest sports star.

The Queen's wealth was said to be £70million when she died in 2022, with Charles now estimated to be £70million richer, rising from £10million last year.

Elsewhere on the list, 29-year-old pop star Dua Lipa was the youngest person in the under-40s table with an estimated worth of £15million.

The Brit and Grammy winner was 34th on the '40 under 40' list, after Ed Sheeran, 34, at number 13 with £70million.

Only personal assets are included by The Sunday Times when assessing the sovereign's wealth. The calculations are based on publicly-available records.

But it is not clear how they are able to come to such an exact figure given so little is known about the Royal Family's personal finances, in line with most individual's private wealth.

The sovereign owns a number of private properties, namely Sandringham in Norfolk, which is on a 20,000-acre estate mostly comprising farms and some rental properties, and Balmoral Castle in Scotland, which comes with 50,000 acres.

The King will also no doubt have a portfolio of private investments based on inherited wealth, as well as antiques, jewellery, cars and horses. This is separate from his income from the Duchy of Lancaster and the £.1 billion Crown Estate. There are strict rules which curtail his ability to sell or profit from them, instead he uses the income to fund his public work as sovereign and living costs.

The Sunday Times claims that the King's investment portfolio could be worth £25 million and the horses he inherited from the Queen £7 million. Buckingham Palace did not respond to request for comment.
