headline: Toff: Kids Brewed up wed to James
author: CHRIS TAYLOR
text:
BREWDOG founder James Watt and Georgia Toffolo's intimate wedding was planned in just 20 minutes by his children.

The I'm A Celebrity winner, 30, told how the beer tycoon's girls Evie, ten, and Mia, eight, pulled together the plans one night after school.

Guests were given only 48 hours notice of the ceremony at a tiny church in Gardenstown, Aberdeenshire, in March.

The newlyweds then posed for pictures on a lobster boat belonging to James' late dad Jim before walking to the local pub for a spread.

Ex-PM Boris Johnson's dad Stanley, 84, played host at their reception after he and the bride hit it off in the ITV jungle.

Georgia said: "The children planned the wedding in 20 minutes.

"There wasn't an ounce of stress. We walked to the pub then zipped to BrewDog HQ for burgers and ceilidh dancing."

headline: Flaunting wealth only fuels this age of envy
author: James Marriott
text:
Social media means the once discreet super-rich are integrated into our lives but how long will we tolerate their excesses?

The term "hen do" is inadequate to the grandiose, interstellar ambition of Lauren Sáchez's prenuptial celebrations. Having whirled across the stratosphere in one of her husband-to-be's Blue Origin spacecraft, Jeff Bezos's intended spent last weekend in Paris in the company of Kim Kardashian, Katy Perry and other members of the 21st-century's gilded entertainment overclass. Once upon a time, this ghastly event might at least have been dignified with an aura of secrecy. This being 2025, it was widely publicised by all the attendees. Sáchez pneumatic, cat-eyed, apparently upholstered has posted picture after picture of herself on Instagram, grimacing glassily from various deluxe restaurants, spas and river cruises.

The public response to this ostentation has been gratifyingly hostile. "Gluttonous" is an adjective that has featured prominently in the newspaper coverage. Had Marie Antoinette offered a guided tour of her model dairy farm to a band of starving sans culottes, she might have met with a more sympathetic reception.

Economists and political scientists have spent the best part of a decade searching for the causes of anti-establishment rage. Inequality, immigration and "elite overproduction" are among the convincing explanations they have supplied. A minor but underrated factor may simply be that we see more of the uber-wealthy than ever before: their yachts, their jet skis, their private islands. You might call this the theory of "elite over-exposure". The result, I suspect, has been to fuel the ambient fug of fury and resentment that has been hanging about our politics for the better part of a decade.

Previous generations of billionaire businessmen did not aspire to become celebrities. The odd deferential journalist might have been ushered through the electronic gates to gather some pearls of entrepreneurial wisdom; the occasional lavish giltand-velvet interior might have made it into a Sunday supplement. But generally, the money was enough.

Nowadays, the masters of the universe require not only riches but fame. And they get it. The spectacle of obscene wealth is all around us. James Watt, the founder of BrewDog, broadcast his recent holiday in the Maldives virtually nonstop. Last week, the billionaire venture capitalist turned longevity influencer Bryan Johnson posted pictures of himself having all the plasma extracted from his body on social media (a health treatment, perplexingly). A few days ago OpenAI's CEO Sam Altman allowed himself to be filmed ineptly cooking "some sort of pasta" in his barren and immaculate kitchen.

Beneath those men on their untouchable pinnacles of wealth are the legions of mere millionaires the influencers, actors and minor Kardashians whose never-ending holidays, poolside selfies and cavernous, tasteless mansions make up a great deal of what millions of people spend hours every day staring at on their phones. Thanks to the internet, the rich are integrated into the fabric of our lives. TV, of course, pioneered the obsession, but it also dwelt on mediocrity, failure and averageness in a way that has no real equivalent in the ever-perfect online world. Eastenders' Albert Square and Del Boy's Peckham would get little traction on Instagram. And so what we mostly look at nowadays is the rich.

The proximity is crucial. The super-rich are not only ubiquitous but closer to us than ever before. In the past when the wealthy were glimpsed in magazines and on television, they arrived in our lives as if from another planet, the alien and glamorously unreachable world of celebrity. Nowadays, thanks to the algorithmic blizzard in which information is presented to us, they make their cameos next to pictures of our friends and family. The juxtapositions invite unhappy comparisons; my damp caravanning holiday in the Scottish borders vs Lauren Sáchez's trip to the near edge of space.

Psychologists since Freud have known that proximity fuels resentment. It is down to the famous "narcissism of small differences" that we brood over our friends' holidays and colleagues' promotions but rarely over the fact the King, not us, gets to live in Buckingham Palace. But social media has the flattening effect of placing the rich on our level and exposing them to bitter, personal resentments that would never have reached them before just look at the comments on Sáchez's Instagram account.

If this sounds trivial as a factor in politics it is worth recalling that the perception of inequality matters as much as its reality. In his classic study of medieval apocalyptic movements, The Pursuit of the Millennium, the historian Norman Cohn explains how urbanisation fed social disorder in the later middle ages. When peasants survived by subsistence farming in remote and impoverished rural areas, they had little context for their poverty. But as more and more people moved to towns they found themselves rubbing up against wealth for the first time. Often they were no poorer than before but their new perception of a contrast produced "a bitter sense of frustration", which fuelled the flagellant cults and peasant prophets who demanded the social order be turned upside down and the world forged anew.

Then as now these resentments were rarely logically directed (into plans for wealth redistribution. for example, or taxes on spice merchants) but they fed a freefloating mood of discontent and rage.

Envy is a powerful force. Though I suspect it is only a small part of the story of 21st-century populism, it is worth considering when we seek to explain the anti-elite turbulence that is shaking dynamic America and stagnant Europe alike. Were I extravagantly wealthy I might consider it prudent to stay off Instagram.

author: RICHARD EDEN
text: ÷BREWDOG co-founder James Watt, who married I'm A Celebrity winner Georgia Toffolo in March, has a confession - he enjoys reading rude online reviews of his pub chain. 'A s*** hipster Wetherspoons', and 'Nobody knew how to brew beer until this pair of t***s graduated', are among his favourites. 'Strong opinions meant we were making waves. If your brand has no haters, it probably has no heartbeat.'

headline: Boss defends private equity
author: Isabella Fish
text:
Asda has struggled because of weak turnaround plans, not because the grocery chain is private equity-owned, its boss insisted.

"The reason that Asda has not performed is not because it's private equity-owned," Allan Leighton, the retailer's chairman, said. "The reason Asda has not performed is it didn't have a plan that made sense in the marketplace and it lost the trust of its customers and the trust of its colleagues. The business has been guilty of too many short-term plans and too many turnarounds."

The Leeds-based grocer has become a poster boy for critics of the private equity industry since it was bought by TDR and the Issa brothers. Morrisons, bought for £ billion in a leveraged buyout by Clayton Dubilier & Rice in 2021, has suffered similar criticism.

"I deal in a lot of private equity businesses and all that is said is often nonsense," Leighton said, adding that TDR had been "incredibly supportive".

Leighton, 71, has led a number of UK and international consumer businesses, including serving as chief executive of Asda from 1996 to 2001, when he helped to deliver a dramatic turnaround. After staving off bankruptcy the grocer went on to enjoy an extraordinary period of growth, lowering prices and launching the George fashion brand, before being bought by Walmart in 1999 for £.7 billion.

Leighton also served as president of Loblaw Companies, North America's second-largest food retail business, and spent nine years as chairman of the Co-op. He has been the chairman of BrewDog, the beer company, since 2021.

headline: Grubs up? I'm a Celeb's Toffolo seeks 'fun chef'
author: RICHARD EDEN
text:
IN THE jungle, Georgia Toffolo retched her way through cockroaches, maggots and a smoothie of crocodile anus and pig testicles.

Back home, the I'm A Celebrity winner prefers finer fare.

Toff, 30, is seeking a chef to serve her and her new husband, BrewDog co-founder James Watt, 43.

'We're looking for a fun young chef to join our family home in London,' she says in an advert on members-only website Radio H-P.

'This is a full-time role with flexible hours, cooking for me, my husband and our children.' Watt has two children with ex-wife Johanna Basford.

headline: CAUGHT ON THE HOPS
author: TARA EVANS;James Flanders;Adele Cooke ; Alice Grahns
text:
money@the-sun.co.uk

mon y

daily

Edited by TARA EVANS with James Flanders, Adele Cooke & Alice Grahns

Craft beer prices rocketing

CRAFT beer prices have rocketed since the start of the year giving drinkers a major hangover.

Prices have jumped by up to 12.5 per cent since January, The Morning Advertiser Pint Price Survey has revealed.

The Campaign for Real Ale (CAMRA) said many landlords are having to charge more just to stay open in the face of rising raw material costs and tax rises.

The survey said the biggest price rise was for BrewDog Punk IPA, which was £.64 a pint in January but is now £.34.

Camden Hells has also climbed to £.34, up 10.8 per cent from £.72.

Others have seen more modest increases, just 1.7 per cent for Camden Pale Ale, which has gone from £.59 to £.69.

The average price of a Costly draught pint across all types has crept up to £.17, compared with £.08 six months ago.

CAMRA chairman Ash Corbett- Collins said: "It's incredibly frustrating for consumers to see the price of a pint rise yet again.

"It's really important that we talk about the reasons that the price of beer is rising it's hikes in the price of raw materials for brewers, energy costs staying high, pubs being squeezed by a challenging business rates system and rises in National Insurance contributions.

"Increasing the price of a pint is sometimes the only option for pubs, as not doing so risks closing their doors for ever."

lWHITBREAD, owner of Beefeater and Brewers Fayre, has reported a 16 per cent fall in food and drink sales as it overhauls its restaurants.

Punk IPA

headline: Brewdog  puts its focus on IPA over IPO
author: Greig Cameron
text:
Brewdog has rowed back on plans to become a public company as its chief executive plots a return to growth with "sensible financial discipline".

A flotation has long been mooted for the maker of craft beers such as Punk IPA and Elvis Juice with suggestions of a valuation of about £ billion.

James Taylor, who stepped up from chief financial officer in March, said an initial public offering was "not on the agenda" as the company moved back into profit under its preferred metric for the first time since 2021.

Brewdog pointed to a growing share of the UK beer market along with a strong performance in Australia and the United States. Net revenue, which does not include excise duty, for last year came in at £80 million, which was flat on the previous 12 months.

The company said its adjusted earnings before deductions were £.5 million last year, compared with a loss of £.5 million in the previous year. It booked a pre-tax loss of £9.2 million in 2023, which included an impairment charge of about £4 million.

Brewdog declined to give a pre-tax figure for 2024 but Taylor said there would be a loss under that measure and there would be no dividend paid for the year. He also said that the plan was to get back into top-line growth this year and improve profitability.

Brewdog was founded by James Watt and Martin Dickie in Aberdeenshire in 2007 and the pair established a reputation for unconventional marketing methods and raising tens of millions of pounds through crowdfunding.

It still has its headquarters and main brewery in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, but has other sites in Australia, Germany and the US. It runs more than 120 bars and hotels employing 2,400 people.

Last year Brewdog secured a deal with Marylebone Cricket Club to provide beer at Lord's and more recently became the official partner for West Ham United at the London Stadium.

Lauren Carrol, chief operating officer, said Brewdog was looking for other opportunities at festivals and stadiums where partners wanted to do more than "slap our logo on something".

Watt and Dickie retain stakes and are still directors but they have stepped back from day-to-day operations.

The culture of the business was criticised in 2021 after an open letter from staff outlined a toxic atmosphere and a culture of fear.

Watt denied claims of inappropriate behaviour but vowed to learn lessons. Allan Leighton, best known for his roles at Asda, was brought in as Brewdog chairman and a review was held.

Taylor said the company was "way beyond" the historical allegations of poor culture and pointed out that 200 staff had been trained as mental health first aiders last year.

headline: BrewDog  leaves punk behind to unleash new era of wellbeing
author: Greig Cameron
text:
Brewdog has trained 200 staff as mental health first aiders as the "punk" craft beer brand aims to distance itself from historic claims of bullying and a "culture of fear".

James Taylor, the new chief executive, insists the business is "well past" the previous controversies which culminated in dozens of former employees signing an open letter claiming they were bullied and treated like objects.

The company is looking to shed its past and usher in an era focused on employee wellbeing and a broader appeal.

The era of provocative advertising including serving beers in taxidermised animals and projecting nearly naked images of the founders on to the Houses of Parliament appears to be on hold. A recent rebrand showcases a softer, more family-friendly image, highlighting BrewDog's diverse menu of offerings across its more than 120 bars and hotels.

Lauren Carrol, its chief operating officer, acknowledged there had been a "fresh direction" since the departure of the co-founders James Watt and Martin Dickie, whose aggressive marketing and rapid expansion were often overshadowed by allegations of a toxic work environment.

"We listen and we learn" is more than just a mantra; it's now embedded in the company's charter. Regular forums empower employees to voice ideas and concerns, fostering an open dialogue that was said to have been absent in previous years. While Watt and Dickie remain directors and shareholders, Taylor described them as "very, very supportive" but hands off.

The so-called beer punks took the business from an Aberdeenshire garage to a global concern. Their mix of highstrength beers, marketing stunts and bold proclamations attracted swathes of fans with many going on to invest as the business raised tens of millions of pounds through crowdfunding. Allegations of a toxic culture emerged in 2021, which Watt denied, although he vowed to learn lessons. "We've updated our charter, which is how we as leaders will behave, how we want the team to behave and really tried to kickstart this fresh direction and energy," Carrol said. "We do staff surveys every six months so we understand how the business is feeling and get that temperature check."

Taylor said that when he and Carrol took over the priority was "looking after the people who work for us".

He added: "We have the most incredible staff, the most incredible crew as we call them here, both in terms of our hospitality, but also in terms of the brewery side and internationally.

"In terms of the historic side of the business, we're way beyond that. That's not recognised in our business."

headline: Ex- BrewDog  boss on high after his first cannabis crop
author: Laura Paterson
text:
A BrewDog co-founder is about to harvest his first full crop of medical cannabis at one of the UK's pioneering legal farms for the drug.

Martin Dickie plans to ramp domestic production and invest £0 million in his new business Waterside Pharmaceuticals in the next five years.

The first full harvest at the high-tech indoor vertical farm near Newburgh, Aberdeenshire, is expected to provide up to 9kg of medical cannabis, but the company plans to increase this to 200kg a year.

Dickie, who founded the craft beer brewer with James Watt in 2007 and retains 21 per cent ownership of the business, expects annual turnover for his new venture will eventually hit tens of millions of pounds.

The Home Office granted the pharmaceutical firm a licence to cultivate and supply medical cannabis last year.

Doctors on the General Medical Council's specialist register have been able to prescribe cannabis-derived medicines for certain conditions in the UK since a change in the law in 2018.

Dickie, 42, said: "Our aim is to become the UK market leader in medical cannabis.

"We have ambitious plans to expand the business, creating up to 100 new jobs in the local economy and investing around £0 million over the next five years.

"Our longer-term ambitions involve building a second facility co-located with a renewable energy source, minimising our environmental impact, creating jobs and diversifying the rural economy."

He added: "The global market for medical cannabis is growing rapidly as its clinical applications become better researched and understood.

"In the UK medical cannabis can now be prescribed by specialist doctors, with an estimated 50,000 to 60,000 patients thought to be benefiting from cannabis-derived medicines. That figure is growing year-on-year, but supply depends largely on imported medicines.

"Waterside Pharmaceuticals is on a mission to provide safe, efficacious and cost-effective medicine that can help thousands of people."

The married father of three has invested millions into the business, the Press and Journal first reported.

He told the newspaper an acquaintance used medical cannabis and found it very helpful for their anxiety and mental health.

"We want to develop domestic production," he added. "I've spent a lot of time in America, where I had exposure to hemp and medicinal cannabis production in a controlled environment."

At the farm, the plants are kept in controlled conditions with 12 hours of bright artificial light, followed by 12 hours of darkness, to stimulate growth.

Morag Thomas, Waterside Pharmaceuticals' chief operating officer, who was previously supply chain head at BrewDog, said: "Our unique approach allows us to control and replicate light, heat and irrigation, without any need for pesticides or fungicides, resulting in a premium quality product.

"We use sustainably sourced energy, and rainwater for irrigation and we are committed to minimising the environmental impact of our operations.

"We are working with researchers at University of Aberdeen and University of Lincoln on projects exploring the use of AI and automation to optimise production.

"We have worked with local and national stakeholders at every stage, liaising with the Home Office, MHRA, local authority, community representatives and Police Scotland, as well as the Aberdeenshire's business and economic development organisations."

headline: FANS' SELECTION HEADACHE
author: DAVID LLOYD
text:
THEY are a very thirsty lot here in St John's Wood. The MCC Media Guide made for an interesting read, particularly the back page where they thanked the partners and suppliers. Among them were BrewDog, Majestic Wine, Veuve Clicquot, Westons Stowford Press and Hendrick's Gin.

headline: GET THE INKS IN
author: STUART PATTERSON
text:
Brewdog offer free tattoos at annual booze-up

BREWDOG is offering fans free tattoos at its annual booze-up but they will have to be breathalysed first.

Bosses at the beer giant are transforming a room at their Aberdeenshire HQ into an inking parlour.

A host of funky designs are on offer at the Annual General

Mayhem event, including cartoon characters Scooby Doo and Garfield.

But anyone who is over the limit will be turned away.

An email from the firm warns: "Anyone under the influence of alcohol or drugs will not be permitted to receive a tattoo.

"We know the AGM can get lively but breathalysers will be used for all participants wishing to be tattooed." Community engagement manager Michael Kenyon said: "I would advise to drink minimal prior to tattoo."

Thousands of ale lovers are set to flock to the craft beer firm's base in Ellon this weekend for the event.

The company has teamed up with local parlour Inked Pleasure to offer the tattoos.

Anyone signing up must agree to a host of conditions and complete a consent form on the day. It is thought all of the 17 slots for the free tatts have already been snapped up.

But one fan said: "I personally think this is the worst idea.

"Despite the safeguards, properly think about it beforehand."

But Michael replied: "Everything has been taken into consideration." stuart.patterson@the-sun.co.uk

headline: Brewdog  to close ten bars
text:
news in brief

Brewdog is to close ten of its bars in Britain, including the first one it ever opened Brewdog Gallowgate in Aberdeen, which was opened in 2010. Its bar in Camden, the first it opened in London in 2011, is also going to close. The company said it had been unable to find a way to keep the locations "commercially viable" and the changes would put its bar division on the road to "long-term and profitable growth". Brewdog's net revenue was flat at £80 million in 2024, although adjusted earnings before deductions were £.5 million, compared with a loss of £.5 million in 2023.

headline: Shock at  BrewDog  axe for ten bars
author: PAIGE BERESFORD
text:
SCOTTISH craft beer chain BrewDog is to shut ten bars across the UK.

Those set for closure include the brewer's flagship first boozer in Gallowgate, Aberdeen.

Another in Dundee is on the list of bars deemed non-viable "due to their size, location and other limiting factors".

Furious union chiefs said staff only learned yesterday their jobs could be gone by Friday.

Unite Hospitality said: "In what universe is three days a morally (or legally) acceptable notice period for a proper redundancy consultation for workers impacted?" BrewDog said it hopes to redeploy some staff.

CEO James Taylor said: "This is not a decision we've taken lightly."

BrewDog based in Ellon, Aberdeenshire was co-founded by James Watt and Martin Dickie in 2007.

headline: 'IT'S YOUR DAY!' TOP TIPS FOR BRIDES BY NEWLYWED TOFF
author: Richard Eden
text:
HAVING married BrewDog pub chain founder James Watt in March, Georgia Toffolo has compiled some advice for brides-to-be.

Here are the seven pearls of wisdom from the I'm A Celebrity winner, 30, who wore a Divine Atelier gown, left, when she exchanged vows with Watt, 43, both pictured right, in Aberdeenshire:

You shouldn't have to invite people for the sake of it. Your guest list isn't a diplomatic summit. Invite people you love. It's your day, not networking. You shouldn't feel guilty for that. Wedding cakes are a waste of money. We had the children bake ours that morning. No fancy tiers, no overpriced price tag. Just made with pure love. Best decision ever.

Flowers are overpriced and overrated. I grabbed daffodils straight out of the vase on our kitchen table, and you know what? They were perfect. £ from the supermarket and not a single florist in sight.

You don't need to spend the morning apart. Who made that rule up? Waking up together on your wedding day is actually really romantic (and calming). The chance to share your nerves and excitement together is something to be cherished.

Children SHOULD be at weddings. Children bring the best chaos, particularly on the dance floor. They add magic, not mess.

No need for a threecourse meal. We had burgers at BrewDog. Grazing tables, pizza ovens, food trucks... all way more fun.

You don't have to change your surname. He can take yours. Or double-barrel. Tradition is optional, your identity isn't.

headline: YOU'VE HAD 1 TATTOO MANY
author: STUART PATTERSON
text:
Sozzled punters barred

BREWDOG BASH INKINGS..

SOZZLED BrewDog fans were turned away when they tried to get free tattoos at the beer giant's annual booze-up.

Bosses offered inkings for nothing at the event but had warned hopefuls they would be breathalysed.

One partygoer was found to be three-times the limit when he turned up looking for his body art freebie at 11am.

An onlooker said: "Despite the time he was already pretty blearyeyed and unsteady on his feet. Staff told him he looked pretty drunk and he agreed, but he wanted to do the breathalyser anyway.

"He seemed pretty happy that he was so much over the limit when it was still morning."

Others who booked appointments also failed the breath test but there were still plenty who passed to get their tatts done.

Thousands of the craft beer company's biggest fans flocked to its HQ in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, for the bash on July 19.

We told how for the first time bosses decided to give away tattoos, teaming up with local studio Painful Pleasure Ink.

A room was transformed into an inking parlour for the day, with only 17 spots available.

They were quickly filled, with one superfan Kat Forbes even getting the brand's logo inked in black on her arm.

It is thought the limit on the breathalyser was set to lower than those used by cops to catch drink-drivers.

Live music, brewery tours and special tastings were on offer, and the firm's Wingman IPA mascot was there too.

One reveller said: "It was a great day and everyone was in a brilliant mood.

"The folk who got turned away were fine with it.

"There was no aggro or anything like that. It was all part of the experience."

On their Instagram, Painful Pleasure said: "We had an absolute blast. Tattoos were flying, good times were flowing. So many people inked, so many memories made and every one of them unforgettable. Massive love to everyone who trusted us with their skin and who made this event one for the books."

The bash took place just days before it emerged BrewDog was to close ten of its bars across the UK, including its first ever boozer in Gallowgate, Aberdeen, and one in Dundee.

We told how the decision sparked fury from union chiefs over a three-day consultation.

Bosses say the pubs are nonibl " d to th ir si location va e ue e ze , and other limiting factors".

headline: BEER AD BAN
text:
A BEER ad has been banned for suggesting it can overcome boredom, loneliness or disappointment.

The poster for Brewdog's Wingman beer vowed it had "always got your back", unlike the wifi, weather and your unpunctual buddy.

The Advertising Standards Authority investigated after receiving one complaint. Brewdog claimed the ad merely suggested the beer was reliable.

However, the ASA concluded it breached its code as it implied alcohol could overcome problems and ruled that it must not appear again.

headline: One complaint leads to ban for  BrewDog 's 'got your back' ad
author: Josie Clarke
text:
A BrewDog poster has been banned for implying that alcohol could overcome boredom, loneliness or disappointment, after one complaint to the advertising watchdog.

The advertisement for the brewer's Wingman beer, seen in May, featured the headline: "BrewDog. Always got your back." It continued underneath: "Some things in life go AWOL wi-fi fails, the weather turns hostile and your buddy's 'five minutes' turns into a fullscale delay. But Wingman? Wingman stands firm. Always on station, always mission-ready, always got your back. Because every great operation deserves a great Wingman."

The Advertising Standards Authority (ASA) received a complaint that the ad implied alcohol was a remedy for disappointment, suffering and isolation.

BrewDog, which is based in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, said the ad did not imply that the beer provided comfort, relief or escape from distress, and positioned the product as being of reliable quality rather than a remedy or coping mechanism. It also did not believe the ad claimed or implied that the beer had any therapeutic or mood-altering effects, or that suggested alcohol was essential or a priority in life.

The ASA acknowledged that the ad had an air force theme, including the product's name, references to "always on station, always mission-ready", while some images included a bird wearing a flying jacket, helmet and goggles. The watchdog acknowledged that the scenarios presented in the ad were mundane and everyday annoyances.

However, it said:"We considered that although the ad used metaphorical language to evoke Wingman's reliability, consistency and a sense of camaraderie, the scenarios presented were reallife problems that were likely to impact people in a negative way and the implication was that Wingman was a solution to overcoming those problems that were likely to result in boredom, frustration and potentially loneliness. For those reasons, we considered the ad implied that drinking alcohol could overcome boredom, loneliness or other problems and concluded it breached the code."

The ASA ruled that the ad must not appear again, adding: "We told BrewDog to ensure their future ads did not imply that drinking alcohol could overcome boredom, loneliness or other problems."

BrewDog said: "We are disappointed with the outcome, which we believe does not reflect the spirit or intent behind the campaign. That said, we won't be running the ad again."

headline: Being an angel investor is tougher than it looks
author: Craig Coben
text:
Former financiers might assume a knack for picking winners, but investing alone requires a different set of capabilities

The writer is a former global head of equity capital markets at Bank of America and is now a managing director at Seda Experts

Not long after I left investment banking, I was invited to an angel investing event in central London. Dozens of early-stage companies had set up stalls in a large reception hall to showcase businesses ranging from clean tech to immersive gaming to sports gizmos. Founders weren’ pushing a hard sell; they just offered demos and a chat. The whole affair was impressively run.

As I looked around the room, I recognised several faces. Many were late-career or retired managing directors from banks and funds, now with time on their hands and, presumably, capital to deploy. I wondered: Was this the “mart money” should invest alongside? How many of us truly knew what we were doing?

Angel investing often appeals to former financiers as a natural next step. It enables them to stay engaged, apply experience, and maybe earn a big return. There’ the draw of meeting visionary founders and the hope of backing the next breakout success. Once you leave investment banking, the funding pitches start pouring in. I’e lost count of how many have landed in my inbox.

It’ all quite beguiling. After years of analysing balance sheets, modelling cash flows and structuring deals, it’ tempting for former bankers to think they can identify start-up winners. But venture runs on a different rhythm. It’ longer and less structured than any investment banking project.

The contrast couldn’ be sharper. In banking, you operate within an institutional machine, supported by an army of analysts, sector specialists and lawyers. Angel investing strips away that infrastructure; it’ just you, a pitch deck, and a half-hour video call. Rigorous financial forecasting and due diligence give way to guesswork around total addressable market and a seat-of-the-pants judgment of management’ capabilities.

The time horizon also poses a challenge. Most early-stage investments remain illiquid for five to 10 years, or longer. You write a cheque and then wait. And wait. Maybe you get asked to put in more money. And then you wait some more. One angel quipped that the real challenge wasn’ the capital locked up, but the boredom. Staying engaged for the long haul, often without visibility or control, takes patience and discipline.

Even when things go well, jackpot returns can prove elusive. Structured notes, dilution and liquidation waterfalls can erode upside. BrewDog offers a stark reminder: as FT Alphaville reported, the company placed preference shares with a private equity firm in 2017, enabling the founders to take out a big chunk of money. But the high cost of servicing the 18 per cent compounding coupon has left ordinary shareholders with scraps. In another case, a former colleague backed a wildly successful fintech unicorn early on, only to see her stake heavily diluted in later rounds. She’ sitting on a respectable paper gain, but it’ no windfall —nd she’ still waiting for her chance to cash out.

Still, ex-bankers can succeed, especially those who approach angel investing with seriousness and stamina. A few I know have built impressive portfolios. One took a board seat early, helped shape strategy, and now chairs a profitable company. Others have clubbed together into syndicates, pooling funds to diversify, negotiate better terms and conduct better due diligence. These aren’ casual successes; they reflect strategic, committed effort over several years.

Former financiers add tangible value. Founders often have deep but narrow knowledge; bankers can provide macro context, sector insight and financial acumen. They can also bring their personal networks, including potential investors, advisers and business partners.

But nothing guarantees success. Top angels assume most investments won’ pan out, relying on a few home runs to make up for a long tail of strikeouts. While tax incentives like the UK’ Enterprise Investment Scheme may boost returns and cushion losses with generous reliefs and offsets, they don’ change the underlying odds of failure.

The maths remains brutal. One ex-colleague, now a seasoned angel, shared his portfolio: 45 investments, with 10 complete losses and two positioned to return capital at a gain. The remaining 33, he says, have “ption value” While the strategy might eventually work, it demands deep pockets and deeper reservoirs of patience. Despite good paydays as an investment banker, he lacks cash flow because so much capital is tied up in these ventures. This explains why he moved recently to a low-tax jurisdiction to work as CFO for a private firm.

Angel investing can be stimulating and, occasionally, lucrative. Bankers have the background and skillset to succeed. But those expecting a road to quick riches will be disappointed. This is no retirement hobby.

Letter in response to this article:

Angel investors’otives include virtuous reasons / From Colin Mason, Emeritus Professor of Entrepreneurship, Honorary Senior Research Fellow, Adam Smith Business School, University of Glasgow, Glasgow, UK

headline: Brewdog : We have plenty to bark about despite pub setback
author: Greig Cameron
text:
The boss of Brewdog is confident of bouncing back even as it emerged almost 1,900 pubs had stopped stocking its draught beers.

James Taylor, chief executive of the company, said he was "excited" by efforts to widen the group's appeal.

The decline, over a period of two years, is understood to be largely a result of a shift in the policies of one customer that has limited the space available for craft products.

The Aberdeenshire business noted it had grown market share in independent hospitality sites to offset the volumes lost from its smaller presence among pub operators. Alongside that it is moving into supplying sports venues, such as Lord's cricket ground and West Ham, as well as the Isle of Wight Festival.

Taylor said: "We could not be more excited about the future. We are bringing Brewdog to a broader and more diverse audience of beer drinkers."

His response came after reports, firstly in the Sunday Telegraph, that 1,860 British pubs had stopped stocking Brewdog's draught range in the past two years. That was described as equivalent to more than a third of distribution with its best-known beer, Punk IPA, particularly hard hit. It has long been held up as the best selling craft beer in the UK in hospitality venues and shops.

Brewdog said it was putting marketing behind its Wingman brand, a session IPA, and that was now among the quickest growing drinks in its category.

Lauren Carrol, chief operating officer, argued that every independent brewer had been affected as pub groups reshuffled their ranges. She said: "We saw the trend coming, which is why we've shifted focus to high-impact channels like festivals, stadiums, and independent [pubs]."

The Scottish Licensed Trade Association recently warned record levels of its members were considering closing because of difficult trading conditions.

Taylor said in June that revenue for 2024 was flat at £80 million but adjusted earnings were £.5 million.

headline: BREWDOG  BOSS DICKIE QUITTING
author: STUART PATTERSON
text:
THE co-founder of BrewDog yesterday announced he is quitting the troubled beer giant.

Martin Dickie, 42, told of his "sadness" at leaving the global chain he set up in his mum's garage wth i pal James Watt and gave "personal reasons".

The firm last month announced the closure of ten pubs, including its first boozer in Aberdeen, following record £3million losses in 2023. Their American arm had a £.7million shortfall last year.

Mr Dickie is expected to focus on his medicinal cannabis-producing firm, Waterside Pharmaceuticals.

He had been in charge of BrewDog's distillery. He said: "I've decided to step away from BrewDog and the wider alcohol industry. This business has been my life for the last 18 years and I've enjoyed (almost) every minute."

Both co-founders were said to have shared a £00million payout after a private investment firm bought a stake in the company they started as a tiny craft beer maker in 2007 in Ellon, Aberdeenshire.

Watt stepped down as boss last year. CEO James Taylor said yesterday: "Martin's passion, creativity and drive have left an indelible mark."

headline: BrewDog  co-founder quits in latest blow for company
author: David Leask
text:
BrewDog has moved further away from its "punk" roots as the second of its two co-founders quit the business.

Martin Dickie announced he had left the Scottish craft beer company, saying he had done so for "personal reasons". The company's other co-founder, James Watt, stepped down as chief executive last year.

Dickie announced "ambitious" plans last month for his cannabis farm near Newburgh, Aberdeenshire, to become the UK market leader in medical cannabis. He said he will invest £0 million.

At the age of 24, the two men launched the beer brand in 2007 in northeast Scotland, making small batches and filling their bottles by hand.

The so-called "beer punks" took their business from a garage in Aberdeenshire to a global concern.

Their mix of high-strength beers, controversial marketing stunts and bold proclamations helped them attract legions of fans, many of whom went on to invest as the business raised tens of millions of pounds through crowdfunding. But there were problems too, including historical claims of alleged bullying and a "culture of fear".

James Taylor, the company's new chief executive, has insisted that the business is "well past" those controversies, which culminated in dozens of former employees signing an open letter claiming they were bullied and treated like objects.

BrewDog announced last month that it was shutting its flagship pub in Aberdeen and nine other bars across the UK, blaming poor trading conditions.

Taylor said: "Martin's contributions to Brewdog have been immeasurable. His creativity, passion and relentless drive have shaped our company over the years and inspired countless others in the industry.

"We would like to thank him for his focus primarily on product quality, new product development, workplace safety and sustainable supplier relationships. Martin will always be part of the Brewdog family and we wish him every success in the future."

Dickie, who is keeping an equity stake in the firm, said: "After over two decades in the brewing and distilling arena, sadly, for personal reasons, it's time for me to leave the industry that I love deeply and hopefully had a positive impact in.

"Leaving BrewDog isn't easy, but I'm ready to spend less time travelling and spend some more time at home with my young family."

The businessman left a parting message to staff, according to the Press & Journal. "People are spending their hard-earned money on something we have made, poured and served," he said. "Make sure the details are there their glass is spotless (bars), the labels straight (packaging) and of course the beer tastes great (procurement, production, quality, supply chain). Make their BrewDog moment special. And always surprise and delight."

headline: BrewDog  losses hit £00m in two years
author: STUART PATTERSON
text:
BREWDOG lost nearly £00million in the last two years including £4million in 2024 alone, accounts reveal.

The troubled Aberdeenshire beer giant, which recently shut ten pubs and was dumped by 2,000 bars, also had to ask investors for £0million.

It comes despite revenues of £57million last year and selling the equivalent of over four cans a second in stores.

The £4,558,000 lost by the Ellon-based firm in 2024 was less than the record £3.5million it shed the previous year.

Investment firm TSG agreed to pump in £0million, documents state. CEO James Taylor said going by his metric the company made a £.5million profit.

He added: "On an operating basis we have returned to profitability."

One investor branded his shares "worthless".

headline: Today in crowdfunding: private hire vehicles for private hire vehicles
author: Bryce Elder
text:
Enlightening Bolt

Want to buy shares in Bolt? Well, tough. You probably can’. Bolt is privately held. The Estonian ride-hailing company last raised cash from a handful of institutions in 2022 at a €.4bn valuation and hasn’ needed to tap markets since.

Want to not buy shares in Bolt? Sure! Crowdcube has you covered:

From the above, a person may get the idea that it’ a share sale on behalf of Bolt. “nvest in Bolt” the countdown box says, above Bolt’ corporate history, some bullet-point financials for Bolt and links to Bolt’ 2024 annual report.

The twist can be found in a “ocuments”ection, visible only to registered and logged-in accounts. Here it’ explained that the share sale is for “øti UK Holding Ltd” a UK-registered special purpose vehicle that holds 30,000 common shares in Bolt Technology OÜ, Bolt’ parent company. What’ for sale are the illiquid shares of a privately held wrapper on some illiquid, unquoted stock.

Bøti’ owner and only director is Indrek Kasela. He’ a prolific angel investor, patron of Estonian arts and occasional film producer. Kasela backed the start-up that became Bolt in 2014, a year after it was founded by the then 19-year-old high school student Markus Villig. We’e contacted Kasela for comment.

Bøti has no connection with Bolt beyond its shareholding. A Bolt spokesman told FTAV that the company had not been aware of its shares being listed on Crowdcube prior to the sale going live. Bolt has not endorsed Bøti’ share offer and is not involved in the transaction, the spokesman said.

The Crowdcube crowdfunding platform is authorised and regulated by the Financial Conduct Authority. Prospective customers are referred to a Due Diligence harter that says it “pproves financial promotions on behalf of companies to ensure that they are clear, fair and not misleading”

The Due Diligence Charter also says:

Crowdcube will not offer an investment opportunity in anything other than a group top company, except in exceptional circumstances (such as where an special purpose vehicle is being used for the investment and this will be clearly disclosed).

Crowdcube told us that Bøti’ indirect sale of Bolt shares had met the Charter’ “xceptional circumstances”riteria, and that the structure of the SPV had been clearly disclosed in the FAQ and Summary of Key Information documents attached to the marketing page.

A company spokeswoman directed us to Crowdcube’ press release of September 2 that announced

... the Crowdcube Growth Series - an initiative designed to help later-stage private businesses unlock liquidity - whether by raising new capital, rewarding early investors through secondaries, or combining both - while also building a loyal shareholder community drawn from their customers.

The first transaction under the Crowdcube Growth Series label is an offer to buy shares in Bolt, the app that’ making cities for people, not cars. Launched in 2013, Europe’ first mobility super-app now operates in over 50 countries, serves 200+ million customers, works with 4.5 million drivers, and generated nearly € billion in revenue in 2024. From today, retail investors can purchase shares via Crowdcube.

The documents attached to the campaign page are where it’ revealed that Bøti has no contractual relationship with Bolt. Shareholders of Bøti are promised a one-for-one “eneficial interest”n Bolt shares, but their voting and pre-emption rights remain with Kasela.

This is Kasela’ second fundraise via Crowdcube: in 2022, he sold 4,053 Bøti shares to raise £72,490 from just over 1,500 retail investors. Those shares were marketed as being priced at a 15 per cent readthrough discount to Bolt’ funding round in the same year.

The new sale jacks the offer price up by nearly 15 per cent with the aim of placing up to 20,000 Bøti shares. The FAQ explains that Kasela intends to remain the SPV’ majority shareholder, so he will put more Bolt shares into Bøti and issue new Bøti shares to keep the ratio one-for-one. Exactly how this mechanism works will only be revealed after the fundraising closes.

The documentation also warns that Bolt has two tiers of shares, common and preference, which under certain circumstances might allow for a wipeout of the former; that Crowdcube has not reviewed Bolt’ shareholder agreement so there might be other claims on the company equity; and that Bolt has €20mn of convertible debt outstanding. Conversion would dilute Bøti’ Bolt stake and its shareholders’ut of any exit event.

Today, with 12 days still to run for Bøti’ share sale, 732 Crowdcube investors have pledged just over £40,000. We wish each and every one of them good luck.

—own in one: how private equity swallowed the BrewDog Unicorn (FTAV)

headline: Prezzo tastes new brand
text:
news in brief

The boss of Prezzo Italian is drawing up plans for a second brand in an attempt to transform the group from a restaurant chain into a "portfolio business". James Brown, appointed as chief executive of the 96-strong chain last year, is hoping to launch a second brand next year, Propel, a hospitality news website, reported. Brown, who joined the business from BrewDog, the Scottish beer and pub chain, has been turning Prezzo Italian around. It was hit hard by the pandemic and inflationary headwinds, but also from having a concept that did not stand out from the crowd, and closed 47 sites in a restructuring two years ago.

headline: IT MAKES ME CRY...
author: SALLY LAND
text:
IT'S perhaps a sign of the times, but I feel sadness at the scaling back of one of Scotland's more recent business success stories.

Aberdeenshire beer giants BrewDog last week announced job cuts after posting a £7million loss.

It's the fifth year running that they have posted pre-tax losses, totalling £48million, despite continuing to close locations and let staff go.

I truly hope the Ellonbased business can turn things around and get operations back in the black.

Scotland's business community is a better place with them growing and thriving.

headline: News; Opinion, Columns Queen of the jungle Toff 's next challenge is starting a family
author: Richard Eden
text:
AS CONTESTANTS on I'm A Celebrity have to eat such delicacies as bull's penis and stick their heads in cages full of creepy-crawlies, one former winner hopes to have put such trials behind her.

Georgia Toffolo tells me: 'Once you've won the jungle, where do you go from there? It's maybe time for me to hang up my TV boots. Maybe everyone's been bored of me on the telly.'

Georgia, 31, married BrewDog pub chain founder James Watt, 43, who has an estimated fortune of £00 million, in March and now has other priorities.

'The next thing on the agenda, though, would be a baby,' she tells me at the Boisdale Life Editor's Lunch and Awards.

headline: Brewdog  'suicide' beer ad outrage
author: DAVID MEIKLE
text:
TASTE ROW

CAMPAIGNERS' FURY

BREWDOG was slammed yesterday by mental health campaigners for using the word "suicide" in a jokey advert for one of their beers.

Charity chiefs called the drinks firm's stunt "entirely inappropriate" as it emerged watchdogs have received a complaint and may be set to launch a probe.

The outrage erupted after BrewDog unveiled the "bad taste" digital outdoor billboards for their flagship brand Punk IPA.

It boasted they'd introduced a new recipe for their India pale ale and, despite costs rising, they'd kept the price the same. The advert's slogan said: "Tastes like commercial suicide".

But that provoked a storm of criticism for the company, based in Ellon, Aberdeenshire.

Kenny Stewart, of Scottish Action for Mental Health, said: "Suicide is not a punchline, it's a tragedy. It's good to talk more openly about suicide but not like this."

A spokesman for Suicide Prevention Scotland added: "It's a hugely disappointing, entirely inappropriate advert.

"Mocking suicide is neither clever nor funny. It adds to the stigma and distress people impacted by suicide feel. And it can cost lives."

He added: "Talking about suicide is the best way to prevent it. We'd encourage business leaders to use their influence responsibly."

The Advertising Standards Authority said they're assessing a complaint about the advert.

BrewDog CEO James Taylor, 50, had said: "Our brewers are buzzing, our finance team are crying, Punk IPA just got more expensive to make.

"'Tastes like commercial suicide' is a direct quote from our head of procurement."

Taylor took over in March, a year after co-founder James Watt, 43, stepped down. We told in September how the firm lost nearly £00million since 2023. We approached BrewDog for comment.

headline: We've been misled by Reeves, say pub chiefs
author: Jessica Newman
text:
Pub bosses have told the chancellor she has "deeply misled" the industry over business rates reform as they claimed the sector faces an additional £60 million annual tax burden, despite promises of relief.

In a letter to Rachel Reeves, over 80 chief executives said she had "blindsided" the industry and they predicted pub closures would further accelerate from the current rate of one a day.

The British Beer and Pub Association, the trade body that co-ordinated the letter, said that "sleepless nights, pay cuts and staff layoffs are the reality for publicans", adding that they had calculated bills showing increases of up to £6,952.

In her budget speech, Reeves boasted she was introducing a "new golden era for hospitality" through a new tiered system for business rates, with the tax charge depending on the size and value of a company's premises.

At the same time, a separate government agency slipped out a new, much higher assessment of the value of the buildings used to calculate business rates, meaning the levy will actually increase significantly for the average high street business.

The BBPA said community pubs were facing an average 63 per cent increase in business rates bills, compared with just 7 per cent for distribution warehouses, and argued that the government had failed to "rebalance the burden" as promised.

In the letter, industry leaders said that pubs had been "overpaying their share of the rates burden" and that the 5p in the pound discount on business rates for small retail, hospitality and leisure businesses fell far short of a 20p reduction the industry had sought.

The signatories of the letter include Admiral Taverns, Greene King, Brewdog, Fuller's, JD Wetherspoon, Punch Pubs and Stonegate, the country's biggest pub operator.

The letter comes before a planned emergency conference of pub bosses hosted by the BBPA this week. It has invited the chancellor or a representative of the Treasury to attend.

headline: Brewdog  'suicide' ads are removed
author: DAVID MEIKLE
text:
BREWDOG has axed a controversial ad for its beer which included the word "suicide".

The Aberdeenshirebased company had used the term as part of a campaign to boost its revamped Punk IPA.

The new recipe was advertised in digital outdoor ads, saying the firm had invested more in the blend's ingredients, but kept the price the same.

But the main tagline said "Sounds like commercial suicide."

It was heavily criticised by suicide charities and the public and has now been shelved.

The Advertising Standards Authority received a complaint, but stopped its investigation after the campaign was pulled.

Kenny Stewart, of Scottish Action for Mental Health, said: "Suicide is not a punchline. It's a tragedy." BrewDog was asked for comment.

headline: Toff airs a jetset gripe
author: RICHARD EDEN
text:
GEORGIA Toffolo has taken aim at airport etiquette, blasting fliers who fail to return their security trays.

She says: 'You know when you're at the airport going through security and the person in front of you hasn't picked up their tray and put it down with the other used trays - I'm like, "I can't believe people don't do that".' Despite her fervour, Georgia, 31, admits she is not without sin. Her husband, BrewDog founder James Watt, 43, has called her out for the same offence, ribbing the former I'm A Celebrity jungle queen: 'You always forget to do that.'

headline: WATT'S UP WITH TV SHOW SNUB..
author: STUART PATTERSON
text:
BREWDOG TYCOON'S BIZ ROW

Applicants 'misled'

BEER tycoon James Watt was last night accused of snubbing fledgling traders amid "silence" on his £million TV business show.

Budding entrepreneurs claim they felt "misled" by the BrewDog cofounder, 43, a year after he posed on a horse to launch House of Unicorns.

Around 200 business owners pitched their ideas to him at a London boozer hoping to make the shortlist for the series that was meant to be announced last March.

But the Scot has not posted about the show since last February and its website and LinkedIn page are dead.

Applicants said they felt they had given their time and energy with no reward.

One said: "What a cruel thing to do, getting people's hopes up like that."

Zachary Stephens, 26, who hoped to set up an Ikea-type home build firm with the show's help, felt "disappointed and a bit misled".

He added: "I spent time preparing a genuine application and business proposal. It's frustrating to have been met with complete silence."

Mr Watt recalled last January how he was knocked back by Dragons' Den in 2008. He had hoped for a £00,000 investment in the craft beer company he set up with pal Martin Dickie in Ellon, Aberdeenshire. He said he wanted to shake up the "tired format" and offer UK telly's biggest ever cash prize.

It would see him put up £million with another windfall coming from investor community Founders Capital.

Businessman Dene Botha claimed: "It was a good concept that seems to have gone nowhere despite the build-up."

It is understood the show is now to become a video podcast with the £million prize still up for grabs.

It is unclear if hopefuls will have to reapply. Mr Watt and producers Whisper TV were asked for comment.

stuart.patterson@the-sun.co.uk

headline: BrewDog  axes spirits
text:
news in brief

BrewDog, the drinks company best known for Punk IPA, will no longer produce any of its spirits, including Duo rum, Abstrakt vodka and Lonewolf gin, at its distillery in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, putting jobs at risk.

The company, which was founded in 2007, said that it made the decision to focus on its beer brands, adding that it would keep its Wonderland cocktail range in place.

Production of its gin, rum, tequila, gin and whisky brands will be wound down during the next few months. The company declined to say how many jobs were at risk but confirmed that all existing supply commitments would be honoured.

BrewDog closed ten bars last year and offloaded a Highland forest it owned while battling with the tough trading conditions in hospitality.

The distillery business was opened in 2015 and since 2023 has been housed in its own facility near BrewDog's main brewery in Ellon.

The most recent annual accounts, which cover the 2024 calendar year, show a £6.7 million pre-tax loss on net revenue of £80.2 million.

The founders, James Watt, 43, and Martin Dickie, also 43, are no longer involved in the day-to-day running of the company. James Taylor, who became chief executive in March last year, has spoken of the need for "sensible financial discipline" to move the business back into profit.

headline: Watt plot for shock return is Brewing
author: STUART PATTERSON
text:
BID 'TO BUY FIRM'

FORMER BrewDog boss James Watt was last night said to be mulling over a bid to buy back the firm.

Industry sources told how he was looking to regain control after the drinks giant revealed it is to close down its distillery.

Last night there was no official comment from the controversial tycoon, 43, or the Scots company.

But Watt posted a snap of him and reality TV star wife Georgia Toffolo, 31, grinning as they visited Marks and Spencer's London HQ.

He stepped down as BrewDog's CEO in May 2024 but remained on its board as "captain and co-founder".

Amid increasing speculation that Watt aims to make a sensational return, one shareholder said: "There is a feeling that BrewDog is in this mess mainly because of him. How would he even pay for it?" Another said: "In the world of BrewDog, you can never say never.

"I just want them to make good beer again and make me excited about visiting their bars like they used to." BrewDog yesterdy a said it will shut down its spirits distillery in Ellon, Aberdeenshire. But chiefs didn't reveal how many jobs will be lost. The site produces vodka, gin, rum and tequila.

Boss James Taylor said: "We need to simplify the business and focus energy where we can truly win."

It's thought Watt and co-founder Martin Dickie, 44, got £00million each in 2017 by selling a stake in BrewDog to private equity firm TSG. The company operates 90 bars and employs more than 2,400 staff.

But it lost £7million in 2024. A spokesman said they don't comment on "industry speculation".

headline: Tariff pain puts Scots' distilleries on the rocks
author: Greig Cameron
text:
Growing numbers of Scottish spirits makers are showing signs of financial distress amid weakening global and domestic demand (Greig Cameron writes).

Research by BTG Begbies Traynor found that 69 Scottish distillers were facing significant or critical issues at the close of the year, up from 49 in the previous three months.

The Scotch Whisky Association, the trade body, estimated there were more than 150 whisky distilleries in Scotland. There were believed to be in excess of 90 which produced gin as well as a handful producing vodka, rum and liqueurs.

Thomas McKay, managing partner of BTG in Scotland, said Scottish distillers were facing a "perfect storm of lowering demand, rising production costs and increased tariffs in key markets".

BrewDog announced plans last month to shut down its distillery and spirit-making operations.

Whisky exports to the United States and China have been affected by tariffs and duties.

Domestically, a number of British pub groups have highlighted trends of customers switching away from spirits for cheaper alternatives such as beer or soft drinks.

Distillers have also been affected by the sharp increase in energy and employment costs over recent years. That is alongside more widespread societal changes of declining alcohol consumption.

McKay said: "Demand for Scotch whisky, gin and other spirits peaked in 2020 during the lockdowns in the UK and globally.

"When this demand fell away the remaining oversupply has seen prices fall, at exactly the same time as additional costs of exporting to the biggest market for Scotch whisky, the US, have risen dramatically."

headline: Craft beermaker  BrewDog  puts itself up for sale
author: Robert Orr in London
text:
UK’ biggest independent beer company appoints restructuring specialist AlixPartners

The UK’ biggest independent beer company BrewDog has put itself up for sale, appointing AlixPartners to run a restructuring process that could involve breaking it up.

The company, at the forefront of the UK’ craft brewing boom before it ran into reputational difficulties, cited a “hallenging economic climate”nd “ustained macro headwinds” In a statement it said it had called in the restructuring specialist to plot its “ext phase of investment”

A person with knowledge of the situation confirmed that a sales process was under way involving BrewDog’ three business areas: its brands, its brewing capacity and its pub estate.

The brands and estate of 72 bars could attract bids from leading leisure operators, which in the UK include Mitchells & Butlers and Stonegate Group.

Its brewing facilities in Scotland, the US, Australia and Germany may be of interest to leading beermakers such as Carlsberg and Heineken, while private equity buyers could purchase the whole business.

The person said the process was at an early stage and would not be drawn on valuations for the businesses. BrewDog made a pre-tax loss of £6.7mn in 2024, down from a loss of £9.2mn a year earlier, as sales growth ground to a halt.

The business, co-founded outside Aberdeen by James Watt and Martin Dickie in 2007, tapped into the growing market for craft beers and expanded rapidly, tempting drinkers with creatively named beverages such as Elvis Juice and Punk IPA.

It raised more than £0mn from a crowdfunder that attracted 130,000 small investors whom the company called “quity punks”

But it later had to postpone a planned initial public offering as it faced claims from staff of a “oxic”ork environment. att was forced to issue a public apology after an open letter from a group of former employees hit out at what they called a “ulture of fear”nd misogyny.

Watt stepped down as chief executive in 2024 and Dickie left the company last year. Both are still shareholders. Its current chief executive is James Taylor. BrewDog is 21 per cent owned by TSG Consumer Partners, which injected £13mn in 2017.

“e have appointed AlixPartners to support a structured and competitive process to evaluate the next phase of investment for the business,”BrewDog said on Saturday. “his is a deliberate and disciplined step with a focus on strengthening the long-term future of the BrewDog brand and its operations.”

The appointment of AlixPartners was first reported by Sky.

headline: 'Sell-off' plan may break up  BrewDog
author: FEDERICA ADRIANI
text:
DRINKS giant BrewDog could be broken up and sold off after consultants were called in to find new investors.

The independent brewery and pub chain behind craft beers such as Punk IPA and Lost lager has appointed consultants to oversee a sale process.

BrewDog said last month it was closing its distilling brands, sparking job loss fears at its base in Ellon, Aberdeenshire.

The firm, founded in 2007, said it had decided to focus on beer products.

A spokesman said the move was a "deliberate step" aimed at strengthening the brand's future.

He said the economy was "challenging" and stressed the firm's breweries, bars and venues were operating normally.

He added: "BrewDog remains a global craft beer pioneer." The firm runs more than 90 bars and four breweries.

headline: for sale
author: HARRY WILLIAMSON
text:
Under-fire beer firm launches bid for new investors

BEER giant BrewDog has been put up for sale with financial gurus drafted in to seek investors.

Chiefs have hired consultants AlixPartners in a move that could see parts of the drinks giant broken up and flogged separately.

The measures come after the Scots brewer shut ten bars last July, including its first boozer in Aberdeen, in the wake of a £7million loss in 2024.

And the cost-cutting di rve continued last month when bosses confirmed the closure of the spirits distillery at their base in Ellon, Aberdeenshire.

But management insisted their existing 72 bars worldwide and 1,400-strong workforce will be unaffected by the latest move. In an email to investors, they said: "This is a normal and prudent step and no decisions have been made. This does not change our day-to-day operations."

We told how ex-boss James Watt, 43, was rumoured to be weighing up a bid to buy back the firm he co-founded.

The tycoon, married to I'm A Celebrity star Georgia Toffolo, 31, stepped down in 2024 but remained on the board as "captain". He and business partner Martin Dickie got £00million each in 2017 by selling a stake to private equity firm TSG.

A BrewDog spokesman said: "As with many businesses operating in a challenging economic climate, we regularly review our options with a focus on longterm strength and sustainability.

"Following a year of decisive action in 2025, we have appointed AlixPartners to evaluate the next phase of investment. BrewDog remains a global pioneer in craft beer."

headline: Brewdog  co-founder and 'world's worst boss' mulls buying back failing firm
author: Matt Strudwick
text:
BREWDOG co-founder and 'worst boss in the world' James Watt is considering buying the firm back after it was put up for sale.

Watt, who stepped down as CEO in 2024, is understood to be canvassing support from financial backers to launch a bid.

The Scottish brewer announced it had appointed restructuring experts AlixPartners to look for potential investors.

With a 21 per cent stake, Watt remains one of the largest shareholders. But the brewer, founded by Watt and Martin Dickie in 2007, has posted a cumulative £48million in pre-tax losses over five years.

In 2021 the firm was accused of fostering a 'culture of fear'. And the following year Watt, who is married to Made In Chelsea's Georgia Toffolo, was accused of inappropriate behaviour and abuse of power.

Former BrewDog USA workers told the BBC how his behaviour made them feel 'uncomfortable' and 'powerless'. Lawyers for Watt denied these claims but a complaint to Ofcom over the documentary was thrown out in February 2024.

In a social media post in which he jokingly referred to himself as the 'world's worst boss', Watt hit back at claims he is the 'Darth Vader of CEOs' by listing the positive moves he made as a boss - including gifting team members £50,000 and rejecting a salary in the pandemic.

BrewDog, which is behind craft beers such as Elvis Juice and Punk IPA, operates 72 bars and four breweries globally. Ten UK bars

headline: BrewDog  funders could be nursing sale hangovers
text:
Asale of BrewDog is likely to leave investors in the Scottish brewer and bar group emptyhanded, experts have warned (Jessica Newman writes).

The company, known for its Punk IPA and Dead Pony Club beers, confirmed over the weekend that it had appointed restructuring experts at AlixPartners to plot "the next phase of investment for the business".

BrewDog, which since 2017 has been part owned by TSG Consumer Partners, the US private equity firm, has raised tens of millions of pounds through crowdfunding after attracting swathes of fans taken with its high-strength beers, controversial marketing stunts and bold proclamations.

However, news of a potential sale fuelled speculation among its crowdfunders, known as "equity punks", that they may be left empty-handed if a deal prioritises TSG, which has a 21 per cent stake.

Mark Northway, director of ShareSoc, which represents individual shareholders, said: "Sadly, BrewDog is a cautionary tale. Earlystage investors take huge amounts of risk. If things go well, they often get diluted or subordinated by large funding rounds; if things go badly they get wiped out."

He argued that either a sale or debt restructuring, while "inevitable", will "likely wipe out shareholders given their structural subordination".

A number of investors have taken to the Equity for Punks bulletin board to air their frustration, with one saying it "would have been nice to have been informed of it by management before reading it in the news. But then again, that's just the BrewDog way nowadays."

In exchange for their investment, equity punks receive benefits such as discounted drinks, free beers on their birthday, early access to new launches and an invite to the boozy annual meeting. The company has around 220,000 investors with an average holding of £00.

With the sale process, expected to result in either a full sale or break-up of the business, still in the early stages, the outcome for equity punks' stakes has not been confirmed. It comes months after BrewDog rowed back on plans for an initial public offering.

A sale could trigger a windfall for founders Martin Dickie and James Watt, who established the company in 2007. Watt, who stepped down as chief executive in 2024, three years after allegations that he oversaw a "culture of fear", is also reportedly mulling a bid to reclaim full ownership of the company.

It still has its headquarters and main brewery in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, but also has brewing sites in Australia, Germany and the United States. It runs more than 120 bars and hotels around the world employing about 2,400 people.

BrewDog rang up revenues of £57 million for the 12 months to the end of December 2024, but that failed to lift the company out of the red as pre-tax losses narrowed to £6.7 million. BrewDog declined to comment.

headline: Danes have a thirst for  Brewdog 's beer brands
author: Greig Cameron
text:
Royal Unibrew Group, a Danish drinks business, is reported to be interested in buying Brewdog (Greig Cameron writes).

The company, which is listed on Nasdaq Copenhagen, is said to be keen on picking up the Scottish group's beer brands rather than taking on its bar estate.

Alix Partners was brought in by Brewdog earlier this month to look at restructuring or refinancing plans.

James Watt, the Brewdog co-founder, who stepped down as chief executive in 2024 and still retains a significant minority stake, is also among those looking to strike a deal. Sky News first reported the story.

Royal Unibrew has a portfolio spanning beer, spirits, wine and soft drinks with most of its sales around Scandinavia, the Balkan states, Italy, Netherlands, France and Belgium. It runs 21 production sites across ten countries although does not have a presence in the UK.

Brewdog closed ten of its bars last year and this year shut its distillery as part of cost saving efforts.

Any sale of the business is likely to see 220,000 small shareholders who bought into the business via crowdfunding, which raised £5 million over several years, miss out on a return.

The value of the preference shares owned by TSG Consumer Partners are likely to exceed the amount of a sale. TSG's terms from the 2017 deal, for a 22 per cent stake valuing Brewdog at almost £00 million, included a compounding 18 per cent return on their stake which will crystallise upon a sale or flotation.

Brewdog has struggled since the pandemic, racking up years of pre-tax losses. It runs more than 70 bars and its beers are among the biggest selling craft products in the UK. Brewdog declined to comment and Royal Unibrew was approached.

headline: BREWDOG  AILING
author: JAMES FLANDERS
text:
Chain closes down 38 out of 67 pubs in takeover

HUNDREDS of workers have lost their jobs with dozens of pubs shut as the craft beer giant BrewDog went bust.

The company fell into administration yesterday but was snapped up by US lifestyle firm Tilray Brands.

While the £3million deal saves the brewery and preserves the brand name, it is a disaster for many staff as 38 bars across the UK have closed with immediate effect more than half of BrewDog's 67-strong estate.

A total of 484 jobs have been axed and the company's 200,000 shareholders are warned they will get nothing back.

Administrators AlixPartners said no bidder wanted to take on the entire business, meaning mass closures were unavoidable.

New owner Tilray specialises in legal and medicinal cannabis in America and Canada and is a major craft brewer in the US.

It secured brewing facilities in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, and the Hop Hub distribution centre in Motherwell. Tilray has also saved 11 bars, so 733 employees keep their jobs.

BrewDog's 18 franchise bars will continue as normal.

The collapse is a major downfall for a brand that once prided itself on being the rebel of the drinks industry. BrewDog, whose bestselling beer is Punk IPA, was founded by James Watt and Martin Dickie in 2007, In 2009, the brewer launched the Equity for Punks fundraising scheme. Last year BrewDog shut ten bars across the UK including its flagship first pub in Aberdeen.

Clare Kennedy, of AlixPartners, said: "In Tilray, we have secured a purchaser with a passion for craft brewing."

james.flanders@thesun.co.uk

headline: BrewDog  axes 500 workers in rescue deal
author: Emily Hawkins
text:
BREWDOG is shutting 38 bars with the loss of nearly 500 jobs - despite being rescued by an American brewer and cannabis firm.

The Scottish company has been snapped up by Tilray Brands in a £3m deal that includes its UK brewing operations and 11 pubs in Britain and Ireland.

But 38 bars will close with the loss of 484 jobs in the latest blow to British hospitality.

The deal came after BrewDog crashed into administration and leaves the brand's investors empty-handed.

More than 200,000 people who ploughed money into the firm's 'equity for punks' scheme will receive no return.

But Tilray - which produces medical cannabis products including craft beer and hemp foods - will preserve 733 jobs in the UK. It will take control of BrewDog facilities including its brewery in Aberdeenshire, which produces beers including Punk IPA and Elvis Juice.

Tilray has also bought The Hop Hub - a distribution centre in Motherwell.

The brewer closed its pubs yesterday as administrators at AlixPartners sought to confirm a takeover.

Irwin D. Simon, chairman and chief executive of Tilray Brands, said the firm's priority was to 'refocus' the business on 'the craft beer excellence that made it beloved in the first place and strategically invest to return the operations to profitable growth'.

BrewDog was founded in 2007 by James Watt and Martin Dickie, but it has faced mounting losses in recent years and has closed a string of bars and axed chunks of its workforce.

Unite general secretary Sharon Graham said: 'This is a devastating day for BrewDog workers, [who] built this brand.

'They deserved respect. Instead, they were treated as disposable pawns.'

headline: Brewdog  bars go in £3m US rescue
author: Greig Cameron
text:
Hundreds of jobs will be lost at Brewdog after the Scottish brewing and hospitality company was bought by a US cannabis and wellness operator for £3 million.

Tilray Brands is buying the firm's intellectual property, its brewery in Aberdeenshire, a distribution hub in central Scotland and 11 pubs across the UK and Ireland. The sale preserves 733 jobs but 38 bars will shut, meaning 484 redundancies with immediate effect.

Alix Partners was appointed last month to look at options for the business after five years of losses. Yesterday, Brewdog closed its bars and cancelled deliveries and customer bookings to comply with licensing requirements related to a change of ownership.

Irwin Simon, Tilray's chief executive, said: "What makes Brewdog truly special has always been its brewers, its brewpubs and its passionate community of beer fans."

He added: "As we begin a new chapter for this great brand, our priority is to refocus Brewdog on the craft beer excellence that made it beloved in the first place, and strategically invest to return the operations to profitable growth."

The 220,000 small investors who put in £5 million through crowdfunding and are known as equity punks look likely to be completely wiped out. However, it is believed Tilray intends to keep some benefits for them, such as discounts in the remaining bars.

Tilray, which is negotiating separately to buy Brewdog assets in the United States and Australia, is listed on Nasdaq and has its headquarters in New York. Along with its cannabis and wellness products it has a craft beer and spirits arm, mainly in North America.

James Watt and Martin Dickie started the business in 2007 and built it into one of the fastest-growing craft beer firms in Europe. Both retained significant minority stakes even after selling 22 per cent to the private equity firm TSG in 2017 for £13 million.

text:

Katie Prescott, page 35
