headline: 500  BREWDOG  JOBS AXE 'WIN'
author: STUART PATTERSON
text:
BREWDOG'S new boss told staff they had scored a "win" after nearly 500 co-workers lost their jobs.

Irwin D Simon made the comment in a conference call after snapping up the bust Scots beer giant for £3million.

Mr Simon, who runs US medicinal cannabis firm Tilray Brands, also vowed to bring "some love" and "innovation" to the business.

Following the takeover, he said: "I want to speak directly to BrewDog employees about why this is a win for you as well.

"Administration creates uncertainty. It impacts teams and confidence. I promise you, today brings clarity."

It came after BrewDog axed 484 staff and 38 pubs across the UK. New Yorkbased Mr Simon visited the firm's HQ in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, where he praised workers.

He said: "There's a lot of excitement, a lot of good energy here.

"I feel good about what this brings to Tilray."

But Unite union's Bryan Simpson said: "Everyone has been treated brutally in recent days, including those who remain in work.

"The new owners must provide a clear picture of what the future holds."

Sun Money Page 41

headline: Only tears for the beer
author: BLATHNAID CORLESS
text:
BREWDOG INVESTORS LOSE CASH BUT GET 1 FREE DRINK

WIPED-OUT BREWDOG investors are up in arms after being told they can still get a free birthday beer as a "thank you" following its £3million sale.

The craft beer giant was flogged in a rescue deal on Monday with the immediate closure of 38 bars and 484 jobs lost.

New owner, US firm TILRAY BRANDS, told them by email and said those who backed Brewdog's "Equity for Punks" crowd-funding would be seen as brand "ambassadors".

It stated: "We intend to continue your key benefits, including bar and online discounts, tattoo discounts, and a free beer on your birthday."

Furious Sun reader Kevin Collett, 46, who invested £,500 in Brewdog in 2019, said: "They're putting a positive spin on it when there's nothing positive about it.

"It's a failed business. There's no other way to describe it."

Consumer expert Martyn James said: "It's a slap in the face for investors who could lose everything. Many had little stock market experience but believed in the product.

"Being palmed off with a few freebies has gone down like flat beer." Brewdog's administrators AlixPartners, and Tilray Brands, did not wish to comment.

headline: BREWDOG  WATT WHINE BLASTED
author: STUART PATTERSON
text:
BREWDOG co-founder James Watt was blasted by investors after whining about a few tough days and insisting he had nothing to do with the firm's collapse.

The tycoon was silent as the beer giant he built fell into administration and laid off almost 500 staff on Monday.

But yesterday he posted about his "heartbreak" on social media.

Mr Watt, 43, revealed he banked £0million after tax by cashing in some of his shares in 2017, when his Aberdeenshire-based venture was valued at £billion.

And he apologised to workers who lost their jobs and the 200,000 investors, dubbed "equity punks", who lost a combined £5million.

Mr Watt, from Fraserburgh, quit as CEO in 2024. Calling the past days "incredibly hard", he said: "I am heartbroken for all of the hard-working and passionate team members who lost their jobs.

"I am heartbroken for all our brilliant equity punks who did not get the return on the investment they wanted."

Angry investors hit back online. One said: "He is a toxic, cash-grabbing individual." BrewDog was bought by US cannabis firm Tilray Brands in a deal that only included three of its 12 Scots bars.

headline: I'm heartbroken for  BrewDog  equity punks, says co-founder
author: Greig Cameron
text:
The co-founder of BrewDog said he was "heartbroken" for those who have lost investments after the company was taken over by a US company this week.

James Watt also said he was sad for those who had lost their jobs, adding there were "so many other things I would have done differently".

Tilray Brands acquired BrewDog in a £3 million deal which led to 38 bars being closed and the loss of 484 jobs, with only 11 pubs in the UK and Ireland left open. The sale meant those who had invested a total of £5 million in BrewDog through its "equity for punks" crowdfunding rounds were left empty-handed.

In a lengthy post on LinkedIn, Watt wrote: "To our equity punks: thank you for having the conviction to believe in the business when this was just two humans, one dog and a crazy idea."

Watt, 43, who co-founded the craft beer business in Aberdeenshire in 2007, also acknowledged there were periods where he should have had tighter control on spending around the company.

The company co-founder, who stood down as chief executive in 2024, said: "I feel that I did not respond to certain crises that we faced (and we faced many) in a way that was authentic and true to who I am. Those decisions sit with me."

Watt had been among the bidders for the company but was unsuccessful. It is understood the package Watt put together would have retained the stake owned by the small investors and reinstated the real living wage across the business.

Watt said: "During my 17 years in charge there were highs, lows, successes, failures, huge gambles and many mistakes along the way. Ultimately, the mistakes hurt far more than the successes console. I would have loved to save every single job and every single equity punk investment. Ultimately, I couldn't. That will stay with me."

headline: I LOST £00 TRANSFER AFTER TYPO BLUNDER
text:
Ask Got a problem? ADELE She'll sort it adele.cooke @the-sun.co.uk

QIN January I transferred £00 from my Monzo account to my Halifax account.

Just moments after I sent the cash, I realised I had accidentally got two of the digits in the sort code wrong.

The money was taken from my account but it never arrived in my Halifax one.

Panicking, I contacted Monzo several times and it initially told me I would get my money back with a £5 gesture of goodwill.

But today I was told there is nothing Monzo can do and I need to take legal action against the person who kept my cash.

I'm 19 years old and was saving the money for a trip to Australia which is less than two weeks away. I can't afford to lose any money. Can you help?

ALICE ATKINSON via email

A We've all become so used to making digital payments and transferring cash in seconds. But as you have learnt, it can have its disadvantages.

Typing in incorrect bank account details and sending money to the wrong account can be a very expensive mistake.

This is because the misdirected payment code of best practice states that if your bank can't track down your money, it doesn't have to refund you.

It should take action to find your cash within two days of you reporting the blunder.

In most instances, the bank should be able to return your cash, but if it's gone, then your only option is to take legal action against the person you accidentally sent the payment to.

Legally, if money is paid into your bank account by mistake then you are not entitled to keep it.

You're not the only one who has made this error - BrewDog co-founder and beer tycoon James Watt nearly lost £1.2million when he gave the wrong bank details for a transaction.

Although you were in the wrong, Monzo said it made a mistake when it said you would get your money back.

With that in mind, it has agreed to refund you the £00 and has offered £5 as a gesture of goodwill.

I'm glad I was able to resolve this issue for you and hope you have an amazing time in Australia.

headline: IT'S INNIS & GONE
author: SIAN MAHER
text:
Tennent's buy crisis-hit beer brand amid job fears

TENNENT'S Lager yesterday snapped up Innis & Gunn in a £.5million rescue deal with job losses on the way.

Owners C&C Group agreed a deal with administrators called in by the struggling Edinburgh-based firm.

C&C has been brewing Innis & Struggling ... booze company Gunn's craft beers under contract at their Tennent's Wellpark site in Glasgow for 16 years.

The company lost £82,000 in the year to March 31, 2025 after a £.3million loss the year before.

Bosses at restructuring specialists FT Consulting blamed a "decline in consumer spending and rising cost pressures".

They admitted yesterday: "It is with deep regret that redundancies will need to be made." They added: "Innis & Gunn, like many across the sector, has faced a number of challenges recently.

"A small number of employees have been retained to support the orderly closure of the brewery business and taprooms."

Dougal Sharp, who launched the company in 2003, said he was "deeply sorry to everyone affected". He added: "I'm immensely proud of everything our team achieved together creating a distinctive Scottish beer brand enjoyed by customers at home and around the world.

"While this is not what any of us hoped for, I'm glad the brand has found a home with C&C Group. They have the scale, distribution and experience to take Innis & Gunn forward."

The deal does not include three taprooms in Edinburgh and Glasgow or a brewery in Perth.

It comes after hundreds of staff were axed on Monday when BrewDog went bust. The brand was sold to a US cannabis firm.

headline: New  Brewdog  boss aims to revive pubs
author: Greig Cameron
text:
The chief executive of Brewdog's new owner wants to reopen more UK pubs and revive its recently closed distillery.

Irwin Simon, chief executive of the US-based Tilray, also wants to hear from potential franchisees who are interested in running any of the 38 bars closed this week.

Simon said he had watched the Scottish brewer for "a long time" and previously held talks about buying it but felt the valuation was too high. He suggested that other suitors would have broken up the business, resulting in more jobs being lost than the 484 that were cut.

He said Tilray, which is listed on the Nasdaq in New York and also has interests in cannabis and wellness products, had considered other potential UK deals after opening a London office last year.

Brewdog announced in January that its distillery arm, which produced rum, gin, vodka and whisky, was shutting, but Simon wants to revive it.

The 11 bars bought from administrators are not the limit of Tilray's ambitions, with initial talks about adding a further four. Irwin is also considering offering franchises for the other sites and said: "I think we have got to learn from the past mistakes that were made, but brew pubs have a major purpose for marketing."

headline: Tennent's owner buys Innis & Gunn as 100 staff made redundant
author: Greig Cameron
text:
A Scottish craft brewer has been bought out of administration by the owner of Tennent's, although it was confirmed that 100 staff are being made redundant.

C&C Group is paying £.5 million for the brand and intellectual property of the Edinburgh-based Innis & Gunn, but is not taking on its three pubs or Perthshire brewery.

Administrators from FTI Consulting confirmed the pre-pack sale and only a small number of employees were being retained. They will oversee the closure of the brewery, as well as the two hospitality outlets in Glasgow and its Edinburgh site.

Roger White, the C&C chief executive, said there would be minimal disruption to supply chains and he intends to develop the Innis & Gunn brand.

Innis & Gunn was founded in 2003 by Dougal Sharp, a former head brewer at the Caledonian Brewery in Edinburgh.

The original beer was flavoured using single malt Scotch whisky casks, but it went on to widen its portfolio to include lagers and IPAs, as well as brands it bought when it acquired the Inveralmond Brewery in 2016.

Sharp said: "Today is a very difficult day, first and foremost for the brilliant people who have worked so hard to build Innis & Gunn over the past 23 years.

"I'm immensely proud of everything our team achieved together, creating a distinctive Scottish beer brand enjoyed by customers at home and around the world.

"I'm deeply sorry to everyone affected, particularly my colleagues who have lost their jobs and the shareholders who believed in what we were building. It's been a bruising process for everyone. While this outcome is not what any of us hoped for, I'm glad the brand has found a home with C&C Group.

"We've worked closely with the team for many years and they have the scale, distribution and experience to take Innis & Gunn forward."

C&C originally took an 8 per cent stake in Innis & Gunn in 2021, alongside a distribution partnership. Many of Innis & Gunn's drinks are made at C&C's Wellpark Brewery in Glasgow where Tennent's is also produced.

White said: "We have worked with Innis & Gunn for many years and while it's under difficult circumstances, we are delighted to bring the brand fully into our portfolio.

"Our existing brewing and route-tomarket platform allows us to integrate the brand effectively and quickly, supporting the ongoing supply of products to customers and consumers."

The Innis & Gunn accounts for the 12 months to March 2025 showed a £82,000 pre-tax loss on revenue of £3.6 million.

FTI Consulting said there had been a decline in consumer spending, as well as rising costs, which had resulted in margin and liquidity pressure, meaning "the business could no longer continue to trade". The administrators said: "It is with deep regret that redundancies will need to be made."

In 2016 Innis & Gunn sold a 4.79 per cent stake through crowdfunding, raising £.5 million.

The next year it received a £5 million investment from the private equity group L Catterton, which took a 27.9 per cent stake. Another crowdfunding drive followed in 2019, but it seems unlikely that the company's thousands of small shareholders will get much return on their investment.

The sale follows BrewDog's administration earlier this week and the purchase of its assets for £3 million by the US firm Tilray Brands. Although that agreement saved more than 730 jobs, there were 484 redundancies across BrewDog's bar estate.

headline: WHILE I'm a Celebrity winner
author: EDENRICHARD
text: ÷HILE I'm a Celebrity winner Georgia Toffolo parades around Cheltenham, her husband, James Watt, has been bowled a googly at Lord's. Marylebone Cricket Club signed a deal only last year under which BrewDog, the trendy-buttroubled beer maker which Watt co-founded, exclusively supplied its beers to the club's notoriously bibulous spectators. Now the club has written to members saying the deal is off. Owzat!

headline: We'd toast tax cuts to stop more pubs calling last orders for good
author: MATT BENDORIS
text:
AFTER BAILOUTS, BREWER TELLS REEVES..

ANDY Slee has seen membership of the Society of Independent Brewers and Associates drained as 20 Scots brewers went to the wall in the last year.

The trade organisation had 131 members in 2024, falling to 111 at the end of 2025, taking many popular pints with them.

But he believes more will close as they face rising costs.

He said: "Taxes are our biggest cost.

"So 40 per cent of our turnover goes in tax.

"Then in the 2024 Budget costs went up with the big increase in National Insurance, which hits labour-intensive industries like ours hard."

Business rates are crippling pubs, with nearly 300 calling last orders in Scotland over the last five years, but they also affect Society of Independent Brewers and Associates (SIBA) members.

Andy, 60, said: "Our success is intrinsically linked to the success of the pub sector because independent brewers sell 80 per cent of their volume into pubs and bars.

"So when pubs and bars are struggling, that has a knock-on effect back to us as we are in 2,500 pubs.

"Another problem is many pubs have tieups with big global breweries like Heineken and Carlsberg which makes it more difficult for our members to get in.

"The likes of Wetherspoons have been a big supporter of independent brewers, and we always sell well in there."

He adds: "But when you complain about the cost of a pint in a pub I can assure you the brewer is not away to buy their second yacht as many are not making any money whatsoever."

The industry has been calling on the Chancellor Rachel Reeves for a "more level playing field" concerning taxation.

Andy explains: "Taxation levels are suffocating the industry. But there are many things the Chancellor could do, like vastly reduce business rates for pubs.

"There's also a mechanism the government could use to reduce the duty on draft beer sold in a pub, whilst leaving the duty on cans and bottles sold in a supermarket alone.

"And they could reduce VAT, as we pay the full 20 per cent rate, while France pays just 12 per cent a huge difference."

The Scottish Government is also looking at restricting alcohol advertising, which Andy believes will have an adverse effect on the industry.

He said: "That will make promoting beer and breweries more difficult. But generally I believe it's a scandal that we allow national institutions like pubs and breweries to struggle paying top-end tax, while big online American tech companies come here and generate a lot of revenue and don't pay their fair share.

"The Chancellor could reduce our tax levels and raise theirs to make it a more level playing field."

Andy reckons successive governments fail to take account of the "social cohesion" of CONCERN ... Andy Slee.

bars or brewery tap rooms. He said: "Sitting in a pub or a brewery's own bar brings people together as they're a place people can go in, and chat to mates and chat to family and socialise.

"A report recently called Friends on Tap highlighted the role that the pub plays in counteracting loneliness.

"If you're widowed or on your own, very often the only human interaction you get is in the pub.

"The challenge is when we're talking to the Treasury collectively as an industry; helping loneliness is not something you can measure.

"But if people aren't drinking in the pub then they're drinking at home and perhaps gambling or worse."

Despite all the issues, Andy insists there is a real thirst for good craft ales.

He said: "There is strong consumer demand and support for independent brewers that make handcrafted beers and create local jobs.

"But there's a role for big global brands and independent beers to sit alongside each other as a good bar will offer both. We just need the Chancellor to give both bars and breweries a chance."

Inset,

SUPS & DOWNS ... brewers' woes TWO Scots brewing giants almost went down the drain this month when they ran into financial difficulties.

Controversial company BrewDog, founded in Ellon, Aberdeenshire, was rescued by a US cannabis company in a £3million deal that saw the immediate closure of 38 bars and nearly 500 jobs lost.

Days later the owner of Tennent's Lager had to bail out Edinburgh-based Innis & Gunn in a £.5million rescue deal with job losses on the way.

Today The Scottish Sun speaks to Andy Slee, Chief Executive of the Society of Independent Brewers and Associates, about the problems facing the industry.

headline: JEREMY CLARKSON EYES LORD'S BEER DEAL
author: MIKE KEEGAN
text:
EXCLUSIVE

JEREMY CLARKSON'S brewery is in the running to take over as the official beer supplier at Lord's. Last week, Daily Mail Sport told how the home of cricket had dropped BrewDog after the selfstyled 'punk' brand was sold for £3million to a US cannabis giant. And it is understood former Top Gear host Clarkson's Hawkstone, the fastest-growing brewery in the country last year, is among those seeking to replace it at the St John's Wood landmark. Clarkson, who famously runs his own farm in the Cotswolds, is co-owner of the Gloucestershirebased Hawkstone, along with local brewers. Their range is sold in supermarkets and around one in 10 UK pubs and is also exported to 10 countries across Europe. Launched in 2021, it features premium British lagers and ciders and a recently launched zero per cent option. Hawkstone will face competition from several industry heavyweights, with Heineken and CarlsbergBritvic among those in the hunt for an opportunity that has generated serious interest. The agreement with Scottish firm BrewDog at the 31,000-capacity venue was terminated shortly over a year into a four-year deal following its sale, amid a sharp fall in its value which saw 38 UK bars closed and 484 staff let go. Tilray, the US medical cannabis and drinks firm, paid a fraction of the £billion it had been valued at a few years ago. The MCC, which owns Lord's, declined to comment.
